lead: 麺屋 極鶏 一乗寺本店
name_ja: 麺屋 極鶏 一乗寺本店
cuisine: 超濃厚 鶏白湯ラーメン（ドロ系・鶏だく）
neighborhood: 一乗寺（叡山電鉄 一乗寺駅 徒歩約5分／ラーメン街道）, 左京区
gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The ramen is built on wheat noodles, and the ultra-thick chicken broth is seasoned with a soy-sauce-based tare (Japanese shoyu almost always contains wheat). No gluten-free noodle or gluten-free preparation is offered, and the kitchen is a single-concept ramen shop with no dedicated allergen handling. Diners requiring strict gluten avoidance cannot eat safely here.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. The entire menu is anchored on an ultra-concentrated chicken (paitan) broth made by simmering chicken carcasses to a near-pottage consistency, plus chicken-based toppings (鶏だく = extra chicken). There is no plant-based broth or vegan bowl. Vegetarians and vegans cannot be accommodated.
address_ja: 京都府京都市左京区一乗寺西閉川原町29-7
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned coordinates [135.78717, 35.04726] for 京都市左京区一乗寺西閉川原町29; within the Kyoto box.
seats_text: About 19 seats: counter (around 5), one table, plus a couple of raised/tatami seating areas. Compact; table-sharing is common at busy times.
hours_raw: 11:30-22:00（火〜日）。月曜定休＋不定休。スープが無くなり次第終了のため、営業時間内でも早じまいの可能性あり。
closed_days: Mondays, plus occasional irregular closures. May also close early once the broth sells out.
cultural_comfort_note: A fast-moving, ticket-machine ramen shop with frequent queues; basic Japanese and reading the ticket machine help, but pointing and a friendly greeting will get you through.

bio.background: Opened in 2011 on Ichijoji's famous "Ramen Street" (ラーメン街道) in Kyoto's Sakyo Ward, Menya Gokkei staked out its own corner of one of Japan's most competitive ramen districts. The name 極鶏 ("Gokkei") fuses 極 (extreme/perfected) with 鶏 (chicken), and the shop has stayed laser-focused on a single idea ever since.
bio.specialty: The signature 極鶏 鶏だく: an ultra-concentrated chicken paitan so thick it is often described as 'doro-kei' (muddy) and famously dense enough that a spoon can stand upright in it. Chicken carcasses are simmered to an extreme, near-pottage consistency to create what the shop calls a 'soup you eat rather than drink.' Variations build on the same base: 赤だく (fiery red chili), 黒だく (fragrant black mayu garlic oil), and 魚だく (a fish-infused twist).
bio.philosophy: A one-bowl, one-idea shop: rather than chase variety, Gokkei pours everything into perfecting a single, unmistakable chicken broth — its own self-described 'one-of-a-kind soup to savor.'

anecdotes.text: The broth is so heavy it is regularly described as standing a spoon upright — closer to a chicken pottage than a conventional ramen soup.
Set on Ichijoji's Ramen Street, the shop draws lines from before opening, and regulars advise arriving early; the day can end abruptly once the broth runs out.
The shop frames its broth as a '超濃厚肉濁鶏白湯' (ultra-rich, meat-clouded chicken paitan) and sells frozen versions to ship its signature soup beyond the counter.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Single-concept ramen shop: wheat noodles and a soy-sauce-seasoned chicken broth are central to every bowl, with no gluten-free option or separate preparation. Not suitable for strict gluten avoidance.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is seasoned with a soy-sauce-based tare; Japanese soy sauce almost always contains wheat, so the soup itself is not gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All bowls are built on chicken broth and chicken toppings; there is no plant-based broth, so vegan diners cannot be served.

caveats: Chef/owner name not found on fetched pages. The kyototravels.com blog returned HTTP 403 and could not be read. No explicit allergen/gluten/vegan policy is published; dietary conclusions are inferred from the standard ramen build (wheat noodles + soy-based tare + chicken broth) confirmed across sources. Hours are listed 11:30-22:00 but the shop closes early when broth sells out, and has irregular closures beyond the regular Monday.

lead: セアブラノ神 壬生本店
name_ja: セアブラノ神 壬生本店
cuisine: 背脂煮干しそば（niboshi・背脂醤油）／豚骨魚介つけ麺・まぜそば
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. This is a back-fat niboshi ramen and mazesoba specialist: bowls are built on wheat ramen noodles with pork/fish-based broth and a wheat-containing shoyu tare, plus tsukemen and mazesoba (also wheat noodles). No gluten-free noodle or dedicated preparation is indicated anywhere. Celiac diners should not eat here.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The core of every signature bowl is animal-based: pork back fat (背脂), niboshi (dried sardine) and pork-bone broth, low-temperature pork chashu and braised pork toppings. No vegan or vegetable broth option is indicated.
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区壬生相合町25-4 デイスターアベニュー 1F
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned coordinates for 京都府京都市中京区壬生相合町25; within the Kyoto box. lng 135.746307, lat 35.000519.
neighborhood: 四条大宮（中京区・壬生）
seats_text: 16席（カウンター6席、テーブル 4人×1卓・6人×1卓）。小ぢんまりした店で昼は行列ができることが多い。
closed_days: 元旦（1月1日）のみ。基本的に通年営業。
cultural_comfort_note: A small, busy local ramen counter where ordering is in Japanese and there is no reservation system; come ready to queue and order simply, ideally with a few words of Japanese or by pointing.

bio.background: セアブラノ神 壬生本店 is a small, much-loved Kyoto ramen counter in the Shijo-Omiya / Mibu neighborhood, tucked into the ground floor of a building on Mibu-Aioi-cho a short walk from Hankyu Omiya Station. It built its reputation on a niche idea: a niboshi (dried-sardine) and pork-bone broth crowned with a generous lid of pork back fat (背脂) — a style that nods to Niigata's Tsubame-Sanjo ramen — and on bold Taiwan-style mazesoba.
bio.specialty: Its signature is the back-fat niboshi soba (背脂煮干そば / 特菜油煮干そば, around ￥1,150): a broth that looks rich from the cap of back fat but drinks surprisingly clean and clear, with a pronounced sardine aroma, served over springy medium-to-thick noodles and finished with low-temperature-cooked pork. The mazesoba (Taiwan-style mixed noodles) is an equally celebrated draw, built on a long-simmered pork-bone base and meant to be stirred from the bottom before eating.
bio.philosophy: Balance over heaviness — the back fat is used for richness and sweetness, not to overwhelm, so the niboshi and the broth still come through clean. Hearty, generous bowls (rare and braised pork, onion, nori, free rice refills) at a neighborhood price.

anecdotes.text: Reviewers describe the back-fat cap as deceptively light: despite the visible fat the soup drinks clean with a clear sardine fragrance, over chewy thick or medium-thick noodles likened to good Okinawan soba.
Local food writers single out the mazesoba as a standout — 'mix it from the bottom and it's delicious; among the mazesoba I've been into lately, this is top-tier.'
The name セアブラノ神 ('god of back fat') points straight at the kitchen's calling card: the generous use of pork 背脂 (back fat) in its bowls.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Bowls are built on wheat ramen noodles (ramen, tsukemen and mazesoba) shared in the same kitchen and water; high cross-contact risk for celiac diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Shoyu/back-fat-shoyu-style tare is used; standard Japanese soy sauce and tare contain wheat. No wheat-free tare is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths are pork-bone and niboshi (fish) based with pork back fat and pork toppings throughout; no vegan or vegetable option indicated.

caveats: Cash only, no reservations, only 16 seats, and lunch queues are common — plan for a wait. No official website found; hours and address confirmed via Tabelog. No allergen/gluten/vegan accommodation information was found from any source, so the dietary safety read is inferred from the menu and rated low-confidence; celiac and vegan diners should treat this as unsafe.
sources: https://msearch.gsi.go.jp/address-search/AddressSearch?q=京都府京都市中京区壬生相合町25-4

cuisine: 町中華・中華そば（黒い醤油スープ／朝ラー）
gf_detail: This is a classic Kyoto chuka soba shop built around wheat noodles in a dark soy-sauce-based broth (soy sauce contains wheat), with a pork-and-chicken-bone foundation. There is no gluten-free noodle or menu option and no indication of any allergen accommodation. Not safe for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: The entire menu centers on a pork-and-chicken-bone broth with chashu (roast pork) toppings; the signature fried rice is also typically cooked with pork fat and egg. There are no vegan or vegetarian ramen options.
address_ja: 京都府京都市下京区東塩小路向畑町569
geocode_note: GSI geocode of address_ja returned [135.761459, 34.985992], within the Kyoto box; matches the known location just east of Kyoto Station on Higashi-Shiokoji / Takabashi.
neighborhood: 京都駅（塩小路通／高橋たかばし、京都駅八条口近く）, 下京区
seats_text: 51 seats total — 1st floor: 3 counter seats plus 6 tables (4 seats each); 2nd floor: 6 tables (4 seats each).
hours_raw: 9:00-20:00 (last order 19:30). Closed Wednesdays; additional 2-3 irregular closure days each month.
closed_days: Wednesdays, plus 2-3 additional irregular closure days each month
cultural_comfort_note: A busy, no-frills old-school ramen counter where ordering in simple Japanese (or by pointing at the short menu) and paying cash makes the visit smoothest.

bio.background: Shinpuku Saikan began life in 1938 as a humble street stall (yatai) near Kyoto Station and has grown into one of the founding names of Kansai ramen. The honten (main shop) still stands on Higashi-Shiokoji Street by the Takabashi crossing, a short walk from Kyoto Station, and remains a cash-only, family-run institution that locals and travelers line up for.
bio.specialty: Its signature is chuka soba in a startlingly jet-black, deeply colored soy-sauce broth built on a chicken- and pork-bone base — the original 'Kyoto Black.' Despite the intense dark appearance, regulars describe the soup as surprisingly mild, clean, and easy to drink, which is why it doubles as a beloved asa-ra (morning ramen) from the 9:00 opening. The pork-fat fried rice (yakimeshi), often ordered alongside a small bowl, is nearly as famous as the noodles.
bio.philosophy: Captured in the shop's own line — roughly, 'a bowl that makes you want to eat it again and again' — the focus is on that one addictive, instantly recognizable black broth rather than menu breadth.

anecdotes.text: The shop is so identified with Kyoto Station that it advertises itself as the 'Kyoto Takabashi chuka soba specialist,' referencing the nearby Takabashi crossing where the main shop sits.
Opening at 9:00 a.m., it is one of the city's classic spots for asa-ra (morning ramen) — the broth is gentle enough to enjoy first thing in the day, and it stays busy from open to close.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The core dish is wheat ramen noodles, so wheat is present and boiled in shared water; no gluten-free preparation exists.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature black broth is built on a soy-sauce tare; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, making the broth itself unsafe for celiac diners.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is a pork- and chicken-bone base and chashu (roast pork) is a standard topping; nothing on the menu is vegan or vegetarian.

lead: 吟醸らーめん 久保田
name_ja: 吟醸らーめん 久保田
cuisine: つけ麺・らーめん（吟醸味噌つけ麺／吟醸醤油らーめん）
neighborhood: 五条・下京区（京都市営地下鉄 五条駅）
gf_detail: No gluten-free options. The entire menu is built on thick wheat noodles (the signature tsukemen noodles are notably thick and udon-like), and the bowls use miso/shoyu tare almost certainly made with wheat-based soy sauce, served with pork- and seafood-based broths. Not suitable for celiac or strict gluten avoidance. No allergen menu or dedicated gluten-free handling is advertised.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The core offerings — miso tsukemen, meat-miso ramen, and chicken-shoyu ramen — all rely on animal products (ground pork, pork/seafood broth, chicken). There is no vegetable or plant-based ramen on the menu, and side rice dishes (chirimen-sansho rice, takana rice) also include animal/fish ingredients.
address_ja: 京都府京都市下京区西松屋町563 フジ萬ビル 1F
geocode_note: Geocoded from address_ja via GSI; first feature coordinates [135.755585, 34.992844], within the Kyoto bounding box.
seats_text: About 10 counter seats only, with partitions between each seat (Ichiran-style individual booths).
closed_days: No regular closing day (open daily from September onward); closed only over the year-end / New Year period.
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, popular counter shop with individual partitioned seats that already draws many overseas visitors; a few words of Japanese and patience for the line and the long noodle-cook time go a long way.
caveats: Noodles take about 15 minutes to boil to order, so expect a wait once seated. Lines form at peak times and the room is very small — luggage is discouraged. Cash only.

bio.background: A small ramen-ya near Gojo in Kyoto's Shimogyo ward, a stretch close to Kyoto Station that has surprisingly few standout eateries, which helped Kubota stand out and draw a steady mix of locals and international visitors. The dining room is intimate — roughly ten counter seats divided by partitions, a layout regulars liken to a see-through version of the famous solo-booth chains.
bio.specialty: The house signature is the ginjo miso tsukemen: thick, glossy, springy noodles — often compared to Sanuki udon for their substance and chew — served with a rich, textured miso dipping broth layered with seafood umami and ground pork, finished with a hint of chili-oil spice that some have likened to Kansai-style spice curry. A ginjo shoyu (chicken-soy) ramen and a meat-miso ramen round out the lineup, with marinated egg and small rice bowls (chirimen-sansho, takana) as add-ons.
bio.philosophy: The cooking leans into a made-to-order ideal — the noodles are boiled fresh for roughly fifteen minutes per order — prioritizing texture and the quality of the noodle itself over speed or piling on toppings.

anecdotes.text: The shop was named to the Tabelog 100 West list in 2025, marking it among the region's notable ramen destinations.

Diners describe the partitioned ten-seat counter as a 'see-through Ichiran,' and the freshly boiled noodles are praised as delicious enough to enjoy on their own, though the made-to-order boil means a wait.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The shoyu ramen and miso tare are made in the standard Japanese ramen style, which relies on wheat-based soy sauce; no wheat-free tare is offered.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broths and toppings center on pork, chicken and seafood; there is no separate vegetable broth or plant-based preparation line.

cuisine: 鶏豚骨 白濁中華そば（こってり白湯）
neighborhood: 一乗寺（叡山電鉄 一乗寺駅 徒歩約4分／ラーメン街道）, 左京区
gf_detail: This is a classic wheat-noodle ramen shop with no gluten-free accommodation. The signature bowl is a creamy chicken-and-pork-bone white broth served with custom wheat-kansui noodles, and the menu includes wheat-battered, curry-dusted karaage. There is no indication of gluten-free options, allergen handling, or a dedicated fryer; the karaage is deep-fried in shared oil. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: The core product is a long-simmered pork-bone and chicken-bone broth, with pork chashu, beef-sinew (suji) ramen, and fried chicken as headline items. No vegan or vegetarian ramen is offered or advertised. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
address_ja: 京都府京都市左京区一乗寺高槻町10
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned coordinates for 京都府京都市左京区一乗寺高槻町１０番地; within the Kyoto bounding box.
seats_text: 24 seats: counter (7+1) plus table seating (two tables of 2, one of 3); varied layout with window, wall, counter, and table sections.
hours_raw: 11:30〜02:00（通し営業、ランチから深夜まで）／定休日：不定休
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular; check website/storefront)
cultural_comfort_note: A bustling, cash-only neighborhood ramen shop on Ichijoji's "Ramen Street" geared toward locals and students; expect a queue and minimal English, so basic Japanese and pointing at the menu help.
caveats: Cash only; no reservations and frequent queues; irregular closures, so confirm before a special trip. Continuous service from late morning into the early hours. Two parking spaces available (rare in this area).

bio.background: Takayasu is a fixture of Ichijoji's famed "Ramen Street" in Kyoto's Sakyo Ward, a neighborhood packed with competing ramen shops and a steady stream of students. It is widely credited as one of the shops that introduced the rich, white-cloudy pork-and-chicken-bone style to Kyoto, and it has remained a perennial line-out-the-door destination.
bio.specialty: The signature Chuka Soba is built on a milky, creamy white broth simmered for roughly 22 to 24 hours from pork bones and chicken bones, paired with custom noodles whose kansui balance is tuned to the soup. The other crowd-pleaser is the oversized, curry-dusted karaage (fried chicken), crisp outside with juices that burst on the first bite; a beef-sinew (suji) ramen runs as a limited item.
bio.philosophy: The shop frames its goal as serving not just good ramen but a whole experience -- atmosphere and space included -- aiming to bring a sense of excitement and an enriched table to every customer.

anecdotes.text: The dining room has an unusually varied, almost cafe-like layout -- window seats, wall seating, a curved counter, and tables under bright lighting -- which, together with generous portions and low prices, makes it a favorite in this student town.
Unusually for the area, the shop offers two parking spaces, a notable convenience since most Ramen Street shops have none, and it runs continuous service from late morning into the early hours.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles boiled in shared water; signature broth served over wheat-kansui noodles. No gluten-free accommodation indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All featured items are pork/chicken-bone broth based with pork, beef-sinew, and fried-chicken toppings; no vegan/vegetarian preparation noted.

lead: ラーメン荘 夢を語れ
name_ja: ラーメン荘 夢を語れ
cuisine: Jiro-style ramen (二郎系・関西二郎インスパイア)
neighborhood: 一乗寺（ラーメン街道）, 左京区
gf_detail: This is a Jiro-style shop built on ultra-thick hand-kneaded wheat flour noodles (極太平打ち手もみ麺), a wheat-based shoyu tare, and a pork-bone broth — there is no gluten-free option and the noodles are the entire point of the bowl. No allergy accommodation is advertised. Not suitable for anyone avoiding gluten or wheat.
vegan_detail: Every bowl is centered on a non-emulsified pork-bone (豚骨) shoyu broth with pork chashu and back fat (背脂), plus a wheat-based tare. There are no vegan or vegetarian options, and the only free toppings are garlic and more fat (アブラ). Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
address_ja: 京都府京都市左京区一乗寺西杉ノ宮町48-1
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for the exact street address; within the Kyoto bounding box.
seats_text: About 10 seats at an L-shaped counter; standing-room queue spills outside during busy hours.
hours_raw: 火～金 17:00-23:00／土 12:00-15:00, 17:00-23:00／日・月 定休（出典により営業時間に多少の差異あり、22:45閉店との記載も）
closed_days: Sundays and Mondays
cultural_comfort_note: A no-frills, fast-paced Jiro-style counter where you order at the ticket machine and call your toppings in Japanese — knowing a few words (ニンニク/ヤサイ/アブラ/カラメ) and the unwritten etiquette of finishing your bowl makes the visit far smoother.
caveats: Hours vary slightly between sources (some list a 22:45 close and differing closed days); cash only via a ticket machine; no allergy, gluten-free, or vegan accommodation. Portions are very large — you can ask for 野菜少な目 (less vegetables) when ordering.

bio.background: Founded in October 2006 in the student town of Ichijoji, this is widely regarded as the pioneer of Kansai-style Jiro ramen. Founder Nishioka Tsuyoshi apprenticed in the Ramen Marji lineage (itself descended from the legendary Ramen Jiro in Tokyo) before opening his own counter here. In 2012 he carried the style abroad, opening 'Yume Wo Katare' in Boston — the first Jiro-style shop to expand overseas.
bio.specialty: A single, towering bowl: ultra-thick hand-kneaded flat wheat noodles in a refreshing, non-emulsified pork-bone shoyu broth, crowned with thick pork chashu, a mountain of bean sprouts and cabbage, and back fat. Free toppings of garlic and extra fat are called out at the counter.
bio.philosophy: The name means 'tell your dream.' True to it, the shop gives diners who wish to a space, after finishing their bowl, to speak their dreams aloud — turning a bowl of ramen into a small ritual of encouragement.

anecdotes.text: After eating, customers who wish are invited to stand and 'tell their dream' (夢を語る) — the practice that gives the shop its name.
Founder Nishioka took the Ichijoji style to Boston in October 2012, making it the first Jiro-style ramen shop to open overseas.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The bowl is built on ultra-thick hand-kneaded wheat noodles, a wheat-based shoyu tare, and pork-bone broth; gluten is unavoidable and there is no GF preparation.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is a soy-sauce (shoyu) seasoned pork-bone base; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Every bowl contains pork chashu, pork-bone broth, and back fat (背脂); no vegan or vegetarian preparation exists.

cuisine: あっさり醤油・塩らーめん（鶏ガラ＋和風だし）
neighborhood: 円町（西ノ京北円町）, 中京区
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区西ノ京北円町1-8
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned coordinates [135.731613, 35.019409] for 京都府京都市中京区西ノ京北円町1-8; within Kyoto box.
seats_text: 9 counter seats only, arranged in an L-shape along the open kitchen.
hours_raw: Sources disagree. Older/Michelin-era listings: 11:30–14:00, 18:00–22:00, closed Tuesdays. More recent Tabelog listing: Thu–Sun 11:30–15:30, closed Mon–Wed. Verify locally before visiting.
closed_days: Recent listing: Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday. (Older listings showed Tuesday only with lunch+dinner service.)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny 9-seat counter shop run by the owner and one assistant; little to no English, lines are common, and the quiet workflow rewards ordering simply and eating without fuss.
gf_detail: Not safe for celiac. Bowls are built on wheat ramen noodles with a soy-sauce (shoyu) or salt tare; shoyu tare almost always contains wheat, and house-made noodles share the same kitchen. No gluten-free menu or accommodation found in any source.
vegan_detail: All bowls are built on chicken-bone broth plus Japanese dashi (bonito/seafood stock) and topped with pork chashu. There is no vegetable-based or vegan broth, and no vegan/vegetarian options were found.
caveats: Opening hours and closed days conflict between sources (older lunch+dinner / Tuesday-closed schedule vs. a more recent Thu–Sun daytime-only schedule); confirm before visiting. Cash only. Expect a queue; lines form before opening on weekends and weekday waits can run 30–40 minutes.

bio.background: A small, quietly run ramen counter near Emmachi station, kept deliberately modest: just nine seats along an L-shaped counter, worked by the owner and one assistant. Despite earning a Michelin Bib Gourmand, the shop has held onto unusually low prices, with the basic bowl staying in the 650-yen range while most acclaimed ramen shops have drifted well past 1,000 yen.
bio.specialty: An 'assari' (light, clean) style: the soy-sauce ramen layers a chicken-bone base with Japanese dashi, while the salt (shio) ramen pairs chicken and seafood stock with a lift of yuzu. Tsukemen is served with house-made medium-thick noodles and a squeeze of sudachi citrus that shifts the flavor as you eat. Chashu is a notably good cut of pork, served sliced or worked into a rice bowl.
bio.philosophy: Refinement without spectacle: a restrained, balanced bowl at an everyday price, prioritizing clean broth and good noodles over flash or markup.

anecdotes.text: Even with Michelin recognition, the shop keeps its signature bowl around 650 yen and stays cash-only with a nine-seat counter, so queues form before opening and weekday waits can reach 30-40 minutes.
The shio ramen finishes with yuzu and the tsukemen comes with sudachi citrus, small aromatic touches that define the shop's light, clean style.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles are the base of every bowl and the shoyu tare is wheat-based; a nine-seat single-counter kitchen means no separation from gluten.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The standard ramen uses a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare, which in Japanese ramen almost always contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths are chicken-bone plus fish/seafood dashi (bonito), and bowls are topped with pork chashu; there is no plant-based broth on offer.

cuisine: 魚介出汁の中華そば（淡麗系・白醤油／黒醤油）
vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian bowls are offered. While the signature broth is uniquely a 100% seafood dashi with no pork or chicken fat, it is fish-based rather than plant-based, and the wheat noodles and soy tare rule out a vegan meal.
gf_detail: The signature dashi soba uses house-made noodles milled from stone-ground whole wheat flour, and the tare is white or black soy sauce (typically wheat-fermented). Both the noodles and the seasoning contain wheat, so the bowls are not celiac-safe and no gluten-free option exists. No allergen-handling or dedicated-equipment information was published.
address_ja: 京都府京都市下京区恵美須之町寺町通仏光寺下ル542
geocode_note: GSI geocoded at the chō level (恵美須之町); coordinates fall within the Kyoto box and match the Teramachi-dori / Bukkoji area south of Shijo.
neighborhood: 京都河原町（四条通 南側・寺町通沿い）、下京区
seats_text: 16席（カウンター8席、テーブル4席×2）
closed_days: 不定休（曜日固定の定休日なし）
cultural_comfort_note: A small, busy 16-seat counter shop with no reservations and frequent queues; a few Japanese greetings and patience with the line will serve you well, though the central tourist location means staff are used to visitors.

bio.background: 麺屋 猪一 opened in July 2013 and moved to its current location on Teramachi-dori, just south of Shijo near Kawaramachi, in 2019. It has grown into one of Kyoto's most recognized ramen destinations, earning the Michelin Bib Gourmand for roughly nine years running.
bio.specialty: The signature is the dashi soba, served in two styles: white (shiro-shoyu) for a clear, delicate, umami-forward bowl, and black (kuro-shoyu) for a mild yet richer one. House-made thin-but-firm noodles are milled from stone-ground whole wheat.
bio.philosophy: The shop is built around dashi culture rather than heavy tonkotsu trends: the broth is a 100% seafood stock with no animal fat such as pork or chicken, drawn from several carefully selected premium dried-fish (fushi) varieties, extracted slowly at low temperature to coax out a clean, layered umami.

anecdotes.text: The broth contains no pork or chicken fat at all — it is built entirely from a blend of top-grade dried fish slowly drawn at low temperature, a deliberate stand for Kyoto's dashi tradition.
The noodles are made in-house from stone-ground whole wheat flour, described as thin but firm.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Bowls are built on house-made whole-wheat noodles plus white or black soy sauce tare; no gluten-free preparation is offered and shared cooking water makes the kitchen unsuitable for celiac diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The defining seasoning is white (shiro) or black (kuro) soy sauce tare, which is normally wheat-fermented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All bowls are based on a seafood (dried-fish) dashi, so no broth is free of animal-derived ingredients.

positives.text: The broth uniquely contains no pork or chicken fat — a 100% seafood dashi — which may matter to diners avoiding mammalian/poultry products specifically.

caveats: Phone number not publicly confirmed. Hours apply daily but closures are irregular (不定休) with no fixed weekday off, so verify before visiting. Geocode is chō-level (恵美須之町), accurate to the block but not the exact storefront.

lead: 中華そば ほそかわ
name_ja: 中華そば ほそかわ 花屋町店
cuisine: 京都ラーメン / 中華そば (Kyoto-style back-fat shoyu ramen)
gf_detail: Not safe for celiac or strict gluten-free diners. The bowl is built on wheat ramen noodles in a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare that is almost certainly wheat-based, served in a chicken-bone-and-back-fat / pork broth. There is no gluten-free noodle or menu option, and the kitchen is a single small ramen line with shared water and equipment, so cross-contact with wheat is unavoidable.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians. The entire menu is animal-based: the broth is simmered chicken bones and pork with floating back fat (背脂), the only dishes are chuka soba and chashu (roast pork) ramen, and the noodles sit in a meat-and-soy broth. There are no plant-based options.
address_ja: 京都府京都市右京区西京極南庄境町15
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 京都府京都市右京区西京極南庄境町15; within the Kyoto bounding box.
seats_text: 22 seats — 16 at the counter plus 3 tables seating about 6; a roomy, family-friendly room for a ramen shop, with parking (about 5 spaces).
hours_raw: 10:00-19:00 (Mon-Sat); closed Sunday. Note: one source lists hours to 20:00 — confirm locally.
closed_days: Sundays
cultural_comfort_note: A no-frills neighborhood ramen counter where ordering is simple and Japanese is expected; point at the menu and a few words will carry you through.
caveats: Broth is chicken-bone-and-back-fat shoyu with pork umami (Kyoto style), not a pork-bone tonkotsu as sometimes labeled. Cash only; no reservations. One source lists closing at 20:00 rather than 19:00 — verify on arrival.
neighborhood: Nishi-Kyogoku, Ukyo-ku (near Nishi-Kyogoku Station)

bio.background: The shop opened in 1985 after the owner spent eight years training at Masutani (ますたに), the Kyoto institution credited with the city's back-fat shoyu ramen style. He carried that foundation forward but added his own touches to build an independent identity rather than a copy.
bio.specialty: A classic Kyoto chuka soba: a glistening back-fat soy-sauce broth that looks rich but finishes clean, built on long-simmered chicken bones with a smooth pork umami, poured over fine straight noodles. The menu is deliberately tight — chuka soba and a chashu (roast pork) version — and the house chili-miso (辛味噌) can be stirred in partway through for a punch of heat.
bio.philosophy: Conservative and assured: a quintessential, standard Kyoto ramen that respects the regional tradition and avoids needless innovation. The aim is the 'back-fat chacha' look with a surprisingly refined, drink-it-to-the-bottom finish.

anecdotes.text: The founder trained for eight years at Masutani, the lineage often credited with originating Kyoto's back-fat shoyu ramen, before opening his own shop in 1985.
Despite the heavy-looking back-fat-and-soy broth, regulars note it drinks surprisingly light and clean thanks to the chicken-bone base, so you can finish the bowl without it feeling heavy.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Single small ramen line built around wheat noodles boiled in shared water; wheat cross-contact is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Broth and tare are soy-sauce (shoyu) based, which is virtually always wheat-fermented; no gluten-free tare is offered.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Every bowl uses a chicken-bone, pork and back-fat broth with meat toppings; nothing on the menu is plant-based.

cuisine: Motsu tsukemen / motsu ramen (offal dipping noodles in chicken-paitan and seafood dip)
neighborhood: Nakagyo-ku, down a hidden alley near Shijo-Karasuma (Shijo / Karasuma Station)
gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The core dish is built on wheat: house-made flat wheat noodles (one of the two noodle choices is explicitly "whole wheat," the other yuzu-flavored — both wheat), plus a rich chicken-paitan-and-seafood dip that in this style of shop is seasoned with wheat-based shoyu tare. Offal (seared hoso tripe) is the headline ingredient, but the broth and noodles make this a hard no for gluten avoidance. No gluten-free menu, no dedicated preparation, and no allergen info is published. A celiac diner cannot eat safely here.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The entire concept is animal-based: chicken-bone paitan broth blended with seafood dashi (bonito-forward), and rare seared beef hoso (offal/tripe) as the signature topping. Only two menu items exist — motsu tsukemen and motsu ramen — both meat/fish-based. No vegetable or plant-based bowl is offered, and there is no published vegan accommodation.
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区観音堂町471-1
geocode_note: GSI address search on 京都府京都市中京区観音堂町471-1 returned [135.757385, 35.004242], within the Kyoto box; consistent with the Nakagyo-ku off-Shijo-Karasuma location.
seats_text: 10 counter seats only (small renovated-townhouse interior with spotlit counter)
hours_raw: Mon–Fri 11:30–15:00 (L.O. 14:50) & 18:00–22:00 (L.O. 21:50); Sat/Sun/holidays 11:30–15:00 (L.O. 14:50) & 18:00–21:00 (L.O. 20:50). Both services end early when sold out (売り切れ次第終了). Closed only year-end/New Year per search summary.
closed_days: No regular weekly closing day (open daily); closed only for year-end/New Year holidays. May close early when sold out.
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny 10-seat counter down a hard-to-find alley with a Japanese ticket-machine ordering system and brisk, locals-paced service — comfortable if you can manage a little Japanese and the kiosk, but not set up for English hand-holding.
caveats: Cash vs cashless: the official site states the shop is cashless-only (キャッシュレス専門店, no IC cards), while Tabelog lists cards/e-money/QR accepted — bring a card and do not rely on cash. Ordering is via a ticket vending machine inside the townhouse; reservations are not accepted; only two dishes are served. Hours can change at short notice and service ends when sold out.

bio.background: Sugari is part of the well-loved Kyoto ramen family centered on "Takakura Nijo," with this Wajo Ryomen Sugari branch occupying a renovated century-old machiya (wooden townhouse) tucked down an easily-missed alley near Shijo-Karasuma. You enter along a stone-paved corridor, buy a ticket from a vending machine that sits almost incongruously inside the rustic space, then pass through to a small, elegantly spotlit 10-seat counter.
bio.specialty: Motsu tsukemen — house-made flat noodles served alongside a thick, intensely rich dip of chicken-bone paitan blended with seafood dashi, crowned with rare seared beef hoso (a prized cut of offal that's torched with a blowtorch tableside-style before serving for a fragrant, aromatic finish). Accompanied by garlic buds and grilled leeks; a seasoned egg is a popular add-on. A motsu ramen (offal in broth) is the only other option.
bio.philosophy: A two-dish, do-one-thing-well house: rare offal treated with care, paired with a deeply concentrated chicken-and-seafood broth and noodles made in-house, served fast in an intimate machiya setting.

anecdotes.text: The hoso tripe is lightly seared with a blowtorch before serving, giving it a fragrant, smoky aroma that defines the bowl.
Noodles come in two styles — a whole-wheat noodle and a yuzu-scented noodle — both house-made and flat, designed to carry the thick dip.
The shop sits down a hard-to-find alley near the Kawaramachi/Shijo-Karasuma area, inside a high-ceilinged traditional townhouse reached via a stone corridor and small courtyard.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core dish uses house-made wheat noodles (both noodle options — whole-wheat and yuzu — are wheat) cooked in shared water, making the kitchen unsuitable for gluten avoidance.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No allergen or tare ingredient disclosure is published; as a standard motsu tsukemen shop the seasoning base is expected to include wheat-based soy sauce, but this is not confirmed on any fetched page.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Both menu items are animal-based (chicken-paitan + seafood dashi broth with beef hoso offal); no plant-based option exists, so all dishes involve meat/fish.

cuisine: 濃厚豚骨魚介 つけ麺・ラーメン (tonkotsu-gyokai tsukemen and ramen)
neighborhood: Nakagyo-ku, near Karasuma-Oike Station
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区観音町64-1
geocode_note: GSI address-search on 京都府京都市中京区観音町64-1 returned [135.762772, 35.013523]; within Kyoto box and consistent with the shop's location ~6 min from Karasuma-Oike Station.
seats_text: Roughly 6-8 counter seats only, arranged around an open kitchen; an intimate single-counter space.
hours_raw: 11:30-15:00, 18:00-21:00 (holidays L.O. 14:50 / 20:50). Open daily; closed only year-end/New Year.
closed_days: Year-end and New Year holidays only (otherwise open daily; may close early when sold out).
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, unsigned counter shop where locals slip in and out quietly; a little Japanese and ramen-counter etiquette go a long way, though the staff are used to visitors who found them by word of mouth.
gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The bowls are built on wheat noodles in a long-simmered pork-bone and dried-fish/bonito broth seasoned with soy-based tare, so gluten is present throughout. No gluten-free noodles or accommodation is advertised, and the kitchen is a single tight counter. Treat as off-limits for strict gluten avoidance.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The core broth is a 15-hour pork-bone (tonkotsu) and seafood (niboshi/bonito) stock, and signature toppings are motsu (offal), char-roasted pork, or wagyu. There is no plant-based broth or vegan ramen option here.
caveats: Seat count reported as ~6 (Tabelog) to ~8 (blog), counter only. Official site is a brand portal for the Takakura Nijo / Sugari group; the individual shop confirms address 観音町64-1. Vegan/GF accommodation status inferred from menu and broth composition, not from an explicit allergen statement by the shop.

bio.background: 麺や高倉二条 is the flagship of a small, tightly run Kyoto ramen family that also includes the Sugari shops and a couple of sibling counters. The shops are deliberately low-key: several have no noren curtain and no signboard, so from the street they read more like a quiet machiya townhouse than a ramen joint, and they fill mostly by word of mouth.
bio.specialty: The signature is a rich tonkotsu-gyokai (pork-bone and dried-fish/bonito) bowl, served as both ramen and the group's celebrated tsukemen. The dipping broth combines pork bones simmered for around 15 hours with niboshi and katsuobushi dashi, balanced toward a deep, slightly sweet-and-sour finish; the noodles are house-made, flat and silky (with a yuzu-noodle option on some bowls), and the prized topping is motsu (offal), alongside char-pork and wagyu choices.
bio.philosophy: The group cooks under the banner of 医食同源 (ishoku-dogen) — 'food and medicine share one source' — aiming for bowls that nourish as much as they satisfy, presented with an understated, distinctly Kyoto restraint that 'doesn't shout to assert itself.'

anecdotes.text: The shopfront is intentionally unmarked — a stylish, almost ryotei-like facade with no signage — so first-timers often walk past it; it has become a quiet local favorite found largely through word of mouth.
Across the group's counters, chopsticks and condiments are tucked into a little drawer at each seat — a small signature quirk of the Takakura Nijo / Sugari shops.
The tsukemen dipping broth is built from pork bones simmered roughly 15 hours blended with dried-fish and bonito dashi, and motsu is the topping most often recommended to newcomers.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen/tsukemen noodles are the core of every bowl and are boiled in shared water; no gluten-free option exists, so cross-contamination is unavoidable for celiac diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Bowls are seasoned with a soy-based (shoyu) tare, which in Japanese ramen is typically brewed from wheat-containing soy sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broths are animal-based (15-hour pork-bone stock plus fish dashi); there is no separate vegan preparation, so any item would contact pork and fish stock.

lead: 京都鶏白湯そば 純
name_ja: 京都鶏白湯そば 純
cuisine: 鶏白湯そば (tori-paitan ramen — creamy Kagoshima-chicken broth)
gf_detail: No gluten-free options. This is wheat-noodle chicken-paitan ramen; the broth and tare carry the usual wheat risk, and no allergen menu or GF accommodation is published. The signature bowl is built on wheat noodles in an emulsified chicken (and small amount of pork-bone) broth — not celiac-safe.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly shop. The core product is chicken-paitan broth made from Kagoshima chicken (with small amounts of Miyazaki/Oita pork bone), char siu, eggs, and seven kinds of dried seafood. Some lighter/"vegetable" menu structure is mentioned but no genuine vegan or vegetarian ramen is offered, and broths are animal-based throughout.
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区三本木町462-5 1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 京都府京都市中京区三本木町462-5 returned [135.760666, 35.01683], within the Kyoto box. Matches the shop's stated location ~1-3 min walk from Marutamachi Station (Karasuma Line), south of the Imperial Palace.
neighborhood: Nakagyo-ku, near Marutamachi Station (Karasuma Line), south of the Kyoto Imperial Palace
seats_text: 18 seats: 4 counter seats plus tables (3 two-tops, 2 four-tops)
hours_raw: 11:00-15:00 (L.O. 14:30) / 17:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:30). Closed Wednesday evenings (per official site).
closed_days: Wednesday evenings (dinner service only); lunch served daily. Note: one blog lists Thursday closed and Tabelog shows no regular closure, so confirm on arrival.
cultural_comfort_note: A small neighborhood ramen counter that has an English website and is used to local diners; basic Japanese greetings smooth things along, but you can manage with pointing and a few words.
caveats: Cash only, no reservations, and seating is limited (18). Closed day is reported inconsistently across sources (official site says Wednesday evenings; one blog says Thursday) — verify before a special trip. A second branch opened in Shijo-Karasuma in August 2025; this record is the original Marutamachi honten.

bio.background: A modern chicken-paitan specialist tucked into a residential pocket of Nakagyo-ku, a short walk south of the Imperial Palace and steps from Marutamachi Station. The owner came up as a Japanese-cuisine (washoku) chef before opening this ramen shop, and that fine-dining training shows in the careful, clean presentation. The honten deliberately serves its neighborhood rather than chasing tourists, even as a second branch later opened in busy Shijo-Karasuma.
bio.specialty: Tori-paitan soba — a creamy, foamy chicken-broth ramen. The kitchen builds the soup mainly from Kagoshima chicken (with a small amount of Miyazaki and Oita pork bone) and finishes each bowl by force-emulsifying it in a blender (kyousei nyuuka), whipping the broth into a smooth, almost espuma-like foam that clings to the medium-thick noodles. The flagship is the rich foamy tori-paitan; variations include a yuzu-salt foam, a miso foam, and a seafood-leaning version made with seven kinds of dried fish. The tokusei (special) bowl piles on extra char siu and egg.
bio.philosophy: Depth and texture through technique rather than long brute-force simmering — emulsifying the broth to coax out umami and a creamy mouthfeel — paired with the polish of a trained washoku cook and a focus on regulars over tourist trade.

anecdotes.text: Each bowl is individually blasted in a blender to force-emulsify the broth, which is what gives the soup its signature foamy, creamy body and deeper umami.
Lemon and three kinds of vinegar are set out so diners can add acidity partway through and shift the broth's flavor — an aji-hen (flavor-change) ritual the shop encourages.
The owner moved from a career as a Japanese-cuisine chef into running this ramen shop, and keeps the focus on serving local residents rather than tourists despite the central location.

cuisine: Ramen (rich tsukemen and J-mazesoba)
neighborhood: Ichijoji, Sakyo-ku
address_ja: 京都府京都市左京区高野泉町6-74
geocode_note: GSI geocode of address_ja returned [135.78421, 35.046616]; within Kyoto box. Ichijoji area, Sakyo-ku.
seats_text: 9 counter seats only
hours_raw: Lunch 11:45-13:30 / 12:00-13:00, Dinner 18:00-22:30. Irregular holidays. Stays limited to under 20 minutes due to the small space.
closed_days: Irregular holidays (not fixed); confirm directly before visiting
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny 9-seat counter shop with a 20-minute time limit, mostly local students, and no reservations; basic Japanese and quick ordering will make the visit smoother.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. The noodles are homemade wheat noodles, the mazesoba uses a sweet soy-sauce (typically wheat-based) dressing, and the tsukemen broth is a rich pork-bone and fish stock. No gluten-free menu, dedicated equipment, or celiac accommodation is indicated. A celiac should not eat here.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. Core dishes are built on pork-bone and fish broth with pork-belly chashu, marinated egg, and meat toppings. No vegan or vegetarian option is offered.

bio.background: The owner ran a ramen website before opening the shop in 2007, turning a fan's obsession into a kitchen.
bio.origin: Ichijoji, Kyoto — the city's famous ramen street, a short walk from Ichijoji Station.
bio.specialty: Two signature bowls anchor the shop: a rich tsukemen with thick, homemade straight noodles (standard 400g) dipped in a heavy pork-bone broth deepened with fish stock and finished with pork-belly chashu, green onion and a marinated egg; and the J-mazesoba, brothless extra-thick wavy noodles tossed with a sweet soy-based dressing, a hit of garlic, a pile of vegetables and a chunky slab of meat.
bio.philosophy: Big, unapologetic flavor and serious volume aimed at hungry diners — the kind of intense, no-frills bowl that put Ichijoji-style tsukemen and mazesoba on the map.

anecdotes.text: The shop is credited as a birthplace of Ichijoji's tsukemen and mazesoba scene, and remains a local landmark drawing ramen fans and hungry students; on a recent visit roughly 80% of customers ordered the mazesoba.
In April 2025 the small interior was refreshed with new floors and walls while the heavy, rich bowls stayed front and center.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The J-mazesoba is dressed in a sweet soy-sauce base, which in standard ramen tare is wheat-containing shoyu; no wheat-free tare is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All bowls are based on pork-bone and fish broth with meat and egg toppings, so the kitchen is not set up for plant-based diners.

caveats: Hours vary between sources (lunch listed as both 11:45-13:30 and 12:00-13:00); holidays are irregular. Confirm same-day. Strict 20-minute dine-in limit due to the 9-seat counter. Cash only, no reservations.

cuisine: 町中華・鶏白湯ラーメン（鶏ガラと野菜を18時間以上煮込んだ甘みのある鶏白湯）
gf_detail: A 1971-founded chicken-paitan ramen shop. The bowls are built on wheat noodles in a chicken-and-vegetable broth, with a soy-based tare that almost certainly contains wheat. No gluten-free options, no allergen menu, and no dedicated preparation are advertised. Not safe for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: The entire menu centers on a chicken-and-vegetable broth simmered for 18+ hours, topped with chashu pork and egg. There is no vegan or vegetarian ramen, and the broth itself is animal-based, so there is nothing for a strict plant-based diner here.
address_ja: 京都府京都市左京区一乗寺西杉ノ宮町49
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates within the Kyoto box (lat 35.046, lng 135.785).
neighborhood: 左京区一乗寺（叡山電鉄 一乗寺駅／一乗寺ラーメン街道）
seats_text: 26 seats total: counter 8, one table for 6, and two raised tatami areas seating 6 each.
hours_raw: 12:00-22:00; closed Wednesdays
closed_days: 水曜日 (Wednesdays)
cultural_comfort_note: A busy, popular local ramen counter with no English menu or allergen support expected, so a guide or some Japanese helps with ordering and customizing your bowl.

bio.background: Founded in 1971 in Ichijoji, the neighborhood now famous as Kyoto's ramen street, Tentenyu Honten is the original shop that gave its name to a wider Tentenyu lineage, including a sister restaurant in Osaka's Suminoe district. More than fifty years on, it remains a fixture of the area's intensely competitive ramen scene.
bio.specialty: A sweet, creamy chicken paitan: chicken and vegetables simmered for roughly 18 hours or more into a rich yet surprisingly gentle broth, paired with medium-thin straight noodles. The signature order is the nitamago chashu-men, a bowl topped with roasted pork and a soft-cooked egg.
bio.philosophy: Depth without heaviness, drawing the natural sweetness out of vegetables and chicken over a long simmer, and letting each diner fine-tune the bowl by choosing noodle thickness and firmness, tare strength, scallion amount, and a soft-boiled or onsen egg.

anecdotes.text: The chicken paitan broth is built over roughly 18 hours of simmering chicken and vegetables, giving it a sweetness diners describe as concentrated yet light.
This Ichijoji location is the original honten of the Tentenyu name, which later extended to a franchise-style sister shop in Osaka's Suminoe district.

caveats: The prompt described this shop as a clear aged-shoyu chicken broth (鶏ガラ熟成醤油の澄んだスープ), but multiple independent sources confirm the signature here is a creamy chicken paitan (鶏白湯), not a clear shoyu soup. Broth description corrected accordingly. No allergen, gluten-free, or vegan information is published, so safety fields are left empty and confidence is low.

cuisine: 京都老舗中華そば（鶏ガラ・背脂醤油系ラーメン）
gf_detail: A traditional Kyoto chuka-soba shop: wheat ramen noodles in a chicken/pork-bone broth with back fat and (typically) wheat-based shoyu tare. There are no gluten-free noodles or menu items, and no indication of allergen accommodation. Not safe for celiacs. Side dishes like fried rice (yakimeshi) and karaage are also breaded/wheat-seasoned. Confidence: no.
vegan_detail: The signature and effectively the entire menu is built on animal stock — a chicken-bone broth enriched with pork back fat — plus chashu pork and meat-based sides. There is no vegan or vegetarian ramen option. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
address_ja: 京都府京都市左京区一乗寺払殿町24
geocode_note: GSI address search returned [135.783829, 35.043819] for 京都府京都市左京区一乗寺払殿町24; within the Kyoto box.
neighborhood: 左京区一乗寺（叡山電鉄 一乗寺駅／一乗寺ラーメン街道）
seats_text: 35 seats: 4 counter seats plus table seating (three tables for 4, one table for 9, one table for 10).
hours_raw: 11:00～24:00（L.O. 23:30）。定休日：水曜日（祝日の場合は営業）
closed_days: 水曜日（祝日の場合は営業）／ Wednesdays (open if a public holiday)
cultural_comfort_note: A busy, no-frills old-school Kyoto ramen shop with Japanese-only menu and ordering; manageable for a confident solo visitor but best approached with a guide or some Japanese, especially for any dietary requests.
caveats: The Tabelog URL supplied in the task brief (26001293) actually resolves to an unrelated, now-closed Sagano tofu restaurant; the correct active listing for this shop is 26000717, used here. No reservations are taken. No gluten-free or vegan options exist — this is a meat-broth wheat-noodle ramen shop.

bio.background: 珍遊 (Chinyu) opened in 1950 (Showa 25) and is one of the founding old-guard ramen houses of Kyoto, now anchoring the famous ramen street of Ichijoji in the city's northeast. Over more than seventy years it has grown from a single counter shop into a small Kyoto family of three locations, with this Ichijoji shop remaining the honten (head store).
bio.origin: Ichijoji, Sakyo-ku, Kyoto — a neighborhood that became one of Japan's most celebrated ramen battlegrounds, partly on the strength of long-running shops like this one.
bio.specialty: Their signature chuka-soba: a chicken-bone broth finished with rendered pork back fat (背脂) and a soy tare, served with straight medium-thin noodles. The shop frames this back-fat shoyu style as one of the roots of Kyoto ramen. Diners can adjust noodle firmness, back-fat richness, and green onion to taste, and popular set meals pair the bowl with fried rice (yakimeshi) or salt-koji karaage.
bio.philosophy: Their tagline sums up the bowl they aim for: '見た目こってり、後味さっぱり' — it looks rich and indulgent, but the finish is clean and light.
roles: head store of a small three-shop Kyoto group

anecdotes.text: Founded in 1950, Chinyu is treated as one of the originators of the Kyoto back-fat shoyu ramen style, and the shop itself calls this bowl a source of Kyoto ramen.
The Ichijoji head store sits on Kyoto's renowned ramen street and offers customer parking (about 15 affiliated spaces), unusual for an old city ramen shop and a sign of how much volume it draws.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The ramen uses a traditional soy-based (shoyu) tare, which in a shop of this style is wheat-based; no gluten-free tare or noodles are offered.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is animal-based throughout — a chicken-bone stock enriched with pork back fat — so there is no non-animal stock prepared on site.

cuisine: 担担まぜそば／濃厚鶏白湯ラーメン・つけ麺 (dan dan mazesoba, rich chicken paitan ramen and tsukemen)
neighborhood: 西院 (Saiin), 右京区
address_ja: 京都府京都市右京区西院追分町7-4
geocode_note: GSI address-search first feature coordinates [135.725906, 34.997768]; within Kyoto box.
seats_text: Counter only, approx. 10 seats
hours_raw: 11:00-15:00 / 18:00-21:30; 無休 (no regular closing day; temporary closures announced on X/Twitter)
closed_days: No fixed closing day (無休); occasional temporary closures announced on X (Twitter).
cultural_comfort_note: A small, busy counter-only ramen shop with lines at peak times; a few words of Japanese and ramen-shop etiquette (order, eat, free your seat) will go a long way.
gf_detail: The bowls are built on house-made wheat noodles with a sesame-based dan dan sauce or a concentrated chicken broth, and Japanese ramen tare typically contains wheat soy sauce. No gluten-free noodle or preparation is offered, and nothing in available sources suggests celiac-safe accommodation. Not suitable for those who must strictly avoid gluten.
vegan_detail: This is a chicken-broth (鶏白湯) and chicken-based shop: the broth is made from chicken and water, the dan dan mazesoba is topped with minced chicken, the ramen carries chicken chashu, and a marinated egg is a common topping. There are no vegetarian or vegan bowls. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
sources: https://msearch.gsi.go.jp/address-search/AddressSearch?q=京都府京都市右京区西院追分町7-4

bio.background: 麺屋 さん田 opened in the Saiin district of Kyoto's Ukyo Ward in 2017, founded by an owner who came up through the well-known Kyoto shop Ginjo Ramen Kubota (吟醸らーめん久保田) before going independent. It rose quickly in a fiercely competitive Kyoto ramen scene to become one of the city's most talked-about noodle shops.
bio.specialty: The two pillars are a concentrated chicken paitan (鶏白湯) — ramen and tsukemen built on a broth made from little more than chicken and water, finished with low-temperature chicken-breast chashu, diced menma and a fragrant caramelized onion-and-garlic oil — and the signature 担担まぜそば (dan dan mazesoba): house-made thick straight noodles coated in a rich, sticky sesame sauce with minced chicken, nuts, onion and bok choy, a numbing-spicy balance, and a small bowl of rice to finish the leftover sauce.
bio.philosophy: An emphasis on noodle craftsmanship and a clean, concentrated broth: the chicken paitan is described as deeply concentrated yet surprisingly light, and the house-made noodles are cut with a textured surface so they grab the thick soup or sauce. The menu is deliberately noodle-focused with few side dishes.

anecdotes.text: The dan dan mazesoba is the shop's cult favorite — a rich sesame sauce with a balance of spice, numbing sensation and umami, finished by mixing the remaining sauce into a bowl of rice.

The chicken paitan is made from essentially just chicken and water for a concentrated but clean flavor, paired with low-temperature chicken-breast chashu and a caramelized onion-garlic oil; the shop regularly draws lines at peak hours.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All bowls use house-made wheat noodles boiled in shared water, with sesame dan dan sauce or chicken broth and (typically) wheat-based soy sauce tare; there is no gluten-free preparation.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Japanese ramen tare for chicken/shoyu-style bowls is normally made with wheat-containing soy sauce; no wheat-free tare is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is chicken-based throughout (chicken paitan broth, minced-chicken and chicken-chashu toppings, egg), so vegan cross-contact is unavoidable.

cuisine: 汁なし担担麺・まぜそば・油そば (shiru-nashi tantanmen / mazesoba / abura soba)
gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. The house specialty is mazesoba and shiru-nashi tantanmen built on house-made flat wheat noodles (described as containing whole-grain wheat flour), with wheat-based tare and toppings. No gluten-free noodle, no dedicated prep, and no celiac accommodation is mentioned by the shop or any source. A wheat-avoidant diner cannot eat the core menu here.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. Bowls are built on Japanese pork and heritage-breed chicken (the owner emphasizes domestic, welfare-conscious livestock), charcoal-grilled pork chashu, ground meat for tantan/curry tantan, and free-range eggs. No vegan or vegetarian bowl is advertised. A vegan would have no suitable option.
address_ja: 京都府宇治市宇治池森20-1
geocode_note: GSI geocoded 京都府宇治市宇治池森20-1 to [135.795044, 34.885777]. Latitude 34.8858 sits just below the curated Kyoto-city box minimum (34.93); this is expected and correct because Uji is south of Kyoto city proper. Longitude is in range. Coordinates retained as valid.
seats_text: カウンター8席＋テーブル4席の計12席 (8 counter seats plus 4 table seats, 12 total). Run by just two staff.
hours_raw: Per the shop's official site: 11:00～14:30 (L.O.), open 金・土・日 (Fri/Sat/Sun) with occasional additional closures. Note: open days have shifted repeatedly over time (older reports list Mon–Thu or Mon/Tue/Wed/Sun), so the schedule is best treated as irregular — confirm via the shop's online reservation page before visiting.
closed_days: Officially closed Mon–Thu (open Fri/Sat/Sun), but days rotate; occasional unscheduled closures.
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny 12-seat owner-run counter shop in a residential pocket of Uji with a ticket/reservation system and no English signage noted — basic Japanese and patience with the queue/booking flow make for the smoothest visit.

bio.origin: Uji, Kyoto — near JR Uji Station
bio.background: The owner brings more than 15 years in food service and previously ran well-loved shops in Fushimi and Kobe before opening here in May 2018 on the site of his family's former restaurant. He cooks with a producer-first ethic, saying he wants to look growers in the eye and feel their intentions toward the ingredients ('自分自身の目で、生産者の顔を見て食材や商品に対する想いを感じ'), and works the tiny counter with just one other person.
bio.specialty: A house style of brothless, no-MSG ('無化調') bowls on thick house-made flat noodles cut with whole-grain wheat. The headline bowls are the 炭火香るチャーシューまぜそば — mazesoba crowned with charcoal-grilled pork that carries a real smoky aroma — and the rich 汁なし担々麺 / カレー担々まぜそば finished with the shop's own ground spice and house-made garam masala. The menu rotates often with daily and weekly specials.
bio.philosophy: Cook honestly from carefully sourced ingredients and waste nothing: low-pesticide or local vegetables, mineral-rich domestic sugar and salt, no MSG or preservatives, Japanese pork (largely Kyoto, grass-fed rotation) and heritage-breed chicken raised with animal-welfare in mind, free-range eggs, and Hyogo charcoal for the pork — all with a stated gratitude for the lives exchanged for the food.

anecdotes.text: The shop opened in May 2018 in the building that once housed the owner's family restaurant, and became a record-book reservation-only phenomenon within its first year.
Demand routinely outstrips the 12 seats: lines of five or six are normal and can top ten at peak, so the shop runs on advance booking and same-day numbered tickets rather than simple walk-ins.
The kitchen grinds its own spice blends — a house garam masala for the curry tantan — and these house spices sometimes sell out during service.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core bowls use house-made flat noodles made with wheat (including whole-grain wheat flour), prepared in a single 12-seat kitchen with no gluten-free handling described — assume heavy wheat presence and cross-contact throughout.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Bowls are built on pork and chicken stock/meat, charcoal-grilled pork, ground meat, and eggs; no separate vegan prep is mentioned.

positives.text: The shop is explicitly no-MSG ('無化調'), with no chemical seasonings or preservatives, and emphasizes carefully traced domestic ingredients — a plus for additive-sensitive diners, though not an allergy-safety measure.

caveats: Open days have changed repeatedly over the shop's history (official site currently lists Fri/Sat/Sun 11:00–14:30; older sources list Mon–Thu or other combinations), and unscheduled closures happen — always confirm via the online reservation page before going. It is reservation/numbered-ticket based, cash only, and seats just 12. No allergen, gluten-free, or vegan accommodation is documented anywhere; treat as unsafe for celiac and unsuitable for vegans.

name_ja: 麺屋 極鶏 一乗寺本店
cuisine: 鶏白湯ラーメン（超濃厚ドロ系・鶏パイタン）
gf_detail: This is a classic wheat-noodle ramen shop. The bowls use wheat noodles in an ultra-thick chicken paitan broth, with tare and toppings that typically involve wheat-based soy sauce; there is no gluten-free menu and no indication of gluten-free accommodation. Not suitable for celiacs or anyone needing strict gluten avoidance.
vegan_detail: The entire concept is built on a super-concentrated chicken (paitan) broth, with pork and chicken toppings. There are no vegan or vegetarian options, and no plant-based broth is offered.
address_ja: 京都府京都市左京区一乗寺西閉川原町29-7
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned coordinates [135.78717, 35.04726] for 京都府京都市左京区一乗寺西閉川原町29; within the Kyoto box.
neighborhood: 一乗寺（叡山電鉄 一乗寺駅 徒歩約5分／ラーメン街道）左京区
seats_text: 19 seats (counter 5, table 1, tatami 2)
hours_raw: 11:30-22:00 (Tue-Sun); closed Mondays
closed_days: Mondays
cultural_comfort_note: A busy, queue-forming neighborhood ramen shop with ticket-machine ordering; basic Japanese helps, and English support should not be expected.

bio.background: Menya Gokkei sits along Ichijoji's famous 'Ramen Street' in Kyoto's Sakyo Ward, one of the most competitive ramen districts in Japan. It has become a destination shop, drawing long lines for its single obsession: an extreme chicken paitan.
bio.origin: Ichijoji, Sakyo Ward, Kyoto. A second location later opened in 2024.
bio.specialty: The signature bowl is the 鶏だく (tori-daku) — a 'super-thick meat-cloudy chicken paitan' broth so dense it is described as a 'drinkable soup' and as the originator of the dorokei (mud-style) chicken paitan. Variations build on the same base: 赤だく (aka-daku, with chili), 黒だく (kuro-daku, with burnt-garlic oil), and 魚だく (uo-daku, with fish powder).
bio.philosophy: A single-minded pursuit of the richest possible chicken broth — the shop frames the soup itself as the dish, an 'one-and-only, eat-it-with-a-spoon' paitan rather than a light, sippable stock.

anecdotes.text: The ultra-concentrated chicken paitan is described as the product of roughly 12 years of trial and error to perfect, and the shop has been a perennial fixture among Kansai's top ramen.
The shop bills its broth as the 'originator of the dorokei (mud-style)' super-rich meat-cloudy chicken paitan.

caveats: Allergen, gluten, and vegan details are not published by the shop; the "no" ratings reflect the inherent nature of wheat-noodle chicken-paitan ramen rather than confirmed kitchen practices. Cash only and no reservations — expect a queue, especially on weekends. Closed Mondays.

cuisine: 濃厚豚骨鶏ガララーメン（こってり極濃）
neighborhood: 一乗寺（左京区）
gf_detail: No gluten-free option. This is a classic wheat-noodle ramen shop built around an ultra-thick pork-and-chicken broth, served with chunky chewy wheat noodles and chashu pork; the tare is almost certainly wheat-based soy sauce. Nothing in any source indicates gluten-free handling, and the tiny one-counter operation has no allergen accommodation. Not safe for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The entire concept is an intensely rich pork-bone and chicken-bone broth with pork chashu toppings. There are no plant-based bowls and no source mentions vegetarian or vegan options.
address_ja: 京都府京都市左京区一乗寺北大丸町5-2
geocode_note: GSI geocode of the chō-level address (一乗寺北大丸町5番地) returned [135.786697, 35.045891], within the Kyoto box.
seats_text: 9 counter seats only
hours_raw: 不定休・不定営業。基本的に休業で、営業する日のみTwitter(@eigyoakihide 昼／@eigyoakihide_2 夜)で告知。多くは18:00頃〜売り切れ次第終了（L.O. 23:00頃の日もあり）。営業前約30分前に告知。一人一杯（子供を除く）、整理券制。
closed_days: 不定休（営業日はTwitterで都度告知）
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny nine-seat counter run on the owner's terms with a one-bowl-per-person rule and numbered-ticket queuing, where you'll need to read the Japanese Twitter feed to know it's even open — comfortable for those who can navigate in Japanese.
caveats: Notoriously irregular: the shop is "basically closed" and only opens on days announced via Twitter (separate lunch and dinner accounts), often posted about 30 minutes before service. Cash only, no reservations, one bowl per person, numbered-ticket queue. Confirm the day's status on Twitter before going.

bio.background: A legendary hole-in-the-wall ramen counter in Kyoto's Ichijoji "ramen street" district, a short walk from Ichijoji Station on the Eizan Electric Railway. The shop is famous less for marketing than for its mystique: it is, in its own words, basically closed, opening only on the days the owner decides to cook and announcing those days on Twitter — to the point that locals describe it as a hidden gem because no one ever knows when it's open.
bio.origin: 一乗寺, 京都 (Kyoto's Ichijoji ramen district)
bio.specialty: An extraordinarily thick, ultra-rich (極濃こってり) pork-bone and chicken-bone broth so concentrated that a spoon reportedly stands upright in it — closer to a glaze than a soup — coating thick, chewy wheat noodles, topped with generous chunks (not slices) of tender chashu pork.
bio.philosophy: Self-described as a specialty shop for the 濃厚民族 — the devoted lovers of intensely rich, heavy flavor — focused entirely on delivering maximum depth and coating power rather than subtlety. The owner runs it strictly on his own schedule and his own rules: one bowl per person (children excepted) and a numbered-ticket system for the queue.

anecdotes.text: The shop is so unpredictable that it's known as the place where you never know when it's open — it stays closed by default and only posts on Twitter, with separate accounts for lunch and dinner service, when it decides to open.
The broth is so dense that reviewers describe a renge (soup spoon) standing upright in it, and the eating experience feels closer to tsukemen than ordinary ramen.
Bowls come crowned with chunks of chashu that are notably tender and stand up to the heavy broth — described as not a fillet but a chunk of roasted pork.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles cooked in a shared pot; broth and tare are not gluten-free. No gluten-free handling described.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Tare is almost certainly wheat-based soy sauce, as is standard for this style; no gluten-free tare offered.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is pork-bone and chicken-bone based with pork chashu; no vegan preparation, and animal stock is the core of every bowl.

cuisine: つけ麺（濃厚豚骨魚介・自家製麺）
neighborhood: 一乗寺（左京区・ラーメン街道）
gf_detail: A tsukemen specialist built around house-made extra-thick wheat noodles dipped in a rich pork-bone-and-fish broth that is virtually always thickened/seasoned with wheat-containing elements. There is no gluten-free noodle, no separate prep, and no indication of allergen accommodation. Not suitable for celiacs or those needing strict gluten avoidance.
vegan_detail: The entire menu is centered on pork-bone (tonkotsu) and fish (gyokai) broth, with meat and egg toppings. There are no vegan or vegetarian bowls. Not suitable for vegan or vegetarian diners.
address_ja: 京都府京都市左京区一乗寺高槻町20-2
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 京都府京都市左京区一乗寺高槻町20-2 returned [135.78537, 35.044338], within the Kyoto box.
seats_text: L字カウンター12席（キッチンを囲むカウンターのみ）
hours_raw: 11:30〜15:30（売り切れ次第閉店）／定休日：火曜日
closed_days: 火曜日
cultural_comfort_note: A small, popular lunch-only counter shop where lines form before opening; ordering is simple but English support is unlikely, so a little Japanese or pointing at the menu helps.
caveats: Lunch only and closes when sold out, so arrive early; cash only and no reservations. Counter-only seating with frequent queues.

bio.background: Opened in 2010 in a quiet residential stretch of Ichijoji, Kyoto's famed 'ramen street,' and grew almost entirely by word of mouth into one of the area's most queued-for tsukemen counters. A tiny L-shaped counter of about a dozen seats wraps around the open kitchen, so you eat just steps from where the bowls are built.
bio.specialty: The signature is the 魚介豚骨つけ麺 — silky, chewy extra-thick house-made noodles (milled from Izawa flour with a whole-grain blend) served beside a deeply concentrated pork-bone-and-fish dipping broth, balanced with fish powder, oil and a touch of spice. The 特製 version piles on double meat, menma and a soft-boiled egg.
bio.philosophy: A deliberately narrow menu — essentially three tsukemen (standard fish-and-pork-bone, spicy, and curry) plus toppings and rice — reflecting a focus on doing one thing exceptionally rather than chasing variety.

anecdotes.text: The shop opened in 2010 and became a word-of-mouth sensation, drawing lines before it even opens for its lunch-only service.
Noodles are house-made using Izawa flour with a whole-grain blend, described as silky, chewy and extra-thick.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Tsukemen is built on house-made wheat noodles dipped in a wheat-seasoned pork-bone-and-fish broth; gluten is unavoidable and no gluten-free option or separate prep is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All bowls use pork-bone and fish broth with meat and egg toppings; there is no vegan or vegetarian preparation.

lead: ラーメン二郎 京都店
name_ja: ラーメン二郎 京都店
cuisine: 二郎系ラーメン（直系）/ Jiro-style ramen
gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. Jiro-style ramen is built on thick wheat noodles, a wheat-based soy-sauce tare, and a pork-and-back-fat broth — all standard wheat-containing components. There is no gluten-free menu, no separate preparation, and the kitchen is a single shared boil-and-assemble line. Celiac and gluten-sensitive diners should not eat here.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The broth is a heavy pork (and back-fat) stock, bowls are built around large chunks of chashu pork, and the tare is animal/soy-sauce based. There are no vegetable or plant-based bowls; even toppings like extra vegetables sit in pork broth. Vegans cannot eat here.
address_ja: 京都府京都市左京区一乗寺里ノ前町4
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned coordinates within the Kyoto bounding box for the chō-level address (一乗寺里ノ前町4); located in Ichijoji, Sakyo-ku, about a 1-minute walk from Ichijoji Station on the Eizan Electric Railway.
neighborhood: 一乗寺（左京区）/ Ichijoji, Sakyo-ku
seats_text: L字カウンターのみ13席 / 13 seats, L-shaped counter only
hours_raw: Reported hours vary by source. Tabelog/official list roughly: weekdays 11:00–14:00 & 18:00–21:30 (lunch service sometimes suspended), Sat 11:00–14:30 & 17:30–21:30, Sun 11:00–14:30 & 17:30–20:30. Closed Wednesdays plus irregular closures. Confirm same-day via the shop's X account (@jiro_kyoto) before visiting.
closed_days: 水曜日＋不定休 / Wednesdays plus irregular closures
cultural_comfort_note: A no-frills, fast-moving counter shop with Jiro's distinctive ordering ritual (the "call" for garlic/vegetables/oil/sauce) and frequent queues — comfortable if you know the system or watch others, but there is little English and the pace expects you to order, eat, and leave promptly.

bio.background: Ramen Jiro Kyoto opened on April 2, 2017, as a fully sanctioned 直系 (direct-lineage) branch of the legendary Tokyo Ramen Jiro empire — for several years it stood as the only direct Jiro branch in all of western Japan (until a Kyushu branch later opened). The proprietor trained at the Jimbocho (神保町) Jiro in Tokyo before bringing the style to Ichijoji, a neighborhood already famous as one of Kyoto's great ramen streets.
bio.specialty: The classic Jiro bowl: a mountain of thick, flat, chewy hand-cut wheat noodles under a tangle of boiled bean sprouts and cabbage, slabs of thick-cut chashu pork, and a rich pork-and-back-fat soy broth. Diners customize at the counter with the Jiro 'call' — ninniku (garlic), yasai (extra veg), abura (back fat), karame (extra tare) — and finish with counter condiments like coarse pepper and chili.
bio.philosophy: Pure, unapologetic Jiro: enormous portions, bold pork-fat richness, and an efficient eat-and-go counter rhythm. The shop keeps the old-school 二郎 atmosphere intact rather than softening it for a tourist crowd.

anecdotes.text: The proprietor and assistant came up through the Jimbocho Jiro in Tokyo and are noted Hanshin Tigers fans.

Long described as Kansai's only direct-lineage Jiro; a blogger revisiting in 2025 noted a new Kyushu branch had since ended its status as the sole branch in western Japan, and that the shop keeps its traditional, masculine old-school Jiro vibe with 13 seats along an L-shaped counter.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All bowls use wheat noodles boiled in a shared pot with a wheat-based soy tare; there is no gluten-free preparation or separation, so cross-contamination is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The tare is a soy-sauce base typical of Jiro-style ramen, which uses wheat-containing soy sauce; soy sauce is also offered as a counter condiment.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The broth is a pork and back-fat stock and bowls include chashu pork; even the vegetable topping (boiled bean sprouts and cabbage) is served in pork broth, so no item is free of animal products.

caveats: Hours differ across sources and the shop posts changes (including suspended lunch service and irregular closures) on its X account @jiro_kyoto — confirm same-day before visiting. Cash only; 13 counter seats with frequent queues using numbered tickets at peak times. Not suitable for gluten-free or vegan diners.

lead: 油そば専門店 ムジコ
name_ja: 油そば専門店 ムジコ
cuisine: 油そば（油そば・まぜそば専門）
gf_detail: Not safe for celiac/gluten-free. Abura soba is a brothless wheat-noodle dish: chewy medium-thick wheat noodles tossed with a soy-sauce-based tare (special 醤油ダレ) plus flavored oils. The noodles are wheat and the tare is soy-sauce based (Japanese soy sauce almost always contains wheat). No gluten-free noodle or tamari option is mentioned anywhere. Diners with celiac disease should avoid this shop.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly shop. The abura soba is built on pork and seafood (fish/niboshi dried-sardine) flavor bases plus a soy tare, and the menu leans into animal components (e.g., a fish-stock niboshi version, and a popular karaage side). No vegan or vegetarian bowl is advertised. A vegan diner could not be accommodated with the standard menu.
address_ja: 京都府京都市上京区千本通竹屋町下ル聚楽町863-25 朝田ビル1F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI on chō-level address (聚楽町863-25); result lat 35.016205, lng 135.743149 falls within the Kyoto box and matches the Nijō / Senbon-Takeyamachi location.
seats_text: Counter seating only (about 6-7 seats with partitions); a small, intimate one-room shop.
hours_raw: 火～日・祝・祝前日 11:30～15:30（料理L.O.15:00）／18:00～22:30（料理L.O.22:00）。定休日 月曜日。（注：食べログには木曜定休・夜L.O.21:30との記載もあり、来店前の確認推奨）
closed_days: Mondays (per HotPepper and local guides; note one listing shows Thursday — confirm before visiting)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny counter-only neighborhood shop run in Japanese with no reservations and cash/QR payment only; a little Japanese or a translation app and quiet patience at the counter will make ordering smooth.
neighborhood: 二条・聚楽（上京区／千本通竹屋町、JR・地下鉄東西線 二条駅から徒歩約7分）
caveats: Closed-day listings conflict (Monday on HotPepper and local guides vs. Thursday on Tabelog) and evening last-order times vary by source; confirm hours before visiting. Cash or QR-code payment only (PayPay/au PAY); no credit cards or e-money. Counter seats only with very limited capacity, so expect to wait or come off-peak.

bio.background: ムジコ (Mujiko) is one of the very few abura soba specialists in Kyoto — a city far better known for its kotteri ramen than for brothless oil noodles — which makes it something of a local curiosity. It sits in the quiet Senbon-Takeyamachi/Shuraku neighborhood of Kamigyo, a short walk from Nijo Station, as a small counter-only shop.
bio.specialty: The signature is the standard 油そば (abura soba): chewy medium-thick curly wheat noodles tossed at the bottom of the bowl with a special soy-sauce-based tare and two house-made flavored oils, no soup. You mix it vigorously, then dial in the flavor yourself with rice vinegar, chili oil and grated garlic. Portion sizes from small to large are all the same price.
bio.philosophy: A focused, do-one-thing-well abura soba bar: a tight menu of four variations on the same idea, customizable at the table, with portion size left up to the diner at no extra charge.

anecdotes.text: The lineup runs four ways on the same theme — the classic abura soba, a fish-stock niboshi (dried-sardine) version, a ginger oil soba, and a numbing-spicy bowl built on the shop's house-made chili oil (痺辛).

One reviewer noted it was their first abura soba in over twenty years and found ムジコ's far more delicious than the version they remembered from the 1990s, the chewy medium-thick noodles clinging perfectly to the special soy tare and oil.
Beyond the noodles, the shop's intensely seasoned karaage (fried chicken) is a favorite side and pairs well with a beer.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The abura soba is dressed with a special soy-sauce-based tare (特製醤油ダレ); Japanese soy sauce typically contains wheat, and no wheat-free/tamari alternative is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Flavor bases include pork and seafood (fish/niboshi dried-sardine) broths and oils, so the noodles are not vegan even before the soy tare.

cuisine: あっさり鶏ガラ醤油・塩ラーメン／つけ麺 (clear chicken-and-dashi shoyu/shio ramen and tsukemen)
gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The bowls are built on wheat ramen noodles, and the shoyu (soy sauce) tare that seasons the shoyu ramen is a standard Japanese soy sauce, which is brewed with wheat. The house-made noodles are explicitly described as having a wheat character. No gluten-free noodle option, no dedicated preparation area, and no allergy accommodation is mentioned at this 9-seat counter shop. A celiac diner should not eat here.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. Every bowl is built on an animal broth (chicken stock combined with Japanese seafood dashi), and toppings include thick-cut char siu pork, marinated egg, and other animal products. There is no vegetable or vegan broth, no plant-based bowl, and the small daytime-only counter offers only three items (shoyu ramen, shio ramen, tsukemen), all animal-based.
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区西ノ京北円町1-8
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned coordinates for 京都府京都市中京区西ノ京北円町1-8; lng 135.731613, lat 35.019409, within the Kyoto box.
neighborhood: 中京区 西ノ京（円町）
seats_text: 9 counter seats only (L-shaped counter); a small waiting area with about 5 spots for the queue outside.
hours_raw: 11:30-15:30; closed Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday (open Thursday-Sunday). Daytime only; sells out / closes when soup runs out.
closed_days: Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, popular local counter run with no-frills focus on the bowl; almost no English is expected, so a few words of Japanese and queue patience help.
caveats: Daytime only and just four days a week (Thu-Sun, 11:30-15:30); expect a queue and possible early sell-out. Cash only. The bowl price is currently around 900 yen for ramen/shio/tsukemen following the 2024 reopening; older listings showing lower prices are out of date.

bio.background: A small owner-run ramen counter in Kyoto's Emmachi neighborhood, open since around 2009.
