After an extended closure it reopened on March 28, 2024 to long lines of regulars who had been waiting for its return. The chef trained at the Osaka ramen shop Menya Inokichi (麺乃家) in the Tanimachi-9-chome / Uehommachi area before opening his own shop.
bio.specialty: A refined, clear (tanrei) bowl in two styles: a mellow chicken-and-dashi shoyu ramen and a crisp shio (salt) ramen, plus tsukemen. The shio bowl is finished with thick-cut char siu, bamboo shoots, two kinds of scallion, and yuzu zest grated to order for aroma. House-made flat, slightly thick noodles are praised for their glossy, smooth texture.
bio.philosophy: Quality over volume: a nine-seat counter, daytime-only and four days a week, with meticulous attention to every element from broth to noodles to toppings. The broth pairs clean chicken stock with Japanese seafood dashi for an elegant, soba-like clarity that reviewers describe as gentle on the body.

anecdotes.text: The broth's refined, dashi-driven clarity is so soba-like that reviewers speculate the chef has a background in traditional Japanese cuisine; yuzu zest is grated over the salt ramen just before serving for fragrance.
The shop reopened on March 28, 2024 after an extended hiatus, drawing consistent lines despite its limited four-days-a-week, lunch-only schedule.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Bowls use wheat ramen noodles and a wheat-brewed soy sauce tare; the house-made noodles are described as having a distinct wheat character. No gluten-free option exists.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature shoyu ramen is seasoned with a Japanese soy sauce tare (standard shoyu is brewed with wheat), giving a mellow soy flavor over a chicken-dashi broth.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broths combine chicken stock with seafood dashi, and toppings include char siu pork and marinated egg; there is no vegetable or vegan bowl.

lead: 本家 第一旭 本店
name_ja: 本家 第一旭 本店
cuisine: 背脂醤油 中華そば（豚骨醤油ラーメン・町中華系）
gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The signature 中華そば is wheat ramen noodles (medium-thick blended-flour noodles aged dry, by Kondo Seimen) in a pork-bone broth seasoned with a wheat-based Kyoto soy sauce tare (生醤油 from Fushimi / Goko Shoyu). Noodles, tare, and gyoza all contain wheat; there is no gluten-free option and no indication of allergen accommodation. Avoid if you require strict gluten-free.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The entire menu is built around a pork-bone (豚骨) broth, char siu (roast pork), and pork-fat finish, with soy-sauce tare; toppings include kujo negi, bean sprouts, menma and pork. No vegetable/plant-based broth or vegan bowl is offered.
address_ja: 京都府京都市下京区東塩小路向畑町845
geocode_note: GSI address-search first feature geometry.coordinates [135.761459, 34.985992]; within Kyoto box (lat 34.93–35.13, lng 135.66–135.83). Matches location ~7 min walk east of Kyoto Station near Takabashi/Shichijo.
neighborhood: 下京区・京都駅東側（七条/たかばし）
seats_text: 31 seats total: 3 counter seats plus 28 table seats across 7 tables; communal seating (相席) during busy periods.
closed_days: Thursdays
cultural_comfort_note: A bustling, no-frills local ramen institution with frequent queues and communal seating; a few basic Japanese greetings and pointing at the simple menu will carry you through comfortably.
caveats: Wheat noodles + wheat soy-sauce tare + pork-bone broth — not suitable for gluten-free or vegan diners. Expect a line, especially mornings and weekends, and shared tables when busy.

bio.background: Born in 1947 near Kyoto Station at the spot locals call "Takabashi," this is the original shop of the celebrated Daiichi Asahi lineage of Kyoto ramen. It started life as a humble eatery (Asahi Shokudo) and grew into a dedicated ramen specialist by the mid-1950s, opening before dawn to feed railway workers heading out on the first trains — a habit that survives today in its famous 6 a.m. opening.
bio.specialty: The signature 特製ラーメン (chuka soba): a clear yet deeply savory pork-bone broth seasoned with raw Kyoto soy sauce, piled with thinly sliced char siu, a generous tangle of Kujo green onions, and bean sprouts, over medium-thick dried-aged wheat noodles.
bio.philosophy: A long-protected, continually refined old-school taste ("守り続け、磨き続けてきた老舗の味") built on careful sourcing — Kyoto-made Kujo negi and sprouts, soy sauce from a long-established Kyoto maker, and char siu from a single specific grade of domestic pork chosen for its lean, clean balance of fat and meat so the soup stays gentle on the palate.

anecdotes.text: Founded in 1947 at the Takabashi site by Kyoto Station; it became a dedicated ramen shop under the Daiichi Asahi name around 1956 and now draws on the order of 240,000 visitors a year, with lines forming from early morning.
The char siu uses only domestic sows that have farrowed twice (around 120 kg), prized for low fat content so no excess grease bleeds into the bowl.

Broth is drawn by simmering pork bones briefly to pull a clear soup of pure umami; noodles are blended-flour, dry-aged medium-thick strands from Kondo Seimen, and the tare uses soy sauce from a historic Kyoto soy-sauce maker.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles (blended-flour, dried-aged) are the base of every bowl, with a wheat-based soy-sauce tare and gyoza on the menu — a high-gluten kitchen with no gluten-free provision.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The tare is made with raw Kyoto soy sauce (生醤油, Fushimi / Goko Shoyu), which is wheat-fermented; it flavors the broth of the standard bowls.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All bowls are pork-bone broth with char siu and pork fat; no plant-based broth is prepared, so vegan separation is not possible.

lead: ますたに 北白川本店
name_ja: 中華そば ますたに 北白川本店
cuisine: 背脂醤油ラーメン（鶏ガラ醤油＋豚背脂・京都ラーメンの源流）
gf_detail: No gluten-free options. The core product is wheat ramen noodles in a chicken-bone soy-sauce (shoyu) broth topped with pork back fat; the soy-based tare is virtually certain to contain wheat. There is no evidence of gluten-free noodles, a dedicated preparation area, or any allergen accommodation. This is not a safe choice for celiac diners — treat as off-limits.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The single signature bowl is built on a chicken-bone (鶏ガラ) broth with pork back fat (背脂) and chashu (roast pork) toppings. The menu is essentially one ramen with no plant-based version, and no vegan broth or substitutions are offered.
address_ja: 京都府京都市左京区北白川久保田町26
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned [135.790359, 35.028439] for 京都府京都市左京区北白川久保田町26; within Kyoto box.
neighborhood: 左京区・北白川（銀閣寺道／今出川通沿い、元田中駅近く）
seats_text: About 11 seats — roughly 6 counter seats plus a few small tatami/table seats. Tiny, so expect a line at peak hours.
hours_raw: 火～日 10:00～15:00, 16:00～19:00（ラストオーダーは閉店前。資料により10:00～14:45・16:00～17:45の記載もあり、時間帯は変動の可能性）
closed_days: 定休日：月曜日、および毎月第3火曜日（火曜が休みになる場合あり。資料により第3水曜の記載もあり、変動の可能性）
cultural_comfort_note: A no-frills neighborhood ramen counter where ordering is simple but staff likely speak little English — a few words of Japanese and pointing at the bowl you want will carry you through smoothly.
caveats: As of June 2026, this flagship location is open but scheduled to close for an indefinite hiatus on July 19, 2026 (cited reasons: building deterioration and rising costs). Confirm it is still operating before making a special trip; reporting indicates operations are consolidating to the group's Umekoji location. Hours and the monthly extra closed day vary between sources, so the listed times are approximate. Cash only.

bio.background: Masutani's Kitashirakawa flagship has stood near the Ginkakuji-michi area of Kyoto's Sakyo ward since the late 1940s, growing from a humble start into a shop with more than seventy years of history. It is widely regarded as the source of Kyoto's signature back-fat soy-sauce ramen — the style locals call 背脂チャッチャ, where rich pork back fat is showered over the bowl. Generations of Kyoto students, workers, and visitors have queued at its tiny counter.
bio.specialty: A single, focused bowl: a chicken-bone broth married with soy-sauce tare and finished with a generous fall of pork back fat (背脂), served over fine, straight noodles with chashu, menma, and Kujo green onions. The fat gives body and sweetness over a clear, familiar shoyu base.
bio.philosophy: Rather than chase trends, the shop keeps its decades-old recipe unchanged — patrons return precisely for that time-honored, lived-in flavor (食べなれたスープ). One bowl, done the same way, year after year.

anecdotes.text: Regarded as the originator of Kyoto's back-fat soy-sauce ramen style (背脂チャッチャ発祥), the shop has carried more than 70 years of history at its Kitashirakawa home.
Because the noodles are on the soft side, regulars recommend ordering them firm ('麺カタ'); the broth carries a momentary sharp poultry aroma that fades as you eat.
The historic Kitashirakawa flagship is set to go on indefinite hiatus from July 19, 2026, largely due to aging of the building, with the owner expressing hope to reopen someday.

roles: founding/originating shop of Kyoto back-fat soy-sauce ramen

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is a chicken-bone soup combined with soy sauce (鶏ガラでとったスープに醤油を合わせた), and the soy-sauce tare in this style is virtually always wheat-based.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The signature bowl uses chicken-bone broth with pork back fat and chashu, with no plant-based version offered.

cuisine: Ramen (tori-shoyu / tori-paitan / niboshi)
neighborhood: 左京区 修学院
gf_detail: This is a classic ramen shop: wheat noodles served in chicken broth with a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare. The signature bowls are built on a raw-pressed soy sauce (nama-age shoyu) chicken-clear broth, and Japanese soy sauce normally contains wheat. With wheat noodles plus a wheat-based tare, there is no gluten-free option here and the kitchen is not set up for celiac-safe service. No gluten-free claims were found on any page; treat as not suitable for celiac/gluten-free diners.
vegan_detail: Every signature bowl is built on a chicken broth (tori-seitan clear soup or tori-paitan), finished with chicken oil, and topped with pork chashu. There is no vegan or vegetable-broth ramen on the menu, and no vegan options were found on any page.
address_ja: 京都府京都市左京区山端壱町田町8-6 京福修学院マンション 1F
geocode_note: GSI address-search geocoded to the chō-level point 京都府京都市左京区山端壱町田町8 at [135.791031, 35.049969]; within the Kyoto box. Building-level precision not available.
seats_text: 14 seats (8 counter, 6 at tables)
hours_raw: Mon, Wed-Sun 11:00-14:30, 18:00-21:00; Tuesday closed. Ticket-vending-machine order; lunch and dinner service may end early when sold out.
closed_days: Tuesdays (火曜日)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, popular neighborhood ramen counter with a ticket vending machine and no reservations; a few words of Japanese and patience for a possible queue make the visit smooth.
caveats: Cash only; no cards, e-money or QR payment. No reservations (walk-in only) and lines are common. May close early once the day's broth sells out. Geocode is chō-level, not building-level.

bio.background: The shop's name とうひち is written 藤七 and read 'touhichi' in the Kyoto dialect. The owner trained at the renowned Kyoto ramen house Senmarushakuriki before opening his own counter in a quiet residential pocket near Kinkaku-ji (Kita Ward) in March 2015. In April 2022 he relocated the shop to its current spot a short walk from Shugakuin Station, into premises that had previously housed his sister shop Suguru.
bio.specialty: The signature bowl is the tori-shoyu raa-men: a crystal-clear chicken broth (tori-seitan) seasoned with raw-pressed nama-age soy sauce and gilded with fragrant chicken oil, topped with two kinds of pork chashu, thick menma and chopped green onion. The repertoire also runs to a richer tori-paitan, a niboshi (sardine) seafood bowl, and tsuke-soba dipping noodles served with a kombu-steeped water bath for the noodles.
bio.philosophy: A restrained, transparency-first aesthetic: a simple-looking but deeply layered broth meant to slide cleanly across the palate, with the raw-pressed-soy-sauce chicken-clear style being the direction the owner says he settled on during his training years.

anecdotes.text: The shop name 藤七 is read 'touhichi' in Kyoto dialect, a local twist that doubles as the shop's identity.
Tsuke-soba is served with a kombu-dashi water bath that the noodles soak in, a refinement on the standard dipping-noodle format.
After the 2022 move to Shugakuin, the shop took over the premises of its own sister shop, Suguru.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature bowls are seasoned with raw-pressed (nama-age) soy sauce, which is normally wheat-based; combined with wheat ramen noodles this makes the dishes unsuitable for gluten-free diners.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broths are chicken-based (tori-seitan clear soup or tori-paitan) and bowls are topped with pork chashu, so the kitchen works with animal products throughout; no vegan preparation is offered.

lead: らーめん セアブラノ神 壬生本店
name_ja: らーめん セアブラノ神 壬生本店
name_en: Ramen Seabura no Kami Mibu Honten
cuisine: Ramen — niboshi / seabura (背脂煮干しそば, Tsubame-Sanjo-style back-fat sardine ramen)
gf_detail: No gluten-free option. The core bowls are built on wheat noodles (chukamen from Kyoto's Mennya Teigaku / 棣鄂) and a soy-sauce-based tare, served with a chicken-and-pork-bone broth. Standard ramen, mazesoba and tsukemen are all wheat-based, and there is no indication of any gluten-free menu or preparation. Not suitable for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The house style is animal-forward: an ~8-hour clear (chintan) broth made from chicken carcass, whole chicken and pork knuckle bones, finished with pork back fat and small dried sardines (niboshi). Toppings center on chashu pork and egg. There is no vegan or vegetarian ramen on the menu.
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区壬生相合町25-4 デイスターアベニュー 1F
geocode_note: GSI address-search geocode of 京都府京都市中京区壬生相合町25-4 returned [135.746307, 35.000519], within the Kyoto box. Matches the Mibu location near Hankyu Omiya / Shijo-Omiya.
neighborhood: 中京区 壬生（阪急 大宮駅／四条大宮 徒歩約6〜7分）
seats_text: 16 seats — 6 counter seats plus two tables (one 4-top, one 6-top).
closed_days: Open daily; closed only January 1 (New Year's Day / 元旦).
cultural_comfort_note: A busy local ramen counter where ordering in Japanese (or by pointing at the ticket/menu) is easiest; staff are accustomed to a brisk lunch and dinner crowd rather than extended English exchanges.
caveats: Cash only — no cards, e-money, or QR payments. No reservations; expect to queue at peak lunch/dinner. Hours include a mid-afternoon break (closes 15:30, reopens 18:00). Wheat noodles, soy-based tare and chicken/pork broth make this unsuitable for celiac or vegan diners.

bio.background: Seabura no Kami began almost by accident. After about three months working as a black-suited host in Gion, the founder found his afternoons free and decided to run a ramen shop again — but only at lunch to start. He chose Niigata's Tsubame-Sanjo style as the signature precisely because nothing like it existed in Kyoto at the time, and the city had no real niboshi (dried-sardine) ramen culture either. A tasting trip to Hakkaisan in Takatsuki sparked the idea ('this is interesting'), and after consulting the noodle maker Teigaku (棣鄂) and the owner of Ken Ramen's Yamauchi shop, he committed to the concept.
bio.origin: 京都 / Kyoto (Tsubame-Sanjo style of Niigata, reinterpreted for Kyoto palates)
bio.specialty: 背脂煮干そば (seabura niboshi soba) — back-fat dried-sardine ramen. The kitchen takes back fat, which most shops use only as a finishing touch to add aroma and keep soup hot, and 'elevates it to the starring role.' Hand-picked domestic back fat from designated farms gives an aromatic, surprisingly clean richness over an ~8-hour clear broth of chicken carcass, whole chicken and pork knuckle bones, layered with four kinds of small sardines and finished with rare chashu and a seasoned egg. Diners choose medium or thick wheat noodles from Mennya Teigaku.
bio.philosophy: Indulgent but not heavy: refuse to merely imitate Niigata's Tsubame ramen and instead build a distinct identity by obsessively refining the back fat itself, aiming for an approachable bowl where the niboshi is present but restrained.

anecdotes.text: The owner reframes back fat from a supporting ingredient into the main attraction: 'Back fat is supposed to be an auxiliary ingredient that adds flavor to the soup and keeps it from cooling — this is ramen that sublimates that back fat into the star.'

The decision to build the shop around Tsubame-Sanjo niboshi ramen came from a tasting trip to Hakkaisan in Takatsuki — 'this is interesting' — followed by consulting noodle maker Teigaku and the Yamauchi owner of Ken Ramen before committing.
The Mibu flagship has been supported since opening by proprietor Ido, described as a mother of two who has kept the shop running while raising her children.
From a single lunch-only counter, Seabura no Kami grew into a brand with multiple shops across Kyoto, credited as a pioneer that introduced niboshi-tonkotsu ramen to the city.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Bowls use wheat chukamen and a soy-sauce-based tare in a shared ramen kitchen; no gluten-free handling is described.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature is described as a combination of dried sardine, pork back fat and soy-sauce soup, indicating a standard wheat-containing shoyu base.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is an ~8-hour chicken-and-pork-bone clear stock finished with pork back fat and dried sardines, so all bowls share animal-based components.

cuisine: Chuka-soba ramen (Shinpuku Saikan-style jet-black soy-sauce ramen and yakimeshi)
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The house bowl is a classic chuka-soba built on wheat ramen noodles in a dark soy-sauce tare over a pork-and-chicken broth, in the Shinpuku Saikan tradition. The deep-color tare is soy-sauce based and almost certainly contains wheat, and the signature yakimeshi (fried rice) is also seasoned with soy sauce. There is no indication of any gluten-free noodle, tamari tare, or celiac accommodation. A celiac should not eat here.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. This is a meat-forward chuka-soba shop: the broth is a pork-bone and chicken base, the bowls are topped with pork-loin chashu, and the famous yakimeshi is cooked with pork and egg-style seasoning. There is no vegan or vegetarian ramen on the menu. A plant-based diner has nothing suitable here.
address_ja: 京都府京都市左京区一乗寺宮ノ東町51-7
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned coordinates [135.790848, 35.043217] for the full street address; within the Kyoto bounding box.
neighborhood: Ichijoji (Sakyo-ku), a short walk from Eizan Railway Ichijoji Station, just off the famous Ichijoji ramen street
seats_text: 12 seats total: 8 counter seats plus one 4-person table. The small room fills quickly at lunch.
hours_raw: 11:30-15:00, 17:00-21:00 (some listings give a continuous 11:30-21:00 with last order around 20:40); closed Wednesdays.
closed_days: Wednesdays (水曜日)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, no-frills neighborhood counter shop where ordering in Japanese is expected; point-and-order works but expect little to no English.
caveats: Cash only (PayPay is accepted, but credit cards are not). Only 12 seats, so expect a wait or full house during the lunch rush. Closed Wednesdays. Strictly a meat-and-wheat chuka-soba shop with no gluten-free or vegan options.

bio.background: Menya Ryugen opened in July 2016 on Ichijoji's quieter back lane, just off the neighborhood's famous ramen street, in the space once occupied by another ramen shop that had closed the year before. The owner is a Shinpuku Saikan man through and through: he started training at that legendary old Kyoto ramen house as a teenager and brings roughly three decades of chuka-soba experience to his own counter. When he opened, Shinpuku Saikan reportedly sent congratulatory flowers, a quiet nod from the parent house to one of its own.
bio.origin: Kyoto, in the Shinpuku Saikan lineage
bio.specialty: The signature bowl is a chuka-soba with the unmistakable Shinpuku-style jet-black broth (漆黒のブラックスープ) -- looks intensely dark from the soy but drinks cleaner and lighter than it appears, with pork-and-chicken depth, thin silky noodles, thin tender pork-loin chashu, bean sprouts and chopped scallion. The other half of the order is the yakimeshi (fried rice), seasoned dark and aromatic with soy; many regulars come as much for the rice as the ramen, and the ramen-and-yakimeshi set is the classic combination.
bio.philosophy: Straightforward, traditional, unpretentious ramen that honors the Shinpuku Saikan template rather than chasing trends -- a bowl that, as one writer put it, tastes like the ramen of twenty years ago, in the best way.

anecdotes.text: The owner began his training at Shinpuku Saikan at age 19 and has around 30 years of chuka-soba experience behind the counter.
Shinpuku Saikan, the parent house, sent congratulatory flowers when Ryugen opened, and the bowl closely mirrors Shinpuku's true-black soy broth.

The shop took over a small Ichijoji space (a former ramen shop that closed in 2015) just off the main ramen-street battleground, and despite the area's high turnover it has steadily grown popular and fills up at lunch.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The defining feature of the bowl is a deep, jet-black soy-sauce broth/tare in the Shinpuku Saikan style; soy-sauce tare of this type is standard wheat-based shoyu, so the ramen should be treated as containing wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is a pork-bone and chicken base with pork-loin chashu, and the yakimeshi is cooked with pork; there are no plant-based items, so cross-contact with animal products is total.

lead: 汁なし担々麺＆麻婆豆腐 ラアノウミ
name_ja: 汁なし担々麺＆麻婆豆腐 ラアノウミ
cuisine: Shiru-nashi tantanmen (brothless dan dan noodles) and mapo tofu
neighborhood: 上京区 今出川
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core dishes are wheat noodles dressed in a sesame-and-chili-oil tantan sauce, and the menu centers on tantanmen and mabo-men (mapo-style noodles) — all wheat-based. The spicy sauces and mapo preparations typically rely on wheat-containing seasonings (soy sauce, doubanjiang). No gluten-free options or allergen accommodations are advertised. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. This is a tantanmen and mapo tofu specialist; the signature bowls use sesame/chili-oil sauce with minced pork, and the mapo tofu is meat-based. No vegan or vegetarian menu items are advertised. A vegan diner could not reliably eat the signature dishes here.
address_ja: 京都府京都市上京区上立売町1-7 瑞祥ビル B1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 京都府京都市上京区上立売町1-7 returned [135.756775, 35.032082], within the Kyoto box.
seats_text: 19 seats (9 counter, 10 table)
hours_raw: Lunch 11:00-15:00 (L.O. 15:15 weekdays / 15:30 weekends & holidays); Dinner 17:00-21:30 (L.O. 21:00). Official site lists 不定休 (irregular closures); Tabelog notes closed only for year-end/New Year holidays.
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular days off); Tabelog lists year-end/New Year holidays
cultural_comfort_note: A small, student-friendly counter-and-table noodle shop near Doshisha; a friendly greeting and pointing at the menu will carry you, though staff communication is in Japanese.
caveats: Reservations are not accepted for regular dining (walk-in only), though large private groups can arrange catering. Official site states irregular closures, so confirm the day before visiting. Cash and PayPay only — no credit cards or e-money.

bio.background: A small independent noodle shop tucked into the basement of the Zuisho Building near Doshisha University's Shinmachi campus, just a short walk from Imadegawa Station. It bills itself as the only specialty shop in Kyoto City devoted to shiru-nashi tantanmen (brothless dan dan noodles), and draws a steady student crowd at lunch.
bio.specialty: The signature bowl is the shiru-nashi tantanmen: wheat noodles tossed in a house-blended sauce of carefully made chili oil and several spices, finished with the aromatic fragrance of sesame so that umami comes through within the heat. The kitchen also makes a mapo-style noodle (汁なし麻婆麺) and a standalone mapo tofu, both with adjustable spice levels, plus rice that pairs with the spicy sauces.
bio.philosophy: The shop emphasizes handmade chili oil and its own custom spice blend, and a commitment to locally sourced (地産地消) Kyoto ingredients, aiming for a balance where the umami of the ingredients is still felt amid the spice.

anecdotes.text: Won the runner-up grand prize (準グランプリ) at the 'Ultimate Ramen Pia Kansai Edition' competition.
Promotes itself as Kyoto City's only shiru-nashi tantanmen specialty shop.

name_ja: ラーメン池田屋 京都一乗寺店
cuisine: Jiro-style ramen (二郎インスパイア)
gf_detail: Not safe for gluten-free or celiac diners. This is a Jiro-inspired ramen shop built around thick, chewy wheat noodles (the shop uses Nisshin Seifun wheat flour), a pork broth finished with a soy-based (wheat-containing) tare, and pork chashu. No gluten-free menu, noodle substitute, or allergen accommodation is advertised, and the kitchen is a single small counter operation with shared cooking water. Treat as off-limits for celiac needs.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians. The bowls are based on a rich emulsified pork (tonkotsu/pork-fat) broth with pork back-fat (abura), pork chashu, and a soy-based tare; vegetables like beansprouts and cabbage are piled on top but cooked in/with the pork system. No plant-based broth or vegan option is offered.
address_ja: 京都府京都市左京区高野玉岡町33-9
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI from the registered address 京都府京都市左京区高野玉岡町33-9; result [135.784607, 35.045246] falls within the Kyoto bounding box and matches the Takano/Ichijoji area near Eizan Railway Ichijoji Station.
neighborhood: 左京区 一乗寺・高野（叡山電鉄 一乗寺駅 近く／ラーメン街道）
seats_text: 9 counter seats only (counter-only, no tables).
hours_raw: Mon-Wed, Fri-Sun: 11:30-15:00, 18:00-23:00; Thursday closed. (Source variance: some listings give lunch 11:30-14:30 and weekday dinner ending 22:00, weekends to 23:00; occasional irregular closures noted.)
closed_days: Thursdays (with occasional irregular/temporary closures)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, busy, cash-only Jiro-style counter where you buy a meal ticket from a machine and call out garlic/vegetable/fat preferences in Japanese at the counter, so a little Japanese or a phrasebook makes it much smoother.
caveats: Cash only and large/high-denomination notes may not be accepted; no reservations; lines are common with typical waits around 15 minutes. Hours vary slightly between sources and the shop takes occasional unscheduled closures, so check the official X account (@ikedayakyoto) before going.

bio.background: Ikedaya began life outside Kyoto, in the Hokuriku region (linked to Nonoichi in Ishikawa and to Fukui), before the owner brought the shop to Kyoto's Ichijoji district in 2011. Ichijoji is one of Japan's most famous ramen battlegrounds, lined with shops along what locals call the 'ramen highway,' and Ikedaya quickly became one of its standout names. The shop later expanded with branches in Fukui and in the Osaka/Amagasaki area, but the small Ichijoji counter near Eizan Railway's Ichijoji Station remains its spiritual home.
bio.specialty: A bowl of Jiro-style ramen built on a thick, intensely porky broth that the kitchen emulsifies until it turns rich yet surprisingly smooth, ladled over fat, chewy wheat noodles you order by weight (from a 180g 'petit' up to a 500g 'large'). It comes loaded with tender beansprouts and cabbage and soft pork chashu, and at the counter you call out how much garlic, vegetables, and back-fat (abura) you want piled on.
bio.philosophy: Ikedaya is proudly an 'inspired' shop rather than an official Jiro branch: the owner never trained at the original and instead developed his own interpretation of the style, chasing maximum pork richness and volume on his own terms. It is unpretentious, ticket-machine, counter-only ramen meant to be eaten with focus rather than slurped daintily.
bio.origin: Hokuriku (Ishikawa/Fukui) origins; established in Kyoto Ichijoji in 2011.

anecdotes.text: Ikedaya is widely cited as one of the leading Jiro-inspired shops in Kyoto despite never being an official Jiro lineage shop, with fans debating whether it rivals the original style.
The shop relocated to Kyoto's Ichijoji ramen district and opened there in 2011, later expanding to Fukui and the Osaka area.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core dish is thick wheat ramen noodles made with Nisshin Seifun wheat flour, served from a small single-counter kitchen with shared boiling water, making gluten cross-contamination effectively unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is finished with a soy-based (shoyu) tare, which in standard ramen contains wheat; no wheat-free tare is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is an emulsified pork-bone/pork-fat base with pork back-fat and pork chashu; vegetables are cooked within this pork-based system, so no vegan-safe preparation exists.

lead: 麺匠 たか松 本店
name_ja: 麺匠 たか松 本店
cuisine: つけ麺・ラーメン（鶏魚介スープ、石臼挽き全粒粉の細麺）
neighborhood: 中京区・四条烏丸
gf_detail: The entire concept is built on stone-ground whole-grain wheat noodles, and the chicken-seafood tsukemen broth is finished with a tare that almost certainly contains wheat-based shoyu. There is no gluten-free menu and no indication of a dedicated preparation area. This is not a safe choice for anyone with celiac disease or a wheat allergy. Confirm directly before any visit if cross-contact is a concern.
vegan_detail: The signature broth is an animal-based chicken-and-fish (鶏魚介) stock combining poultry with katsuo, saba and scallop, so the core offering is not vegan and not vegetarian. No plant-based ramen option is advertised. A strict vegan or vegetarian cannot eat the standard bowls here.
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区一蓮社町312
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned the 312-banchi point for 一蓮社町; within the Kyoto bounding box.
seats_text: 15 seats total (13 counter, 2 at a table) — a compact, counter-focused shop.
hours_raw: Mon–Fri 11:00–23:00 (L.O. 22:30); Sat/Sun/holidays open from 10:30. Tabelog lists last-order based hours as 11:00–22:30 weekdays, 10:30–22:30 weekends.
closed_days: 年末年始（年末年始のみ休業）
cultural_comfort_note: A popular, fast-moving counter tsukemen shop near Shijo-Karasuma; a friendly greeting and pointing at the menu is plenty, though staff are used to a steady mix of locals and visitors.

bio.origin: Founded in 2011 in the Shijo-Karasuma district of central Kyoto, this is the flagship (honten) of the Takamatsu noodle house, which has since grown to several shops around the city.
bio.background: Takamatsu built its name on tsukemen (dipping noodles) at a time when the style was still uncommon in Kyoto, and the main shop remains a small counter-driven room where the noodles take center stage.
bio.specialty: The signature bowl is a tsukemen of thin, roughly 2mm stone-ground whole-grain noodles served with a clean, unthickened chicken-and-seafood dipping broth that blends select chicken with katsuo, saba and scallop. The kitchen leans on whole-grain flour milled from Nagano-grown wheat, giving the noodles a rustic, wheat-forward character closer to country soba than typical glossy tsukemen noodles.
bio.philosophy: The house motto is 'a single bowl of happiness, every day' (毎日に、一杯の幸せを) — an everyday, repeatable comfort rather than a special-occasion meal.

anecdotes.text: Diners are encouraged to shift the flavor mid-meal with a trio of accents — fresh sudachi citrus, a Kyoto black shichimi, and onion — so a single bowl moves from bright and citrusy to deeper and spicier as you eat.
The whole-grain noodles are deliberately thinner than usual tsukemen noodles and show visible rough edges; the shop treats this rustic texture as a point of pride rather than a flaw.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat noodles and a wheat-shoyu-based tare are central to every bowl; there is no separate gluten-free preparation and cross-contact with wheat is unavoidable in a small tsukemen kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The chicken-seafood dipping broth is built on a soy-based tare, which in standard Japanese ramen/tsukemen contains wheat unless stated otherwise; no wheat-free shoyu is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All bowls use an animal (chicken plus katsuo/saba/scallop) broth; no plant-based stock or vegan option is offered, so there is nothing safe for a strict vegan.

cuisine: 貝出汁あっさり中華そば（貝ラーメン）
neighborhood: 中京区・烏丸御池
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区場之町588
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned [135.759399, 35.009243] for 京都市中京区場之町588; within Kyoto box.
seats_text: Counter seating on the 1st floor and table seating on the 2nd floor; exact count not stated.
closed_days: Thursdays
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, cafe-like counter ramen shop in a Kyoto office district; no reservations and cash-only, so a guide or a few words of Japanese helps but it is approachable for solo diners.
gf_detail: The house noodles are made fresh daily from a blend of whole-wheat and udon flour, and the broth uses a soy-sauce (shoyu) based tare, which almost always contains wheat. This is a wheat-noodle, wheat-tare ramen and is not safe for celiac diners; no gluten-free option is indicated.
vegan_detail: All bowls are built on a shellfish (oyster, clam, shrimp) dashi combined with a soy-based tare, and the signature topping is low-temperature seared rare pork charshiu. There is no vegan or vegetarian ramen on the menu.
caveats: Exact seat count not published. Hours and closed day (Thursday) confirmed consistently across the official site and Tabelog.

bio.background: 麺屋 優光 is a small, independent ramen shop tucked into the Karasuma Oike office district of central Kyoto. Its owner trained at the celebrated Osaka ramen shop 人類みな麺類 (Jinrui Mina Menrui) before opening here. The space deliberately feels more like a bright, stylish cafe than a typical ramen counter, with seats at the counter downstairs and tables upstairs, and it draws a notably high share of women and solo diners.
bio.specialty: A shellfish-based 中華そば (kai-dashi ramen) that is genuinely rare for Kyoto: the soup layers the umami of oysters, clams and shrimp into a soy-based tare for a clean but pronounced, slightly sweet savoriness. The three regular bowls are named after types of bamboo — 淡竹 (Hachiku), 真竹 (Madake) and 黒竹 (Kurochiku) — with Hachiku the most popular, finished with several thick slices of soft, low-temperature seared rare pork charshiu.
bio.philosophy: The shop frames itself around caring for the customer during the short time they spend eating — making a quick bowl of ramen feel like a considered, hospitable experience.

anecdotes.text: The owner trained at the famous Osaka ramen shop 人類みな麺類 (Jinrui Mina Menrui) before opening 麺屋 優光.
The noodles are made in-house daily from a blend of whole-wheat flour and udon flour, giving a distinctive aroma and a firm, powerful texture.
A lunch-only 炙りレア焼豚丼 (torched rare pork rice bowl) made with diced charshiu is offered as an add-on.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is built on a soy-sauce (shoyu) based tare, which conventionally contains wheat.

lead: あいつのラーメン かたぐるま 本店
name_ja: あいつのラーメン かたぐるま 本店
cuisine: 鶏×魚介・豚骨の泡系ラーメン／つけ麺（こくまろ塩・濁とろ塩・うるとらつけ麺）
gf_detail: This is a wheat-noodle ramen and tsukemen specialist. The bowls use wheat-based noodles, the tare is built on pork-bone/salt and soy bases (the dinner menu shifts to soy-pork-bone variations), and broths are long-simmered pork bone with chicken and fish elements. No gluten-free noodles, tare, or dedicated preparation are indicated anywhere. Not suitable for celiac or strict gluten avoidance.
vegan_detail: A meat- and fish-forward shop: signature broths are pork-bone (豚骨) simmered 10+ hours, with chicken and seafood, and bowls are topped with generous rare chashu (9-11 slices). No vegan or vegetarian ramen is offered or mentioned.
address_ja: 京都府京都市下京区西七条西八反田町77 ガーディアン青山1F
geocode_note: GSI address search on 京都府京都市下京区西七条西八反田町77 returned first feature coordinates [135.736237, 34.993118], within the Kyoto box. Consistent with the Tambaguchi (JR) area location described by sources.
neighborhood: 下京区西七条（JR丹波口駅 徒歩約10分）
seats_text: 15 counter seats (counter only). Children of preschool age and under are not admitted.
closed_days: 不定休（年末年始は休業）。臨時休業あり。
cultural_comfort_note: A busy, locals-focused single-person-oriented ramen counter that runs a Japanese-language LINE numbered-ticket queue system, so a little Japanese or a translation app helps; staff are used to a fast lunch turnover rather than detailed allergy consultations.
caveats: Daytime service is salt-based broths; evening shifts to soy/pork-bone variations, so the exact bowl differs by time of day. Hours are listed identically every day but the shop takes irregular days off (不定休) — confirm the day's status via the shop's LINE/Instagram before traveling. A second branch exists at Kitano-Hakubaicho; this record is the 本店 (main shop).

bio.origin: 下京区西七条、JR丹波口駅から徒歩約10分。2014年5月開業。
bio.background: The owner trained at 俺のラーメンあっぱれ屋 (Ore no Ramen Appareya), the famous ramen shop tucked in the hills of Kyoto, before striking out on his own and opening this shop in the city. He kept the mentor shop's menu DNA — even the dish names nod to Appareya (こくまろ塩 to its こくとん, 濁とろ塩 to its 濁とん, うるとらつけ麺 to its スーパーつけ麺) — while building a distinct identity of his own.
bio.specialty: The signature is the うるとらつけ麺 (Ultra Tsukemen): a濃厚豚骨, foam-rich dipping broth carrying assertive salt and spice, paired with house-made chewy flat noodles with a smooth finish. The shop is also known for kaiseki-like plating piled with generous rare chashu (often 9-11 slices). By day the lineup leans on salt-based broths; by night it shifts to soy/pork-bone variations.
bio.philosophy: A counter-focused, single-diner-friendly shop (おひとり様専門店) that opens unusually early at 10:30 and runs a flavored-oil 'no-leftover' approach so guests can vary the soup's character as they eat — the kind of detail-obsessed, owner-run craft inherited from its Appareya training.

anecdotes.text: The owner apprenticed at the celebrated mountain ramen-ya 俺のラーメンあっぱれ屋 before opening on his own, and pays homage through parallel dish names while diverging from Appareya's more refined seafood-leaning dip toward a thicker, spicier pork-bone style.
Opened in May 2014 under representative Yasunori Ueshima; styled as an おひとり様専門店 (solo-diner specialty shop) with a LINE numbered-ticket queue.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat noodles, wheat-containing tare (salt and soy/pork-bone bases), and shared broth pots mean gluten is pervasive; no separate gluten-free handling is indicated.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Evening menu shifts to soy/pork-bone (醤油系) variations; ramen shoyu tare in Japan is standardly wheat-based.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All bowls are built on pork-bone/chicken/fish broth and topped with chashu; no vegan preparation exists, so cross-contact with animal products is unavoidable.

lead: 京つけめん つるかめ 六角
name_ja: 京つけめん つるかめ 六角
cuisine: 京つけめん・魚介系ラーメン（tsukemen / gyokai shoyu ramen）
gf_detail: Not gluten-free and not celiac-safe. The house specialty is self-made thick wavy noodles made with whole wheat flour (自家製の全粒粉入り加水麺), served with a seafood-and-pork shoyu dipping broth that uses wheat-based soy sauce tare. Both the noodles and the tare contain wheat; broths are animal/fish based. No gluten-free menu, dedicated preparation, or published allergy program were found. A celiac or strict gluten-avoider cannot eat the core dishes here.
vegan_detail: No vegan options. The kitchen is built around fish (dried sardine/bonito) and pork/chicken broths, with chashu pork as a standard topping; the signature is a seafood-pork shoyu tsukemen. No plant-based broth or vegan bowl is offered. A vegan diner has nothing suitable on the menu.
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区新京極通六角東入松ヶ枝町456-3 中野ビル1F
geocode_note: Geocoded from the full street address via GSI; result (lng 135.751389, lat 35.009998) falls within the Kyoto box and is consistent with the Shinkyogoku/Rokkaku location near Kyoto City Hall-mae Station. A chō-level retry (松ケ枝町) returned 135.76828, 35.00758; the full-address result was preferred as more precise.
neighborhood: 中京区・新京極通六角（京都市役所前駅／三条・河原町エリア）
seats_text: About 10 counter seats plus one 4-person table (very small, compact space; a rear entrance is used for customer flow)
closed_days: 無休（年中無休 / open year-round）
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, no-frills counter tsukemen shop where solo diners are welcome and staff will explain the menu, but it is a Japanese-language local spot, so a few words of Japanese or a translation app will smooth the visit.
caveats: Listed hours differ slightly by source (11:00-21:30 vs. 11:30-22:00); confirm on arrival. Payment is best treated as cash; one aggregator mentions PayPay but Tabelog lists no cashless options. No reservations.

bio.background: A small, tucked-away tsukemen specialist on Rokkaku-dori in the Shinkyogoku/Teramachi shopping warren, just a short walk from Shijo-Kawaramachi and Kyoto City Hall-mae Station. The room is famously compact — an L-shaped counter of roughly ten seats and a single table, so tight that a separate rear door is used to manage the flow of customers — which gives it the feel of a true neighborhood noodle den.
bio.specialty: The signature bowl is the gyokai shoyu tsukemen (魚介醤油つけ麺): self-made, thick wavy noodles cut with whole-wheat flour, given free in a large portion, dipped into a concentrated seafood-and-pork shoyu broth balanced between animal umami and dried sardine/bonito notes, finished with tender chashu and a dusting of aonori. The shop also runs ramen, cold mixed noodles, a miso-tantan tsukemen, and rotating limited bowls.
bio.philosophy: Straightforwardly delicious, honest tsukemen — chewy house-made noodles in generous portions with broth that clings to every strand, the kind of bowl regulars return to rather than a one-time novelty.

anecdotes.text: The noodles are made in-house from domestic whole-grain wheat for a notably chewy bite, and large portions are offered at no extra charge — with free white rice for orders before 2 p.m., often finished as tamagokake-gohan or charred-chashu rice.
The space is so small it is described as an 極小スペース (ultra-compact space), an intimate counter that gives it an authentic, lived-in ramen-shop atmosphere.
Staff are known for attentively explaining the menu, which makes it approachable even for a first-time tsukemen eater.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The dipping broth is a shoyu-based seafood/pork tare, which in a Japanese tsukemen shop is made with conventional wheat-containing soy sauce; no wheat-free soy sauce is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths are fish (dried sardine/bonito) and pork/chicken based and chashu pork is a standard topping, so there is no separation from animal products for a plant-based diner.

positives.text: Staff are noted for friendly, attentive menu explanations, so a diner with questions can ask about a dish before ordering.

lead: ラーメン荘 地球規模で考えろ
name_ja: ラーメン荘 地球規模で考えろ 伏見本店
name_en: Ramen So Chikyu Kibo de Kangaero (Fushimi Honten)
cuisine: 二郎系ラーメン（豚骨醤油ベース、味噌の提供あり）
gf_detail: This is a Jiro-style ramen shop: thick house-made wheat noodles in a pork-bone broth seasoned with a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare, which is virtually always wheat-based. Generous garlic and pork chashu round out the bowl. There is no gluten-free noodle option and no indication of any allergen accommodation. Not suitable for celiac or gluten-avoidant diners.
vegan_detail: A meat-forward Jiro-style shop built on pork-bone broth and large amounts of pork chashu/back-fat. Toppings (extra vegetables, garlic, fat, chili) sit on top of an animal broth, so even a "vegetable-heavy" bowl is not vegan. No vegan or vegetarian option is offered.
address_ja: 京都府京都市伏見区撞木町1153-9
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates [135.765091, 34.945484] for 京都府京都市伏見区撞木町1153, within the Kyoto bounding box. House-number 1153-9 resolves to the chō-level point.
neighborhood: 京都市伏見区（近鉄 桃山御陵前駅 / 京阪 伏見桃山駅 周辺）
seats_text: About 12 counter seats (counter only).
closed_days: Monday and Tuesday (some sources list Monday only; subject to change)
cultural_comfort_note: A no-frills, high-volume Jiro-style counter where you buy a ticket, then call out your topping preferences (garlic, vegetables, fat) in Japanese when the noodles go in; some basic Japanese and familiarity with the ordering ritual make the visit much smoother.

bio.origin: Ichijoji, Kyoto (parent shop Yume wo Katare)
bio.background: Opened in 2009 as a spinoff of the famous Ichijoji Jiro-inspired shop "夢を語れ" (Yume wo Katare), this Fushimi flagship grew into one of the standard-bearers of the Kansai Jiro-kei (Jiro-style) ramen scene and went on to spawn multiple branches across the region.
bio.specialty: A thunderous Jiro-style bowl built on house-made flat, extra-thick noodles and a non-emulsified pork-bone-and-soy broth, piled with mountains of vegetables, garlic and back-fat. The pork-heavy "豚ラーメン" comes loaded with roughly 200g of chashu.
bio.philosophy: Big, generous, made-to-order satisfaction: the kitchen prizes the sharp cut of the soy tare layered over deep umami, and lets each customer dial in the bowl with the classic Jiro call of garlic, vegetables and fat for an "overwhelmingly satisfying" finish.

anecdotes.text: Customers buy a meal ticket and queue; staff ask your topping preferences (vegetables, garlic, fat, chili) just as the noodles are being cooked, in the classic Jiro ordering ritual.
Described as a representative presence of the Kansai Jiro-inspired scene and one of the area's most reliably lined-up shops.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Bowls center on house-made wheat noodles boiled in a shared pot; there is no gluten-free preparation, so cross-contamination is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is seasoned with a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare, which in a Jiro-style shop is virtually always wheat-containing soy sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All bowls share a pork-bone broth and pork back-fat; vegetable, garlic and fat toppings are added on top of the animal broth, so no bowl is free of animal products.

cuisine: 貝だし塩ラーメン（kai-dashi shio）— clam/shellfish dashi salt ramen
neighborhood: 下京区・京都駅周辺 (Shimogyo-ku, near Kyoto Station)
gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The bowls are built on wheat ramen noodles — the shop uses Hokkaido "yumechikara" wheat for its straight thin noodles — and a salt (shio) tare that, like nearly all Japanese ramen seasoning, is very likely to contain wheat-based ingredients. The shellfish dashi broth itself (hamaguri, asari, hotate) is naturally gluten-light, but the noodles and tare make the dish unsafe for anyone with celiac disease or wheat allergy. No gluten-free menu, no separate prep, and no dedicated equipment were found. A wheat-avoiding diner should not eat the ramen here.
vegan_detail: This is a shellfish-broth ramen specialist — every signature bowl is built on a clam/scallop (hamaguri, asari, hotate) clear dashi, and bowls are topped with pork-loin chashu. There is no vegan or vegetarian broth, and no plant-based bowl was found on the menu. Not a destination for vegan or vegetarian diners.
address_ja: 京都府京都市下京区北不動堂町570-3
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned coordinates for 京都府京都市下京区北不動堂町570-3; within Kyoto box.
seats_text: About 11 seats — roughly 7 counter seats plus two small tables. A tight, single-room shop.
hours_raw: 7:00–24:00 daily (L.O. 23:30). Opens at 7:00 for morning ramen (朝ラー). Note: some sources list closing at 22:00 / L.O. 21:30; hours can vary, so confirm late-night service before a midnight visit.
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular closures — no fixed day off)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, busy local ramen counter near Kyoto Station with no reservations and frequent queues — a warm greeting and a little patience go a long way, but English support should not be assumed.
caveats: Hours vary by source (midnight vs. 22:00 close) and the shop takes irregular days off, so confirm before a late or early trip. No parking and no restroom on-site. Expect a line, especially mid-morning.

bio.background: A specialist ramen counter a short walk from Kyoto Station, Kitada built its reputation on one idea done very well: a clear, fragrant broth drawn entirely from shellfish. The shop opens early — its doors are up at 7:00 a.m. — which has made it a favorite stop for 'asa-ra' (morning ramen), with a line of regulars and travelers already forming mid-morning.
bio.specialty: Kai-dashi shio ramen — a clear, salt-seasoned broth simmered from generous amounts of hamaguri (hard clam), asari (littleneck clam) and hotate (scallop), giving a clean, sweet shellfish umami rather than the usual pork-bone heaviness. The signature 'specialty' bowl and the 蛤らぁ麺 (hamaguri ramen) lead the menu; the regular 貝だし麺 layers in a house-made aromatic shellfish oil for a richer hit. Straight thin noodles milled from Hokkaido 'yumechikara' wheat, pork-loin chashu, red onion and green sprouts round out the bowl, with miso (貝だし辛味噌) and a classic chuka-soba also offered.
bio.philosophy: Let the shellfish speak — a clear, delicate broth that is light enough to drink in the morning yet still eats like a full bowl of ramen.

anecdotes.text: Kitada opens at 7:00 a.m., which has made it one of Kyoto Station's go-to spots for morning ramen; reviewers describe queues of several people already waiting through the mid-morning hours.

The broth is built on scallop, littleneck clam and hard clam in large quantity, finished with salt to lift the natural sweetness — described as a clear soup carrying the distinct sweet umami of shellfish.
The shop's 蛤らぁ麺 is noted for a concentrated clam broth that stays refreshing thanks to the salt seasoning, paired with thin straight whole-grain noodles.

cuisine: 京都ラーメン（鶏ガラ醤油・背脂系／中華そば）— 焼めしセットの地元店
gf_detail: This is a classic Kyoto ramen shop: wheat noodles in a chicken/pork soy-sauce (shoyu) broth, plus a fried-rice set. The shoyu tare is wheat-based and the broth is chicken/pork — there is no gluten-free menu, no dedicated fryer, and no indication of celiac-safe preparation. Not suitable for someone who must strictly avoid gluten.
vegan_detail: The core offering is a chicken-gara / pork-bone soy-sauce ramen with back-fat, served with chashu pork; the fried rice is also savory soy-sauce based. There are no vegetarian or vegan ramen options, and the broth itself is animal-based. Not a fit for vegan or vegetarian diners.
address_ja: 京都府京都市左京区下鴨高木町30-1
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to chōme-level address 京都府京都市左京区下鴨高木町30; coordinates fall within the Kyoto bounding box.
seats_text: About 23 seats — counter seating plus four four-person tables; more spacious inside than the modest exterior suggests.
hours_raw: 11:00-14:30 (L.O. 14:20), 18:00-20:50. Closed Wednesdays. (Some listings show evening service to around 21:00.)
closed_days: Wednesday
cultural_comfort_note: A small, no-frills neighborhood ramen shop where ordering is in Japanese; staff are described as kind, but expect minimal English and a quick-turnover local atmosphere.
caveats: Cash only (some QR payments accepted; no cards/e-money). No reservations — walk-in only. Closed Wednesdays.
neighborhood: 左京区 下鴨高木町（下鴨神社近く／松ケ崎駅 徒歩約11分、バス停「下鴨東本町」徒歩約2分）

bio.background: Ebisuya opened in the Shimogamo neighborhood in January 2013, calling itself a maker of "original Kyoto ramen" (元祖京都らーめん) — a deliberate nod to the back-fat chicken-and-soy-sauce style that defined the city's noodle culture before the wave of creamy tonkotsu shops.
bio.specialty: The signature bowl is a chicken-gara soy-sauce ramen crowned with pork back-fat (背脂チャッチャ系), built on thin, slightly curly Kyoto-style noodles; a tonkotsu version rounds out the menu. The fried-rice set (焼めしセット) is a local favorite, praised for an aromatic soy fragrance and a gentle, well-made character likened to a proper Chinese kitchen.
bio.philosophy: The shop is unapologetically nostalgic — it preserves the older Kyoto ramen identity its owner grew up with rather than chasing modern trends.

anecdotes.text: Reviewers describe the chicken-soy broth as mellow and deeply savory without harsh saltiness, with thin curly noodles that suit it well; the interior is surprisingly roomy behind a modest storefront, and staff stayed kind even to late-arriving customers.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles are boiled in shared water and the menu is built around wheat noodles and a wheat-based soy-sauce tare, so cross-contact with gluten is inherent throughout the kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Both the ramen tare and the fried rice are soy-sauce based; Japanese soy sauce is brewed with wheat, so these dishes contain gluten.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All ramen broths are animal-based (chicken-gara / pork-bone) and bowls include pork back-fat and chashu, so there is no vegan-safe preparation.

cuisine: 煮干し中華そば・つけ麺・汁なし担担麺 (niboshi chuka soba, tsukemen, shiru-nashi tantanmen)
neighborhood: 下京区 朱雀（梅小路京都西駅／西大路八条）
address_ja: 京都府京都市下京区朱雀正会町1-16
geocode_note: GSI address-search first feature; coordinates within Kyoto box.
seats_text: 17 seats: 9 counter seats plus 2 tables seating 4.
hours_raw: 11:30-14:30, 18:00-22:00 (last entry approx 21:13-21:14); closed Wednesdays.
closed_days: Wednesdays
cultural_comfort_note: A small, busy local counter shop where ordering in Japanese is expected; non-Japanese diners are welcome but should not expect English support.
gf_detail: This is a classic ramen shop built on wheat noodles, wheat-based shoyu tare, and duck/fish (niboshi) broths, plus tsukemen and tantanmen that are likewise wheat-heavy. There is no indication of gluten-free options or any celiac-safe accommodation; not safe for celiacs.
vegan_detail: The menu is centered on animal-based broths (Kyoto duck, nodoguro and other niboshi fish, pork chashu, chicken) with no vegan or vegetarian ramen offering noted. Not a vegan-friendly destination.
caveats: Cash only and no reservations; the 17-seat shop fills up at peak hours so expect a wait. The owner underwent medical treatment and the shop reopened in August 2025 — confirm current operation before a special trip.

bio.background: 拳ラーメン (Kobushi Ramen) sits a few minutes' walk from Umekoji Park near Umekoji-Kyotonishi Station in Kyoto's Shimogyo Ward. The shop relocated and was renamed from its earlier incarnation 'こぶ志' in March 2011, and has grown into one of the area's most respected ramen counters. After the owner stepped away for a period of medical treatment, the shop reopened in August 2025.
bio.specialty: The signature bowl is the 京鴨とのどぐろ煮干しそば — a niboshi chuka soba built on a base of Kyoto duck stock layered with the rich umami of nodoguro (blackthroat seaperch), finishing clean and elegant. Yuzu vinegar is offered on the side to shift the flavor mid-bowl. The shop also serves tsukemen, tantanmen and shiru-nashi (brothless) tantanmen.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen works from a 'fusion of washoku technique and ramen' approach, bringing Japanese-cuisine precision to every element. Pork-shoulder chashu is sliced to order just before serving to keep its aroma and fat at their best, and the bowl pairs medium-thin straight noodles from the well-known Kyoto noodle maker Teigyaku (棣鄂) to suit the delicate broth.

anecdotes.text: The shop's washoku-meets-ramen style and its refined Kyoto-duck-and-nodoguro niboshi broth have earned it Michelin Bib Gourmand recognition and repeated selection among Kyoto's top ramen shops.
Chashu is sliced fresh right before each bowl is served so the meat's aroma and fat richness come through at their peak.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Shoyu-based ramen tare at Japanese ramen shops is normally made with wheat-containing soy sauce, and the noodles are wheat; no gluten-free preparation is indicated here.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths and toppings are animal-based (duck, fish niboshi, pork, chicken); no separate vegan preparation is noted.

lead: セアブラノ神 伏見剛力
name_ja: セアブラノ神 伏見剛力
cuisine: 二郎系ラーメン（神二郎）・背脂中華そば・まぜそば
gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. This is a Jiro-style ramen specialist: the signature "Kami Jiro" uses thick, heavily-crinkled wheat noodles, and the bowls are seasoned with a wheat-based soy sauce tare (Kyoto Gokko shoyu) over a pork-and-fish broth finished with seasoned back fat. There are no gluten-free noodles, tare, or dedicated preparation, and the kitchen is small and shared. No published allergen handling. Anyone with celiac disease should avoid; a gluten-sensitive diner should treat everything here as containing wheat.
vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian option. The entire menu is built on pork-bone and fish broth, pork back fat, roasted/braised pork, and ground pork toppings. Even the vegetable-forward items (bean sprouts, corn) sit in a meat-and-fish broth. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
address_ja: 京都府京都市伏見区深草柴田屋敷町23-27
geocode_note: GSI address-search first feature, within Kyoto box. Matches Fushimi-ku, just by Fushimi Station (Keihan/Kintetsu).
neighborhood: 伏見区 深草柴田屋敷町（伏見駅前）
seats_text: About 13 seats: a counter (roughly 7-9 stools) plus a small raised tatami area for a few diners at the back. A compact shop.
hours_raw: 11:30-15:00 (L.O. 14:30) / 18:00-21:30 (L.O. 21:00). Closed Wednesdays and Jan 1. Some limited/"guerrilla" items sell out earlier in the day.
closed_days: Wednesdays; also closed Jan 1 (New Year's Day)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, locals-focused Jiro-style ramen counter where ordering is in Japanese and English support shouldn't be assumed; knowing how to call your noodle size and toppings (and a few words of Japanese) makes it smoother.
caveats: Cash only, no reservations, and just ~13 seats, so expect to queue at peak times. Hours and the lineup of "limited" bowls shift; the shop reworked its style toward a rougher, more powerful "Goriki" presentation, so daily specials may differ from older write-ups. Wheat- and pork-heavy throughout — not for GF, vegan, or vegetarian diners.

bio.background: This is the Fushimi outpost of Seabura no Kami, the Kyoto ramen group whose flagship is in Mibu (Shimogyo), with additional shops including a Nishiki location. Set right by Fushimi Station in southern Kyoto, the Goriki branch grew its own identity rather than simply copying the main shop, and at one point reworked its concept toward a rougher, more forceful 'Goriki' (powerful) style with notes borrowed from the bold ramen culture of Tsubame-Sanjo.
bio.origin: Kyoto (Seabura no Kami group; flagship in Mibu, Shimogyo-ku)
bio.specialty: Kami Jiro — the shop's own take on Jiro-style ramen, served with a dedicated extra-thick, heavily-crinkled noodle in 200g/300g/400g sizes, over a pork-and-fish broth crowned with back fat that's been seasoned with Kyoto Gokko soy sauce for a sweet, umami finish. The Fushimi shop also runs frequent 'guerrilla' limited bowls (miso Kami Jiro, niboshi back-fat soba, tantan tsukemen and more) alongside its mazesoba.
bio.philosophy: An approachable, Kyoto-tuned read on Jiro: serious volume and back fat, but tempered — finer-than-typical Jiro noodles and a lighter, less heavy broth (often with grated fresh ginger offered in place of mandatory raw garlic) so newcomers can enjoy a Jiro-style bowl, while the renovated 'Goriki' direction keeps it satisfying for hardcore fans.

anecdotes.text: The 'Kami Jiro' began as an occasional limited special before becoming a regular menu item at the Fushimi shop, and uses a noodle made specifically for that bowl.

A renovation pushed the Fushimi branch toward a stronger, rawer 'Goriki' presentation influenced by Tsubame-Sanjo ramen, swapping refined rare chashu for braised pork belly and leaning into bigger, bolder bowls.

Diners describe the Fushimi bowls as deliberately more approachable than hardcore Jiro — finer noodles, gentler white broth, with ginger tempering the richness — positioned as a friendly entry point to the style.

gf_cross_contamination.text: A dedicated Jiro-style ramen kitchen using wheat noodles and wheat soy-sauce tare in a small shared space; no gluten-free preparation and high risk of wheat throughout.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The back fat and tare are seasoned with Kyoto Gokko (五光) soy sauce, a standard wheat-containing shoyu.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is pork-bone and fish based with back fat and pork toppings; even vegetable items are served in the meat/fish broth, so no dish is free of animal products.

lead: 麺匠 一粒万倍 寺町店
name_ja: 麺匠 一粒万倍 寺町店
cuisine: 淡麗系 鴨・大山どり清湯ラーメン(醤油/塩)、濃厚魚介豚骨つけ麺
neighborhood: 京都河原町・寺町綾小路
address_ja: 京都府京都市下京区貞安前之町586 寺町綾小路ビル 1F
geocode_note: GSI address search for 貞安前之町586 returned coordinates within the Kyoto box.
seats_text: 26席(カウンター14席、テーブル4卓)
closed_days: 無休 (no regular closing day)
cultural_comfort_note: An English menu is available and the shop is accustomed to international visitors, so ordering should be straightforward for non-Japanese speakers.
gf_detail: This is a classic wheat-noodle ramen and tsukemen shop. The noodles are wheat-based (the thick tsukemen noodles are made with bran/ふすま), and the soy (醤油) tare is almost certainly wheat-based shoyu. There is no indication of any gluten-free option or accommodation, and broths are duck/chicken or pork-bone-and-fish based. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: All bowls are built on animal stocks — a light duck-and-Daisen-chicken clear broth for the shoyu/shio bowls, and a rich pork-bone-and-fish (魚介豚骨) base for the tsukemen. Toppings include pork and chicken chashu. No plant-based ramen is offered.

bio.background: A Kyoto-grown ramen shop. The first 一粒万倍 opened near Sanjo Kawaramachi in 2019, and this Teramachi branch followed in January 2022, on Teramachi street just south of Shijo near Kyoto-Kawaramachi station; sister shops have since opened in Saiin and Gion. The name 一粒万倍 comes from 一粒万倍日, an auspicious day in the Japanese calendar meaning that a single grain of rice multiplies ten-thousandfold — a wish for prosperity that the shop extends to its guests.
bio.specialty: A light, refined (淡麗系) clear broth built on duck and branded Daisen chicken (大山どり, from Tottori), served as both 醤油 (soy) and 塩 (salt) bowls. The premium soy bowl pairs a duck-and-chicken double broth with both pork and chicken-breast chashu. The Teramachi branch also has its own signature: a rich fish-and-pork-bone tsukemen offered in bonito, spicy, and shrimp variations.
bio.philosophy: Bringing good fortune to the diner — the shop leans into its auspicious name to make a bowl of ramen feel lucky as well as satisfying.

anecdotes.text: The shop sits across from a shrine on Teramachi and frames eating its ramen as a small piece of good luck, playing on the meaning of 一粒万倍 — one grain becoming ten thousand.

What began as a single Sanjo-Kawaramachi shop in 2019 has expanded into a small local group, with the Teramachi branch (2022) adding its own exclusive rich fish-pork-bone tsukemen alongside the house clear-broth bowls.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat noodles are the core product and the thick tsukemen noodles contain bran (ふすま); shared noodle-cooking water and a wheat-based soy tare make this a high-gluten environment.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The 醤油 (soy) ramen uses a soy-based tare, which in standard Japanese shoyu contains wheat. No wheat-free tare is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Every broth is animal-based (duck/chicken clear soup or pork-bone-and-fish tsukemen) and toppings include pork and chicken chashu; no plant-based preparation exists.

positives.text: An English menu is available and the shop is used to international customers, which can make it easier to ask questions about ingredients.

caveats: Cash-only (ticket vending machine; no cards or e-money). No reservations and queues are common in the evening. Not gluten-free or vegan-friendly. Owner/chef name not found in sources.

lead: 麺屋 優光 河原町
name_ja: 麺屋 優光 河原町
cuisine: 貝出汁の淡麗醤油ラーメン／鴨だしつけ麺 (tanrei kai-shoyu ramen / duck-dashi tsukemen)
gf_detail: No gluten-free provision. This is a classic wheat-noodle ramen shop: the bowls use wheat noodles, and the shoyu (soy sauce) tare is built on Kyoto soy sauces that almost certainly contain wheat. Broths are shellfish (oyster/clam) and duck/bonito/kombu based. There is no indication of gluten-free noodles, separate preparation, or allergen accommodation, so it should be treated as not celiac-safe.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. Every signature bowl is animal-based: shellfish (oyster, clam, shimijiri) dashi shoyu ramen, duck-dashi tsukemen, and bonito/kombu/meat-enriched shoyu ramen, plus chashu pork and soft-egg toppings. No plant-based ramen is offered.
address_ja: 京都府京都市下京区稲荷町329 エリッツ四条河原町ビル 1F
geocode_note: Geocoded from 京都府京都市下京区稲荷町329 via GSI; result (35.00222, 135.769043) falls within the Kyoto box and matches the Shijo-Kawaramachi area just south of Takashimaya / Good Nature Station.
neighborhood: 四条河原町（高島屋南・下京区稲荷町）
seats_text: 11 counter seats (counter only).
hours_raw: Official site lists 11:00-22:00, open daily (年中無休). A listing aggregator shows extended hours of 11:00-00:30 (L.O. 00:00); hours may vary, so confirm before a late visit.
closed_days: None (open year-round / 年中無休)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, welcoming counter ramen shop in a busy central tourist area; a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu go a long way, and solo diners (including women) are explicitly welcomed.
caveats: Hours differ between sources (official 11:00-22:00 vs an aggregator's 11:00-00:30); the daily duck-dashi tsukemen is limited (around 15 servings) and may sell out. No reservations and counter-only seating, so expect a queue at peak times.

bio.background: Menya Yukou began as a humble yatai (street food stall) called 屋台優光 in Kyoto's Kiyamachi district before relocating to a permanent storefront in the heart of the city at Shijo-Kawaramachi, just south of Takashimaya and beside Good Nature Station. The owner trained at the renowned Osaka ramen shop 人類みな麺類 (Jinrui Mina Menrui) before striking out on his own. The Kawaramachi shop opened on September 23, 2021, and is the flagship of a small group that also runs 麺屋聖~kiyo~, a shop devoted to spreading Kyoto's dashi culture.
bio.origin: Kyoto (originally a yatai stall in Kiyamachi, now Shijo-Kawaramachi)
bio.specialty: A light, refined shellfish-dashi shoyu ramen (貝らーめん) that condenses the umami of oyster, clam and shijimi over a base of blended Kyoto soy sauces - a shellfish-broth style that is relatively rare in Kyoto. The shop is equally known for its rich duck-dashi tsukemen (鴨つけ麺), a group-first creation limited to roughly 15 bowls a day, and a Kyoto-style shoyu ramen layered with bonito, kombu and meat umami. A torched rare-chashu rice bowl (炙りレア焼豚丼) is a favorite side.
bio.philosophy: Built around Kyoto's dashi culture and freshly made noodles - the team makes noodles fresh each morning and leans on carefully blended Kyoto soy sauces to keep the broths clean and delicate rather than heavy.

anecdotes.text: The shop grew from a single Kiyamachi yatai (street stall) called 屋台優光 into a permanent Shijo-Kawaramachi storefront, with the owner having trained at Osaka's celebrated 人類みな麺類.
Designed to feel more like a cafe than a typical ramen shop, the interior plays Mr. Children throughout service and explicitly welcomes solo diners, including women.
The duck-dashi tsukemen was a first for the Yukou group and is limited to around 15 servings per day.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Broths and tare are built on blended Kyoto soy sauces, which in Japan are standardly brewed with wheat; combined with wheat ramen noodles this makes the bowls unsuitable for celiacs.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broths are animal-based (shellfish dashi, duck/bonito/kombu, meat-enriched shoyu) and bowls carry pork chashu and egg, so there is no plant-based separation.

lead: 濃厚京鶏白湯らーめん めんや 美鶴
name_ja: 濃厚京鶏白湯らーめん めんや 美鶴
name_en: Nōkō Kyō Tori Paitan Rāmen Menya Mitsuru
cuisine: 濃厚鶏白湯ラーメン（tori-paitan）／中華そば
neighborhood: 四条烏丸（菊水鉾町、中京区）
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区菊水鉾町587
geocode_note: GSI address search returned chō-level point for 菊水鉾町; within Kyoto box.
seats_text: 17 counter seats
hours_raw: Mon-Thu 11:00-22:30 (L.O. 22:00); Fri-Sat 11:00-23:30 (L.O. 23:00); Sun 11:00-17:00 (L.O. 16:30). Open year-round (sources vary slightly on exact open/close times).
closed_days: None (open year-round)
cultural_comfort_note: A modern, clean counter-only ramen shop near a major station that draws a broad crowd including solo diners and tourists; a few Japanese greetings go a long way but no special preparation is needed.
gf_detail: This is a wheat-noodle ramen shop. The bowls use standard wheat ramen noodles and a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare in the black version, both of which contain wheat. No gluten-free noodles, dedicated prep, or allergen accommodation are mentioned by any source. Not suitable for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: The entire concept is a chicken-based broth (tori-paitan), simmered for hours from domestic chicken and aromatic vegetables, with toppings of chicken chashu and chicken tsukune (meatballs). There is no plant-based broth or vegan bowl. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.

bio.background: Opened in 2015 in the Shijo-Karasuma area of central Kyoto, Menya Mitsuru built its reputation on a single idea done well: a rich, collagen-heavy chicken paitan broth. The proprietor came from a mizutaki (chicken hot-pot) specialty background, and that pedigree shows in how the broth is built — fresh domestic chicken simmered in volume over high heat for long hours until it turns creamy and white. Pointedly, the shop uses no pork at all; the depth here is all chicken and aromatics. The shop has since added a sister location in the Gion / Nawate area.
bio.specialty: The signature is the 濃厚鶏白湯そば (rich chicken paitan soba), offered in a salt-based 'white' version that showcases the broth's natural character and a soy-tare 'black' version built to amplify umami. There is also a bonito-enhanced 京鰹節鶏白湯 and an extra-rich 超濃厚 version. Bowls come dressed with chicken chashu, chicken tsukune meatballs, kikurage wood-ear mushroom, and scallion, with fine straight noodles chosen to carry the thick broth.
bio.philosophy: Refinement over heaviness — the broth aims to be intensely flavorful yet clean rather than cloying, expressing 'the paitan's original character' in the white version while the black leans into layered umami.

anecdotes.text: The owner previously worked at a mizutaki (chicken hot-pot) specialty restaurant, bringing that refined chicken-broth craft to a ramen format — and deliberately uses only domestic chicken and aromatic vegetables, with no pork in the broth.
The broth is made by simmering large quantities of fresh domestic chicken over high heat for an extended time to draw out a creamy, white paitan.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The soy-tare 'black' version of the paitan soba is seasoned with shoyu, which is wheat-containing soy sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All bowls are built on a chicken broth with chicken chashu and chicken tsukune toppings; the shared broth and kitchen make any plant-only request unworkable.

caveats: Two sources give slightly different operating hours (Tabelog: Mon-Thu 11:00-22:30, Fri-Sat to 23:30, Sun to 17:00; another lists 11:30 opens and later closes). hours_week follows the Tabelog listing. Name reading taken from Tabelog furigana ミツル (Mitsuru); one blog glossed it differently. No official website found.

lead: 珍遊 河原町六角店
name_ja: 珍遊 河原町六角店
cuisine: Kyoto-style chuka-soba — chicken paitan broth finished with back fat and aged shoyu tare (鶏白湯背脂の京都ラーメン・中華そば)
gf_detail: This is a wheat-noodle chuka-soba shop. The bowl is built on wheat ramen noodles, a soy-sauce tare (Japanese shoyu almost always contains wheat), and a chicken-bone broth — none of it is celiac-safe, and there is no gluten-free menu or noodle option. Not suitable for anyone needing a strict gluten-free meal.
vegan_detail: The signature broth is a long-simmered chicken paitan (chicken bones, whole chicken, chicken feet) finished with rendered back fat, and bowls are topped with chashu pork. There are no advertised vegan or vegetarian ramen options. Not a fit for plant-based diners.
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区新京極通三条下ル東入ル松ヶ枝町457-7 六角会館1F
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned [135.751389, 35.009998] for the chō-level street address (松ヶ枝町457-7); within the Kyoto box. Pinpoints the Shinkyogoku/Sanjo block where the Rokkaku Kaikan building sits.
neighborhood: Kyoto City Hall-mae / Shinkyogoku (Nakagyo-ku, Matsugae-cho)
seats_text: Around 36-38 seats (roughly 8-10 counter seats plus several four-top tables / sofa seating); listings vary slightly. Spacious for a ramen shop, comfortable for solo diners and families alike.
hours_raw: 11:00–24:00 (daily); some listings show 11:00–23:00. Open year-round (年中無休).
closed_days: None — open year-round (年中無休).
cultural_comfort_note: A roomy, modern, walk-in chuka-soba shop in the central Shinkyogoku/Sanjo tourist zone — easy for a visitor to enter, order at the counter, and slurp a bowl with just a few words of Japanese.
caveats: Payment policy is reported inconsistently across listings: one shows cards / e-money / QR accepted, another states cash-only — carry cash to be safe and confirm at the register. Reported hours also vary between 11:00–23:00 and 11:00–24:00 depending on the source; verify if arriving late.

bio.background: Chinyu traces back to 1950 (Showa 25), when it began as a single ramen yatai (street stall) wheeled out in a still-recovering postwar Kyoto. The founder eventually set down roots in an eight-tsubo storefront along what would become Ichijoji's famous 'ramen street,' and the house is now counted among the wellsprings of the Kyoto chicken-paitan, back-fat style. The Kawaramachi Rokkaku branch carries that lineage into the center of town, on the first floor of the Rokkaku Kaikan just off Shinkyogoku.
bio.specialty: Chuka-soba (中華そば): a chicken-bone white broth simmered long with whole chicken and chicken feet for a collagen-rich body, mellowed with an aged soy-sauce tare and finished tableside with a generous scatter of fresh back fat. It looks rich but drinks surprisingly clean, and you can dial in noodle firmness, the amount of green onion, and how much back fat you want.
bio.philosophy: The shop's stated creed is plain and stubborn: 奇をてらわない、手を抜かない — no gimmicks, no shortcuts — pouring everything into a single honest bowl while still pushing to refine the craft.

anecdotes.text: The brand's own telling: born in 1950 from a postwar Kyoto street stall, it grew into a tiny eight-tsubo Ichijoji shop and became one of the source points of Kyoto's chicken-broth, back-fat ramen tradition.
Listings highlight the trademark move of the style — fresh back fat scattered over the bowl to order — and a broth that reads heavy by sight but lands light and mellow on the palate.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles are boiled in shared water and the broth/tare contain wheat-based shoyu; a shared ramen kitchen offers no gluten-free path or separation.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The tare is described as an aged/traditional soy sauce ('老舗の醤油でまろやかな風味に'), and Japanese brewed shoyu is almost always made with wheat — treat the seasoning as wheat-containing.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The base broth is chicken paitan (chicken bones, whole chicken, chicken feet) finished with animal back fat, so every bowl is animal-based with no plant-based stock available.

cuisine: 熟成鶏そば (aged chicken / tori-paitan ramen), 無化調
gf_detail: This is a wheat-noodle ramen shop. The bowls are built on house-made wheat noodles (medium-thick curly and straight flat-cut varieties) served in chicken broth, with the salt and "thick soy" (shoyu/tare) versions almost certainly using wheat-based soy sauce. There is no indication of any gluten-free noodle, gluten-free tare, or allergen accommodation. Not suitable for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: The entire menu is built around chicken — clear chicken broth, concentrated aged chicken paitan, chicken char siu, seared chicken (tataki), and roast-pork butter rice. There are no plant-based broths or vegan bowls. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
address_ja: 京都府京都市右京区西院四条畑町1-22
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned coordinates within the Kyoto box for the full street address.
neighborhood: 右京区西院（京福 山ノ内駅 / 阪急 西院駅）
seats_text: 12 seats (counter and table)
hours_raw: Mon–Fri 11:15–14:30, 18:05–22:00; Sat/Sun/Holidays 11:00–15:00, 18:00–22:00. Closes early when sold out.
closed_days: Not clearly specified; open year-round per shop info (closes when sold out).
cultural_comfort_note: A small, locals-focused counter ramen shop with no English support expected; ordering is straightforward but basic Japanese or pointing will help most.
caveats: Cash only; no reservations; can sell out and close early; lines are common, especially on weekends. Wheat noodles and chicken broth throughout — not for celiac, gluten-free, vegan, or vegetarian diners.

bio.background: A refined neighborhood ramen-ya in the Saiin area of Kyoto, set between the Keifuku Yamanouchi and Hankyu Saiin stations. The room has a quiet, almost traditional-Japanese-restaurant feel rather than the look of a typical ramen counter, and it draws steady weekday crowds with lines on weekends despite not sitting on a prime corner.
bio.specialty: The signature is its chicken soba, offered in two registers: a clear, no-MSG (無化調) tori-soba, and the namesake jukusei (aged) chicken soba — a deeply concentrated, high-viscosity chicken paitan that the kitchen finishes without sweetness, ending on a clean, almost bitter note. The chicken char siu is a standout, praised as approaching proper chicken cookery in its own right, and the kitchen's confidence with chicken extends to seared tataki preparations.
bio.philosophy: Cooking without chemical seasonings (無化調), leaning on the quality and combination of ingredients, and keeping the kitchen open later than most local rivals (past 9 PM).

anecdotes.text: House-made noodles — a medium-thick, slightly chewy curly noodle and a straight flat-cut version with firm, mochi-like bite — are matched to the broths so they grip the concentrated soup.
A small side of butter rice with chicken and roast pork (around 300 yen) is singled out as punching well above a typical side dish.
The chicken char siu is described as exceptional, 'reaching the realm of chicken cuisine itself.'

gf_cross_contamination.text: Ramen here is wheat-noodle based and broths/tare are chicken and (for the soy version) wheat-soy-sauce based; no gluten-free handling is indicated anywhere.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The shoyu ('thick soy' / 鶏そば soy-style) bowls use soy sauce tare, which in a standard Japanese ramen-ya is wheat-based; no wheat-free tare is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broths are chicken-based and toppings include chicken char siu and roast pork; there are no plant-based options, so no meaningful vegan handling exists.

cuisine: ラーメン (chicken paitan / tori-paitan ramen)
gf_detail: This is a chicken-paitan ramen shop built on wheat. Noodles are made in-house daily from a blend of Kyoto-grown "Kyo-komugi" wheat and Hokkaido "Kitahonami"/"Haruyutaka" wheat flours, and the tare/seasoning is shoyu-based (soy sauce, which in Japan is typically brewed with wheat). The broth itself is chicken plus pork bones with aromatic vegetables. There is no indication of any gluten-free noodle, gluten-free tare, or celiac accommodation. Not safe for celiac; a gluten-avoider has essentially nothing safe on the core menu. Confirm directly with the shop if you must, but treat as not gluten-free.
vegan_detail: All bowls are built on an animal broth — whole chicken (from Omi) plus pork bones and aromatic vegetables — and the tare uses kombu, shiitake, and dried scallop (dried scallop is animal-derived).
