Toppings include pork shoulder rare chashu. There is no vegan or vegetarian ramen on the menu. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
address_ja: 京都府京都市右京区西院矢掛町28-2
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates [135.730316, 35.000267] for 京都府京都市右京区西院矢掛町28-2, within the Kyoto bounding box.
neighborhood: 右京区西院（阪急・京福 西院駅 / 西大路三条）
seats_text: 11 counter seats (counter-only); child seats and child cutlery are kept on hand, so the shop genuinely welcomes families with small children.
hours_raw: Mon/Wed/Sat/Sun: 11:00-14:00, 18:00-21:00. Tue/Thu: 11:30-14:00, 18:00-21:00. Fri: closed. (Lunch and dinner service with a mid-afternoon break.)
closed_days: Fridays
cultural_comfort_note: A small, family-run neighborhood counter ramen shop that is unusually welcoming to families and newcomers, but expect Japanese-only ordering, so a few polite words and pointing at the menu will carry you through.
caveats: Closed Fridays (note this differs from many shops). Counter-only with just 11 seats and no reservations, so expect a wait at peak lunch/dinner. Soy-based tare and in-house wheat noodles make this off-limits for celiac/gluten-free and for vegans/vegetarians.

bio.background: Owner-chef Yoshiaki Nishimura was originally a programmer. The birth of his second child became the turning point: he wanted to create a ramen shop that parents could comfortably walk into with kids in tow. He trained at the long-established Kyoto ramen house Chinyu before going independent and opening his own shop in 2012.
bio.specialty: The signature bowl is a salt-based chicken-paitan (tori-paitan) ramen. The broth is built from whole chicken from Omi plus pork bones and aromatic vegetables, simmered about 30 minutes while the scum is carefully skimmed; the tare is drawn from kombu, shiitake, and dried scallop steeped in water from the day before and slowly cooked down. The result is light on the palate yet creamy and mellow. The rare chashu is pork shoulder seasoned with salt alone to let the meat's own flavor come through, and the house menma is rehydrated over four days then seasoned with soy and mirin, never made in advance.
bio.philosophy: So the whole family can eat here with peace of mind. To that end the shop uses no preservatives and no chemical seasonings (MSG), and keeps child seats and child cutlery at the counter, with kids openly welcomed.

anecdotes.text: The noodles are made fresh every day on the shop's own noodle machine, using a blend of Kyoto-grown Kyo-komugi wheat with Hokkaido Kitahonami and Haruyutaka flours; each portion is hand-massaged and aged into smooth, springy medium-thick wavy noodles.
Nishimura left a programming career to open the shop in 2012 after training at Kyoto's old-guard ramen house Chinyu, specifically wanting a place easy to enter with children.

gf_cross_contamination.text: In-house wheat noodles are made daily on the shop's own noodle machine, so the kitchen is a wheat-heavy environment.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The seasoning/tare and items like the house menma are soy-sauce (shoyu) based; Japanese soy sauce is typically brewed with wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broth is animal-based (whole chicken plus pork bones and vegetables) and the tare includes dried scallop; toppings include pork chashu. No vegan/vegetarian option exists.

positives.text: No preservatives and no chemical seasonings (MSG) are used, reflecting an explicit safe-for-the-whole-family ethos.
Child seats and child cutlery are provided and children are actively welcomed.

cuisine: Ramen / tsukemen (chicken paitan and shellfish-dashi)
gf_detail: No gluten-free offering. The signature bowls are wheat ramen noodles (sourced from Kyoto's Teikaku / 麺屋棣鄂) served in chicken-paitan or shellfish broth, and the tare almost certainly uses wheat-based soy sauce. There is no celiac-safe option and no published allergen handling; not suitable for strict gluten avoidance.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly shop. Both signature broths are animal-based — a creamy chicken paitan and a clear shellfish (clam/scallop/oyster) dashi — and toppings include meat. No vegan or vegetarian ramen is offered.
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区壬生森町56-10
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned coordinates for 京都市中京区壬生森町56-10; within the Kyoto bounding box.
neighborhood: Saiin / Nishinokyo area, Nakagyo-ku — about 300m (3 min walk) from Saiin Station (Keifuku), also near Hankyu Saiin Station and Nishioji-Sanjo
seats_text: 9 seats at an L-shaped counter facing the open kitchen
hours_raw: Tue–Sun: 11:00–16:00 (L.O. 15:30/15:45) and 17:00–21:00 (L.O. 20:45). Monday: morning ramen service 8:00–10:30 only. (Tabelog also lists a daily 09:00–16:00 / 17:00–21:00 pattern; the morning/asa-ramen slot on Monday and rotating limited bowls mean times can shift.)
closed_days: No regular fixed closing day reported; Monday runs a morning-only "asa ramen" service rather than the standard daytime/evening hours. Hours and limited bowls rotate, so confirm before visiting.
cultural_comfort_note: A small 9-seat counter ramen shop with a ticket-machine order system and cash-only payment, so a few words of Japanese and basic point-and-order etiquette go a long way; staff are unlikely to handle detailed English allergy requests.
caveats: Lines often form before the 11:00 opening. Cash only; orders are placed at a ticket machine. Menu includes rotating limited-time bowls, and the tsukemen (a chicken-paitan dipping-noodle version) is offered on a limited basis rather than always available — call ahead if it's your goal.

bio.background: The shop opened in late August 2023 in the Saiin area of western Kyoto, near Saiin Station. Its chef trained at 鶏soba座銀 (Tori Soba Zagin), a highly regarded Osaka ramen shop, and carries that pedigree into a calm, design-forward counter space with gray finishes and wood furniture.
bio.specialty: A two-style 'twin-broth' lineup: a creamy chicken paitan (鶏白湯そば) whose foamed, emulsified soup is blended just before serving, and a clear shellfish dashi (貝出汁/貝清湯そば) built from Shinji-ko clams, hamaguri, scallops and oysters for a clean oceanic aroma. A limited-run tsukemen (dipping-noodle) version of the chicken paitan rounds out the menu. Noodles are sourced from Kyoto maker 麺屋棣鄂.
bio.philosophy: The name KATAJIKENAI (忝い) means 'grateful / much obliged' — an expression of gratitude that frames the shop's hospitality, paired with a focus on clean, refined broths and quality ingredients.

anecdotes.text: The chef trained at the popular Osaka shop 鶏soba座銀 before opening here in August 2023.
The kai-dashi (shellfish) broth is drawn from a mix of Shinji-ko clams, hamaguri, scallops and oysters for a layered seafood umami and an 'aroma of the sea'.
The chicken paitan soup is foamed/emulsified with an immersion blender just before service for a light-yet-creamy texture.
Lines often form before the 11:00 opening, reflecting its quick rise in popularity.

lead: ラーメンムギュ VOL.1本店
name_ja: ラーメンムギュ VOL.1本店
cuisine: こだわりラーメン (specialty ramen)
neighborhood: 中京区西ノ京（円町）
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation. This is a classic ramen shop: wheat noodles ("chewy, thick noodles") in a chicken/Nagoya-Cochin broth blended with soy sauce (Japanese shoyu, which contains wheat). The signature bowls (Onibara White/Black, rich chicken soba) and the mazesoba all use wheat noodles, and the soy-sauce tare adds wheat. No celiac-safe option exists here.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians. The broth is built on Nagoya Cochin chicken and chicken bones, and the shop is known above all for its char siu (roasted pork belly) — there are no plant-based ramen options.
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区西ノ京円町22-10
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates within the Kyoto box for 京都府京都市中京区西ノ京円町22; point falls at the 円町 chō level matching the shop address.
seats_text: About 15 seats: counter seating around the kitchen plus one large shared table seating roughly 6-8 in the back.
hours_raw: 11:00-22:30 (L.O. 22:00). Closes early when the day's char siu sells out (チャーシュー売り切れ次第終了). Official site lists open year-round (年中無休); some listings note irregular closures, and one review noted a Wednesday closure, so it is best to confirm before a special trip.
closed_days: Open year-round per the official site (年中無休); closures, if any, are irregular and the shop may close early once char siu is sold out.
cultural_comfort_note: A small, casual, popular neighborhood ramen counter where ordering is straightforward; little to no English is expected, so a few Japanese phrases or pointing at the menu helps.
caveats: Hours and closed days vary by source (official site: 11:00-22:30, year-round; other listings: 11:00-22:00, irregular closures; one review: closed Wednesdays). The kitchen closes early if char siu sells out. Cards and e-money are not accepted; cash and PayPay (QR) only. No reservations.

bio.background: Ramen Mugyu opened its Vol.1 main shop in the Enmachi area of Kyoto's Nakagyo Ward in January 2017, just a couple of minutes' walk from JR Enmachi Station. It is a small, glass-fronted counter shop that has grown into a local favorite, since expanding to additional Mugyu branches around the city.
bio.specialty: The signature bowls are the 'Onibara' series — Onibara White, a clear shio (salt) ramen built on a broth of pure Nagoya Cochin chicken blended with light soy sauce, and Onibara Black, a darker soy version — alongside a richer thick chicken soba (濃厚鶏そば). All are crowned with the shop's celebrated char siu: roasted pork belly sliced generously over chewy, full-bodied noodles. There is also a mazesoba with springy thick noodles and Cochin-chicken oil.
bio.philosophy: The shop sums itself up as '自慢のスープ、こだわりの焼き豚' — pride in the soup and devotion to the char siu. The kitchen leans on quality Nagoya Cochin chicken for a clean, deeply savory base, and a small flourish of minced (rather than sliced) onion adds a crisp texture in place of the usual scallion.

anecdotes.text: The char siu is the shop's calling card — one Kyoto ramen reviewer rated the firm, meaty, fat-controlled roasted pork belly as their No.1 among Kyoto ramen shops, with four generous slices on a regular bowl.
The Onibara White shio bowl is praised for a clear, clean taste with pleasant saltiness and no off-notes, the Nagoya Cochin broth lifted by a blend of light soy sauce.
A musician credited as YAGI MASAHIKO (pen name X.L.H.) — a producer, composer and guitarist — created a theme song for the Mugyu brand, a quirky touch for a ramen shop.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is described as Nagoya Cochin chicken blended with light soy sauce (薄口醤油); Japanese soy sauce is normally brewed with wheat, making the tare a wheat source on top of the wheat noodles.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The broth is built on Nagoya Cochin chicken and chicken bones and the shop centers on roasted pork belly char siu, so all bowls contain animal products.

lead: ラーメン荘 地球規模で考えろ ソラ
name_ja: ラーメン荘 地球規模で考えろ ソラ
name_en: Ramen-so Chikyu Kibo de Kangaero Sora
cuisine: 二郎系ラーメン (Jiro-style ramen) — garlic, back-fat, mountain of bean sprouts and cabbage over thick house-made noodles in a sweet-soy tonkotsu broth
neighborhood: 右京区 西院 (Saiin, Ukyo Ward)
gf_detail: This is a Jiro-style ramen shop: the bowl is built on thick wheat noodles in a sweet-soy (wheat shoyu) tonkotsu broth, so it is not celiac-safe. No gluten-free noodles, broth, or accommodations are mentioned anywhere. A celiac diner should not eat here.
vegan_detail: A pork-based Jiro-style shop — tonkotsu (pork-bone) broth, pork back-fat (アブラ), char siu, and pork toppings are central to every bowl. No vegan or vegetarian options are mentioned. Not suitable for vegans.
address_ja: 京都府京都市右京区西院追分町11-3
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned [135.726593, 34.998302] for 京都府京都市右京区西院追分町11-3; within the Kyoto box.
seats_text: Counter only, approximately 10 seats in a U-shape layout, plus a small waiting area; self-service water, towels, chopsticks and spoons.
hours_raw: Tue/Thu/Sat 11:00-14:45, 18:00-23:45; Wed/Fri 18:00-23:45; Sun 11:00-21:45; Mon closed. (Some listings give the broader window as 11:00-15:00 / 18:00-24:00 weekdays, Sun 11:00-22:00.)
closed_days: Monday
cultural_comfort_note: A cash-only, ticket-machine Jiro-style counter shop run for regulars; you order by calling out toppings in Japanese (ニンニク garlic, ヤサイ vegetables, アブラ fat, カラメ stronger flavor), so a little Japanese and knowing the system helps a lot.

bio.background: Sora is the Saiin branch of the Kyoto Jiro-style chain Ramen-so Chikyu Kibo de Kangaero, which grew out of the group's roots in Kyoto's Fushimi area. This Ukyo Ward location opened on April 24, 2017.
bio.specialty: Jiro-style ramen built on thick, chewy house-made noodles (ワシワシとした自家製極太麺) under a towering mound of bean sprouts and cabbage, with char siu, garlic and pork back-fat in a sweet-soy tonkotsu broth. The large (大) bowl uses 300g of noodles at the regular size, scaling up further for big appetites.
bio.philosophy: Classic inspired-by-Jiro abundance: pile it high, let the diner dial in garlic, vegetables and fat to taste.

anecdotes.text: Ordering uses the Jiro-style call system, and this chain has its own signature extra-fat call nicknamed 'chomo' that regulars layer onto the standard ニンニク/ヤサイ/アブラ chant.
The Saiin shop opened April 24, 2017 as a branch of the group with roots in Kyoto's Fushimi area.
Even on a rainy day the counter drew about a 40-minute wait, a sign of its local following.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth/tare is a sweet soy-flavored tonkotsu, which in Jiro-style ramen relies on wheat-containing shoyu; combined with the wheat noodles this bowl is not gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Pork is fundamental — tonkotsu broth, pork back-fat (アブラ), and char siu — so there is no vegan-safe preparation.

caveats: Hours differ slightly between listings: Tabelog shows lunch only on Tue/Thu/Sat (and Sun) with dinner most days, while blog listings give a simpler weekday 11:00-15:00 / 18:00-24:00 and Sun 11:00-22:00. Treated as irregular; confirm before a lunch visit. Phone number is not published. No allergy/GF/vegan information was found on any source.

lead: 麺飯食堂 てら田や
name_ja: 麺飯食堂 てら田や
cuisine: 町中華 chuka-soba / ramen — pork chintan (clear pork broth) blended with fish-forward wafu dashi; known too for soy-garlic karaage
neighborhood: 中京区壬生坊城町 (Shijo-Omiya / Omiya / Nijo)
gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. The core dishes are wheat-noodle chuka-soba and ramen, and the shoyu/soy-based tare and seasonings almost certainly contain wheat. The signature karaage is flour-coated fried chicken. No gluten-free menu, no dedicated fryer, and no celiac accommodation is advertised. A celiac diner cannot eat the ramen here; call ahead (075-432-8878) if you want to ask about anything, but assume cross-contamination throughout a small single-operator kitchen.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly shop. The broth is built on pork clear soup plus fish/seafood dashi, bowls come with chashu, wonton and egg, and the headline side is fried chicken. There is no vegan or vegetarian ramen option. Vegans should choose a different restaurant.
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区壬生坊城町48-3 壬生坊城第2団地1号棟 1F
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned [135.745117, 35.005299] for 京都府京都市中京区壬生坊城町48-3; within Kyoto box.
seats_text: 23 seats — 9 counter seats plus tables (two 4-person tables and one 6-person table)
hours_raw: 11:00–15:00 / 18:00–22:00 (Mon, Tue, Wed, Fri, Sat, Sun). Closed Thursdays plus irregular closures.
closed_days: Thursdays, plus irregular closures (check Instagram before visiting)
cultural_comfort_note: A casual neighborhood noodle-and-rice diner run largely by one operator; little to no English is expected, so a few Japanese phrases or a translation app will smooth ordering.

bio.background: Teradaya started life as a wildly popular karaage popup operating out of a shared kitchen (kashikiri / magari) near Kyoto's Sanjo-kai shopping arcade. After the fried chicken built a following, the owner added ramen to the lineup, and on July 1, 2023 the business graduated to its own storefront in the Mibu-Bojo No. 2 housing complex near Shijo-Omiya, rebranding as a sit-down menhan shokudo (a noodle-and-rice diner) while keeping the Teradaya name.
bio.specialty: The signature bowl is the tokusei chuka-soba: a pork clear broth (chintan) layered with a fish-forward Japanese wafu dashi for a clean, restrained umami, finished with low-hydration, slightly angular noodles prized for a smooth slurp. The 'special' version is a generous spread of extra chashu, three wontons and a soft egg. Alongside it, Teradaya's original soy-garlic karaage — four big, juicy, piping-hot pieces — remains the shop's calling card.
bio.philosophy: A friendly, unpretentious diner doing two things well: a balanced, light-leaning chuka-soba and seriously good fried chicken, with rice bowls and beer to round out a casual meal.

anecdotes.text: The owner has been described, with affection, as looking more like a hip-hop musician than a traditional ramen-ya master.
The karaage is the dish that made the name: four large, soy-garlic-marinated pieces served sizzling and juicy — the kind of plate locals call 'beer-progressing.'

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen/chuka-soba noodles are the core product, boiled in shared water; the soy-based tare and seasonings almost certainly contain wheat. Not suitable for celiac diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Broth/tare is built on soy-based seasoning (chuka-soba shoyu profile), which in Japan is typically brewed with wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is pork clear soup plus fish/seafood dashi; bowls carry chashu, wonton and egg. No vegan options and pervasive animal stock.

caveats: Hours are reliable for split lunch/dinner service but closures are irregular (Thursday is the regular off-day per aggregator listings, but additional ad-hoc closures occur) — check the shop's Instagram before going. Seat layout and payment options from the listing page. Chef's name not published in fetched sources, so left null. Vegan/GF accommodation: none advertised in any source; treat as wheat- and animal-based throughout.

cuisine: つけ麺（濃厚魚介豚骨・自家製極太麺）
neighborhood: 伏見区 中書島（東浜南町）
address_ja: 京都府京都市伏見区東浜南町660-3
geocode_note: GSI banchi-level geocode returns lat 34.927959 / lng 135.757965, which falls ~0.002° (about 200m) south of the conservative Kyoto box minimum (34.93). The coordinate is nonetheless correct: it matches the real address beside Keihan Chushojima Station's north exit along Takeda-dori. Chō-level retry returned the consistent 34.928162 / 135.758484. Coordinates retained as accurate.
seats_text: 11 seats, counter only
hours_raw: 月・火・金・土・日 11:00-14:30（スープ売り切れ次第終了）／水・木 定休
closed_days: Wednesday, Thursday (and earlier if the broth sells out)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny 11-seat counter lunch-only tsukemen shop with regular queues and no reservations; expect a Japanese-speaking, fast-paced local environment where a little Japanese and tsukemen etiquette go a long way.
gf_detail: This is a tsukemen specialist built on house-made extra-thick wheat noodles served with a rich pork-bone-and-fish (gyokai-tonkotsu) dipping broth that almost certainly uses wheat-based shoyu tare. There is no gluten-free noodle or menu option and no published allergen handling. Not suitable for celiac or gluten-avoidant diners.
vegan_detail: The entire menu centers on a pork-bone and fish (魚介豚骨) broth with chashu pork, egg, and other animal toppings. There is no vegan or vegetarian option, and the dipping broth itself is animal-based. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.

bio.background: A small, independent counter-only tsukemen shop tucked just west of Keihan Chushojima Station's north exit along Takeda-dori in Fushimi. It opens only for lunch and routinely draws lines on both weekdays and weekends, sometimes forming before the doors open.
bio.specialty: Tsukemen built on house-made extra-thick, chewy straight noodles with a smooth finish, paired with an intensely rich pork-bone-and-fish (gyokai-tonkotsu) dipping broth that balances sweetness and a touch of sourness. Toppings include thick chashu, soft-boiled egg, bamboo shoots, and green onion.
bio.philosophy: A focused, do-one-thing-well lunch operation: make the noodles in-house, simmer a deeply concentrated broth, and sell only until the soup runs out. The counter is set up to coach first-timers on how to eat tsukemen, with sudachi, garlic, and a thinning broth (wari-soup) on hand so diners can adjust the flavor mid-bowl.

anecdotes.text: The shop has repeatedly earned a spot on the regional Ramen WEST best-100 list and joined the summer 'Ra-no-Michi JAPAN Hiya RUMBLE 2025' event, serving a limited chilled tsukemen (10 bowls a day, Aug 8-18, 2025).
Lines form throughout the day and the shop closes early once the broth sells out, so arriving at opening is recommended.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Single small kitchen built entirely around wheat tsukemen noodles boiled in shared water; heavy gluten environment with no gluten-free handling described.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The rich tsukemen dipping broth is a pork-bone-and-fish base that, like virtually all tsukemen tare, almost certainly contains wheat-based soy sauce; no allergen breakdown is published.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All bowls use an animal (pork-bone and fish) broth with pork chashu and egg toppings; no vegan preparation exists, so cross-contact with animal products is unavoidable.

caveats: Lunch-only and closes when the broth sells out, so hours effectively end before the listed 14:30 on busy days. Cash only, no reservations, 11 counter seats with frequent queues. Geocode falls marginally (~200m) south of the conservative Kyoto box but is the correct address location. No published allergen, gluten-free, or vegan information; dietary assessments are inferred from the tsukemen menu type and should be confirmed in person.

cuisine: 濃厚鶏豚骨ラーメン（とりとんこつ・煮干しとりとんこつ・濃厚醤油とりとんこつ・鶏中華そば・まぜそば）
gf_detail: Not suitable for celiac/gluten-free diners. This is a classic chicken-pork-bone ramen shop: the noodles are wheat (sourced from noodle maker Menya Teigaku / 麺屋棣鄂), and the broths and tare almost certainly use wheat-based soy sauce, especially the 濃厚醤油 (rich soy) bowls. There are no gluten-free options and no indication of allergen handling. Avoid if you have celiac disease.
vegan_detail: No vegan options. Every bowl is built on a chicken-and-pork-bone (とりとんこつ) base, with niboshi (dried sardine) used in the 煮干し variant and pork chashu, pork-fat (背脂) mazesoba, and karaage on the menu. Nothing on the menu is plant-based.
address_ja: 京都府京都市伏見区深草西浦町6-62 キャッスル深草1F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI from 京都市伏見区深草西浦町6-62; coordinates fall within the Kyoto bounding box.
seats_text: About 15 seats: 7 counter seats plus 2 tables seating 4 each.
hours_raw: 11:00-14:00 (lunch only); closed Mondays. Note: one local article listed extended evening hours (11:00-14:30 / 17:30-22:00) and "Monday Ganko Men" special events, but the consistent current listing is lunch-only.
closed_days: Mondays (定休日: 月曜日)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, ticket-machine ramen counter where lines form at lunch; little to no English is expected, so basic Japanese or pointing at the ticket machine helps.

bio.background: 頑固麺 sits in the Fukakusa stretch of Fushimi, one of Kyoto's most competitive ramen districts, a short walk from Fujinomori Station and the Meishin/Route 24 area. The owner is said to have trained at a well-regarded Fushimi-Momoyama ramen shop before striking out on his own, and the place has become a fixture that draws lines even on weekday lunches despite being open only midday.
bio.specialty: The signature is a thick chicken-pork-bone bowl (とりとんこつ): chicken slowly simmered down to a rich, almost paste-thick consistency, with pork umami arriving on the finish, yet kept clean and approachable rather than heavy. It is paired with snappy medium-thin, low-hydration noodles from Kyoto noodle maker Menya Teigaku (麺屋棣鄂), topped with large rare chashu, menma, onion and a hit of mayu (burnt-garlic oil). Variations include 煮干しとりとんこつ (with dried sardine), 濃厚醤油とりとんこつ (rich soy), 鶏中華そば, and a back-fat 背脂 mazesoba.
bio.philosophy: The shop's name plays on 頑固 ('stubborn'); the kitchen leans into a single-minded pursuit of a fully-realized, concentrated bowl. A house condiment nicknamed the 'Ganko sauce' is offered as a flavor intensifier so diners can dial the broth up to one of the densest in Kyoto.

anecdotes.text: The chef is reported to have trained at a popular Fushimi-Momoyama ramen shop before opening his own counter in the Fukakusa ramen battleground.
The 'Ganko sauce' on the table lets guests intensify the broth into what one guide calls one of Kyoto's most concentrated bowls.
The broth is built by slowly simmering chicken for richness and body, with pork umami lingering in the finish, yet staying clean without off-flavors.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles are the base of every bowl, sourced from noodle maker Menya Teigaku (麺屋棣鄂); shared noodle-boiling water means heavy gluten cross-contact throughout the kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The lineup includes 濃厚醤油とりとんこつ (rich soy chicken-pork-bone) and a 背脂醤油 mazesoba, indicating wheat-based soy tare is used in the broths/seasoning.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All bowls are chicken-and-pork-bone based, with niboshi (dried sardine), pork chashu, back-fat and karaage on the menu; there is no plant-based preparation line.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy information is provided by the shop or in available write-ups.

caveats: The Tabelog URL supplied in the task (26015573) actually points to an unrelated yakiniku restaurant in Maizuru; the correct page for 頑固麺 is 26022742, which I used. Hours sources disagree: most current listings and gourmet guides show lunch-only 11:00-14:00 closed Mondays, while one kyotopi article lists 11:00-14:30 / 17:30-22:00 with Monday special events — likely outdated. Phone number not reliably published; no official website found. Prices (~¥900-980 per bowl) confirm under-¥30 USD.

lead: 汁なし担担麺 ラアノウミ
name_ja: 汁なし担々麺＆麻婆豆腐 ラアノウミ
cuisine: 汁なし担担麺専門（広島流・京都市内唯一の汁なし担担麺専門店）／麻婆豆腐
neighborhood: 上京区・同志社大学今出川キャンパス近く（今出川駅）
address_ja: 京都府京都市上京区上立売町1-7 瑞祥ビル B1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 京都府京都市上京区上立売町1-7 returned [135.756775, 35.032082], within the Kyoto bounding box and consistent with the Imadegawa / Doshisha University area.
seats_text: 19席（カウンター9席＋テーブル10席）
hours_raw: 11:00-15:00 / 17:00-21:30（L.O. 21:00）。平日昼のみ営業の場合あり。営業時間・定休日は変更となる場合あり。
closed_days: 不定休（年末年始休あり）
cultural_comfort_note: A small, student-friendly basement counter shop near Doshisha University where a few words of Japanese and a willingness to mix your own noodles 20+ times go a long way; ordering is straightforward and casual.
gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The dish is built on wheat ramen noodles, and the sesame tare uses 再仕込み醤油 (re-brewed soy sauce, standard wheat-containing) plus chicken-bone broth. Even the spice-free "カラサゼロ" (oil-free) option is still wheat noodles with seasoned ground meat and soy-based tare. No gluten-free noodles, dedicated prep, or allergen program is mentioned anywhere.
vegan_detail: Not vegan or vegetarian. The signature sauceless tantanmen is topped with seasoned ground meat and dried shrimp, and the tare is made with chicken-bone broth and soy sauce; mapo tofu likewise uses meat. No plant-based version is offered or noted.

bio.background: The owner hails from Hiroshima and brought a regional specialty rarely seen in Kyoto — Hiroshima-style sauceless tantanmen (汁なし担担麺) — to a basement spot a minute from Doshisha University's Shinmachi campus, opening in October 2020.
bio.specialty: The signature bowl is the sauceless tantanmen: straight firm noodles tossed with a sesame-forward tare (re-brewed soy sauce, Sichuan pepper, chicken-bone broth) and a deeply aromatic house-made chili oil built on star anise, cinnamon, aged citrus peel and red chili, finished with seasoned ground meat, dried shrimp, and Kyoto vegetables. You stir it vigorously (about 20 times) the moment it arrives, and a splash of table vinegar mellows the heat. Spice runs across a wide range from a zero/oil-free 'カラサゼロ' up to fiery top tiers.
bio.philosophy: The owner's stated mission is to spread awareness of Hiroshima-style sauceless tantanmen and, eventually, see it take root as a mainstream Japanese dish.

anecdotes.text: Billed as Kyoto's only dedicated sauceless tantanmen specialist, the basement shop has a hidden-gem feel and draws a steady student crowd thanks to affordable bowls.
The owner is from Hiroshima and opened near Doshisha University specifically to broaden recognition of Hiroshima-style sauceless tantanmen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The tare is described as using 再仕込み醤油 (re-brewed soy sauce), a standard wheat-containing soy sauce, tossed directly with the wheat noodles.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Bowls are topped with seasoned ground meat and dried shrimp, and the tare uses chicken-bone broth, so shared utensils and cooking surfaces carry animal products.

caveats: Owner's name not published. Hours are confirmed irregular ("不定休") and the shop explicitly warns that hours/closed days may change — confirm before visiting. Tabelog lists the seven-day schedule as the same lunch+dinner window (used for hours_week), but weekday service is sometimes lunch-only and the shop closes around year-end/New Year. No allergen or vegetarian program documented; GF/vegan assessments are based on described ingredients, not a stated kitchen policy.

cuisine: 鶏白湯・鶏そば（地鶏100%のラーメン）
neighborhood: 河原町・花遊小路（中京区）
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区中之町565-23 ハレの日 花遊小路 2F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to the chō-level address (中之町565-23); coordinates fall within the Kyoto bounding box.
seats_text: 約19席（カウンター約5席＋テーブル席）。少人数向けの小さな店。
closed_days: 不定休（曜日固定の定休日なし）
cultural_comfort_note: A small counter-and-table ramen shop tucked on the 2nd floor of a narrow shopping lane; ordering is straightforward but basic Japanese (or a translation app) helps, and it is cash/PayPay only.
gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The bowls use the shop's own house-made Japanese wheat noodles, and the soy-based (鶏そば) bowls use wheat soy-sauce tare; no gluten-free noodle or dedicated prep is offered. Even the salt/paitan bowls are served with wheat noodles. Avoid if you require strict gluten avoidance.
vegan_detail: This is a chicken specialist — every signature bowl is built on 100% jidori (local chicken) broth made from chicken gara, whole chicken and water, with chicken/duck chashu toppings. There are no vegan or vegetarian ramen options.
caveats: Name kanji is the rare variant 虵 (often written/read as 蛇の目屋 / "Janomeya"); some listings render it differently. Cash or PayPay only — no credit cards or other electronic money. No reservations; expect to queue at peak times. Hours are listed as regular daily but the shop takes irregular (不定休) closures, so confirm before a special trip.

bio.background: The owner came to ramen from fine dining: he started his career in French cuisine and trained at several restaurants before opening his own shop, and he brings those classical kitchen techniques to every bowl.
bio.origin: The shop moved to its current 2nd-floor spot on the narrow Hanayu-koji lane just off Kawaramachi in December 2020.
bio.specialty: Chicken in every form. The two pillars are the 鶏そば — a clear (清湯) chicken broth with a sharp, bright wheat-soy edge and deep chicken-oil richness — and the 鶏白湯, a creamy, concentrated chicken-paitan bowl. A jidori salt bowl rounds out the lineup, all served over house-made Japanese wheat noodles with low-temperature chicken chashu and charcoal-grilled toppings.
bio.philosophy: Radical simplicity in service of the bird: the broth is built from nothing but chicken bones (gara), whole chicken, and water, letting the flavor of 100% jidori (local heritage chicken) speak for itself. Toppings are charcoal-grilled to add aroma rather than mask the broth.

anecdotes.text: The kitchen leans on the owner's French-cooking discipline — low-temperature-cooked chicken chashu and charcoal-grilled aromatics show a chef applying restaurant technique to a bowl of ramen.
The house philosophy is summed up as using 'only gara, whole chicken, and water' so the chicken itself carries the flavor.
Reviewers single out the 鶏そば for its balance of bright soy seasoning over a clear chicken broth with real chicken-oil depth, with a side of tori-meshi (chicken rice) as the classic pairing.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Bowls are served over the shop's own house-made Japanese wheat noodles, so wheat is intrinsic to every dish; there is no gluten-free option.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature 鶏そば is a soy-seasoned (醤油) chicken broth; standard Japanese shoyu tare contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broths are 100% chicken (gara, whole chicken, water) with chicken/duck chashu toppings; there is no meat-free preparation, so any bowl will contain animal products.

lead: 京都千丸 しゃかりき 本店
name_ja: 京都千丸 しゃかりき 本店
cuisine: 濃厚魚介つけ麺・煮干し（豚骨魚介Wスープ） / Rich seafood-and-pork tsukemen and niboshi ramen
gf_detail: This is a tsukemen and ramen specialist: wheat-flour noodles served with a pork-bone-and-fish (niboshi) double broth and a tare that is virtually always wheat-based soy sauce. There is no indication of any gluten-free noodle, broth, or accommodation, and no dedicated preparation area. Not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
vegan_detail: The shop is built around an animal-heavy broth (tonkotsu pork bone plus dried-fish niboshi). The signature bowls are all meat/fish based and there is no listed vegetarian or vegan option. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区聚楽廻東町3-9
geocode_note: GSI address-search first feature geometry.coordinates = [135.741852, 35.01852]; within Kyoto bounding box.
neighborhood: 中京区・聚楽廻 / 千本丸太町（二条城北西）
seats_text: 12 seats total: 8 counter seats plus one table for 4.
hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Thu, Fri, Sat, Sun: 11:00-15:00 (L.O. 14:30) and dinner service in the evening; closed Wednesdays plus occasional irregular closures. Sources differ slightly on evening hours: one lists 17:30-21:00, another lists 18:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:30). Confirm on the day of visit.
closed_days: Wednesdays, plus occasional irregular closures.
cultural_comfort_note: An English menu is available and the counter-style shop is used to a broad, family-friendly clientele, so non-Japanese-speaking visitors should manage comfortably.
caveats: Two sources give slightly different evening hours (17:30-21:00 vs 18:00-22:00); closures can be irregular, so verify before visiting. No reservations are taken, so expect a possible queue at peak times.

bio.background: Opened in 2004 near Senbon-Marutamachi, just northwest of Nijo Castle, Shakariki built its name as one of the early standard-bearers for tsukemen (dipping noodles) in Kyoto, a city long defined by its own back-fat shoyu ramen tradition. It has run in the same neighborhood spot for over two decades.
bio.specialty: The house style is a rich seafood-and-pork double soup: dried sardine (niboshi) aromatics layered over a deep tonkotsu pork-bone base. The flagship is the 濃厚魚介 (rich seafood) tsukemen, served with thick noodles, and the shop deliberately prepares several broth intensities from light to ultra-rich, each matched to a different noodle thickness.
bio.philosophy: An obsessive, customize-to-the-bowl approach: rather than one fixed soup, the kitchen offers five broth variations and matches noodle gauge to each, described as a dedication to the single bowl that sets aside pure business calculation.

anecdotes.text: Shakariki is credited as a tsukemen pioneer in Kyoto and as a training ground whose lineage includes the Michelin-recognized ramen shop Toetsuhichi (らぁ麺 とうひち) and the related shop Isshoji Boogie, marking it as an influential mentor in the local ramen scene.

The shop draws a notably broad crowd; observers describe not just male regulars but women and even elementary-school children happily slurping the dipping noodles, a sign of how approachable the rich broth is.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a tsukemen and ramen shop, the dipping tare is virtually certain to be standard wheat-based Japanese soy sauce, and the noodles are wheat flour; no gluten-free preparation is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The broth is a pork-bone (tonkotsu) and dried-fish (niboshi) double soup; all bowls are animal-based with no listed vegetarian or vegan option, and shared broth/equipment makes cross-contact unavoidable.

lead: 本家 第一旭 たかばし本店
name_ja: 本家 第一旭 たかばし本店
cuisine: 京都中華そば（豚骨醤油・澄んだ清湯スープ）
neighborhood: 下京区・京都駅東（高橋）
address_ja: 京都府京都市下京区東塩小路向畑町845
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned [135.761459, 34.985992] for 京都府京都市下京区東塩小路向畑町845; within Kyoto box.
seats_text: 31席（カウンター3席、テーブル7卓28席）
closed_days: 木曜日
cultural_comfort_note: A famous, perpetually busy Kyoto-Station ramen institution with ticket-machine ordering, shared seating when crowded, and frequent foreign visitors — a friendly konnichiwa and pointing at the menu will carry you through.
gf_detail: Classic Kyoto chuka-soba: wheat noodles plus a wheat-based raw soy sauce (Fushimi nama-shoyu) tare in a pork-bone broth. No gluten-free noodles or accommodation is mentioned anywhere. Not celiac-safe.
vegan_detail: There is a "肉なし（野菜入）ラーメン" (no-chashu, vegetable) bowl, but it is still served in the pork-bone (chintan) broth with wheat noodles and wheat soy-sauce tare, so it is neither vegan nor vegetarian. No plant-based broth option exists.

bio.background: 本家 第一旭の起こりは1947年（昭和22年）。創業者・田口雄二が、京都ラーメンの源流とされる隣の「新福菜館」（1938年創業）を手伝いながらラーメンづくりを学び、まず大衆食堂『旭食堂』を開いた。ところが供したラーメンが思いのほか評判を呼び、1956年に『第一旭』と改名してラーメン専門店へと業態転換したのが現在の姿の始まり。京都駅烏丸中央口から徒歩約5分、高橋（たかばし）の地に本店を構える。
bio.origin: 京都・下京区／京都駅東（高橋）
bio.specialty: 看板は素朴な一杯の「ラーメン」、そして人気の「特製ラーメン」。豚骨を白濁させる前に引き上げて澄ませた清湯（ちんたん）スープに、京都・伏見の無添加生醤油のタレを合わせ、ニンニクや野菜の臭み消しに頼らない澄んだ醤油味に仕立てる。麺は近藤製麺の特注ブレンドの中太ストレート。チャーシューは脂が控えめで旨みの濃い国産雌豚（出産経験のある豚）を使い、赤身（赤）か脂身（白）かを無料で指定できる。たっぷりの京都・九条ねぎを惜しみなく盛るのも持ち味。
bio.philosophy: 夜明けの最初の光（第一旭）のように京都で輝く一杯を——白濁させない澄んだスープと無添加・素材本位の味を、守り続け磨き続ける。

anecdotes.text: 京都ラーメンの真の源流とされる老舗『新福菜館』（1938年創業）が本店のすぐ隣に並び、二軒は定休日をあえてずらして（第一旭は木曜、新福菜館は水曜）80年近く隣同士で共存してきた。両店をはしごする『たかばしはしご』は地元の楽しみ方として親しまれている。
年間およそ24万人が訪れる京都ラーメンの代表格で、早朝から行列ができることでも知られる。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles are boiled in shared water and served in a pork-bone broth seasoned with wheat-based soy-sauce tare — no separation from gluten is possible.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The tare uses additive-free raw soy sauce (nama-shoyu) from Fushimi, Kyoto, which is brewed from wheat and soybeans — it contains gluten.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The base broth is pork-bone (chintan) for every bowl, including the no-chashu 'vegetable' ramen, so no bowl is free of animal product.

positives.text: Many free customizations (noodle firmness, broth richness, soy intensity, chashu lean/fatty, extra negi and bean sprouts) let diners adjust a bowl, though none remove the core wheat/pork allergens.

caveats: Hours are commonly listed as 6:00 to 1:00 the next day (encoded here as 06:00-25:00); Thursday closed. Seat count (31) and payment options (cash plus cards/e-money/QR) are confirmed across the official-adjacent guide and listings. The "no-meat vegetable ramen" is NOT vegan/vegetarian — same pork broth and wheat components.

cuisine: 中華そば (chuka-soba) / つけ麺 — refined dashi-shoyu ramen with scallop and sansho
neighborhood: Nishi-shichijo, Shimogyo-ku (near JR Tamba-guchi / Umekoji, Kyoto-West)
address_ja: 京都府京都市下京区西七条掛越町12-5
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned [135.731216, 34.992764] for 京都府京都市下京区西七条掛越町12-5; within the Kyoto box.
seats_text: 9 counter seats only. One bowl per seat is requested (children excepted).
hours_raw: Mon 11:00-14:00; Tue-Fri 11:00-15:00 & 18:00-20:00; Sat 11:00-15:00 & 17:30-20:00; Sun 10:30-14:00. Times listed are open hours; last orders earlier. Hours and closures are irregular — check the shop's SNS before visiting. (Some listings show different/condensed hours, e.g. closed Mondays and Sunday evenings.)
closed_days: Irregular holidays; confirm via the shop's social media before going.
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny 9-seat counter ramen-ya run by a serious young chef; little to no English is expected, so a few words of Japanese and following counter etiquette (one bowl per seat) will go a long way.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan or vegetarian place. The signature bowls are built on chicken, pork and seafood dashi (and feature scallop, beef or duck), so the broths and toppings are animal-based. No vegan ramen is advertised.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free or celiac-safe. The whole concept is wheat: custom thin/flat wheat noodles milled from a specific wheat variety, served in a dashi-shoyu broth (soy-sauce tare typically contains wheat). No gluten-free noodle or menu option is offered. Anyone strictly avoiding gluten should not eat here.
caveats: Hours and closing days differ across listings and are described as irregular; the seat/one-bowl rule and cash-only policy are firm. Confirm the day's hours via the shop's social media before visiting.

bio.background: The owner-chef is a ramen obsessive who started a ramen-research club while at Doshisha University, then trained at respected shops (including Tsurumusha and Menya Yutaka). Before opening, he went to work for a major flour milling company so he could understand wheat and noodle-making from the grain up — and that wheat-first knowledge is built into everything the shop serves.
bio.specialty: The signature bowl is the scallop-and-sansho chuka-soba: a clear, refined dashi-shoyu built from chicken, pork and seafood, finished with the bright, citrus-tingly bite of sansho (Japanese pepper). A beef-shoyu-and-sansho version and a sansho tsukemen round out the core lineup, with seasonal limited bowls (a straw-grilled duck-and-sansho ramen, chilled corn ramen, and a sesame cold tsukemen) appearing through the year.
bio.philosophy: Pepper as a Kyoto signature, and wheat treated with a miller's precision: the house noodles are thin and flat, milled from a specific wheat variety, with a smooth, almost inaniwa-udon-like texture meant to match the delicate broth rather than overpower it.

anecdotes.text: The shop opened in February 2022 and earned a Michelin Bib Gourmand almost immediately, with the chef in his twenties — described as the youngest to be recognized in the ramen category.
The straw-grilled duck-and-sansho limited bowl is so sought-after that it has sold out within about five minutes of opening; reviewers single out the powerful aroma of the straw-fired duck.
Before opening, the chef worked for a flour milling company to master milling so he could develop the shop's custom thin, flat noodles from a chosen wheat variety.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The entire kitchen is built around wheat: custom wheat noodles are the centerpiece and are made and handled throughout the space, so flour/gluten cross-contact is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The bowls are dashi-shoyu (soy-sauce based). Standard ramen soy-sauce tare typically contains wheat, and no wheat-free tare is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths are made from chicken, pork and seafood dashi and bowls feature scallop, beef or duck, so there is no animal-free preparation here.

lead: ギオンダックヌードル (Gion Duck Noodles)
name_ja: ギオンダックヌードル
cuisine: 鴨ラーメン (duck ramen) / tsukemen
neighborhood: 祇園 (Gion), 東山区
gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The house-made noodles are made with German rye and whole-grain (wheat) flour — rye and wheat both contain gluten. The shoyu tare in this style of ramen is almost certainly wheat-based, and no gluten-free menu or accommodation is advertised. People with celiac disease or wheat allergy should avoid.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The entire concept is built on a free-range duck (Kishu/紀州鴨) broth, with duck-meat toppings (loin or thigh) and marinated egg. No plant-based broth or vegan bowl is offered.
address_ja: 京都府京都市東山区祇園町北側329 1F D号
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 京都市東山区祇園町北側329 returned [135.775787, 35.002338], within the Kyoto box and consistent with the Gion location near Yasaka Shrine / Gion-Shijo Station.
seats_text: 9 counter seats
hours_raw: 11:00 - 21:30 (L.O. 21:00), 定休日なし (no closing days)
closed_days: None (open daily)
cultural_comfort_note: English-speaking staff are available and the shop regularly serves international visitors, so foreign diners can order comfortably.
caveats: Tiny 9-seat counter in a hard-to-find Gion back alley; the shop sign is just duck and ramen emoji rather than text. No reservations, so expect a line at peak times. Cards accepted but electronic money and QR payments are not.

bio.background: A duck-ramen specialist tucked into a narrow Gion back alley a few minutes from Gion-Shijo Station and Yasaka Shrine. It is run by a French-trained chef who brings a fine-dining sensibility to ramen, and is a sister shop to the acclaimed Osaka (Hirakata) noodle bar Menroku (麺麓), which opened in December 2018.
bio.specialty: Duck ramen built on a clear, intensely aromatic broth made from branded free-range Kishu duck (紀州鴨) and kombu, served with house-made noodles milled with German rye and whole-grain flour. Diners choose their duck topping — loin (ロース) or thigh (もも) — and there are rich-white-broth tsukemen variations finished with fruit sauces (cherry or mandarin) plus a Western-style duck pilaf.
bio.philosophy: A 'restaurant-like' (レストランのような) approach to ramen: refined, clean umami over heaviness, an unusually pure duck aroma, and a minimalist identity expressed through an emoji-only sign rather than traditional ramen-shop branding.
bio.origin: Kyoto (Gion), as a sister location to Osaka's Menroku

anecdotes.text: The shop's sign is composed entirely of emoji (a duck and a bowl of ramen) rather than any written name, fitting its understated, fine-dining-inspired identity.
Reviewers single out the broth for a depth of duck aroma rarely found elsewhere — 'the first dish where I felt the fragrance of duck to this degree.'
Organic grape sansho (ブドウ山椒) is offered as a flavor-changer mid-bowl.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No ingredient breakdown of the tare/soy sauce is published; standard ramen shoyu tare is wheat-based, so wheat should be assumed present.

cuisine: 塩ラーメン (shio ramen) — natural-salt and duck-salt ramen, plus shio tsukemen
neighborhood: Shijo-Karasuma / Shimogyo-ku
gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. This is a classic ramen shop built on hand-kneaded wheat noodles, served in clear salt (shio) broths blending animal and seafood/vegetable stocks. Even the salt-forward bowls rely on wheat noodles, and no gluten-free menu, dedicated equipment, or allergen accommodation is documented. A diner with celiac disease should not eat here; gluten-avoiders by preference would still be consuming wheat noodles in every bowl.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The signature broths are built on animal stock (the menu includes charcoal-grilled local-chicken ramen and duck-salt ramen) combined with seafood and vegetable stocks, and bowls are topped with chashu pork. No vegan or vegetarian ramen is offered or documented.
address_ja: 京都府京都市下京区三軒町551
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 京都府京都市下京区三軒町551番地; within Kyoto box.
seats_text: 17 counter seats only
hours_raw: Tabelog lists 11:30-13:50 and 18:00-21:00 daily; some guides list the bands slightly wider (lunch to 14:00, dinner to 22:00). Closures are irregular (不定休) and announced on the shop's X/Twitter.
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular holidays; closures announced on the shop's X/Twitter account)
cultural_comfort_note: A serious, owner-run counter shop where mindful eating is expected (no reading or phones at the counter, and young children are not admitted); little to no English, so basic Japanese or a guide helps.

bio.background: Born in 1954, Uemura worked for a fish company before moving to Kansai and running several restaurants. He opened Shinjuku Menya Fuhka in Kyoto in 2001 at age 47, at a time when salt ramen was virtually unknown in a city dominated by rich, fatty pork-bone broths — many people warned him the concept would fail. He briefly closed early on to refine his recipe after realizing Kyoto's hot, humid climate shifted customers' taste preferences away from the cold-northern style he had trained in.
bio.specialty: The 天然塩らーめん (natural-salt ramen) is the signature bowl — a clear, golden broth built on a rare cooking technique. Uemura whisks egg whites with salt and repeatedly skims the surface foam, drawing off harshness until a mild, refined clarity remains, then blends animal and vegetable stocks behind it. He builds the salt itself from Ishigaki Island salt blended with two other salts, and the noodles are kneaded by hand each day. The bowl is famously kept very affordable despite costly ingredients. The duck-salt ramen, charcoal-grilled local-chicken ramen, and salt tsukemen round out the menu, and the long-cooked, tender chashu is a frequent favorite.
bio.philosophy: Uemura rejects a profit-first approach, saying he doesn't think of ramen as a money-making tool and prioritizing quality over margin. He wants guests to taste attentively — phones and reading are discouraged at the counter, and young children are not admitted because he wants diners with a developed enough palate to appreciate the subtlety of the broth.

anecdotes.text: When Uemura opened in 2001, Kyoto ramen meant heavy, fatty pork-bone broth and salt ramen barely existed locally; people told him it would fail, and he briefly closed to re-tune his recipe after learning that Kyoto's humid climate changed how the broth landed on customers' palates compared with the cold northern region where he trained.
His broth uses an uncommon clarifying technique — egg whites whisked with salt, heated, and repeatedly skimmed until the harsh saltiness is drawn off — to leave a mild, transparent golden soup.
He recalculates his hand-made noodle recipe to the flour and the season's humidity and stays in regular contact with the mill, noting the differences between Kansai and Kanto flour.
Now in his late 60s, he has been trying to train a successor since around 2022, though earlier apprentices gave up during the demanding broth-preparation stage.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Every bowl is built on hand-kneaded wheat noodles; no gluten-free option or allergen handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Chashu and some bowls carry a soy-sauce element typical of ramen, which is normally wheat-based; no wheat-free tare is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths are animal- and seafood-based (chicken/duck plus seafood and vegetable stocks) and bowls are topped with chashu pork; no separate vegan preparation exists.

caveats: Hours vary slightly between sources (Tabelog: 11:30-13:50 / 18:00-21:00; some guides list 11:30-14:00 / 18:00-22:00) and closures are irregular and announced on the shop's X/Twitter, so confirm before visiting. Cash only. No reservations. Young children (roughly under elementary-school age) are not admitted, and quiet, phone-free dining is expected. No official website found; the listed price (~800 yen for natural-salt ramen) is from a guide article and may have changed.

cuisine: 油そば・まぜそば (abura soba / mazesoba)
neighborhood: 東山区 清水 (Kiyomizu, Higashiyama), at the foot of Kiyomizu-dera near Kiyomizu-Gojo Station
address_ja: 京都府京都市東山区清水4-138
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 京都府京都市東山区清水4-138 returned [135.777405, 34.997185], within the Kyoto box.
seats_text: About 20 seats total — counter and table seating in a small shop (source listings vary: roughly 6 counter plus table seats).
hours_raw: 10:00–20:30 (L.O. 20:00); closed Sundays. Note: some listings show 11:00–21:00 or daily operation with irregular closures; current Instagram indicates 10:00–20:30, Sunday closed.
closed_days: Sundays (per current Instagram; other listings vary — call ahead)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, casual abura-soba counter shop in a heavy tourist zone near Kiyomizu-dera; a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu will carry you through, though staff are used to international visitors.
caveats: Hours and closed day differ across sources (Sunday vs Wednesday closure; 10:00–20:30 vs 11:00–21:00). Cash only, no reservations. No published allergen or gluten-free/vegan information — confirm in person if you have dietary restrictions.
gf_detail: Abura soba is built on chewy wheat flat noodles dressed with a soy-based (wheat-containing) tare and sesame oil, so the core dish is not gluten-free. No gluten-free menu, dedicated equipment, or allergen accommodation is advertised. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: This is a meat-and-fish-forward abura soba shop — the soy tare and broth flavorings typically involve animal products, and side dishes include chicken tempura, cutlet skewers and dumplings. No vegan options are advertised. Toppings like chopped onion and green onion exist but the base dish is not vegan.

bio.background: ねこまた sits right in the thick of the Kiyomizu tourist district, on the Higashioji/Matsubara-dori approach between the Kiyomizu-yon and Kiyomizu-go intersections at the foot of Kiyomizu-dera. It is described as Kansai's first dedicated abura-soba specialty shop, opening before the soupless-noodle craze caught on in the region — which earns it a quiet reputation for the completeness and polish of its bowls.
bio.specialty: Soupless abura soba: thick, chewy flat noodles tossed at the bottom of the bowl with sesame oil and a soy-based tare so the oil, sauce and noodles cling together into a rich, mellow tangle. Three core styles are offered — the soy-based original, a spicy miso (辛味噌), and a shrimp-scented natural-salt version. Chopped raw onion and green onion are added for crunch, and diners finish by stirring in chili oil and vinegar to taste.
bio.philosophy: A single-minded focus on abura soba — neither ramen nor yakisoba, but its own noodle culture — done simply and well in a small shop.

anecdotes.text: Billed as Kansai's first abura-soba specialty shop, started before mazesoba/abura-soba became popular, which is part of why locals praise how dialed-in the bowls are.
Diners are encouraged to load on the complimentary chopped onion and then adjust with chili oil and vinegar, customizing each bowl as they eat.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The abura soba is dressed with a soy-based tare; standard Japanese soy sauce/tare contains wheat, and the noodles themselves are wheat — no wheat-free tare is advertised.

cuisine: 町中華 / 京風中華 (Kyoto-style Chinese) — signature カラシソバ (karashi-soba / 撈麺), Hommai (鳳舞) lineage chuka-soba
neighborhood: 中京区 河原町二条 (Kawaramachi-Nijo), near 京都市役所前駅 Kyoto City Hall Station
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区河原町二条上ル清水町359 ＡＢビル１Ｆ
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 中京区清水町359 returned [135.769608, 35.013969], within the Kyoto box.
closed_days: 月曜日・火曜日 (Mondays and Tuesdays, including holidays)
cultural_comfort_note: A beloved, busy local Kyoto-Chinese counter-and-table spot where the owner is known as warm and easygoing; a few words of Japanese and patience at peak lunch/dinner go a long way, and cash is essential.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The signature karashi-soba (撈麺) is boiled wheat Chinese noodles tossed in mustard-soy sauce, topped with a thickened chicken-bone and kombu broth "an" — wheat noodles plus wheat-based soy sauce. Spring rolls, shumai and fried rice likewise rely on wheat wrappers/seasoning and shared woks. No indication of gluten-free options or accommodation; treat as unsafe for celiac.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly kitchen. Kyoto-Chinese cooking here is built on chicken-bone and kombu dashi broths, pork, and shrimp/chicken (the karashi-soba is officially listed as エビカシワソバ — shrimp and chicken). No vegan dishes are indicated.
caveats: Cash only — no cards or e-money. Closed Mondays and Tuesdays (including public holidays). Very popular; phone reservations are taken only for the 11:30 or 17:00 opening seatings. The owner-romaji "Ikuo/Isao Fukuda" appears in source reporting (kanji 功雄); romanization may vary.

bio.origin: Kyoto, in the lineage of the landmark Kyoto-Chinese restaurant 鳳舞 (Hommai)
bio.background: Hosen carries the soul of 鳳舞, the restaurant widely regarded as the source of Kyoto-style Chinese cooking, founded in 1967 by Cantonese chef Takahashi (高華吉), who softened bold Cantonese cooking into the subtle, dashi-forward 京風中華 that Kyoto came to love. When 鳳舞 closed in 2009 after a much-mourned run, its kitchen chiefs carried the recipes forward — Hosen was opened that same year by 鳳舞 alumni 福田功雄 and 三野真人 (Masato Mino), reportedly at the urging of a devoted regular, so the flavor would not be lost.
bio.specialty: カラシソバ (karashi-soba), officially listed as 撈麺 / エビカシワソバ: boiled wheat Chinese noodles tossed in a mustard-soy sauce and crowned with a glossy 'an' thickened sauce made from chicken-bone and kombu broth, with shrimp and chicken. The mustard kick is the signature — and the kitchen lets you dial it to taste.
bio.philosophy: 京風中華 — keeping the refined, lighter, dashi-driven Kyoto interpretation of Cantonese cooking alive exactly as it was passed down from 鳳舞, rather than chasing mainland intensity.

anecdotes.text: Hosen was born the same year 鳳舞 closed (2009): a longtime regular reportedly approached the former 鳳舞 kitchen chief and urged him to reopen the kitchen so the beloved Kyoto-Chinese flavor would survive.
The mustard in the karashi-soba is fully adjustable — regulars order it mild, extra-hot, or even as a 'karashi-soba with no karashi (mustard) at all.'
The original karashi-soba style traces to Takahashi (高華吉), a Cantonese chef who opened 鳳舞 in 1967 and reworked his cooking to suit Kyoto's subtle, dashi-forward palate.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The signature dish is wheat Chinese noodles tossed in mustard-soy sauce under a chicken/kombu broth an; spring rolls, shumai and fried rice are cooked in shared woks — gluten is pervasive throughout the menu.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The karashi-soba is dressed in カラシ醤油 (mustard soy sauce); standard Japanese/Chinese soy sauce is wheat-based.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths are made from chicken bone and kombu, and the signature noodle is shrimp-and-chicken (エビカシワソバ); no plant-only preparations indicated.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The kitchen will adjust the amount of mustard to order, including making the dish with no mustard at all — showing willingness to customize, though this is a seasoning preference and not an allergen-handling policy.

lead: らーめん千の風 四条河原町店
name_ja: らーめん千の風 四条河原町店
cuisine: 塩・醤油ラーメン / つけ麺 (Kyoto-style shio & shoyu ramen, tsukemen)
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The ramen and tsukemen use wheat noodles, the broth is pork-bone (tonkotsu) based, and a shoyu (soy sauce, normally wheat-containing) tare is used for the 京の醤油 bowl. No gluten-free noodles, tare, or preparation is offered, and there is no information indicating any allergen accommodation. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. The broth is a pork-bone (tonkotsu) base and the bowls are topped with grilled chashu pork; signature items are meat-based. No vegetable/vegan ramen option was found.
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区新京極通四条上ル中之町580
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned coordinates [135.751389, 35.009998] for the full street address; within the Kyoto box.
neighborhood: Shinkyogoku / Shijo-Kawaramachi, Nakagyo-ku (near Hankyu Kyoto-Kawaramachi Station)
seats_text: 15 seats total: 8 counter seats, one 3-person table, one 4-person table (private room available, up to 6).
hours_raw: Mon, Thu-Sun 12:00-21:00; closed Tue & Wed.
closed_days: Tuesday and Wednesday
cultural_comfort_note: A compact, tourist-friendly counter shop in the busy Shinkyogoku arcade that regularly draws international visitors, so a friendly konnichiwa and pointing at the menu will get you through fine.

bio.background: A small, long-running ramen counter tucked into the Shinkyogoku covered arcade just off Shijo-Kawaramachi, open for roughly fifteen years and a familiar fixture in one of central Kyoto's busiest shopping streets.
bio.specialty: The signature bowl is the 京の塩 (Kyo-no-shio, Kyoto salt ramen): a pork-bone broth that is creamy in body yet surprisingly light and clean to the last sip, finished with fragrant, lightly grilled chashu pork. A 京の醤油 (Kyoto soy sauce) version and tsukemen dipping noodles round out the menu, with gyoza on the side.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen aims for a refined, easy-drinking bowl: richness without heaviness, so the soup stays pleasant from first spoonful to last.

anecdotes.text: The little Japanese-fronted shop has become a hit with overseas visitors and often has a queue of around half an hour at lunch.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The 京の醤油 (Kyoto soy sauce) ramen is built on a soy-sauce tare, which in standard Japanese ramen contains wheat.

caveats: No official website or allergen information was located; dietary and safety conclusions are inferred from the standard ramen composition (wheat noodles, pork-bone broth, wheat-based shoyu tare, pork chashu) described in listings. No staff allergy-handling, dedicated-fryer, or cross-contamination details were found, so safety arrays are left empty/low-confidence. Cash only.

name_ja: ギオンダックヌードル
cuisine: 鴨ラーメン・鴨つけ麺（紀州鴨の清湯）
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature noodles are house-made from German whole-grain rye flour, and rye contains gluten; the duck broth and tare have not been confirmed as wheat-free. This is a duck-broth ramen specialist and is not suitable for celiacs. Ask directly if you have a severe wheat/gluten allergy — no dedicated GF preparation or allergen protocol has been published.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. Every signature bowl is built on a duck (and at times bonito/fish) broth with duck loin and thigh as the centerpiece. There is no published vegan or vegetarian option; the entire menu is centered on Kishu duck.
address_ja: 京都府京都市東山区祇園北側329 1F D号
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 京都市東山区祇園北側329 returned [135.775787, 35.002338], within the Kyoto box. Matches the alley location near Yasaka Shrine, ~5 min from Keihan Gion-Shijo Station.
neighborhood: 東山区・祇園（八坂神社近く、祇園四条駅）
seats_text: 9 counter seats
hours_raw: 11:00 - 21:30 (L.O. 21:00), open 7 days a week, no regular closed days
closed_days: No regular closed days
cultural_comfort_note: English-speaking staff are on hand and the shop is used to international visitors, so first-timers from abroad can order comfortably.
caveats: Tiny 9-seat counter in a hard-to-find alley, so expect a wait at peak times; no reservations. Prices run higher than a typical ramen shop (roughly 2,000-3,000 yen range). Not suitable for gluten-free or vegan diners.

bio.background: Gion Duck Noodles opened in December 2018 down a narrow alley just a couple of minutes from Yasaka Shrine in Gion. The chef trained in French cooking and brings fine-dining sensibility to the bowl — the intimate room, with red-brick walls, duck-grey counters and even siphon coffee gear, feels more like a secret hideaway than a noodle shop.
bio.origin: Kyoto (Gion/Higashiyama); described in sources as connected to the Osaka-Hirakata ramen lineage 'Menroku'
bio.specialty: Duck ramen and duck tsukemen built on Kishu/Kii duck (紀州鴨) raised in Wakayama — antibiotic-free, free-range birds prized for the balance of lean and fat. The clear duck-and-kombu broth coaxes out deep duck umami without heaviness, paired with ultra-thin (about 1.1mm) house-made noodles using German whole-grain rye flour and slices of duck loin and thigh.
bio.philosophy: Meticulous sourcing and refined, French-influenced technique applied to ramen — the much-used phrase is 'こだわり抜かれた' (uncompromisingly crafted).

anecdotes.text: The shop is famous for a signage and menu written almost entirely in emoji (🦆🍜✨), turning a visit into a playful puzzle-like 'adventure' down the Gion backstreets.

The signature duck tsukemen is served as a 'three-stage' experience: dip the noodles in a choice of berry or orange sauce, then add bonito broth to the leftover sauce for a finishing soup, accented with sansho (mountain pepper).

positives.text: English-speaking staff are available and accustomed to helping international visitors, so it is possible to ask about ingredients at the counter.

lead: 俺のラーメン あっぱれ屋
name_ja: 俺のラーメン あっぱれ屋
cuisine: 濃厚豚骨魚介つけ麺・ラーメン
neighborhood: 城陽市奈島 (Najima, Joyo City) — rural hillside off National Route 307, near Yamashiroaodani Station
address_ja: 京都府城陽市奈島下小路11
geocode_note: GSI street-level geocode of 京都府城陽市奈島下小路11 returned [135.831314, 34.839905]. Latitude 34.84 falls below the defined Kyoto box floor (34.93), but this is correct: the shop is in southern Joyo City (城陽市), well south of central Kyoto City. Chō-level geocode of 城陽市奈島 returned [135.792572, 34.827374], confirming the area lies south of the box. Coordinates retained as accurate.
seats_text: Counter seats only (around 16-18) surrounding the kitchen; one wheelchair-accessible seat with ramp access. Spacing was widened to ~14 usable seats at points.
hours_raw: 11:30-14:00. Closed Sundays and public holidays; typically also closed the 4th Saturday of the month. Daily limited items (e.g. Super Tsukemen, 20 servings) often sell out before or shortly after opening; check the shop's blog/social for irregular closures.
closed_days: Sundays and public holidays; usually also the 4th Saturday of the month
cultural_comfort_note: A famous, fast-moving lunch-only ramen counter run by a husband-and-wife team in a rural hillside spot; expect a queue and Japanese-only ordering, so basic Japanese or pointing helps and English support should not be assumed.
gf_detail: Not safe for celiac/gluten-free. Core dishes are wheat ramen/tsukemen noodles in a rich tonkotsu-gyokai (pork bone and fish) broth, and the tare is virtually certain to contain wheat-based soy sauce. No gluten-free menu, no dedicated equipment, and the kitchen is a single shared counter operation. Treat as off-limits for strict GF needs.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The shop is built around pork-bone and fish broths with pork chashu, soft-boiled egg, and dashi-based components; there is no vegan or vegetarian ramen on the menu. Nothing on offer is suitable for a vegan diner.
caveats: Lunch-only and very popular: the signature Super Tsukemen is limited to ~20 servings a day and frequently sells out before or shortly after the 11:30 open, with queues of dozens forming early. Rural location effectively requires a car (parking for ~15); cash only; closed Sundays/holidays and usually the 4th Saturday, with occasional irregular closures announced on the shop's blog. Seat count is approximate and has varied with spacing changes.

bio.background: A husband-and-wife team has run this counter-only ramen shop for over a decade and a half, tucked into a developing hillside in rural Joyo City, well off the beaten path from central Kyoto and reachable mostly by car. Despite the remote setting, it has become one of Kansai's most talked-about ramen destinations, drawing dozens of people who queue before the 11:30 opening.
bio.specialty: The signature draw is the limited Super Tsukemen (スーパーつけ麺): a creamy, aerated tonkotsu-gyokai (pork-bone and fish) dipping broth that the chef whips/blends to a frothy, almost cappuccino-like texture, layered with fish powder and a parmesan-like savory note for a subtly Western character. It comes with firm, ice-chilled noodles offered at three portion sizes (about 150/300/450g) for the same price, topped with tender pepper-crusted rare pork shoulder chashu, generous menma, daikon sprouts, green onion, and a soft-boiled egg, finished with the shop's house-made pepper oil. Rich salt and soy chashu ramen round out a deliberately tight menu.
bio.philosophy: The couple keeps the menu small and the operation disciplined — only a handful of items and a strict daily cap on the Super Tsukemen — running the counter as a tightly coordinated, high-speed two-person team rather than chasing volume or rotating gimmicks. The result is a focused, quality-first bowl that people are willing to drive into the hills and wait in line for.

anecdotes.text: The Super Tsukemen is capped at roughly 20 servings a day and routinely sells out before or just after opening, with reports of around 40 people already queued at 11:30 even on a weekday.
The chef aerates the dipping broth with a blender to give it its signature creamy, foamy body, and pairs it with deliberately firm, ice-water-chilled noodles for a crisp 'boki-boki' bite.
It sits in a hillside spot away from central Joyo that effectively requires a car, yet still pulls a large crowd that forms a line before the doors open.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Single shared counter kitchen built entirely around wheat ramen/tsukemen noodles boiled in common water; no separation possible for gluten-free needs.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soy- and salt-based tare and dipping broths are standard; wheat-containing soy sauce is to be expected in the seasoning, though no allergen breakdown is published.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broths are pork-bone and fish based with pork chashu, egg, and dashi components; no vegan or vegetarian preparation exists, so any dish involves animal-derived broth and toppings.

cuisine: 町中華・食堂（中華そば／皿盛）— machichuka diner serving chuka-soba and rice dishes
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation. The signature chuka-soba is wheat ramen noodles in a soy-based (wheat shoyu) broth, and the famous sara-mori features a breaded, deep-fried pork cutlet plus a curry-udon-style ankake sauce — all wheat-heavy. This is a traditional 1904 family diner with no allergen program; not celiac-safe.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly option. The chuka-soba broth is chicken-based, the tare is soy/wheat shoyu, and the menu centers on pork cutlet, beef-and-onion curry sauce, and other meat/fish dishes. No vegan menu or substitutions noted.
address_ja: 京都府京都市東山区三条通大橋東入大橋町111
geocode_note: GSI geocode of full address returned [135.775436, 35.00703], within the Kyoto box. Matches the shop's location just east of Sanjo Ohashi bridge near Sanjo Keihan Station.
neighborhood: 東山区・三条京阪 (Sanjo Keihan / Sanjo Ohashi)
seats_text: About 36 seats: 8 tables of 4, plus two small raised tatami-style areas seating 2 each.
hours_raw: Tabelog: Mon–Fri & Sun 11:30–15:00; Saturday closed. (A secondary source lists 11:00–15:00 / 16:30–19:00 with Friday closing at 15:00; hours may vary — confirm locally.)
closed_days: Saturday
cultural_comfort_note: A no-frills, century-old neighborhood lunch counter run by an older family; little to no English, so it is most comfortable with a guide or some Japanese and a relaxed, respectful manner.

bio.background: Founded in 1904 (Meiji 37) and now run by the fourth generation, Shinodaya is a beloved retro shokudo just east of the Sanjo Ohashi bridge, steps from Sanjo Keihan Station. The shop name comes from Shinoda Village in Aichi, the founder's home. The current owner Kutsumi Shuichi works the kitchen and counter alongside his wife Midori and his mother Tsugiko — three generations keeping a sturdy old wooden building (its thick central pillar reputedly kept every dish in place during the 1995 Hanshin earthquake) and its recipes essentially unchanged for over a century.
bio.specialty: Two signature bowls define the place. The chuka-soba is a clear, gentle chicken-and-soy broth with thin, custom-made straight noodles — a plain, nostalgic ramen that regulars come back for again and again. The other icon is the sara-mori: a heaping plate of rice with a lard-fried pork cutlet, blanketed in a piping-hot, curry-udon-style ankake sauce with green onion and beef. It looks like katsu curry but tastes nothing like roux curry.
bio.philosophy: Continuity and care: traditional cooking methods (rice cooked in a clay okudosan oven), unchanged recipes, and a warm family-run welcome where Kyoto families have built memories across generations.

anecdotes.text: The famous sara-mori began as an off-menu request from Keihan Railway workers about 30 years ago — rice topped with a cutlet and curry-udon sauce. Customers asked for it on a plate so it would cool faster, and it joined the official menu roughly 25 years ago.
The shop's solid traditional construction, anchored by a heavy central pillar, is said to have come through the 1995 Hanshin-Awaji earthquake without a single dish falling.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The chuka-soba broth is a soy-based (shoyu) chicken stock; standard Japanese shoyu contains wheat.

caveats: Hours differ between sources: Tabelog lists 11:30–15:00 (Sat closed), while another guide lists 11:00–15:00 / 16:30–19:00 with a Friday 15:00 close — confirm locally before visiting. No official website found; details drawn from Tabelog and food-media coverage. Prices cited (chuka-soba ¥500–700, sara-mori ¥650) vary by source/date.

cuisine: 町中華・中華そば（街の食堂） / Machi-chuka shokudo: chuka-soba (old-school Chinese noodle soup) plus udon, rice bowls and set meals
gf_detail: No gluten-free options. The chuka-soba is built on wheat noodles in a soy-tare/pork-style broth, and the kitchen also turns out udon, katsu curry and other wheat-and-fry dishes. There is no celiac-safe offering and no indication the kitchen accommodates gluten avoidance. Not suitable for anyone who needs to avoid wheat or gluten.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly place. The signature chuka-soba comes with char siu (pork) and kamaboko (fish cake) over an animal-based broth, and the wider menu is meat-, fish- and egg-centric (katsu curry, nabeyaki udon, rice bowls). No dedicated vegan or vegetable-only ramen is offered.
address_ja: 京都府京都市下京区綾小路通御幸町東入ル足袋屋町330
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned the first feature at [135.76329, 35.002567] (chō-level, 下京区綾材木町 vicinity); within the Kyoto box and consistent with the Shijo-Kawaramachi / behind Fujii Daimaru location.
neighborhood: Shijo-Kawaramachi (Shimogyo-ku) — on the Ayakoji-Miyukicho corner just behind Fujii Daimaru, about a 2-3 minute walk from Hankyu Kyoto-Kawaramachi Station
seats_text: 30 seats
hours_raw: Mon-Fri 11:00-22:00; Sat 11:00-16:30; Sun & public holidays closed. (Hours and closed days may change; check before visiting.)
closed_days: Sundays and public holidays
cultural_comfort_note: A no-frills neighborhood shokudo with a Showa-retro feel and little English; basic Japanese or pointing at the menu will carry you through comfortably.
caveats: Hours and closed days may change, so confirm before a special trip. Credit cards are not accepted (electronic money and QR-code payments are, but carry cash to be safe). No reservations.

bio.background: Yamanoya is an old-school neighborhood eatery (machi-chuka shokudo) that has been feeding the Shijo-Kawaramachi crowd for well over half a century, tucked onto the Ayakoji-Miyukicho corner right behind the Fujii Daimaru department store. Despite sitting in one of Kyoto's busiest shopping districts, it has kept the unpretentious feel of a working-people's lunch counter rather than a specialist ramen bar.
bio.specialty: Chuka-soba: a gentle, everyday bowl of springy wheat noodles in a mild broth topped with bean sprouts, green onion, char siu and kamaboko (fish cake) — a homey, cafeteria-style take rather than a fashionable ramen.
bio.philosophy: Tasty, genuinely cheap, everyday food you could happily eat any day — the comfort of a real local diner over any specialist gimmick.

anecdotes.text: Regulars and writers single out the Showa-retro atmosphere: it feels like a nostalgic neighborhood gohan-ya, and the chuka-soba's char siu and fish-cake toppings mark it as a homey diner dish rather than a serious ramen-ya creation. The broth is praised as so gentle you could eat it every day.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The chuka-soba uses wheat noodles in a soy-style broth; standard Japanese soy sauce and tare contain wheat. No wheat-free preparation is offered.

cuisine: 汁なし担担麺・担々麺専門店 (Sichuan-style tantanmen and shiru-nashi tantanmen)
gf_detail: Not safe for celiac. The core dishes are tantanmen and shiru-nashi tantanmen built on straight wheat noodles (sourced from a noodle factory), a sesame-paste tare, and Sichuan seasonings that typically include wheat-based soy sauce. No gluten-free noodle, menu, or preparation is offered, and the tiny 10-seat counter kitchen has no separate gluten-free workflow.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly option. Every dish centers on sweet ground pork: the tantanmen, shiru-nashi tantanmen, mapo tofu/mapo rice, and Chinese-style broth all use meat. There is no plant-based bowl on the menu.
address_ja: 京都府京都市左京区田中里ノ内町64
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI from the full street address; result (lat 35.034092, lng 135.780991) falls within the Kyoto bounding box and sits at Tanaka Satononaicho, immediately by Mototanaka Station as expected.
neighborhood: 左京区 田中里ノ内町 / 元田中駅 (Mototanaka Station, Eizan Railway)
seats_text: 10 counter seats only; non-smoking
closed_days: Sundays
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny 10-seat, cash-only neighborhood counter shop with a short Japanese menu and no reservations: comfortable if you can order simply in Japanese and follow counter etiquette.

bio.background: A specialist tantanmen counter shop near Mototanaka Station in Kyoto's Sakyo ward, relocated to its current Tanaka Satononaicho address in January 2024. It keeps a deliberately tight focus: just ten counter seats and a short menu built around Sichuan-style dan dan noodles.
bio.origin: Kyoto, Sakyo-ku (Mototanaka), specializing in Sichuan-influenced tantanmen.
bio.specialty: The signature is the shiru-nashi tantanmen (soupless dan dan noodles): straight wheat noodles topped with a mountain of green onions, a sweet ground-pork mince, sesame, and Sichuan peppercorns, with adjustable heat across five levels. The brothy tantanmen (rich sesame Chinese-style broth) and a mini mapo-rice set are equally popular.
bio.philosophy: A single-minded focus on tantanmen done well: rich sesame depth balanced against stimulating Sichuan spice, served from a small counter where each bowl gets full attention.

anecdotes.text: The shop is repeatedly singled out as one of Kyoto's best tantanmen specialists, with the soupless version cited as the dish that turns first-timers into regulars.
A professional chef who runs a Korean restaurant is quoted calling Shisentei 'Kyoto's best dan dan noodles.'
Reviewers note the heat is adjustable across five levels; the brothy tantanmen is approachable while the soupless tantanmen is noticeably spicier and considered the more flavor-concentrated choice.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All bowls use straight wheat noodles sourced from a noodle factory; there is no gluten-free noodle and the entire menu is wheat-based.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The dishes are Sichuan-style tantanmen and mapo, which conventionally use wheat-based soy sauce in the tare and sauces; no wheat-free soy sauce is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Every dish uses sweet ground pork and Chinese-style meat broth, including the tantanmen, soupless tantanmen, and mapo tofu/rice; no plant-based option exists.

lead: ラーメン 大栄 本店
name_ja: ラーメン 大栄 本店
cuisine: 京都ラーメン（豚骨醤油・味噌）／ラーメン
gf_detail: No gluten-free options. This is a traditional Kyoto ramen shop: wheat noodles (sourced from a local noodle maker), a soy-sauce or miso tare almost certainly made with wheat-based shoyu/miso, and a pork-based broth. No dedicated fryer, no allergen menu, and no indication of celiac awareness. Not safe for celiac diners; cross-contamination from shared noodle water and a single small counter kitchen is essentially certain.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The broth is pork-based (豚骨/豚清湯) and bowls are built around generous chashu pork; free toppings are chives and grated garlic. There is no vegetable or vegan broth option. Vegans and vegetarians have no viable bowl here.
