address_ja: 京都府京都市南区東九条西明田町20
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 京都府京都市南区東九条西明田町20; within the Kyoto bounding box.
seats_text: 11 seats, counter only (small L-shaped counter)
hours_raw: 月～土 11:00-14:30、18:30-21:30／定休日 日曜日（一部情報では土曜の夜営業が短縮との記載あり）
closed_days: Sundays (日曜日)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, busy, cash-only neighborhood counter shop with a Japanese-only menu and no English service expected; comfortable for visitors who can order simply in Japanese or point at the menu.

bio.background: A neighborhood ramen institution in the Kujo area just south of Kyoto Station, in business for more than 40 years (locally cited as founded in 1981). It is the original 'honten' of the Daiei name and remains a small, independently run counter shop that draws lines even on weekday afternoons.
bio.specialty: Generously loaded bowls built on a pork-based broth: the shoyu ramen and the miso ramen are the signatures. Both come piled with thinly sliced chashu (often more than ten slices, even on a regular bowl), local Kujo green onions, and bean sprouts, over medium-thick springy noodles from a local noodle maker. Free chives and grated garlic at the counter let regulars tune the flavor.
bio.philosophy: A minimalist, value-driven menu (essentially shoyu and miso) executed consistently for decades, prized for volume, balance, and a clean pork broth rather than novelty.

anecdotes.text: Regulars describe the bowls as overflowing with thin-sliced chashu and topped with Kujo negi, with free chives and grated garlic at the counter to change up the flavor mid-meal.
The miso ramen is noted for a subtle acidity and a clean, refreshing finish over a pork broth ('ほんのり酸味を感じ、スッキリとした美味しさ').

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles are boiled in shared water in a single small counter kitchen; gluten cross-contamination is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Shoyu and miso ramen are the house specialties; the soy-sauce/miso tare is almost certainly wheat-based, as is standard for this style. No wheat-free tare is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is pork-based and bowls are built around chashu pork; no vegan or vegetarian broth is offered, so all items share animal-based stock.

caveats: Founding year (1981) and the ~40-year history are reported by secondary sources rather than an official site; no official website was found. One source notes Saturday evening hours may be shorter than weekdays, so evening hours could vary slightly. Owner/chef name not confirmed. GF/vegan unsuitability is inferred from the ramen style (wheat noodles, wheat shoyu/miso tare, pork broth) plus the absence of any allergen accommodation, not from an explicit statement.

cuisine: 町中華・四川料理（担々麺）/ Sichuan machichuka, tantanmen
neighborhood: 中京区 / 二条駅前 (Nijo Station)
gf_detail: No gluten-free provision. The tantanmen and ramen use wheat noodles (the dry tantanmen uses thin Kagawa-style wheat udon noodles), and the kitchen is a Sichuan/Chinese operation built on soy-sauce-based sauces, fried dishes, and wheat-containing seasonings. No dedicated gluten-free menu, no allergen handling claims found in any source. Not suitable for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan or vegetarian operation. The menu centers on meat and animal-based dishes: glazed-pork teridon, beef mapo tofu, Shanghai sweet-and-sour pork, drunken chicken, and meat/pork-and-chili tantanmen. No vegan ramen or dedicated plant-based options were found in any source.
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区西ノ京星池町149
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates [135.74054, 35.012348], within the Kyoto box; consistent with Nijo Station vicinity.
seats_text: 30 seats, a mix of small tables and larger tables.
hours_raw: 11:30–14:15 / 17:30–21:00 (L.O. 20:30). Closed Tuesdays, with occasional additional Wednesday closures some months.
closed_days: Tuesdays (occasional additional Wednesday closures some months)
cultural_comfort_note: A bustling, locals-packed neighborhood Chinese spot with no indication of English service; basic Japanese or a translation tool will make ordering smoother.
caveats: Lines form at both lunch and dinner; arrive early or off-peak. Closed Tuesdays plus occasional extra Wednesday closures, so confirm before a special trip. No allergen, gluten-free, or vegan accommodation was documented in any source, so this is not a celiac-safe or vegan-friendly destination.

bio.background: Founded in 1974 near Nijo Station, the shop reached its 50-year milestone in 2024. It is run by a parent-and-child team: the first generation built its reputation as a beloved casual neighborhood Chinese restaurant (machichuka), and the second generation studied Sichuan cooking, layering authentic Sichuan flavors over the original homey menu.
bio.specialty: The house signature is the teridon (照り丼) family of glazed-pork rice bowls, especially the teridon kinshi topped with silky egg ribbons and richly glazed pork in a sweet-savory sauce. On the noodle side, the kitchen offers both a brothed tantanmen and a sesame-free dry tantanmen (汁なし担々麺) built on thin Kagawa wheat noodles dressed with Sichuan chili and Sichuan pepper aromatics; the beef mapo tofu is another standout.
bio.philosophy: A flavor for everyone, from children to seniors: sweet-and-savory comfort cooking that keeps the room full at both lunch and dinner, with the second generation's Sichuan training giving the menu real depth and heat alongside the nostalgic favorites.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant has earned the Michelin Bib Gourmand, recognition for great food at a friendly price.
Locals consider it an indispensable name in Kyoto Chinese cooking; lines regularly form for both the midday and evening services.
The dry tantanmen is made without sesame and uses thin Kagawa-prefecture wheat noodles topped with Sichuan chili and Sichuan pepper.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Noodles are wheat-based (the dry tantanmen uses thin Kagawa wheat noodles), and the kitchen runs Sichuan/Chinese dishes with soy-based sauces and fried items, so heavy gluten cross-contact is inherent.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is meat-forward (glazed pork, beef mapo tofu, chicken, pork tantanmen); no vegan dishes or separated plant-based prep were documented.

name_ja: 西麻布カレーつけ麺 なんだっ亭
cuisine: 濃厚カレーつけ麺・カレーらぁ麺（つけ麺/ラーメン） — curry tsukemen, curry ramen and curry abura-soba
neighborhood: 中京区 / 京都河原町駅 (Kyoto-Kawaramachi Station), Shinkyogoku arcade area
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区中之町565-18
geocode_note: GSI first feature geometry.coordinates = [135.767471, 35.004189]; within Kyoto box.
seats_text: L-shaped counter, 13 seats (small counter-only shop)
hours_raw: Tabelog: 11:30-16:00, 18:00-21:00. Closed days: 不定休 (irregular). Blog sources note earlier hour formats (e.g. 11:30-15:00, 17:00-23:00 with Monday closures); hours have varied over time, so confirm before visiting.
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular closures); some sources note Mondays — confirm before visiting
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny 13-seat counter shop tucked in a Shinkyogoku side street; comfortable for an independent traveler but expect Japanese-language ordering and a no-frills local pace.
gf_detail: Not safe for celiac/gluten-free. Every signature dish is wheat-noodle based — the noodles are custom wheat noodles from Asakusa Kaikaro — and the curry broths and tare standardly contain wheat. No gluten-free menu, no dedicated equipment, and no published allergen handling. Avoid if you require strict gluten avoidance.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan or vegetarian option. The curry broths are built on beef cheek and a seafood/fish dashi base, and the dishes center on meat-and-fish curry. No vegan or vegetarian bowls are advertised, and the kitchen is animal-broth based.

bio.background: A curry-noodle specialist that carries the Nishi-Azabu name in its full title — 西麻布カレーつけ麺 なんだっ亭. The owner trained at a Tokyo Nishi-Azabu curry-ramen specialist before opening here, and is described as a former French chef who crossed over into noodles, which shows in the layered, refined curry broths. The shop sits down a quiet Shinkyogoku-area side street near Kyoto-Kawaramachi, an unassuming little counter that has built a loyal regular following.
bio.specialty: Curry tsukemen — the signature bowl. The dipping broth is a curry built on slow-cooked beef cheek and a seafood/fish dashi base, balancing gentle sweetness against spice, with a milky, almost coconut-like roundness. It is paired with custom medium-thick, chewy wheat noodles ordered from the long-established Asakusa Kaikaro (founded 1950). Curry ramen (カレーらぁ麺) and curry abura-soba round out the lineup; a finishing scoop of rice into the leftover broth is a beloved ritual that shows off the soup's depth.
bio.philosophy: A small, focused counter built around one idea executed carefully — curry-and-noodles done with a chef's attention to broth-building rather than a big menu.

anecdotes.text: The owner is a former French chef who retrained in ramen at a Nishi-Azabu (Tokyo) curry-ramen specialist before opening in Kyoto.
Diners are encouraged to drop a small bowl of rice into the leftover curry dipping broth at the end — locals say that finish is where the quality of the soup really shows.
The noodles are custom-made by Asakusa Kaikaro, a noodle maker founded in 1950, giving them a notably chewy, substantial texture.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All signature dishes use custom wheat noodles from Asakusa Kaikaro; there is no gluten-free noodle option and no separate preparation, so cross-contamination with wheat is unavoidable.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Curry broths are made from beef cheek and a seafood/fish dashi base, so all bowls share an animal-and-fish broth; no vegan/vegetarian preparation exists.

caveats: Hours and closed days vary between sources and the official site is offline (404); the current Tabelog hours are used here but should be confirmed before visiting. Cash only. No allergen-handling or vegan/GF accommodation is published — treat as unsafe for celiac and unsuitable for vegan/vegetarian diners.

cuisine: 京醤油 鶏ガラ醤油ラーメン (Kyoto-style soy sauce ramen)
neighborhood: 上京区 古木町 / 今出川・鞍馬口
vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian options found. The signature bowls are built on a dashi-forward broth with chashu made from Iga mochi pork; the miso bowl uses rendered beef fat. Animal protein is central to every bowl on the menu.
gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. This is wheat-noodle ramen, and the signature bowl is dressed with a blend of two soy sauces (from a local Gosho-nishi maker) — Japanese soy sauce normally contains wheat. The kitchen is a single small counter operation with no documented gluten-free accommodation or dedicated prep, so cross-contact with wheat is unavoidable.
address_ja: 京都府京都市上京区寺之内通新町東入古木町407
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned coordinates for the full street address; within the Kyoto bounding box.
seats_text: 12 seats total: an L-shaped counter (8 seats) plus a small table area (4 seats).
hours_raw: 11:00-14:30 (lunch service only; evening service suspended)
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular closures)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny lunch-only counter shop run by a chef who is described as bright and attentive; basic Japanese for ordering will go a long way, and there is no indication of English support.

bio.background: The owner trained as a kappo chef in Japanese haute cuisine before his love of ramen grew strong enough that he opened this small counter shop near Imadegawa to pursue it full-time.
bio.specialty: The 京醤油らぁ麺 (Kyoto soy sauce ramen) is the signature bowl: a refined, dashi-forward broth seasoned with a blend of two soy sauces from a local Gosho-nishi maker (Sawai), topped with chashu made from prized Iga mochi pork prepared with traditional kappo technique. The shop also offers a 極塩らぁ麺 (salt) and a 香辛味噌らぁ麺 (spicy miso) using white and red miso from a historic Shimogyo shop with Sichuan pepper and star anise.
bio.philosophy: To let the umami of carefully chosen ingredients carry the bowl rather than leaning on heavy seasoning — ramen approached with the restraint and ingredient focus of a kappo kitchen.

anecdotes.text: The proprietor's kappo (Japanese haute cuisine) training shows in the details: the chashu is cut from Iga mochi pork and finished with traditional Japanese culinary technique, and the soy tare blends two soy sauces from a neighborhood Gosho-nishi maker for a mellow, smooth mouthfeel.
He opened the shop because his passion for ramen outgrew his kappo career, building a small counter near Imadegawa to serve refined, dashi-led bowls at lunch.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Single small counter kitchen serving wheat-noodle ramen; no dedicated gluten-free prep or separate equipment is documented, so wheat cross-contact is effectively unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature Kyoto soy sauce bowl is seasoned with a blend of two soy sauces from a local maker; Japanese soy sauce normally contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths and toppings are animal-based (dashi-forward broth, Iga mochi pork chashu; the miso bowl uses rendered beef fat), so no vegan-safe path on the menu.

caveats: Lunch-only, cash-only, tiny counter with irregular closures — confirm the day is open before going. Tabelog notes evening service was suspended. Hours listed as 11:00-14:30 daily but closures are irregular (不定休).

lead: 京都担担麺 金鵄楼 本店
name_ja: 京都担担麺 金鵄楼 本店
cuisine: 担担麺（汁あり・汁なし・冷やし）／ Tantanmen specialist
neighborhood: 中京区・御所南（堺町夷川）
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区絹屋町128-4
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 京都府京都市中京区絹屋町128-4 returned [135.763321, 35.015572], within the Kyoto box.
seats_text: Counter only. Sources vary between 6 and 7 counter seats; small, solo-friendly space.
hours_raw: 11:00-14:30 (L.O. 14:00) daily except Sunday; dinner service 17:00-20:30 (L.O. 20:00) Thursday and Friday only. Closed Sundays.
closed_days: 日曜日 (Sunday)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny counter-only specialist popular with locals and solo diners; ordering is via a customization slip, so a few words of Japanese and patience with possible lines help, but it is approachable.
vegan_detail: Tantanmen here is built on a sesame-rich creamy broth and topped with coarsely ground meat miso (pork), so the signature bowls are not vegan. No vegan or plant-based option is advertised; ask if you want to know whether the broth can be modified.
gf_detail: This is a wheat-noodle tantanmen shop (the noodles are hand-cut crimped wheat noodles from Sano, Tochigi), and tantanmen tare/sauce typically contains wheat-based soy sauce. There is no gluten-free offering and no allergen information published. Not suitable for celiac diners.
caveats: Seat count differs between sources (6 vs 7). No owner/chef biography or allergen details were found in any fetched source. Dinner service runs only Thursday and Friday.

bio.background: 京都担担麺 金鵄楼 (Kinshiro) opened on March 25, 2024 in the quiet Gosho-minami neighborhood at the Sakaicho-Ibikawa corner of Nakagyo Ward, a stylish counter-only shop that quickly drew lunchtime lines from its 11:00 opening.
bio.specialty: Tantanmen offered three ways - with broth (汁あり), without broth (汁なし), and chilled (冷やし). The signature bowl is a sesame-rich, lightly whipped creamy white broth that stays surprisingly light on the palate, paired with chewy thick (rather than the usual thin) noodles for a strong textural presence, and crowned with a praised coarsely ground meat miso. Diners customize noodle firmness, chili oil (rayu) and Sichuan pepper (花椒) levels on an order slip.
bio.philosophy: A focused single-dish specialist: do tantanmen well, let guests dial in their own heat and numbing spice, and keep an intimate counter where solo diners feel welcome.

anecdotes.text: Became a local craze almost immediately after opening, with lines forming from the 11:00 start - the shop earned a reputation for being addictively good among Kyoto tantanmen fans.
Goes against the tantanmen norm by using chewy thick noodles, hand-cut crimped wheat noodles sourced from Sano City, Tochigi, that hold their own against the creamy broth.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Tantanmen tare is typically made with wheat-containing soy sauce; no allergen breakdown is published for this shop, so wheat should be assumed present in the sauce as well as the noodles.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The broth is sesame-rich and bowls are topped with pork meat miso; no separate vegetarian/vegan preparation is described.

cuisine: 町中華・京風北京料理（中華そば・五目焼そば等の麺、名物胡麻餃子）
neighborhood: 左京区 浄土寺・銀閣寺エリア（元田中駅）
address_ja: 京都府京都市左京区浄土寺馬場町39-4
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 京都府京都市左京区浄土寺馬場町39-4 returned [135.793198, 35.024471]; within Kyoto bounding box.
seats_text: About 37–40 seats, including table and tatami/zashiki seating; private rooms available.
closed_days: Mondays; also closed 1st and 3rd Tuesdays.
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional local Kyoto-Chinese restaurant where Japanese is the working language; a few words of Japanese and a relaxed, polite manner will serve you well.
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation indicated. This is a wheat-heavy Chinese kitchen: gyoza/shumai/spring-roll wrappers, Chinese noodles and fried rice seasonings, and wheat-based soy sauce/seasoning are central. The signature sesame gyoza even has roasted sesame kneaded into a wheat wrapper. Not suitable for celiac diners; no dedicated handling found.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly venue. Pork is central to the menu (gyoza, shumai, fried rice, noodle dishes), and dishes are seasoned with soy oil, sake and likely meat/seafood-based stocks. No vegan-specific options or labeling were found.
caveats: Cuisine is 京風北京料理 (Kyoto-style Beijing/town-Chinese) rather than a dedicated ramen shop; it does serve Chinese noodle dishes (中華そば/八宝麺/五目焼そば) alongside its famous gyoza. Closed-day schedule is irregular (Mondays plus 1st/3rd Tuesdays) — confirm before visiting. Seat count cited as 37–40 across sources.

bio.background: Seikatei opened in 1982 in the quiet temple district near Ginkaku-ji. Its roots run back to 盛京亭 (Seikyotei), a Beijing-style restaurant founded in 1951 in the heart of Gion's hanamachi (geisha district). The founder's father served as head chef at Seikyotei and was sent to run a branch that eventually became this independent shop, carrying most of the original menu with it.
bio.specialty: Goma-gyoza (sesame dumplings): roasted sesame is kneaded right into the wrapper, with a filling of only cabbage, Chinese chives, pork and sake, then pan-fried in heavy cast iron until the surface turns crisp. It began as an off-menu request from a regular and became the house signature.
bio.philosophy: A Kyoto take on Chinese food: because the original Gion shop cooked for geiko and refined patrons, it deliberately dialed back heavy aromatics like garlic and excess oil. Seikatei keeps that restraint — light white-pressed soybean oil, local Matsui-brewery sake instead of cooking wine, and clean, delicate flavors over punch.

anecdotes.text: The famous sesame gyoza started life as an off-menu dish made for one regular customer before becoming the shop's signature.

The shop traces back to 盛京亭, opened in Gion's flower district in 1951, where the founder's father was head chef; the garlic-light, oil-light style was developed to suit the refined palates of Gion's patrons and geisha.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat is pervasive: gyoza, shumai and spring-roll wrappers, Chinese noodles, and fried-rice/stir-fry seasonings all involve wheat, with shared woks and fryers typical of a town-Chinese kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes are seasoned in the Chinese-Japanese style; standard soy sauce/seasonings contain wheat. No wheat-free seasoning is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Pork is used throughout (gyoza, shumai, fried rice, noodle dishes) and stir-fries share oil and pans, so genuine vegan separation is not provided.

positives.text: Cooking is intentionally light on garlic and heavy oil — a gentler, cleaner style of Chinese food, though this is about palate rather than allergen safety.

lead: 中華のサカイ 本店
name_ja: 中華のサカイ 本店
cuisine: 町中華・中華そば（名物：焼豚冷麺）
gf_detail: This is a traditional town-Chinese (machi-chuka) noodle shop whose signature bowls are wheat-based: the famous cold noodles (冷麺) use custom wheat noodles dressed in a vinegar-and-sesame sauce, and the chuka-soba is a wheat-noodle soy-broth noodle. Wheat is fundamental to the core menu and the sauces, with no gluten-free preparation indicated. Not suitable for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: The house specialty cold noodles are topped with roast pork (焼豚), cucumber, and seaweed, and the kitchen is a meat-and-fish Chinese kitchen. No vegan ramen or vegan menu is indicated. Not a vegan-friendly destination.
address_ja: 京都府京都市北区紫野上門前町92 新大宮商店街
geocode_note: GSI address search on 京都府京都市北区紫野上門前町92 returned a single feature at [135.748825, 35.044498], within the Kyoto bounding box (Murasaki/Shinomiya shopping street, near Daitoku-ji, Kita-ku).
seats_text: 41 seats, including counter seating
hours_raw: Wed-Fri 11:00-15:00 (L.O. 14:45) & 17:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:30); Sat-Sun 11:00-16:00 (L.O. 15:30) & 17:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:30). Lunch may end earlier if sold out. Closed Mon & Tue (open if a public holiday).
closed_days: Monday and Tuesday (open if a public holiday)
cultural_comfort_note: A beloved local town-Chinese institution where most ordering is in Japanese and lines of regulars are common; a little Japanese or pointing at the famous cold noodles goes a long way.

bio.origin: Murasaki / Shin-Omiya shopping street, Kita-ku, Kyoto, near Daitoku-ji
bio.background: Founded in 1939 (Showa 14) as a coffee shop, the business reinvented itself during wartime as a Western-style restaurant and, after the war, became a specialist Chinese noodle shop. Traces of those earlier lives still linger on the menu in the form of kissaten-style cafe items kept on as a nod to the past.
bio.specialty: The legendary cold noodles (冷麺) — roast pork, cucumber and seaweed over custom wheat noodles in a vinegar-and-sesame sauce. Unusually, the shop serves them year-round rather than only in summer, and roughly 90% of customers order them. The other pillar is a classic soy-based chuka-soba.
bio.philosophy: Quietly perfectionist about the basics: the cold-noodle sauce is built on honjozo vinegar of the grade used in Kyoto's high-end kaiseki kitchens, and the noodles themselves were dialed in over dozens of trial batches in collaboration with a local noodle maker so they would marry perfectly with the sauce.

anecdotes.text: The signature cold noodles were created by the current owner's parents, who refined an earlier recipe that had included shrimp and egg ribbons down to a spare trio of pork, cucumber and seaweed; getting the custom noodles right reportedly took dozens of failed attempts and plenty of arguing with the noodle manufacturer.
A favorite insider move: pay about 100 yen extra to swap your rice for fried rice, then pour the leftover cold-noodle sauce over it for a surprisingly delicious finish.
Because it started life in 1939 as a cafe before becoming a Chinese restaurant, the shop has long kept some kissaten-style cafe dishes on the menu as a remnant of its origins.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The chuka-soba is a traditional soy-based Chinese noodle soup; soy sauce in this style of cooking is conventionally wheat-based, though the shop publishes no specific allergen details.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is a meat-and-fish Chinese kitchen and the signature cold noodles are topped with roast pork, so cross-contact with animal products is pervasive.

neighborhood: 紫野・新大宮商店街（大徳寺近く）, Kita-ku

lead: にぼ魂ラーメン 祇園本店
name_ja: にぼ魂ラーメン 祇園本店
cuisine: 煮干しラーメン（niboshi / dried-sardine ramen）
gf_detail: This is a niboshi (dried sardine) ramen specialist serving thick curly wheat noodles in a fish-based broth. As with virtually all Japanese ramen, the noodles are wheat, the tare is almost certainly a wheat-based shoyu, and there is no indication of any gluten-free option or accommodation. Not suitable for celiacs or anyone requiring strict gluten avoidance. No dedicated GF preparation, menu, or cross-contamination controls are advertised.
vegan_detail: The entire concept is built on a dried-sardine (niboshi) fish broth, with chashu pork, soft-boiled egg, and nori as standard toppings. There is no vegan or vegetarian ramen on offer, and the broth itself is fish-based, so even broth-free items cannot be assumed plant-based. Not a vegan-friendly destination.
address_ja: 京都府京都市東山区富永町141
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 京都府京都市東山区富永町141番地; within the Kyoto bounding box.
neighborhood: 祇園（祇園北エリア）・縄手通／東山区
seats_text: Small late-night shop: box/table seating (three 4-person tables and one 2-person table) plus roughly 4 counter seats in front of the kitchen — about 11 seats in a compact, bright interior.
hours_raw: 食べログ掲載: 火・水・木・金・土・日(祝) 22:00〜07:00（L.O. 06:30）／月・日 定休。早仕舞いあり。開店時の店舗・ブログ情報では 20:00〜翌05:00、日・月定休との記載もあり、深夜営業のため時間は変動する可能性あり。
closed_days: 日曜・月曜（Sunday & Monday）
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, cash-only late-night ramen counter in Gion with no sign of English support — basic Japanese and pointing at the menu will go furthest, and quiet ordering etiquette is appreciated.
caveats: Deep-night operation (opens late and runs into the early morning) with "早仕舞い" early-closing on slow nights, so hours genuinely vary — go relatively early in the window rather than near closing. Cash only. The defining niboshi broth is intentionally bitter and assertive, which not everyone enjoys. Hours differ between sources (opening-era 20:00–05:00 vs. current listing 22:00–07:00); treat the schedule as approximate and confirm locally.

bio.background: にぼ魂 opened on August 8, 2025 in the heart of Gion, just north of Gion-Shijo Station off Nawate-dori, taking over a space that previously housed a katsudon-style shop. It was conceived from the start as a deep-night niboshi-ramen stop — a bowl to land in after an evening out in Gion's bars and teahouses, when most kitchens have long closed.
bio.specialty: The signature is the Special Nibodamashii Ramen: an unapologetically bitter, dried-sardine (niboshi) broth ladled over thick, curly noodles, finished with marbled chashu, a soft-boiled egg, nori, and a single whole niboshi as garnish. The kitchen deliberately leaves the sardine heads and innards in the stock rather than removing them to soften the bitterness.
bio.philosophy: Lean into the niboshi rather than tame it — a punchy, sea-forward bowl built for the late-night palate, served bright and clean in a small room. Diners can dial the flavor themselves with the tabletop kit of spiced tare, black and white pepper, and niboshi vinegar, and free extra-large noodle portions reward a hungry post-drinking crowd.

anecdotes.text: The shop intentionally keeps the sardine heads and guts in the broth instead of removing them, embracing the sharp bitterness as the point of the bowl rather than a flaw to correct.
Every table is set with a niboshi-forward condiment kit — spiced tare, black pepper, white pepper, and niboshi vinegar — so guests can tune the intensity of the dried-sardine flavor to taste.
Opened August 8, 2025 as a late-night destination for Gion's after-hours crowd, occupying a former katsudon shop and running into the early morning.

gf_cross_contamination.text: A wheat-noodle ramen shop with fried side dishes prepared in a tiny shared kitchen; no gluten-free handling or separation is indicated, so cross-contact with wheat is effectively certain.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The tare is a Japanese ramen seasoning, which is overwhelmingly a wheat-containing shoyu base; no wheat-free soy sauce is advertised.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All bowls are built on a dried-sardine fish broth with pork chashu and egg; there is no plant-based broth or vegan preparation, so no item can be considered free of animal cross-contact.

cuisine: 京都ラーメン（豚骨醤油・九条ネギ／チャーシュー山盛り）
neighborhood: 上京区・神宮丸太町駅／丸太町通（河原町丸太町）
gf_detail: This is a traditional Kyoto pork-bone-shoyu ramen shop. The bowls are built on wheat noodles (custom-made by Kondo Seimen) plus a soy-based tare, which is virtually always wheat-containing. There is no indication of any gluten-free options, gluten-free noodles, or celiac handling. Not safe for those who must strictly avoid gluten.
vegan_detail: Every bowl is built on a pork-and-bone broth (described as crystal-clear shoyu tonkotsu made from premium Iga pork), and chashu pork is the signature topping. There is no vegan or vegetarian ramen on the menu; even a plain bowl uses animal broth. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
address_ja: 京都府京都市上京区河原町丸太町上ル出水町275 ドムス河原町1F
geocode_note: GSI address-search geocode of 京都府京都市上京区出水町275 returned [135.76918, 35.018932], within the Kyoto bounding box and consistent with the Kawaramachi-Marutamachi location.
seats_text: About 16 seats, mostly an L-shaped counter plus one table; small, intimate room.
closed_days: 2nd, 4th, and 5th Sundays (some sources list 2nd & 4th Sundays); also closes early when sold out.
cultural_comfort_note: A no-frills, cash-only local counter ramen shop favored by regulars; a friendly greeting and a few words of Japanese will go a long way, with no English menu expected.
caveats: Cash only; no reservations. Hours can end early once the day's broth/ingredients sell out, and the closed-Sunday schedule is irregular (varies by week). Closing times reported slightly differently across sources (14:00/18:00 vs 14:15/18:15); confirm before a late visit.

bio.background: The original Ramen Daiei was founded in 1980 in Kyoto's Minami ward. The current owner is the founder's nephew, who apprenticed under him for about 20 years before opening this Kawaramachi-Marutamachi shop in 2003 to carry the lineage forward. When the founder passed away in 2017, he left a charge to keep the recipe alive.
bio.origin: Kyoto (lineage from Daiei Ramen, Minami ward)
bio.specialty: A clear, aromatic Kyoto-style shoyu tonkotsu ramen crowned with a mountain of freshly hand-sliced chashu and a generous heap of Kujo negi. The popular order is the shoyu chashu-men with extra negi; a miso version is also offered, and a yaki-meshi (fried rice) set rounds out the menu.
bio.philosophy: The founder's words guide the shop: 'ラーメンはスープが命' (the broth is the soul of ramen). The kitchen keeps the broth pure and clean - made only from premium pork and bones, with no cloudiness, bitterness, or gaminess - aiming for a refreshing, never-tiring bowl.

anecdotes.text: The broth uses Iga mochibuta premium pork and noodles made to order by Kondo Seimen; the chashu is sliced fresh to order so it stays tender and full of umami.
Beneath the generous Kujo negi, shredded shiitake adds a textural contrast, and free condiments at the counter include spicy nira, beni-shoga, and fresh garlic to customize each bowl.
Just steps from the Kawaramachi-Marutamachi intersection, it keeps a clean, unpretentious counter atmosphere beloved by locals and ramen fans rather than tourists.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Bowls are built on wheat ramen noodles plus a soy-based tare; the shop and broth are not designed around gluten avoidance, so cross-contamination from shared pots and noodle water should be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature is a shoyu (soy sauce) tonkotsu broth; Japanese soy sauce almost always contains wheat, so the tare should be treated as wheat-containing.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All bowls use a pork-and-bone broth and pork chashu; there is no vegan/vegetarian preparation, so no vegan-safe option exists.

positives.text: The broth is made only from premium pork and bones with no additives or muddiness, so ingredients are simple and transparent; toppings like Kujo negi, mung bean sprouts, and chashu are clearly identifiable.

lead: 麺屋 たけ井 本店
name_ja: 麺屋 たけ井 本店
cuisine: つけ麺（濃厚豚骨魚介・全粒粉極太麺）
gf_detail: This is a tsukemen specialist built on whole-wheat (全粒粉) thick house-made noodles, a pork-bone-and-fish broth, and a wheat-based shoyu tare. Every core element of the signature bowl contains wheat, and there is no indication of gluten-free noodles, tare, or any allergen accommodation. Not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
vegan_detail: The house specialty is a thick pork-bone and fish (豚骨魚介) dipping broth, finished tableside with fish dashi, and bowls are topped with pork chashu, smoked chicken, and egg. The menu is animal-based throughout with no vegan or vegetarian option noted.
address_ja: 京都府城陽市観音堂甲田48-2
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 京都府城陽市観音堂甲田48 returned [lng 135.791275, lat 34.83569]. Longitude is within range but latitude (34.836) falls south of the specified Kyoto box (lat 34.93–35.13); Joyo City sits in southern Kyoto below that range, so coordinates are nulled per instructions. The geocoded point is consistent with the shop's actual location near Nagaike Station in Joyo City.
neighborhood: 城陽市観音堂・長池駅（京都南部・宇治エリア）
seats_text: 17 seats total: 5 counter seats plus 3 tatami tables seating 4 each.
hours_raw: 11:00-15:00 (Mon, Wed-Sun); closed Tuesdays. Food-ticket system; no reservations.
closed_days: Tuesdays
cultural_comfort_note: A popular but rural counter-and-tatami tsukemen shop run on a ticket-machine and cash-only system with no reservations and no English signage noted, so a little Japanese and familiarity with ordering at a ticket machine smooths the visit.
caveats: Cash only and no reservations; ordering is via a food-ticket machine. Lunch-only hours (11:00-15:00) and closed Tuesdays. Rural location roughly a 13-minute walk from Nagaike Station; the shop is known to draw queues. No dietary/allergen accommodation found, and no English-language service confirmed.

bio.background: Menya Takei opened its original shop in rural Joyo City, south of central Kyoto, in January 2011. The founding chef trained at 中華蕎麦 とみ田 (Chuka Soba Tomita) in Matsudo, Chiba — one of Japan's most celebrated tsukemen houses — and is said to carry that lineage into Kyoto, where the style was relatively rare at the time.
bio.specialty: Thick pork-bone-and-fish (濃厚豚骨魚介) tsukemen served with house-made whole-wheat (全粒粉) extra-thick noodles. The signature special tsukemen layers three kinds of meat — pork belly chashu, pork loin chashu, and smoked chicken — with a soft-boiled egg, brightened by a note of yuzu. After the noodles are finished, the staff dilute the remaining dip with fish dashi to drink as a lighter, refined soup (スープ割り).
bio.philosophy: A focus on a single perfected dish: a concentrated yet balanced fish-and-pork broth paired with chewy whole-wheat noodles, served honestly from a small counter rather than chasing aggressive intensity.

anecdotes.text: The shop is described as carrying the 'DNA' of Chuka Soba Tomita, the chef having trained there before opening in Kyoto.
Despite an out-of-the-way rural setting in Joyo City, the shop draws steady crowds and grew into a small group of locations across Kyoto and Osaka.
After the noodles, staff add fish dashi to the leftover dipping broth, transforming it into an elegant, Japanese-style finishing soup.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The defining noodle is a whole-wheat (全粒粉) extra-thick wheat noodle, and the dipping broth uses a wheat-based shoyu tare, so the signature dish is fundamentally wheat-based.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The broth is a pork-bone and fish stock finished with fish dashi and topped with pork and chicken, so there is unavoidable animal content throughout the kitchen.

cuisine: 豚骨鶏ガララーメン（東龍そば=塩味／福仙楼そば=醤油味の京都北白川系）
gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. Both signature bowls use house-made egg (wheat) noodles, and the 福仙楼そば is shoyu-seasoned (wheat-based soy sauce). Broths are pork-bone and chicken-gara based. No gluten-free menu, no gluten-free noodle substitution, and no allergen handling is mentioned in any source. A celiac diner cannot safely eat the ramen here.
vegan_detail: Not vegan or vegetarian friendly. The 東龍そば broth is built on pork bone, chicken carcass and maple-leaf (chicken feet) gelatin; the 福仙楼そば is a pork-bone/chicken paitan. Toppings include pork-belly chashu and braised tendon/minced pork. Noodles are egg noodles. No plant-based bowl is offered.
address_ja: 京都府京都市左京区北白川上別当町1 第２青山荘 1F6号
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned coordinates for 北白川上別当町1; within the Kyoto bounding box.
neighborhood: 左京区 北白川（茶山・京都芸術大学駅／銀閣寺方面）
seats_text: About 24 seats across counter, tables, and a small terrace.
closed_days: Tuesday and Wednesday
cultural_comfort_note: A casual neighborhood ramen counter where a little Japanese smooths things along; no English service is indicated, and the parking-friendly suburban setting draws mostly local regulars.

bio.background: 東龍 opened in December 2000 in the Kitashirakawa neighborhood of Kyoto's Sakyo Ward. The owner had previously worked as a manager at Karako, a well-known ramen shop in Higashiyama, and brought that pedigree to his own counter on the quiet north-eastern edge of the city.
bio.specialty: The signature bowl is 東龍そば, described as a kind of 'kaiseki in a bowl' — a creamy salt-based broth simmered from pork bone, chicken carcass, chicken-feet gelatin and around ten kinds of vegetables, served with house-made curly egg noodles, Kyotamba-highland pork-belly chashu and Kujo green onion. A second house specialty, 福仙楼そば, is a Taiwanese-street-stall-inspired pork-and-chicken paitan in a shoyu key with slow-braised tendon and minced pork over flatter, thicker egg noodles.
bio.philosophy: An emphasis on hand-made work and fresh ingredients runs through the kitchen, down to the rice — the owner serves Koshihikari grown on his family's farm in Kutsuki.

anecdotes.text: A sister shop, Fukusenro, operated next door from April 2009 until May 2020; when it closed, its Taiwanese-style bowl was folded into the main 東龍 menu, which is why diners can now order both the salt 東龍そば and the shoyu 福仙楼そば under one roof.
The owner studied Taiwanese noodle dishes in order to recreate the street-stall flavors of the 福仙楼そば.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The 福仙楼そば is described as a 醤油味 (soy-sauce-seasoned) bowl; standard ramen shoyu tare is wheat-based.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broths are animal-based (pork bone, chicken carcass, chicken-feet gelatin) and noodles are egg noodles, so the entire ramen line is cooked in shared animal-derived stock.

cuisine: 濃厚魚介豚骨つけ麺
neighborhood: 山科区
gf_detail: Tsukemen specialist. Homemade wheat noodles, fish-and-pork-bone (魚介豚骨) dipping broth typically built on wheat-based shoyu tare, and side dishes finished with soy reduction. No gluten-free menu, dedicated equipment, or allergen accommodation indicated. Not suitable for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: The entire menu is built on a fish-and-pork-bone broth, with pork chashu toppings and butter rice. No plant-based bowls or vegetarian options indicated. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
address_ja: 京都府京都市山科区御陵中内町38-14 都ビル1F
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned [135.80896, 34.99028] for the full street address; within the Kyoto box.
seats_text: 13 seats total: 7 counter seats, one table for 4, and one table for 2. Compact space; queues common at peak times.
closed_days: 水曜日（日曜は夜営業なし）
cultural_comfort_note: A small, queue-heavy local tsukemen counter where ordering and a few words of Japanese go a long way; no English service is indicated.

bio.background: Yumenchu is a much-loved Yamashina tsukemen shop with a comeback story. The original shop near Miryoji closed for roughly three years while the owner recovered from an injury; it reopened on October 1, 2018, in a roomier space near Kyoto Pharmaceutical University, drawing a line of around 50 people on opening day.
bio.specialty: Tsukemen only. The house bowl pairs thick, angular, homemade noodles with a fish-and-pork-bone broth, including a signature charred burnt-oil version that adds aromatic depth. A torch-seared butter rice topped with chashu and a soy reduction is a popular side.
bio.philosophy: Rather than chasing the heaviest possible broth, the shop aims for a cleaner, balanced dip where both the fish and pork-bone umami read clearly, with a touch of acidity and citrus, an approachable style meant to win over tsukemen newcomers as much as devotees.

anecdotes.text: After about three years dark while the owner recovered from injury, the shop returned on October 1, 2018, with roughly 50 people queuing on reopening day.
The dip is described as cleaner and more balanced than a typical heavy tonkotsu tsukemen, framed as friendly to first-time tsukemen eaters.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Tsukemen-only shop: homemade wheat noodles in a fish-and-pork-bone broth with wheat-based soy seasoning typical of the style. No gluten-free handling or separation is described.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Side dishes such as the butter rice are finished with a soy (shoyu) reduction; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat. No tamari/GF soy is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is fish-and-pork-bone based with pork chashu toppings; no plant-based preparation exists, so cross-contact is inherent to the whole menu.

caveats: Owner's personal name not found in sources. Sunday is lunch-only (no dinner). Cash only; no reservations. Geocode resolved to the street address within the Kyoto box.

anecdotes.text: The same concentrated pork-bone-and-chicken broth has been simmered the same way since 1974, before the modern ramen boom; despite its richness it finishes with a surprisingly light, clean aftertaste.

bio.background: Ramen Ginkaku opened in 1974 in Shimotoriba, Fushimi, along National Route 1, and has run for over fifty years.
bio.philosophy: A single unchanging flavor: pork-bone/chicken-collagen shoyu, no miso/shio/seafood variations.
bio.specialty: Rich creamy pork-bone and chicken-collagen broth finished with a soy sauce tare; signature Ramen and Chaashuu-men.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is finished with a wheat-based soy sauce (shoyu) tare; no wheat-free alternative indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Every bowl uses a pork-bone and chicken-collagen broth; the kitchen also handles char siu, egg, and lard.

cuisine: 油そば・まぜそば（アブラソバ専門）
neighborhood: 東山区・清水（清水五条駅）
gf_detail: No gluten-free option. Abura soba is built on wheat (中華麺) noodles tossed in a soy-sauce-based tare (almost always wheat-containing shoyu) with sesame oil; toppings include pork chashu and menma. There is no published gluten-free menu or dedicated handling, and the entire dish is wheat-centric. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The standard bowls use pork chashu, a soy/animal-savory tare, and a popular onsen-egg topping; this is a meat-and-egg abura soba specialist with no advertised vegan or vegetarian version. Confirm directly if you must, but plan to eat elsewhere.
address_ja: 京都府京都市東山区清水4-138
geocode_note: GSI address search returned a single feature for 京都府京都市東山区清水4-138; coordinates within the Kyoto bounding box.
seats_text: 20 seats (about 6 counter, 14 table). Reviewers note a regular portion is filling; it fills up and lines can form at peak tourist hours.
hours_raw: 10:00–20:00 (L.O. 19:45); 不定休 (irregular closures). Note: other sources list 11:00–21:00 closed Wednesdays, and a 10:00–15:00 daily window — hours appear to vary, so confirm before visiting.
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular). Some sources cite Wednesday as the regular closing day; verify before going.
cultural_comfort_note: Sitting in the heart of the Kiyomizu tourist district, the shop is used to international visitors and offers an English menu, so a non-Japanese-speaking diner can order comfortably.
caveats: Operating hours and closed day disagree across sources (Tabelog: 10:00–20:00 / irregular; a local feature: 11:00–21:00 / closed Wed; a directory: 10:00–15:00 / daily). Hours_week reflects Tabelog as the most authoritative current listing but should be treated as approximate. Cash-only per Tabelog (no cards, e-money, or QR). No dietary-accommodation info published.

bio.background: ねこまた bills itself as Kansai's first dedicated abura-soba specialist, tucked into the slope below Kiyomizu-dera on the well-trodden path between Gojo and Shijo along Higashioji-dori. It is the kind of small, serious noodle counter that holds its own amid the souvenir shops and temple crowds, drawing both local regulars and curious travelers.
bio.specialty: The signature is the 新元祖油そば (shin-ganso abura soba) — a brothless bowl where flat, chewy noodles are dressed at the bottom of the bowl with sesame oil and a soy-based tare, then mixed thoroughly with pork chashu, menma, and a generous handful of cold, crisp diced raw onion. Variations include a spicy miso (辛味噌) version and an ebi-shio (shrimp salt) version. Adding an onsen egg and mixing it through turns the bowl carbonara-rich.
bio.philosophy: Eat it the shop's way: dig the tare and oil up from the bottom and toss everything together so the noodles, oil, and toppings bind into one concentrated, savory mouthful — the chilled raw onion is kept cold until service for crunch and contrast.

anecdotes.text: Despite being surrounded by Kiyomizu tourist traffic, it keeps genuine noodle-shop credentials, pulling in both neighborhood enthusiasts and international visitors.
The onsen-egg topping is a fan favorite: mixed into the noodles it turns the bowl into something close to carbonara.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Abura soba is fundamentally a wheat-noodle dish in a soy/wheat-based tare; no gluten-free handling is published.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The tare is described as a soy-sauce (醤油) based seasoning, which in Japan is virtually always wheat-containing shoyu.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Standard bowls include pork chashu and an onsen-egg topping; no vegan/vegetarian version is advertised.

positives.text: English menu available, easing communication for non-Japanese-speaking diners.

lead: ラーメン藤 京都本店
name_ja: ラーメン藤 京都本店
cuisine: 豚骨醤油ラーメン（京都・清湯系／製麺所直営）
neighborhood: 南区 東九条（地下鉄烏丸線 十条駅近く・新十条通沿い）
vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly shop. The signature bowl is built on a pork-bone soy (豚骨醤油) broth with pork toppings; gyoza and pork rice (豚めし) are also pork-based. No plant-based ramen is offered. A vegan diner could not be reliably accommodated here.
gf_detail: Not safe for celiac/gluten-free. As a noodle-factory-direct shop (近藤製麺), the bowls center on wheat ramen noodles, and the soy-based tare/broth almost certainly contains wheat-based shoyu. No gluten-free noodle or menu option is indicated, and there is no allergen-handling information. Should be avoided by anyone requiring strict gluten avoidance.
address_ja: 京都府京都市南区東九条南石田町111-1
geocode_note: GSI address search returned [135.760178, 34.970947] for 京都府京都市南区東九条南石田町111-1; within Kyoto box.
seats_text: 24 seats, counter only (L-shaped counter, open-kitchen Showa-era space)
hours_raw: 10:00-19:00 (closed Thursdays; 3rd Wednesday closes earlier ~17:00). Note: Tabelog lists 10:00-22:00, but the official site and multiple recent blog reports state 10:00-19:00 continuous service — the 19:00 close reflects current operation.
closed_days: Thursdays (3rd Wednesday earlier close ~17:00)
cultural_comfort_note: A no-frills, cash-only neighborhood ramen counter where ordering is done verbally in Japanese; a little Japanese and an easygoing attitude go a long way, and there is no English support to rely on.
caveats: Hours conflict between sources (Tabelog 10:00-22:00 vs official site/blogs 10:00-19:00); the 19:00 close is the more recent, consistent report. Founding year is given as 1972 or 1973 depending on source. Cash only; counter seating fills at peak meal times.

bio.origin: Minami-ku, Kyoto — near Jujo Station along Shin-Jujo-dori
bio.background: A beloved old-guard Kyoto ramen shop founded in the early 1970s (1973, with some sources citing 1972), Ramen Fuji has fed the south-Kyoto neighborhood for over half a century. It is the flagship of a small local family of shops, with branches around the city (Fushimi Ote, Gojo, Fukuchiyama, Kyotanabe).
bio.specialty: A classic Kyoto-style pork-bone-and-soy bowl: a 豚骨醤油 broth in the clear (清湯) idiom — rich yet transparent, savory with a clean soy finish — piled with green onion and bean sprouts over thin, straight noodles. Beyond the standard ramen there is a 特製 (special), a miso version, and a menma bowl, with gyoza and a pork rice bowl (豚めし) on the side.
bio.philosophy: The shop's edge is the noodle itself: it is run as a direct outlet of Kondo Seimen (近藤製麺), the family noodle factory a few minutes' walk away. That factory has long supplied the noodles behind some of Kyoto's most storied ramen names, so the springy, well-cut house noodle is the heart of every bowl here.

anecdotes.text: Ramen Fuji is the direct retail face of Kondo Seimen, the Kyoto noodle maker said to supply heavyweights of the local scene such as Daiichi Asahi and Shinpuku Saikan — so the noodles are made by people who have shaped Kyoto ramen for decades.
The room keeps a thick Showa-era mood: an L-shaped wooden counter, neon signage, and an open kitchen you order at out loud — a working-class Kyoto ramen counter rather than a polished modern shop.
A playful local tradition: on days when the rain probability is high, the shop has offered free noodle refills (kaedama) — a small wink to its neighborhood regulars.

gf_cross_contamination.text: A noodle-factory-direct shop built around wheat ramen noodles; flour is central to the operation and cross-contact with wheat is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is a soy-based 豚骨醤油 tare; standard Japanese shoyu contains wheat, so the soup base should be assumed to contain wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth and toppings are pork-based (pork-bone soy broth, pork rice, gyoza); no plant-based options noted.

lead: ラーメンとまぜそばのお店 Lee
name_ja: ラーメンとまぜそばのお店 Lee
name_en: Ramen to Mazesoba no Omise Lee
cuisine: まぜそば・カレーまぜそば・煮干し中華そば
neighborhood: 山科区 椥辻（地下鉄東西線 椥辻駅 / 外環状線沿い）
address_ja: 京都府京都市山科区椥辻草海道町10-1 シャレー山科 1F
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned the first feature at lng 135.815048, lat 34.973576 for 京都府京都市山科区椥辻草海道町10-1 — within the Kyoto bounding box and consistent with the Natsutsuji/Aeon Town Yamashina area along the gaikanjō ring road.
seats_text: 11 counter seats
closed_days: Mondays
cultural_comfort_note: A small 11-seat counter ramen shop in a residential Yamashina neighborhood; expect Japanese-only ordering and menu, so a translation app or pointing helps.
gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The core menu is wheat ramen and mazesoba noodles (sourced from Kyoto noodle maker Menya Teigaku / 棣鄂) served with wheat-based shoyu and curry sauces. No gluten-free noodle or dedicated preparation is advertised, and a busy single-counter kitchen means heavy shared-equipment cross-contact. There is no indication staff can accommodate a gluten allergy.
vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian options. Ramen broths are chicken and pork-bone (and chicken-seafood) based; mazesoba and the keema curry use ground pork/meat, homemade bacon, egg yolk, cheese and pork back fat. There is no plant-based bowl on the menu.
caveats: Hours and the no-reservation/card-accepted policy are from aggregator listings and may change; the shop posts current status and sold-out items (curry-based mazesoba sometimes sells out) on its Instagram. No allergen or dietary accommodation information is published, so any GF/vegan need should be confirmed directly and treated as unsupported.

bio.background: Lee started life in May 2021 as 'Curry Shop Lee,' a curry-focused spot on the gaikanjō ring road in the Natsutsuji neighborhood of Yamashina, near Aeon Town. Over time the kitchen leaned more and more into noodles, and in May 2024 it fully rebranded into a ramen-and-mazesoba shop while keeping its curry roots front and center.
bio.specialty: The signature bowl is the keema curry mazesoba: springy Y-shaped 'wing' noodles from the long-established Kyoto maker Menya Teigaku (棣鄂) tossed with a layered keema curry, finished with egg yolk, cheese, house-made bacon, pork back fat, tomato and parsley. Staff ask each guest about garlic before serving it. Alongside it sit a gyokai-shoyu mazesoba and a Taiwan mazesoba, plus three ramen styles: tori-niboshi (chicken-sardine), tori-tonkotsu (rich chicken and pork bone), and gyokai tori-tonkotsu.
bio.philosophy: Curry expertise carried into the noodle bowl — the keema curry is built to be spice-forward but balanced, 'spicy without being overwhelming,' reflecting the shop's years as a curry kitchen. The clean, café-like counter room is a deliberate departure from the typical ramen-shop feel.

anecdotes.text: The keema curry mazesoba layers egg yolk, cheese, house-made bacon, pork back fat, tomato and parsley over Teigaku's grippy 'wing' noodles, and the counter asks your garlic preference before it goes out.

Lee began as 'Curry Shop Lee' in May 2021 and completed its turn into a dedicated ramen-and-mazesoba shop in May 2024; the curry-based mazesoba is popular enough that it can sell out, leaving only the chilled and niboshi bowls late in service.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen and mazesoba noodles from Kyoto maker Menya Teigaku (棣鄂) are the base of every bowl, cooked in a shared single-counter kitchen, so cross-contact with gluten is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Bowls use wheat-based shoyu/curry sauces (e.g. gyokai-shoyu mazesoba and shoyu-seasoned ramen), which contain wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths are chicken/pork-bone based and mazesoba/keema use ground meat, house-made bacon, egg yolk, cheese and pork back fat; no plant-based preparation exists.

cuisine: 濃厚豚骨ラーメン（超濃厚系）
gf_detail: This is a rich tonkotsu ramen shop built on wheat noodles, almost certainly a wheat-based shoyu/seasoning tare, and a long-simmered pork-bone broth. There is no indication of any gluten-free noodle or preparation, and no allergy accommodation is mentioned anywhere. It should be treated as not safe for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
vegan_detail: The entire concept is an animal-based pork-bone (tonkotsu) broth simmered for hours from roughly 300 kg of bones daily, topped with chashu pork. There are no vegetarian or vegan bowls; this place is not suitable for plant-based diners.
address_ja: 京都府木津川市梅谷髯谷15-3
geocode_note: GSI geocoding resolves the address to lng 135.8449, lat 34.7215 (chō-level: lng 135.8491, lat 34.7151). This is the genuine, correct location of the shop in Kizugawa City in far-southern Kyoto Prefecture near the Nara border, but it falls outside the expected Kyoto validation box (lat 34.93–35.13, lng 135.66–135.83), which does not cover this southern part of the prefecture. Coordinates nulled per instructions; true position is approximately lat 34.72, lng 135.84.
seats_text: 20 seats total (12 counter, 8 at tables)
hours_raw: Tuesday–Sunday 11:00–15:00 and 18:00–23:00; closed Monday (open if Monday is a public holiday).
closed_days: Monday (open when Monday falls on a public holiday)
cultural_comfort_note: A no-frills, locally-loved tonkotsu specialist in a rural setting with a ticket-machine ordering system; basic Japanese (or pointing at the ticket machine) will get you through, and English support should not be assumed.

bio.origin: Kizugawa City, in the far south of Kyoto Prefecture near the Nara border
bio.background: Founded in 1998, this log-cabin-style shop on the Kyoto–Nara border grew from a single rural ramen stand into the flagship (sohonten) of a tonkotsu brand that now has branches across Nara, Osaka and beyond. Despite being well off the beaten path several kilometers from JR Kizu Station, it draws regular queues of devotees who make the trip specifically for the bowl.
bio.specialty: An intensely rich tonkotsu ramen. The kitchen simmers only domestic pork bones (genkotsu knuckle and back bones, primarily from Miyazaki) with nothing but water, boiling roughly 300 kg of bones a day until the marrow dissolves completely. The signature is the tonkotsu chashu ramen, and the broth is offered in different density levels so diners can dial in how heavy they want it; thinner kaedama noodle refills let you finish the collagen-thick soup.
bio.philosophy: Purity and patience over shortcuts: no lard or added oil is used, so the soup, for all its visual heaviness, finishes with a surprising sweetness and is less punishing than it looks. The whole bowl is built on the conviction that long-simmered bone-and-water broth needs no embellishment.

anecdotes.text: Roughly 300 kg of pork bones are boiled every day until the marrow fully melts into the broth, and the kitchen deliberately adds no lard or oil, which is why the finish reads as sweet and clean rather than greasy.
The original shop sits in a remote spot several kilometers from the nearest station yet still pulls steady lines of customers who come purely for the tonkotsu.

caveats: This is a wheat-noodle, pork-bone tonkotsu specialist with no published allergy, gluten-free, or vegan information; it is unsuitable for celiac and plant-based diners. Cash only, ticket-machine ordering, no reservations (first-come basis), and a rural location far from the station. Note it is the flagship of a multi-location chain rather than a single independent shop. Coordinates were nulled because the verified address lies just south of the expected Kyoto bounding box.

cuisine: 鶏清湯・醤油／塩ラーメン
neighborhood: 福知山駅前（京都府北部）
gf_detail: A wheat-noodle ramen shop. The signature bowls use thick house-made noodles blended from whole-grain and regular wheat flour, and the broth/tare are chicken-and-pork based in the standard ramen style. There is no indication of gluten-free noodles, separate preparation, or celiac accommodation, and ramen here should be considered unsafe for anyone needing strict gluten avoidance.
vegan_detail: The broth is built on local-chicken clear stock plus pork bones (and bonito/katsuo aromatics), and bowls feature chashu pork, wonton, and egg. There are no vegan or vegetarian ramen options noted; this is an animal-broth shop.
address_ja: 京都府福知山市駅前町24
geocode_note: Geocoded from GSI to 35.298962, 135.119186. This is correct for Fukuchiyama in northern Kyoto Prefecture but falls outside the provided Kyoto-city bounding box (lat 34.93–35.13, lng 135.66–135.83), which only covers the Kyoto city area, not the prefecture's north. Coordinates retained as accurate for the actual location.
seats_text: About 16 seats: a counter (roughly 10 seats) plus small table sections (a 4-seat and a 2-seat).
hours_raw: 11:00-13:45 (lunch only). Open Mon, Fri, Sat, Sun and holidays; closed Tue/Wed/Thu. Irregular closures occur — owner posts on blog/social before visiting.
closed_days: Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday (plus occasional irregular closures)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, busy local counter ramen shop in a regional town; expect Japanese-only ordering and a simple point-or-name-the-bowl approach.

bio.origin: Fukuchiyama, northern Kyoto Prefecture (the owner is a local of the area)
bio.background: The owner is a Fukuchiyama native who previously ran a vintage-clothing store before opening this ramen shop in 2013. That second-hand-clothing sensibility carries over into the shop's bold, slightly Americana feel — including the name. 'Somie's' is an English-flavored rendering of his wife's name, Sonomi (そのみ).
bio.specialty: Chicken clear-soup (鶏清湯) ramen served with no MSG and no yeast extract. The kitchen builds a salt-based foundation layered with local-chicken umami and bonito (katsuo) aromatics, and pairs it with thick, wide, chewy house-made noodles (described as 一反麺) that use a mochi-wheat blend for a springy bite. The showpiece is the loaded 'Super Special Ramen', topped with two styles of chashu (seared and rare), wonton, seasoned egg, scallion and bamboo shoots, often served in a striking blue patterned bowl.
bio.philosophy: An additive-free approach — no chemical seasoning and no yeast extract — relying on time-intensive chicken-and-pork stock and house-milled noodle flour (a blend including whole-grain plus flour from Nagano and Hokkaido) to carry the flavor.

anecdotes.text: Open since 2013, the shop has been named to Tabelog's ramen Top 100 (百名店) multiple times and took the top prize at the 2024 Kansai Ramen Grand Prix, making it a standout destination in the Fukuchiyama ramen scene.
The shop name 'Somie's' is an Americanized spin on the owner's wife's name, Sonomi (そのみ) — a personal touch reflecting the owner's vintage-clothing-shop past.

gf_cross_contamination.text: House-made wheat noodles using a whole-grain/wheat-flour blend are central to every bowl; no gluten-free option exists and the shared ramen kitchen makes cross-contamination unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Bowls include shoyu (soy-sauce) style ramen; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, and no gluten-free tare is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is chicken clear-soup plus pork bones with bonito (katsuo) aromatics, and toppings include pork chashu, wonton and egg — no vegan preparation is available.

positives.text: The kitchen explicitly avoids chemical seasoning (MSG) and yeast extract, building flavor from chicken-and-pork stock cooked over time.

caveats: Lunch-only, very limited hours (about 11:00–13:45) on open days, and irregular closures beyond the fixed Tue/Wed/Thu — confirm via the shop's blog/social before going. Cash only. No reservations; expect to queue. Confidence high on identity/operation and bowl style; seat count and exact hours per Tabelog/blog, treat exact daily times as approximate.

cuisine: 鶏ベースの清湯醤油・塩らぁ麺（ラーメン）
gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The bowls are built on house-made wheat noodles (blends such as Haruyokoi and Kitahonami) and a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare, which is brewed from wheat. There is no gluten-free noodle or tare option and no indication of allergen accommodation, so a strict gluten-free or celiac diner cannot safely eat here.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The signature broths are chicken-based (clear chicken shoyu/shio, plus a chicken-paitan variant), finished with chashu pork, wonton, and similar animal toppings. There is no vegan or vegetarian bowl on the menu.
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区東洞院錦小路下ル阪東屋町653-1
geocode_note: Geocoded from 京都市中京区阪東屋町653-1 via GSI; result (lat 35.004517, lng 135.761276) is within the Kyoto box and matches the Shijo-Karasuma / Higashinotoin-Nishikikoji block near Karasuma Station.
neighborhood: 四条烏丸（中京区・烏丸駅近く）
seats_text: About 16 counter seats only (high counter and low counter); no tables.
hours_raw: 11:00〜15:00 / 17:30〜21:30（火曜定休）。営業日程の変更はInstagram・Twitterで告知。
closed_days: 火曜定休（Tuesdays）
cultural_comfort_note: A quiet, refined counter ramen-ya in central Kyoto that draws international visitors; staff are gracious but the room is calm and Japanese-first, so a friendly greeting and pointing at the menu go a long way.
caveats: Lunch and dinner run as separate services (roughly 11:00-15:00 and 17:30-21:30) with a mid-afternoon break; the dinner start time is listed as 17:30 on Japanese sources and 18:00 on Tabelog, so confirm on the day. One English visitor account also reported Wednesday closures in addition to Tuesday, but Japanese listings show Tuesday only — verify via the shop's Instagram. This is a chicken clear-shoyu/shio specialist; despite the prompt's "まぜそば系" tag, no mazesoba was found on the menu.

bio.origin: An offshoot of the celebrated Sapporo shop Japanese Ramen Noodle Lab Q, brought to Kyoto as the brand's first Kansai venture; the Shijo-Karasuma shop opened on April 9, 2022.
bio.background: Producer Hiroshi Hiraoka built his reputation in Sapporo with Lab Q, known for crystal-clear, multi-layered broths. He travels around Japan to meet producers in person and source ingredients, then works out the flavor pairings and techniques himself before they reach the bowl.
bio.specialty: Clear chicken shoyu ramen (醤油らぁ麺) built from prized Japanese poultry such as Hinai-jidori (Akita) and Nagoya Kochin (Aichi), alongside a salt (shio) version and a limited chicken-paitan; bowls come plain or dressed up with wonton or chashu, on fine house-made wheat noodles that stay firm.
bio.philosophy: Summed up in the shop's motto 温故知新・日本の食材・醸造文化・伝統を丼の中に — honoring the old to create the new by gathering Japanese ingredients and the country's fermentation (brewing) culture into a single bowl, a modern reconstruction of classic Japanese shoyu ramen.

anecdotes.text: Hiraoka's stated wish was to 'gather ingredients from all over Japan and make the very best ramen possible,' which is why he personally visits producers nationwide rather than buying off a supplier list.
The dining room is deliberately calm — one craftsman works the line with a couple of helpers, and staff even see guests to the door — so the focus stays on the bowl rather than bustle.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Bowls are served on house-made wheat noodles (wheat blends such as Haruyokoi and Kitahonami), so wheat is central to the kitchen and there is no gluten-free preparation.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature tare is a soy-sauce (shoyu) base; Japanese shoyu is brewed from wheat, so the broth itself contains gluten.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths are chicken-based (clear chicken shoyu/shio and a chicken-paitan), with pork chashu and wonton among the toppings; the kitchen is built around animal products with no vegan option.

lead: 麺屋 やまひで 京都聖護院店
name_ja: 麺屋 やまひで 京都聖護院店
cuisine: 台湾まぜそば（ラーメン・つけ麺）
neighborhood: 神宮丸太町駅／聖護院
gf_detail: This is a Taiwanese maze-soba (brothless mixed noodle) specialist built on house-made medium-thick wheat noodles, finished with a savory tare and spicy pork mince — all classic wheat-and-soy components. No gluten-free menu, gluten-free noodles, or allergen accommodation is mentioned on the official site, the shop's listings, or any review. Not suitable for celiacs; assume heavy gluten throughout.
vegan_detail: The core dishes are animal-based: the signature 台湾まぜそば is topped with spicy minced pork and raw egg yolk, and the other house specialty is a chicken paitan (鶏白湯) ramen. No vegan or vegetarian option is advertised anywhere. Not a place a vegan can eat.
address_ja: 京都府京都市左京区聖護院西町3-2
geocode_note: GSI address search on 京都府京都市左京区聖護院西町3-2 returned the first feature at [135.779205, 35.018944], within the Kyoto box.
seats_text: Seat count not published; a small counter/table shop occupying a converted former salon space in a residential apartment building.
closed_days: Wednesday
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, walk-in-only noodle counter where ordering is simple and a few friendly words of Japanese go a long way; it sits in a guesthouse-heavy pocket near Kumano Shrine so foreign diners are not unusual.
caveats: Cash only and no reservations — come prepared to pay in yen and to wait at peak meal times. Closed Wednesdays. No published allergen handling, so anyone with celiac disease or a strict vegan diet should not rely on this shop.

bio.background: An offshoot of a Nara maze-soba house, this Kyoto outpost opened on November 26, 2022 in the quiet Shogoin neighborhood near Kumano Shrine and Kyoto University Hospital, a short walk from Jingu-Marutamachi Station. It took over a former beauty salon in a residential apartment block, and the room keeps that pale-wood, almost cafe-like calm rather than the usual steamy ramen-shop bustle.
bio.origin: A branch of Menya Yamahide, whose main shop is in Nara.
bio.specialty: Taiwanese maze-soba (brothless mixed noodles): house-made medium-thick straight noodles deliberately scuffed as they boil so the surface turns glossy and clings to the sauce, then piled with spicy minced pork, raw egg yolk, garlic, nori and bonito for a punchy, savory-spicy mix you stir together yourself — with a scoop of rice to chase the leftover sauce at the end. A chicken-paitan shio ramen and a Kyoto-only chuka soba round out the board.
bio.philosophy: A noodle-first shop: the kitchen makes its own noodles and treats the texture and how the sauce grips them as the whole point of maze-soba.

anecdotes.text: The shop occupies a converted former hair salon in an apartment building, giving it an unusually bright, white-wood, cafe-like feel for a ramen counter, and it draws both neighborhood regulars and travelers staying at nearby guesthouses.
The medium-thick noodles are scratched at the surface while boiling so they turn silky and hold the heavy maze-soba sauce, served at around 200g per bowl.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Maze-soba tare is built on a soy-sauce-based savory seasoning, which in Japan is overwhelmingly brewed with wheat; no wheat-free tare is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Signature bowl is topped with spicy minced pork and raw egg yolk, and the alternate house bowl is a chicken paitan; the kitchen is animal-broth and meat based throughout.

lead: つけ麺 京都わたなべ
name_ja: つけ麺 京都わたなべ
cuisine: つけ麺（豚鶏骨＋魚介ベースの選べるスープ・麺のつけ麺専門店）
neighborhood: 右京区・西院（阪急西院駅 徒歩1分）
gf_detail: A wheat-noodle tsukemen specialist. The dipping broths are built on a pork-chicken bone and seafood base (the five options include rich seafood, vegetable-potato, spicy minced meat, curry, and shrimp), and tsukemen tare almost always contains wheat-based soy sauce. There is no dedicated gluten-free menu, gluten-free noodles, or stated allergen handling, so this is not suitable for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan or vegetarian establishment. All broths derive from a pork-chicken bone stock with seafood umami, and even the vegetable-potato (ベジポタ) option is built on the same animal base. No vegan or vegetarian bowl is offered.
address_ja: 京都府京都市右京区西院高山寺町12-11
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned coordinates within the Kyoto box for 京都市右京区西院高山寺町12-11.
seats_text: 11 seats (7 counter, 4 table)
closed_days: Mondays
cultural_comfort_note: A small, locally beloved counter-and-table tsukemen shop on a backstreet by the station; English support is not advertised, so a little Japanese or pointing at the broth/noodle choices helps.

bio.background: A tsukemen specialist tucked on a backstreet a one-minute walk from Hankyu Saiin Station. The shop has long roots in the Saiin neighborhood, where it formerly operated under the name Watanabe Seimen (渡辺製麺) before becoming Tsukemen Kyoto Watanabe.
bio.specialty: Choose-your-own tsukemen: five dipping broths built on a pork-chicken bone and seafood base — rich seafood (濃厚魚介), vegetable-potato (ベジポタ), spicy minced meat (辛肉ミンチ), curry (カレー), and shrimp (えび) — paired with a choice of three noodle styles: thick, medium-thick, and flat thick.
bio.philosophy: A 'new-sensation' tsukemen format where the diner customizes broth and noodle to taste, served continuously from late morning through the evening.

anecdotes.text: At lunch the shop offers a complimentary small bowl of rice (一口ライス) so diners can finish their leftover dipping broth risotto-style.
The counter is lined with autographed cards from entertainers, a mark of the shop's local fame.
Formerly known as Watanabe Seimen, the shop has been a familiar fixture in the Saiin area for years.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Tsukemen dipping tare is conventionally based on wheat-containing soy sauce; no allergen breakdown is published for this shop.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All five broths share a pork-chicken bone and seafood base, including the vegetable-potato option, so no broth is free of animal products.

caveats: No official website found; details drawn from Tabelog and Kyoto food blogs. No allergen, gluten-free, or vegan/vegetarian handling is published, so diners with celiac disease or strict plant-based needs should avoid this shop. Cash plus QR (PayPay) accepted; cards and e-money not accepted.

cuisine: Sichuan Chinese / town-Chinese (汁なし担々麺・四川料理)
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The house specialty 汁なし担々麺 (broth-less tantanmen) is built on wheat noodles — here unusually made with thin udon from Kagawa, which is also wheat — and the Sichuan sauces and town-Chinese dishes lean on wheat-based soy sauce. No gluten-free menu, dedicated prep, or celiac accommodation is indicated. A celiac diner should treat this place as unsafe.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly destination. This is a meat-forward Sichuan/town-Chinese kitchen: the signature bowls and rice dishes center on pork and beef (tantanmen, mapo tofu with beef, the teridon pork dish, yodare-dori chicken), and broths/sauces are animal-based. No vegan or vegetarian options are advertised, and none were found in available sources.
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区西ノ京星池町149
geocode_note: GSI address search resolved to lng/lat [135.74054, 35.012348], within the Kyoto bounding box.
neighborhood: 中京区・西ノ京 / 二条 (near JR & subway Nijō Station)
seats_text: About 30 seats, a mix of table sizes; a small neighborhood Chinese restaurant that draws lunch lines.
closed_days: Tuesdays
cultural_comfort_note: A busy local Sichuan-Chinese spot where ordering is in Japanese and dietary tailoring beyond choosing your dish should not be assumed; basic Japanese or a translation app will smooth the visit.

bio.background: Founded in 1974, Taiho is a multi-generational family kitchen tucked into a residential pocket by Nijō Station. The first generation built it as a beloved neighborhood Chinese diner; the second generation layered on the Sichuan technique they trained in, so the menu reads as a conversation between old-school town-Chinese comfort food and sharper Sichuan heat.
bio.specialty: The signature bowl is the 汁なし担々麺 (broth-less tantanmen), distinctive for its base of thin udon from Kagawa rather than ordinary ramen noodles, dressed in Sichuan chili and tongue-tingling sansho pepper. The kitchen says it nudges the aroma and spice level by season. Other house favorites include a beef mapo tofu heavy on sansho and the original 'teridon' pork-over-shredded-egg rice dish.
bio.philosophy: A stated motto of evolving day by day, with seasonal adjustments to spice and aroma rather than a fixed recipe.

anecdotes.text: In Kyoto the phrase goes that when you say tantanmen, you mean Taiho; the lunch counter regularly draws a line.
The tantanmen famously uses thin Kagawa udon as its noodle, an unconventional choice, with the aroma and heat fine-tuned by season.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat is unavoidable here: the signature broth-less tantanmen is built on wheat udon noodles, and town-Chinese dishes use wheat-based soy and sauces.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Sichuan and town-Chinese cooking at a shop like this relies on standard (wheat-containing) soy sauce and chili/bean sauces; no wheat-free tare or sauce is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen centers on pork, beef and chicken; broths and sauces are animal-based, so cross-contact with meat is pervasive and no vegan prep is offered.

caveats: Payment sources conflict: Tabelog lists card accepted with a ¥12,000 minimum, while another listing says cash only (カード不可); marked cash_only=true to be safe — confirm at the shop. The romanization name in romaji is "Taiho" (full sign reads 中國菜 大鵬). Reservations are accepted (予約可) but not stated as required; lunch lines are common. The 汁なし担々麺 (broth-less tantanmen) is a defining dish, though some listings also note a soup tantanmen — the kitchen is best known for the broth-less version made with Kagawa thin udon.

lead: 中国四川料理 駱駝
name_ja: 中国四川料理 駱駝
cuisine: Sichuan Chinese; tantanmen and soupless tantanmen (汁なし担々麺), mapo tofu
neighborhood: Sakyo-ku, Kitashirakawa / Chayama (near Kyoto University of the Arts, Chayama-Kyoto Geijutsu Daigaku Station)
address_ja: 京都府京都市左京区北白川瀬ノ内町27-4
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned coordinates [135.790482, 35.03643] for the full address; within Kyoto box.
seats_text: 13 seats: 9 counter seats plus one large table (can accommodate up to ~20 for a private booking)
hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-13:45 (last order); Dinner 17:30-20:45 (last order). Closed Mondays (open if holiday) and 1st & 3rd Tuesdays (open if holiday).
closed_days: Mondays (open if a public holiday); also closed 1st and 3rd Tuesdays (open if a public holiday)
cultural_comfort_note: A small neighborhood Sichuan counter where Japanese is the working language; a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu go a long way.
gf_detail: This is a Sichuan noodle and stir-fry house: the tantanmen and soupless tantanmen are wheat noodles, and Sichuan staples lean on doubanjiang (broad-bean/chili paste), tianmianjiang (sweet wheat-flour paste) and soy-based seasonings that typically contain wheat. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented; not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Core dishes are built on pork mince, dried shrimp, scallop and animal-based broths (tantanmen mince blends scallop, dried shrimp, sweet miso and pork; mapo tofu and yun-bai-rou are meat dishes). No vegan or vegetarian menu is documented; not a plant-based-friendly destination.

bio.background: A tiny, long-running Sichuan counter tucked into the Kitashirakawa/Chayama neighborhood beside Kyoto University of the Arts. The owner-chef trained in Yokohama's Chinatown before opening Rakuda, which has been serving authentic Sichuan food in the same spot for well over fifteen years.
bio.origin: Kyoto (Sakyo-ku); cuisine rooted in Sichuan, China, by way of Yokohama Chinatown training.
bio.specialty: Tantanmen and the soupless tantanmen (汁なし担々麺) drenched in fragrant chili oil, plus a benchmark mapo tofu made with local Kyoto tofu, Sichuan peppercorn and doubanjiang. The yun-bai-rou (雲白肉, 'cloud white meat') is the most popular appetizer.
bio.philosophy: Cooking real, unapologetically numbing-and-spicy Sichuan food in an intimate room rather than chasing a crowd — the kind of place enthusiasts mean when they say 'in Kyoto, there is Rakuda.'

anecdotes.text: Among Sichuan-food lovers there is a saying, '京都に駱駝あり' ('In Kyoto, there is Rakuda'), nodding to its reputation as a destination for the real thing.
The owner trained in Yokohama's Chinatown before opening Rakuda, which has run for around 17 years.

The soupless tantanmen uses chili oil as its tare, with the star-anise aroma of genuine Sichuan cooking clinging to every noodle; the soup tantanmen runs intensely hot, its mince layered with scallop, dried shrimp, pickled mustard greens, sweet miso, star anise, cinnamon and peanuts.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Sichuan seasonings used here (doubanjiang, sweet wheat-flour paste, soy-based sauces) typically contain wheat, and the tantanmen noodles are wheat; assume wheat throughout.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes are built on pork, dried shrimp and scallop; the tantanmen mince and broths are animal-based.

caveats: Official site (kyoto-rakuda.com) is reachable only over HTTP; the HTTPS upgrade fails with a TLS certificate-name mismatch, so its menu/allergy pages could not be fetched directly. Hours and seating confirmed via Tabelog (last orders ~13:45 lunch / ~20:45 dinner); one blog lists slightly later closing (14:00/21:00) — treat exact last-order times as approximate. No allergy, gluten-free, or vegan accommodation is documented anywhere; GF/vegan statuses are based on the inherent ingredients of Sichuan noodle cooking, not on a stated policy.

lead: 中華そば らんたん
name_ja: 中華そば らんたん
cuisine: 中華そば（淡麗）・鰹昆布水つけ麺
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The ramen uses custom wheat noodles made with whole-grain flour (全粒粉入りの特注麺), and the signature 鰹昆布水つけ麺 noodles incorporate tapioca but are still wheat-based. Broths are chicken/bonito/dried-sardine (鶏・鰹・煮干) and the tare is aged soy sauce (熟成醤油), which is conventionally wheat-fermented. There is no indication of any gluten-free preparation, dedicated equipment, or allergen accommodation. A celiac diner should not eat here.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. Every bowl is built on animal-derived dashi — chicken, bonito (鰹), and dried sardines (煮干) — and menu items feature duck fat, shellfish dashi (貝ダシ), and egg-based dishes such as TKM and the "まんげつ濃厚卵." There is no vegan or vegetable-broth option noted.
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区木屋町通三条上る上大阪町528 ユリカビル 1F
