geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to the chome/banchi level (上大阪町528); first feature coordinates [135.766159, 35.014957] fall within the Kyoto bounding box.
neighborhood: 中京区・三条木屋町（三条駅／木屋町三条上ル）
seats_text: 14席（カウンター・テーブル）。和食店のような落ち着いた奥行きのある店内。
closed_days: 年中無休（定休日なし、祝日も営業）
cultural_comfort_note: A small 14-seat counter-style ramen shop near Sanjo-Kiyamachi where a friendly greeting in Japanese goes a long way, though the simple point-and-order format makes it manageable for visitors with little Japanese.
caveats: Owner/chef personal background and the meaning of the name "らんたん" (lantern) were not stated in any fetched source, so the biography is limited to verifiable facts about the food and concept. Allergen and cross-contamination details are not published; the dietary assessment is inferred from the documented wheat noodles, soy-sauce tare, and animal-based broths.

bio.background: 中華そば らんたんは2024年5月31日に三条木屋町（木屋町通三条上ル）にオープンした淡麗系の中華そば専門店です。和食店を思わせる落ち着いた奥行きのある店内で、ラーメンというより一杯の出汁料理として味わえる一品を目指しています。
bio.origin: 京都・三条木屋町（三条駅近く、木屋町三条上ル）
bio.specialty: 看板は鰹昆布水つけそば。北海道産がごめ昆布と土佐清水の鰹からとった昆布水に全粒粉入りの特注中細麺を浸し、熟成醤油と国産鴨脂を合わせたつけ汁で味わう。レギュラーの熟成醤油中華そば（900円）は国産鶏・鰹・煮干から引いたクリアで力強いスープに柚子が香る淡麗系の一杯。
bio.philosophy: 「わざわざ食べに行きたいお店」として、味のクオリティーだけでなくおもてなしと高級感のある趣を大切にしている。秋田県大潟村直送のあきたこまちや濃厚な『まんげつ濃厚卵』など素材にもこだわる。

anecdotes.text: 全粒粉入りの特注麺は中細でツルっとした食感とすすり心地のよさが特徴。
鰹昆布水つけそばは、北海道産がごめ昆布と土佐清水産の鰹でとった昆布水につけ、熟成醤油と国産鴨脂のつけ汁で食べる京都ではまだ貴重な昆布水つけ麺。
国産鶏と鰹・煮干から抽出されるクリアながらも力強い味わい、秋田県大潟村直送のあきたこまち、濃厚な『まんげつ濃厚卵』を使用。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Custom wheat noodles made with whole-grain flour (全粒粉入りの特注麺) are the core of every bowl; the kitchen is a wheat-noodle ramen operation with no gluten-free handling noted.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature ramen is built on an aged soy sauce tare (熟成醤油 中華そば), and the tsukemen dipping sauce uses aged soy sauce (熟成醤油) — conventionally wheat-fermented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broths are animal-based (国産鶏・鰹・煮干 — chicken, bonito, dried sardine); the tsukemen uses 国産鴨脂 (domestic duck fat) and shellfish dashi (貝ダシ) and egg dishes appear on the menu. No vegetable broth offered.

lead: 京都二条 麺屋 啜る
name_ja: 京都二条 麺屋 啜る
cuisine: 煮干し・中華そば（鶏清湯＋鰹節・煮干し・昆布の和出汁を合わせた熟成醤油らぁ麺。鶏白湯、胡麻担々、A5和牛らぁ麺に加え、ヴィーガンらぁ麺も用意）
gf_detail: 小麦の中華麺と熟成醤油（小麦由来）のかえしが基本のラーメン専門店で、グルテンフリー対応の表示は確認できません。スープも鶏・鰹節・煮干しなどの和出汁が中心で、つけ麺・担々など全般に小麦麺を使用。セリアック／グルテン不耐の方には安全に提供できる一杯は確認できず、原則として不可と考えてください。
vegan_detail: 動物性不使用をうたう専用メニューとして「ヴィーガン白湯らぁ麺（豆乳と香味野菜のスープ、トッピングにがんもどき・赤水菜／約1,200円）」と「ヴィーガン担々らぁ麺（約1,250円）」の2品を常設。ただし同じ厨房で鶏清湯・煮干し・A5和牛・豚餃子など動物性メニューを大量に調理しており、麺の茹で湯・調理器具の共用や交差接触の管理についての明記は確認できません。プラントベースとして注文できますが、厳格なヴィーガン／重度アレルギーの場合は来店時に必ず確認を。
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区聚楽廻南町1-9 ルミエール二条 1F-105
geocode_note: GSI住所検索で聚楽廻南町1-9の座標を取得（lat 35.01487, lng 135.741852）。京都ボックス内で妥当。
neighborhood: 中京区・二条（聚楽廻南町／JR二条駅1番出口 徒歩約3分）
seats_text: 19席（カウンター＋テーブル）。公式プレスリリースは19席、グルメ媒体では15席表記もあり、いずれにせよ小規模店。
closed_days: 定休日なし（無休）。ただし媒体により不定休との記載もあり、来店前に確認推奨。
cultural_comfort_note: 「世界と京都をつなぐ」をテーマに観光客・訪日客も意識した新店で、英語での来店にも比較的対応しやすい雰囲気。

bio.background: 2025年10月16日、JR二条駅からほど近い聚楽廻南町に開業した新しいラーメン専門店。名古屋に本社を置く株式会社エフリード（代表・足立諒太、2019年設立）が手がけ、『世界と京都をつなぐラーメン』を掲げてオープンしました。
bio.specialty: 看板は『熟成醤油らぁ麺』。国産鶏からていねいに引いた澄んだ鶏清湯に、鰹節・煮干し・昆布の和出汁を重ね、まろやかな熟成醤油のかえしを合わせた一杯。クリアでありながら奥行きのある旨味と、熟成醤油の豊かな香りが身上です。
bio.philosophy: 『伝統と革新』がコンセプト。日々の一杯を求める地元客、思い出の一食を求める旅行者、本物の日本文化に触れたい訪日客の三者すべてに応えることを目指しています。

anecdotes.text: 看板の熟成醤油らぁ麺に加え、濃厚鶏白湯、自家製胡麻だれと香味油の胡麻担々、A5ランクの和牛ローストビーフをのせた『A5和牛らぁ麺』まで、幅広い一杯を揃える。さらに動物性不使用の『ヴィーガン白湯らぁ麺』『ヴィーガン担々らぁ麺』も常設し、プラントベースの来店者にも対応する。
開業を記念し、10月16〜17日にはInstagramフォロワー向けに看板の熟成醤油らぁ麺を通常900円のところ500円で提供するなど、地域とSNSを通じたオープン企画も実施した。

gf_cross_contamination.text: 中華麺（小麦）と熟成醤油のかえしが基本で、グルテンフリー麺や専用調理ラインの記載は確認できない。小麦の交差接触は避けられないと考えるのが安全。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: 看板メニューは『熟成醤油』のかえしを用いる。一般的な醸造醤油は小麦を含むため、醤油由来の小麦混入を前提に判断するのが妥当。

vegan_cross_contact.text: 『ヴィーガン白湯』『ヴィーガン担々』は動物性不使用をうたう専用メニューだが、同一厨房で鶏清湯・煮干し・A5和牛・豚餃子などを調理しており、麺の茹で湯や調理器具の共用、交差接触の管理についての明記はない。

positives.text: 豆乳と香味野菜でつくる動物性不使用のヴィーガン白湯らぁ麺（トッピングはがんもどき・赤水菜）と、ヴィーガン担々らぁ麺を常設しており、プラントベースの一杯を注文できる。

caveats: 2025年10月16日開業の新店。座席数は公式19席・グルメ媒体15席と表記差があり、定休日も『無休』と『不定休』の両記載が見られるため来店前に確認推奨。ヴィーガンらぁ麺は動物性不使用をうたうが交差接触の管理は未確認で、厳格なヴィーガン／重度アレルギーは要確認。グルテンフリー対応は確認できず原則不可。

cuisine: 濃厚鶏白湯ラーメン・辛まぜそば (tori-paitan ramen / spicy mazesoba)
gf_detail: This is a standard ramen shop built on wheat noodles (sourced from Menya Teigyaku), wheat-based shoyu tare, and a long-simmered chicken (tori-paitan) broth. Wheat is present in the noodles and almost certainly in the soy-based tare. There is no indication of gluten-free noodles, gluten-free tare, a dedicated fryer, or any celiac accommodation. Not safe for celiacs.
vegan_detail: The entire menu is built around chicken (tori-paitan / chicken clear soup made from three heritage chicken breeds including Nagoya Cochin simmered ~8 hours) plus pork and chicken chashu toppings. There are no vegan or vegetarian ramen options noted; even the broths are animal-based.
address_ja: 京都府京都市南区唐橋西平垣町17
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to [135.73233, 34.980724], within the Kyoto box. Matches the stated location next to JR Nishioji Station in Minami Ward.
neighborhood: 南区 唐橋 / JR西大路駅前 (Karahashi, Minami Ward, by JR Nishioji Station)
seats_text: Counter seating for about 12 on the first floor, with an upper level reached by an iron staircase.
hours_raw: Mon-Fri 11:00-14:30 and 18:00-21:00; Sat 11:00-14:30 (lunch only); closed Sunday. (Some sources list slightly different evening/Saturday hours; Tabelog's listing used here.)
closed_days: Sundays (and no Saturday dinner service)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, busy local counter ramen shop with no English noted and cash-only payment, so a little Japanese or a translation app and a willingness to order at the counter will make the visit smooth.

bio.background: Menya Ikku opened on April 10, 2018, right beside JR Nishioji Station as the second shop and sister brand of Fukakusa Seimen Shokudo, a well-regarded ramen maker from the Fukakusa neighborhood of Fushimi Ward. Its debut was marked by a storefront crowded with congratulatory flowers. The space is deliberately stylish and modern, closer to a cafe-bar than a traditional ramen counter, with industrial iron detailing and an iron staircase leading to an upper floor.
bio.specialty: A rich chicken paitan (tori-paitan) ramen. The broth concentrates the umami of three heritage chicken breeds, including Nagoya Cochin, simmered with aromatics like garlic and ginger for roughly eight hours. The shop offers chicken shoyu, chicken shio, and paitan variations, plus a spicy mazesoba (karauma mazesoba). Bowls are finished with pork and chicken chashu, green onion, and premium hosaki menma (tender bamboo shoot tips) over straight medium-thick house noodles.
bio.philosophy: Coax maximum depth from quality regional chicken through long, careful simmering, presented in a clean, design-forward setting rather than a rough-and-ready ramen joint.
roles: second shop / sister brand of Fukakusa Seimen Shokudo

anecdotes.text: The broth is built from three heritage chicken breeds including Nagoya Cochin, simmered roughly eight hours with aromatic vegetables to concentrate the chicken umami.
Opened in 2018 about a one-minute walk from JR Nishioji Station, with the debut storefront surrounded by many congratulatory flowers and styled like a cafe-bar rather than a typical ramen shop.
Noodles are sourced from Menya Teigyaku, and bowls use premium hosaki menma (bamboo shoot tips) rather than standard menma.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The menu centers on shoyu (soy-sauce-based) and shio chicken ramen; standard ramen shoyu tare is wheat-based, and no gluten-free tare is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broths are animal-based (long-simmered chicken paitan/clear chicken soup) and bowls are topped with pork and chicken chashu, so vegan diners cannot be accommodated.

caveats: No official website found; details drawn from Tabelog and Kyoto food blogs. Hours vary slightly between sources (notably evening close and Saturday hours); Tabelog's listing was used for the structured hours. Seat count (~12 counter) is from a blog, not officially confirmed. No allergy or gluten-free information is published, so GF/vegan diners should treat this as unsuitable.

cuisine: まろやか醤油 中華そば (chuka-soba / Kyoto-style shoyu ramen)
neighborhood: 左京区 一乗寺 (Ichijoji, Sakyo Ward)
gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. This is a shoyu ramen specialist built on wheat: house-made wheat noodles fresh each morning, a wheat-based soy-sauce (shoyu) tare, and a dashi/shellfish broth. Gyoza and other sides also contain wheat. No gluten-free menu, no dedicated equipment, and noodles cook in shared water. A celiac diner cannot be safely accommodated here.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The core bowls are built on a pork (chashu) and shellfish dashi (oyster/clam/shrimp) broth, and the signature draw is the customer-thickness-cut pork chashu simmered over two hours. No vegan or vegetarian ramen is offered; even side dishes center on pork. A strict vegan would have nothing suitable on the menu.
address_ja: 京都府京都市左京区一乗寺払殿町48
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned [135.784103, 35.04335] for 京都府京都市左京区一乗寺払殿町48; within the Kyoto box.
seats_text: Counter seating (カウンター席); exact seat count not published. Small house-style shop.
hours_raw: 11:00-15:00 / 17:30-22:00; closed Thursdays (irregular closures may occur)
closed_days: Thursdays (plus occasional irregular closures)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, busy counter ramen shop on Ichijoji's competitive ramen street; ordering is typically in Japanese and the flow is quick, so a little Japanese or a translation app helps, though pointing at the menu works fine.

bio.background: 麺屋 聖 ～kiyo～ opened on October 1, 2018 as the second shop of Kyoto's Menya Yukou group, planting its flag on Ichijoji's famously competitive ramen street. Its stated mission is to carry Kyoto's dashi (出汁) culture from this neighborhood out to Japan and the wider world.
bio.origin: Kyoto (Ichijoji, Sakyo Ward); second branch of the Menya Yukou group founded in Kawaramachi-Oike, Kyoto.
bio.specialty: A mellow Kyoto-style shoyu chuka-soba: a special soy-sauce broth offered in three variations, built on shellfish dashi (oyster, clam, shrimp) married to traditional Kyoto soy sauce. The house-made noodles are pressed fresh every morning at the group's own noodle factory for a chewy, firm (モチモチとしたコシのある) bite, and the slow-simmered pork chashu is cut to the thickness each guest prefers right at the counter. Signature bowls carry playful names such as Gifto, Shimple, and Shiroshi.
bio.philosophy: The parent group runs on the idea that staff are not employees but companions (仲間); the original shop was even named after its manager rather than its owner to honor that spirit. At kiyo the kitchen wants guests simply to taste the depth of the dashi and shoyu, with the chashu fat melting into the broth.

anecdotes.text: The pork chashu is tenderized by a unique method and simmered for over two hours, then sliced to the customer's chosen thickness at the counter.
kiyo was the Menya Yukou group's second shop, opened in 2018 on Ichijoji's fiercely competitive ramen street with the aim of spreading Kyoto's dashi culture to Japan and the world.

gf_cross_contamination.text: House-made wheat noodles are boiled to order in shared ramen water; no gluten-free preparation exists, so cross-contamination with wheat is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth's defining element is a special soy-sauce (shoyu) tare made with traditional Kyoto soy sauce; Japanese soy sauce is normally brewed with wheat, so the broth contains wheat-derived gluten.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is built on shellfish dashi (oyster, clam, shrimp) and the shop's centerpiece is pork chashu; no vegan or vegetarian bowl is offered, so there is no animal-free option to protect from cross-contact.

caveats: Hours and seat count are taken from the official group page and Tabelog; the shop notes irregular closures in addition to its regular Thursday closure, so confirm before a special trip. No allergy/gluten-free or vegan accommodation information was found on any source — treat the dietary fields as honest defaults for a wheat-and-pork shoyu ramen shop, not as confirmed policy.

lead: 名前のないラーメン屋
name_ja: 名前のないラーメン屋
cuisine: 濃厚魚介・煮干し系ラーメン／つけ麺・カレーつけ麺（niboshi-gyokai ramen & tsukemen）
neighborhood: 中京区 木屋町三条
gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe for celiacs. This is a classic ramen/tsukemen shop: wheat noodles, a soy-based tare (Japanese soy sauce almost always contains wheat), and meat/fish broths. Even the signature bowls that mention burdock or "less wheat-heavy" texture are not gluten-free, and no GF menu, GF noodles, or dedicated prep is advertised. Anyone with celiac disease or a wheat allergy should treat this shop as unsafe.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The menu is built around animal-based broths (seafood/niboshi, chicken, and pork) and the proteins offered are offal (motsu), chashu pork, and wagyu beef. There is no vegan or vegetarian ramen option, and the tare/broth foundations are animal-derived. Vegans cannot be accommodated here.
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区恵比須町534-31 CEO木屋町ビル B1F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI on the chō-level address (恵比須町534番地); coordinates [135.769714, 35.009483] fall within the Kyoto box and match the Kiyamachi-Sanjo location.
seats_text: 12 counter seats only (no tables); solo-dining friendly.
closed_days: 不定休（Irregular closing days — no fixed weekly day off)
cultural_comfort_note: A signboard-less, locals-in-the-know spot with a touch-panel ticket machine and a modular Japanese-language menu; some Japanese ability or studying the menu beforehand makes ordering much smoother.

bio.background: Tucked into a B1F space along the Takasegawa canal at Kiyamachi-Sanjo, this is the famous "ramen shop with no name" — there is no signboard and no storefront marking, just an unmarked staircase leading underground. It is run as a sister project of well-known Kyoto ramen shops Takakura Nijo and Sugari, and the same obsessive attention to detail carries through here.
bio.specialty: A modular bowl ordered from a ticket machine: choose your noodle style (ramen, tsukemen, or curry tsukemen), your broth (delicate/tanrei, layered/juso, or rich/noko), and your protein (offal, chashu, or wagyu beef). The layered seafood-leaning broth is a signature — described as so clean and balanced you can drink it straight — finished with charred and roasted vegetables, including roasted tomato, as accents.
bio.philosophy: Understated elegance over conventional ramen-shop styling: the room reads more like a hidden cafe, chopsticks, napkins and condiments are tucked away in drawers beneath the counter for a minimalist presentation, and bowls are served on premium tableware (Royal Copenhagen, Staub). Diners are encouraged to adjust the flavor themselves using condiments kept at the counter.
roles: sister shop of Takakura Nijo and Sugari

anecdotes.text: The shop deliberately has no name and no sign — you reach it by an unmarked underground staircase beside the Takasegawa canal, which is part of its hidden-gem appeal.

Condiments, chopsticks and napkins are stored neatly in drawers rather than displayed, and food is served on Royal Copenhagen and Staub tableware — the space feels like a hidden cafe rather than a ramen shop.
It is run as a sister/subsidiary shop of the popular Kyoto ramen establishments Takakura Nijo and Sugari.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Standard ramen/tsukemen shop with wheat noodles and a shared kitchen; no gluten-free handling, dedicated equipment, or separate prep is advertised anywhere.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature broths are described as soy-based; Japanese soy sauce used in ramen tare almost always contains wheat, so the tare should be assumed to contain gluten.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths are animal-based (seafood/niboshi, chicken, pork) and proteins are offal, chashu pork, and wagyu beef; there is no vegan option and animal products are pervasive in the kitchen.

caveats: Hours: sources differ on dinner close — official Tabelog listing gives weekdays 18:00-22:00 and weekends/holidays 18:00-21:00 (used here as authoritative); one blog quoted lunch L.O. 14:50. Closing days are irregular (不定休), so confirm before visiting a signboard-less B1F shop. No official website found; QR/PayPay payments accepted per Tabelog. No allergy/dietary info is published, so GF/vegan judgments are inferred from the standard ramen format and stated soy-based/animal broths.

cuisine: Kyoto-style Cantonese Chinese (京都中華 / 鳳舞系)
neighborhood: 祇園新橋 (Gion Shinbashi), Higashiyama-ku
address_ja: 京都府京都市東山区新橋通花見小路西入ル橋本町390
geocode_note: GSI geocode of full street address returned [135.772263, 35.007458], within the Kyoto box and consistent with the Gion Shinbashi / Hanamikoji location.
seats_text: 115 total: 1st floor 45 (24 box/tatami seats + 18 chair seats), 2nd floor 70 (banquet/private-room seating for course meals). Course parties accommodated from 5 up to 56 guests.
hours_raw: 17:00–21:00 (L.O. 20:20); banquets/course parties until 21:30. Closed Tuesdays. Lunch banquets available by reservation for parties of 10+.
closed_days: Tuesdays
cultural_comfort_note: A long-established Gion neighborhood Chinese restaurant beloved by geiko and maiko; service is in Japanese and the experience suits a diner comfortable navigating a traditional local establishment, ideally with some Japanese or a guide.
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is indicated. This is a Cantonese restaurant whose signature dishes (spring rolls in wheat wrappers, sweet-and-sour pork, fried rice seasoned with soy sauce) rely heavily on wheat: wheat-flour wrappers, soy-sauce-based seasonings, and likely wheat starch/flour for frying. No sources mention gluten handling, a dedicated fryer, or wheat-free options. Not suitable for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan venue. The kitchen does cook some vegetable-forward dishes — notably a water-spinach (空心菜) stir-fry and other seasonal-vegetable plates praised for light, gentle Kyoto-style seasoning — but these are prepared in a meat-and-seafood kitchen and the seasonings are soy/oyster-style sauces of unknown composition. A vegan could potentially eat a vegetable plate but only after asking carefully; no dedicated vegan menu exists.
caveats: IMPORTANT: Despite the lineage tag, this is a Cantonese Chinese restaurant (京都中華 / 鳳舞系), not a ramen shop. None of the sources fetched (official site, kyotokurasu, otonano-shumatsu, yamazen, Tabelog) list 中華そば (chuka-soba) or ラーメン on Takeka's menu — its signature dishes are spring rolls, sweet-and-sour pork, roast/steamed pork, and fried rice. The "中華そば 鳳舞系" framing reflects the broader Hobu/Fuyo-en Kyoto-Chinese tradition the founder trained in, not a confirmed noodle dish here. Treat any "ramen" expectation with caution. Cash only; 10% service charge on course orders.

bio.background: Takeka opened in 1966 at the foot of Gion Shinbashi, beside the Shirakawa stream, and has spent close to six decades cooking for the geiko and maiko, teahouse patrons, and locals of the district. Its first owner trained at Fuyo-en (芙蓉園), one of the foundational houses of Kyoto's distinctive Cantonese tradition, before going independent. The shop's name comes from Takeka, the proprietor's great-grandmother, a celebrated Gion geisha of the Taisho and early Showa eras; her vintage hairpins and combs are still displayed in the restaurant.
bio.specialty: Spring rolls (春巻き) are the house signature, said to be ordered by roughly 80% of customers: a homemade crepe-like wrapper packed with finely shredded bamboo shoots, pork and crab, then cut into eight small pieces specifically so geiko could eat them without smudging makeup or staining a kimono. Also renowned are the sweet-and-sour pork (酢豚), the glossy sweet-vinegar meatballs, the roast pork (焼豚), and a fried rice (やきめし) that the kitchen has quietly refined over the years.
bio.philosophy: A gentle, light, 'Kyoto-fied' Cantonese cooking that deliberately omits pungent aromatics — garlic, onion, ginger — so that the food would never interfere with a geiko's or maiko's work. The result is a refined, understated cuisine built around seasonal vegetables and clean flavors.
roles: owner-proprietor (female proprietor, descendant of the geisha Takeka)

anecdotes.text: The spring rolls are deliberately cut into eight small bites so that geiko and maiko could eat them cleanly without ruining their makeup or kimono.
Dishes such as the sweet-and-sour pork are made without garlic, onion or ginger so the lingering aromas would not interfere with the geiko's evening work.
The restaurant is named after Takeka, the present proprietor's great-grandmother, a famous Gion geisha; her old hairpins and combs are still on display in the shop.
Cultural figures including Kitaoji Rosanjin (北大路魯山人) and the painter Hashimoto Kansetsu (橋本関雪) are said to have visited the establishment.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No sources address gluten or wheat cross-contamination. As a Cantonese kitchen serving wheat-wrapper spring rolls and soy-sauce-seasoned dishes alongside fried items, cross-contact with wheat should be assumed pervasive.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Signature dishes such as the roast pork are seasoned with soy sauce, and the soy sauce is not specified as wheat-free; standard Japanese/Chinese soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegetable dishes (e.g., the water-spinach stir-fry) are cooked in a kitchen handling pork, crab and other seafood; no vegan separation is indicated.

positives.text: The kitchen's gentle, aromatics-free Kyoto-Cantonese style means many dishes are lightly seasoned and free of garlic, onion and ginger, which may help diners avoiding those specific ingredients.

lead: ラー麺 陽はまた昇る 伏見稲荷駅前本店
name_ja: ラー麺 陽はまた昇る 伏見稲荷駅前本店
name_en: Ra-men Hi wa Mata Noboru Fushimi Inari Ekimae Honten
cuisine: 魚とりとんこつラーメン・つけ麺（tori-tonkotsu ramen / tsukemen）
neighborhood: 伏見区深草（京阪 伏見稲荷駅前）
address_ja: 京都府京都市伏見区深草一ノ坪町38-15
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 京都府京都市伏見区深草一ノ坪町38-15 returned [135.768753, 34.968201]; within Kyoto box (lat 34.93–35.13, lng 135.66–135.83). Matches stated location 1 min from Keihan Fushimi Inari Station.
seats_text: About 22-25 seats: counter seating (approx. 11 stools) plus a few tables; stylish interior extending toward the back, mostly counter with some table seats. (Sources vary between 22 and 25.)
hours_raw: 11:00–21:00; closed Wednesdays. A Jiro-style option is offered after 18:00.
closed_days: Wednesdays (毎週水曜日)
cultural_comfort_note: A busy counter ramen shop on the main approach near Fushimi Inari that draws both tourists and locals; basic Japanese greetings help but you can manage with minimal Japanese, and there is an English site/menu.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The bowls center on house-made wheat noodles (now a whole-wheat flat thick noodle for tsukemen) and a fermented soy-sauce (shoyu) tare, served over chicken-and-pork-bone broth. No gluten-free menu, dedicated equipment, or wheat-free noodle/tare options were found; a celiac-safe meal is not realistic here.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. The house specialty is a chicken-and-pork-bone (tori-tonkotsu) double broth simmered with ~130kg of bones per batch; a beef-bone broth variant is also offered. Toppings include chashu pork belly and house-made pastrami. No vegetable- or plant-based broth or vegan menu was found.
caveats: Seat count differs across listings (22 vs 25); used 25 from the detailed Tabelog breakdown. Payment: cash plus QR-code payments accepted; credit cards and electronic money reported as not accepted, so card users should carry cash/QR backup. A Jiro-style bowl is available only after 18:00.

bio.background: An independent neighborhood ramen shop on the busy approach street near Fushimi Inari, run by representative Shunji Otsubo under his company Hinobo. The shop relocated to its current spot by the Keihan Fushimi Inari station front, growing into a local favorite that now also draws the steady stream of shrine visitors.
bio.origin: Kyoto (Fushimi-ku, Fukakusa), near Fushimi Inari Taisha
bio.specialty: Tori-tonkotsu ramen and a rich tsukemen: a chicken-and-pork-bone double broth simmered 8+ hours from roughly 130kg of bones per batch, paired with house-made noodles. The tsukemen broth is kept hot in a stone bowl and topped with seared pork-belly chashu and house-made pastrami, menma and green onion.
bio.philosophy: A stated belief in valuing the natural flavor of the ingredients and keeping food safe enough for children to eat: the kitchen avoids leaning on chemical umami additives, relying instead on careful preparation and heat control to get a broth that is rich but clean-finishing rather than heavy, seasoned with naturally fermented soy sauce.

anecdotes.text: The broth is built from about 130kg of chicken and pork bones simmered for over eight hours per batch, aiming for a result that is concentrated yet elegant with a clean aftertaste.

The tsukemen dipping broth is served in a heated stone bowl to hold its temperature, with whole-wheat flat thick noodles and house-made pastrami plus seared pork-belly chashu among the toppings; it has become a draw for local ramen fans despite the touristy location.
The shop relocated from near Kintetsu Fushimi station to its current spot by Fushimi Inari, where it sits on the busy Sandan-kaido approach drawing both tourists and locals.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Bowls are built on house-made wheat noodles and a fermented soy-sauce tare, so the dish is wheat-based throughout; no gluten-free preparation or separate handling is mentioned.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Seasoning uses naturally fermented soy sauce (and a tamari-style soy sauce for the beef-bone variant), which is wheat-containing unless specified otherwise; no wheat-free tare is offered.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths are animal-based (chicken-and-pork bone, plus a beef-bone option) and toppings include pork chashu and pastrami; there is no separate vegetable broth line, so vegan separation is not possible.

positives.text: The kitchen states it avoids chemical seasoning additives and prioritizes ingredient safety so that even children can eat the food, relying on careful preparation rather than synthetic umami enhancers.

name_ja: 京都中華 ハマムラ 府庁前店
cuisine: Kyoto-style Chinese (京風町中華) — 中華そば / からしそば
gf_detail: No gluten-free options. The signature noodle dishes — 中華そば and the famous からしそば — use wheat noodles, and the からしそば is finished with a wheat-thickened ankake sauce and mustard. Chinese-style cooking here relies on soy sauce and wheat-based seasonings throughout. Not suitable for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly restaurant. The Kyoto-Chinese cooking is built on chicken-bone and kombu dashi plus pork, and broths/sauces are animal-based. No dedicated vegan menu was found. Core items are not plant-based.
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区丸太町通釜座東入梅屋町175-2
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to Umeyacho 175-2 (Nakagyo-ku); within the Kyoto box, just south of the Kyoto Prefectural Office near Marutamachi Station.
seats_text: 31 seats total (about 20 table seats and 11 counter seats).
closed_days: Mondays
cultural_comfort_note: A welcoming, long-running neighborhood Chinese restaurant where solo and first-time visitors fit in easily; a little menu guidance helps since service is in Japanese.
neighborhood: Nakagyo-ku, across from the Kyoto Prefectural Office (Fuchomae), near Marutamachi Station
caveats: Although framed as a ramen shop, ハマムラ is a Kyoto-style Chinese (kyo-chuka) restaurant whose famous noodle dishes (中華そば, からしそば) are ramen-adjacent rather than a pure ramen specialty. No allergy/GF/vegan accommodation could be confirmed; celiac and vegan diners should not rely on this venue.

bio.background: Hamamura was founded in 1924 (Taisho 13) and is widely credited as the birthplace of Kyoto Chinese (kyo-chuka). What began as a humble noodle-and-Chinese shop grew into one of the city's defining culinary lineages, now run by the founder's grandson, the third-generation Hamamura Yoshiyuki. The Fuchomae branch sits across from the Kyoto Prefectural Office and moved to this Marutamachi spot from Kawaramachi in 2014.
bio.specialty: The signature dish is karashi-soba: noodles bound in a glossy ankake sauce sharpened with vinegar-loosened Japanese mustard, described as spicy yet paradoxically light and refined. Their straightforward chuka-soba is the other anchor of the menu.
bio.philosophy: Kyoto Chinese holds back the garlic and heavy oil of mainland Chinese cooking, leaning instead on chicken-bone and kombu dashi for a clean, restrained flavor. The style is said to have grown out of Kyoto's geisha districts, where strong-smelling foods were discouraged.

anecdotes.text: Hamamura is regarded as a legend of Kyoto Chinese, tracing back to the original Shina-ryori Hamamura credited with founding the kyo-chuka genre in 1924.
The karashi-soba is celebrated as spicy from the mustard yet still light and refined, a one-plate symbol of Kyoto Chinese.
The Fuchomae location relocated from Kawaramachi in 2014 and is known as an easy place to drop into, even for a solo diner.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat noodles, wheat-thickened ankake sauce, and soy/wheat seasonings are central to the menu; no gluten-free handling or dedicated equipment information was found.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Chinese soy-sauce-based seasonings are used throughout; assume wheat-containing shoyu unless the shop confirms otherwise.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths and sauces are built on chicken-bone and kombu dashi with pork dishes prominent; shared woks and stocks make vegan separation unlikely.

lead: ラーメン また此処で
name_ja: ラーメン また此処で
cuisine: Jiro-style ramen (二郎系) — house-made extra-thick hand-massaged noodles, pork-bone broth, call-board customization
vegan_detail: This is a Jiro-style ramen shop built on a pork-bone (tonkotsu) broth with back fat and pork toppings; the bowls are fundamentally pork-based with mounded vegetables on top. No vegan or vegetarian option is offered or mentioned anywhere.
gf_detail: Not safe for gluten-free or celiac diners. The signature draw is house-made extra-thick wheat noodles (極太手揉み麺), and the tare is a soy-sauce (wheat-based) seasoning over a pork-bone broth. No gluten-free noodles, separate prep, or allergen accommodation is offered or mentioned.
address_ja: 京都府京都市下京区西七条北衣田町9
geocode_note: GSI address search returned [135.730896, 34.989464] for 京都府京都市下京区西七条北衣田町9; within the Kyoto bounding box.
neighborhood: Nishi-Shichijo / Nishioji-Shichijo, Shimogyo-ku (near JR Nishioji Station)
seats_text: Counter seats only (カウンター席のみ); open-kitchen counter with a few additional seats toward the back. Exact count not published.
hours_raw: 営業時間 11:00〜14:00 / 17:00〜22:00、金曜17:00〜22:00、日曜11:00〜16:00。定休日：月曜。(Note: Tabelog listing shows 11:00-15:00 / 18:00-22:00, closed Mon & Thu — local/shop sources give the times used here.)
closed_days: Mondays (Friday has no lunch service; Sunday is daytime-only)
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, counter-only neighborhood Jiro-style shop with a ticket machine and a posted call board, so a first-timer can point and order without much Japanese, though staff interaction is in Japanese and you'll be asked the standard "garlic?" question at the counter.

bio.origin: Kyoto — opened on the site of a former ramen shop that relocated to Osaka in mid-2025
bio.background: The shop is the realization of a 13-year dream for owner-chef Takuya Kozasa, who trained for 13 years at the influential Kyoto Jiro-style house 'ラーメン荘 地球規模で考えろ／そら' (Ramen Sho 'Chikyu-kibo de Kangaero' / Sora) before striking out on his own. It opened on October 6, 2025.
bio.specialty: Jiro-style (二郎系) ramen built on house-made extra-thick, hand-massaged noodles (極太手揉み麺) cut on the shop's own noodle machine, in a sweet-edged pork-bone broth crowned with mounded vegetables, back fat (アブラ), garlic, and thick chashu. The standard bowl is 300g of noodles with two slices of pork; a flagship 'また此処ラーメン' with special toppings tops the menu. A brothless 'sojiru-nashi/tsukemen' version is also offered.
bio.philosophy: Despite the big, brawny Jiro format, the cooking aims for balance — the soy tare is kept deliberately restrained in saltiness to let the broth's natural pork sweetness come through, tuned toward Kyoto palates, with garlic and a touch of chili (一味) for punch. The chef keeps refining the recipe by tracking room temperature and humidity day to day.

anecdotes.text: The name 'また此処で' (mata koko de — 'see you here again') doubles as the staff's parting line to guests: 'また此処でお待ちしています' ('we'll be waiting for you here again').

On opening, the shop announced the chef's roots plainly on social media: '元ソラの人です！地球規模で13年間修行してました！' ('I'm a former Sora guy — I trained for 13 years at Chikyu-kibo'), framing the launch as '13年間追い続けた夢が遂に動き出します' ('the dream I chased for 13 years finally takes off').

A call board (コール表) is posted at the counter so even newcomers to Jiro-style can pick their toppings — extra vegetables (野菜マシ), extra seasoning (カラメ), extra garlic — without having to call them out by heart.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core product is house-made extra-thick wheat noodles boiled in shared pots; gluten is unavoidable and there is no indication of any gluten-free handling.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The tare is a soy-sauce-based (kaeshi) seasoning — standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat — kept light to let the pork-bone broth's sweetness show.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is pork-bone (tonkotsu) with back fat and pork toppings; vegetables are cooked into and mounded over the same animal-based bowl, so no component is free of animal contact.

caveats: Hours differ between sources: the Tabelog listing shows 11:00-15:00 / 18:00-22:00 closed Mon & Thu, while the shop-sourced local reports (kyo-soku) give 11:00-14:00 / 17:00-22:00, Friday dinner-only, Sunday 11:00-16:00, closed Monday — the latter (used here) appears to reflect the shop's own posting. Confirm before visiting. Seat count is not published. No allergen, gluten, or vegan information is available from any source — dietary fields reflect the inherent nature of Jiro-style ramen, not a stated shop policy.

cuisine: 油そば（あぶらそば・まぜそば） / Abura soba (brothless oil noodles)
vegan_detail: No vegan option. This is an abura soba (brothless oil noodle) shop whose bowls center on a wheat-based oil/tare sauce, pork fat (背脂), char siu / fatty pork toppings, eggs, and mayo-based extras. The "背脂野菜油そば" (back-fat vegetable) bowl is built on pork fat in a Jiro style. No plant-based bowl is offered; a vegan diner could not be safely accommodated here.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free / not celiac-safe. All bowls use wheat noodles (the regular and spicy abura soba use house-made wheat noodles; the back-fat vegetable bowl uses a thick Jiro-style wheat noodle), and the oil/tare sauce is a typical abura-soba sauce that contains wheat-based soy sauce. There is no gluten-free menu, no separate preparation, and no indication of allergy handling. A celiac diner should avoid this shop.
address_ja: 京都府京都市左京区田中門前町66
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 京都府京都市左京区田中門前町66 returned [135.779297, 35.029888], within the Kyoto box. Matches the Hyakumanben location north of the Hyakumanben crossing near Kyoto University.
seats_text: Small shop: counter seating plus a few 2-person tables, in a narrow traditional Kyoto-style space. Exact seat count not published.
hours_raw: 11:00–15:00 / 17:30–22:30 (Friday dinner until 22:00). Sources vary slightly on lunch close (one lists 11:00–14:30). Irregular closures announced on Instagram; some sources note Monday evening closed.
closed_days: Irregular holidays (announced via Instagram); some reports note Monday evenings and Sundays closed. Confirm via Instagram before visiting.
cultural_comfort_note: A casual student-district ticket-machine shop where ordering and eating are wordless and self-directed, so basic Japanese (or just pointing at the machine) is plenty, but there is no English support or allergy accommodation.
caveats: Opened October 10, 2025, so hours and closed days are still settling and reported inconsistently across sources (lunch close listed variously as 14:30 or 15:00; closed-day notes range from Sunday to Monday evening to irregular) — verify on the shop's Instagram before visiting. Cash only at present (electronic payment said to be planned). No GF or vegan accommodation.
neighborhood: 百万遍・田中門前町（左京区、京大近く） / Hyakumanben, Sakyo-ku, near Kyoto University

bio.background: Yumin is the abura-soba spin-off of Bishiya (美しが), a ramen shop in Ichijoji known for drawing lines. The team opened this second, oil-noodle-focused shop in the Hyakumanben student quarter just north of the Hyakumanben crossing near Kyoto University, taking over a space that had been a curry restaurant. Staff from the Ichijoji parent moved over to run it. It opened on October 10, 2025, and was generating buzz before it even opened.
bio.specialty: Abura soba — brothless wheat noodles tossed with an oil-and-tare sauce. Three bowls anchor the menu: the standard abura soba and a spicy version (辛味油そば), both on house-made noodles, plus a 背脂野菜油そば (back-fat vegetable) bowl built in a Jiro style on thick, chewy noodles with pork fat and a mound of vegetables. The base bowl is about 750 yen, with cheap noodle upsizes up to 540g.
bio.philosophy: Make oil soba approachable: lighter on the palate than its rich look suggests, with a good balance of acidity and aromatics, and table signage that walks first-timers through how to eat it. Diners mix everything themselves, dial in garlic to taste, and use the counter condiments (vinegar, chili oil, pepper, diced onion) to shift the flavor partway through the bowl.

anecdotes.text: The shop took over a former curry restaurant in Hyakumanben and was already a talking point among Kyoto ramen fans before opening, on the strength of the Bishiya name.
One writer summed up the bowl as 'lighter than it looks, with a good balance of acidity and seasonings,' and noted the table signage guiding newcomers through mixing and topping their noodles.
The back-fat vegetable bowl deliberately breaks from the plain look of typical oil soba, piling on Jiro-style pork fat and vegetables on a thick crinkly noodle.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The bowls are abura soba dressed in an oil-and-tare sauce of the standard type, which is based on wheat-containing soy sauce; no wheat-free tare is offered.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Bowls are built on pork fat (背脂), fatty pork, eggs and mayo-based toppings shared across the kitchen; there is no plant-based bowl and no separation from animal ingredients.

lead: 台湾まぜそば専門店 脳天喜。
name_ja: 台湾まぜそば専門店 脳天喜。
name_en: Taiwan Mazesoba Senmonten Noutenki (Kyoto Men Koubou Noutenki)
cuisine: 台湾まぜそば（Taiwanese mazesoba; signatures: 東坡肉台湾まぜそば / dongpo-pork mazesoba, 台湾まぜそば）
neighborhood: 上京区・千本出水（二条駅／二条城北西）
address_ja: 京都府京都市上京区十四軒町394-1
geocode_note: Geocoded from address_ja via GSI; first feature coordinates [135.742172, 35.021019]. Within Kyoto box (lat 34.93–35.13, lng 135.66–135.83). Block-level (十四軒町) precision near Senbon-Demizu / NW of Nijo Castle.
seats_text: 6 seats; counter/table seating, all non-smoking. Small intimate space.
hours_raw: Mon–Fri 11:00–14:00, 18:00–21:00; Sat/Sun/Holidays 11:00–21:00. Closed: irregular (不定休).
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular holidays / no fixed closed day published)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny 6-seat neighborhood noodle counter with no indication of English service, so a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu will make ordering smooth.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core dish is Taiwanese mazesoba built on wheat noodles (the shop even lets you choose between two wheat-flour noodle types), tossed with a soy-sauce-based Taiwanese mince — both standard wheat-containing components. No gluten-free noodle, tare, or accommodation is mentioned anywhere. Celiac diners should treat this as unsafe.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. The menu centers on pork — signature 東坡肉 (skin-on braised pork belly / dongpo pork), spicy Taiwanese pork mince, chashu rice bowls, and a chicken-paitan ramen. Eggs (味玉) feature prominently. No plant-based bowl is advertised.

bio.origin: Kyoto, Kamigyo-ku (Senbon-Demizu, northwest of Nijo Castle)
bio.background: A specialty mazesoba shop that opened on November 20, 2024, on Senbon-dori. The signature dongpo-pork dish is prepared under a Chinese chef who keeps to an authentic recipe while tuning it to Japanese palates.
bio.specialty: 東坡肉台湾まぜそば — Taiwanese mazesoba crowned with dongpo pork (皮付きの豚の角煮, glossy skin-on braised pork belly slow-cooked with star anise). The house bills itself as Japan's first mazesoba shop that lets you pick your noodle, choosing between a medium-thick full-wheat noodle and a wavy 'wing' noodle so you can enjoy the bowl your way to the last bite.
bio.philosophy: Quality ingredients plus customization: the owner frames the two noodle choices as 'selecting the two ultimate options so you can enjoy Taiwanese mazesoba's deliciousness to the very end,' and sets out 10-plus condiments (garlic vinegar, kombu/anchovy vinegar and more) to encourage mid-meal 味変 (flavor-shifting).

anecdotes.text: The shop sources top-grade Kujo negi normally reserved for high-end kaiseki restaurants, and 'triple-folate' eggs from the Tanba/Mizuho area.
Beyond mazesoba the counter also serves a chicken-paitan ramen (泡鶏白湯拉麺) and chashu rice, with Taiwanese beer on offer to pair with the bowls.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen is built around wheat noodles and a soy-sauce-based Taiwanese mince; no gluten-free handling or separate prep is described, so heavy gluten cross-contact should be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Taiwanese mazesoba tare is conventionally a wheat-based soy-sauce mince; no wheat-free tamari is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is pork- and egg-forward (dongpo pork, pork mince, chashu, 味玉); no vegan bowl, and shared equipment with meat dishes should be assumed.

caveats: Opened Nov 2024; a very small (6-seat) counter with irregular days off (不定休) — worth confirming the day before visiting. No allergen, gluten-free, or vegan information is published; phone listing was incomplete on Tabelog. Hours and details drawn from Tabelog plus two Kyoto food outlets (DigiStyle Kyoto, Kyoto-soku) and the shop's Instagram.

cuisine: 町中華そば（大衆食堂、あっさり中華そば）
gf_detail: This is a traditional Showa-era noodle diner. The signature 中華そば uses fine wheat ramen noodles in a dashi-style broth, and the menu is built around wheat-based noodles (udon, soba, chūka soba), rice bowls and inari sushi. There is no indication of gluten-free preparation, dedicated equipment, or allergen handling. Broth and tare almost certainly involve wheat-containing soy sauce. Not suitable for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: A meat-and-fish family diner: the 中華そば comes with thick char siu pork, two kinds of fish-cake kamaboko, and a dashi-based broth. Rice bowls and set meals are similarly animal-based. No vegan options are advertised and the kitchen is not set up for plant-based requests.
address_ja: 京都府京都市伏見区深草直違橋2丁目425-1
geocode_note: GSI geocode of the chō-level address returned [135.771469, 34.954536], within the Kyoto box.
neighborhood: 伏見区・深草商店街（JR藤森/京阪深草駅）
seats_text: 28 seats (7 tables of 4); compact, classic Showa-era interior.
hours_raw: 10:00-18:00 (some listings note 10:00-17:30), closed Sundays.
closed_days: Sundays
cultural_comfort_note: A neighborhood Showa-era diner where the owner couple knows the regulars by name and casual Japanese greetings are the norm, so basic Japanese or a guide helps; little to no English is expected.
caveats: Hours vary slightly by listing (close at 17:30 vs 18:00); confirm before a late-afternoon visit. Cash only.

bio.chef_name: Seiji Kakichu (founder's family; run today by the proprietor couple)
bio.background: Part of the historic Rikimochi (力餅) lineage that began in 1889 in Toyooka as a confectionery and spread across Kansai as a franchise of homey eateries; fewer than a handful of these old shops survive today. This Kakichu branch was opened independently in 1966 (Showa 41) by the founder's father, Seiji Kakichu, after receiving permission to operate under the Rikimochi name, and remains a fixture of the Fukakusa shopping street.
bio.specialty: 中華そば (chūka soba) at around 680 yen: a deliberately light, pale broth closer to a gentle udon dashi than a heavy ramen soup, with fine straight noodles, thick-cut char siu, two kinds of kamaboko fish cake, generous Kyoto negi and menma. Their oversized inari sushi (about 150 yen), packed with black sesame, carrot and burdock, is a beloved side.
bio.philosophy: あっさりと飽きのこない家庭的な味 — a light, never-tiring homestyle flavor served in an unpretentious old-fashioned shokudo where everything stays under 1,000 yen and a solo diner is always welcome.

anecdotes.text: The shop keeps a classic Showa-era atmosphere down to the television in the corner, and the owner couple know their regulars personally, trading easy small talk as customers come and go.
The 中華そば broth is so pale and gentle that it reads more like a soothing udon dashi than a typical ramen soup, the kind of bowl described as quietly soaking into your body.
Opened in 1966 by Seiji Kakichu under the historic Rikimochi name, it is one of the dwindling number of these old Kansai diners still in operation.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat-noodle-centric diner (chūka soba, udon, soba) sharing one small kitchen; no gluten-free accommodation noted.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Signature bowl uses char siu pork, fish-cake kamaboko and a dashi-based broth; no plant-based preparation indicated.

cuisine: 鴨出汁中華そば（chuka-soba / kamo-dashi ramen）
neighborhood: 河原町四条（GOOD NATURE STATION 2F）
gf_detail: This is a refined chuka-soba (Chinese-style ramen) specialist. The dish is built on custom-made wheat noodles, almost certainly served with a wheat-based shoyu/Chinese-style tare, and a meat broth drawn from five bone stocks (duck, local chicken, venison, beef, pork). Wonton toppings are wheat-wrapped. No gluten-free menu, dedicated preparation, or wheat-free option is advertised on any source reviewed. Not suitable for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: The entire concept is centered on an animal broth extracted from five types of bone (duck, free-range chicken, venison, beef, pork), and the signature wontons contain pork. There is no vegetarian or vegan ramen on the menu and no plant-based broth option. Not suitable for vegan or vegetarian diners.
address_ja: 京都府京都市下京区河原町通四条下ル2丁目稲荷町318-6 GOOD NATURE STATION 2F
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned no rooftop match for the full building address. Used the chō-level result for 下京区稲荷町318 ([135.769043, 35.00222]), which matches the GOOD NATURE STATION location near Kyoto Kawaramachi Station and falls within the Kyoto box. A ward-only fallback ([135.755478, 34.987606]) was also returned but is less precise.
seats_text: 12 counter seats only
hours_raw: Lunch 11:00-15:00 (L.O. 14:30) / Dinner 18:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:30). Closes early when the broth runs out.
closed_days: Wednesdays, plus irregular additional closures; also closes when the broth sells out. (Sources differ slightly on the fixed closed day — one lists Mondays with some Wednesdays — so confirm before visiting.)
cultural_comfort_note: A small 12-seat counter ramen shop inside a modern, visitor-friendly retail complex (GOOD NATURE STATION) by Kyoto Kawaramachi Station, so it is approachable with basic greetings even if deep English support is not guaranteed.

bio.background: Roku is produced by chef Yuji Iwasaki, who earned two Michelin stars at his high-end Chinese restaurant VELROSIER. The shop began as 鴨出汁中華そば ROKU (kamo-dashi / duck-broth chuka-soba) and was renewed and renamed to 中華そば 六 when it reopened on the second floor of GOOD NATURE STATION on May 13, 2023, narrowing its focus to a single, refined Chinese-style noodle soup.
bio.specialty: The signature 中華そば (Chuka Soba, around ¥1,100): a clear yet deeply layered broth that draws umami from five kinds of bone stock — duck, free-range chicken, venison, beef and pork — and builds further depth with porcini, longan fruit, tomato and dried shiitake. Straight noodles are custom-made to match the clear soup, and hand-made wontons (pork, onion, arrowhead and celery) are added partway through the bowl as a second act.
bio.philosophy: Applying the techniques of fine Chinese cuisine to ramen to create what the shop calls a 唯一無二 (one-of-a-kind) soup — an elevated, restaurant-grade take on the everyday chuka-soba.

anecdotes.text: A two-Michelin-star Chinese chef (VELROSIER) channeling his craft into a tiny counter ramen shop, the bowl is engineered around Chinese stock technique rather than typical ramen broths — five animal bones layered with porcini, longan, tomato and shiitake.
The shop has been recognized in Michelin's Bib Gourmand listing for its chuka-soba.
Originally 鴨出汁中華そば ROKU, the restaurant relaunched as 中華そば 六 inside GOOD NATURE STATION on May 13, 2023, refocusing entirely on its Chinese-style noodle soup.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The dish is wheat ramen noodles in a meat broth, with wheat-wrapped wontons, prepared in a small 12-seat shared kitchen; no gluten-free handling is advertised. Treat as not celiac-safe.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a Chinese-style chuka-soba, the tare is almost certainly soy/shoyu-based, which standardly contains wheat. No wheat-free seasoning is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The broth itself is animal-based (five bone stocks: duck, chicken, venison, beef, pork) and wontons contain pork, so there is no vegan-compatible base; cross-contact is moot because no plant-based option exists.

lead: 京・清水 油そば ねこまた
name_ja: 京・清水 油そば ねこまた
cuisine: 油そば・まぜそば (abura-soba / brothless mixed noodles)
gf_detail: Abura soba is a brothless wheat-noodle dish built on a soy-based (wheat) tare with sesame oil, topped with chashu pork — wheat is present in both the thick flat noodles and the tare. No gluten-free noodle or tamari option is advertised, and there is no indication of allergen accommodation. Not suitable for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: The signature bowls are built on animal ingredients: diced chashu pork, soy-based tare, and toppings such as naruto (fish cake) and menma. No vegan or vegetarian version is mentioned. The side of 卵かけご飯 (egg-on-rice) is also non-vegan. Not suitable for vegans.
address_ja: 京都府京都市東山区清水4-138
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned coordinates for 京都市東山区清水4-138, within the Kyoto box.
neighborhood: 東山区・清水（松原通／清水寺参道入口付近、清水五条駅・清水道バス停近く）
seats_text: 20 seats total (14 table seats, 6 counter seats)
hours_raw: 10:00 - 20:00 (L.O. 19:45), daily (Tabelog). 不定休 (irregular closures).
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular closures; no fixed weekly day off per Tabelog)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, casual tourist-area noodle counter where pointing at the picture menu and a friendly greeting will carry you through; do not expect English allergen support.
caveats: Cash only (no cards, e-money, or QR payment). No reservations. Closures are irregular (不定休), so confirm before a special trip. Note: a secondary listing showed 11:00-21:00 / closed Wednesdays — hours here follow the Tabelog page (10:00-20:00 daily), but day-of confirmation is wise.

bio.background: Billed as Kansai's first abura-soba specialty shop, this small noodle counter sits in the thick of the Kiyomizu tourist district, on Matsubara-dori between Gojo and Shijo, near the approach to Kiyomizu-dera.
bio.specialty: Abura soba — 'ramen without the soup.' Thick, flat kishimen-like noodles are tossed with a soy-based tare and fragrant sesame oil pooled at the bottom of the bowl, dressed with thick menma, diced chashu, green onion and naruto. The house signature is the 新元祖油そば (around 800 yen), and the lineup runs across soy, spicy-miso, and shrimp-salt versions, with a side of egg-on-rice (卵かけご飯) a popular add-on.

anecdotes.text: The shop hands you a little dish of minced raw onion to scatter on top — the way to eat it is to lift the tare up from the bottom of the bowl and mix everything thoroughly so noodles, toppings and sauce come together before you dig in; the onion adds sharpness and crunch.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat-based abura soba noodles and a soy (wheat) tare are the core of every bowl; no gluten-free preparation is offered or mentioned.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The dish is built on a soy-based (shoyu) tare, which in standard Japanese practice contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Bowls include diced chashu pork plus naruto fish cake and menma; no vegan/vegetarian version is described.

lead: コムギノキラメキ 上桂前田町本店
name_ja: コムギノキラメキ 上桂前田町本店
name_en: Komugi no Kirameki Kamikatsura Maedacho Honten
cuisine: 鶏白湯ラーメン・台湾まぜそば（自家製麺）
gf_detail: This is a wheat-noodle ramen and mazesoba shop with its own on-site noodle factory, so the noodles are wheat-based; the chicken-paitan ramen and Taiwan mazesoba are built on wheat noodles plus a shoyu/tare seasoning that typically contains wheat. There is no indication of gluten-free noodles, dedicated equipment, or any celiac accommodation. Not suitable for someone who needs to avoid gluten.
vegan_detail: The core menu is animal-based: chicken-paitan (tori-paitan) broth simmered from chicken, and Taiwan-style mazesoba topped with minced pork, egg yolk, chives and garlic, plus a back-fat "小麦のチカラ" with pork chunks. No vegan or vegetarian ramen is offered, and broths/tare are meat-based. Not a vegan-friendly option.
address_ja: 京都府京都市西京区上桂前田町51-1
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned [135.700256, 34.988976] for 京都市西京区上桂前田町51-1; within the Kyoto box.
seats_text: Spacious interior with counter and table seating (exact seat count not published); on-site glass-walled noodle factory visible from the dining area. Family- and solo-friendly, non-smoking. On-site parking for roughly 10–11 cars.
hours_raw: 11:00～23:00（LO 22:30）／定休日：無休（臨時休業する場合があります）
closed_days: 無休（不定休／臨時休業あり）
neighborhood: 西京区・上桂駅（上桂前田町）
cultural_comfort_note: A bustling, family-friendly suburban ramen shop with a ticket-vending machine ordering system and no English menu, so a guide or translation help is useful for navigating toppings and noodle-portion choices.
caveats: Exact seat count is not published; hours are reliable but the shop notes occasional unscheduled closures, so confirm before a special trip. No allergy/gluten/vegan accommodation information was found in any source.

bio.background: コムギノキラメキ〈小麦〉は、京都発のラーメンチェーン「キラメキグループ」（運営：キラメキノ未来株式会社）の店舗のひとつで、上桂前田町の国道9号沿いに構える広々とした郊外型の一軒。本店ブランドである丸太町の「キラメキノトリ」から京都市内・福知山・綾部、さらに大阪（吹田・高槻）へと勢力を広げてきたグループの一翼で、店内にはガラス越しに見える自家製の製麺所を併設し、麺を自前で打っているのが大きな特徴。
bio.specialty: 看板は二本立て。長時間炊き出した鶏のうまみが凝縮した白濁スープの『鶏白湯ラーメン』（醤油・塩あり）と、豚ミンチ・卵黄・ニラ・にんにくをのせたガツンと濃い『台湾まぜそば』。さらに背脂系で麺量を最大500gまで増やせる『小麦のチカラ』など、自家製の太麺を主役にした食べごたえ重視の品が揃う。
bio.philosophy: 自家製麺と濃厚な一杯で“小麦（コムギ）”そのものの力を主役にする、というコンセプトを店名に込め、子ども向けラーメンの用意やポイント制度など、地元のファミリーや一人客が気軽に通える普段使いの一杯を目指している。
roles: キラメキグループ店舗

anecdotes.text: ガラス張りの製麺所を併設し、自家製の麺をその場で作っている様子が客席から見える。
鶏白湯スープは白濁してコクが強く、飲んだ後に舌へ粉末状のうまみがザラッと残るほど鶏が凝縮していると評される。
キラメキグループ5店舗目として国道9号沿いの上桂に出店し、使い勝手のよい郊外型の店として紹介されている。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Self-described as a wheat-noodle shop ('コムギ／小麦') with an on-site noodle factory, so wheat is central and pervasive throughout the kitchen; no gluten-free option exists.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The chicken-paitan ramen is offered in a soy-sauce (醤油) variety; standard ramen shoyu tare is typically wheat-based, and no wheat-free seasoning is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All signature bowls are animal-based — chicken broth, minced pork, egg yolk; no vegan items are prepared, so there is no separated vegan line.

cuisine: 町中華・中華そば（醤油チャーシュー麺）／町家中華 — Chinese town-style cooking with a soy-sauce chuka-soba
neighborhood: 北区・北大路／堀川北大路（紫野）
address_ja: 京都府京都市北区紫野西御所田町23
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned coordinates for 京都府京都市北区紫野西御所田町23; lng 135.750259, lat 35.040928 — within the Kyoto box.
seats_text: Small renovated machiya laid out like an eel's-nest townhouse: a couple of tables near the entrance plus a roomy counter toward the back; counter suits 1-2 diners, tables for groups of 3 or more. Exact seat count not published.
hours_raw: 11:30-13:45 (L.O.) ランチ／17:30-21:00 (L.O.) ディナー。月曜定休。営業時間は食べログ上に明記なし、不定休との記載もあり、来店前にFacebook等で確認推奨。
closed_days: Mondays (月曜定休); some sources note irregular closures (不定休) — confirm before visiting.
cultural_comfort_note: A neighborhood machiya Chinese spot where you remove your shoes at the door and order from a Japanese-only menu, so basic Japanese or a translation app and a relaxed attitude help a lot.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. The signature chuka-soba uses standard wheat ramen noodles in a soy-sauce (醤油) broth, and the wider menu (gyoza, fried chicken/karaage, shumai, stir-fries) leans heavily on wheat flour and wheat-based soy sauce. No gluten-free menu, no celiac accommodation, and no allergen handling is advertised.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan or vegetarian specialist. This is a meat-and-seafood town-Chinese kitchen — char siu, fried chicken, shrimp dumplings, crab omelette, pork trotters — and the ramen broth is animal-based. A few vegetable dishes exist (e.g. a zha cai/celery/dried-tofu salad, mapo-style and stir-fried items) but they typically use meat-based seasoning, soy sauce and shared woks, so a vegan would have to ask directly and expect very limited options.

bio.background: 中華 六六 sits in a renovated Kyoto machiya just west of the Horikawa-Kitaoji crossing, in the Murasakino district a short walk from Daitoku-ji temple. The building once housed a Japanese restaurant; its high ceilings and shoes-off, eel's-nest layout — a few tables by the entrance and a long counter deep in the back — were kept and turned into a warm, retro setting for Chinese home-style cooking, which locals affectionately call '町家中華' (machiya Chinese) rather than ordinary '町中華'.
bio.specialty: The kitchen is known for hearty, generous lunch sets well under 1,000 yen — a fried-chicken (yurin-chi) set, mapo and weekly-rotating set menus — alongside a soy-sauce chuka-soba. That bowl leads with a clean shoyu base carrying a gentle tang and the sweetness of scallion and onion, medium straight noodles, a thick slab of char siu, and menma that one local guide called the best they had ever eaten.
bio.philosophy: A neighborhood kitchen that prizes authentic ingredients — it sources Chinese dried tofu and other goods from China — and leans on volume and comfort over flash, the sort of filling, hospitable lunch that keeps regulars coming back.

anecdotes.text: Reviewers distinguish it from a plain 'town Chinese' by calling it '町家中華' — townhouse Chinese — because the traditional architecture is as much the experience as the food; you take your shoes off at the entrance.
One diner stumbled on the shop walking back from Daitoku-ji, drawn in purely by the machiya frontage, and singled out the bamboo-shoot menma as the finest they had tasted.
A Kyoto food guide praised the volume-packed sub-1,000-yen lunches — 'お腹パンパン、ボリューム満点のランチ' — in a clean, nostalgic space favored by locals.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles are boiled and animal/soy-based broth, gyoza and karaage are prepared in the same small kitchen, so heavy wheat cross-contact is unavoidable for a celiac.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The chuka-soba broth is built on a soy-sauce (醤油) base, which in a town-Chinese kitchen is standard wheat-containing soy sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is meat- and seafood-centered (char siu, fried chicken, shrimp dumplings, crab omelette, pork trotters) with shared woks; vegetable dishes likely use animal-based seasonings.

cuisine: 鶏白湯ラーメン (tori-paitan / chicken white-broth ramen), also Taiwan mazesoba and karaage
gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. The ramen is built on in-house wheat noodles (made from 100% Hokkaido wheat at the group's own noodle workshop), and the menu also features Taiwan mazesoba (wheat) and karaage (wheat-dredged fried chicken). The chicken-paitan broth comes in shio/shoyu/miso tares; the shoyu and miso versions involve wheat-based soy sauce/miso. There is no published gluten-free preparation, dedicated equipment, or allergen-handling information. A celiac diner should not eat here.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The entire concept is chicken: the broth is a chicken-bone paitan simmered with chicken thigh meat and skin, toppings include chicken-thigh chashu, and the shop is paired with karaage. No vegetable or plant-based broth or noodle option is published.
address_ja: 京都府京都市上京区出水町281 永田ビル1F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI from address_ja (永田ビル as 出水町281); coordinates [135.769394, 35.01881] fall within the Kyoto bounding box. Near Keihan Jingu-Marutamachi Station, west of the Kyoto Imperial Palace.
seats_text: 16 seats (8 counter, 8 table)
hours_raw: 11:00-15:00 (L.O. 14:30) / 18:00-23:00 (L.O. 22:30). 定休日：無休（臨時休業する場合があります）— open daily, occasional temporary closures.
closed_days: None (open 7 days a week; occasional irregular temporary closures)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, busy local ramen counter with ticket-machine ordering and self-service water; a little Japanese or pointing helps, and no special accommodations should be expected.
neighborhood: Kamigyo-ku, Demizu-cho — west of the Kyoto Imperial Palace (Gosho), near Keihan Jingu-Marutamachi Station

bio.background: This is the original flagship (一号店) of Kirameki no Tori, opened in 2013 near the Kawaramachi-Marutamachi crossing in Kyoto's Kamigyo ward. From this single counter the brand grew into a chicken-focused group (operated by Kirameki no Mirai Co.) with around two dozen shops across Kyoto, Osaka, Shiga and Nara, plus a noodle workshop in Nishikyo ward — but the Demizu-cho shop remains where it all started.
bio.specialty: Tori-paitan ramen — a creamy chicken white-broth bowl offered in shio, shoyu and miso. The signature shio version is finished with chicken-thigh chashu, white leek, mizuna, thread chili, menma and a slice of lemon for brightness. Taiwan mazesoba and karaage round out the menu.
bio.philosophy: Everything revolves around the chicken: the broth is built from chicken bones simmered with thigh meat and skin for a rich, sweet umami that is full-bodied without being heavy, and the noodles are made in-house from 100% Hokkaido wheat for a fragrant, crisp bite. The group's stated aim is to keep taste and service at a high level even as it expands.

anecdotes.text: The broth is made by carefully simmering chicken bones together with chicken thigh meat and skin, giving a soup with clear chicken richness and sweetness that avoids feeling overly heavy.
The noodles are produced at the group's own noodle workshop in Nishikyo ward using 100% Hokkaido wheat, prized for their aroma and crisp texture.
Opened in 2013 as the very first shop, it became the seed of a chicken-ramen group that now spans Kyoto, Osaka, Shiga and Nara.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen handles wheat ramen noodles, Taiwan mazesoba (wheat noodles) and wheat-dredged karaage, so heavy gluten cross-contact is unavoidable in this environment.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The chicken-paitan bowls are offered in shoyu and miso versions, whose tare uses standard wheat-containing soy sauce/miso; even the shio version is a wheat-noodle dish.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The broth is a chicken-bone paitan simmered with chicken meat and skin and topped with chicken chashu; there is no plant-based broth or noodle option, so no vegan service is possible.

caveats: Verified as a real, currently-operating Kyoto ramen shop and the 2013 flagship of the Kirameki no Tori group (operated by Kirameki no Mirai Co.), so independent=false and category set to OMNI rather than MOM_AND_POP. Cash only; ticket-machine ordering; no reservations. Hours and the no-fixed-closed-day policy are from Tabelog and the official site; occasional temporary closures noted by the shop. No allergen/gluten-free or vegan accommodation information is published — dietary fields are honest "no" based on a wheat-noodle, chicken-broth menu.

cuisine: 町中華 / 中華そば (neighborhood Chinese, Chinese-style ramen)
neighborhood: 上京区 北大路大宮（船岡山・大徳寺前 / 地下鉄鞍馬口駅）
gf_detail: No gluten-free provision. This is an old-school neighborhood Chinese diner whose core dishes — 中華そば (wheat ramen noodles), fried rice, gyoza, ankake/oyster-sauce stir-fries over rice (the signature 御旅丼) — all rely on wheat noodles, wheat-flour dumpling skins and soy-based (wheat-containing) seasonings, typically cooked on shared surfaces and woks. No dedicated gluten-free menu, prep, or fryer was found. Not suitable for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly venue. The kitchen centers on pork, egg and seafood (the signature 御旅丼 is stir-fried pork, cabbage, carrot, onion, wood-ear and egg over rice; menu spans yakiniku, fried shrimp and gyoza). Broths and tare are meat/seafood based. No vegan dishes were identified, and there is no indication the kitchen can prepare a meaningful plant-based meal. A vegan diner should ask, but should not expect a workable option here.
address_ja: 京都府京都市上京区北大路大宮下東側108
geocode_note: Street-number geocode returned empty; used chō-level geocode for 上京区若宮横町 (the chō Tabelog/Yahoo Map list for this address), giving [135.748718, 35.039062] — within the Kyoto box and consistent with the Kitaoji-Omiya / Funaokayama–Daitokuji area.
seats_text: Small old-style shop: roughly 12 counter seats plus a few small tatami sections (~15 total).
hours_raw: 11:00–14:30, 17:30–23:00（水曜定休）。一部情報では昼の開始を11:30、夜の終了を22:00とするものもあり。
closed_days: Wednesdays
cultural_comfort_note: A no-frills local Chinese diner where Japanese-language ordering and casual local manners are the norm; a friendly spot but expect little to no English support.
caveats: Wheat-heavy, meat/seafood-based menu with no GF or vegan provision; cash only; closed Wednesdays. Address sits at street-number level that did not geocode precisely, so the pin reflects the chō (block) rather than the exact storefront.

bio.background: 御旅飯店 is a long-standing neighborhood Chinese diner (町中華) tucked into the old Kitaoji-Omiya quarter near Funaokayama and the Daitokuji temple precinct. Its name comes from the adjacent 御旅所 (otabi-sho) — the resting place for the portable shrine during Imamiya Shrine's festival each May. This pocket of the city flourished as a merchant district in the Edo period and quieted after the early Showa era as the Nishijin textile trade declined, leaving behind exactly the kind of unhurried local shop the restaurant still embodies.
bio.specialty: The house pride is the 御旅丼 (Otabi-don), a local bowl that is, in the words of one writer, 'barely known outside the neighborhood': stir-fried pork with cabbage, carrot, onion, wood-ear mushroom and egg, bound in a Chinese-style sauce over a generous mound of rice (said to hold roughly three servings). The shop also serves a proper 中華そば and offers ramen as an add-on to many dishes.
bio.philosophy: Hearty, affordable, community-first cooking — big portions, free rice refills, and a deep two-sided menu of Chinese standards (fried rice, gyoza, noodles, fried and grilled dishes) priced for everyday eating.

anecdotes.text: The shop sits right by the otabi-sho used during Imamiya Shrine's May festival, which is where its name '御旅' comes from.

It is a narrow, old-school place with counter seats and small tatami sections, stocked with comics, magazines and a TV, full of neighborhood workers and university students; staff move briskly through the lunch rush.
Rice refills are free, and the kitchen still runs deliveries the old way using traditional okamochi carrying boxes.
Known locally for big, well-balanced portions, including the signature 御旅丼.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles, wheat-flour gyoza skins and soy-based seasonings dominate the menu, cooked on shared woks and surfaces in a small kitchen — high cross-contact risk for gluten throughout.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes are seasoned with Chinese-style soy/oyster sauces (the 御旅丼 sauce is described as a Chinese sauce/oyster-sauce base); standard Japanese and Chinese soy sauces contain wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Cooking centers on pork, egg and seafood across stir-fries, fried rice and gyoza; broths and sauces are meat/seafood based, so any vegetable dish would share equipment and seasonings with animal ingredients.

lead: いいちょ ラーメン
name_ja: いいちょ ラーメン
cuisine: あっさり醤油 中華そば（鶏豚合わせ出汁・幻の岩倉ラーメン系譜）
gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The bowl is built on wheat ramen noodles (from Rairaiken/Kairaikian seimen) and a wheat-based soy sauce tare (Sawai twice-fermented "double-koji" shoyu), with no gluten-free menu or allergen accommodation noted anywhere. Treat as off-limits for strict gluten avoidance.
vegan_detail: Not vegan or vegetarian friendly. The signature broth is a chicken-bone and pork-bone blend (roughly 7:3) with back fat and sliced chashu pork; the popular yakimeshi side uses egg and pork fat. No plant-based option is offered.
address_ja: 京都府京都市左京区下鴨東半木町70-10 エイブル東半木 1F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI on the chō-banchi address 京都府京都市左京区下鴨東半木町70-10; first feature coordinates [135.766998, 35.044155], within the Kyoto box.
neighborhood: 左京区下鴨（地下鉄・北大路駅／下鴨）
seats_text: 16 seats: 8 at the counter, 4 at tables, and 4 on a tatami area. All non-smoking.
hours_raw: 11:00-15:30 (L.O. 15:00); closes early if sold out. Lunch-only operation since February 2025. Closed Wednesday and Thursday plus occasional irregular closures. (Prior to Feb 2025 also served dinner 17:30-21:00, L.O. 20:30.)
closed_days: Wednesday and Thursday, plus occasional irregular closures; also closes early once sold out.
cultural_comfort_note: A small, classic neighborhood ramen counter where a few words of Japanese and an easygoing attitude go a long way; no English service is advertised, so a friendly konnichiwa and pointing at the menu work best.
caveats: Since February 2025 the shop runs lunch-only (11:00-15:30); older listings still show evening hours, so do not count on dinner. It sells out and closes early on busy days, takes cash only, and does not accept reservations. One reliable account credits the founder's training to the Yamasan / Iwakura ramen lineage, while the shop's own site separately mentions a long apprenticeship under Kazuo Inamori-named mentorship; the lineage claim is the better-corroborated one and is reflected in the bio.

bio.background: Iicho opened in 1998 in the Shimogamo neighborhood of Sakyo-ku, a short walk east of Kitaoji across the Kamogawa. The shop carries on the lineage of the so-called 'phantom Iwakura ramen' (幻の岩倉ラーメン), a Kyoto street-cart ramen whose flavor passed to Yamasan Ramen before that shop eventually moved away to Shiga; the founder learned the style from that tradition and brought it into the modern era.
bio.specialty: An ashari shoyu chuka-soba: a lightly cloudy broth of chicken bone (specifically the shin) and pork bone in roughly a 7:3 ratio, finished with back fat, thin chashu, and Kyoto negi. The tare is Sawai shoyu, a twice-fermented 'double-koji' soy sauce that gives the bowl its punchy backbone. Medium-thin straight noodles from Rairaiken keep an old-Showa chuka-soba character. The egg-and-pork-fat yakimeshi, with heavily caramelized edges, is a beloved companion.
bio.philosophy: The proprietor sums up his approach as 'people are moved by heart,' treating each day's open as the start of a fresh battle and refining the bowl through constant, patient improvement.

anecdotes.text: The recipe descends from the legendary Iwakura cart ramen, later carried by Yamasan Ramen; Iicho opened in 1998 to keep that disappearing flavor alive.
The broth leans on aged chicken rather than young birds for deeper flavor, with small pork bones added gradually to build umami and exacting quality checks on the ingredients.
The kitchen has used the same Rairaiken/Kairaikian noodles since opening, aiming to keep a strong nostalgic Showa-era chuka-soba feel while still tasting contemporary.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The bowl is built on wheat ramen noodles and a wheat soy-sauce (Sawai shoyu) tare, so gluten is central to the dish; no gluten-free preparation is offered.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The tare is Sawai shoyu, a twice-fermented double-koji soy sauce, which is wheat-based.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is chicken-bone and pork-bone based with back fat and chashu; the yakimeshi uses egg and pork fat. No vegan/vegetarian item exists, so all cooking surfaces and stock are animal-based.

cuisine: 濃厚豚骨醤油ラーメン・まぜそば
neighborhood: 伏見区深草（藤森駅周辺）
address_ja: 京都府京都市伏見区深草キトロ町33-14 トミヤビル1F
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned chō-level coordinates for 深草キトロ町33-14; within the Kyoto bounding box (lat 34.93–35.13, lng 135.66–135.83).
seats_text: L字カウンターのみの小さな店（席数の正確な記載は未確認）
closed_days: 木曜・日曜
cultural_comfort_note: A small local counter ramen shop with cash-only payment and no reservations; some basic Japanese helps for ordering and customization, with little to no English support expected.
gf_detail: This is a standard wheat-noodle ramen and mazesoba shop. Noodles are wheat (from maker 麺屋棣鄂), the tare is a soy-sauce (typically wheat-containing) shoyu base, and the broth is a concentrated pork-bone-and-chicken stock. There is no indication of gluten-free noodles, dishes, or accommodation. Not celiac-safe.
vegan_detail: Every documented bowl is built on an animal base: a concentrated pork-bone (豚骨) and chicken (鶏ガラ) broth, with toppings such as chashu pork, ajitsuke (egg) tamago, and karaage. No vegetarian or vegan ramen option is mentioned anywhere in the sources.

bio.background: 麺屋 誉 (Menya Homare) opened on October 22, 2025 as the reborn version of a neighborhood favorite, フカクサ製麺食堂 (Fukakusa Seimen Shokudo). That predecessor began life humbly as a mazesoba specialist tucked into the corner of a parking lot, later moved into a proper storefront, and built a following on chicken-paitan (鶏白湯) ramen. When it closed at the end of September 2025, the shop reopened weeks later under the new Homare name with a completely retooled bowl.
bio.specialty: 特製濃厚豚骨醤油ラーメン — a concentrated pork-bone-and-chicken soy-sauce ramen with a soy-forward, punchy, smooth and full-bodied broth. The default bowl arrives with two kinds of chashu (a thick, smoky pork and a black-pepper pastrami-style loin), a half-cooked ajitsuke egg, a blend of spinach/komatsuna greens, and sheets of nori, over straight medium-thick custom noodles from the long-established maker 麺屋棣鄂 (Teigaku).
bio.philosophy: A carefully tended broth above all: 豚骨と鶏ガラを手間暇かけた濃厚スープは雑味が少なく、まろやかでとろみのある — a long-simmered pork-and-chicken stock prized for being rich yet clean, with little harshness and a smooth, slightly thick body. Bowls can be tuned to the guest, with noodle firmness, broth saltiness, and chicken-oil volume adjustable to taste.

anecdotes.text: The shop carries forward two things from its フカクサ製麺食堂 days: the popular まぜそば, and a per-piece karaage that guests order by the number (about ¥100 each).
A student discount lets those with a student ID upgrade to a large noodle portion for free — and it applies to the mazesoba too.
The new 濃厚豚骨醤油 bowl reads less like classic Iekei family-style ramen and more like a straight, intense pork-bone-soy ramen, with the soy notes leading.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles, a soy-sauce-based tare, and shared broth/cooking water make cross-contamination with gluten effectively unavoidable for a celiac diner.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature bowl is a 濃厚豚骨醤油 (pork-bone soy-sauce) ramen; the tare is soy-sauce based, which in standard ramen contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broths and dishes are animal-based (pork bone and chicken stock with chashu, egg, and karaage); no vegan preparation or separation is described.

caveats: Business hours differ between sources: the shop's review pages list Mon-Wed/Fri-Sat lunch 11:00-15:00 with Fri-Sat dinner 18:00-21:30 (closed Thu/Sun), while Tabelog lists 11:00-14:00 plus 17:00/17:30-21:00 with only Sunday closed. Hours treated as irregular; confirm directly. Exact seat count not published. official_url is the shop's X (Twitter) account, not a dedicated website. No allergy/dietary accommodation is documented.

lead: 和麺ぐり虎 京都本店
name_ja: 和麺ぐり虎 京都本店
cuisine: 鶏塩・鯛塩ラーメン／つけ麺・まぜそば（塩ラーメン専門）
neighborhood: 中京区 六角通東洞院（烏丸御池／四条烏丸）
gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The custom noodles are made from domestic wheat flour, and the kitchen handles wheat throughout. While the bowls are salt-based (shio) rather than soy-sauce-based, there is no gluten-free menu, no dedicated preparation area, and the same broth/noodle line is used for all dishes. Cross-contact with wheat is unavoidable. The shop also offers a separate fresh-soy-sauce (生醤油) ramen, which would contain wheat. No GF accommodation is advertised.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians. Both signature bowls are animal-based: the chicken-salt (鶏塩) broth is an eight-hour chicken consommé (with added shijimi clam at the Kyoto branch), and the sea-bream-salt (鯛塩) broth is built on wild domestic sea bream. The shio tare also layers in bonito (katsuo) dashi. Toppings include chashu pork and egg. No plant-based broth or vegan bowl is offered.
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区六角通東洞院御射山260 ロイヤルプラザ 1F
geocode_note: Full banchi (御射山260) geocode returned empty from GSI; coordinates are the chō-level centroid for 中京区御射山町 (lng 135.761093, lat 35.006737), within the Kyoto box. Treat as approximate to the block.
seats_text: Counter seating (roughly 10+ seats) plus three 4-person tables / box seats; bright, modern, solo-diner-friendly space. Approximate.
hours_raw: 11:00–22:00 daily (L.O. 21:30); through-service. May close earlier when the soup/ingredients sell out.
closed_days: No regular closing day listed (open 7 days); may close irregularly and when ingredients run out.
cultural_comfort_note: A modern, welcoming counter-and-table ramen shop where ordering is done by QR code at the table, so a few words of Japanese and a friendly greeting go a long way, though the visual menu makes it manageable.

bio.origin: Okayama (original shop); the chef trained in Tokyo
bio.background: Chef Sho Isogai came up as an Italian cook before turning to ramen, apprenticing at celebrated Tokyo shops including Mensho. In 2017 he opened his own 麺処ぐり虎 (Mendokoro Guriko) in Okayama, which went on to earn a Michelin Bib Gourmand and repeat recognition across the Kyoto, Osaka and Okayama guides, plus top finishes at national ramen festivals and appearances at the Tokyo Ramen Show. 和麺ぐり虎 is his second brand, and this Kyoto branch on Rokkaku-dori opened on July 23, 2025 as his first foray into Kyoto.
bio.specialty: Two salt (shio) bowls define the shop. The 鶏塩 (chicken-salt) is a clear, eight-hour chicken consommé — deepened at the Kyoto branch with shijimi clam — finished with a house salt tare that blends salts from Japan, Italy, France and Mongolia and a layered dashi of kombu, shiitake and bonito. The 鯛塩 (sea-bream-salt) showcases wild domestic sea bream cooked low and slow for a clean, aromatic, umami-deep broth, with a tare built on Okinawan and Niigata sea salts. Custom thin straight noodles made from 100% domestic wheat are matched to the soups.
bio.philosophy: Isogai approaches ramen 'like a course,' marrying Italian and Japanese technique and deliberately restraining seasoning so the integrity of carefully chosen ingredients comes through. The 和麺 (wamen) concept centers on preserving and nurturing Japan's exceptional ingredients and food culture.

anecdotes.text: The chef's Italian-kitchen roots shape the salt tare, which draws on salts from Japan, Italy, France and Mongolia — an unusually cosmopolitan seasoning for a Japanese shio ramen.
