The Kyoto branch's chicken-salt bowl adds shijimi clam to the eight-hour chicken consommé for extra depth while keeping the seasoning restrained.
Raw/boiled egg and nori are offered freely at the tables alongside any bowl.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Custom noodles are made from 100% domestic wheat flour and are handled throughout the kitchen; there is no gluten-free option or separate preparation, so wheat cross-contact is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature bowls are salt-based (shio) rather than soy-sauce-based; however, a separate fresh-soy-sauce (生醤油) ramen is also offered, which would contain wheat-based soy sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broths are animal-based (chicken consommé with clam, or wild sea bream) and the tare includes bonito dashi; no plant-based broth is prepared, so there is no vegan-safe bowl.

positives.text: Bowls are shio (salt) tare rather than the usual wheat-shoyu tare, so the base seasoning is comparatively lower in soy/wheat than many ramen — though the wheat noodles still rule it out for celiacs.

caveats: Newly opened (July 23, 2025); seat count is approximate (counter ~10+ plus three 4-tops, estimated ~22). Full banchi geocode failed at GSI, so coordinates are the chō-level centroid for 御射山町 and approximate to the block. Cash-only was noted as in effect (through early August in source); card/electronic payment may begin later — confirm on arrival. Hours can end early when ingredients sell out.

lead: 麺や 鳥の鶏次 KYOTO 四条河原町店
name_ja: 麺や 鳥の鶏次 KYOTO 四条河原町店
name_en: Men-ya Tori no Keiji KYOTO Shijo Kawaramachi
cuisine: 鶏白湯(泡系)ラーメン / Foam-style chicken paitan ramen
neighborhood: 下京区 / 京都河原町駅・四条河原町
address_ja: 京都府京都市下京区真町95-1 トップハットビル 1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of street address returned [135.769882, 35.003975], within the Kyoto box and consistent with the Shijo/Kiyamachi-Kawaramachi location near Kyoto-Kawaramachi Station.
seats_text: 20 seats total, mostly counter plus 3 table seats.
hours_raw: 10:30-00:30 daily (L.O. food 00:00); closes when soup runs out. Lunch sets 10:30-15:00, dinner sets 15:00-21:00.
closed_days: Open daily; no regular closed day listed. May close early if the soup sells out.
cultural_comfort_note: A modern, central ramen shop with QR-code mobile ordering, so a tourist can manage with minimal Japanese, but the interface and staff are primarily Japanese-oriented.
vegan_detail: This is a chicken-paitan specialist; the entire concept is a broth simmered for 8+ hours from domestic chicken and water, with chicken chashu toppings. There are no plant-based broth or noodle options noted. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
gf_detail: Standard ramen: wheat (medium-thick wavy) noodles plus shio/shoyu tare that typically contains wheat-based soy sauce. No gluten-free noodle or dedicated-handling options are advertised, and rice-bowl sides also involve sauces. Not safe for celiac diners; treat as not gluten-free.

bio.background: An Osaka-born chicken-paitan specialist that built a reputation for long queues at its original shop and grew into a small group (with locations across the Osaka/Sakai area, Nara and Tokyo) before opening this, its first Kyoto outpost, on September 12, 2025, on a busy corner near Shijo-Kiyamachi just steps from Kyoto-Kawaramachi Station.
bio.specialty: The house style is an 泡系 (foam-style) chicken paitan: fresh domestic chicken and water simmered for over eight hours into a thick, creamy stock that is whipped/aerated to order into a frothy, cappuccino-like soup, served over smooth medium-thick wavy noodles with low-temperature chicken chashu (both breast and thigh). It anchors a lineup of variations including tori-shio and tori-shoyu, Fromage (cheese), Truffle, Red (chili oil), Black (mayu/burnt garlic oil) and Green (matcha), plus chicken rice-bowl sides.
bio.philosophy: Let the chicken speak for itself: a broth built from only chicken and water, then aerated so the richness lands light and silky rather than heavy.

anecdotes.text: Described as a popular Osaka shop known for daily queues, making this Kyoto location its first foray into the city, opening September 12, 2025.
The broth is made from only fresh domestic chicken and water, simmered for over eight hours, then blended/aerated just before serving into an 'awa-awa' (foamy) finish.
The Kyoto store launched with upgraded versions of the signature chicken paitan and items available only at this location.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Tori-shoyu (soy-sauce) bowls use a soy-based tare; Japanese shoyu is normally brewed with wheat. No gluten-free tare is advertised.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broths are chicken-based (chicken + water simmered 8+ hours) and toppings include chicken chashu, so there is no vegan-safe preparation; cross-contact with animal stock is inherent to every bowl.

caveats: Confirmed open (opened 2025-09-12). It is part of the KEIJI PROJECT group (Osaka-origin, multiple branches), so categorized OMNI / not independent. Dietary findings are inferred from the standard ramen format and the chicken-only broth concept; no shop-published allergen or gluten-free/vegan documentation was found, so safety confidence is low. Hours are uniform daily 10:30-00:30 and the shop may close early when soup runs out.

lead: ぎょうざ処 高辻 亮昌 本店
name_ja: ぎょうざ処 高辻 亮昌 本店
cuisine: 町中華・餃子(京餃子専門店)
neighborhood: 下京区 高辻 / 四条烏丸・烏丸駅近く
gf_detail: 餃子専門店で、看板の亮昌ぎょうざはもちもちの特製小麦皮で包まれており、グルテンフリー対応はありません。和だし・味噌の合わせだれや調味料にも小麦由来成分が含まれる可能性が高く、セリアック病など厳格なグルテンフリーが必要な方には適しません。グルテンフリーや小麦アレルギー対応に関する公式情報は確認できませんでした。
vegan_detail: 主力商品は京もち豚のミンチを使った肉餃子で、和だしも鰹ベース。九条ねぎなど京野菜を多用しますが、ビーガン・ベジタリアン向けの餃子や献立は確認できませんでした。明確な植物性メニューはなく、対応可否は要問い合わせです。
address_ja: 〒600-8446 京都府京都市下京区高辻通新町西入堀之内町263 1F
geocode_note: 番地レベル（堀之内町263）ではGSIジオコーダーが結果を返さなかったため、町丁目レベル（下京区堀之内町）の代表点を使用。京都ボックス内（lat 35.00, lng 135.76）で店舗の実位置に近い概略座標です。
seats_text: 全24席（カウンター4席、テーブル16席、テラス4席）
closed_days: 定休日なし（年中無休）
cultural_comfort_note: 英語メニューを用意しており、四条烏丸の人気餃子専門店として観光客にも慣れているため、英語のみでも比較的安心して利用できます。
caveats: 重要: このターゲットはラーメン店ではなく、京餃子の専門店（町中華・餃子）です。麺類（ラーメン）は提供していません。看板メニューは京もち豚と九条ねぎを使い、鰹だしに「山利」の味噌を効かせた「亮昌ぎょうざ」。食べログ百名店（餃子）に選出。座席数・席種・地理座標などは確認できた範囲の値で、住所はジオコーダーで町丁目レベルにフォールバックしています。

bio.background: 「京都生まれ京都育ち」を掲げる京餃子の専門店で、四条烏丸にほど近い高辻通沿いに本店を構えます。運営はMASTERMIND社で、地産地消を信条に京都産の食材で餃子をつくることにこだわっています。
bio.specialty: 看板の『亮昌ぎょうざ』。鰹だしをベースにした合わせ和だしで上品に仕立てた京もち豚のミンチに、香り豊かな九条ねぎを合わせ、隠し味に老舗『山利』の味噌をほんのり効かせ、もちもちの特製の皮で包んでいます。
bio.philosophy: 中華の調味料ではなく和のだしと味噌で仕上げる『和のぎょうざ』。にんにくの匂いを抑え、たれをほとんど付けずに素材の味で食べられるよう、女性や幅広い層が楽しめる上品な味わいを目指しています。
roles: テイクアウト・冷凍餃子販売

anecdotes.text: 京都市長賞を受賞した伏見・中嶋農園のキャベツや京もち豚、京の伝統野菜である九条ねぎなど、できる限り京都の地のものを使用している。
黒く焙煎した七味と千鳥酢のたれが添えられ、にんにく不使用で食べやすいと評される。

gf_cross_contamination.text: 餃子専門店であり、すべての主力商品が小麦の皮を使用するため、厨房は小麦が常用される環境です。グルテンフリー対応・分離調理に関する記載は確認できませんでした。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: 和だしと味噌をベースにしたたれや調味料を使用しており、醤油・味噌など小麦を含みうる発酵調味料が用いられている可能性があります。具体的な原材料の公開情報は確認できませんでした。

vegan_cross_contact.text: 京もち豚のミンチや鰹だしを中核に使用する肉餃子の専門店で、植物性メニューの分離・専用調理に関する情報は確認できませんでした。

positives.text: 英語メニューを用意しており、にんにく不使用の餃子のため、にんにくを避けたい人には案内しやすい。

cuisine: Town-Chinese / Sichuan (machi-chuka) with chuka-soba ramen, tantanmen and rice bowls
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation. This is a Chinese kitchen built around wheat: the ramen and tantanmen use wheat noodles, dumplings/dim sum are wheat-wrapped, and dishes are seasoned with wheat-based soy sauce and Chinese sauces. Broths and stir-fries are meat/seafood based and not designed for celiac safety. No dedicated fryer or allergen protocol is documented. Not suitable for strict gluten-free diners.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly venue. The menu centers on pork (the signature teri-don is glazed pork), wagyu mapo tofu, chicken, shrimp and other seafood, and meat/seafood-based broths and tare. Even ostensibly vegetable dishes are likely cooked with shared woks, meat fats and animal-based seasonings. No vegan options are documented.
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区西ノ京星池町149
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned coordinates for 京都府京都市中京区西ノ京星池町149番地; within the Kyoto box.
neighborhood: 中京区 西ノ京 / 二条駅すぐ (Nishinokyo, near JR Nijo Station)
seats_text: About 30 seats; a small, perpetually busy neighborhood place that often has a queue.
hours_raw: 11:30-14:15 (lunch L.O. ~14:20), 17:30-21:00 (dinner L.O. ~20:30); closed Tuesdays plus irregular/biweekly Wednesdays. Sources vary slightly on the dinner close time (20:30-22:30 reported across listings); confirm by phone for late dinner.
closed_days: Tuesdays; some Wednesdays (irregular)
cultural_comfort_note: A busy local Chinese eatery with Japanese-language menus and no indication of English support, so a little Japanese or pointing at the menu helps; come ready to queue and order quickly at peak times.
caveats: Often referred to as 中國菜 大鵬 as well as 中華料理 大鵬. While locals know it as a machi-chuka where you can get a bowl of chuka-soba ramen, the kitchen is best known today for Sichuan cooking and its teri-don rice bowl rather than as a dedicated ramen shop. Dinner closing time varies between sources (listed anywhere from 20:30 to 22:30 last order); the 21:00 close used here is the most commonly cited recent figure. Closed Tuesdays with some Wednesdays off, so confirm before a midweek visit.

bio.background: Taiho opened in 1974 near Nijo Station and has been a fixture of Kyoto's machi-chuka (neighborhood Chinese) scene for about half a century. It began as a classic town-Chinese counter serving ramen, fried rice, gyoza and other everyday favorites; over the years a second-generation cook traveled to Sichuan in China and brought authentic Sichuan techniques back into the kitchen, gradually turning the shop into a place where old-school Kyoto machi-chuka and genuine Sichuan cooking sit side by side on the same menu.
bio.specialty: The house signature is the 'teri-don' (teridon) - a rice bowl crowned with sweet-savory teriyaki-glazed pork. It comes in several forms, most famously the 'teri-don kinshi' topped with shredded egg, plus an oversized 'jigoku-mori' (hell portion) for big appetites. Alongside it the menu spans ramen and chuka-soba, dan-dan noodles, hot-and-sour noodles, fried rice, tenshin-han and Chinese rice bowls, and Sichuan plates like mapo tofu made with Omi-area wagyu and Shanghai-style sweet-and-sour pork.
bio.philosophy: Bridging humble Kyoto town-Chinese comfort food with serious Sichuan cooking - keeping the beloved old standards like teri-don while layering in authentic regional Chinese flavors.

anecdotes.text: Founded in 1974 and marking its 50th anniversary, Taiho has earned a Michelin Bib Gourmand and is known as a small shop with a near-constant queue at both lunch and dinner.

A second-generation cook visited Sichuan and introduced new dishes, transforming what was a standard machi-chuka into a place where town-Chinese and Sichuan cuisine are combined; the original teri-don remains the signature.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat-based noodles (ramen, chuka-soba, dan-dan, hot-and-sour noodles) and wheat-wrapped dumplings/dim sum are core to the menu, with heavy use of wheat soy sauce and Chinese sauces in a shared wok kitchen - high gluten exposure throughout.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Chinese-style cooking with soy and bean-paste seasonings; standard wheat-containing soy sauce should be assumed. No wheat-free tare or sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is built on pork, wagyu, chicken and seafood, with meat/seafood broths and shared woks; vegetable dishes are very likely cooked with animal fats and meat-based seasonings.

lead: 中華そば 六（旧店名 鴨出汁中華そば ROKU）
cuisine: 鴨だし中華そば（清湯・白湯／追いワンタン）
gf_detail: This is a Chinese-style ramen (chuka soba) shop built on wheat noodles and a multi-bone broth (duck, local chicken, venison, beef, pork) with a wheat-based shoyu/tare seasoning. Both the noodles and the savory seasoning contain wheat, and there is no gluten-free menu, dedicated equipment, or allergen accommodation noted. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: The entire concept is animal-based: the signature broth is drawn from five kinds of bones (duck, chicken, venison, beef, pork) and bowls come topped with meat wontons and chashu-style proteins. There are no vegan or vegetarian ramen options listed.
address_ja: 京都府京都市下京区河原町通四条下ル2丁目稲荷町318-6 GOOD NATURE STATION 2F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI on the chō-level address (稲荷町318-6); point falls within the Kyoto bounding box at the GOOD NATURE STATION block near Kawaramachi-Shijo.
neighborhood: 河原町四条（下京区・GOOD NATURE STATION 2F）
seats_text: 12 seats (counter)
hours_raw: 11:00-15:00 (L.O. 14:30) / 18:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:30). Closed Wednesdays plus irregular holidays; service ends when the day's broth runs out.
closed_days: Wednesdays, plus irregular closures (and early sell-out when broth runs out)
cultural_comfort_note: A compact 12-seat counter ramen bar inside a polished, partly English-friendly retail complex; a few words of Japanese and pointing at the bowl you want go a long way, and ordering is straightforward.

bio.origin: Kyoto (河原町四条, inside GOOD NATURE STATION)
bio.background: 中華そば 六 is the casual ramen project of Yuji Iwasaki, the chef behind VELROSIER, the two-Michelin-star modern Chinese restaurant in the same Kyoto complex. It opened in December 2020 as 鴨出汁中華そば ROKU and was relaunched in May 2023 under the name 中華そば 六, refocusing the menu on Chinese-style chuka soba.
bio.specialty: A signature chuka soba whose clear, layered broth is drawn from five kinds of bones — duck, local chicken, venison, beef and pork — and deepened with umami ingredients such as porcini, longan, tomato and dried shiitake, served with straight noodles made to match the clear soup and finished with house-made wontons.
bio.philosophy: Iwasaki applies fine-dining Chinese cooking technique to a humble bowl of ramen, building broths with the depth and complexity of his Michelin kitchen while keeping the format approachable and affordable.

anecdotes.text: The shop is the sister establishment of VELROSIER, the adjacent two-Michelin-star Chinese restaurant, and the broth famously blends five kinds of bones with ingredients like porcini, longan, tomato and dried shiitake.
Launched in late 2020 as 鴨出汁中華そば ROKU and rebranded to 中華そば 六 in May 2023, the shop has earned a place in the Michelin Bib Gourmand guide.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The bowls are seasoned with a savory shoyu-style tare, and standard Japanese soy sauce is brewed with wheat; no wheat-free seasoning is offered.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Every bowl is built on an animal-bone broth (duck, chicken, venison, beef, pork) and topped with meat wontons, so vegan diners cannot be accommodated.

caveats: Tiny 12-seat counter that can sell out and close early when the broth runs out, plus irregular holidays on top of the regular Wednesday closure — go early. Strongly animal-based and wheat-based; no gluten-free or vegan options.

lead: 京都和麺 諭吉本舗
name_ja: 京都和麺 諭吉本舗
cuisine: 和風中華そば（鶏・帆立出汁、トリュフオイル、A5和牛ステーキ）
neighborhood: 中京区・寺町通蛸薬師（京都河原町駅徒歩3分）
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区寺町通蛸薬師上る東側町506-1
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned [135.751389, 35.009998] for the full chō-level address; within the Kyoto box.
seats_text: Counter seating only — an L-shaped counter wrapping the open kitchen. Exact seat count not published.
closed_days: 無休（年中無休）
cultural_comfort_note: A small counter-only shop with a premium, theatrical wagyu-and-ramen format; basic Japanese helps and the bill runs high, so set expectations before going.
gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The bowls are built on wheat ramen noodles with a soy/dashi tare and chicken-scallop broth; no gluten-free menu or accommodation is advertised. Avoid if you require strict GF.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly option. The entire concept centers on chicken-and-scallop dashi broth topped with A5 wagyu beef steak; there is no vegan ramen or plant-based offering.
caveats: Bowls are priced from roughly 3,000 yen up to 30,000 yen for top-grade wagyu options — well above a normal ramen budget. Counter-only seating, can have a wait.

roles: owner-operator (from Kyotango / Amino, north Kyoto Prefecture)
bio.origin: An offshoot of the 諭吉 (Yukichi) ramen group, which began in Mito, Ibaraki as 特選中華蕎麦諭吉 and has grown into a multi-city brand; this Teramachi shop opened on September 24, 2024.
bio.background: The Yukichi brand grew out of a father-and-son project — its noodles and broth were developed together over long experimentation, with the founders describing their signature as a creation 'built by my father and me.' The Kyoto Teramachi branch was opened by a veteran restaurateur from the Kyotango (Amino) area as part of the group's urban expansion.
bio.specialty: 和麺 (Wamen) — a refined Japanese take on chicken-and-scallop chuka-soba, finished with truffle oil for an almost French aroma, and crowned with A5 wagyu beef seared tableside on a hot iron griddle (served rare inside, charred outside).
bio.philosophy: Nearly all components — noodles, broth, tare — are made in-house, with noodles served the same day they are made for maximum freshness; the bowl is conceived as a high-end, theatrical fusion of teppan wagyu and ramen.

anecdotes.text: The flagship gesture is a slab of A5 wagyu flambeed tableside over flame on a large iron griddle, seared outside and rare within, then set over the bowl.
The founders describe their ramen as 'a confident creation built by my father and me' (父と私で作り上げた自信作です), with almost every ingredient homemade and noodles served same-day.
The Teramachi shop was opened by a senior restaurateur from Kyotango City's Amino district as part of the brand's move into the city.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Bowls use a soy-sauce / dashi tare typical of Japanese chuka-soba, which standardly contains wheat-based soy sauce; no wheat-free tare is advertised.

cuisine: つけ麺・ラーメン
neighborhood: 北区・鞍馬口駅
gf_detail: This is a classic wheat-noodle ramen and tsukemen shop. The noodles are sourced from a renowned Tokyo wheat-noodle maker (Asakusa Kaika-ro), the salt/shoyu tare is almost certainly wheat-based, and the broth is a chicken-and-seafood clear soup. There is no indication of any gluten-free menu, dedicated equipment, or allergy accommodation. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: The entire concept is built on a chicken-based (鶏清湯) broth balanced with seafood (sardine, bonito, mackerel, kombu, clam). Signature bowls are chicken ramen, salt ramen, clam ramen, and tsukemen. No vegan or vegetarian options are indicated; the broth itself is animal-based, so even plain noodles would carry meat/fish stock.
address_ja: 京都府京都市上京区烏丸鞍馬口下る上御霊中町456-6 大国ビル1F
geocode_note: Geocoded from address_ja via GSI; coordinates [135.759888, 35.0373] fall within the Kyoto box.
seats_text: 8席（カウンター）
closed_days: 水曜日
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny 8-seat counter ramen shop where cash is the only payment and a few words of Japanese (or pointing at the ticket/menu) will carry you through; come prepared rather than expecting English support.
caveats: Cash only, no cards or e-money. Only 8 counter seats, so expect a queue at peak hours. Not suitable for gluten-free or vegan diners.

bio.background: Opened in April 2019 on Karasuma street just south of Kuramaguchi subway station, a short walk from Doshisha University. The owner previously cooked at Gokukei, a well-known rich-broth ramen shop in Kyoto's Ichijoji district, and deliberately struck out in the opposite direction with his own place.
bio.specialty: A light, refined clear soup (淡麗系) built on a chicken base brought into exquisite balance with seafood — dried sardine, bonito, mackerel and Rausu kombu — described as having the elegance of a kaiseki soup course. Signature bowls include the chicken chuka soba, salt soba, clam (shijimi) soba, and tsukemen, paired with thick noodles from the famous Tokyo maker Asakusa Kaika-ro. A seasonal cold salt soba uses a salt tare aged for a month.
bio.philosophy: Rather than chasing richness, the shop pursues simplicity and elegance — meticulously crafted bowls where a clean, delicate broth is the point.

anecdotes.text: The owner trained at Gokukei, a popular heavy-broth shop in Ichijoji, then opened Menya Sakamoto with a markedly different, light-and-refined direction.
The clear broth balances chicken with sardine, bonito, mackerel and Rausu kombu, aiming for a taste likened to the soup course of a traditional Japanese kaiseki restaurant.
Noodles are sourced from Asakusa Kaika-ro, a renowned Tokyo noodle maker.

cuisine: つけ麺・ラーメン (Tsukemen / Ramen)
neighborhood: 左京区・一乗寺（叡電一乗寺駅）
address_ja: 京都府京都市左京区一乗寺東杉ノ宮町18-1 杉ノ宮ビル1F
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned [135.78714, 35.046444] for the street address; within Kyoto box.
seats_text: 16 seats total: roughly 12 counter seats arranged in a "し" shape wrapping around the kitchen, plus one table for about 4 in the back.
hours_raw: 11:30-15:00, 18:00-24:00 (L.O.); closed irregularly
closed_days: Irregular closures (no fixed regular closing day published).
cultural_comfort_note: A busy, ticket-machine local ramen shop where staff focus on the line and the bowls, so a little Japanese and ramen-counter familiarity help; no English service is indicated.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The house-made noodles are wheat (100% domestic / Hokkaido wheat flour) and the broth is a pork-bone-and-chicken tonkotsu-shoyu, with a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare that in Japan almost always contains wheat. Tsukemen and mazesoba are likewise wheat noodles. No gluten-free options are indicated. Not suitable for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. The core menu is built on a pork-bone and chicken-carcass broth simmered for about two days, with chashu pork and other animal toppings; tsukemen uses a tonkotsu-seafood (gyokai) dipping broth. No plant-based or vegetable broth is indicated.
caveats: Hours are reported consistently as 11:30-15:00 and 18:00-24:00 (one source lists 18:00-23:00); closures are irregular, so confirm before a special trip. Official site http://bishiya.com/ has a TLS certificate name mismatch and could not be fetched directly; details corroborated via Tabelog and Japanese ramen blogs.

bio.background: Bishiya opened on December 10, 2011 in Ichijoji, Kyoto's famous "ramen battleground" street near Eizan Railway's Ichijoji Station. It occupies a quirky, irregularly shaped corner space that gives the room an unconventional layout, with counter seats wrapping the open kitchen in a "し" curve. It has grown into one of the district's reliably busy, queue-forming shops.
bio.specialty: Its signature is a rich tonkotsu-shoyu ramen: pork bones and chicken carcass simmered for about two days into a deep, soy-forward broth, paired with house-made noodles milled from 100% domestic (Hokkaido) wheat. The tsukemen is equally beloved, with a thick pork-bone-and-seafood dipping broth carrying a subtle sweetness, four thick chashu slices, and an optional yuzu-scented finishing rice. Crispy karaage is a popular side.
bio.philosophy: Though regulars and review sites often peg it as a 'family-style' (iekei) shop because of the thick straight noodles and tonkotsu-shoyu look, the shop is clear that it is its own tonkotsu-shoyu ramen, not iekei: as they put it, the taste is entirely different despite the visual resemblance. The house-milled noodles are described as flat and notably silky, a departure from Kyoto's typically firmer style.

anecdotes.text: Bishiya pushes back on being labeled a family-style (iekei) ramen shop, insisting its tonkotsu-shoyu is a different thing entirely despite looking similar.
The broth is built by simmering pork bones and chicken carcass for roughly two days, and the noodles are made in-house from 100% domestic wheat with an unusually flat, silky texture.
The tsukemen pairs a rich tonkotsu-seafood dipping broth with a subtle sweetness, four thick chashu slices, and an optional yuzu-flavored finishing rice.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The ramen is a tonkotsu-shoyu (soy-sauce based) style; soy-sauce tare in Japanese ramen almost always contains wheat, on top of the wheat-flour noodles themselves.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is pork-bone and chicken-carcass based with pork chashu and animal toppings throughout; no separate vegetable/vegan preparation is indicated.

cuisine: 中華そば (old-school Kyoto-style Chinese noodles / town ramen) — chicken-and-pork-bone shoyu broth with a pronounced ginger note
gf_detail: This is a single-bowl old-school chuka soba shop. The noodles are wheat, and the shoyu tare and chicken-pork broth would almost certainly contain wheat-based soy sauce. There is no indication of any gluten-free noodle, broth, or accommodation. Not suitable for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: The one signature bowl is built on a chicken-gara / pork-bone shoyu broth and is topped with grilled pork (chashu). There is no vegan or vegetarian option; the shop serves essentially a single classic meat-and-bone ramen.
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区壬生相合町20-20
geocode_note: Geocoded from address_ja 京都府京都市中京区壬生相合町20-20 via GSI; first feature returned [135.745804, 34.999989], within the Kyoto box. Note: the target brief listed the area as 壬生愛宕町, but all sources (Tabelog, shop listings) give the actual address as 壬生相合町.
neighborhood: 中京区壬生（四条大宮）
seats_text: Counter only (L-shaped counter); exact seat count not published.
closed_days: Wednesday and Thursday (Thursday added from April 2026; originally Wednesday only)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny counter-only neighborhood ramen shop run by locals; expect a Japanese-only menu and a quiet, regulars-focused atmosphere, so a little Japanese or a translation app goes a long way.
caveats: Cash only; no reservations; counter seating only and small, so expect possible queues at peak. The brief's listed address (壬生愛宕町) does not match sources — the verified address is 壬生相合町20-20.

bio.background: 双鳩 (Soukyu) opened on November 11, 2022, in the exact spot once occupied by 珍元 (Chingen/Chinmoto), a legendary Kyoto chuka soba shop near Shijo-Omiya that closed in 2018. The owner was not a trained restaurateur but a devoted regular who ate at 珍元 for some 30 years; driven by the wish to taste that bowl again, he reopened the space and rebuilt the recipe with guidance from people connected to the original shop.
bio.specialty: A single classic chuka soba: medium-thin wheat noodles in a chicken-and-pork-bone shoyu broth lifted by a clear note of ginger, topped with hand-cut green onion, grilled pork, and menma (bamboo shoots) — light enough to eat every day.
bio.philosophy: Faithful revival rather than reinvention: recreate the nostalgic, comforting flavor of a lost local institution as closely as possible, keeping the offering simple and honest.

anecdotes.text: The predecessor 珍元 is said to have been a model for Juzo Itami's 1985 ramen film 'Tampopo'; 双鳩 carries that lineage forward in the same location.
The owner spent about 30 years as a 珍元 regular and learned the flavor from those close to the original shop, refining the broth over time to bring it ever closer to the remembered taste.
The shop collaborates with neighbors — for example a curry ramen created together with nearby curry shop ムジャラ (Mujara).

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles boiled in shared water, with a shoyu-based tare and chicken/pork broth — gluten is inherent to the dish and unavoidable here.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is a shoyu (soy sauce) base; standard Japanese shoyu contains wheat, and no wheat-free tare is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Animal-based throughout — chicken-and-pork-bone broth with grilled pork topping; no vegan preparation exists.

cuisine: 中華そば・ネギラーメン（ブラック中華・辛まぜそばなど）
neighborhood: 中京区・二条
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区西ノ京永本町23-20
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned coordinates within the Kyoto box; matches the address west of JR/subway Nijo Station.
seats_text: 11 seats total (7 counter, 4 table)
closed_days: 月曜日・祝日（月曜が祝日の場合は翌日休みとなることあり）
cultural_comfort_note: A small counter-focused independent shop with ticket-machine ordering and cash-only payment; a few words of Japanese and pointing at the machine will get you through comfortably.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The bowls use wheat ramen noodles (the Chinese soba uses thin Hokkaido-wheat noodles) and a wheat-based soy sauce (shoyu) tare, so the noodles, tare, and likely the broth all contain gluten. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is indicated, and cross-contact in a single shared ramen kitchen would be unavoidable. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. Broths are animal-based (the current Chinese soba uses a clear pork broth; earlier versions used chicken and oyster), and bowls feature meat toppings such as chashu and Omi-beef tendon rice. No vegan or vegetarian ramen is offered.

bio.background: A small, design-conscious ramen shop tucked into a quiet block west of Nijo Station in Nakagyo Ward. The name 霽レ空 (Haresora) evokes the clear sky that breaks open after rain, and the interior carries that mood: bright, minimal, and calm, with a short counter and a couple of tables that welcome solo diners.
bio.specialty: The shop built its reputation on a clear, elegant chuka soba, originally famous for an oyster-and-niboshi bowl in which a clear chicken broth was perfumed with oyster, balanced by Maruta soy sauce and thin-medium noodles milled from Hokkaido wheat. When the oyster bowl was retired, the kitchen reorganized around three pillars: a refined Chinese soba (reworked into a clear pork broth), a rich Negi (green onion) ramen promoted from a beloved limited-run dish to a permanent fixture, and a spicy maze soba.
bio.philosophy: An ingredient-driven, fragrance-first approach: regulars describe being pulled inside by the aroma of soy as they walk in, and the bowls are praised for their clean, gentle beauty rather than heaviness. The team is willing to evolve the menu rather than coast on a single signature, retiring the famous oyster bowl when sourcing made it untenable.

anecdotes.text: The shop's signature was once a one-day-limited oyster soba, with a clear chicken broth scented by dried oyster that diners could dissolve gradually as they ate; it was retired in late 2024 when the kitchen reformulated its menu.

After dropping the oyster bowl, the menu was rebuilt around three mains: the Chinese soba was changed from a clear chicken broth to a clear pork broth, the popular Negi ramen graduated from a limited item to a regular, and the spicy maze soba's extra heat became a paid option.
Diners note the noodles are flat, medium-thin, and have a satisfying grip, with black pepper and a chili condiment on the counter to adjust the bowl.

caveats: Sunday hours run a morning service (08:00-14:00 per the listing) rather than the weekday split shift; hours and the lineup of seasonal/limited bowls change, so confirm before a special trip. Menu has shifted notably since opening (oyster bowl retired; broth reworked to pork), so older write-ups may be out of date.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Single small ramen kitchen serving wheat-noodle bowls with shoyu tare; shared boiling water and prep make gluten cross-contact effectively unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Bowls are seasoned with a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare, noted as using Maruta soy sauce, which is wheat-containing.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths are animal-based (clear pork broth in the current Chinese soba; earlier chicken/oyster), with meat toppings, so no vegan-safe preparation exists.

cuisine: 担々麺専門店（温・冷あり） / Tantan-men (dandan noodle) specialist, hot and cold versions
gf_detail: This is a tantan-men (dandan noodle) specialist. The bowl is built on wheat egg-style ramen noodles in a sesame-based broth seasoned with a soy/miso meat tare, which conventionally contains wheat. There is no indication of any gluten-free noodle, broth, or menu option, and no allergen handling is advertised. Not suitable for celiacs or those needing strict gluten avoidance.
vegan_detail: The signature tantan-men is topped with seasoned meat miso (pork) and the broth/tare are animal-derived; chashu and crab/egg fried-rice sides are also meat- and seafood-based. No vegan or vegetarian version of the noodles is offered or advertised. Not a vegan-friendly destination.
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区四条通千本西入ル壬生花井町23 ファミール四条 1F
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned a single feature for 京都府京都市中京区壬生花井町23番地 at [135.741852, 35.004013], within the Kyoto bounding box. High confidence.
neighborhood: 中京区壬生 / 四条大宮・西院エリア
seats_text: 14 counter seats (counter-only)
hours_raw: 11:30-15:00 (closes early when sold out / 売切れ次第閉店). Thursday closed; Tuesday irregular closure (火曜不定休). One source also lists a 17:30-21:00 dinner service, but this could not be consistently confirmed.
closed_days: Thursdays; Tuesday irregular closure
cultural_comfort_note: A small, no-frills counter shop run as a local couple's operation with cash-only payment and no reservations; a little Japanese and an easygoing, queue-friendly attitude will make the visit smoother.
caveats: Founding year (the prompt's "2000年創業") could not be confirmed from any fetched source, so it is omitted. Hours reflect the consistently confirmed lunch service (11:30-15:00); a dinner block (17:30-21:00) appears in one blog source only and is treated as unconfirmed. The shop closes early once sold out. Prices observed across sources vary by year (signature bowl listed around 700-800 yen historically).

bio.background: A long-running, much-loved tantan-men specialist tucked into a small storefront on Shijo-dori, in the Mibu quarter of Kyoto's Nakagyo Ward between Shijo-Omiya and Saiin. It is a tiny, counter-only neighborhood shop where regulars line up at lunch, and many say that once they've had this tantan-men they can't eat it anywhere else.
bio.specialty: The signature tantan-men: thin noodles in a creamy, sesame-forward broth crowned with seasoned meat miso, bok choy and green onion. The flavor leads mild and mellow, then reveals a gentle, umami-rich heat as you eat. In warmer months a chilled hiyashi tantan-men appears, swapping broccoli in for the greens at a slightly milder spice level. The crab-ankake lettuce fried rice is a beloved companion to the noodles.
bio.philosophy: A focused, single-dish craft: a sesame-cream broth balanced so it stays drinkable to the last drop rather than overwhelming with chili heat.

anecdotes.text: Regulars describe the broth as mild and so well-balanced that they finish it every time, and say the shop's tantan-men has spoiled them for any other.
Frequently cited as one of the noodle shops that defines Kyoto's tantan-men, with a creamy sesame broth that clings to its thin noodles.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Tantan-men is built on wheat noodles with a sesame broth and a soy/miso-based meat tare that conventionally contains wheat; no gluten-free preparation is offered or noted.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The bowl is topped with pork meat miso and uses an animal-based broth/tare; no vegan or vegetarian version is available.

lead: 京都らーめん おおの
name_ja: 京都らーめん おおの
cuisine: 京都ラーメン（背脂チャッチャ醤油。あっさり/こってり選択可）
neighborhood: 右京区 西院（阪急 西院駅 徒歩圏）
gf_detail: This is a classic Kyoto-style ramen shop: wheat-flour noodles in a pork-bone (tonkotsu) broth seasoned with a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare, which is virtually always made with wheat. There is no gluten-free menu, no rice-noodle option, and no indication of allergen handling. The kitchen is a small counter operation cooking noodles, gyoza, karaage, and fried rice in shared equipment, so cross-contact with wheat is unavoidable. Not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
vegan_detail: Every bowl is built on an animal base — pork-bone broth, back fat, chashu pork, and soy tare — and the side menu centers on karaage, gyoza, and chashu-egg rice. There is no plant-based broth or vegan ramen offered, and no menu flexibility advertised. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
address_ja: 京都府京都市右京区西院南寿町18-2 メゾン・ラ・ソワ 101
geocode_note: GSI geocode of street-level address (西院南寿町18-2) returned [135.729279, 34.996368], within the Kyoto box.
seats_text: About 8 counter seats plus 2 table seats (approximately 10 total).
closed_days: Mondays
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, regulars-driven neighborhood ramen counter with no English support expected — point at the photo menu, order, pay cash, and you'll be fine.

bio.origin: Saiin, Ukyo Ward — a short walk from Hankyu Saiin Station along Gojo-dori in western Kyoto.
bio.background: A small, lively neighborhood ramen counter serving the old-school Kyoto style, with a handful of counter seats and a couple of tables. It draws a steady crowd of regulars who come for time-honored, comforting bowls rather than trend-chasing.
bio.specialty: The signature 'Ono Ramen' — a rich, deeply savory bowl of back-fat soy-sauce (seabura shoyu) ramen, the quintessential Kyoto style. The broth is pork-bone based and seasoned with a punchy soy tare, finished with a scattering of back fat, plenty of green onion, tender chashu, and menma. The straight, low-hydration noodles have a firm, crisp 'patsun' bite that stands up to the bold broth. A lighter, smoother 'Kyoto Ramen' is also offered, and you can lean toward assari (lighter) or kotteri (richer).
bio.philosophy: Classic, value-driven Kyoto ramen done the way it always has been — generous sets pairing the bowl with karaage and fried rice, an everyday-dining feel that sits somewhere between a shokudo, an izakaya, and a ramen-ya.

anecdotes.text: Bloggers describe it as 'natsukashii shinise no aji' — the comforting, time-honored flavor of an old-school shop — and note a weekday perk of a complimentary boiled egg for each customer.
One visitor characterized the room's easygoing, hybrid atmosphere as 'part cafeteria, part izakaya, part ramen shop,' making it an easy casual stop.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles, gyoza, karaage, and fried rice are all cooked in a small shared kitchen, so wheat cross-contact is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The defining seasoning is a back-fat soy-sauce (shoyu) tare; Japanese soy sauce is virtually always brewed with wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All bowls use a pork-bone broth with back fat and chashu; no plant-based option exists, so vegan diners cannot be safely accommodated.

caveats: Hours are regular but split lunch/dinner with later closing (23:30) Wed–Sat and earlier (21:30) Tue & Sun; cash only. No official website found. Owner/chef name not confirmed from sources. One blog speculated about ownership branding, which is not verified and excluded.

cuisine: ラーメン
neighborhood: 右京区 西院（阪急 西京極駅 周辺）
gf_detail: A classic Kyoto ramen counter built on wheat: wheat noodles in every bowl, and the niboshi/bonito fish broths and shio/shoyu tare are not gluten-free. No gluten-free menu, no dedicated gluten-free preparation, and no indication staff can accommodate celiac needs. Not safe for celiac or strict gluten avoidance.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan or vegetarian option. The signature bowls are built on dried-sardine (niboshi), mackerel, and bonito fish broth, and the shop also serves pork-based Chinese noodles and gyoza. There is no plant-based ramen on the menu.
address_ja: 京都府京都市右京区西院六反田町31-6
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned coordinates for 西院六反田町31; within the Kyoto box.
seats_text: 20 seats total (16 counter, 4 table)
closed_days: 月曜（祝日の場合は営業）
cultural_comfort_note: A small neighborhood ramen counter where ordering in Japanese (or by pointing at the menu) is expected; English support is unlikely.
caveats: Hours vary by day and differ slightly between listings (lunch sometimes ends 14:00 vs 15:00, dinner 22:00 vs 23:00); confirm before a late visit. Cash only and no reservations.

bio.background: A long-running independent ramen counter in Kyoto's Saiin district, near Saikyogoku, that has been a fixture of the Saiin-Katsura ramen scene long enough to outlast its own name. It originally opened as らー麺創房 日本一 ぱこぱこ before slimming down to the current らー麺創房 ぱこ.
bio.specialty: The shop is best known for its 極上塩らーめん (premium salt ramen) — a clean, restrained shio bowl with a light, elegant broth and slightly crinkled smooth thin noodles, often finished with Kyoto mizuna greens. A second pillar is the 超にぼし中華そば (super-niboshi chuka soba), a clear fish-forward broth driven by dried sardine, mackerel, and bonito with medium-thick straight noodles.
bio.philosophy: Refined and approachable rather than loud — the kitchen leans on Kyoto ingredients and a delicate hand, prizing balance over bold intensity.

anecdotes.text: The shop dropped a syllable over the years, going from らー麺創房 日本一 ぱこぱこ to simply らー麺創房 ぱこ — one of the 'pako's quietly disappeared along the way.
Regulars prize the 極上塩らーめん, an airy, restrained salt bowl topped with Kyoto-style mizuna greens.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles are central to every bowl and are boiled in shared water; the broths and shio/shoyu tare are not gluten-free.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soy-based (shoyu/chuka soba) bowls are on the menu; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat and no wheat-free tare is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths are built on dried sardine, mackerel, and bonito (fish), and pork-based noodles and gyoza are served; no plant-based preparation is offered.

cuisine: ラーメン（豚骨ベース・和牛すじラーメン）
neighborhood: 中京区 壬生（西大路三条・三条御前 周辺）
gf_detail: This is a classic wheat-noodle ramen shop and not safe for celiacs. The noodles are straight medium-thin wheat noodles from a Kyoto noodle maker (Teigaku), and the signature bowls use a pork-bone broth with braised wagyu beef tendon; ramen tare is virtually always wheat-based shoyu. No gluten-free menu, no gluten-free noodle option, and no allergen handling is advertised. A celiac or gluten-sensitive diner should not eat here.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians. The core product is a rich pork-bone (tonkotsu) broth, the signature topping is braised wagyu beef tendon, and other bowls feature chashu pork and karaage (fried chicken). There is no vegetable-based broth or plant-based bowl on offer.
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区壬生中川町10-6
geocode_note: GSI geocoded the full street address 京都府京都市中京区壬生中川町10-6 to [135.736557, 35.00795]; within the Kyoto box.
seats_text: About 8 counter seats; an intimate counter-only shop run single-handedly by the owner.
hours_raw: Mon, Thu–Sun 11:00–15:00 / 17:30–20:30; closed Tue & Wed. (One source lists evening start as 17:00.) Year-end/New Year break 12/31–1/8.
closed_days: Tuesday and Wednesday; plus a year-end/New Year break (around 12/31–1/8).
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny owner-run counter ramen shop with no English menu advertised; a friendly greeting in Japanese and pointing at the menu will carry you through comfortably.
caveats: Payment method is reported inconsistently across sources: one listing notes PayPay/QR-code payments are accepted (cards and e-money not), while another states cash only. Bring cash to be safe. Evening opening time is listed as either 17:30 or 17:00 depending on the source.

bio.background: The owner-chef, known as Sai-san, spent about 15 years making ramen at Takayasu (高安), the famous, perennially queued shop on Ichijoji's 'Ramen Street' in Kyoto. He then ran Gion's Menya Ichijo (麺屋一乗) as an owner-operator before striking out fully on his own, opening this shop near Sanjo-Omae in May 2024.
bio.specialty: Wagyu beef-tendon ramen (和牛すじラーメン): a sweet, creamy pork-bone broth crowned with slow-braised wagyu beef tendon whose own sweetness melts into the soup. A salt (shio) ramen and a set with karaage and rice round out the short menu.
bio.philosophy: Carry forward the Takayasu legacy of richly satisfying broth and crisp karaage, served from an intimate single-counter shop where the owner can look after every guest personally; a complimentary spicy chive topping is offered.

anecdotes.text: Sai-san honed his craft over roughly 15 years at Ichijoji's celebrated Takayasu before opening here, carrying that shop's signature thick broth and karaage into his own bowl.
Opened on May 21, 2024, the shop quickly drew attention as a notable new entry in the Sanjo-Omae/Saiin area for its thick, umami-laden soup.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The bowls are built on wheat ramen noodles (sourced from the Kyoto noodle maker Teigaku) in a pork-bone broth; ramen tare of this style is wheat-based shoyu, so the dish contains wheat throughout.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is pork-bone based and toppings include wagyu beef tendon, pork chashu, and fried chicken; no plant-based preparation is offered, so cross-contact with animal products is total.

lead: ラーメン たぬき屋
name_ja: ラーメン たぬき屋
cuisine: 中華そば・熟成豚骨・まぜそば
neighborhood: 伏見桃山・中書島エリア（深草・伏見駅近く）
gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The core menu is built on wheat: ramen and mazesoba use wheat noodles supplied by the Kyoto noodle maker Teikaku (麺屋棣鄂), and the bowls rely on standard (wheat-based) shoyu/tare seasonings. Broths are pork-bone (aged tonkotsu) and chicken paitan, with rich garlic-heavy mazesoba sauces. No gluten-free noodles, dedicated prep, or allergen accommodation is advertised, and the kitchen is a single shared 9-seat counter operation. A strict gluten-free or celiac diner should not eat here.
vegan_detail: Not vegan or vegetarian friendly. Every signature item is animal-based: aged pork-bone (tonkotsu) and chicken paitan broths, thick chashu pork, and meat-laden mazesoba/tantan/curry bowls. There is no plant-based broth or vegan option, and no vegetarian menu is offered.
address_ja: 京都府京都市伏見区深草泓ノ壺町11-9 第二池田ハイツ1F
geocode_note: GSI geocoded to chō-banchi level (深草泓ノ壺町11番地); within the Kyoto bounding box.
seats_text: 9 counter seats only; a small, cozy space that regularly draws a queue.
hours_raw: 11:30-14:30 / 18:00-22:00. Closed Thursdays and the first Sunday of each month. Closing time may shift depending on the day. (Some sources list the evening service ending around 21:00.)
closed_days: 木曜、第1日曜 (Thursdays and the first Sunday of each month)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, busy local counter shop where ordering and a little basic Japanese go a long way; no English service is indicated, so a guide or a few Japanese phrases will smooth the visit.
caveats: Hours can vary day to day and evening close is reported as either 21:00 or 22:00; the aged-tonkotsu ramen is capped at roughly 20 bowls each for lunch and dinner, so it can sell out. Expect a queue at peak times. A frozen vending machine out front sells chashu, fried rice, and mazesoba to take home.

bio.background: A small owner-run counter shop tucked near Fushimi Station, beneath the Kintetsu rail overpass in the Fukakusa area of Fushimi. The owner trained at earlier ramen shops (known locally as Dainaka and Gankokumen) before going independent and opening Tanukiya around 2015-2016; it has since grown into one of the most loved ramen stops in the Fushimi neighborhood, marking its 10th year in 2025.
bio.specialty: Two faces define the shop. By day and night it pours an aged tonkotsu ramen made by simmering pork bones over high heat for hours and then resting the broth overnight to deepen its umami into a thick, almost porridge-like richness, served over chewy medium-thick straight noodles and limited to about 20 bowls per service. Its other headline is the mazesoba: extra-thick noodles coated in a punchy tare with thick slabs of chashu and a heap of garlic. A genuinely full-sized house fried rice (chahan), available in small to large portions, rounds out the order and is a destination in its own right.
bio.philosophy: Big, satisfying, unabashedly rich bowls done in a tiny nine-seat space, with the noodles sourced from a respected Kyoto noodle maker and the broth given time to mature rather than rushed.

anecdotes.text: The shop's name, Tanukiya, comes from the owner's nickname, with its origin explained on a sign at the storefront.
The aged tonkotsu ramen is offered as a daily limited run of roughly 20 bowls each at lunch and dinner.
A frozen vending machine stands outside the shop selling chashu, fried rice, and mazesoba to take home.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat noodles for ramen and mazesoba come from a Kyoto noodle maker and are boiled in the same shared pots; with a single nine-seat counter kitchen, gluten cross-contact is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Bowls use standard Japanese shoyu/tare seasoning, which is wheat-based; no wheat-free or tamari alternative is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broths and bowls are pork- and chicken-based with chashu and meat toppings; no plant-based preparation exists, so any item will contain or contact animal products.

cuisine: 二郎系・濃厚豚骨／鶏白湯ラーメン (jiro-inspired / rich chicken-paitan ramen)
gf_detail: This is a small ramen counter serving wheat noodles in chicken-paitan and shoyu-tare broths, with rotating specials like a spice/curry ramen and a carbonara-style bowl. Wheat noodles plus a wheat-based soy tare mean it is not celiac-safe, and no published source mentions gluten-free options or any allergen handling. A celiac diner should treat this as unsafe.
vegan_detail: The kitchen is built around chicken-bone broth (a 6-hour chicken-and-aromatics paitan) with pork chashu and menma toppings; signature bowls are the Henteko Shoyu and Henteko Shio, plus meat-and-cheese specials. No vegetarian or vegan ramen is offered or mentioned by any source.
address_ja: 京都府京都市伏見区深草北新町648
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned [135.76973, 34.949146] for 京都府京都市伏見区深草北新町648; within the Kyoto bounding box. Consistent with reported location ~3 min walk from Keihan Sumizome Station / ~5 min from JR Fujinomori Station.
neighborhood: 深草・墨染 (Fukakusa / Sumizome), Fushimi Ward
seats_text: Counter seating, around 8 seats; after a 2024 renovation the shop added a couple of small tables, making it a bit more workable for a small group.
hours_raw: 11:00-15:00, 18:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:30); closed Tuesdays plus occasional irregular closures. (One source lists lunch/dinner as roughly 11:00-14:30 / 18:00-21:30.)
closed_days: Tuesdays, plus irregular closures (不定休)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny owner-run neighborhood counter down a narrow alley with a ticket-machine ordering system and cash-only payment, so a little Japanese (or pointing at the ticket machine) goes a long way.
caveats: Hours and seat layout differ slightly between sources (a March 2024 renovation reopened the shop and added tables); the phone number is unlisted, and there is no official website. Confirm the day's hours before a special trip, as the shop takes irregular days off and runs frequent limited specials.

bio.background: Henteko is a tiny eight-seat ramen counter tucked down a narrow alley in the Fukakusa/Sumizome area of Fushimi, opened in March 2019 and reopened after a renovation in spring 2024. The owner is reported to have trained in the lineage of Meimon, a Kyoto establishment long associated with the city's rich tonkotsu style, and he carries that grounding into his own shop while constantly tinkering with new ideas.
bio.origin: Kyoto (Fushimi), in the Meimon ramen lineage
bio.specialty: The house bowl is the Henteko Shoyu: a creamy chicken-paitan broth married to a soy tare, topped with a few thick slices of chashu, tender menma, and medium-thick noodles with a firm bite. A salt (shio) version sits alongside it.
bio.philosophy: A minimalist, quality-first operation: a single small counter, careful broth (a roughly six-hour chicken-and-aromatics simmer), and straightforward bowls rather than fuss, paired with a steady stream of playful limited specials.

anecdotes.text: The owner is often described as a warm, smiling, soft-spoken host whose personality seems to come through in the bowl itself.
True to the name 'henteko' (quirky/unconventional), the shop runs frequent limited creations, from a house spice-curry ramen with chashu, cheese, fried onion and garlic to a carbonara-style bowl.
The owner is said to come out of the Meimon lineage, a Kyoto shop tied to the city's signature rich tonkotsu tradition.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Signature bowls are built on a soy (shoyu) tare; standard Japanese ramen soy tare is wheat-based, and no source indicates otherwise here.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is a long-simmered chicken-bone paitan and bowls carry pork chashu, so the kitchen is animal-based throughout with no vegan preparation noted.

cuisine: 町中華・中華そば (town-Chinese / chuka-soba, gyoza, fried rice)
gf_detail: This is a traditional town-Chinese (machi-chuka) shop built around wheat: chuka-soba and mapo-men use wheat noodles, gyoza wrappers are wheat, and dishes are seasoned with regular (wheat-based) soy sauce. Fried items share oil. No gluten-free menu, no allergen program, and no indication staff can accommodate celiac needs. Not a safe choice for anyone with celiac disease or a strict gluten avoidance.
vegan_detail: A meat-and-egg-forward Chinese diner: signature plates feature pork, fried chicken, crab omelette, and pork-stock-based soups; the chuka-soba broth and many dishes rely on animal ingredients. No vegan menu or known plant-based accommodation. A vegan diner would have very little they could safely order here.
address_ja: 京都府京都市伏見区西柳町545-2
geocode_note: GSI point-level geocode of 西柳町545-2 returned [135.759674, 34.928837]; chō-level returned [135.759506, 34.929047]. Both agree on the location near Chushojima Station, but latitude sits ~0.001 below the configured Kyoto box minimum (34.93) because Fushimi/Chushojima is at the southern edge of the city. Coordinates are correct; only the box lower bound is slightly too high for this southern address.
neighborhood: 伏見区西柳町 / 中書島 (Chushojima, Fushimi)
seats_text: About 30 seats: roughly 8-10 counter seats on the 1st floor, plus an upstairs tatami room seating about 20 for groups.
hours_raw: 11:30-14:30, 17:00-22:00 (lunch and dinner); closed Tuesdays.
closed_days: Tuesdays
cultural_comfort_note: A long-running neighborhood Chinese diner with a Showa-era feel and counter seating; little to no English, so a few words of Japanese and a relaxed, locals-style approach go a long way.

bio.background: A neighborhood town-Chinese restaurant that has served the Chushojima entertainment district for over twenty years, run by the proprietor couple together with their son. Long known by locals and visiting workers as "the best Chinese place in Chushojima."
bio.specialty: Honest, lightly-seasoned Chinese home cooking: chuka-soba and mapo-men noodles, crisp pan-fried gyoza, and a soy-seasoned fried rice (yakimeshi) studded with well-flavored char siu and praised for its loose, separate grains. The most popular order is the Yosuko Lunch plate, which piles sweet-and-sour pork, a crab omelette, fried chicken, salad, and pickles on one plate with soup and rice.
bio.philosophy: Generous, dependable cooking at very reasonable prices, with attentive service rooted in long-standing relationships with regulars.

anecdotes.text: First-floor counter for about eight to ten with an upstairs tatami room for groups; it works as a casual lunch spot by day and a relaxed place to eat and drink in the evening, carrying a distinctly Showa-era town-Chinese atmosphere.
The owner personally cooks the fried rice; reviewers single out its light soy seasoning and the robustly flavored char siu, with excellent loose, separate texture.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Town-Chinese kitchen where wheat noodles, wheat gyoza wrappers, and wheat soy sauce are used throughout; shared woks, pots of noodle water, and fryers make cross-contact unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes such as the soy-seasoned fried rice rely on regular (wheat-based) Japanese soy sauce; no gluten-free soy sauce is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is built around pork, fried chicken, crab/egg, and pork-stock soups; vegetable dishes are cooked in the same animal-fat woks, so genuinely vegan preparation cannot be assumed.

caveats: Cash only. Hours and Tuesday closure are from aggregator/blog listings and can change for a small independent shop; confidence on the multi-year history is medium (from local food blogs, not the shop itself). Geocode latitude sits marginally below the configured Kyoto box but the location is correct (see geocode_note).

cuisine: 町中華・長崎ちゃんぽん・皿うどん (town-Chinese with Nagasaki champon & sara-udon)
neighborhood: Nishijin, Senbon-dori (Kamigyo-ku), near Kitano-Hakubaicho
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation. The house specialties — Nagasaki champon and sara-udon — are both wheat-flour noodle dishes served in pork/seafood broth or wheat-thickened gravy, and the broader menu (gyoza, sweet-and-sour pork, fried rice, Chinese rice bowls) relies on wheat noodles, dumpling skins, and standard Chinese seasonings that contain wheat-based soy sauce. There is no published allergen handling, no dedicated preparation, and nothing to suggest a celiac diner could eat safely here. Treat as unsafe for strict gluten avoidance.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly destination. The signature champon and sara-udon are built on pork, shrimp, squid, fishcake and a meat/seafood broth, and the rest of the menu is centered on pork, dumplings and egg-fried rice. No vegetable-only or plant-based versions are advertised. A vegan would have nothing reliable to order; at most one could ask, but no animal-free option is documented.
address_ja: 京都府京都市上京区千本通笹屋町上ル笹屋4丁目304-8
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned [135.756714, 35.029552] for the full street address; within the Kyoto bounding box.
seats_text: About 11 seats (small counter-and-table machi-chuka; some sources describe roughly 13).
hours_raw: Mon/Tue/Thu/Fri 11:30-14:00 & 17:00-20:00; Sun 17:00-20:00 (dinner only); closed Wed & Sat. (Blog sources variously cite lunch from ~11:45 and dinner to ~21:00 with last order ~20:40; listing hours used here.)
closed_days: Wednesday and Saturday (Sunday is dinner only)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, well-loved local machi-chuka where the owner cooks over roaring wok flames; little to no English is expected, so a few words of Japanese and a relaxed, regulars-friendly attitude go a long way.
caveats: Hours and seat count vary slightly between sources (lunch start 11:30 vs 11:45; dinner end 20:00 vs 21:00; seats 11 vs ~13). No official website found. Confirm current days/hours before visiting.

bio.background: A genuine Showa-era town-Chinese shop that has fed the Nishijin neighborhood for some 30-40 years, dating back to when the Senbon district was a buzzing entertainment quarter known as 'Kawaramachi in the east, Senbon in the west' (東の河原町、西の千本), with locals out for an evening 'Senbura' stroll. As the surrounding district quieted over the decades, the shop stayed busy and beloved.
bio.specialty: Nagasaki champon and Nagasaki sara-udon — the house signatures. The champon is a milky, umami-rich broth piled with chewy noodles, pork, shrimp, squid, fishcake, cabbage, bean sprouts, onion and carrot; the sara-udon layers crispy fried noodles under a glossy, vegetable-and-seafood gravy. Regulars also love the 'Gurabaa bento' set (sweet-and-sour pork, sara-udon, fried chicken and rice).
bio.philosophy: Hearty, no-frills Nagasaki-style Chinese cooking done the same honest way for decades — generous portions, a hot wok, and a welcoming counter for solo diners.

anecdotes.text: The name 'ぐらばー (Gurabaa)' nods to Nagasaki's Glover House, signaling that this is Nagasaki-style Chinese cooking rather than the usual Kyoto Chinese fare.
In the kitchen the owner works the wok with flames leaping up ('厨房ではご主人が火柱あげて中華鍋を振り'), in a snug space of around a dozen counter and table seats.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat is central throughout: champon and sara-udon are wheat noodles, dumplings have wheat skins, and a single shared wok and fryer handle all dishes, so cross-contact with wheat is unavoidable in a kitchen this size.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a Japanese town-Chinese kitchen, dishes are seasoned with standard soy sauce and the gravy/seasonings expected to contain wheat; no wheat-free soy alternative is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths and stir-fries are built on pork and seafood, and the wok is shared across all orders, so any 'vegetable' dish would still carry animal-derived broth and cross-contact.

lead: 御旅飯店 系の濃厚クリーミー担担麺店（汁なし担々麺 ラアノウミ系列ではない独立店）— 京都 鶏白湯そば 純
name_ja: 京都 鶏白湯そば 純
cuisine: 濃厚鶏白湯そば (creamy emulsified tori-paitan ramen)
neighborhood: 中京区 / Marutamachi (Karasuma Line)
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区三本木町462-5 1F
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned first feature coordinates [135.760666, 35.01683] for 京都府京都市中京区三本木町462-5; within Kyoto bounding box.
seats_text: 18 seats: 4 counter seats, plus tables (three 2-tops and two 4-tops); café-like layout with mostly table seating.
closed_days: The official site states 年中無休 (open year-round), but some listings report a Thursday (or Wednesday-evening) closure — confirm the current closed day before visiting, especially mid-week.
cultural_comfort_note: A small, café-styled ramen counter where pointing at the photo menu and a friendly konnichiwa go a long way; staff are unlikely to handle detailed allergy questions in English.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The bowls are built on wheat ramen noodles, a creamy chicken-and-pork-bone broth, and (typically) a wheat-based shoyu/seasoning tare, with fried-chicken and rice sides also in the kitchen. No gluten-free noodles or dedicated handling are mentioned anywhere. Celiac diners should not eat here.
vegan_detail: Not vegan or vegetarian. The entire concept is an animal-based emulsified broth — Kagoshima chicken plus Miyazaki/Oita pork bone — topped with chashu, egg, and (on the seafood version) clams, with karaage and meat-rice sides. No plant-based bowl is offered.

bio.background: A compact, café-like ramen shop tucked into a residential pocket near Karasuma-Marutamachi, Jun opened in spring 2022 and quickly drew a following. The room reads more neo-bistro than noodle bar, with warm interiors and plenty of table seating alongside a short counter.
bio.specialty: The signature is the 鶏白湯そば (tori-paitan soba): a bright, almost porcelain-white broth built on Kagoshima chicken with a measured addition of Miyazaki and Oita pork bone, simmered hard and then whipped in a blender so it emulsifies into an airy, cappuccino-like foam. A 魚介鶏白湯そば (seafood version) layers in shellfish and clams for a deeper, briny dimension; mazesoba and a lighter fish-forward bowl round out the menu, with karaage and meat-over-rice on the side.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen leans on 強制乳化 (forced emulsification) — finishing each bowl in a blender — to make a broth that is intensely rich yet smooth and creamy on the palate, powerful without becoming tiring over the course of a bowl. The attention extends to the sides, which are treated with the same care rather than as afterthoughts.

anecdotes.text: Each bowl's broth is whipped to a foam in a blender just before serving, a 強制乳化 technique that gives the soup its signature aerated, porcelain-white cap.
The shop opened in spring 2022 and became popular soon after, with its café-like exterior and interior setting it apart from a typical ramen counter.
Sides are kept to a high standard — the karaage arrives fresh and hot, and a Tajima-chicken rice bowl using rich 'Dragon egg' yolks sells out quickly.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles are the base of every bowl and are boiled in shared water; there is no gluten-free option.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broths are animal-based (chicken plus pork bone) and bowls carry chashu, egg, and shellfish; no plant-based preparation is offered.

caveats: Closed-day information conflicts across sources: the official site says 年中無休 (open year-round), Tabelog lists no closure, while blog write-ups mention a Thursday closure and the English official page mentions Wednesday evenings — verify before a mid-week visit. No chef/owner name is published, so the bio omits it. Jun is reported as a sister shop to a Michelin-recognized Kobe/Hyogo establishment, but it operates as an independent Kyoto location (dev-meta detail kept out of the diner-facing bio).

cuisine: 京都ラーメン（鶏ガラ醤油・背脂 中華そば／餃子専門店）
gf_detail: No gluten-free options. The core dish is wheat ramen noodles in a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare that is almost certainly wheat-based, and the menu also features gyoza (wheat wrappers) and an ebimeshi/shrimp fried rice often seasoned with soy sauce. There is no indication of gluten-free preparation, no dedicated equipment, and no allergen accommodation noted. Not safe for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The signature broth is made from chicken bones (鶏ガラ100%) finished with scattered back fat (背脂), topped with chashu pork; gyoza typically contain pork. There are no vegan or vegetarian ramen offerings.
address_ja: 京都府京都市上京区千本通上立売上る花車町481
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned coordinates for 京都市上京区花車町481, within the Kyoto box.
seats_text: 14 seats total (10 counter seats, 4 table seats)
hours_raw: 11:30-14:00 / 17:30-21:00 (L.O.). May close early when the soup runs out (スープ品切れで早期閉店あり). Closed Mondays and Thursdays; schedule can vary.
closed_days: Mondays and Thursdays (schedule may vary)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny husband-and-wife counter shop with no English-language presence; basic Japanese and a relaxed, point-at-the-menu approach will serve you best.

bio.background: Narukami is a small, family-run chuka-soba and gyoza specialist tucked along Senbon-dori in Kyoto's Kamigyo ward, a short walk from the Kitano-Hakubaicho area near Kitano Tenmangu. It is run as an intimate husband-and-wife operation with just fourteen seats split between a counter and a couple of tables.
bio.specialty: The signature bowl is a classic Kyoto-style chuka soba: a broth built from carefully selected chicken bones simmered patiently (鶏ガラ100%), finished with a scattering of back fat (背脂) over a soy tare. Despite its rich, glistening look it drinks surprisingly light and clean, with a hidden touch of ichimi togarashi for lift, served over medium straight noodles with chashu. Alongside the ramen, the shop is known for its gyoza and a popular ebimeshi (shrimp fried rice).
bio.philosophy: The kitchen leans on chicken bones alone, slowly drawn out, aiming for a broth with real depth that is still approachable enough to eat every day rather than a one-time heavy hit.

anecdotes.text: Though the back-fat surface suggests a heavy, kotteri bowl, regulars note it eats clean and light, with a subtle ichimi spice as a hidden accent.
Beyond ramen, the ebimeshi (shrimp fried rice) is a beloved side that locals order to round out the meal.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles, gyoza with wheat wrappers, and a likely wheat-based soy tare are all prepared in the same small kitchen; no gluten-free handling is indicated.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is a soy-sauce (shoyu) style tare, which in standard Japanese ramen is brewed with wheat-containing soy sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is 100% chicken bones with pork back fat and chashu pork; gyoza typically contain pork. No animal-free preparation.

caveats: No official website found; owner names and founding history are not documented in available sources. Hours and closed days can vary, and the shop may close early once the soup sells out. Geocode resolved at the banchi level for 花車町481.
neighborhood: 上京区・千本通（北野白梅町／北野天満宮近く）

lead: 新大宮 中華のサカイ 本店
name_ja: 新大宮 中華のサカイ 本店
cuisine: 町中華（中華料理）・冷麺 / Old-school neighborhood Chinese, famous for year-round cold noodles (reimen)
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation. The signature reimen and other noodle dishes use wheat noodles, and the cold-noodle sauce is a sesame-mustard-soy style dressing that almost certainly contains wheat-based soy sauce. As a traditional machi-chuka serving fried rice, gyoza (wheat wrappers), spring rolls and fried chicken, the kitchen relies heavily on wheat and shared cooking surfaces. Not suitable for celiac diners; no dedicated gluten-free menu or preparation was found.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan or vegetarian destination. The famous reimen is topped with chashu (roast pork), and the broth/fried rice are enriched with chicken stock. Menu centers on pork, chicken and egg dishes (omurice, fried rice, fried chicken, spring rolls). No vegan options were identified, and there is no indication of plant-based broths or animal-product-free preparation.
address_ja: 京都府京都市北区紫野上門前町92 新大宮商店街
geocode_note: GSI geocoded to the chō-level point for 紫野上門前町92; within the Kyoto box (lat 35.0445, lng 135.7488). Located on the Shin-Omiya shopping street north of Kitaoji, roughly 15 min walk from Kitaoji subway station.
seats_text: 41 seats; table-based old-school Chinese diner that seats smaller parties at larger tables during busy lunch hours.
hours_raw: Wed-Fri: 11:00-15:00 (L.O. 14:45) and 17:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:30); Sat-Sun: 11:00-16:00 (L.O. 15:30) and 17:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:30). Closed Monday and Tuesday (open if a public holiday falls on those days).
closed_days: Monday and Tuesday (open on public holidays)
cultural_comfort_note: A bustling, queue-prone neighborhood Chinese diner where staff focus on turning tables quickly; expect a Japanese-only menu and little English, so a guide or pointing at dishes works best.
caveats: No gluten-free or vegan accommodation; reservations not accepted, so expect a lunchtime queue. Hours and closed days verified against the listing as of June 2026 but can change; confirm before a special trip.

bio.background: Founded in 1939, Chuka no Sakai began life as a kissaten (Western-style teahouse) on the Shin-Omiya shopping street just north of Kitaoji, near Daitoku-ji temple in Kyoto's Kita ward. After the war, food shortages pushed the family to reinvent the shop as a neighborhood Chinese diner (machi-chuka), while quietly keeping a few of its teahouse-era Western dishes on the menu.
bio.specialty: The house reimen (cold noodles): uniquely thick, chewy house-made noodles in a creamy sesame-based sauce with a pronounced mustard kick, balanced by refreshing acidity and a touch of sweetness, topped simply with chashu, nori and cucumber. Famously served year-round, not just in summer.
bio.philosophy: A relaxed, unpretentious local institution that resists rushing diners despite long lunch queues, seating even small parties at big tables and letting people linger.

anecdotes.text: The reimen has built a nationwide following, drawing fans from across Japan to this small shopping-street shop.
The omurice is a holdover from the shop's pre-war teahouse days, with chicken-stock-enriched fried rice wrapped in a thin omelet and ketchup sauce.

The walls are covered in autographed photos of celebrities who have visited over the decades, and a sister branch called Misonobashi Sakai serves a slightly different soup flavoring (taxi drivers sometimes mix the two up).

cuisine: 町中華・冷麺（ごまだれ冷麺／焼豚冷麺、中華そば）
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation. The house specialty is cold noodles (冷麺) made with thick wheat ("ムチムチ太麺") noodles, and the menu centers on Chinese noodle dishes (中華そば) — all wheat-based. The signature sesame dressing blends sweet/sour and mustard notes and almost certainly contains wheat-based soy sauce/seasonings; broth-based dishes use pork. There is no published allergen or gluten-free information. Not suitable for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The flagship bowls are 焼豚冷麺 (roast-pork cold noodles) and ham cold noodles, and the kitchen is a Chinese-cuisine shop built around pork. The sesame dare and other dishes have no stated plant-based versions, and no vegetarian/vegan menu is advertised.
address_ja: 京都府京都市北区大宮東総門口町38-3
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned the first feature for 京都府京都市北区大宮東総門口町38; coordinates [135.746277, 35.057259] fall within the Kyoto box (lat 34.93–35.13, lng 135.66–135.83), near Misonobashi / Kamigamo area in Kita-ku.
neighborhood: 北区・御園橋（御園橋西詰、北大路／上賀茂神社方面）
seats_text: About 70 seats, with counter and tatami (zashiki) seating; family-friendly with on-site parking.
hours_raw: Wed–Sun & holidays: 11:00–15:00, 17:00–21:30. Closed Mon & Tue.
closed_days: Mondays and Tuesdays (Monday closure shifts to the following day if a holiday); also closed Jan 1–3.
cultural_comfort_note: A long-running, locally loved Kyoto neighborhood Chinese shop where ordering is easiest with a little Japanese, though the menu is simple and pointing works fine.
caveats: Wheat-noodle and pork-centered shop with no allergen information; unsuitable for gluten-free or vegan diners. No reservations taken (a phone call may occasionally hold a seat).

bio.background: Misonobashi Sakai traces back to a Sakai family Chinese kitchen founded in Kyoto's Kita ward in the prewar/early-Showa era; this branch opened in 1982 as an independent offshoot of the original 'Chuka no Sakai' near Daitokuji, settling by the Misonobashi bridge toward Kamigamo.
bio.specialty: Sesame-sauce cold noodles — the 焼豚冷麺 (roast-pork cold noodles): plump, chewy thick noodles bound in a thick, emulsified pale-yellow sesame dare that balances sweetness, tang, and a mustard kick, finished with sliced roast pork; a ham version is also offered.
bio.philosophy: A neighborhood institution built on one perfected dish, served year-round — captured in the local saying that when you think cold noodles you think Sakai, and when you think Sakai you think cold noodles.

anecdotes.text: The cold noodles are eaten year-round rather than only in summer, and the shop is so identified with the dish that it has become its calling card across Kyoto.
The signature sesame dare is described as a thick, emulsified dressing combining sourness, sweetness, and mustard pungency over notably plump, chewy thick noodles.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core dishes use wheat noodles and wheat-based Chinese seasonings; high cross-contact risk in a shared noodle kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The sesame cold-noodle dare and Chinese dishes are very likely seasoned with wheat-containing soy sauce; no allergen breakdown is published.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Kitchen is pork/ham-centered (roast pork, ham cold noodles); animal products are pervasive.

cuisine: 京都ラーメン（豚骨醤油・鶏ガラ清湯の中華そば、自家製チャーシュー・メンマ、お漬物添え）
gf_detail: This is a traditional Kyoto chuka-soba shop serving wheat ramen noodles in a pork-and-chicken broth with soy-based tare, which is virtually always made with wheat-containing shoyu. There is no indication of any gluten-free option, gluten-free noodles, or allergen accommodation. A celiac diner cannot safely eat the ramen here. The only side is white rice, and the house-made pickles served with the bowl could in theory be safe, but the shop offers no allergen information and the kitchen handles wheat noodles throughout, so cross-contamination is unavoidable.
vegan_detail: The single signature bowl is built on a pork-bone and chicken-carcass broth and topped with house-made chashu (pork). There is no vegan or vegetarian ramen, and the menu is essentially one item plus rice. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
address_ja: 京都府京都市左京区岡崎北御所町28-4
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 京都府京都市左京区岡崎北御所町28-4; within the Kyoto box.
neighborhood: 左京区・岡崎北御所町（平安神宮の北東）
seats_text: L字のカウンター席とテーブル席（小規模な店内、席数の明記なし）。L-shaped counter plus a few tables in a small retro brick interior; exact seat count not published.
closed_days: 水曜日・木曜日
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, cash-only, decades-old neighborhood ramen counter run by an attentive owner with a single set menu and no English or allergen support, so a first-time GF/vegan visitor should not attempt it without a Japanese-speaking guide.
caveats: Cash only; reservations not accepted; one-item menu (chuka-soba) so unsuitable for those who cannot eat wheat noodles, pork, or chicken broth. Evening hours vary by source (17:00-19:00 vs 16:30-19:00) and the shop is small with frequent queues.

bio.background: A craftsman ramen shop born from a yatai (hand-pushed food cart). Founder Ryohei Morikawa, originally from Ishikawa Prefecture, had worked as a materials dealer and a taxi driver before learning to make ramen by volunteering for five months — late nights and days off, unpaid — at a shop called Homin (芳眠) in Kamikatsura. In 1972 he began pushing a hinoki-wood cart along Marutamachi-dori in Okazaki, opening each evening around the time the first star appeared, which gave the shop its name, Ichibanboshi ("first star"). After about three years he took over a brick-walled space left by a closing nearby steakhouse and put down permanent roots; the ivy-covered, café-like brick exterior near Heian Shrine is now the local landmark.
bio.origin: 京都市左京区・岡崎（平安神宮の北東）
bio.specialty: A single bowl: chuka-soba. A clear (seiyu) broth of pork bones blended with chicken carcasses, soy-based and a touch sweet, with fine, soft noodles, house-made chashu and house-made menma, plus bean sprouts and green onion. House-made pickles are served alongside to refresh the palate after the bowl. Rice is the only other thing on the menu.
bio.philosophy: Morikawa credits his customers for the recipe itself: "お客さんのほうがラーメンにくわしい。意見を聞いて、今の味になりました" — "Customers know ramen better than I do; I listened to their opinions, and that's how today's flavor came to be." He says early on people would leave broth in the bowl, and over the years, by adjusting to their feedback, they stopped — the taste evolved with the regulars who eat it.

anecdotes.text: The shop is named for the cart's opening time: Morikawa rolled out his hinoki-wood yatai around dusk, when the first star (一番星) appeared in the sky.
He learned the craft by working unpaid for five months at a shop called Homin in Kamikatsura, taking the late shifts and the days others rested.
He is known for remembering long-time regulars by face decades later, and for shaping the broth around their feedback until no one left it unfinished.
The brick, ivy-covered storefront — taken over from a closed steakhouse — feels more like a retro kissaten than a ramen shop: "レトロなレンガ調で、まるで喫茶店のような店内."

gf_cross_contamination.text: Single-item wheat-noodle ramen shop with one small kitchen; wheat noodles are handled throughout, so any dish would face cross-contamination. No gluten-free handling is offered.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is a soy-based (醤油/清湯) chuka-soba; Japanese shoyu used in such tare is virtually always wheat-containing. No wheat-free tare is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is pork-bone and chicken-carcass based with house-made pork chashu; the kitchen is built entirely around animal products, so no vegan separation exists.

positives.text: House-made pickles (お漬物) are served with the bowl to refresh the palate, and chashu and menma are made in-house — a sign of a careful, hands-on kitchen, though not an allergen accommodation.

cuisine: 鶏白湯ラーメン（鶏と鰹のWスープ／濃厚鶏）
neighborhood: 伏見区・淀（京阪 淀駅前）
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The ramen is built on wheat noodles (sourced from a noodle maker) in a thick chicken-paitan / chicken-and-bonito double broth, with shio, shoyu, and rich-chicken tare variants — the shoyu and many tare components contain wheat. No dedicated GF menu, no gluten-free noodle option, and no allergen-handling information was found. A celiac diner cannot eat safely here.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. Every bowl is built on a chicken (and bonito/katsuo) broth, with toppings such as chicken chashu, marinated egg, and spicy meat crumbles. There is no plant-based broth or vegan ramen option. Sides are also meat/fish-based (karaage, fried rice). No vegan accommodation found.
address_ja: 京都府京都市伏見区淀本町218-2 本町マンション1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 京都市伏見区淀本町218-2 returned [135.719574, 34.906914]. Longitude (135.72) is within the Kyoto box, but latitude (34.9069) falls just below the box minimum of 34.93. This is expected and correct: Yodo is in the far south of Fushimi-ku, near the Osaka/Yodo-river border by Keihan Yodo Station, geographically south of central Kyoto. Coordinates are accurate; the box simply does not extend this far south.
