seats_text: 17 seats — counter (about 9) plus a couple of small tables; intimate neighborhood ramen shop with generous chair spacing.
hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Thu, Fri, Sat: 12:00-15:00 and 18:00-23:30; Sunday: 12:00-23:00; closed Wednesday. (Note: a local Kyoto travel write-up lists alternate hours of 11:00-15:00 / 17:00-21:00, with Sun/Wed/holidays lunch-only — hours appear to vary, so confirm before visiting. Tel: 075-631-8855.)
closed_days: Wednesday
cultural_comfort_note: A small, locals-oriented neighborhood ramen counter near Keihan Yodo Station with no indication of English support — basic Japanese or pointing at the menu will help, and a friendly konnichiwa goes a long way.

bio.background: ぶんや is a small, easy-to-miss ramen shop tucked about 300 meters from Keihan Yodo Station, marked simply by a tent reading "鰹だし" (bonito stock). It has quietly built a reputation as a hidden gem (穴場) among Kyoto ramen fans, holding its own against the city's heavyweight rich-chicken names like Tenkaippin, Gokkei, and Menya Sanda.
bio.origin: Fushimi-ku, Yodo — far southern Kyoto, by the Yodo River near the Osaka border.
bio.specialty: The signature is the 濃厚鶏 (rich chicken) bowl — and the dressed-up 味付け玉子濃厚鶏, topped with chicken chashu (breast), a meatball, scallion, sesame, a marinated egg, and a fragrant garlic-infused burnt oil. The broth is famously thick: chicken bones are crushed and simmered down to a near mud-like (ドロ系) viscosity, so dense that regulars joke the renge (soup spoon) could stand straight up in it.
bio.philosophy: A 'W-soup' (double-stock) approach: rather than chicken alone, every bowl marries concentrated chicken paitan with katsuo (bonito) dashi, layering glutamate, inosinate, and guanylate umami. The menu splits into bonito-forward bowls (spicy, wakame) and chicken bowls in three tares — shio, shoyu, and the flagship rich style — all carrying that bonito backbone.

anecdotes.text: The broth is reduced until a soup spoon practically stands upright in it — the shop is locally famous for this 'renge stands up' level of thickness.
Often called a 穴場 (hidden gem) near Yodo Station, punching above its weight against Kyoto's best-known rich-chicken ramen shops.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles are boiled in shared water, and the shop also serves karaage and fried rice — heavy gluten presence throughout the kitchen with no separation noted.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: One of the core tare options is shoyu (鶏醤油), and chicken bowls are described as carrying a soy/bonito base; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat. No wheat-free tare is offered.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broths are chicken and/or bonito based with meat and egg toppings; no plant-based stock or vegan-safe item exists on the menu.

caveats: Two different hour sets circulate (Tabelog: 12:00-15:00/18:00-23:30, Sun to 23:00, closed Wed; a Kyoto travel site: 11:00-15:00/17:00-21:00 with Sun/Wed/holiday lunch only). Phone 075-631-8855 to confirm. Cash only, no reservations. Coordinates are correct but sit just south of the defined Kyoto box because Yodo is in far-southern Fushimi-ku. No GF or vegan options and no allergen-handling info found — not suitable for celiac or vegan diners.

cuisine: 京都ラーメン（ますたに系 豚骨醤油 中華そば）
neighborhood: 左京区・元田中
address_ja: 京都府京都市左京区田中樋ノ口町13-2
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 田中樋ノ口町13 returned [135.784042, 35.034138], within the Kyoto box and consistent with the Mototanaka residential location north of Kyoto University's Faculty of Agriculture.
seats_text: L字カウンター6席に小上がり（座敷）席を加えた、計7席ほどの小さな店。
closed_days: 月曜定休（加えて不定休あり）
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny counter-only neighborhood ramen shop with a quiet owner, cash only, no English and no menu accommodations expected — best approached with a guide or basic Japanese and a willingness to just order the one bowl.
gf_detail: This is a classic wheat-noodle Kyoto chukasoba shop. The bowl is built on wheat (komugi) straight noodles plus a tonkotsu-shoyu broth whose tare is almost certainly standard wheat-based soy sauce, with no gluten-free menu, no separate prep, and a shared boiling pot. Not suitable for celiac diners. No GF information is published anywhere.
vegan_detail: A single-bowl pork-based shop: the broth is tonkotsu-shoyu (pork bone) and every bowl is topped with pork-belly chashu, with rice/egg-over-rice as the only sides. There is no vegetarian or vegan ramen option and no published accommodation.
caveats: Hours and Monday closure are confirmed across Tabelog and two blogs; the shop also notes irregular closures (不定休), so confirm before a special trip. Seat count (~6 counter + 1 small raised tatami area) and cash-only are reported by blog sources. Owner-background detail differs slightly between sources: one describes a quiet/taciturn owner, another a personable one, and one notes he previously managed "Daioh Ramen" (大王ラーメン); treated as reported, not certain. No official website found.

bio.background: Jiei opened on February 4, 2016 in a quiet residential pocket of Mototanaka in Sakyo Ward, just north of Kyoto University's agriculture campus, taking over a small storefront that earlier sources describe as a former bistro/izakaya space. Now approaching its tenth year, it has become a low-key neighborhood fixture rather than a tourist destination.
bio.specialty: A single classic Kyoto-style chukasoba (中華そば) in the Masutani/Takara-ya lineage: a tea-colored tonkotsu-shoyu broth with mellow, comforting umami, a touch of fine back-fat and chili, served over medium-thin straight noodles and topped with thick-cut pork-belly chashu, sweet-balanced menma (bamboo shoots) and freshly hand-cut scallions. Offered as regular or large, plus a chashu version; rice and egg-over-rice round out the short menu.
bio.philosophy: Do one bowl, and do it carefully. The owner is described as working quietly and methodically, plating a nostalgic, old-fashioned (昔ながらの) Kyoto ramen meant to feel homely and warming rather than flashy.

anecdotes.text: The shop occupies a space that earlier housed a small French bistro before becoming Jiei, part of its low-profile, residential-street character.
Reported that the owner previously worked as a manager at the Kyoto ramen shop Daioh Ramen before opening Jiei in the Takara-ya/Masutani Kyoto-ramen tradition.
The space leans into Showa-era nostalgia, down to a television playing in the corner of the small counter room.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Bowl is built on wheat straight noodles boiled in a shared pot, with a tonkotsu-shoyu broth and (standard wheat) soy-based tare; no gluten-free preparation exists.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Broth is a tonkotsu-shoyu (pork bone + soy sauce) base; soy sauce in a traditional Kyoto chukasoba tare is wheat-based unless stated otherwise, and no allergen labeling is published.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Every bowl uses pork-bone broth and is topped with pork-belly chashu; there is no animal-free option.

cuisine: Kombu-specialist ramen and tsukemen (Rishiri/ma-kombu/rausu kelp dashi; gagome kombu tsukemen)
gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The noodles are wheat — a custom whole-grain blend built on Hokkaido "Haruyo Koi" first-grade flour — so the core dish contains gluten. Unusually for ramen, the signature kombu ramen broth is described by the shop as using no meat and no kaeshi (no soy-sauce tare), built only from blended ma-/rausu-/rishiri kombu plus seafood and bonito; that removes the usual wheat-shoyu tare risk from the broth, but the wheat noodles themselves rule out a gluten-free meal. No gluten-free noodle option and no dedicated GF handling is indicated.
vegan_detail: Not vegan and not vegetarian. Although the signature kombu ramen broth uses no meat stock (a kombu-and-seafood dashi), it is built on bonito (katsuo) flakes and other seafood, so it is not vegetarian/vegan. The gagome kombu tsukemen is also described with pork chashu and a soy-sauce tare in at least one account. There is no stated vegan or vegetarian option.
address_ja: 京都府京都市上京区西五辻東町74-2 五辻の昆布 2F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to lng 135.742645, lat 35.031174 (within the Kyoto box). Building-level match on 西五辻東町74-2 (the 五辻の昆布 main shop, ramen on 2F).
neighborhood: 上京区・西五辻東町 (Kitano-Hakubaicho / Kitano Tenmangu area)
seats_text: 10 seats per seating (counter-style), run as a 3-seating-per-day reservation system; each seating is a single seated group of up to 10.
hours_raw: 3-seating reservation system: ①11:00～ ②12:00～ ③13:00～ (3部制・各10席予約制), each seating ~45 minutes. Arrive by the 10-minutes-prior assembly time or entry is denied. Tabelog lists Sunday closed; official site notes 不定休 (irregular closures) — confirm via Instagram @kiichi5224 before going.
closed_days: Sundays per listing; the shop also states 不定休 (irregular closures), so the day-off can vary — check ahead.
cultural_comfort_note: A reservation-only, fixed-timing seating with a guided kombu tasting and a one-product purchase rule — smoothest if you can manage the online booking and follow along in Japanese, though the experience is welcoming.
caveats: Completely reservation-only (online), with fixed seating times and a strict on-time policy — late arrivals are turned away. A "One Kombu" rule (ワンコンブ制, since June 2023) means each diner must also buy at least one product from the 五辻の昆布 shop downstairs. Closures are irregular. Hours are modeled as the 11:00–13:00 seating window per day (kitchen closes shortly after the 13:00 seating).

roles: Operated by 五辻の昆布 (Itutuji no Konbu)
bio.background: Kiichi is the ramen room of Itutuji no Konbu, a Kyoto kombu specialist founded in 1902 (Meiji 35) on Nishi-Itsutsuji in Kamigyo-ku. The ramen counter sits on the second floor above the family's historic kelp shop, near Kitano Tenmangu.
bio.origin: Kyoto, in the Kitano-Hakubaicho / Kitano Tenmangu district of Kamigyo-ku.
bio.specialty: The signature 昆布らぁめん (kombu ramen) is built on a broth blending ma-kombu, rausu and rishiri kelp, lifted with bonito and seafood and — unusually for ramen — made with no meat stock and no soy-sauce kaeshi. The other headline bowl is the がごめ昆布つけ麺 (gagome kombu tsukemen), whose dipping water leans on sticky gagome kelp; whole-grain wheat noodles built on Hokkaido 'Haruyo Koi' flour are made to the shop's own spec.
bio.philosophy: The shop exists to help people rediscover the value of kombu: 昆布専門店が『昆布の価値を再認識してもらう』ために作った『昆布らぁめん』 — a kombu specialist's answer to falling kelp consumption and changing kitchens.

anecdotes.text: Each seating is a guided experience, not just a meal: diners first water-taste three kelp varieties side by side, then watch oboro-kombu shaved fresh by hand and laid over the noodles, finishing with a kombu rice ball and the shop's tsukudani.
Since June 2023 a 'One Kombu' rule asks every guest to buy at least one product from the kelp shop downstairs, tying the ramen experience back to the 120-year-old family trade.
The fresh oboro kombu is described as just-shaved and still soft (削り出された出来立てホヤホヤのおぼろ昆布), demonstrated live with the shop's own equipment.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The dish is wheat-noodle ramen/tsukemen with no gluten-free alternative noted, so cross-contamination is moot for celiacs — the meal itself contains gluten.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature kombu ramen is stated to use no kaeshi / no soy-sauce tare (かえしは不使用), so the flagship broth avoids the usual wheat-shoyu seasoning; however, an account of the gagome kombu tsukemen describes a soy-sauce tare.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth relies on bonito and seafood (and at least one bowl uses pork chashu), so it is not suitable for vegan or vegetarian diners.

positives.text: The signature kombu ramen broth uses no additives and no meat stock — a purely kombu-and-seafood dashi (添加物、肉類、かえしは不使用), unusually transparent and animal-stock-free for ramen.

cuisine: Shoyu chuka-soba (Kyoto dashi-style soy-sauce ramen)
neighborhood: Ichijoji, Sakyo-ku
gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The shop is a shoyu (soy-sauce) ramen specialist serving house-made wheat noodles produced fresh daily, in a soy-sauce tare that in Japan is almost always brewed with wheat. Broth and chashu add further risk. There is no indication of gluten-free noodles, tare, or any celiac accommodation. A celiac diner cannot safely eat the ramen here.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The signature bowls are built on an animal-based dashi (described as drawing on shellfish such as oysters, clams and shrimp) combined with Kyoto soy sauce, and topped with slow-cooked pork chashu. No vegan or vegetarian ramen is advertised.
address_ja: 京都府京都市左京区一乗寺払殿町48
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 京都府京都市左京区一乗寺払殿町48; within the Kyoto bounding box.
seats_text: Counter seating; deliberately spacious layout to let diners relax. Exact seat count not stated. Non-smoking; no private rooms.
hours_raw: Mon-Wed, Fri-Sun 11:00-15:00, 17:30-22:00; Thursday closed (plus occasional irregular closures).
closed_days: Thursdays, plus occasional irregular closures
cultural_comfort_note: A friendly neighborhood ramen counter in Kyoto's competitive Ichijoji ramen district; a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu go a long way, as English support is not guaranteed.

bio.background: Menya Kiyo opened on October 1, 2018, as the second shop of the Kyoto-born Menya Yukou group, planting its flag in Ichijoji, one of Japan's most fiercely contested ramen districts. The aim from the start was to carry Kyoto's dashi (broth) culture to the neighborhood, the rest of Japan, and the wider world.
bio.specialty: A Kyoto-style shoyu (soy-sauce) chuka-soba: a clear, mellow soy-sauce broth layered over a delicate dashi, paired with house-made noodles. The pork chashu is tenderized by the shop's own method and simmered for over two hours, then sliced to each diner's preferred thickness right at the counter.
bio.philosophy: The shop mills and makes its noodles fresh every morning at an in-house noodle facility, chasing a chewy texture that clings to the soy-based broth, and treats the spacious counter as a place where guests can slow down and relax over a bowl.

anecdotes.text: Chashu is slow-simmered for more than two hours and then carved to the customer's chosen thickness at the counter.
Noodles are produced fresh each morning in the group's own in-house noodle factory for a chewy bite that suits the soy-sauce broth.
Opened October 1, 2018 as the second branch of the Kyoto Menya Yukou group, with the stated mission of spreading Kyoto's dashi culture.

gf_cross_contamination.text: House-made wheat ramen noodles are produced fresh daily; the kitchen is built around wheat noodles with no gluten-free line, so cross-contamination with wheat is effectively unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The shop is a shoyu (soy-sauce) ramen specialist whose broth is finished with Kyoto soy sauce; Japanese soy sauce / shoyu tare is conventionally brewed with wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth relies on an animal/shellfish-based dashi and bowls are topped with pork chashu, so there is no vegan-safe preparation path.

caveats: Hours and closed days confirmed from the official site and Tabelog; exact seat count is not published (counter seating only). Soy-sauce tare and noodles are presumed wheat-based per standard Japanese ramen practice and the shop's shoyu specialty, but no explicit allergen statement was published by the shop. Coordinates from GSI geocoding of the registered address.

cuisine: Asian "no-borders" (mukokuseki) dining bar — Thai and Vietnamese-leaning fare (Khao Man Gai, Khao Soi, pho, pad thai, green curry, Thai salads)
neighborhood: Ichijoji, Sakyo-ku (near Ichijoji Station, Eizan Railway)
address_ja: 京都府京都市左京区高野玉岡町33-4 Nature bldg 2F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 高野玉岡町33 returned [135.784607, 35.045246], within the Kyoto box. Chō/banchi-level match (Nature bldg 2F).
seats_text: About 20-25 seats: a 5-seat counter overlooking the kitchen plus table/sofa seating for groups (sofa for 4-6, a large table for ~10). Counts vary slightly by source (Tabelog 20, one blog 25).
closed_days: Mondays
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, foreigner-friendly spot (a guesthouse occupies the floor above, so international guests are common), but English support is limited — a few words of Japanese go a long way.
gf_detail: No gluten-free program is advertised. The kitchen leans on naturally rice-based Southeast Asian dishes — Khao Man Gai (jasmine rice with poached chicken), pho and pad thai (rice noodles), and green curry over rice — which can be lower-gluten than the wheat-noodle ramen the street is known for. However, Thai/Vietnamese cooking commonly uses fish sauce, oyster sauce, and soy-based seasonings, and no cross-contamination handling is documented. A celiac diner must ask about each sauce; treat as not verified safe.
vegan_detail: Some plant-forward items exist (green salad, chilled tofu, vegetable salads, and green curry described as having a vegetarian option), and the building's ground floor is an organic vegetable market that supplies the kitchen. But Thai/Vietnamese dishes here typically use fish sauce, chicken broth, and egg, so a strict vegan must confirm preparation; full vegan is not guaranteed.
caveats: Despite the Tabelog "Ramen" tag and the Ichijoji ramen-street location, this is NOT a ramen shop. The menu is Thai/Vietnamese-leaning Asian "no-borders" cooking (Khao Man Gai, Khao Soi, pho, pad thai, green curry, Thai salads) with no chuka-soba or town-shokudo ramen on offer. The premise that it is a 町中華-style ramen shop does not hold. Hours differ between sources (Tabelog gives split lunch/dinner with lunch only Fri–Sun; other listings give 11:30–15:00 / 18:00–24:00 daily-ish) — confirm before visiting.

bio.background: Inaho Shokudo sits on the second floor of a small building in Ichijoji, above a ground-floor organic vegetable market and below a guesthouse — a layout that shapes the whole experience: produce comes from just downstairs, and travelers staying upstairs drift in, giving the room an easy international mix. Rather than a ramen counter, it is an Asian 'no-borders' (mukokuseki) dining bar leaning hard into Thai and Vietnamese home cooking, with herbs and seasonal vegetables grown by the kitchen.
bio.specialty: Khao Man Gai — jasmine rice cooked in concentrated chicken broth, topped with poached chicken and served with Thai chili and scallion sauces — is the dish reviewers single out. Khao Soi (Chiang Mai curry noodles), pho, pad thai, and green curry round out the noodle-and-rice section, with crispy herb-filled spring rolls and Yam Woon Sen among the small plates.
bio.philosophy: Lunch is framed as Asian street food in a plant-filled, greenery-draped space; evenings turn into a relaxed dining bar with music, drinks, and food. The cooking emphasizes fresh herbs and seasonal vegetables, partly sourced from the organic market downstairs.

anecdotes.text: The building stacks an organic vegetable market on the ground floor, the restaurant on the second, and a guesthouse on the third — so produce is local to the building and the crowd skews international.
Despite standing on Ichijoji's famous ramen street, the kitchen serves Thai and Vietnamese-inspired dishes — Khao Man Gai, Khao Soi, pho, green curry — rather than ramen.

positives.text: Many menu items are naturally rice-based rather than wheat-noodle — Khao Man Gai (jasmine rice + poached chicken), pho and pad thai (rice noodles), and curries over rice — which can suit a gluten-avoiding diner better than the wheat ramen the street is known for, though sauces should still be checked.
An organic vegetable market on the ground floor supplies the kitchen, and the menu includes plant-forward salads, chilled tofu, and a green curry with a vegetarian option.

lead: コムギノキラメキ〈小麦〉
name_ja: コムギノキラメキ〈小麦〉
cuisine: Tori-paitan (creamy chicken) ramen and Taiwan mazesoba
gf_detail: Not safe for celiacs. The house specialty is in-house-milled, in-house-made wheat noodles (a four-wheat blend), and the bowls pair them with chicken-paitan broth and typically wheat-based shoyu/miso tare. The flagship dish, Komugi no Chikara, is a thick-noodle Jiro-style mazesoba — entirely wheat. No gluten-free noodle or dedicated preparation was found, and no allergen program is advertised.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly stop. The broth is a chicken-paitan (creamy chicken) base and the Taiwan mazesoba is built on pork mince, egg yolk, and chicken-derived components; the Komugi no Chikara mazesoba features braised pork and back fat. No plant-based broth or vegan option was found on the menu.
address_ja: 京都府京都市西京区上桂前田町51-1
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 京都市西京区上桂前田町51-1; within the Kyoto bounding box.
seats_text: Around 21 seats: roughly 13 counter seats plus about 8 seats across 2 tables in the back. Spacious for a ramen shop and comfortable for families and solo diners.
closed_days: No regular closing day, but occasional/irregular temporary closures occur — worth checking ahead.
cultural_comfort_note: A casual ticket-machine ramen shop — buy a meal ticket at the vending machine before sitting; a smile and a few Japanese words go a long way, though the system is easy to navigate visually.

bio.background: A Kami-Katsura neighborhood ramen shop in Kyoto's Nishikyo Ward, this is one branch of the fast-growing Kirameki group, which began with its flagship Kirameki no Tori in Marutamachi and has spread across Kyoto and into Osaka. The shop sits about a 7-minute walk from Kamikatsura Station on the Hankyu Arashiyama line, marked by a bright yellow-green storefront and on-site parking.
bio.specialty: Two pillars: a creamy tori-paitan (chicken) ramen offered in salt, soy, and miso, and richly built mazesoba. The signature bowl is Komugi no Chikara ('the power of wheat'), a Jiro-style thick-noodle mazesoba piled with bean sprouts, menma, and big chunks of tender pork and back fat. The Taiwan mazesoba layers pork mince, egg yolk, garlic, chives, scallion, nori, and fish powder.
bio.philosophy: The name means roughly 'the sparkle of wheat,' and the shop leans hard into the noodle itself: noodles are milled and made in-house in a glass-fronted production room, using a custom blend of four wheats sourced from Nagano, Hokkaido, Australia and elsewhere — thick and chewy for mazesoba, medium for ramen, prized for their bite.

anecdotes.text: Komugi no Chikara, the house Jiro-style mazesoba, is offered at only a handful of the group's Kyoto shops, making this branch one of the places to try it.
Noodles are produced on-site behind glass, from a four-wheat blend (Nagano, Hokkaido, Australia and more), which gives the shop its 'sparkle of wheat' identity.

gf_cross_contamination.text: House noodles are wheat (a four-wheat blend), milled and made in-house, and used across every bowl; there is no gluten-free noodle and no allergen-handling program advertised, so cross-contamination from shared noodle water and prep is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soy- and miso-tare ramen are on the menu; standard ramen tare and miso in Japan are wheat-containing, and no wheat-free tare is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is chicken-paitan and dishes feature pork, egg yolk, and fish powder; no plant-based options were found, so vegan diners cannot eat here safely.

cuisine: Ramen (tonkotsu / pork-bone)
neighborhood: Ichijoji / Takano, Sakyo-ku
address_ja: 京都府京都市左京区高野竹屋町33-20
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned chō-level point for 高野竹屋町33 within the Kyoto box (lat 35.0426, lng 135.7812); behind Takano Post Office, a short walk from the Ichijoji ramen district.
seats_text: Counter only, around 6-7 seats; intimate one-person operation in a converted former snack bar.
hours_raw: 11:00-14:00 / 18:00-20:00 (sources also list the dinner block as 17:30-19:30). Open until sold out. Closed Wednesdays and Thursdays plus irregular holidays.
closed_days: Wednesday and Thursday, plus occasional irregular closures
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny counter run single-handedly by an approachable owner; ordering is done in Japanese when he comes out to greet you, and a little Japanese goes a long way in this snug local spot.
gf_detail: This is a wheat-noodle tonkotsu shop with no gluten-free offering. The noodles are wheat, the tare is almost certainly wheat-based shoyu, and the menu (kotteri/assari/tsukemen) is built around standard ramen components. No celiac-safe option and no published allergen handling. Not suitable for anyone requiring strict gluten avoidance.
vegan_detail: Every bowl is built on a pork-bone (tonkotsu) broth and topped with roast-pork chashu; there is no vegetable or vegan broth and no meat-free option on the menu. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
caveats: Target brief described a "niboshi-blended" broth, but every source consulted describes a pure pork-bone (tonkotsu) focus with no mention of niboshi/sardine in the blend; recorded as tonkotsu only. Dinner closing time differs between sources (20:00 on Tabelog vs 19:30 on review blogs); cooks until sold out. Closed days appear as Wed+Thu on Tabelog while some write-ups mention only Thursday plus irregular holidays.
sources: https://msearch.gsi.go.jp/address-search/AddressSearch?q=京都府京都市左京区高野竹屋町33-20

bio.background: Ramen Viska opened in summer 2023 (July 14) in a quiet residential pocket behind Takano Post Office, on the fringe of Ichijoji's famously crowded ramen battleground. It is a true one-person shop: the owner cooks, serves, and chats single-handedly. The space is a renovated former snack bar with a glossy bar-style counter, and the owner greets guests in a suit and tie rather than the usual ramen-cook garb. There is no noren curtain out front; a simple OPEN sign is the only signal that he is cooking that day.
bio.specialty: Super-rich kotteri tonkotsu: a pork-bone broth simmered until gelatinous and thick enough that, as locals joke, the spoon could stand up in it, yet it stays mild and clean on the palate with no porky funk, drinking almost like a potage. The lineup is deliberately spare — kotteri (rich), assari (a remarkably clear tonkotsu), a Bisuro (ビス郎) bowl, and tsukemen — paired with medium-straight noodles and lean roast-pork chashu. There are no gyoza or fried sides; the focus is entirely the bowl.
bio.philosophy: A minimalist, single-minded devotion to tonkotsu done well — quality broth over a broad menu, served personally and explained in detail to anyone curious.

anecdotes.text: The shop name 'Viska' comes from Catalan, used as a celebratory cheer meaning roughly 'long live!' / 'hooray!'
Among the table condiments sits an unexpected jar of harissa, the North African chili paste, which regulars say melds surprisingly well with the rich pork broth.

Despite a wall of posted notices, the owner is warm and talkative, coming out from behind the counter to take orders one guest at a time; diners can self-serve rice from a cooker and add takana and pickled ginger, with free meat and green-onion top-ups offered.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles are boiled in shared water, making the broth and any shared utensils unsuitable for strict gluten avoidance.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Tare/seasoning is standard ramen-shop shoyu, which is wheat-containing soy sauce; no wheat-free tare is offered.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broths are pork-bone based and every bowl is cooked in the same tonkotsu kitchen with pork chashu; no vegan-safe preparation exists.

cuisine: Iekei (Yokohama-style) tonkotsu-shoyu ramen
neighborhood: Ichijoji (Sakyo-ku)
address_ja: 京都府京都市左京区一乗寺北大丸町14 フラッツ一乗館 1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 京都府京都市左京区一乗寺北大丸町14 returned [135.786697, 35.045891]; within Kyoto box.
seats_text: 8 counter seats plus 2 tables of 4 (approx. 16 total)
hours_raw: Mon, Wed-Sat: 11:00-14:00, 18:00-23:30; Tue: 11:00-14:00; Sun: 11:00-14:00, 18:00-22:00 (one source lists 11:00-14:00, 18:00-22:00, closed Mondays). Hours subject to change.
closed_days: No fixed weekly closure listed on Tabelog (one blog reports closed Mondays); hours and days subject to change.
cultural_comfort_note: A small local ramen counter on Ichijoji's ramen street; little to no English, so a few Japanese phrases or pointing at the menu will smooth the visit.
vegan_detail: All bowls are built on pork-bone (tonkotsu) or pork-clear broth with chashu and other animal toppings; no vegan or vegetarian ramen is offered.
gf_detail: Standard ramen here uses wheat noodles (Goto noodles for the iekei bowl) and a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare that almost certainly contains wheat, plus pork broth. No gluten-free noodles or menu are offered, and there is no indication of celiac-safe handling.
caveats: Hours differ between sources (Tabelog lists evening service to 23:30 most nights; a local blog lists 18:00-22:00 and a Monday closure) — treat hours as irregular and confirm on arrival. Seat count is from a local blog, not the listing. Cash-only per the listing. No official website found.

bio.background: Menya Sawaki sits on Ichijoji's famous 'ramen street,' a stretch in northeast Kyoto packed with competing shops. It first opened in 2021 as Chuka Soba Nishiki (中華そば 錦) and was rebranded as Menya Sawaki in October 2023, making it one of the area's notable iekei (Yokohama-style) entrants in a district better known for Kyoto's heavier back-fat shoyu and assari styles.
bio.specialty: Iekei ramen: a shoyu-tonkotsu (pork-bone-and-soy) bowl described as rich yet surprisingly clean, served over thick Goto-style noodles and topped with pork chashu, spinach, nori, chopped white negi, kikurage, and a quail egg. A popular add-on set brings karaage and rice. The kitchen also ranges across Hakata yatai-style thin-noodle tonkotsu and a classic chuka-soba shoyu bowl.
bio.philosophy: A deliberately wide-ranging menu - iekei, Hakata, Kyoto shoyu, classic chuka soba and more - so that a single counter can satisfy very different ramen cravings, with evening service leaning toward small plates for a casual izakaya-style stop.

anecdotes.text: Diners single out the karaage as unusually fluffy and soft rather than the typical crisp-shelled style, a small signature alongside the ramen.
The iekei bowl is praised as 'looks thick but tastes clean,' a balance the shop aims for in its shoyu-tonkotsu broth.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The bowls are shoyu-tonkotsu based; the soy-sauce tare used in this style of ramen standardly contains wheat.

cuisine: Tantanmen (dandan noodle specialist)
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. This is a tantanmen specialist: wheat noodles (curly noodles from the noted maker Teikaku/棣鄂), a sesame-and-nut tare that in dandan noodles is typically built on wheat-based soy sauce and miso, and pork. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is advertised, and the standard rice-based finish is cooked together with the same sauce and toppings. A celiac diner cannot eat safely here.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. The bowls are built around ground pork and char siu, and there is no advertised vegan or vegetarian tantanmen. The shop highlights a vegetable-forward dumpling and some vegetable-topped variations, but the broth/sauce and proteins are meat-based throughout. No plant-based option is offered.
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区西洞院通姉小路下る姉西洞院町545 ホワイトプラザ1F
geocode_note: Geocoded from chō-level address (姉西洞院町545) via GSI; within Kyoto bounding box.
neighborhood: 中京区 / 烏丸御池駅（西洞院通姉小路下ル）
seats_text: 25 seats total: 6 counter seats, table seating for two (10 seats), and one table for six.
hours_raw: 11:30-15:00 / 17:30-21:00. Monday is lunch service only (no dinner). Closed for Obon (around Aug 16) and New Year holidays, plus occasional temporary closures.
closed_days: Open daily; Monday is lunch-only (no dinner). Closed Obon (around Aug 16) and New Year's; occasional irregular closures.
cultural_comfort_note: A casual neighborhood noodle counter with no indication of English support, so a little Japanese or pointing at the menu will smooth the visit.

bio.background: Goriki opened in 2016, back when dedicated tantanmen counters were still a rarity in Kyoto, taking over a space that had previously run as a night-only spot before expanding into lunch service. It has since grown into a fixture among the city's dandan-noodle lovers.
bio.specialty: The house specialty is its tantanmen built on a thick yet mellow sesame-and-nut sauce, balancing the numbing-and-spicy 'karashibi' character with nutty richness rather than chasing pure heat. The lineup runs from THE Tantanmen to a brothless (shirunashi) version, dipping-style tsukemen, the chili-heavy Karashibi, and a stamina version, plus seasonal limiteds.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen aims for a broth that is concentrated and smooth (noko de maroyaka) rather than punishingly hot, letting the Sichuan-pepper aroma and sesame depth carry the bowl.

anecdotes.text: Goriki's signature move is the shime finish: after you have worked through most of the noodles, the staff scrape the remaining meat and toppings into a bowl and cook it down with rice and a raw egg over the table's built-in burner, turning it into a crackly, stone-bowl-bibimbap-style 'second dish' whose egg mellows the lingering spice.
The brothless tantanmen uses curly noodles from the well-regarded Kyoto noodle maker Teikaku (棣鄂), tossed with minced meat, char siu, bean sprouts, scallions and nuts.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dandan-noodle tare is conventionally built on wheat-based soy sauce and miso; no wheat-free tare is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Bowls are built around ground pork and char siu and share a meat-based sauce; no plant-based separation is offered.

caveats: No published allergen, gluten-free, or vegan/vegetarian information was found; dietary assessments are inferred from the dish type (wheat noodles, wheat-based tare, pork) and should be confirmed in person. Tabelog lists dinner closing at 21:00 while some blogs cite 22:00; the 21:00 listing was used. Monday dinner is closed.

cuisine: Tantanmen (dandan noodles) specialty — yatai/food-cart style
gf_detail: This is a tantanmen (dandan noodle) specialist. The bowls are built on wheat noodles (straight thin noodles from a noodle supplier) in a sesame/miso/pork-based spicy broth, which in this style virtually always contains wheat-based shoyu/miso tare. No gluten-free options or gluten-free preparation were found, and there is no indication of a dedicated fryer or any cross-contamination control. Not suitable for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: A meat-based tantanmen shop: bowls feature ground meat (挽肉) and the broth is built on a pork/sesame/miso base. No vegan or vegetarian ramen is advertised. Not suitable for vegans.
address_ja: 京都府京都市西京区樫原山ノ上町7-19
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates within the Kyoto box for the full street address 樫原山ノ上町7-19.
neighborhood: Katsura / Kashihara (Nishikyo-ku) — a residential street near Route 9, roughly a car ride from Katsura Station
seats_text: About 13 seats — roughly 5 counter seats plus two 4-person tables.
closed_days: Tuesdays
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny owner-run neighborhood tantanmen counter on a residential street; expect a Japanese-only experience and a warm, regulars-driven atmosphere, so a few words of Japanese or a translation app will help.

bio.background: Marutan Ogawa began life as a yatai-style operation, with the owner serving tantanmen from the garage/parking space of his own home in a quiet residential pocket of Katsura. A few years ago, after renovating the house, the food-cart setup was converted into a proper indoor shop on the same spot — but it has kept the intimate, handmade, hole-in-the-wall feel of its food-cart roots.
bio.specialty: Spicy 'uma-kara' (delicious-spicy) tantanmen. The miso tantanmen pairs a miso-and-sesame broth with ground meat, bamboo shoots, Chinese greens and bean sprouts over straight thin noodles; seasonal bowls include a chilled tantanmen served over ice and topped with kimchi and crunchy vegetables, and a brothless (shiru-nashi) version.
bio.philosophy: A small, personal kitchen focused on doing one thing — tantanmen — well, in a casual, home-style setting that grew straight out of a neighborhood food cart.

anecdotes.text: What started as the owner serving noodles out of his own home garage in food-cart style was turned into an indoor shop a few years ago when he renovated the house.

The shop sits in a residential neighborhood near Route 9 that you basically need a car to reach; regulars include students from the nearby Kyoto University Katsura campus, who are on friendly terms with the owner.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Tantanmen here is made with wheat noodles in a sesame/miso/pork-based broth; this style of tare is wheat-based, so the dish is not gluten-free and no GF handling is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth and toppings are meat-based (ground meat plus a pork/sesame/miso broth), so vegan diners cannot be accommodated.

caveats: Prices and menu vary; the kyotopi article lists a miso tantanmen at 600 yen and notes seasonal limited items (chilled tantanmen, brothless tantanmen), but current pricing was not independently confirmed. No allergy/gluten-free/vegan accommodation information was found from any fetched page; dietary judgments are inferred from the dish type. The ramendb page returned 403 and could not be read.

cuisine: Motsunabe (beef-offal hotpot) izakaya with a lunch-only ramen service — hormone/offal ramen, clam (asari) ramen, seasonal cold ramen
neighborhood: Tanaka Sekidacho / Demachiyanagi, Sakyo-ku
address_ja: 京都府京都市左京区田中関田町29-4
geocode_note: Geocoded from full address via GSI; first feature coordinates [135.773849, 35.028057], within the Kyoto box.
seats_text: Around 40-53 seats including tatami zashiki seating in a converted machiya townhouse (sources vary); roughly 40 per one diner account, 53 per the listing.
hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-14:00 (L.O. 13:30); Dinner 17:00-23:30 (L.O. 23:00). Wed & Thu dinner only (no lunch). Ramen is a lunch-only offering.
closed_days: No regular full closing day; lunch service is not offered on Wednesday and Thursday. Closed for year-end/New Year holidays. (One diner account cites Wednesday lunch closed — exact mid-week lunch days vary by source; confirm directly.)
cultural_comfort_note: A casual neighborhood machiya izakaya where ordering and any dietary questions will go most smoothly in Japanese; an English menu is not advertised.
gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The lunch bowls are standard wheat ramen noodles, and the kitchen is built around motsunabe with chicken/garlic broths and soy-based seasonings — no gluten-free noodles, dedicated handling, or allergen accommodation is advertised. Cross-contamination with wheat is effectively guaranteed in a shared ramen and hotpot kitchen.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly destination. The house identity is beef offal (motsu) and the ramen leans on offal, clams, duck, and chicken broth; even the cabbage-heavy "hormone ramen" is built on meat/animal stock. No plant-based ramen or vegan set is offered.
caveats: Primary identity is a motsunabe (beef-offal hotpot) izakaya that added a lunch-only ramen service; it is not a dedicated ramen specialist. The target's "niboshi (dried sardine) ramen" angle could not be confirmed on any fetched page — verified lunch ramen styles are an offal/cabbage "hormone" bowl, a clam (asari) salt bowl, and a seasonal cold (duck) bowl. Seat count and exact mid-week lunch-closure days differ between sources; treat as approximate and confirm directly.

bio.background: Kyogame occupies a restored Kyoto machiya townhouse along Imadegawa-dori, a short walk from Demachiyanagi. The owner, known affectionately as Kame-san, runs it less like a polished restaurant and more like a friend's home — courtyard garden, tatami rooms where you can stretch your legs, and the unhurried feel of an old popular izakaya.
bio.specialty: The house centerpiece is a hearty garlic motsunabe: premium select beef offal in a quality chicken-bone broth, with white miso, soy, or salt variations, plus Kumamoto horse-meat sashimi and grilled seasonal offal. At lunch the kitchen turns to ramen — the signature being a cabbage-piled hormone (offal) ramen, alongside a light clam (asari) salt bowl and, in summer, a cold duck-and-vegetable bowl, all served with a generous 1.5 portions of noodles.
bio.philosophy: Generous, unfussy, and deliberately affordable — the lunch bowls are priced so that students and regulars can come often, in a room meant to feel like dropping by someone's house rather than dining out.

anecdotes.text: A writer who once worked at a related shop describes the owner Kame-san as encouraging and supportive of his staff's ambitions, the kind of proprietor people stay loyal to.
The cabbage-heavy hormone ramen and the affordable lunch sets are pitched as a welcome deal for students.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Lunch ramen uses standard wheat noodles and shares a kitchen with motsunabe and fried sides, so wheat cross-contact is unavoidable; no gluten-free option is mentioned anywhere.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Broths and seasonings are soy- and miso-based (a soy-shoyu motsunabe option is offered), which in Japan normally means wheat-containing soy sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Even the vegetable-forward cabbage 'hormone ramen' is built on beef offal and animal-based broth; all advertised bowls contain meat, clams, duck, or chicken stock.

lead: らぁ～めん京 祇園本店
name_ja: らぁ～めん京 祇園本店
cuisine: 鶏白湯ラーメン（鶏・野菜ベースの白湯／醤油・塩・味噌）
gf_detail: This is a wheat-noodle ramen shop. The bowls use the house 低温熟成細麺 (low-temperature aged thin wheat noodles), a paitan broth built on chicken and vegetables, and tare options including soy (醤油, typically wheat-based) and miso, finished with pork chashu. There is no gluten-free menu, no published allergen handling, and no indication of a dedicated gluten-free preparation area. Not safe for celiac diners; cross-contamination from shared noodle water and broth is effectively unavoidable. Confirm directly with the shop if you must, but treat as not gluten-free.
vegan_detail: The signature broth is a chicken-and-vegetable paitan (鶏白湯) and bowls come standard with pork chashu, egg, and other animal toppings; the broth itself is animal-based. There is no vegan or vegetarian ramen offering noted on any source. Not suitable for vegans.
address_ja: 京都府京都市東山区祇園町北側303
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned coordinates for 祇園町北側303; within Kyoto bounding box.
neighborhood: 東山区祇園（八坂神社すぐ北側）／祇園四条駅 徒歩約8分
seats_text: 26 seats total — 10 counter seats plus four 4-person tables.
hours_raw: 月～木・日 11:00～22:00（L.O. 21:30）／金・土 11:00～翌3:00（L.O. 翌2:30）。※当面の間、営業時間を変更している場合あり（要確認）。定休日なし。
closed_days: 定休日なし（No regular closing day）
cultural_comfort_note: A busy walk-in ramen counter beside Yasaka Shrine in Gion; tourists are common, but a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu go a long way, and it is cash-only.

bio.origin: Gion, Higashiyama — the main shop sits just north of Yasaka Shrine in the heart of Kyoto's old geisha district.
bio.background: The Gion honten is the flagship of the Raamen Miyako (らぁ～めん京) group, which has since grown to additional locations including Hyogo and Shiga, and runs a much-praised stall in Kyoto Station's Kyoto Ramen Koji (ramen alley), where it opened in June 2023.
bio.specialty: The signature bowl is the 'ぎをん白湯' (Gion paitan) — a chicken-and-vegetable white broth, slightly enriched with back-fat for body without heaviness, paired with the shop's own low-temperature aged thin noodles. The 'みやこらぁ～めん' tops it with a wide slice of chashu, soft egg, scallion and menma.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen states it uses only carefully selected ingredients and does meticulous prep every day, slowly drawing chicken umami and vegetable sweetness into a broth that is rich yet light enough to drink to the bottom.
roles: Gion flagship of a small Kyoto-based group

anecdotes.text: The broth is described as having solid body from chicken and a touch of back-fat, yet staying savory rather than heavy — 'finished enough to drink down as the close of a meal.'
The shop developed its own 'low-temperature aged thin noodles' (低温熟成細麺) prized for a chewy-yet-smooth texture that clings to the Gion paitan broth.
Its side-dish fried rice uses rice from the noted Kyoto brand Hachidaime Gihey, and the group's bowl was a top seller at Kyoto Station's ramen alley.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Bowls are built on house wheat thin noodles boiled in shared water, with soy/miso tare and chicken-vegetable broth; no gluten-free preparation is offered.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soy (醤油) is one of the standard tare options; ordinary Japanese shoyu contains wheat, and no wheat-free tare is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The base broth is animal (chicken paitan) and bowls include pork chashu and egg; no vegan option is offered.

caveats: Hours are flagged as temporarily modified on Tabelog ("当面の間、営業時間変更しております") — confirm before a late-night Fri/Sat visit. Phone listed as both 075-541-1385 (shop) and a booking-service number. No allergen/gluten/vegan accommodation information was found on any source; dietary assessments are inferred from the standard wheat-noodle, chicken-paitan, pork-topped ramen format.

cuisine: 町中華・ラーメン (生姜中華そば / ginger shoyu ramen)
gf_detail: This is a wheat-noodle ramen and town-Chinese shop. The bowls are built on medium-thick wheat ramen noodles in a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare over an animal-based broth, plus fried rice and other Chinese dishes — all standard sources of gluten. Soy-sauce tare is essentially always wheat-containing, and there is no indication of any gluten-free menu, dedicated equipment, or allergen handling. Not suitable for celiacs; anyone strictly gluten-free should avoid.
vegan_detail: The core menu is built on an animal-based (pork/meat) dashi broth with pork chashu toppings, and the shop is a meat-forward town-Chinese ramen place. No vegan or vegetable-broth ramen is indicated. Not a vegan-friendly option.
address_ja: 京都府京都市下京区四条堀川東入る柏屋町14
geocode_note: Full-address geocode returned empty; used chō-level (京都市下京区柏屋町) fallback, which resolves near the Shijo-Horikawa intersection matching the shop's stated location. Coordinates are approximate (block-level) and within the Kyoto box.
neighborhood: 下京区 (Shijo-Horikawa / near Omiya Station)
seats_text: About 14 seats: counter seats with partitions on the ground floor, plus a second floor with one table for four and small raised tatami-style seating for about six.
hours_raw: 11:30〜22:00 (L.O. 21:30). Sundays: Uber Eats delivery only.
closed_days: No fixed regular closing day confirmed for dine-in; Sundays are Uber Eats delivery only (no in-store dining). Confirm before visiting.
cultural_comfort_note: A small, locals' town-Chinese ramen counter where ordering in basic Japanese (or by pointing at the menu) is expected; no English service indicated.
caveats: Dine-in hours and the lack of a confirmed fixed holiday should be verified directly, since Sunday is delivery-only. Coordinates are block-level approximate due to a geocoding fallback. No gluten-free or vegan accommodation; not suitable for those diets.

bio.origin: Kyoto (Shimogyo ward, near the Shijo-Horikawa intersection and Omiya Station)
bio.background: Mentaiken is a small neighborhood 'machi-chuka' (town-Chinese) ramen shop in central Kyoto, the kind of unpretentious local counter where regulars come for a comforting bowl. It is run essentially as a near one-person operation, with the owner-chef handling most of the cooking.
bio.specialty: The signature bowl is the ginger ramen (生姜ラーメン / 生姜中華そば): an animal-based broth seasoned with soy-sauce tare and finished with a generous amount of freshly grated ginger, so that a soup which looks mild actually delivers a real punch of ginger warmth. It is served with medium-thick, low-hydration straight noodles that have a chewy bite (extra-firm available on request) and sliced pork chashu with chopped green onion. The shop also serves a 'Kyoto black' deep-soy-sauce ramen, ramen-and-fried-rice sets, spicy mala noodles, and negi-chashu.
bio.philosophy: In-store signage sums up the aim as making 'memorable ramen that stays in your heart' — a straightforward, regulars-first approach to a satisfying everyday bowl.

anecdotes.text: The shop expanded its set options with a 'nami-nami set' (regular ramen plus regular fried rice) so customers could finally order full-size portions of both together, rather than only the smaller combinations offered before.
The ginger ramen is described as especially popular with women, its grated-ginger soup giving a flavor punch that belies the broth's mild appearance.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All ramen is built on wheat noodles and a soy-sauce tare, and the kitchen also makes fried rice and Chinese dishes, so shared cookware and gluten exposure throughout are inherent.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The bowls are shoyu (soy-sauce) ramen, including a 'Kyoto black' deep-soy-sauce style; soy-sauce tare for ramen is essentially always wheat-based.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is animal-based dashi with pork chashu; no vegan/vegetable-broth option is indicated.

cuisine: 京都ラーメン（豚骨・鶏ガラ醤油・背脂）
neighborhood: 中京区河原町 / 京都河原町駅
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation. This is a classic Kyoto-style ramen shop: the noodles are wheat, the tare (seasoning) is soy-sauce based (typically wheat-containing shoyu), and the broth is a pork-bone and chicken soy-sauce base. There is no published allergen handling, no gluten-free noodle option, and no dedicated prep area. A celiac diner cannot eat safely here.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians. Every bowl is built on an animal broth (pork bone / chicken bones) and topped with chashu (braised pork). The menu is limited to ramen, chashu ramen, rice and green onion add-ons; there is no plant-based broth or vegetable ramen.
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区米屋町380-6
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned [135.769669, 35.004475] for 京都府京都市中京区米屋町380-6; within Kyoto box.
seats_text: About 10–11 counter seats facing the kitchen, plus two traditional sunken-floor (horigotatsu) tatami table seats in the back.
hours_raw: Weekdays 11:30-15:00 / 17:00-23:00; Saturdays & holidays roughly 11:30-23:00; Sunday until 22:30. Sources vary on exact midday break and on a possible Monday closure — confirm locally before a special trip.
closed_days: Sources conflict: the listing shows no fixed regular holiday, while some write-ups note closed Mondays (open if a public holiday). Confirm before visiting.
cultural_comfort_note: A small, classic counter ramen shop where ordering is straightforward but staff and menu are Japanese; a little Japanese or pointing goes a long way.
caveats: Hours and closed-day details differ between sources (midday break vs. continuous service; possible Monday closure); verify locally before a special trip. No allergen, gluten-free, or vegan information is published.

bio.background: Sugichiyo is the revival of a legendary Kyoto ramen shop. The original opened in 1999 in the Uzumasa area of western Kyoto and drew daily lines for roughly a decade before it closed at the end of 2009 — reportedly because the crowds it attracted had become difficult to sustain. After about twelve years dormant, the name returned in June 2021 in a renovated spot down a Takase River alley just west of Shijo-Kawaramachi, in the heart of central Kyoto's nightlife district. The current team is said to have inherited the complete original recipe directly from the founder.
bio.specialty: Chashu ramen — the bowl that built the shop's reputation. The pork-bone and chicken soy-sauce broth carries the deep richness of Kyoto-style ramen with a thin veil of back fat on top, yet drinks lighter and cleaner than its color suggests; the soy tare is comparatively restrained, letting the broth's umami lead. It comes over low-hydration straight thin noodles, finished with melt-tender homemade chashu (made by the founder's original slow-braise method), menma, nori and green onion. A touch of chili pepper is added by default, giving a gentle heat from the first sip.
bio.philosophy: Faithfully carry forward a beloved recipe rather than reinvent it — a nostalgic, unpretentious bowl served with quiet, courteous hospitality instead of the high-intensity style of many modern ramen shops.

anecdotes.text: Sugichiyo's flavor was famous enough that Nissin Foods produced it as a cup-noodle product — a rare nod from a major manufacturer.
The original shop closed in 2009 reportedly because of complaints about the long lines it generated, and was reborn in 2021 at Shijo-Kawaramachi.
The current operators say they received the full original recipe from the founder 'without any leaks,' aiming to reproduce the legendary taste exactly.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare base; standard Japanese shoyu contains wheat, and the shop publishes no gluten-free or wheat-free tare.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All bowls use animal broth (pork bone / chicken) and pork chashu; no separate vegetable stock or vegan prep is mentioned.

cuisine: 鶏白湯・鶏そば（鶏出汁の塩ラーメン専門）
gf_detail: This is a salt ramen specialist built on wheat noodles (curly noodles supplied by Kyoto noodle maker Menya Teigaku/麺屋棣鄂), so the core dish is not gluten-free. The broth is chicken-based and the seasoning is a salt (shio) tare rather than a wheat-shoyu tare, which avoids one common wheat source, but the noodles themselves contain wheat and there is no indication of a gluten-free noodle option, dedicated prep, or celiac-level cross-contamination control. Not suitable for celiac diners; a wheat-allergic diner should treat it as off-limits unless they confirm directly.
vegan_detail: Every bowl is built on a chicken broth (described as roughly 99% chicken stock, using Tanba black chicken bones and Kyoto red-breed chicken) with chicken chashu and egg toppings. There is no vegan or vegetarian ramen on the menu and the entire concept is animal-based, so it does not work for vegans.
address_ja: 京都府京都市左京区下鴨西半木町70-2
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to the chō-level address (下鴨西半木町70); within the Kyoto bounding box. Note: although the curated area lists it under Kitaoji Station / Kitayama, the registered address is in Sakyo Ward (Shimogamo), just east across the river from Kitaoji, near the Kyoto Botanical Garden.
seats_text: 7 counter seats only
hours_raw: Tue–Sun 08:00–15:00; additional evening service Sunday 18:00–21:00; closed Mondays. May close early when the day's soup stock runs out.
closed_days: Mondays
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny 7-seat counter shop run by one owner where Japanese is the working language; basic Japanese or a translation app helps, and ordering is straightforward once seated.
caveats: Only 7 counter seats and it sells out when the day's stock is gone, so morning visits are safest. Cash only. The registered address is in Shimogamo (Sakyo Ward), a short walk east of Kitaoji Station rather than in Kitayama proper.
neighborhood: 下鴨・北大路（Shimogamo / Kitaoji）

bio.background: Torisoba 223 is a one-man salt-ramen specialist that opened in 2020 in a renovated storefront just south of the Kyoto Botanical Garden, a short walk from Kitaoji. The shop's odd name is a play on the owner's surname: 2-2-3 reads as 'tsu-tsu-mi' (Tsutsumi).
bio.specialty: An unusually delicate, additive-free (無化調) salt ramen built on a clear chicken broth made from Tanba black-chicken bones and Kyoto red-breed chicken meat, finished with fragrant chicken oil and paired with curly wheat noodles from Kyoto maker Menya Teigaku. Bowls carry playful Italian-flavored names, and a table-side olive/special sauce lets diners shift the flavor mid-bowl.
bio.philosophy: Tsutsumi openly states his goal is to be 'No.1 in salt ramen.' He is a certified salt sommelier and blends four salts (including Andes rock salt and a sea salt) to build a smooth, layered savoriness rather than a sharp saltiness, and avoids chemical seasonings.

anecdotes.text: The shop name 223 is read 'Tsutsumi' after the owner's surname.
The owner is a certified salt sommelier who blends four kinds of salt (including Andes rock salt) to design the broth's seasoning.
It opens early at 8:00 for ramen-as-breakfast and is treated as a morning-ramen spot in Kyoto.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The bowls are seasoned with a salt (shio) tare rather than a soy-sauce tare, so wheat soy sauce is not the primary seasoning; however this is not a substitute for being gluten-free because the noodles are wheat.

positives.text: No chemical seasonings are used (無化調); the broth is a clear chicken stock.

cuisine: 鶏白湯ラーメン（泡系白湯・丸鶏／鶏ガラ）
neighborhood: 左京区松ヶ崎（松ケ崎駅）
vegan_detail: No vegan options. The signature bowls are built on a chicken-based broth (whole chicken and chicken bones), with chashu, flavored egg, and other animal toppings; the fish-soy ramen uses fish-flake dashi. Nothing on the menu is plant-based.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free and not suitable for celiacs. This is a classic ramen shop: wheat noodles served in a chicken paitan or fish-soy broth finished with soy-sauce-based tare (Japanese soy sauce / white soy sauce, both wheat-containing). Tsukemen (dipping noodles) is also wheat. No gluten-free menu, no documented allergen accommodation.
address_ja: 京都府京都市左京区松ケ崎泉川町16-2
geocode_note: GSI geocode of the chō-level address returned [135.775757, 35.04781], within the Kyoto box.
seats_text: Around 20 seats in a renovated single-family house: counter and table seating plus a beach-house-style balcony/terrace area on the upper floor.
hours_raw: 11:00-15:00 / 17:00-20:30 (L.O. 20:00). Wednesday and Sunday evenings closed (calendar-dependent). Lunch served daily.
closed_days: Wednesday evening and Sunday evening (irregular / calendar-dependent); lunch served every day
cultural_comfort_note: A small residential-neighborhood ramen shop with friendly, attentive staff; ordering is straightforward but expect a Japanese-only experience, so a little Japanese or a translation app helps.
caveats: Hours are irregular (Wednesday and Sunday evenings typically closed, calendar-dependent); cash only and no reservations, with queues common at peak times. Confirm the day's schedule before visiting.

bio.background: 麺屋 しな埜 began life in February 2020 as TETSUpan Style+ しな埜, a yakisoba specialty shop, before reinventing itself as a ramen-focused restaurant in March 2021 with chicken paitan as its new signature. A further full renovation in September 2024 refreshed the menu and the space. It sits, almost unexpectedly, in a quiet residential pocket of Matsugasaki near the ward office, drawing queues to a converted white single-family house with sunlit counter seating and a breezy balcony area.
bio.specialty: The house bowl is the 泡白湯 (awa paitan): a broth simmered from whole chicken and chicken bones, then whipped into a creamy, mousse-like foam and finished with a Japanese-style soy tare for a surprisingly clean, light aftertaste. The kitchen also offers a chicken paitan, a fish-soy (gyokai-shoyu) ramen built on a low-temperature dashi of three fish flakes, kombu and dried shiitake with chicken oil, and a rich fish-pork tsukemen.
bio.philosophy: Rather than the heavier bowls Kyoto is often known for, the shop leans on technique and restraint, using the whipped-foam broth and carefully drawn dashi to keep flavors smooth and the finish clean.

anecdotes.text: The shop is a renovated white house with second-floor counter seating and a beach-house-style balcony, and it has become a quiet favorite among neighbors and nearby university students who line up despite the out-of-the-way residential location.

Visitors note the broth's creamy, foamed texture is achieved with an espuma-style whipping technique, accented with coarse black pepper, giving a lighter, less-salty profile than typical for the city.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broths are finished with soy-sauce-based tare (Japanese soy sauce / white soy sauce), which is wheat-containing, and the noodles are wheat ramen noodles.

lead: 中華そば アッキー
name_ja: 中華そば アッキー
cuisine: 町中華・中華そば（背脂ブラック中華そば）
neighborhood: 左京区下鴨（松ケ崎駅・下鴨本町バス停）
gf_detail: This is a pork-broth chuka soba (ramen) shop built around wheat noodles and a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare, both of which contain wheat. The signature bowls are a back-fat "black" soy ramen and a back-fat tonkotsu-shoyu ramen. There is no published gluten-free menu, no gluten-free noodle option, and no statement about gluten handling. A celiac or gluten-sensitive diner cannot safely eat the core menu here.
vegan_detail: The shop's entire identity is a 100% pork broth ("豚100％のスープ"), with back fat, chashu pork, and pork-bone soy bases across every bowl. There are no vegetarian or vegan ramen options on the published menu and no plant-based broth offered.
address_ja: 京都府京都市左京区下鴨東本町26-3 デミ洛北 1F
geocode_note: GSI address search on 京都府京都市左京区下鴨東本町26-3 returned the first feature at [135.773941, 35.043804], within the Kyoto box.
seats_text: Counter seating only, about 7 seats (in front of the kitchen plus a few set slightly apart).
hours_raw: Mon 10:30-14:30; Tue closed; Wed-Fri 10:30-14:30 and 17:00-20:30; Sat 10:30-14:30; Sun 08:00-14:30. Note: evening service is suspended for the time being ("当面の間、夜営業は無し"), so currently lunch-only daytime hours apply.
closed_days: Tuesday (evening service currently suspended on all days)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny counter-only neighborhood ramen shop run by a local owner; expect Japanese-only service and a quick, casual lunch flow, so a little Japanese or a translation app helps.
caveats: Opened August 2025 and still settling in: evening service is suspended for the time being, so plan for daytime/lunch hours. Closed Tuesdays. Cash only (PayPay also accepted). No allergen, gluten, or vegan information is published, so anyone with dietary needs should treat this as a standard pork-broth, wheat-noodle ramen shop.

bio.chef_name: Mr.京都アッキー (Mr. Kyoto Akky)
bio.background: Akky is a Kyoto native who became well known across the city's ramen scene as a food creator, running the YouTube channel 'Mr.京都アッキーのぶらり飲食店巡り' along with TikTok, Instagram and X, where he tirelessly reviewed Kyoto ramen shops. Before opening this place he spent about two years running a ramen shop on borrowed premises in Fushimi, learning the craft hands-on, and studied under experienced ramen makers before launching his own standalone shop in Shimogamo in August 2025.
bio.specialty: His signature is the 'Triple GO' back-fat black chuka soba (トリプルGO 背脂ブラック中華そば) - a dark soy-and-back-fat bowl in the classic Kyoto 'black' style - alongside a back-fat tonkotsu-shoyu chuka soba.
bio.philosophy: Akky builds everything on a 100% pork broth simmered carefully from early morning, drawing out the depth of pork bone and pork meat while keeping the finish clean rather than heavy. He describes the goal as a soup that is rich yet sharp ('コクがありながらもキレのある'), an everyday bowl that people of all ages can enjoy.

anecdotes.text: His shop is a personal homage to the three Kyoto ramen styles he loves most, blending the black broth of Shinpukusaikan, the pork-bone style of Dai-ichi Asahi, and the back-fat-forward approach of Masutani into one bowl of what he himself most wants to eat.
Among Kyoto ramen fans he is described as someone 'there isn't a single ramen lover in Kyoto who doesn't know' - a familiar face who turned his fandom into his own shop.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The bowls are built on a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare, including a dark-soy 'black' version; standard Japanese shoyu is brewed with wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Every broth is a 100% pork soup with back fat and pork toppings, so there is unavoidable animal content throughout the kitchen.

cuisine: つけ麺・ラーメン（タピオカ粉配合麺・土鍋／石鍋の熱々つけ汁、豚骨魚介系濃厚つけ汁）
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. This is a tsukemen specialist: the signature noodles are wheat-based (with tapioca flour blended in, but still wheat), and the rich pork-and-fish (豚骨魚介) dipping broth is built on wheat-containing shoyu/miso tare — the spicy "辛醤つけめん" is miso-based. There is no mention anywhere of gluten-free options, a separate fryer, or any celiac accommodation. A celiac diner cannot eat safely here.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The core product is a thick pork-bone-and-seafood (豚骨魚介) dipping broth, and add-ons include raw egg; the menu centers on animal-based tsukemen and ramen with no plant-based broth or noodle option mentioned. No vegan offering is advertised.
address_ja: 京都府京都市左京区下鴨西本町44
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned first feature geometry.coordinates=[135.769363, 35.043739] for 京都府京都市左京区下鴨西本町44; within Kyoto box.
neighborhood: 下鴨西本町（北山駅・洛北高校前バス停エリア）
seats_text: 14 seats (counter and table seating; woody interior). Order via a ticket machine at the entrance, choosing noodle thickness (thick or medium-thick) and garlic preference.
closed_days: 火曜・水曜（Tuesday and Wednesday）
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, ticket-machine ramen counter popular with local students; minimal English and a fast-paced lunch flow, so a little Japanese (or pointing at the machine) goes a long way.

bio.background: A small, independent tsukemen specialist tucked into the Shimogamo neighborhood of Kyoto's Sakyo Ward, just west of where Shimogamo-hondori meets Kitaoji. It has built a loyal following among nearby students and locals as a destination for serious, rich dipping noodles.
bio.specialty: The signature is a thick, rich pork-bone-and-seafood (豚骨魚介) tsukemen with a pronounced seafood depth, served with chewy noodles made by blending tapioca flour into the dough. Its calling card is the dipping broth served bubbling in a clay/stone pot (土鍋・石鍋): the pot is brought to a boil and its heat retention keeps the tsukejiru piping hot from the first bite to the last.
bio.philosophy: Generous and unfussy — large portions (up to 400g) are offered at the same price as a regular serving, and diners customize noodle thickness and garlic to taste.

anecdotes.text: The defining feature is the screaming-hot dipping broth: it is brought to the table in a clay/stone pot kept at a rolling boil, so the tsukejiru stays piping hot (アツアツ) right to the end of the meal instead of cooling off the way ordinary tsukemen broth does.

The noodles are made with tapioca flour worked into the dough for a distinctive chew, and the broth is a thick pork-and-seafood (豚骨魚介) style; portions run up to 400g at the same price, making it a favorite of the area's students.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The dipping broth is a thick pork-bone-and-seafood tare-based tsukejiru, and the spicy version is miso-based (辛醤つけめん); these standard ramen/tsukemen seasonings contain wheat-based soy/miso.

caveats: Cash only and no reservations; very popular with students, so lunchtime can be busy. Order at the ticket machine and pick noodle thickness/garlic. No gluten-free or vegan options; the clay-pot broth is intentionally bubbling-hot, so handle the pot with care. Closed Tuesday and Wednesday.

cuisine: 京都背脂醤油ラーメン（唐辛子トッピングが名物）
gf_detail: This is a classic Kyoto pork-and-chicken-bone soy ramen shop. The bowl is built on wheat noodles with a soy-sauce tare and a pork/chicken-bone broth enriched with pork back-fat, so it is not celiac-safe. There is no indication of gluten-free noodles, a gluten-free tare, or any allergen accommodation, and the kitchen is a single shared ramen line. A celiac diner should treat this as off-limits; even the signature chili topping and the soy-and-pepper fried rice involve wheat-based seasoning.
vegan_detail: The entire menu centers on a long-simmered pork-bone and chicken-bone broth finished with pork back-fat, and the bowls come with pork chashu and menma; the fried rice is cooked with pork and egg-style seasoning. There is no plant-based broth or vegan option here. A vegan diner has nothing to eat at this shop. The chili topping can be omitted on request, but that does not change the animal-based broth.
address_ja: 京都府京都市北区紫野西蓮台野町57 ＵＫハイム１Ｆ
geocode_note: GSI geocode of the chō-level address (紫野西蓮台野町57) returned [135.739014, 35.046566], inside the Kyoto box and consistent with the Kitayama-dori / Senbon-Kitaoji area near Kinkaku-ji.
neighborhood: 北区 紫野・衣笠（千本北大路・北山通／金閣寺方面）
seats_text: About 20 seats: counter seats facing the kitchen plus a small raised tatami area with a few low tables.
closed_days: No fixed regular closing day; closures are irregular and announced ad hoc. Closed on New Year's Day.
cultural_comfort_note: A no-frills, cash-only neighborhood ramen counter run for locals with no English service, so come prepared to point at the menu and keep it simple.

bio.background: Tanpopo is a Kyoto ramen institution out near the western end of Kitayama-dori, in the Murasakino/Kinugasa pocket toward Kinkaku-ji. Sources describe it as a shop loved by Kyoto residents for around 70 years, with a founding commonly cited as 1947, making it one of the old-guard shops that carry the history of Kyoto-style ramen into the present.
bio.origin: Kyoto, in the Kita-ku Murasakino area near Bukkyo University and Kitayama-dori.
bio.specialty: The signature bowl is a Kyoto back-fat soy-sauce ramen famous for a topping of red chili spread across the entire surface, so the bowl arrives looking fiery red. The broth is a rich pork-bone and chicken-bone soup simmered for many hours and finished with sweet pork back-fat; the chili sits on top rather than being cooked into the soup, so the actual heat is mild-to-moderate rather than punishing. Medium noodles, pork-belly chashu that turns crumbly-tender, green onion and menma round it out. The other house favorite is an old-fashioned, hearty fried rice (yakimeshi) with pork and onion, popular as a ramen-and-fried-rice set.
bio.philosophy: A deceptively dramatic, comforting bowl: the chili makes a strong first impression, but the flavor underneath is the deep, fatty, soy-rich savoriness of classic Kyoto ramen. The shop leans into a warm, unpretentious neighborhood feel rather than polish.

anecdotes.text: The bowl is famous for being blanketed in red chili so it looks intensely spicy, but because the chili is sprinkled on top rather than mixed into the broth it is only mildly spicy, and you can order it with the chili left off entirely while the soup stays delicious on its own.

Described as loved by Kyoto people for about 70 years and as a legend shop conveying the history of Kyoto ramen, with a retro exterior, humorous handwritten signs around the room, and a regular crowd of locals rather than tourists.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles are cooked in shared boiling water on a single ramen line, so any bowl carries gluten and cross-contact risk; no gluten-free preparation is mentioned anywhere.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The shop is a Kyoto soy-sauce (shoyu) ramen specialist; the tare is a standard soy-based seasoning, which in this style is wheat-containing soy sauce, with no gluten-free alternative noted.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Every bowl is built on a multi-hour pork-bone and chicken-bone broth finished with pork back-fat and topped with pork chashu, so the cooking line is fundamentally animal-based with no separate vegan preparation.

positives.text: The signature chili topping can be omitted on request, since it is sprinkled on top rather than cooked into the broth.

caveats: Hours vary by source: Tabelog's summary listed 11:30-21:00, but the more detailed Kyoto food sources give Mon-Sat 11:30-23:00 and a split Sunday (11:30-15:00, 17:00-21:00); used the detailed sources. Closures are irregular and announced ad hoc, so confirm before visiting. No official website found. Founding year of 1947 is widely repeated but is from secondary sources, not a primary shop page.

cuisine: 京都豚骨醤油 中華そば（第一旭・旭系の懐かしい醤油ラーメン）
gf_detail: This is a classic Kyoto-style soy-sauce ramen shop. The bowls are built on wheat ramen noodles, a wheat-based shoyu tare, and a pork-driven broth — none of which is gluten-free, and there is no indication of any GF accommodation. Not suitable for celiacs. The only other listed item is gyoza (wheat wrappers). Assume cross-contamination throughout a single shared kitchen.
vegan_detail: The menu is limited to shoyu and miso ramen built on a pork-based (旭系) broth with chashu pork, plus gyoza. There are no vegan or vegetarian options and no plant-based broth. Not a vegan-friendly destination.
address_ja: 京都府京都市下京区七条大宮西入ル花畑町582
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 京都府京都市下京区七条御所ノ内西町 (lng 135.728683, lat 34.986683), within the Kyoto box and consistent with the Shichijo-Omiya / Umekoji area. House-number-level match not available, so this is street/chō-level.
neighborhood: 下京区 七条大宮・梅小路（梅小路京都西駅近く）
seats_text: 18 seats: 12 counter seats, one table for 2, one table for 4
hours_raw: 11:00–19:00. Closed Mondays and Thursdays. (One listing notes possible irregular Sunday closures; confirm before a Sunday visit.)
closed_days: Mondays and Thursdays (some Sundays may also be closed — verify before a Sunday trip)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, old-school neighborhood counter shop frequented by taxi drivers, locals, and older regulars; a little basic Japanese and easygoing ramen-shop etiquette will go a long way, and English support should not be expected.

bio.background: A small, time-worn ramen counter tucked into the Shichijo-Omiya block near Umekoji Park and Umekoji-Kyoto-Nishi Station. It belongs to Kyoto's beloved 'Akira-kei' (旭系) lineage — the nostalgic Kyoto soy-sauce ramen tradition associated with the famous Daiichi-Asahi. The owner is said to have trained at a shop called Taiki before opening his own counter, and the place has the lived-in, slightly aged storefront of a long-standing local fixture.
bio.origin: 京都市下京区 七条大宮・梅小路
bio.specialty: Kyoto-style shoyu ramen — a clear, pork-forward broth where the pig's umami and richness are balanced against a restrained soy edge, served over soft medium-thick straight noodles that soak up the broth, finished with loin chashu, a generous pile of scallions and bean sprouts. A miso version and a notably good spicy miso are also offered.
bio.philosophy: A taciturn owner with a quiet, single-minded devotion to ramen — no frills, no trend-chasing, just an honest bowl in the old Kyoto idiom. Regulars describe the flavor as deeply nostalgic, evoking long-gone neighborhood shops.

anecdotes.text: Regulars often pitch it as the under-the-radar alternative to the famous station-side Daiichi-Asahi: its broth is described as sitting between the intensity of Daiichi-Asahi and Ganso Ramen Taiee, refreshing yet rich.
The clientele skews to taxi drivers, salarymen, and older locals rather than a young crowd — a true neighborhood ramen stop.
One Kyoto food guide notes the bowl tastes remarkably close to a now-defunct beloved shop near the Higashiyama-Tono intersection, and singles out the abundant loin chashu and excellent spicy miso.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Single small counter kitchen built entirely around wheat ramen noodles, wheat-based shoyu tare, and gyoza; no separation possible and no GF handling mentioned.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The defining flavor is a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare, which in a traditional Kyoto ramen shop is wheat-based; assume the broth/tare contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is pork-based with chashu pork; no plant-based broth or vegan options, so vegan cross-contact is unavoidable.

caveats: Operating and verified across Tabelog, Ramen Database, and multiple Kyoto food blogs. No official website found. Hours/closed days have a minor discrepancy: Tabelog lists closed Mon & Thu (11:00–19:00 otherwise), while one search snippet cited Monday plus 2nd/4th Sundays — Tabelog used as primary; verify Sunday before visiting. Geocode is street/chō-level (GSI returned the surrounding chō, not the exact house number), but well within the correct Shichijo-Omiya/Umekoji area.

cuisine: 町中華・大衆食堂（カレー中華そば／中華そば）
neighborhood: 中京区 四条大宮（阪急大宮駅すぐ）
gf_detail: This is a classic Showa-era Chinese-noodle diner. The signature dishes are wheat-noodle bowls — curry chuka soba and chuka soba — built on a chicken-and-secret-ingredient broth with curry roux (curry roux is itself wheat-thickened) plus, typically, wheat-based seasonings. There is no indication of any gluten-free accommodation, dedicated equipment, or allergen handling. Not suitable for celiac diners; assume wheat throughout.
vegan_detail: A meat-and-broth-centered old-style shokudo: the broth is chicken-bone based, the curry chuka is finished with braised wagyu beef, and other mains include katsudon. No vegan or vegetarian ramen is offered, and there is no sign of plant-based broth or accommodation. The dessert menu (zenzai, parfaits) is a legacy of the shop's origins as a sweet-bean (zenzai) shop but is not a vegan offering.
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区壬生坊城町1-4
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 京都府京都市中京区壬生坊城町1-4 returned [135.747574, 35.004299]; within the Kyoto box, consistent with the four-jo-omiya / Hankyu Omiya Station location.
seats_text: 約60席（テーブル中心の大衆食堂レイアウト）
hours_raw: 11:00-20:00。定休日は木曜、および第3・第4水曜。
closed_days: 木曜定休（加えて第3・第4水曜も休み）
cultural_comfort_note: A genuine local Showa-era diner where regulars, workers and students drop in — friendly and casual, but expect a Japanese-only ordering experience and no special accommodation, so a little Japanese (or pointing at the menu) goes a long way.
caveats: Wheat is everywhere here (noodles + curry roux) and the broth is animal-based, so this is not a fit for celiac, gluten-free, or vegan diners; it is best framed as a culturally rich omnivore destination. Closed days are irregular (Thursdays plus the 3rd and 4th Wednesdays) — confirm before a special trip.

bio.background: Founded in 1948 (Showa 23) at Shijo-Omiya, Kyoichi Honten has anchored the same corner for over 75 years. The shop began life as a zenzai (sweet red-bean) shop before becoming the bustling everyday diner it is today; the current building was rebuilt in 1976 but keeps its retro, time-capsule feel. It is run by the Miyazaki family, with a hands-on female proprietor who happily steers customers toward her favorite pairings.
bio.specialty: Curry chuka soba (カレー中華) — Chinese wheat noodles buried under a glossy, spicy-sweet curry an (sauce) built from a long-simmered broth, multiple curry rouxs and secret seasonings, finished with braised wagyu beef for depth. The sauce is famously so generous it hides the noodles, and regulars drink it to the last drop. The clearer chicken-bone chuka soba is the other house favorite.
bio.philosophy: The shop's long-standing motto is 'umakute, hayakute, yasukute' — delicious, fast, and cheap — the creed of a true neighborhood diner serving locals, laborers and students alike.

anecdotes.text: The shop's origins as a zenzai (sweet-bean) house live on in its unusually rich dessert lineup — zenzai and several parfaits — and the proprietor likes to suggest a cream dessert as the perfect cool-down after the spicy curry chuka.

The first-generation owner has remarked that along this stretch almost nothing has changed over the decades except themselves, the 'Hattori' coffee shop and the 'Yaoiso' fruit stand — a snapshot of how rooted the place is in the Shijo-Omiya streetscape.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core dishes are wheat ramen noodles and a wheat-thickened curry roux sauce; gluten is unavoidable across the menu and no gluten-free handling is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is chicken-bone based and the signature curry chuka is finished with braised wagyu beef; the kitchen is built around animal stocks and meat, with no plant-based option noted.

cuisine: 京町中華（鳳舞系）— Kyoto-style Cantonese / Chinese noodle house; からしそば・中華そば・焼売
neighborhood: 北区 紫野／堀川北大路
address_ja: 京都府京都市北区紫野下鳥田町37-1
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI from full address 京都府京都市北区紫野下鳥田町37-1; within Kyoto box.
seats_text: 31 seats total, including counter seating and one private room (capacity 4-5).
