hours_raw: 12:00-13:30, 17:00-20:00 (L.O. 19:50). Closed Tuesday and Wednesday; additional closures on the first and third Sunday-through-Wednesday of each month.
closed_days: Tuesday and Wednesday; plus the first and third Sunday-Wednesday stretches of each month.
cultural_comfort_note: A no-reservations neighborhood counter where you order by writing dish numbers on a slip of paper; little to no English, so a few Japanese phrases or a translation app will smooth the visit.
gf_detail: The headline dishes here are wheat-noodle plates — からしそば (mustard noodles) and 中華そば — served with house-made wheat noodles, and the kitchen leans on soy-based seasonings. Spring rolls, shumai, gyoza and fried noodles are all wheat-based as well. There is no indication of any gluten-free preparation, dedicated equipment, or allergen accommodation, so this is not a safe choice for celiacs.
vegan_detail: This is a meat-and-seafood Chinese kitchen: the からしそば is built on chicken and shrimp, signature plates include からし鶏 (fried chicken), juicy pork shumai, and 酢豚 (sweet-and-sour pork), and broths/stir-fries rely on animal stock. No vegan menu or dedicated plant-based options are advertised; a vegan diner would have very little to eat.
caveats: Hours are short and irregular — a 90-minute lunch window (12:00-13:30) and a 17:00-20:00 dinner, with closures on Tuesday/Wednesday plus extra first- and third-week Sunday-Wednesday closures, so confirm the calendar before going. No reservations; arrive early as regulars queue for the first seating. Cash only. からしそば is sometimes treated as an off-menu request, so ask if you don't see it listed.

bio.background: 鳳飛 is part of Kyoto's celebrated 鳳舞系 — the lineage of Chinese cooks who trained under master 高華吉 at the legendary 鳳舞, which opened in 1967 and closed, much mourned, in 2009. 高 is credited with inventing 京町中華: he reworked Chinese cooking for Kyoto palates and the kimono-clad clientele of the geisha districts, who found classic Chinese food too oily and too garlicky, into something light, restrained and broth-forward. Disciples who struck out on their own each carry forward a character from the master's name; 鳳飛 inherited the 鳳 (phoenix) and is regarded as a direct descendant of that tradition.
bio.origin: Kyoto (北区 紫野, on Horikawa near Kitaoji)
bio.specialty: からしそば — a noodle dish found only at 鳳舞系 shops: house-made noodles bound in a lightly sweet, glossy sauce sharpened with mustard, topped with chicken, shrimp, pickled cucumber, green pepper and scallion. The version here is known to run notably spicier than at sibling shops. The kitchen is also loved for its bamboo-packed spring rolls, juicy pork shumai, からし鶏 (mustard-spiced fried chicken), and a light, never-greasy fried rice.
bio.philosophy: Gentle, unshowy, deeply Kyoto: minimal oil, no aggressive aromatics, prices kept low enough for neighbors to eat here regularly — Chinese food rebuilt as everyday comfort food rather than a feast.
roles: keeper of the 鳳舞系 からしそば tradition

anecdotes.text: You order by writing the dish numbers on a paper slip; regulars line up before opening to claim the first seating in the short 90-minute lunch window.
Diners describe the cooking as 'optimistic' and tender — light, lightly seasoned plates where the flavor deepens bite after bite, in keeping with the restrained Kyoto-Chinese style.
からしそば is a Kyoto-only noodle dish that exists essentially only within the 鳳舞 lineage of shops, of which 鳳飛 is a direct heir.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Kyoto-Chinese noodle and stir-fry dishes here are seasoned with soy-based sauces, which in Japan are standard wheat-containing shoyu; assume gluten is present throughout.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Signature plates are chicken-, pork- and shrimp-based (からし鶏, pork shumai, 酢豚, and the chicken-and-shrimp からしそば), so animal ingredients run through the menu.

cuisine: サバ出汁・魚介系ラーメン／つけ麺 (mackerel/kombu fish-broth ramen & tsukemen)
neighborhood: 上京区・北野白梅町（北野天満宮/上七軒方面）
address_ja: 京都府京都市上京区笹屋町5-323-2
geocode_note: GSI geocode of address_ja returned [135.740051, 35.027969], within the Kyoto box. Chō-level match to 笹屋町五丁目323番地.
seats_text: Counter seating (about 7 seats) plus 3 tables (about 8 seats); all non-smoking, no private rooms.
hours_raw: 11:30-22:00 (L.O. 21:30), Tuesday-Sunday & holidays; closed Mondays (shifted to the following day if Monday is a holiday).
closed_days: Mondays (next day if Monday is a public holiday)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, cash-only neighborhood counter ramen shop run by a veteran Japanese-cuisine chef; no reservations and likely Japanese-only service, so a guide or translation help is useful for ordering and asking about ingredients.
gf_detail: Standard ramen and tsukemen built on wheat noodles, and the mackerel karaage topping (tatsuta-age) is wheat-floured and fried; tare is almost certainly wheat-based shoyu. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is indicated. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: The entire concept is built on a mackerel-and-kombu fish dashi (鯖と昆布の出汁) with a fried-mackerel topping, so the broth and signature dishes are fish-based and not vegan. The shop notably avoids animal/pork products and chemical seasonings (無化調), but it is a fish-broth specialist, not a vegan one.

bio.background: Nabura was opened on 8 August 2022 by a chef who spent about 35 years cooking traditional Kyoto-style Japanese cuisine (kappo/washoku) before turning that craft to ramen, in the Kitano Tenmangu / Kamishichiken area of Kamigyo-ku.
bio.origin: The owner is a keen fishing enthusiast, and the shop name 'Nabura' refers to a school of small fish leaping at the sea surface as they are chased by larger fish - a fisherman's sign of opportunity and good fortune. The name was suggested by a fishing-loving staff member, and the staff wear Nabura T-shirts as a maritime good-luck symbol.
bio.specialty: The signature bowl is a completely additive-free (無化調) ramen whose broth is drawn only from kombu (kelp) and saba (mackerel) - no animal products, no fish powder, no chemical seasoning - finished with a piece of fried mackerel tatsuta-age floating in the soup. Tsukemen is also a mainstay, including a rich pure-seafood paitan-style dipping broth.
bio.philosophy: The shop deliberately keeps salt low and the broth clean and digestible so that anyone, regardless of age or gender, can eat it as an everyday meal - the owner says you can finish all the soup and still feel light afterward (誰でも美味しく食べられる).

anecdotes.text: The owner condensed 35 years of experience as a Kyoto Japanese-cuisine chef into this ramen, building the entire broth from just kombu and mackerel with no chemical seasonings.

The name 'Nabura' was chosen by a fishing-loving staff member after the term for a school of small fish breaking the surface while chased by bigger fish - an auspicious sign at sea - and staff wear Nabura T-shirts to carry that good-luck symbolism.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen/tsukemen noodles are the base of every bowl and the kitchen handles wheat flour for the mackerel tatsuta-age topping, so flour and gluten are present throughout the cooking surfaces.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broths are fish-based (mackerel and kombu dashi); there is no vegan broth, so no vegan bowl is possible even setting cross-contact aside.

positives.text: Broth is completely additive-free (無化調) using only kombu and mackerel, with no chemical seasonings, fish powder, or animal/pork products - and the kitchen keeps salt low for a lighter, cleaner bowl.

caveats: Cash only; no reservations; counter-heavy small shop. Official standalone website not confirmed (listings via Tabelog/Leaf Kyoto/Osumituki). Seat count (~15) and exact split estimated from a single blog source. Chef's name not published in the sources reviewed. A second branch (元田中店 / Mototanaka) opened in 2024; this record is the original 本店.

cuisine: French-inspired creative ramen (フレンチビストロ系創作ラーメン) — potage and tripe-tomato ramen, white/black shoyu, plus bistro small plates and wine
gf_detail: No gluten-free options. The ramen is built on wheat noodles, and the shoyu-based tare is almost certainly wheat-containing soy sauce; broths and creative dishes are not allergen-controlled. No celiac-safe accommodation is indicated on any source. Not suitable for strict gluten avoidance.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly destination. Core bowls use chicken broth, dashi (bonito/fish-based 節), shoyu tare, and dairy-rich potage soups (cream-based), plus meat dishes (pork, chicken) and seafood-leaning items. No dedicated plant-based ramen is offered. A vegan could not reliably eat the signature dishes.
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区錦小路通新町西入西錦小路町269 松広ビル1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of the street-level address returned [135.74881, 35.004276], within the Kyoto box. Address is in 中京区 (Nakagyō-ku) near Shijō-Karasuma / Hankyu Karasuma Station, roughly a 5-minute walk; note this differs from the "下京区" label in the source brief but matches Tabelog and the official site.
neighborhood: 四条烏丸 (Shijō-Karasuma), 中京区
seats_text: 22 seats — table seating only (four 4-tops and three 2-tops); spacious for a ramen shop, in a former French-restaurant space
hours_raw: Tue–Sat 11:30–15:00 (L.O. 14:30) and 18:00–22:00 (L.O. 21:30); Sun & holidays 11:30–15:00 (L.O. 14:30) and 18:00–21:00 (L.O. 20:30); closed Mondays. (Some sources list lunch starting 12:00.)
closed_days: Mondays
cultural_comfort_note: An unusually relaxed, refined ramen room with table seating and an open, bistro-like feel, so a foreign visitor can settle in comfortably; a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu go a long way.
caveats: Cards are not accepted, but electronic money and QR-code payments are. Reservations are not taken. The Japanese-dashi-and-French-herb broths, cream potages, and shoyu tare make this unsuitable for gluten-free or vegan diners.

bio.background: Men Bistro Nakano is run by a chef who came up through French kitchens and now pours that training into a bowl of noodles. The shop took over the space of a former French restaurant, and it kept that room's calm, grown-up feel rather than turning it into a typical narrow ramen counter.
bio.origin: The space was previously a French restaurant, and it was opened by someone who had run a French establishment — so the bistro atmosphere is not a gimmick but the literal history of the room.
bio.specialty: The kitchen builds broths that nod to Japanese cooking — using katsuobushi-style dashi (節) — and finishes them with French herbs and technique. The white and black shoyu bowls anchor the regular menu alongside a tripe-and-tomato ramen and a spicy mazesoba, while seasonal creamed 'potage' noodles (corn, new-onion, edamame, spinach, vichyssoise) rotate through and have become the shop's signature draw. The flavored soft-boiled egg is singled out by regulars as one of the best they've had.
bio.philosophy: The guiding idea is a genuine fusion of French, Japanese, and ramen — treating a bowl of ramen with the same care, plating, and finish as a bistro plate. You can pair it with a glass of wine, start with small plates like ratatouille or roasted chicken, and end with a little dessert, which is almost unheard of at a ramen shop.

anecdotes.text: Water is served in wine bottles, and the room is so elegant that one writer joked it could go back to being a French or Western restaurant tomorrow — it is notably welcoming even for a solo diner, unusual for a ramen shop.
It is described as a rare 'ramen shop where you can drink wine,' with glasses from around 500 yen and sparkling by the glass, in a space far roomier than a classic ramen counter.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature bowls are white and black shoyu (soy-sauce) ramen, and shoyu tare in Japan is virtually always made with wheat-containing soy sauce; treat the tare as a wheat source.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths lean on chicken stock and Japanese fish dashi (節), and several signature noodles are cream/dairy-based potages, so vegan cross-contact is inherent to the kitchen, not just incidental.

positives.text: Table-only seating in a calm, bistro-style room with small plates and wine makes for an easy, unhurried meal, though the menu itself is not built around dietary restrictions.

cuisine: ゆずラーメン・讃岐うどん（柚子香る出汁系 / yuzu chuka-soba and hand-pulled Sanuki udon）
neighborhood: 中京区・四条大宮
gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The core menu is wheat: the signature yuzu ramen uses wheat noodles (a low-hydration, low-kansui country-style noodle developed with a noodle maker), and the shop is also a hand-pulled Sanuki udon house, so wheat flour is heavily used throughout the kitchen. The tare/broth is built on soy sauce infused with the udon-shop dashi, and ordinary Japanese soy sauce contains wheat. No gluten-free menu, no dedicated equipment, and high airborne-flour and shared-water cross-contamination risk from on-site noodle making. Anyone with celiac disease should avoid; a wheat-allergic diner should not eat here.
vegan_detail: Not vegan/vegetarian-friendly. The signature broth is a 7-hour chicken-bone stock combined with katsuo (bonito) first dashi and Hokkaido kombu, and the yuzu ramen is finished with pork back fat (背脂). Soy-sauce tare carries fish/animal dashi as well. Evening menu centers on wagyu shabu-shabu and udon-sukiyaki hot pots. No advertised plant-based bowl. A vegan would need to ask, and realistically there is no safe option.
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区壬生坊城町5番地2
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 京都市中京区壬生坊城町5番地; within the Kyoto bounding box. Building-number-2 precision not resolved but block-level point is accurate.
seats_text: 16 seats (counter/table); private events for up to about 20 people can be arranged.
closed_days: Sundays (with occasional irregular/special opening); confirm current schedule before visiting.
cultural_comfort_note: A small, long-running neighborhood noodle shop where a few words of Japanese and a friendly konnichiwa go a long way; English support is not guaranteed.
caveats: Reopened in November 2024 at the new Shijō-Ōmiya location after the previous shop closed in June 2024; the business itself has roughly four decades of history. Sunday is the regular closed day but special openings happen irregularly, so verify hours before a special trip. The menu is wheat- and animal-broth based; not suitable for celiac, wheat-allergic, or vegan/vegetarian diners.

bio.background: Eitaro began roughly four decades ago as a Kyoto noodle shop and built its name first as a hand-pulled Sanuki udon specialist before its owner — a devoted ramen lover — folded a second craft into the kitchen. After years at Shijo-Karasuma where it drew a loyal following and media attention, the shop closed in June 2024, and reopened in November 2024 just steps from Shijo-Omiya Station, bringing back the bowl people kept asking for.
bio.specialty: Yuzu ramen — the shop's signature bowl. The broth is a 7-hour chicken-bone stock layered with katsuo (bonito) first dashi and Hokkaido kombu, seasoned with a soy tare carried over from the udon side of the house, enriched with pork back fat, and finished with a generous squeeze of fresh yuzu so the citrus cuts through the richness. Alongside it the shop still serves hand-pulled Sanuki udon and, in the evening, hot-pot courses including wagyu shabu-shabu and udon-sukiyaki.
bio.philosophy: Cook so every guest leaves thinking mata tabetai — I want to eat that again. The owner spent years tasting ramen across Kyoto and reportedly three years developing the low-hydration, low-kansui noodle with a noodle maker before he was satisfied enough to put the bowl on the menu.

anecdotes.text: The shop closed in June 2024 and only came back because customers kept saying mata ano aji o tabetai — they wanted that flavor again — which pushed the owner to reopen near Shijo-Omiya in November 2024.

The signature yuzu ramen was born from the owner's own obsession: as a ramen lover running an udon shop, he spent years researching ramen around Kyoto before merging his udon dashi expertise with a ramen of his own.
Over its decades in business the shop became known as a spot frequented by entertainers and celebrities.

gf_cross_contamination.text: On-site hand-pulling of Sanuki udon means heavy wheat flour use in the kitchen, with airborne flour and shared boiling water creating cross-contamination risk for any wheat-sensitive diner.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The ramen tare is built on a soy sauce infused with the shop's udon dashi; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, so the seasoning base is not gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is chicken-bone stock plus bonito (katsuo) dashi and kombu, and the yuzu ramen is finished with pork back fat; evening menu centers on wagyu shabu-shabu and sukiyaki, so animal products are pervasive.

cuisine: Kyoto kotteri ramen (ultra-rich tonkotsu / pork-bone)
neighborhood: Ichijoji, Sakyo-ku (Ichijoji Ramen Street)
address_ja: 京都府京都市左京区一乗寺北大丸町5-2
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned [135.786697, 35.045891] for 京都府京都市左京区一乗寺北大丸町5; within Kyoto box.
seats_text: 9 counter seats (L-shaped counter only)
hours_raw: Basically closed; opens on irregular, unannounced days. When open, dinner service is often around 18:00-20:00 with last order around 23:00. Opening days/times are posted on Twitter/X roughly 30 minutes beforehand (@eigyoakihide for lunch, @eigyoakihide_2 for dinner).
closed_days: No fixed schedule — the shop is closed by default and opens only on sporadic, self-announced days.
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny 9-seat Japanese-only counter shop with no fixed hours; you'll need to track its Twitter/X in Japanese to know when it's open, so it suits diners comfortable navigating a Japanese-language local ramen experience.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. The bowls are built on wheat ramen noodles and an ultra-thick pork-bone (tonkotsu/kotteri) broth with a tare that almost certainly contains wheat-based soy sauce. No gluten-free menu, options, or accommodation are advertised, and the single small kitchen makes cross-contact unavoidable. Celiac diners should avoid.
vegan_detail: Not vegan or vegetarian. The shop is a specialist in extremely rich pork-bone (tonkotsu) broth and tops its ramen with chunks of charred pork chashu; the entire concept is animal-based. No plant-based ramen is offered.
caveats: Official site (akihide.nh-m.com) was unreachable at time of research, so menu prices and current operating details are taken from secondary sources. The shop opens on irregular, self-announced days only; confirm via its Twitter/X before visiting. Hours when open are approximate.

bio.background: Akihide is a cult ramen shop tucked along Ichijoji's famous Ramen Street in Sakyo-ku, northeast Kyoto. It is one of the area's most talked-about bowls precisely because it is so hard to catch: the shop is closed by default and opens only on sporadic days that the owner announces on Twitter/X, often just before the doors open. With a single L-shaped counter of nine seats, no phone, no reservations, no parking, and cash-only service, it has the feel of a hidden, owner-run obsession rather than a conventional restaurant.
bio.specialty: An ultra-thick kotteri tonkotsu (pork-bone) ramen so concentrated that a ladle is said to stand upright in the soup. The broth has the consistency of a sauce rather than a soup, paired with notably thick, chewy noodles and chunks of soft, fragrantly charred chashu pork that crumble when eaten. The intensity and texture sit somewhere between ramen and tsukemen.
bio.philosophy: The shop bills itself as a specialty house for the 'rich-broth tribe' (濃厚民族専門店) — an unapologetic pursuit of maximum richness and intensity over conventional ramen balance, made for diners who want the heaviest, most concentrated bowl possible.

anecdotes.text: The shop keeps no regular hours at all — it is 'basically closed,' and the only way to know it is open is to follow its Twitter/X accounts, which post the operating day (lunch via @eigyoakihide, dinner via @eigyoakihide_2), sometimes roughly half an hour before opening. On the days it does open it draws lines.
The broth is described as so dense that it 'stands a ladle upright,' resembling a thick sauce more than a soup — the shop pitches itself directly to the 'rich-broth tribe.'
A visitor tracked the shop's Twitter to catch an open day during a rainy spell and ate the kotteri bowl topped with two chunks of tender roasted pork and a large sheet of nori.

lead: ラーメン あかつき
name_ja: ラーメン あかつき
cuisine: 背脂醤油ラーメン（京都背脂ちゃっちゃ系・手切りチャーシュー／back-fat shoyu ramen）
neighborhood: 左京区・北白川
gf_detail: This is a traditional back-fat shoyu ramen shop. The bowls are built on wheat ramen noodles and a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare, which is essentially always brewed with wheat, over a pork-bone and chicken broth. There is no published gluten-free menu, no dedicated preparation, and shared boiling water for the noodles. Not suitable for celiacs; a wheat-sensitive diner should treat this place as off-limits.
vegan_detail: The shop's identity is meat- and fish-based: a pork-bone and chicken broth finished with a Japanese-dashi soy tare, topped with thick hand-cut/braised char siu pork, plus sides like gyoza and karaage. There is no published vegan or vegetarian ramen, and even a plain bowl rests on an animal broth. Not a vegan-friendly option.
address_ja: 京都府京都市左京区北白川別当町13
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned the exact banchi (北白川別当町13番地); coordinates fall within the Kyoto box.
seats_text: About 10 counter seats — a small, intimate shop.
closed_days: 無休（年中無休）
cultural_comfort_note: A no-frills neighborhood counter ramen-ya open into the small hours; little to no English, so basic Japanese or pointing at the menu will smooth the visit.

bio.background: Ramen Akatsuki is a long-running neighborhood ramen-ya in Kyoto's Kita-Shirakawa quarter of Sakyo Ward, a stone's throw from the Kitashirakawa-kōmae city-bus stop and within walking distance of Mototanaka Station. Reports describe it as approaching its 30th year in business — a genuine local fixture rather than a trend shop. It runs from late morning straight through to around 3:00 a.m. every day, making it a dependable late-night bowl for students and locals alike.
bio.specialty: A Kyoto-style back-fat shoyu ramen: long-simmered pork-bone (gankotsu) and chicken broth rested a day and finished with a Japanese-dashi soy tare, crowned with a scattering of sweet back-fat. Despite the rich-looking, glistening surface, the soup drinks surprisingly clean and light, with no heavy off-notes. The thick, tender braised char siu is treated as a star in its own right, and regulars are pointed toward extra char siu or the loaded 'Akatsuki Special.'
bio.philosophy: A straightforward, satisfying bowl that lets a clean pork-and-chicken broth and generous back-fat do the talking — comfort-food Kyoto ramen built for everyday, all-hours eating.

anecdotes.text: Despite the rich, oily-looking back-fat surface, the broth is described as drinking considerably lighter than it looks, with no distinctive smell or off-flavor.
Beyond the standard bowl, the menu stretches to a loaded 'Akatsuki Special,' a spicy version, and a fish-based ramen, plus sides like gyoza and karaage and set meals.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles are boiled in shared water, and the broth is finished with a soy-sauce tare that is essentially always wheat-based — pervasive gluten with no separation.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The bowl is a shoyu (soy sauce) ramen finished with a Japanese-dashi soy tare; standard Japanese shoyu is brewed with wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The base soup is a pork-bone and chicken broth with back-fat and braised pork char siu; there is no animal-free preparation, so all bowls carry animal product.

caveats: Owner/chef name and exact founding year not confirmed by a fetched page (described only as "approaching 30 years"). No allergen, gluten-free, or vegan information is published anywhere found, so dietary flags are inferred from the dish type (wheat noodles + wheat shoyu tare + pork/chicken broth) rather than a shop statement. Official site is the shop's Instagram; no standalone website confirmed.

lead: 特製ラーメン 大中 本店
name_ja: 特製ラーメン 大中 本店
cuisine: 豚骨醤油ラーメン (rich tonkotsu-shoyu ramen)
neighborhood: 伏見区・桃山（近鉄 桃山御陵前駅すぐ、伏見桃山の高架下）
gf_detail: Not safe for celiac or strict gluten-free diners. The core dish is wheat ramen noodles (straight-cut noodles from a noodle maker) served in a pork-and-chicken-bone broth seasoned with a shoyu (soy sauce) tare, which is virtually always wheat-based. Recommended toppings include garlic chips and gyoza (wheat dumplings) are on the menu. There is no indication of gluten-free noodles, tare, or any allergen accommodation. Treat as off-limits if you must avoid gluten.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The entire identity of the shop is its rich animal broth — large quantities of pork bones (tonkotsu) and chicken bones simmered continuously for 24 hours. Bowls come with chashu (pork) and a soft-boiled egg is a topping; the broth itself is meat-based with no plant-based option mentioned. There is no vegetable or vegan ramen on offer.
address_ja: 京都府京都市伏見区観音寺町217（高架下）
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned [135.765701, 34.933331] for 京都府京都市伏見区観音寺町高架下217; within Kyoto box. Matches the under-the-railway location at Kintetsu Momoyamagoryomae Station. Note: official branch page lists street number as 215 vs Tabelog's 217 — same building beneath the viaduct.
seats_text: 23 seats — 7 counter seats plus four 4-person tables, in a narrow corridor-style space under the railway.
closed_days: 無休（年中無休 / open year-round, no regular closing day）
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, no-reservations local ramen joint where a few polite Japanese words and pointing at the customizable order sheet will carry you through; staff are used to a steady all-hours crowd rather than overseas guests.

bio.background: A Fushimi institution tucked beneath the elevated railway tracks right outside Kintetsu Momoyamagoryomae Station, Daichu has been serving its rich tonkotsu since opening in 1995. The narrow, corridor-shaped space — counter up front, tables behind — keeps a nostalgic Showa-era atmosphere, marked from the street by a yellow signboard and a big red lantern. In a district full of izakaya, it runs almost around the clock, drawing a steady stream of locals from lunch through late night.
bio.specialty: The house bowl is the 大中ラーメン, a 濃厚豚骨醤油 — an intensely rich pork-bone-and-chicken broth seasoned with soy tare, thick enough that it is said a chopstick can stand upright in it, yet finishing with umami rather than heaviness. There is also a 特濃 (extra-rich) version for those who want it even more concentrated. The shop is generous with free customization: noodle firmness, broth richness, extra bean sprouts and green onion, soft-boiled egg, and kimchi, with burnt-garlic oil and garlic chips offered to round out the flavor. Chashu comes in different cuts (shoulder, belly, loin), and a lunch-set adds a side bowl of fried rice, karaage, gyoza, or chashu-don.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen prides itself on a flavor 'you can't get at a chain' — breaking the bones in-house and simmering large batches of pork and chicken bones for a full 24 hours rather than reconstituting a pre-made base. The shop even posts daily plaques naming that day's noodle-maker and broth-maker, a small signal of how seriously it takes the craft behind each bowl.

anecdotes.text: The broth is famously viscous — described as thick enough to stand a chopstick in — and ranks among Kyoto's most intensely rich kotteri ramen.

Set beneath the railway viaduct with a retro Showa feel, the shop is especially evocative in the evening; the noodles are straight-cut to grab the concentrated broth, and garlic chips are recommended 'to complete the flavor.'

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core dish is wheat ramen noodles in a pork/chicken broth with a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare; gyoza (wheat) and garlic chips are on the menu. No gluten-free option or allergen handling indicated.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature broth is 濃厚豚骨醤油 (rich pork-bone soy sauce), meaning a wheat-based soy tare is integral to the bowl.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is made by simmering pork bones and chicken bones for 24 hours; there is no plant-based broth or vegan bowl available.

caveats: Street number differs between sources (215 on official branch page vs 217 on Tabelog) — same building under the viaduct. Founding year 1995 and 24-hour broth process per official/aggregator sources; no allergen or GF/vegan accommodation found, consistent with a classic tonkotsu-shoyu shop.

lead: 元祖熟成細麺 香来 壬生本店
name_ja: 元祖熟成細麺 香来 壬生本店
name_en: Ganso Jukusei Hosomen Kourai Mibu Honten
cuisine: 京都中華そば（熟成細麺・背脂醤油／Kyoto-style chuka soba）
neighborhood: 中京区・壬生（四条大宮／二条城方面）
gf_detail: This is a classic Kyoto back-fat shoyu ramen shop. The signature bowls are built on wheat noodles (their custom aged thin noodles), a soy-sauce-based tare (typically brewed soy sauce contains wheat), and a chicken-and-pork bone broth simmered for hours. There is no indication of any gluten-free noodle, tamari tare, or allergen handling. A celiac diner cannot safely eat the ramen here. No dedicated gluten-free preparation exists.
vegan_detail: All core bowls are animal-based: the broth is chicken-bone and pork-bone simmered for about 12 hours with back fat, and bowls are topped with chashu pork. There is no vegan or vegetarian ramen option indicated. Even the lighter white-shoyu bowl uses Kyoto vegetables but is built on the same animal broth and finished with bonito flakes. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区壬生馬場町35番地5
geocode_note: GSI geocoder resolved chō-level address 京都府京都市中京区壬生馬場町35 to [135.745132, 35.00639], within the Kyoto bounding box. Block-level precision (壬生馬場町35番地5).
seats_text: 25 seats total (15 counter, 10 table)
hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Thu, Fri, Sat, Sun 11:00-03:00 (next day); Wed closed. Note: from June 2026, open on public holidays and closed the following day instead.
closed_days: Wednesdays (with holiday exceptions — from June 2026 the shop opens on public holidays and closes the following day instead)
cultural_comfort_note: A bustling, casual neighborhood ramen counter open very late; comfortable for solo diners, but expect a Japanese-only menu and minimal English, so come ready to point and order simply.

bio.background: Kouri is a well-known purveyor of nostalgic Kyoto-style chuka soba in the lively Shijo-Omiya / Mibu quarter, a dense pocket of affordable, beloved eateries near Nijo Castle and a roughly five-minute walk from Hankyu Omiya station. The Mibu shop is the flagship (honten) of the brand.
bio.specialty: Their signature is the classic Kyoto back-fat shoyu ramen: custom-ordered aged thin noodles with a springy, clean-cutting bite, in a soy-sauce broth made from carefully prepped chicken and pork bones and aromatic vegetables simmered about 12 hours, finished with back fat. It is typically crowned with tender, melt-in-the-mouth chashu.
bio.philosophy: A devotion to the aged thin-noodle style the shop is named for (熟成細麺, 'aged thin noodle'), pairing custom noodles with a long-simmered broth that balances vegetable sweetness, soy richness and back-fat depth.

anecdotes.text: Beyond the classic bowl, the shop has introduced contrasting shoyu variations: a dark 'black' soy ramen with concentrated, robust broth, thicker chashu and menma, and a refined 'white' soy ramen with a clear golden broth, Kyoto vegetables, bonito flakes and fried onions.
The kitchen leans on custom-ordered aged thin noodles prized for a springy texture and clean bite that carries the broth well.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The bowls are built on a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare; brewed Japanese soy sauce typically contains wheat, and no wheat-free/tamari alternative is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The shared broth is chicken-bone and pork-bone based and bowls are topped with chashu pork; there is no separate vegan preparation.

caveats: Tabelog lists this as currently operating with no 掲載保留/閉店 flag. Geocode resolved to block precision within the Kyoto box. No official website found; brand/social presence exists (Facebook). Hours note: from June 2026 the shop opens on holidays and closes the following day, making the schedule irregular — verify locally. No GF or vegan options confirmed.

cuisine: 京都背脂系・町中華 — 鶏ガラ背脂醤油の中華そば (Kyoto-style back-fat chicken-gara shoyu chuka-soba, Masutani lineage)
gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The bowl is built on wheat ramen noodles in a chicken-bone broth finished with back fat and an aged soy-sauce (shoyu) tare; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat. There is no gluten-free menu, no dedicated equipment, and no indication staff accommodate gluten avoidance. Anyone with celiac disease should not eat the ramen here.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly option. The signature and essentially only style is a chicken-bone broth enriched with pork back fat and topped with braised pork chashu, so the broth, fat and toppings are all animal-derived and there is no plant-based ramen on the menu.

Coordinates are from a chō-level fallback for 上京区下之町 ([135.73613, 35.019508]), the named district nearest the shop's Nishi-Horikawa frontage; within the Kyoto bounding box but accurate only to the neighborhood block, not the exact storefront. The shop sits along the Horikawa Shopping Street on the west side of Horikawa-dori, just north of Shimodachiuri-dori.

seats_text: 25 seats (9 at the counter, 16 at tables)
closed_days: Mondays
cultural_comfort_note: A no-frills local Kyoto ramen counter where ordering is straightforward but everything is in Japanese and the welcome is brisk and old-school; a little Japanese or pointing at the menu goes a long way.

bio.background: Open since 1993, Shomen is a small, well-worn ramen counter tucked into the Horikawa Shopping Street on the west side of Horikawa-dori, a short walk toward Marutamachi and Imadegawa. It belongs to the Kyoto back-fat shoyu tradition that traces back to the famous Masutani style — a lineage some regulars argue Shomen executes even better than the original.
bio.origin: Kyoto, Japan — a neighborhood町中華 (town-Chinese / local ramen) shop serving the surrounding residents and after-work salarymen.
bio.specialty: Chuka soba: thin, straight noodles in a chicken-bone (鶏ガラ) broth finished with a glistening layer of pork back fat (背脂) and an aged soy-sauce tare, topped with chashu braised until meltingly tender (とろとろ) and firm menma. A bowl runs about ¥650; the ramen-and-fried-rice set about ¥850.
bio.philosophy: An almost obsessive devotion to the back fat — the fat is sourced from Kyushu — so that the rich, collagen-laced sheen on the surface is the defining character of the bowl rather than an afterthought.

anecdotes.text: A long-time reviewer rates Shomen as the high point of the Kyoto Masutani-style lineage, saying its devotion to the back fat surpasses even the original Masutani shop.
First-time visitors are served the noodles on the softer side by default; regulars who like them firmer just ask for harder noodles when ordering.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Single small ramen kitchen built around wheat noodles boiled in shared water; no gluten-free protocol or separate prep is indicated, so cross-contact is effectively unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is finished with an aged soy-sauce (shoyu) tare; standard Japanese soy sauce is brewed with wheat, making the seasoning a wheat source.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is chicken-bone based, enriched with pork back fat and served with pork chashu; no plant-based broth or toppings exist, so there is no vegan path on the menu.

caveats: Coordinates are chō-level (上京区下之町 fallback), accurate to the neighborhood rather than the exact storefront — GSI could not resolve the street-frontage address. No GF or vegan accommodation; cash only.

cuisine: Ramen
gf_detail: Not safe for celiacs. This is a classic wheat-noodle ramen shop; the noodles are wheat, the shoyu (soy sauce) tare almost certainly contains wheat, and the kitchen boils flour-based noodles throughout. No gluten-free menu, no dedicated equipment, and no published allergen handling.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The entire concept is animal-based: the signature bowl is a super-concentrated tonkotsu made from roughly 1kg of pork bones plus water per bowl, and the other mainstay is a chicken paitan. Even the complimentary warmed vegetables share a meat-broth kitchen.
seats_text: Counter 7 seats plus 4 tables (each seating about 4), roughly 23 seats; feels like a former canteen/izakaya with a fairly spacious kitchen.

closed_days: No fixed regular closing day (irregular holidays); Thursday dinner service is closed.
cultural_comfort_note: A small, locally focused independent ramen shop with no sign of English support, so a little Japanese or a translation app will smooth the visit.
caveats: Cash only and irregular holidays, so confirm the day before visiting; Thursdays are lunch-only. Seat layout comes from a visit report rather than the shop itself.

bio.background: A newcomer to Kyoto's ramen scene, Menya Ichimen Senshin opened in March 2024 in a roomy former eatery space in Kamigyo Ward, a short walk northwest toward Nijo Castle. The name carries the spirit of devoting oneself wholeheartedly to a single bowl of noodles.
bio.specialty: The headline bowl is a super-concentrated tonkotsu ramen made from nothing but pork bones and water, with roughly 1kg of bones per bowl, yielding a thick, viscous, deeply porky broth with restrained sweetness. Alongside it is a chicken paitan (tori-paitan) and a special shoyu ramen priced at just 700 yen.
bio.philosophy: Single-minded focus on the broth: the tonkotsu is built from bones and water alone, with no extra additives, letting pure pork umami carry the bowl. Diners are also offered free extras on request such as garlic, onions, and a side of warmed vegetables.

anecdotes.text: The signature ultra-rich tonkotsu is made with bones and water only, using about 1kg of pork bones per bowl for a thick, high-viscosity soup.
A 700-yen special shoyu ramen is called out as exceptional value.
Warmed vegetables are served alongside the ramen, an unusual touch for a ramen shop.

gf_cross_contamination.text: A wheat-noodle ramen shop with no gluten-free offering; wheat noodles are boiled throughout the kitchen, so cross-contamination is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The menu includes a special shoyu (soy sauce) ramen; standard ramen shoyu tare is wheat-based, and no wheat-free tare is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All bowls are animal-based, a pork-bone tonkotsu and a chicken paitan, and even the complimentary warmed vegetables come out of a meat-broth kitchen.

The single menu item is built on wheat noodles (from Menya Teikoku) in a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare that is normally wheat-based, over a fish/dashi broth (horse mackerel, bonito, mackerel). No gluten-free noodle or tare option and no indication of allergen accommodation, so not safe for celiacs. Anyone strictly avoiding gluten should not eat here.
vegan_detail: The bowl is fundamentally a fish/dashi-based broth (horse mackerel, mackerel and bonito stock) topped with chashu pork and roast beef. Not vegetarian or vegan, and there are no plant-based options on the menu, which serves only the ginger soy-sauce ramen.

caveats: Confirmed as a real, currently operating ramen shop (opened Feb 18, 2025) via Tabelog and multiple Kyoto food blogs. Hours 11:00-21:30; closures irregular rather than a fixed weekly day, so per-day schedule reflects the daily operating window. No owner/chef name reliably confirmed across independent sources, so chef_name left null. No allergen, gluten-free, or vegan/vegetarian accommodations mentioned anywhere; safety details inferred from the standard composition of shoyu ramen.

bio.background: Ramen Yucho opened on 18 February 2025 on the second floor of the Iidaya Building, a short walk from Kyoto-Kawaramachi Station in the busy Shijo-Kawaramachi quarter. Styled as a tucked-away hidden-gem shop with a clean, modern Japanese interior and a U-shaped counter, it set out to do one thing well in a city where ginger-forward ramen is genuinely uncommon.
bio.specialty: The signature and essentially only dish is the Kyoto ginger soy-sauce ramen. A crystal-clear seafood broth is drawn from horse mackerel, mackerel and bonito-style dashi, then driven by ginger both dissolved in the soup and laid on top as fresh needle-cut strips. It is served with thin, supple straight noodles from the respected Kyoto noodle maker Menya Teikoku, and topped with chashu pork, roast beef, naruto, Kujo green onion, menma and spinach. Diners choose Single, Double or Triple by the number of chashu and roast-beef slices, and a side of ginger dashi rice is also offered.
bio.philosophy: A focused, single-bowl shop: pour everything into one warming, refined ginger soy-sauce ramen rather than a long menu, with broth and ginger meant to leave you physically warmed from the inside.

anecdotes.text: The shop leans into the rarity of ginger ramen in Kyoto, and the broth's pronounced ginger is widely described as leaving diners warmed up and toasty from the inside out.
Diners customize the bowl by selecting how many slices of chashu pork and roast beef they want, scaling from Single (900 yen) to Double (1,250 yen) to Triple (1,700 yen).

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles are boiled in shared water and the soy-sauce tare is normally wheat-based, so the dish is not gluten-free and no GF accommodation is indicated.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The bowl is a ginger soy-sauce ramen built on a special ginger shoyu tare, which in Japanese ramen is virtually always brewed from wheat-containing soy sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The broth is a fish/dashi base (horse mackerel, mackerel, bonito) and toppings include pork chashu and roast beef, so cross-contact with animal products is inherent to every bowl.

gf_detail: Not gluten-free. This is a Jiro-style shop built on extra-thick wheat noodles with salt or soy (typically wheat-based) tare, served in or with a beef-fat/dashi base — not safe for celiac diners. No gluten-free option is advertised.
caveats: Allergen and cross-contamination details are not published; assume a shared kitchen handling wheat noodles. Hours and the recent seat expansion are from listing data and may change.

bio.background: Menya Kanae opened in September 2025 in the Shijo-Omiya / Mibu area of Kyoto, tucked along a quiet alley off Koin-dori in a renovated machiya (traditional townhouse) known as the 'Wagyu Kaikan.' You slip off your shoes to enter a snug counter space with a retro, handmade feel, then order from your smartphone by QR code — an appealing contrast of old-house atmosphere and modern convenience.
bio.specialty: The signature bowl is 'KANAE no Jiro' — a new-school take on Jiro-style ramen. In place of the usual pork back-fat it uses the fat of wagyu (Kobe and Omi beef), and instead of pork chashu it tops the bowl with tender, gelatinous beef Achilles tendon, with no cabbage. Extra-thick, wide noodles from veteran maker Tieoh carry the bowl, which can be ordered with soup (ari) or brothless (nashi), and seasoned with salt (shio) or soy (shoyu).
bio.philosophy: The shop's idea is that wagyu beef fat brings a refined umami and a clean sweetness that pork back-fat lacks, giving a salt-based dashi described as having 'no sharp edges' — a more elegant, mellow spin on the famously heavy Jiro genre. Sourcing such beef fat is difficult, which the shop frames as a mark of its commitment to quality.

anecdotes.text: Students get a free upsize of noodles and bean sprouts.
Diners can also order select dishes from the sister izakaya 'Toyo-ta' alongside their ramen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The shop offers a soy-sauce (shoyu) seasoning option; Japanese soy sauce is typically brewed with wheat, so this is not gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The bowls are built on wagyu beef fat, beef Achilles tendon, and a dashi-based broth, so the kitchen is not vegan-safe.

bio.background: Shinshintei is widely credited as the birthplace of Kyoto-style white miso ramen. The lineage began as a humble food stall around 1960 and grew into a brick-and-mortar shop opened in 1972 in the Fushacho area of central Kyoto. Over nearly four decades it built a devoted following before the original first-generation owner retired and closed that location in 2009 due to age. Rather than let the recipe disappear, the founder's younger brother inherited the name and the noren and reopened the shop in the Ichijoji district, where it carries the tradition forward today as a lunch-only neighborhood institution.
bio.specialty: The signature bowl is white miso ramen (白味噌ラーメン) - a gentle, mellow, slightly sweet white-miso broth that is distinct from the heartier Sapporo style. It pairs a concentrated dashi with light white miso, is piled with plenty of vegetables, finished with a touch of shichimi togarashi for a quiet kick, and served with firm Kyoto-style medium-thin noodles. A plate of yakimeshi (fried rice) is the classic companion.
bio.philosophy: Keep the original white-miso recipe alive and unchanged - a mellow, comforting, deeply flavored bowl made the same way it has been for decades, served simply at lunch.
roles: successor (founder's younger brother carrying on the recipe)

anecdotes.text: The original Fushacho shop became famous enough to draw notable guests, said to include singer Miyuki Nakajima and actor Keanu Reeves, before it closed in 2009.
When the first-generation owner retired, the founder's younger brother took over the name and reopened in Ichijoji to keep the white-miso ramen tradition going (一乗寺で暖簾を受け継いで開業したのが今の新進亭).
Regulars praise the long-unchanged, mellow and deeply flavored miso ramen as something you can only really experience in Kyoto.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The shop's core product is white miso ramen with wheat noodles and a miso-based tare; miso and the broth components typically contain wheat, and no gluten-free preparation is offered.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Bowls are built on dashi and meat elements with chashu and other meat toppings; no vegan or vegetarian broth is offered.

cuisine: 京都中華・町中華（名物からしそば、鳳舞系）
neighborhood: 中京区・新京極/六角

cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, old-school Showa-era neighborhood Chinese counter run by a veteran chef; very little English, so a few Japanese phrases and a relaxed, respectful manner go a long way.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan place. The signature karashi soba is built on a chicken-bone and kombu broth with chicken, shrimp, and animal-based ankake; the wider menu is meat- and seafood-centric town-Chinese. No vegan ramen or vegan set is offered.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. The karashi soba uses wheat noodles, a wheat-based mustard/soy ankake sauce, and Japanese soy sauce (contains wheat); gyoza and spring rolls are wheat-wrapped. There are no celiac-safe options and no gluten-free accommodation here.
caveats: Hours vary by source (some list lunch-only 11:30-16:00, others 11:30-19:30 with a Wed closure, and a few add Thursday); confirm before visiting. Cash only, no reservations, and seating is very limited (17 seats) so expect queues at peak times. All seats permit smoking.

bio.background: 龍鳳 is a small, unpretentious Kyoto town-Chinese counter tucked into the Rokkaku lane just off the Shinkyogoku arcade, where it has operated for roughly four decades. Its proprietor, Terada Yasuhiro, trained under two pillars of Kyoto-style Chinese cooking — 第一樓 (Daiichiro), the postwar restaurant opened around 1951, and the legendary 鳳舞 (Houmai), widely regarded as the fountainhead of 京都中華. He worked as a skilled chef at both before opening his own shop, carrying forward that lineage alongside sister-spirit establishments like 鳳泉.
bio.origin: Kyoto-style Chinese (京都中華), Cantonese-rooted but refined and lightly seasoned, in the 鳳舞 (Houmai) / 第一樓 (Daiichiro) lineage.
bio.specialty: からしそば (karashi soba) — straight noodles pre-coated with mustard paste under a gentle, mildly sweet soy-based ankake, topped with chicken, shrimp, dried shiitake, komatsuna greens and scallion. The mustard gives a sharp 'tsuun' hit to the nose that plays against the sauce's sweetness — a grown-up, unpredictable heat the shop calls a 大人の味.
bio.philosophy: Faithful preservation of the original Daiichiro recipe and taste. The dish was born as a course finale at Daiichiro because it travelled to the table more easily than soup noodles; Terada keeps that heritage intact while cooking his version longer to deepen the broth's umami, and historically swapping in seasonal ingredients (matsutake for shiitake in season, fresh bamboo shoots over canned).

anecdotes.text: The owner, Terada Yasuhiro, trained at both 第一樓 (Daiichiro, opened around 1951) and the legendary 鳳舞 (Houmai) before opening 龍鳳, and keeps the original Daiichiro karashi-soba recipe alive.
Karashi soba was originally created at Daiichiro as the closer to a Chinese course because it was easier to carry to the table than soup noodles — 'カラシソバはコースの〆によう出してたんです。スープ麺より運びやすいから僕らも重宝してね。'
A craftsman of this trade for a lifetime, the chef is described as over 70 years old yet still running the shop himself — 'この道ひと筋で、御歳70オーバー'.
The dish is celebrated as a beloved Kyoto-Chinese specialty even cherished by maiko, served in a nostalgic Showa-era room still set with vintage table condiment dispensers, soy and vinegar bottles.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Karashi soba is wheat-noodle based with a wheat mustard/soy ankake; gyoza and spring rolls are wheat-wrapped, and a small shared kitchen means no gluten isolation is possible.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature dish uses a soy-based ankake sauce (Japanese soy sauce contains wheat).

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is chicken-bone and kombu based, with chicken and shrimp in the dish; no vegan preparation exists, so all cookware contacts animal products.

bio.background: Kyomenya opened in December 2016 along Ichijoji's famously competitive ramen street in Kyoto's Sakyo Ward, just south of the well-known Ramen Yokozuna. It carved out its niche by going against the grain: while Ichijoji leans toward lighter chicken-based broths, Kyomenya planted its flag on unapologetically heavy, ultra-rich tonkotsu.
bio.specialty: The flagship bowl is the Kyo-no Tonkotsu (驚濃とんこつ) — a viscous, cream-thick pork-bone broth rounded out with seafood dashi, using roughly three times the pork bone of an ordinary tonkotsu ramen. The lineup also includes the Mega Tonkotsu (water and pork bones only, simmered for hours), a chicken-enriched Tori Tonkotsu, a milder Maroyaka Tonkotsu, and mazesoba.
bio.philosophy: The aim is a broth that is intensely rich and creamy — dorodoro thick — yet kept smooth and balanced rather than merely heavy, with the pork bone simmered long and combined with seafood stock for depth.

anecdotes.text: The shop earned runner-up in the tonkotsu category of 'Ultimate Ramen 2021,' a nod to how seriously its dorodoro broth is taken among ramen enthusiasts.
Diners customize each bowl at ordering — noodle firmness, amount of green onion, and amount of pork back fat — and a free topping bar offers pickled ginger, spicy mustard greens, and garlic chives.

lead: 家系ラーメン斎藤家 一乗寺店

There is no gluten-free menu, no gluten-free noodle option, and no indication of allergen accommodation. Not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners. Rice bowls are available as sides but are prepared in the same kitchen alongside wheat ramen.
vegan_detail: The entire concept is a pork-bone (tonkotsu) and chicken-oil broth with chashu pork, egg, and a soy-sauce tare. There are no vegetarian or vegan ramen options. Toppings such as spinach, seaweed, and negi exist but only as additions to the meat-based bowl. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.

closed_days: Mondays (closed the following Tuesday instead when Monday is a public holiday)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, busy local ramen counter where ordering is in Japanese and there is no English menu — comfortable for travelers used to Japanese ramen shops, but English support should not be expected.

bio.background: Saitoya is a Yokohama iekei ramen shop whose owner trained at Tagamiya (田上家), a respected Yokohama family-ramen house founded in 2014, and whose lineage traces back to Yoshimuraya (吉村家), the founding shop of the entire iekei genre. The original Saitoya opened in Yokohama's Negishi district in August 2022; this Ichijoji shop, opened July 27, 2024, is the third Saitoya location and the brand's first venture into the Kansai region.
bio.specialty: Chashu-men: a rich tonkotsu-and-chicken-oil broth with a soy-based tare, served over wavy Sakai Seimen noodles and topped with tender smoked pork chashu, spinach, nori seaweed, and egg. Diners can customize noodle firmness, oil level, and tare strength in classic iekei fashion, and add cabbage or white negi the Yokohama way.
bio.philosophy: The owner brews the broth in-house and embraces the iekei idea that the bowl naturally shifts through the day — the flavor you get at the lunch opening differs from the late-evening pour, which they treat as a feature of honest, freshly-simmered cooking rather than a flaw.

anecdotes.text: Brought direct-lineage Yokohama family ramen to Ichijoji, one of Kyoto's most competitive ramen districts, billed as the area's first authentic iekei specialist.
Uses Sakai Seimen's premium A-grade noodles, which are reserved for recognized iekei shops in the lineage.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat noodles (Sakai Seimen) and a wheat-based shoyu tare are core to every bowl; there is no gluten-free preparation and no separate handling, so cross-contamination is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The tare is a soy-sauce base, which in iekei ramen is standard wheat-containing shoyu; reviewers specifically praised the soy-sauce flavor of the broth.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is pork-bone and chicken-oil based with pork chashu and egg; all bowls are animal-based with no vegan option.

caveats: Hours are irregular and split between dinner-only weekdays and all-day Sundays/holidays; confirm before a lunch visit. "Independent" set to false because this is the third branch of a small Yokohama-based brand (still owner-operated, not a large chain). Seat count (~16) is from a blog rather than an official source. Some listings differ slightly on whether Friday includes lunch service.

cuisine: つけ麺（魚介醤油つけ麺・胡麻豆乳担々つけ麺）
gf_detail: No gluten-free option. This is a dedicated tsukemen specialist: both bowls are built on wheat noodles (described as thick, whole-grain-flour-containing noodles) dipped in a soy-sauce-based fish broth (gyokai shoyu) or a sesame-soy-milk tantan broth, both of which use wheat-based soy sauce tare. The wheat noodles plus wheat shoyu mean neither bowl is safe for celiac or gluten-sensitive diners, and no gluten-free preparation is offered or mentioned anywhere.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The fish-soy tsukemen broth is made from bonito (katsuo) and dried sardines (niboshi/iriko), and the sesame-soy-milk tantan bowl is topped with meat-miso (niku-miso); both are animal-based. Although the tantan broth uses soy milk (tonyu), it is not a vegan preparation. No vegan or vegetarian option is offered.
address_ja: 京都府京都市左京区一乗寺赤ノ宮町22
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned coordinates [135.783173, 35.042885] for 京都府京都市左京区一乗寺赤ノ宮町22; within the Kyoto bounding box.
neighborhood: 左京区 一乗寺（叡山電鉄 一乗寺駅）
seats_text: Counter only, 8 seats
hours_raw: 11:00-14:30 (L.O. 14:00) / 17:00-21:30 (L.O. 21:00). Closed Sundays. (Tabelog lists the lunch/dinner blocks as 11:00-14:00 and 17:00-21:00; blog sources list 14:30/21:30 close with L.O. at 14:00/21:00.)
closed_days: 日曜日（Sundays）
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny 8-seat counter shop with mobile-order, cash-only service in Kyoto's ramen-mad Ichijoji district; little English is expected, so come ready to order by phone-app and pay cash.
caveats: Hours differ slightly between Tabelog and blog coverage; the close times (14:30/21:30) reflect last-order plus closing per recent blog reports. Cash only and ordering is via a mobile-order system. Very new shop (opened May 25, 2026), so hours and closed days may still settle.

bio.background: 本気つけ麺山下 opened on May 25, 2026 in the heart of Ichijoji, the neighborhood widely regarded as Kyoto's most fiercely competitive ramen district. It took over the space of the well-loved shop "Tsurukame Ichijoji," which closed at the end of March 2026, refreshing the storefront into a clean, orange-accented counter room. The proprietor is understood to have trained at a well-known ramen establishment before striking out on his own here.
bio.specialty: A two-bowl tsukemen specialist. The signature 魚介醤油つけ麺 pairs thick, springy noodles with a concentrated bonito-and-dried-sardine fish broth balanced by an aromatic soy tare and a touch of yuzu. The second pillar, 胡麻豆乳担々つけ麺, folds rich roasted sesame and creamy soy milk into a tantan dip crowned with meat-miso for a richness-meets-balance bowl.
bio.philosophy: The shop keeps its focus deliberately narrow, offering only its two tsukemen and letting the noodles and dip do the talking, with a stated wish to become a place loved by many.

anecdotes.text: The thick noodles are reportedly from a little-known maker called Hanaki and are a particular point of pride, praised for a smooth, silky, slurpable texture.
The shop occupies the former premises of the popular Tsurukame Ichijoji, which closed on March 31, 2026.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Both tsukemen use wheat noodles dipped in a wheat-soy-sauce-based tare, so there is no celiac-safe option and gluten is intrinsic to every bowl.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The fish broth is a soy-sauce (shoyu) base, which in Japanese ramen is standard wheat-containing soy sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Fish broth uses bonito and dried sardines; the tantan bowl is topped with meat-miso, so animal products are central to both dips.

cuisine: 魚介清湯・出汁そば（昆布＋いりこ＋節の100％魚介出汁／自家製ストレート麺）
neighborhood: 北区 紫野下柏野町（北野白梅町・北野天満宮エリア、住宅街）
address_ja: 京都府京都市北区紫野下柏野町56-32
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 京都府京都市北区紫野下柏野町56-32 returned [135.738968, 35.034054], within the Kyoto box; matches the residential location near Kitano Hakubaicho / Imamiya area.
seats_text: カウンター10席のみ（L字カウンター）
closed_days: 木曜（不定休あり）
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny 10-seat counter shop run quietly by its owner-chef in a residential alley; basic Japanese helps and a calm, attentive manner is appreciated in such an intimate space.
gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The signature bowl uses house-made wheat noodles (Kyoto "Setokirara" wheat), so the ramen contains gluten by definition. The tare/seasoning is unspecified but standard ramen tare typically contains wheat shoyu. No gluten-free menu or noodle option is advertised, and the kitchen is a single small counter operation with shared equipment. Anyone needing strict gluten avoidance should not eat the ramen here.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. Although the broth is unusually animal-free for ramen — a 100% seafood dashi from two kombu varieties, Ibuki iriko (dried sardines) and saba/urume/katsuo bushi (fish flakes) with no pork or chicken stock — it is fish-based, not plant-based, and the bowl is topped with pork chashu (京の都もち豚). There is no vegan or vegetarian ramen option advertised. The kombu component is plant-derived but the dish as served contains fish and pork.
caveats: Hours and closed day are best-effort: listings show 木曜定休 with notes of 不定休 (irregular extra closures), so confirm before visiting. Address number 56-32 is consistent across listings. No official website found; reservations are accepted but not required for a 10-seat counter, so arriving outside peak rush is wise.

bio.background: Owner-chef who trained at 猪一 (Inoichi), a Michelin-recognized Kyoto dashi-soba/ramen establishment, before opening Menya Rennosuke in April 2023 in a renovated traditional wooden house tucked into a quiet Murasakino residential alley.
bio.specialty: らぁ麺 — a delicate clear seafood-dashi bowl. The broth is 100% fish-based, built from two kinds of kombu and Ibuki iriko (dried sardines) layered with saba, urume and katsuo bushi, with no animal stock at all. House-made straight noodles use Kyoto 'Setokirara' wheat with Inner Mongolian natural kansui and Kiritimati salt, cut at #22 for a slightly salty profile that meets the gentle broth. The chashu is Kyoto 京の都もち豚 aged about four days, sliced thin and nearly shabu-like to showcase the pork's natural sweetness, and dessert is a house-made pudding.
bio.philosophy: The chef set out to 'ラーメン屋というイメージを払拭したかった' — to dispel the everyday image of a ramen shop — pursuing refined, additive-free (無化調; no chemical seasoning, yeast extract or preservatives) cooking that expresses local terroir, served in a minimalist, jazz-scored space with Kiyomizu-ware bowls. He also plans seasonal evolution such as cold noodles in summer.

anecdotes.text: Opened April 2023 and within its first year became the only newly selected ramen shop in the Michelin Guide Kyoto-Osaka 2024, earning a Bib Gourmand — recognition it has held for three consecutive years.
Hidden in a residential alley near Kitano Tenmangu, the shop is famously hard to find without directions, and its stylish, izakaya-like counter with jazz feels unexpected for a ramen establishment.
The chef apprenticed at Michelin-recognized Inoichi (猪一) before going independent.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Single small 10-seat counter kitchen built around wheat ramen noodles; no separate gluten-free preparation is advertised, so cross-contact with wheat is effectively unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Tare/seasoning composition is not published; standard ramen tare typically contains wheat-based soy sauce, so assume wheat unless the shop confirms otherwise.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Although the dashi itself is animal-free (kombu, iriko and fish flakes), it is fish-based and the bowl is topped with pork chashu; no plant-based bowl is offered, so the kitchen is not set up for vegan service.

positives.text: Cooking is explicitly 無化調 — no chemical seasonings, yeast extract or preservatives — which some additive-sensitive diners value, though this does not make it gluten-free or vegan.

The broth is unusually a 100% seafood dashi (no pork or chicken stock), which is relevant for diners avoiding meat/poultry stock specifically — but it still contains fish and the bowl includes pork chashu.

cuisine: 二郎系ラーメン (Jiro-style ramen)
neighborhood: 北区 紫野上野町（北大路／今宮神社近く）
gf_detail: No gluten-free option. This is a Jiro-style ramen shop built around house-made wheat noodles (the owner invested in a professional noodle machine specifically to make her own wheat noodles), with a typical wheat-soy-sauce tare and pork-based broth. Not suitable for celiacs or strict gluten avoidance.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan venue. Jiro-style ramen is built on a pork broth with pork (buta) toppings; the signature bowls feature large pork slices. No vegan ramen offering was found.
address_ja: 京都府京都市北区紫野上野町121-2
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 京都府京都市北区紫野上野町121-2; within Kyoto box.
seats_text: Counter seating only (L-shaped counter), roughly 10 seats; intimate, homey atmosphere. No private rooms.
closed_days: Tuesday, Wednesday
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny owner-run counter shop with ordering by ticket machine and no English presence; basic Japanese and familiarity with Jiro-style ordering (noodle quantity and the "yasai/ninniku/abura" toppings call) will make the visit smoother.
caveats: Non-emulsified (非乳化) broth was not explicitly confirmed in the sources fetched; articles describe a relatively mellow, pork-umami-forward Jiro-style broth with house-made noodles. Phone number is listed as unknown on Tabelog. No official website found. Seat count (~10) is approximate, based on counter-only descriptions.

bio.background: The owner spent years as a hospital nurse, earning her nursing credentials while raising her family and returning to study around age 38 after her children grew more independent. Working grueling night shifts, she found that a bowl of ramen eaten after coming off a night shift restored both her stamina and her spirit. That comfort became a calling: at 53 she set out to open her own shop.
bio.specialty: Jiro-style ramen with generous pork and vegetable toppings; the signature is the 小豚ラーメン (kobuta / small-pork ramen). Bowls are customized at order time by noodle quantity and the classic Jiro toppings of garlic, extra vegetables, and oil.
bio.philosophy: She refused to cut corners with wholesale noodles, investing in a professional noodle machine (from Daikoku Manufacturing, around 2.7 million yen) so she could make her own noodles in-house, because she wants customers to taste the difference of truly handmade noodles. Sitting near the universities, she also wants to send students and young people off well-fed and happy, an extension of her caretaking instincts from nursing into feeding her community.

anecdotes.text: The shop name 夢 (yume, dream) carries a double meaning: it is both her dream of running her own ramen shop and the name of her beloved pet dog.
Her path to ramen began on the mornings after night shifts, when a restorative bowl of ramen moved her so deeply that it eventually inspired a mid-life career change at 53.
She crowdfunded her dream of serving house-made (自家製麺) noodles, buying her own noodle machine rather than relying on store-bought noodles.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Built entirely around house-made wheat ramen noodles; flour is handled and cooked on-site, so cross-contact with wheat is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Jiro-style tare is soy-sauce based; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat. Articles describe a soy/salt-balanced broth.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Pork broth and pork toppings are central to the bowls; no vegan or vegetarian preparation found.

cuisine: 町中華・京都中華そば（背脂醤油豚骨／背脂チャッチャ系、ますたに系譜の独立店）
neighborhood: 左京区 浄土寺（元田中／銀閣寺道・北白川エリア）
address_ja: 京都府京都市左京区浄土寺西田町63-1
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned coordinates for 浄土寺西田町63-1; within Kyoto box (lat 34.93–35.13, lng 135.66–135.83).
seats_text: 15席。厨房前のカウンター席と、奥に座敷風のテーブル席が数席。一人でもグループでも利用しやすい造り。
closed_days: 金曜日（定休）
cultural_comfort_note: A small, old-school neighborhood Kyoto ramen counter run by a local owner; English is unlikely, so a little Japanese or pointing at the menu will help, but it is a casual and welcoming spot.
gf_detail: This is a classic Kyoto back-fat shoyu ramen shop. The bowl is built on wheat ramen noodles with a soy-sauce tare (Japanese soy sauce almost always contains wheat) over a pork-and-chicken back-fat broth, so the signature dish is not gluten-free. No gluten-free menu, gluten-free noodles, or dedicated handling has been documented, and a shared broth/noodle-boiling workflow makes cross-contact likely. Not suitable for celiac diners; if avoiding gluten you should not rely on this shop.
vegan_detail: The core product is a meat-based bowl: a pork/chicken-bone broth finished with back fat (背脂チャッチャ系) and topped with chashu and green onion. There is no documented vegan or vegetarian ramen, and the broth itself is animal-based, so there is effectively nothing for a strict vegan here. A vegan diner should plan to eat elsewhere.
caveats: Friday is the regular closing day per Tabelog; other listings sometimes describe lineage timing loosely (a review notes the owner went independent roughly 4–5 years before that review). No official website found. Salt level and exact prices vary by reviewer/era.

bio.background: Shirakawa is a small independent Kyoto-style ramen shop tucked into the Jodoji neighborhood west of Ginkaku-ji, within the Ginkakuji-michi / Kitashirakawa stretch of Sakyo-ku famous as the heartland of Kyoto back-fat shoyu ramen. The owner trained in the renowned Masutani (ますたに) lineage — the Kitashirakawa house generally credited as the origin of Kyoto's back-fat, soy-sauce-and-pork-bone style — before going out on his own to run this counter.
bio.origin: Kyoto (Sakyo-ku, Jodoji), in the Masutani back-fat shoyu ramen tradition
bio.specialty: A Kyoto-style back-fat shoyu ramen (背脂チャッチャ系): a pork-and-chicken-bone broth blanketed with melted back fat, soy-sauce tare, wheat noodles, chashu and green onion. Compared with Masutani, regulars describe Shirakawa's bowl as having lighter back fat and a slightly stronger, saltier soy edge — a recognizably personal take on the house style. There is also a popular fried-rice (chahan) set.
bio.philosophy: Faithful to old-school Kyoto ramen: an unpretentious, affordable everyday bowl served in a homey, diner-like room. A telling detail noted by writers is that vinegar is set out on the counter even though gyoza is not on the menu — a sign the owner understands that a splash of vinegar rounds out and lightens this rich Kyoto broth, a small nod to genuine Kyoto-ramen know-how.

anecdotes.text: Diners come specifically because they have heard it is run by someone who trained at Masutani: a reviewer opens with 『ますたに』で修行した人がやってるラーメン屋さんと聞いて (“I heard this is a ramen shop run by someone who trained at Masutani”).

Writers point to the counter vinegar as proof of the owner's Kyoto-ramen sensibility: 餅子がメニューにないのに、お酢を置いているということは、店主がちゃんと京都ラーメンにお酢が合うことをわかっている証拠 (the fact that vinegar is offered despite no gyoza on the menu shows the owner knows vinegar suits Kyoto ramen).

gf_cross_contamination.text: A back-fat shoyu ramen shop with a single shared workflow (one pot of wheat noodles, shared utensils, soy-based tare); cross-contact with wheat is effectively unavoidable for a gluten-sensitive diner.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The bowl is a soy-sauce (shoyu) ramen; Japanese soy sauce used for ramen tare almost always contains wheat, and no wheat-free tare is documented here.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The broth itself is animal-based (pork/chicken bone with back fat), so even non-meat menu items would share an animal-stock kitchen; no vegan-safe preparation is documented.

cuisine: 鶏白湯・鶏出汁醤油／塩（無添加・国産鶏100%）
gf_detail: This is a wheat-noodle ramen shop. The noodles are wheat, the shoyu and miso tare are almost certainly wheat-based, and the bowls are built on a chicken broth — none of which is gluten-free. No gluten-free noodles, gluten-free tare, or celiac accommodation is advertised anywhere we could find. A celiac diner should not eat here; there is no safe bowl.
vegan_detail: Every bowl is built on a 100% domestic-chicken broth (clear chicken dashi or whipped chicken paitan), and chashu toppings use chicken breast and Kyoto pork. There is no vegetable- or plant-based broth and no advertised vegan or vegetarian option. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区壬生坊城町1-3
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned coordinates for 京都府京都市中京区壬生坊城町1-3; within the Kyoto box. Located just west of the Shijo-Omiya intersection.
neighborhood: 四条大宮・大宮（中京区）
seats_text: 8 seats, L-shaped counter only
closed_days: Sundays (日曜日)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny 8-seat counter ramen bar where Japanese is the working language; basic phrases and pointing at the menu will carry you, but expect little to no English support.
caveats: No dietary-accommodation information could be confirmed from any source; anyone with a wheat or other allergy should ask in person before ordering, ideally in Japanese.

bio.background: A small independent ramen counter that opened in the summer of 2022 a short walk west of the Shijo-Omiya intersection, in the Mibu neighborhood of Kyoto's Nakagyo Ward. With just eight counter seats and a kitchen open until 2 a.m., it has built a following as a late-night spot for a carefully made bowl of chicken ramen.
bio.specialty: The signature is the chicken-paitan shoyu ramen: a creamy chicken broth that the kitchen whips with an immersion blender so it turns foamy and emulsified, concentrating the savory depth, paired with fine noodles, chicken-breast chashu and green onion. The shop also pours a lighter clear chicken-dashi shoyu and shio, plus a chicken-paitan miso.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen is committed to an additive-free bowl built on 100% domestic chicken, cooking the broth without chemical seasonings (muka-cho) and topping it with chicken breast and Kyoto pork.

anecdotes.text: The creamy chicken broth is deliberately whipped with a hand blender until it foams, a technique meant to promote emulsification and intensify the umami in each bowl.
The shop leans into its location and hours as a rare additive-conscious ramen stop that stays open into the small hours near Shijo-Omiya.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Bowls are seasoned with a shoyu (and miso) tare; standard Japanese soy sauce and miso contain wheat, and no wheat-free tare is advertised.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Every broth is made from 100% domestic chicken and chashu uses chicken breast and pork, so all bowls contain animal products with no plant-based option.

positives.text: The kitchen states its broth is additive-free, made without chemical seasonings from 100% domestic chicken.

lead: 中華そば おにぎり ◯△
name_ja: 中華そば おにぎり ◯△
cuisine: 和風中華そば（生姜中華そば・貝だし中華そば、鶏×魚介）／おにぎり
neighborhood: 山科区 竹鼻竹ノ街道町（京阪山科／JR山科駅前）
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The thin noodles are made with wheat (and contain 全粒粉/whole-grain wheat flour), the tare uses aged wheat-based soy sauce (老舗蔵元の再仕込み熟成醤油), and the onigiri are an additional wheat-free side but the ramen itself is built on wheat. Broth is chicken-and-seafood based. No dedicated gluten-free preparation or menu; a celiac diner should treat all the ramen here as unsafe.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan venue. Both signature bowls are animal-based: the ginger chuka soba is a domestic-chicken broth with chicken oil and rare chashu pork; the kai-dashi (shellfish) bowl uses scallop, clam, dried flying-fish (ago) and bonito-style seafood stock. No advertised vegan or vegetarian ramen. A vegan would have essentially nothing here beyond possibly a plain rice ball, which should be confirmed ingredient-by-ingredient in person.
address_ja: 京都府京都市山科区竹鼻竹ノ街道町35-32
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates within the Kyoto box for the full street address.
seats_text: 14 seats — about 10 counter seats plus two 2-person tables (counts vary slightly between sources; counter ~9–10).
hours_raw: 11:00–15:00 / 17:30–22:00, open daily (no fixed closed day listed).
closed_days: No regular closed day listed; confirm before visiting as small shops may close irregularly.
cultural_comfort_note: A small, brisk neighborhood ramen counter with a ticket-machine ordering system and cash-only payment, so a little basic Japanese and self-service comfort go a long way; English support should not be assumed.

bio.background: Opened December 27, 2023 right by Yamashina Station, this compact ramen-and-onigiri counter is a produce/concept shop developed by the Nagoya-based food company F-Lead (株式会社エフリード). The idea is unusual and very deliberate: a bowl of chuka soba designed expressly to be eaten one hand on the chopsticks, the other holding a freshly formed rice ball.
bio.origin: Yamashina, Kyoto — a casual local spot a few minutes' walk from JR / Keihan Yamashina Station.
bio.specialty: The headline bowl is the 生姜中華そば (ginger chuka soba): a domestic-chicken broth finished with aged re-brewed soy sauce from a long-established maker, with ginger aroma drawn into the oil by low-temperature infusion so it reads, as one reviewer put it, 'like ginger-pork sauce with the sweetness taken out.' Thin, firm whole-grain noodles, rare-sliced chashu, red onion and menma round it out. The second pillar is the 貝だし中華そば, a clearer bowl layering scallop and clam stock with dried flying-fish (ago) and Rishiri kelp.
bio.philosophy: Build a ramen whose flavor is tuned specifically to pair with rice — fragrant and savory rather than heavy — and serve loosely hand-formed, crumble-in-the-mouth onigiri with carefully chosen rice and generous fillings alongside it.

anecdotes.text: The onigiri are intentionally formed loosely so they fall apart softly in the mouth, with fillings ranging from truffle-kombu to salmon-and-roe (oyako) and rare chashu with egg yolk.
Opening was announced for December 27, 2023 under the tagline 'eat ginger chuka soba with a fluffy onigiri in one hand,' framing the rice ball as a core part of the meal rather than a side.

roles: operator: 株式会社エフリード (F-Lead)

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The tare is described as aged re-brewed soy sauce from an established brewery (老舗蔵元の再仕込み熟成醤油), a wheat-based soy sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Both signature broths are animal-based (domestic chicken with chicken oil; or scallop/clam/flying-fish/bonito-style seafood stock), so shared pots and water mean cross-contact with animal ingredients is inherent.

caveats: Hours and seat count are from listing/blog sources and may shift; confirm the daily schedule before visiting. No allergen, gluten-free, or vegan accommodation information was found from any source — treat the noodles (wheat, incl. whole-grain), wheat soy tare, and chicken/seafood broths as unsafe for celiac and vegan diners unless verified in person. Operated as a produce shop by F-Lead Corp (not strictly owner-independent), though it functions as a small single local counter.

name_ja: Oike Café × HACHINOBO（オイケカフェ ハチノボウ）
cuisine: 泡系・カプチーノ仕立ての濃厚豚骨ラーメン
gf_detail: No gluten-free offering. The house bowls are built on wheat ramen noodles with a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare over a rich pork-bone broth — both the noodles and the tare contain wheat, so the standard bowls are not suitable for celiacs or those strictly avoiding gluten. No dedicated GF menu, no gluten-free noodle option, and no allergen accommodation is documented. Anyone needing gluten-free should treat this as unsafe and confirm directly.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan specialist. The signature bowls use pork-bone broth and pork/chicken chashu. The original 八の坊 was known for a "ベジそば" (vegetable soba), and the revived shop's broader menu includes vegetable-leaning items, curry and rice dishes, but there is no confirmed fully plant-based ramen — any "vegetable" bowl may still use animal-based broth or tare. A strict vegan must ask in person whether the broth, tare and toppings are entirely plant-based before ordering.
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区西横町180 パークホテル京都御池 1F
geocode_note: GSI address search on 京都府京都市中京区西横町180 returned [135.757568, 35.010986], within the Kyoto box. Matches the Karasuma-Oike / Park Hotel Kyoto Oike location.
neighborhood: 中京区・烏丸御池（パークホテル京都御池1F）
seats_text: About 30 seats inside the ground-floor hotel café space; spacious, walk-in friendly with a street-facing entrance.
closed_days: None listed (open daily); may close earlier if sold out.
cultural_comfort_note: Set inside a hotel café with a street-facing entrance and walk-in seating, it is welcoming and easy to enter; a few words of Japanese or pointing at the menu will carry you comfortably.

bio.background: This is the second life of a beloved Kyoto ramen shop. The original 八の坊 (Hachinobo) opened in Enmachi on February 1, 2015 and was one of the shops that introduced Kyoto to the foam-style (泡系) ramen pioneered in Fukuoka. It later reworked itself as 八ノ家, leaning into a Yokohama-inspired family-style tonkotsu, before closing on September 30, 2023. On August 24, 2024 the name and its signature 'cappuccino' bowl were revived as Oike Café × HACHINOBO on the ground floor of Park Hotel Kyoto Oike near Karasuma-Oike.
