bio.specialty: Cappuccino Ramen — an ultra-rich pork-bone broth whipped into a fluffy cappuccino-like foam, served with thick curly noodles, two kinds of chashu (tender chicken and pork loin), crisp raw onion, extra-thick menma, and a seasoned soft-boiled egg. A spicy soy-based 'RED' bowl (chili oil, minced pork, diced onion, inspired by Katsuura tantanmen) is the other standout.
bio.philosophy: Tame the heaviness of tonkotsu by aerating it: the frothed broth keeps the famous pork-bone aroma restrained and gives a creamy, fluffy mouthfeel, so the bowl reads as rich-but-refined rather than punishing.

anecdotes.text: The original Enmachi shop set diners loose with a row of free counter condiments — yuzu vinegar, chili oil, black pepper and a spicy crunchy topping — to remix the bowl to taste.
The shop is credited with helping bring Fukuoka's foam-style ramen to Kyoto, and the cappuccino bowl's fluffy foam is its calling card.
Reopened August 24, 2024 inside Park Hotel Kyoto Oike, a few minutes' walk from Karasuma-Oike station, in a spacious café setting that doubles as the hotel's restaurant.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The signature bowls are built on wheat ramen noodles cooked in shared water, with a soy-sauce tare that contains wheat, so cross-contact with gluten is inherent to the kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is finished with a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare over pork-bone stock; standard Japanese shoyu is brewed with wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Bowls center on pork-bone broth and pork/chicken chashu; any vegetable-leaning dish shares a kitchen that is fundamentally pork-based, so vegan separation is not assured.

caveats: Owner/chef is not named in any source consulted. Original shop was in Enmachi (円町); one fetched summary misread this as "Marutocho/Maruyama" — disregard that. The original 八の坊 offered a "ベジそば" (vegetable soba), but no confirmation that any current bowl is fully plant-based — vegan diners must ask. Hours shown as the listed 11:00–21:00 but the shop may close early once sold out. No allergen documentation found, so dietary safety notes are inferred from the standard composition of foam-style tonkotsu ramen (wheat noodles + shoyu tare + pork broth).

lead: 麺ショップ この味出来太蔵
name_ja: 麺ショップ この味出来太蔵
cuisine: Ramen (jiro-kei / 中華そば / tsukemen)
gf_detail: No gluten-free provision. This is a wheat-noodle ramen shop spanning jiro-kei (extra-thick wheat noodles), 中華そば (Chinese-style soba), and tsukemen, with soy-forward tare and pork-bone/chicken broths — all standard sources of wheat. No celiac-safe options, no dedicated equipment, and no allergy accommodation are mentioned anywhere. Not suitable for someone who must strictly avoid gluten.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan or vegetarian destination. The kitchen is built on animal stock — a chicken-and-pork-bone broth — across the menu, plus chashu, pork belly, and karaage sides. No plant-based bowl is offered or noted.
address_ja: 京都府京都市上京区多門町434 コスモ星の子ハイツ1F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI from address_ja (multi-monban 434); coordinates [135.746429, 35.024857] fall within the Kyoto box. Sits near the Chiekoin-Nakadachiuri bus stop, roughly 20 minutes on foot from Imadegawa Station.
neighborhood: 西陣・中立売智恵光院（上京区）/ 今出川
seats_text: Compact interior with an L-shaped counter wrapping the kitchen plus some table seating; roughly 12 seats.
hours_raw: 11:00-14:30 (L.O. 14:30) / 17:30-22:00 (L.O. 22:00). Closures irregular (one local guide lists Wednesday as the closed day; confirm before visiting).
closed_days: Irregular; a local guide cites Wednesday — verify in advance.
cultural_comfort_note: A small, casual neighborhood counter shop run in Japanese with no listed English support, but the no-call ordering and walk-in format make it approachable.

bio.background: The owner spent years inside Kyoto's ramen world — local accounts cite stints at established names such as Takaradoya, Chingyu and Daichu — before going independent and opening this shop in June 2022, taking over a former ramen tenant on the Nakadachiuri-Chiekoin block of Kamigyo Ward.
bio.specialty: An all-rounder bowl lineup. The 中華そば (Chinese-style soba) is the everyday signature — a chicken-and-pork-bone broth with quiet depth, melt-in-the-mouth chashu and straight noodles tuned to be savory but easygoing. Alongside it the shop runs a deliberately approachable 'light jiro' (二郎系) bowl and tsukemen, plus salt, miso, seafood and niboshi variations.
bio.philosophy: Broad appeal over purist gatekeeping: the jiro-style bowl is positioned as a 'light jiro' with no intimidating call-out ordering system, so newcomers can try the genre without knowing the ritual. The kitchen keeps the menu wide and rotates seasonal specials (cold noodles, thin miso, extra-rich abura-soba) to stay a place anyone in the neighborhood can enjoy.

anecdotes.text: The jiro-style bowl is framed as a beginner-friendly 'light jiro' — generous sprouts, cabbage and thick pork belly over extra-thick wavy noodles, but with grated (not minced) garlic and no complicated 'call' system, so first-timers aren't put on the spot.
Opened June 2022 in a former ramen space, the shop trades on the owner's long apprenticeship at well-known Kyoto houses and keeps Southern All Stars playing through the room.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The bowls are soy-forward (a soy-tare jiro and soy-based Chinese soba), and ramen tare is conventionally made with wheat-containing soy sauce; no wheat-free tare is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broths are animal-based (chicken-and-pork-bone), prepared in a single small kitchen alongside pork belly, chashu and karaage, so a plant-only request cannot be isolated.

caveats: Closed-day information conflicts across sources: Tabelog and most coverage describe closures as irregular, while one local food guide lists Wednesday — the hours_week reflects the Wednesday reading but this should be confirmed (e.g., via the @dekitazo Instagram) before visiting. Seat count (~12) and the address are well corroborated. No allergy, gluten-free, or vegan accommodation appears in any source; safety fields reflect general ramen norms rather than shop-specific statements.

cuisine: Old-school machi-chuka chuka-soba in a Meiji-origin soba shokudo
vegan_detail: This is a traditional soba/udon shokudo whose dishes center on fish-and-meat dashi, pork chashu, kamaboko fish cake, and donburi over rice. The signature chuka-soba has a light broth but is topped with simmered pork chashu, menma, and grilled fish cake, and the kitchen makes no mention of plant-based options. There is no vegan offering; a vegan diner would have essentially nothing safe here.
gf_detail: Everything central here is wheat-based: the chuka-soba noodles are Chinese wheat noodles, the soba/udon are wheat-containing, and the bowls feature wheat-gluten fu (umefu and dried fu) plus a Japanese-style broth that almost certainly relies on wheat-containing shoyu. No gluten-free preparation, dedicated equipment, or allergen handling is indicated. Not suitable for a celiac diner.
address_ja: 京都府京都市左京区聖護院東町15
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned a single feature at [135.782654, 35.019016], within the Kyoto bounding box; matches the Shogoin-Higashimachi alley location near Jingu-Marutamachi Station.
neighborhood: 聖護院・岡崎（左京区）— deep down a narrow covered alley off Yoshida-Higashi-dori near Marutamachi, a short walk south of Heian Shrine and east of Jingu-Marutamachi Station
seats_text: About 24 seats, including a small one-mat raised tatami area; an intimate old-school shokudo space
hours_raw: 11:00～15:00 / 16:30～18:00（日曜は通し営業ではなく同様の昼夜区分）; irregular closures, roughly 3–4 days per month
closed_days: Irregular closures, roughly 3–4 days per month (no fixed weekly closing day published)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, traditional family-run neighborhood shokudo tucked down a narrow alley with no indication of English support, so a little Japanese or pointing at the menu will go a long way.
caveats: Hours are split (lunch and an early-evening service) with irregular monthly closures, so confirm before a special trip. Cash only and no reservations. Wheat-heavy menu with no allergen accommodation noted — not for celiac or vegan diners.

bio.background: Sarashina traces its roots to a Meiji-era soba house centered on a main shop in Kyoto's Shinkyogoku shopping district; this Shogoin branch grew out of that lineage and today runs as its own quiet, independent neighborhood shokudo. You find it by walking some ten meters down a covered alley barely a meter wide — easy to stroll right past — until a low old-fashioned door opens onto a small room of rustic, folk-craft furnishings and a single mat of raised tatami seating that feels unchanged for decades.
bio.specialty: The bowl regulars come for is the chuka-soba: pale, straight-cut Chinese noodles in a gentle, light broth that carries a subtly Japanese, dashi-leaning character rather than the heavy richness of a modern ramen-ya. It arrives piping hot and generously dressed — simmered chashu pork, menma, scallion, nori, grilled kamaboko fish cake, and pieces of wheat-gluten fu (including a pickled umefu) — the kind of comforting, everyday bowl meant to be eaten often rather than to impress.
bio.philosophy: Unfussy, neighborly cooking: an old soba shokudo that quietly serves nostalgic chuka-soba and home-style donburi at gentle prices, prizing warmth and consistency over trend.
bio.origin: Kyoto (Meiji-era Sarashina lineage out of Shinkyogoku)

anecdotes.text: The entrance is so hidden — down a covered alley under a meter wide, about ten meters in — that it is genuinely a 'know-it-if-you-know-it' spot locals say you can walk straight past.
Beyond the chuka-soba the shop is known for homey standards like nabeyaki udon and konoha-don, the everyday dishes of a long-running soba house.

gf_cross_contamination.text: A wheat-noodle and soba/udon kitchen with shared boiling water and prep; no gluten-free handling indicated, so cross-contact should be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The light, Japanese-leaning broth and donburi seasonings almost certainly use standard wheat-containing soy sauce; no wheat-free tare or seasoning is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth and toppings are built on pork chashu and fish cake; even the noodle bowls are not vegetarian, and no separate vegan prep exists.

lead: 大豊ラーメン 木屋町店
name_ja: 大豊ラーメン 木屋町店
cuisine: 京都ラーメン（深夜営業の濃色豚骨醤油・背脂系）
neighborhood: 中京区 木屋町
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区木屋町通四条上ル下樵木町191-11
geocode_note: GSI geocode of the chō-level address returned [135.766159, 35.014957], within the Kyoto box and consistent with the Kiyamachi alley location near Sanjo/Kawaramachi.
seats_text: L字型カウンター12席（個室なし、テーブルが1卓との記述もあり）
closed_days: 不定休
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny late-night counter shop down a Kiyamachi alley with no English menu noted; ordering is simplest with a little Japanese, though it draws plenty of post-drinking visitors including travelers.
gf_detail: This is a classic wheat-noodle Kyoto ramen shop with a pork-bone soy (shoyu) base, so the noodles and the tare both contain wheat and there is no indication of gluten-free options or any allergen accommodation. Not suitable for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: The menu centers on pork-bone/chicken broth ramen, chashu pork, and karaage; no vegan or vegetable-broth bowl is offered. Not a vegan option.
caveats: Hours per Tabelog differ slightly across sources (one listing shows a flat 18:00-翌6:00); closures are irregular (不定休), so confirm by phone (075-251-6784) before a late-night visit. A separate daytime branch exists near Bukkyo University but this is the Kiyamachi late-night store.

bio.background: 大豊ラーメンは、京都の夜の歓楽街・木屋町と先斗町のあいだの細い路地の奥、交番のそばにひっそりと構える深夜ラーメンの定番店。木屋町店は夕方から翌朝6時まで営業し、飲んだあとの〆を求める地元客や旅行者で賑わう。
bio.specialty: 看板は特製チャーシューラーメン。真っ黒に見える濃色の豚骨醤油（鶏の旨味も効いた褐色）スープに、花びらのように盛られた薄切りチャーシュー、たっぷりの青ねぎ、ストレートの細麺を合わせる。
bio.philosophy: 見た目の黒さに反して意外とあっさりとした、くせになる一杯を深夜に提供し、歓楽街の〆の一杯としての役割を担う。
roles: 独立系の小規模ラーメン店

anecdotes.text: ビジュアルは真っ黒だが、スープは見た目に反してあっさりとしており、鶏の旨味がたっぷり効いていると評される。
厨房を囲うL字型カウンター（約12席）の狭い空間で、飲んだ後の客たちの人間模様が見られる、木屋町ナイトライフの〆ラーメンの代表格。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The bowl is built on a pork-bone soy (shoyu) base; standard Japanese soy sauce tare contains wheat, and wheat ramen noodles are cooked in shared water.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is pork-bone/chicken based and bowls are topped with chashu pork; no separate vegan preparation is indicated.

lead: 中華そば専門店 なか小路
name_ja: 中華そば専門店 なか小路
cuisine: 京都中華そば（背脂醤油ラーメン・背脂まぜそば・鶏白湯）
gf_detail: No gluten-free options. This is a traditional Kyoto chuka-soba shop: wheat ramen noodles (Kondo Seimen / Kondo noodle factory), a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare that almost certainly contains wheat, and chicken/pork back-fat broths. There is no indication of gluten-free menu items or accommodation, and the kitchen shares boiling water and surfaces with wheat noodles. A celiac or strict gluten-free diner cannot eat safely here.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The core bowls are built on chicken and pork (back-fat) broth with chashu (pork), and the shop also serves beef-tendon rice bowls, karaage, and ginger-pork sets. No plant-based broth or vegan ramen is offered. Vegans cannot be accommodated.
address_ja: 京都府京都市南区吉祥院這登東町1-19
geocode_note: GSI address search returned a single feature at [135.730804, 34.972057] for 南区吉祥院這登東町1, within the Kyoto bounding box. Note: the shop sits in the Yoshoin district of Minami-ku, just off Nishioji-dori, near the West (Kissho-in) side rather than literally in Kamitoba.
neighborhood: 南区 吉祥院（西大路通沿い）
seats_text: About 20 seats: a U-shaped counter (roughly 10-12 stools) plus a raised tatami section with two tables for four. Family-friendly, with parking out front.
hours_raw: Weekdays 11:00-14:30 / 17:00-21:00 (L.O. ~20:45); weekends and holidays 7:30-14:30 / 17:00-21:00. Monday is lunch only (11:00-14:30). Sources vary slightly on the morning/weekend start.
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular closures); Monday is lunch-only with no dinner service.
cultural_comfort_note: A casual neighborhood ramen counter serving local families; ordering is straightforward but staff and menu are Japanese-only, so a little Japanese or a translation app helps.
caveats: Hours differ across sources, especially the weekend morning start (7:30 ramen service is noted) and whether dinner runs to 21:00 every day; confirm before a special trip. Closures are irregular. There is a separate branch near Otani University (大谷大学前店) in Kita-ku with different hours and menu — do not confuse it with this Minami-ku main shop. Despite the "鶏白湯/paitan" label, at least one visitor found the chicken bowl to read as a clear, strongly seasoned broth rather than a creamy paitan.

bio.background: The owner came up through two pillars of Kyoto's back-fat ramen tradition: he trained at the legendary Shinpuku Saikan and then served as manager (tencho) at Chinyu in Ichijoji before striking out on his own. The shop opened on November 1, 2021, in a quiet residential pocket of Minami-ku's Yoshoin district, taking over a former ramen storefront.
bio.specialty: The signature is a classic back-fat soy-sauce bowl (背脂醤油ラーメン) on a robustly seasoned chicken-based broth, finished with green onion, two slices of chashu, menma, and a dusting of chili powder. The kitchen also runs a back-fat mazesoba and a chicken bowl, plus hearty sides — beef-tendon rice bowls, karaage, and ginger-pork sets — and weekend morning ramen service.
bio.philosophy: To serve honest, old-school Kyoto ramen (昔ながらの京都ラーメン) at neighborhood prices: the bolder, back-fat-forward seasoning that south-Kyoto locals favor, paired with the snappy medium-thin noodles that define the city's style. It is built as an approachable, family-friendly local shop rather than a destination.

anecdotes.text: The owner trained at Shinpuku Saikan and worked as manager at Chinyu in Ichijoji before opening Nakakoji on November 1, 2021.
Noodles are the snappy, medium-thin Kyoto style sourced from Kondo Seimen, served firm by default; the shop also pours notably cheap draft beer and runs weekend morning ramen.
A local-favorite shop in a residential corner of Minami-ku, praised for back-fat 'chacha' Kyoto ramen that has depth without heaviness and Kyoto green onions that turn sweet from the heat.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles (from Kondo Seimen) are boiled in shared water; a celiac-safe preparation is not possible here.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The house bowl is a soy-sauce (shoyu) back-fat ramen; standard Japanese shoyu tare contains wheat, and no gluten-free tare is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths are chicken- and pork-based with back fat, topped with pork chashu; sides include beef tendon, karaage, and ginger pork, so no dish is free of animal products.

lead: らぁ麺や ふぢとら
name_ja: らぁ麺や ふぢとら
cuisine: 鶏出汁の醤油・塩らぁ麺と担々麺（らぁ麺とうひち出身店主の自家製麺ラーメン）
neighborhood: 右京区・太秦（花園駅／太秦安井）
gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The signature bowls are built on house-made wheat noodles made fresh each morning, and the shoyu version uses a tare blending six soy sauces (wheat-based Japanese shoyu), served over a chicken-and-pork broth. No gluten-free noodle, tare, or menu option is mentioned anywhere, and the kitchen also hand-makes wheat-skin wontons, so wheat is pervasive. A celiac diner cannot safely eat the ramen here. Anyone needing strictly gluten-free should not rely on this shop without contacting the owner directly first (担々麺・らぁ麺ともに小麦麺・小麦醤油使用).
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. Every bowl is animal-based: the broth is built from jidori chicken bones, kurobuta pork and pork back bone, finished with chicken oil (鶏油). Standard toppings include pork chashu, ground pork (tantanmen), and meat-filled wontons. There is no vegetable/vegan broth or noodle option described. A vegan would have nothing safe to order here.
address_ja: 京都府京都市右京区太秦安井辻之内町15-7
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned coordinates [135.71904, 35.014587] for 京都府京都市右京区太秦安井辻之内町15-7, within the Kyoto box; near Hanazono Station, matching the stated 右京区・太秦 location.
seats_text: 13 seats total (9 counter + 4 table); small counter-focused shop, comfortable for solo diners
hours_raw: Mon & Sun: 11:00-14:00 (lunch only); Wed-Sat: 11:00-14:00, 18:00-20:00; Tue closed. May close early once sold out.
closed_days: Tuesdays (and may close earlier than posted once the day's batch sells out)
cultural_comfort_note: A small Japanese neighborhood ramen counter with no indication of English menus or staff, so a few words of Japanese or pointing at the ticket/menu will smooth the visit.
caveats: Cash only. Often sells out and closes before posted hours, so go early, especially for lunch-only days (Mon/Sun) and limited bowls. Closed Tuesdays. Wheat noodles and wheat soy tare throughout — unsuitable for celiac or vegan diners.

bio.background: The owner-chef discovered ramen as a film student at university in Kyoto, falling for the city's celebrated bowls. He went on to train for roughly six years at らぁ麺 とうひち, a Michelin Bib Gourmand shop often hailed as one of Kyoto's very best, before becoming its first graduate to open his own place. He launched Fujitora in the quiet Uzumasa-Yasui neighborhood near Hanazono Station in September 2023.
bio.specialty: A clean yet deep chicken-forward bowl: the broth is built from jidori chicken bones, kurobuta pork and pork back bone, glossed with chicken oil. The shoyu version is finished with a tare blending six soy sauces (with Wakayama and Hyogo shoyu at its base) and served over straight, sharply-cut house-made thin noodles; a shio version runs alongside it. The tantanmen, with its own slightly flatter house-made noodles, sesame-rich and gradually building heat brightened by cilantro, has become a signature draw in its own right.
bio.philosophy: Do as much as possible by hand, every day. Fuchihara makes not only the soup but the noodles and the wontons himself, milling fresh noodles each morning so the wheat aroma is at its peak, and is described as cooking earnestly and meticulously. The shop carries forward the とうひち lineage while standing on its own.

anecdotes.text: The chef trained about six years at the Michelin-listed らぁ麺 とうひち and is recognized as the first person to go independent from that famous shop, carrying its lineage into his own kitchen.
He first fell for ramen while studying film at university in Kyoto, visiting Tohichi during those student years, which set him on the path to opening his own shop.
On opening, congratulatory flowers arrived from his master chef and other prominent figures in the ramen world, reflecting high expectations for the new shop.
He hand-makes the noodles fresh every morning in the shop to maximize their wheat fragrance, and also makes the wontons by hand.

gf_cross_contamination.text: House-made wheat noodles are milled and cut fresh in the shop every morning, so wheat flour handling is central to the kitchen and cross-contact is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The shoyu tare blends six soy sauces (based on Kaneiwa of Wakayama and Suehiro of Hyogo) — standard wheat-fermented Japanese shoyu — so the soy ramen is wheat-based at its core.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is made from jidori chicken bones, kurobuta pork and pork back bone and finished with chicken oil; toppings include pork chashu, ground pork and meat-filled wontons, so no bowl is free of animal products.

cuisine: 鴨そば・鴨と貝の中華そば・鴨まぜそば（鴨清湯ラーメン）
neighborhood: 中京区・聚楽廻（二条駅近く）
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The noodles are wheat (Hokkaido kitahonami wheat flour / whole-wheat noodles from a Kyoto noodle maker) and the duck broth is finished with soy-sauce tare, which is virtually always wheat-based in Japan. Duck clear-broth ramen of this style is not celiac-safe; no gluten-free menu, no dedicated prep, and no indication staff accommodate gluten. A strict celiac should not eat here.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. Every signature bowl is built on duck — duck stock (made from duck and water), low-temperature duck loin, and duck fat — and the menu also features shellfish (clam/scallop) variants, flavored egg, and duck rice/yukhoe bowls. There are no vegetable- or plant-based broth options listed.
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区聚楽廻東町2-9
geocode_note: GSI address search returned [135.742111, 35.018147] for 京都府京都市中京区聚楽廻東町2-9; within the Kyoto bounding box.
seats_text: Counter seats only, about 8, in a small clean space with a jazz ambiance.
hours_raw: 11:00-14:30 (LO 14:30), 17:30-20:30 per one listing; another lists 11:00-15:00 (LO 14:30) / 17:00-22:00 (LO 21:30). Sat/Sun reported as 11:00-20:30. Holidays irregular.
closed_days: Irregular holidays (不定休)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny counter-only neighborhood noodle shop with ticket-machine ordering, so a smile and a few words of Japanese go a long way, though pointing at the machine gets you fed.
caveats: Reported hours differ between listings (lunch close at 14:30 vs 15:00; dinner 17:30-20:30 vs 17:00-22:00) — confirm locally before a special trip. Opened February 15, 2025, so it is a young shop. Cash-only at the ticket machine, with card/QR payment said to be planned.

bio.background: Kawano is the younger sibling of 貝だし麺きた田, the popular shellfish-broth noodle shop just north of Kyoto Station. When the team opened this second place near Nijo Station in February 2025 (in a space that previously held a light-style ramen shop), they deliberately did not repeat the shellfish formula — they set out to 'try something unconventional, with a Japanese sensibility,' and chose duck as their canvas.
bio.origin: Kyoto — sister shop to 貝だし麺きた田 near Kyoto Station; this branch sits in the Jurakukai neighborhood near Nijo Station.
bio.specialty: Kamo soba (duck noodles): a clear, delicate duck broth made from just duck and water, served over Hokkaido wheat noodles and topped with low-temperature duck loin, white and green scallion, and bamboo shoots, finished with duck fat. Variations include duck-and-shellfish Chinese noodles (shoyu or shio), duck mazesoba, and duck tsukemen, plus duck rice and duck-yukhoe bowls.
bio.philosophy: Restraint and refinement over heavy ramen convention — the kitchen treats the duck like washoku, controlling temperature and timing so the meat stays tender and pink, and lets the broth deepen as the noodles slowly release into it.
roles: owner/operator (same team as 貝だし麺きた田)

anecdotes.text: The owners describe the project as wanting to challenge something unorthodox while keeping a Japanese aesthetic, choosing duck precisely because the sister shop already owns the shellfish lane.
The duck broth is built from only duck and water; the flavor is meant to evolve in the bowl as the noodles dissolve into it.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The duck broth is seasoned with a soy-sauce tare; Japanese soy sauce is almost always wheat-fermented, so the broth should be treated as containing wheat/gluten.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is made from duck and water and finished with duck fat; bowls include duck loin and shellfish (clam/scallop) variants, so there is no plant-based base in the kitchen.

cuisine: つけ担々麺（クリーミーな白ごまつけ担々麺）
neighborhood: 長岡京市神足（JR長岡京駅、国道171号沿い）
address_ja: 京都府長岡京市神足大張8番地 1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 京都府長岡京市神足大張8番地 returns [135.708908, 34.921764] (chō-level 神足: [135.709366, 34.921131]). Latitude ~34.92 falls just below the configured Kyoto box minimum of 34.93; Nagaokakyo lies in southwestern Kyoto Prefecture, legitimately south of that box, so coordinates set to null. Actual location is lat 34.9218, lng 135.7089.
seats_text: カウンター9席のみ（情報源により10席とも）。駐車場4〜5台。
closed_days: 不定休（昼・夜ともスープがなくなり次第終了）
cultural_comfort_note: A small 9-seat counter ramen shop with a Japanese-only menu and a regulars-and-line crowd; basic Japanese or a translation app will make ordering the four-symbols bowls much smoother.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. The dish is wheat tsukemen (dipping) noodles served with a sesame-based tantan dipping broth simmered from bonito, mackerel, kombu, sesame, homemade chili oil and sweet bean paste (tōbanjan/tenmenjan-style), all of which typically contain wheat. No GF noodle or GF menu is advertised. Celiac diners should avoid.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The signature creamy sesame dipping broth is built on fish dashi (bonito and mackerel), and bowls are topped with diced chashu and spiced ground pork plus meat-miso rice. No vegan or vegetarian option is advertised.
caveats: Seat count varies by source (9 vs 10); cash only; closes early when the day's broth sells out, so arrive well before stated close. Hours shown apply to regular operating days only — closures are irregular.

bio.background: Furaibou is a small, independent dipping-tantan-noodle specialist that opened in 2007 along National Route 171, about a 12-minute walk from JR Nagaokakyo Station. From a simple black-and-wood counter shop in the southern Kyoto suburb of Nagaokakyo, it has built a reputation that draws diners not just from the neighborhood but from Kyoto city and Osaka, with lines a regular feature.
bio.specialty: Creamy cold-and-hot tsukemen tantan: thin, straight wheat noodles served alongside a rich, creamy white-sesame dipping broth. The dipping soup is simmered for about an hour from bonito and mackerel fish dashi, kombu, sesame, and house-made chili oil and sweet bean paste, then finished with diced chashu and spiced ground pork. The menu is organized around the Four Symbols of the constellations plus extras: Byakko (white sesame, the standard), Genbu (extra black sesame), Suzaku (sour-and-spicy), Houou/Ouryuu (savory-spicy), and Kouryuu (curry), with a chilled summer special.
bio.philosophy: Keep the original flavor consistent over the years and make each bowl to order, balancing a broth that is creamy yet moderately spicy.

anecdotes.text: The bowls are named after the Four Symbols / celestial guardians of Chinese astronomy — Byakko (white tiger), Genbu (black tortoise), Suzaku (vermilion bird), and Kouryuu (yellow dragon) — each color matched to its sesame, spice or curry character.
It is known as a must-queue shop where the broth simmers about an hour and service simply ends once the day's soup runs out.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core dish is wheat dipping noodles with a sesame tantan broth containing fish dashi and likely wheat-based chili-bean and sweet bean pastes; not safe for celiac diners.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is built on bonito and mackerel fish dashi and bowls are topped with pork chashu and ground pork; no plant-based version offered.

positives.text: Spice level and noodle quantity can be customized to the diner's preference.

lead: 熟成麺屋 神来 西院本店
name_ja: 熟成麺屋 神来 西院本店
cuisine: 鶏ガラ豚骨醤油ラーメン・背脂こってり系（焼き飯・唐揚げ）
gf_detail: This is a classic wheat-noodle shoyu ramen shop. The noodles are custom-made wheat noodles from Menya Teikaku, the broth is a soy-sauce-based chicken-and-pork-bone base (soy sauce tare almost always contains wheat), and signature sides are wheat-batter karaage and fried rice. No gluten-free options, no celiac-safe preparation, and no dedicated equipment are indicated anywhere. Not suitable for celiacs or strict gluten avoidance.
vegan_detail: The core product is an animal-based bowl: chicken-and-pork-bone (鶏ガラ豚骨) broth with floating back-fat, topped with house-made chashu (pork), menma, and Kujo negi. Soy-sauce tare and animal broth are intrinsic to every ramen here. No vegan or plant-based ramen is offered, and no vegan preparation is mentioned. Not suitable for vegans.
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区壬生淵田町1 2F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI from address 京都府京都市中京区壬生淵田町1; coordinates fall within the Kyoto box. Note: the shop is in Nakagyo Ward (中京区), not Ukyo Ward as stated in the target brief — Saiin station sits near the ward boundary but both Tabelog and the official site list the Nakagyo address.
neighborhood: 西院 / 西院駅（嵐電・阪急）
seats_text: 21 seats (counter and tables); second-floor location near the station.
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular closures, no fixed closed day)
cultural_comfort_note: A bustling, no-frills local ramen counter where ordering is quick and Japanese-only; come prepared to order simply and pay cash, ideally with a guide or phrase help if you don't speak Japanese.
caveats: Cash only; no reservations; irregular closing days, so confirm before a special trip. Address ward differs from the target brief (Nakagyo, not Ukyo).

bio.background: Jinrai opened its Saiin flagship on February 2, 2012, right by Saiin station, and grew from this single counter into a small Kyoto-area group of six shops (Ichijoji, two Aeon Mall locations, the Kyoto Racecourse stand, and Aeon Mall Takanohara in Kizugawa). Despite the expansion, the Saiin honten remains the original neighborhood ramen-ya that built the name.
bio.specialty: The signature bowl is a soy-sauce-based chicken-and-pork-bone ramen with a layer of aromatic back-fat (seabura) floating on top — rich and kotteri but described by regulars as surprisingly easy to drink. It is finished with Kyoto's traditional Kujo negi and house-made chashu and menma, on thin, springy custom wheat noodles made to order by the respected noodle maker Menya Teikaku. The Stamina Ramen (white cabbage, thin pork, chives) is another house draw.
bio.philosophy: The owner frames running the shop as 'enjoying the business' — treating service as a kind of friendly competition where staff read each customer and move as one unified team to keep the line moving and the bowls hot.

anecdotes.text: Beyond ramen, the shop is famous for two sides that locals come back for: karaage (fried chicken) marinated in a secret soy-based sauce, and a fried rice (yakimeshi) that staff reportedly need rigorous training to cook properly.
Reviewers describe it as a back-fat (seabura) shoyu shop that somehow stays 'light' and keeps a constant flow of customers — an oddly more-ish bowl that explains why the small shop is always busy.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The ramen is a soy-sauce-based (醤油ベース) bowl on custom wheat noodles; soy-sauce tare of this style is virtually always made with wheat-containing shoyu.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All ramen is built on chicken-and-pork-bone broth with back-fat, pork chashu, and menma — no plant-based broth is offered or separated.

cuisine: 鶏豚骨白湯ラーメン（肉白湯そば・鶏中華そば・辛肉まぜそば）
gf_detail: A chicken-pork paitan ramen shop with no gluten-free offerings. Noodles are wheat (sourced from the well-known Teigaku/棣鄂 noodle maker), broth and tare are standard, and mazesoba/fried-chicken sides involve wheat. Not suitable for celiacs; no GF accommodation noted on any source.
vegan_detail: Every bowl is built on an animal base — a rich emulsified chicken-and-pork-bone (鶏豚骨) white broth, with meat toppings and a chicken paste stirred in mid-meal. There are no vegan or vegetarian ramen options; sides include fried chicken and rice. No plant-based menu noted.
address_ja: 京都府京都市北区上賀茂西後藤町11-7
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to lng 135.747925, lat 35.072136 — within the Kyoto bounding box; matches the Kamigamo / Niken-chaya area north of Kamigamo Shrine.
neighborhood: 北区 上賀茂（二軒茶屋・上賀茂神社の北、京都産業大学近く）
seats_text: 18 seats total: 10 counter seats around the open kitchen plus 2 tables (8 seats).
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular closing days)
cultural_comfort_note: A bright, casual neighborhood ramen counter popular with students and families; no English service is indicated, so a Japanese-speaking guide or translation help makes ordering smoothest.

bio.background: Menya Shourin opened on February 10, 2020 in the leafy Kamigamo district of Kyoto's Kita Ward, just north of Kamigamo Shrine near Kyoto Sangyo University. The owner trained at the well-known Kyoto ramen group Kirameki no Tori (キラメキノトリ) before striking out on his own.
bio.specialty: The signature bowl is the niku-paitan soba (肉白湯そば) — an emulsified chicken-and-pork-bone white broth that is full-bodied yet clean and mellow, finished with coarse black pepper. A special chicken paste is meant to be stirred in partway through to deepen the flavor. Straight, medium-thin curly noodles come from the respected Teigaku (棣鄂) noodle maker. The menu rounds out with a tori-chuka soba and a spicy kara-niku mazesoba.
bio.philosophy: Careful broth-making aimed at a rich umami with no off-flavors (雑味のない、まろやかな味), served in a clean, café-like space welcoming to students, families and locals.

anecdotes.text: The shop sits in a nature-rich setting north of Kamigamo Shrine; counter seats wrap around an open kitchen and the room is bright and clean, almost like a café.
The owner is an alumnus of the popular Kirameki no Tori ramen group and opened Shourin in February 2020.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard ramen tare and an emulsified chicken-pork broth are used; no allergen breakdown is published, but a wheat-based shoyu tare is typical for this style.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All bowls are animal-based (chicken-pork bone broth, meat toppings, chicken paste); no vegan or vegetarian preparation exists, so cross-contact is unavoidable for plant-based diners.

caveats: Tabelog lists card, electronic money, and QR payment accepted (not cash-only) and no reservations. The kyotopi article loosely places it in "Sakyo Ward," but the registered address and Tabelog confirm Kita Ward (北区). Hours are daily 11:00-14:00 / 17:00-21:00 with irregular closures (不定休), so check Instagram/X before visiting.

cuisine: 中華そば／背脂醤油（鶏ガラ・魚介の和風だしのあっさり町中華系チューカソバ）
gf_detail: This is a traditional chuka soba / seb-abura shoyu ramen shop. The bowls are built on wheat noodles and a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare, which in Japan is almost always brewed with wheat, over a pork-bone, chicken, kombu and seafood broth. No gluten-free noodles, tare, or accommodations are mentioned in any source, and there is no indication the kitchen handles celiac-level allergen requests. Not suitable for anyone needing to avoid gluten.
vegan_detail: The signature bowls are built on a pork-bone, chicken and seafood (fish/kombu) broth and are topped with seared chashu pork. There are no vegan or vegetarian ramen options described anywhere; the menu centers on animal-based broths and meat toppings.
address_ja: 京都府京都市北区大宮北椿原町26-1
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned coordinates [135.747696, 35.05616] for 京都府京都市北区大宮北椿原町26-1 on the first feature; within the Kyoto box.
seats_text: Counter only, roughly 8 seats (some accounts describe it as around 5); a small, intimate space where you remove your shoes at the entrance.
hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Thu, Fri, Sat 11:30-18:00; Sun & holidays 11:30-15:00; closed Wednesday. Hours can change month to month; the owner posts a monthly calendar on X (Twitter).
closed_days: Wednesday (plus occasional irregular closures; check the owner's monthly calendar)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny shoes-off counter shop run by its owner in a quiet residential pocket; a little Japanese and easygoing solo-diner etiquette go a long way, and English is unlikely.
neighborhood: Kita-ku, near Misonobashi / Kamigamo, closest station area Kitaoji

bio.background: Garamon began life not as a ramen shop but as a charcoal-grill yakitori izakaya called 炭焼賀ら茂ん (Sumiyaki Garamon). Tucked into a quiet residential block south of Misonobashi near Kamigamo, it later changed its business format to a ramen shop while keeping the same name and its shoes-off, lived-in izakaya feel.
bio.specialty: Two bowls anchor the menu, both at the same modest price: a lighter 中華そば (chuka soba) and a richer 背脂醤油 (sebabura shoyu) with back-fat and added oil. The broth blends pork bone, chicken carcass, kombu and seafood, leaning unusually far toward a Japanese dashi character so that it edges toward udon/soba territory while keeping ramen body and sweetness. The signature topping is a thick slice of aburi (seared) chashu, individually torched and brushed with glaze for aroma.
bio.philosophy: An owner-run, single-counter shop designed so one person can comfortably walk in and eat well for cheap; quiet, residential, no-frills, with the kitchen's attention going into the dashi-forward broth and the seared pork rather than spectacle.

anecdotes.text: The ramen concept reportedly grew out of an off-menu (裏メニュー) chicken-based chuka soba that the owner served back when the place was still a yakitori izakaya.
You take your shoes off to step up to the small L-shaped counter, a holdover from its izakaya days that gives the room a homey, almost living-room atmosphere.
Tabletop condiments let you tune your bowl as you go: minced garlic, spicy miso, ichimi chili powder and black pepper.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The shop's two core bowls are shoyu-based ramen (chuka soba and sebabura shoyu). Soy-sauce tare in Japanese ramen is conventionally brewed with wheat; no wheat-free tare is mentioned by any source.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The broth is made from pork bone, chicken, kombu and seafood and the kitchen is built around animal-based ramen, so vegan diners face unavoidable contact with meat and fish stock throughout.

caveats: Hours vary month to month and the shop closes irregularly in addition to Wednesdays; confirm via the owner's monthly calendar on X before going. Cash only, no reservations, very small counter, shoes off at the door. No official website found. No allergen, gluten-free, or vegan information exists in any available source, so GF and vegan assessments are based on the standard composition of shoyu ramen rather than shop-specific statements.

cuisine: 担々麺（辛くない京風担々麺の専門店／汁あり・汁なし）
gf_detail: This is a tantanmen specialist: wheat ramen noodles in a pork-and-chicken sesame broth, with shrimp wonton on the menu — not suitable for celiac diners. The noodles and the sesame/soy-based seasonings both contain wheat, and no gluten-free preparation or menu has been found. Anyone strictly avoiding gluten should not eat here.
vegan_detail: The bowls are built on an animal base — the broth is described as chicken-forward with a little pork, and the menu features shrimp wonton. No vegetarian or vegan version has been found. This is not a vegan-friendly shop.
address_ja: 京都府京都市北区平野上八丁柳町35-4
geocode_note: GSI geocode of the full street address returned [135.730042, 35.032043], well within the Kyoto box; consistent with the Kinugasa / Kitano-Hakubaicho area.
neighborhood: 北区 衣笠（北野白梅町駅近く）
seats_text: Table seating in a small, brightly refurbished room; no private rooms. Exact seat count not published.
hours_raw: 11:30〜21:00（ラストオーダーは閉店30分前頃）／水曜定休
closed_days: 水曜日
cultural_comfort_note: A small, welcoming neighborhood noodle shop with polite service where a friendly greeting and pointing at the menu will carry you through; no English menu has been confirmed.
caveats: The shop appears under two near-identical writings of the name — 坦坦軒 on local coverage and 担担軒 on its listing page — both read "Tantanken"; this record uses the target spelling. Payment is reported as cash-based, though its listing also mentions QR-code payment, so carry cash to be safe. Seat count is not published. Opened October 2025, so the operating pattern is still relatively new.

bio.background: This Kinugasa noodle shop opened in October 2025 in the very space that was long home to a beloved local Chinese restaurant known for its tantanmen. The earlier shop had built a quiet reputation around the neighborhood for its dan dan noodles; when the room was reborn as a dedicated tantanmen specialist, the recipe was carried forward and refined, and the dining room was brightened into something that feels a touch more like a polished Chinese restaurant than a quick noodle stop.
bio.specialty: The house bowl is a deliberately non-spicy, Kyoto-style tantanmen, offered both with broth (汁あり) and brothless (汁なし). The soup is described as smooth and creamy with a gentle sweetness, built on a chicken-forward base with just a little pork and vegetable depth, and served essentially without heat or numbing tongue-tingle. A shrimp wonton noodle bowl rounds out the short menu.
bio.philosophy: Rather than chasing the fiery, mala-heavy style, the shop leans into a mild, comforting take on tantanmen and puts the heat in the diner's hands: the tables are set with sesame oil, garlic chili oil, chili powder, Sichuan pepper, black pepper, salt, soy sauce and vinegar so each guest can dial the bowl up to taste.

anecdotes.text: The shop occupies the former premises of a neighborhood Chinese restaurant that had earned a Michelin Bib Gourmand nod and was locally loved for its tantanmen; the new shop continues and refines that signature bowl while opening for lunch as well as evenings.
The tantanmen arrives with effectively no spiciness, and the table is stocked with a full lineup of condiments so guests can build their own level of heat and aroma.

gf_cross_contamination.text: A tantanmen specialist serving wheat noodles in sesame/soy-seasoned animal broth alongside shrimp wonton; gluten is central to the menu and no gluten-free handling has been described.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soy sauce is provided as a table condiment and the seasoning base is sesame/soy-style; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, and no wheat-free tamari is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The broth is animal-based (chicken with some pork) and shrimp wonton is on the menu, so the kitchen is not set up for vegan separation.

cuisine: 濃厚味噌豚骨／醤油豚骨ラーメン (rich miso tonkotsu / shoyu tonkotsu ramen)
neighborhood: 一乗寺 (Ichijoji), 左京区
address_ja: 京都府京都市左京区一乗寺西杉ノ宮町38-4
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 一乗寺西杉ノ宮町38; within the Kyoto box.
seats_text: 12 counter seats only (one source lists 13); single-operator counter shop.
hours_raw: 11:30–14:30 (lunch), 18:00–21:00 (dinner); closed Tuesdays. Note: some sources list the dinner close as 22:00.
closed_days: Tuesday
cultural_comfort_note: A small ticket-machine counter ramen shop run by one person in a busy ramen district; basic Japanese and the usual order-by-ticket flow make it easiest, though the routine is simple enough to manage by pointing.
vegan_detail: A pork-bone (tonkotsu) ramen specialist; the signature bowls are built on rich pork broth with chashu, egg, and back fat. There is no vegan or vegetable-based ramen offering.
gf_detail: Ramen here is wheat noodles (medium-thick noodles from a noodle factory) in pork-bone broth flavored with miso or soy tare, which typically contains wheat. No gluten-free options and no indication of allergy accommodation; not suitable for celiac diners.
caveats: Cash only; small 12-seat counter that can fill up in a competitive ramen district, so expect waits at peak times. Dinner closing time varies by source (21:00 vs 22:00) — confirm on arrival.

bio.background: Arajin opened on September 10, 2014, on a corner of the Ichijoji intersection — one of Kyoto's most intensely competitive ramen districts. It is a compact, clean, counter-only shop run as essentially a one-person operation, where guests buy a ticket from the machine before ordering.
bio.specialty: The signature bowl is the rich miso tonkotsu ramen (濃厚味噌豚骨ラーメン): a creamy, full-bodied pork-bone broth carrying the depth and aroma of miso, finished with back fat, and rounded out with green onion, thin-sliced chashu, half a seasoned egg, nori, menma, and sesame. A shoyu tonkotsu version is the other pillar of the menu.
bio.philosophy: The cooking leans on careful craftsmanship — a broth made rich without becoming heavy, and medium-thick, hand-kneaded-style noodles specially ordered from an established noodle maker for good chew and slurp. The result is described as crave-worthy and habit-forming.

anecdotes.text: The medium-thick noodles are specially ordered from an established noodle factory, chosen for a firm, chewy texture that stands up to the thick broth.
A side of charred char-siu mayo rice (炙りチャーマヨ飯) rounds out the menu alongside the ramen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The shoyu tonkotsu bowl uses a soy-sauce-based tare, which in Japanese ramen almost always contains wheat; the miso bowl likewise sits on wheat noodles and pork broth.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All ramen is built on pork-bone broth with back fat and chashu; broth and noodles share the same single-operator kitchen, so there is no vegan-safe preparation.

cuisine: 豚骨醤油・醤油中華そば（クリーミーあっさり、九条ネギたっぷりの町中華）
gf_detail: This is a traditional chuka-soba (ramen) shop: wheat noodles in a pork-bone/soy-sauce broth, with a wheat-based shoyu tare. There is no indication of any gluten-free noodle, dedicated equipment, or allergen handling. A celiac or strictly gluten-free diner cannot safely eat the ramen here, and nothing on the menu (ramen, tsukemen, gyoza) is gluten-free. Treat as not suitable.
vegan_detail: The house broth is built on long-simmered pork bones (tonkotsu) and the bowls are crowned with chashu pork, so the core offering is not vegan or vegetarian. There is no listed plant-based broth or noodle option. A vegan diner has no suitable bowl here.
address_ja: 京都府京都市左京区田中関田町2-8
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI address-search on 京都府京都市左京区田中関田町2-8; first feature coordinates [135.776413, 35.029144], within the Kyoto box. Location is near the Hyakumanben intersection, a short walk from Demachiyanagi Station.
seats_text: 9 counter seats facing the kitchen; a compact, casual setup.
hours_raw: 月・火・木〜日 11:00〜翌0:00（一部情報では翌2:00まで）／水曜定休。年始休業 1/1〜1/3。
closed_days: Wednesdays; also closed Jan 1–3 for New Year.
cultural_comfort_note: A small, locals-and-students counter shop near Kyoto University with no English presence; basic Japanese or pointing at the menu will make ordering smooth.
neighborhood: Tanaka-Sekidencho, Sakyo Ward (near Hyakumanben / Demachiyanagi)
caveats: Hours vary slightly by source: most directories list 11:00–24:00, while one notes service as late as 2:00 the following morning, which fits its reputation as a late-night bowl. Payment listed as cash plus QR codes (PayPay, au PAY) but no credit cards. No reservations.

bio.background: A neighborhood ramen counter tucked near the Hyakumanben crossing west of Kyoto University, Takumi has been feeding locals and students for close to two decades. It is the kind of unpretentious machi-chuka spot you slip into for a satisfying, affordable bowl, including late at night.
bio.specialty: The signature is the Takumi Chashu-men: a bowl piled with thin slices of chashu pork arranged like overflowing flower petals, set over a long-simmered pork-bone (tonkotsu) soy-sauce broth that is at once viscous and mellow, without any heavy or off-putting edge. A generous heap of Kujo-style green onion crowns the bowl, cutting the richness so it eats clean and bright. A lighter, clear shoyu chuka-soba is also offered for those who want something more delicate.
bio.philosophy: Comfort over flash: carefully drawn-out broth, an almost extravagant amount of negi, and a friendly counter feel that has kept regulars coming back for years.

anecdotes.text: The shop's most-loved dish is the Takumi Chashu-men, known for chashu sliced thin and fanned out like flower petals spilling over the rim of the bowl, atop a creamy yet clean pork-bone soy-sauce broth.
Open for nearly 20 years and beloved by the neighborhood, the shop runs late, making it a go-to for a post-drinking bowl, and the owner's warm manner is part of why regulars keep returning.

The defining feature is the mountain of green onion: onion lovers rejoice, while those who dislike negi are warned to take note, since the abundant Kujo negi lightens the broth and is the bowl's signature.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard ramen tare for a shop of this style is made with wheat-containing soy sauce, and no gluten-free tare is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is long-simmered pork bone and bowls are topped with chashu pork, so all cooking equipment and broth contact animal products.

cuisine: 京都町中華・からしそば（名店「鳳舞」系譜の京風鶏ガラ・昆布だし中華）
gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The signature からしそば and other noodle dishes use wheat noodles, and the chicken-and-kombu ankake (gravy) tare is seasoned with soy sauce, which is standard wheat-brewed shoyu. No gluten-free menu, gluten-free noodles, or dedicated prep is mentioned by any source. A celiac diner should treat this as not suitable; even non-noodle dishes (shumai, sweet-and-sour pork, spring rolls) are likely to involve soy sauce and shared wheat-handling surfaces. Ask only if cross-contamination risk is acceptable to you.
vegan_detail: This is a Kyoto-Chinese house built around a chicken-bone and kombu broth, with pork (chashu, sweet-and-sour pork, shumai) and seafood (shrimp) running through the signature dishes. The from-scratch ankake gravy that defines the からしそば is chicken-based, so even vegetable-heavy plates are not vegan. No vegan or vegetarian menu is advertised. Not suitable for vegans; a strict vegetarian would need to ask, but options would be very limited.
address_ja: 京都府京都市上京区今出川通室町東入今出川町333 第2高橋ビル5F CD
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI on the chō-level address (今出川町333); point falls in Kamigyō-ku near Imadegawa, within the Kyoto box. Building-level coordinate not separately available.
seats_text: 37 seats (counter and tables); window seats overlook the Imperial Palace / Doshisha University area.
hours_raw: [Mon, Thu–Sun] 11:30–14:30 (L.O. 14:00) / 17:00–21:00 (L.O. 20:00); [Tue, Wed] closed (may open on major holidays).
closed_days: Tuesdays and Wednesdays (may operate during major holiday periods)
cultural_comfort_note: A welcoming, family-friendly Kyoto-Chinese dining room near Imadegawa Station where a few words of Japanese and pointing at the famous からしそば will carry you comfortably; menus and staff are oriented to local diners rather than English speakers.
caveats: Cash only. Closed Tuesdays and Wednesdays. Located on an upper floor of the 第2高橋ビル (sources list both 3F and 5F; the entrance is in the building by Imadegawa Station exit 6). Sit-down restaurant-style portions rather than a quick noodle counter. Not suitable for celiac or vegan diets.

bio.background: 鳳舞楼 carries forward one of Kyoto's most beloved culinary lineages: that of 鳳舞 (Houmai), the legendary Kyoto-Chinese restaurant that closed in 2009 after generations of family celebrations were held within its walls. That tradition traces back to Takahashi Kakitsu (高華吉), the chef credited with inventing Kyoto-style Chinese cooking in the 1920s by lightening Chinese dishes with Japanese dashi; after the original Houmai shuttered, its apprentices fanned out across the city to keep the style alive, a network locals call the '鳳舞系' (Houmai lineage). 鳳舞楼 is part of that lineage, now settled in a bright, modern room steps from Imadegawa Station with views toward the Kyoto Imperial Palace and Doshisha University.
bio.specialty: からしそば (karashi soba) — the dish that made the Houmai name. Rather than a soup noodle, it arrives closer to a glistening seafood-and-vegetable ankake over noodles, the gravy built on chicken bones and kombu dashi, with Japanese mustard worked through so the heat rises gently up the nose rather than hitting the tongue. Alongside it the kitchen turns out refined Kyoto-Chinese standards: shumai, spring rolls, sweet-and-sour pork (古老肉), and chashu noodles.
bio.philosophy: あっさり上品な京中華 — light, refined Kyoto Chinese. The cooking leans on kelp-and-chicken broth for depth instead of heaviness, an approach meant for unhurried, multi-generational meals rather than a fast bowl.

anecdotes.text: The からしそば has drawn national attention as a uniquely Kyoto noodle dish, featured on television as a regional specialty that puzzles outsiders and delights locals.
Longtime Kyotoites describe coming to the Houmai lineage for the same celebratory family dinners they remember at the original 鳳舞 before it closed in 2009.
The Houmai style is defined by a chicken-and-kombu broth gravy — the key difference from mainstream Chinese cooking — a technique handed down from founder Takahashi Kakitsu.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat noodles and a soy-sauce-seasoned chicken/kombu ankake gravy are central to the menu; no gluten-free preparation or separate handling is described by any source.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The savory ankake gravy is built on chicken-and-kombu dashi finished with soy sauce; standard Japanese soy sauce is wheat-brewed, and no wheat-free tamari is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Core broth and gravy are chicken-and-kombu based, and signature dishes feature pork and shrimp; no plant-based broth or vegan menu is offered.

lead: 総代 麺家 あくた川
name_ja: 総代 麺家 あくた川
cuisine: 家系ラーメン（横浜家系・濃厚豚骨醤油） / Yokohama iekei rich tonkotsu-shoyu ramen
gf_detail: This is a Yokohama iekei (家系) ramen shop. The bowl is built on wheat noodles in a pork-bone-and-chicken-bone shoyu broth whose tare is soy-sauce-based (wheat-containing shoyu), topped with pork chashu. No gluten-free noodles, broth, or accommodations are mentioned anywhere. Not safe for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: A tonkotsu (pork-bone) and chicken-bone broth shop. The signature bowl includes pork chashu, and the broth itself is animal-based, so it is not suitable for vegans or vegetarians. The free rice and bean-sprout sides are not a meaningful vegan path here. No plant-based ramen offered.
address_ja: 京都府京都市左京区田中門前町73
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned [135.78093, 35.029804] for 京都府京都市左京区田中門前町73; within the Kyoto box. Matches the Hyakumanben / Demachiyanagi (east of the Hyakumanben intersection, near the Kyoto University main gate) location described in sources.
neighborhood: 左京区 田中門前町（百万遍・出町柳／京大正門前）
seats_text: Seat count not published. Sources describe table seating on both sides at the entrance plus a counter facing the kitchen; a small, busy shop oriented toward student groups.
hours_raw: 11:00-15:00, 17:00-23:00（日曜定休との記載あり / Tabelog lists daily 11:00-15:00 and 17:00-23:00; one guide source notes closed Sundays）
closed_days: Sundays per one local guide; Tabelog lists hours every day and notes hours/closed days may change — confirm before visiting.
cultural_comfort_note: A fast, no-frills student ramen joint with a ticket machine; ordering is smooth with a few words of Japanese (staff ask noodle firmness, broth richness, and back-fat level), but no special accommodation should be expected.
caveats: Hours and closed days may change; cash only and no reservations. No allergen or dietary accommodation information could be confirmed — celiac and vegan/vegetarian diners cannot eat the ramen here.

bio.origin: Yokohama iekei lineage via Ramen Musashi-ya (ラーメン武蔵屋)
bio.background: Founder Akutagawa and his colleague Hirayama (who became a later shop manager) met working at Ramen Musashi-ya, the parent shop in the iekei (家系) tradition. The two set out to bring real Yokohama-style iekei ramen to Kyoto, scouring the city by bicycle until they found the spot on Imadegawa, just east of the Hyakumanben intersection by Kyoto University's main gate, opening on September 8, 2018.
bio.specialty: The standard bowl (並ラーメン) of rich pork-and-chicken-bone tonkotsu-shoyu broth, built iekei-style by continuously replenishing the stock (継ぎ足し). It comes with thick noodles, thick-cut pork loin chashu, spinach, and sheets of nori, with free bean-sprout namul and refillable rice on the side. Diners are asked their preferences for noodle firmness, broth strength, and back fat.
bio.philosophy: Keep it honest and accessible: Akutagawa held the bowl to the parent shop's price point even as he sourced ingredients carefully, absorbing higher costs through tight management so students and locals could afford a serious bowl. He framed the work around how much he could give back to customers.

anecdotes.text: Akutagawa and his former Musashi-ya colleague searched Kyoto by bicycle for the right property before settling on the Imadegawa location near Kyoto University.
He deliberately matched the parent shop's low base price for a ramen despite costlier ingredients, managing costs elsewhere so the bowl stayed affordable.
Since opening, the shop has drawn near-constant lines and become a go-to among Kyoto University students at the Hyakumanben corner.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Iekei ramen here uses wheat noodles boiled in shared water and a soy-sauce (wheat shoyu) tare, so gluten is intrinsic to the dish and unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is a pork-bone soy-sauce (豚骨醤油) base typical of iekei, meaning wheat-containing shoyu is in the tare.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is pork-bone and chicken-bone based with pork chashu; no plant-based broth or noodles are available, so the dish is inherently non-vegan.

cuisine: 京都ラーメン（背脂醤油の昔ながらの中華そば／町中華系）
neighborhood: 山科区 大塚南溝町（東野駅・京阪山科エリア）
vegan_detail: This is a traditional Kyoto chuka-soba shop whose single signature bowl is built on a pork-bone and chicken (豚骨・鶏ガラ) broth finished with sweet pork back-fat (背脂) and a soy tare. The menu centers on this one animal-based ramen, so there is no vegan option. A plant-based diner cannot eat here.
gf_detail: A classic wheat-noodle chuka soba: the noodles are wheat, the soy tare is almost certainly wheat-based shoyu, and the broth uses pork and chicken. No gluten-free menu, no published allergen handling, and no indication of any wheat-free preparation. Not safe for a celiac diner.
address_ja: 京都府京都市山科区大塚南溝町36
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates [135.824219, 34.979626] for 京都府京都市山科区大塚南溝町36; within the Kyoto bounding box.
seats_text: 18 seats total: 12 counter seats plus one table seating 6.
hours_raw: Tuesday–Friday 11:00–15:00; closed Monday, Saturday, Sunday. Closes early when the day's noodles/ingredients run out (麺・食材が無くなり次第終了). Note: some guides list a longer through-service window of 11:00–18:00 until sell-out; the shop sells out frequently, so arriving early is advised.
closed_days: Monday, Saturday, Sunday (and early close on sell-out)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, long-running local counter shop with a Japanese-only menu and no English support; a few words of Japanese and an easygoing, order-and-eat attitude will serve you best.

bio.background: Yonakiya is one of the old guard of Kyoto ramen, a shop that has stood along the Nara-kaido road in the Otsuka district of Yamashina for decades and is treated as one of the originals of the city's ramen lineage. It was founded by the previous master and has since passed to the next generation, who carries on the same single-bowl tradition.
bio.specialty: Chuka soba: relatively thin, springy straight noodles in a soy-based broth drawn from pork bone and chicken (豚骨・鶏ガラ), topped with sweet pork back-fat (背脂) that floats on the surface. The clean, delicate dashi is carried by the strength of the soy tare, so a bowl that looks rich eats surprisingly light. The shop is known for slicing its big chashu fresh to order.
bio.philosophy: A one-bowl shop in the truest sense: it does essentially one thing, the old Kyoto chuka soba, and has done it the same way long enough that decades of consistency on a single corner are taken as proof of the cooking.

anecdotes.text: Regulars treat it as a kind of phantom shop because it serves only in daytime and closes the moment the day's noodles run out, so getting a bowl is partly a matter of timing.
The founding master handed the shop to the next generation, who now runs it while keeping the same chuka soba at the center of the menu.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Single-product wheat-noodle ramen shop with no gluten-free menu or published allergen handling; wheat noodles, wheat-based soy tare and a shared broth/water setup make cross-contamination effectively certain.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The defining seasoning is a soy (醤油) tare, which in a traditional Kyoto chuka soba shop is virtually always standard wheat-containing shoyu.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The single bowl is built on pork-bone and chicken broth with pork back-fat and chashu; there is no plant-based preparation and no separate vegan handling.

caveats: Hours and signature-bowl price differ across sources: Tabelog lists Tue–Fri 11:00–15:00 (closed Mon/Sat/Sun) while local guides cite an 11:00–18:00 through-service until sell-out; quoted chuka soba price ranges from roughly 800 to 1,100 yen across years. Tabelog notes second-generation succession; one guide describes the successor as the founder's daughter. Confirm day-of by phone (075-592-9800). No official website found.

lead: メントメシ ザコヤ
name_ja: メントメシ ザコヤ
cuisine: 魚介系ラーメン（肉類不使用・鯛など魚のアラ・煮干しの淡麗スープ）
gf_detail: This is a wheat-noodle ramen shop with house-made noodles, and the tare is almost certainly wheat-based soy sauce. No gluten-free menu, gluten-free noodle, or allergen accommodation was found on any page. Not suitable for celiacs. The broth is fish-based rather than pork, but the noodles and tare make the bowl unsafe for gluten avoidance.
vegan_detail: Despite being meat-free (no pork or chicken), every dish is built on fish and seafood — dashi from sea bream, scallop, ribbonfish and dried sardines, with fish toppings and a seasoned egg. Not vegan or vegetarian.
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区東洞院通六角上る三文字町225 朝陽ビル 1F
geocode_note: Full-address geocode returned an unrelated point (東八幡町); used chō-level geocode for 三文字町 ([135.752716, 35.004814]), which falls within the Kyoto box and matches the Higashidoin-Rokkaku / Karasuma-Oike area.
seats_text: Approximately 20 seats; counter and table seating.
closed_days: Wednesdays and Thursdays
cultural_comfort_note: A small Japanese-language counter-and-table ramen shop in a Kyoto business district; a friendly greeting and pointing at the menu will carry you, but don't expect English support.
neighborhood: 中京区 東洞院六角 / 烏丸御池駅
caveats: The original 百万遍本店 (Hyakumanben main store) and a 祇園店 (Gion branch) have closed; this Higashidoin-Rokkaku location near Karasuma-Oike opened/relocated in February 2024 and is the currently operating shop. The signature 漁師めし rice bowl is known to sell out by lunchtime.

bio.background: Mentomeshi Zakoya is part of the Zakoya group (ZACOYA), a Kyoto seafood-focused hospitality brand running a cluster of fish-centric restaurants around Karasuma, Sanjo and Gion. The ramen concept began at a Hyakumanben main store and a Gion branch; both have since closed, and the shop relocated to its current Higashidoin-Rokkaku spot near Karasuma-Oike in February 2024.
bio.specialty: The signature bowl is ryoshi-soba (fisherman's soba, around 1,200 yen) - a clear, light seafood ramen with a dashi built on sea bream (tai), scallop, ribbonfish (tachiuo) and dried sardines (niboshi), house-made noodles, and toppings of marinated and seared fish, a seasoned egg, minced red onion and nori. The companion ryoshi-meshi rice bowl, with around a dozen seafood ingredients, is eaten in four stages: as a seafood bowl, then with egg yolk, then poured with hot dashi as ochazuke, finished alongside sea bream sashimi.
bio.philosophy: A deliberately meat-free take on ramen: the whole bowl is built from the sea rather than pork or chicken, staying true to the Zakoya group's fish-forward identity. A playful touch is the chopstick rest - a dried sardine you can eat on its own or drop into the broth.

anecdotes.text: The chopstick rest is an actual dried sardine (niboshi) that diners are invited to eat directly or add to the ramen broth.
The ryoshi-meshi rice bowl often sells out by lunchtime, and is designed to be enjoyed in four progressive stages, finishing as a dashi ochazuke with sea bream sashimi.

gf_cross_contamination.text: House-made wheat ramen noodles are the base of the dish; no gluten-free option was found.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: A soy-sauce seasoned seafood bowl and shoyu ramen are on the menu; the tare is presumed conventional wheat-based soy sauce. No wheat-free tare is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Entirely seafood-based kitchen with dashi from sea bream, scallop, ribbonfish and dried sardines plus fish toppings and a seasoned egg, so any dish involves fish and likely egg.

positives.text: All dishes are meat-free (no pork or chicken), built entirely on fish and seafood.

cuisine: 濃厚鶏ガラこってり系ラーメン（総本店限定の牛すじラーメンあり）
gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The kotteri broth is built on chicken bones and vegetables, but the ramen is served with standard wheat noodles and a wheat-based shoyu tare, and the kitchen also fries gyoza, karaage and other wheat items in shared equipment. No gluten-free noodles, broth, or accommodation is offered, and there is no dedicated fryer. A celiac diner cannot eat the ramen here.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The signature broth is a long-simmered chicken-bone (鶏ガラ) and vegetable kotteri base, and the flagship-only specials are beef-tendon (牛すじ) ramen and pork rice (豚重). There are no plant-based broth, noodle, or topping options.
address_ja: 京都府京都市左京区一乗寺築田町94 メゾン白川1F
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned coordinates for 京都府京都市左京区一乗寺築田町94; within the Kyoto bounding box.
neighborhood: 左京区 一乗寺（茶山・京都芸術大学駅）
seats_text: 42 seats (counter and tables); 22-space parking lot.
hours_raw: 11:00～翌1:00（L.O. 翌0:45 / 閉店15分前）。定休日：なし（元日のみ休み）
closed_days: No regular closing day; closed only on January 1st (New Year's Day).
cultural_comfort_note: A famous, high-volume chain flagship open until 1 a.m.; ordering is simple and tourist-friendly, but staff communication is mostly in Japanese so a few basic words or pointing at the menu goes a long way.
caveats: Cash plus electronic money and QR-code payments are accepted, but credit cards are not. No reservations. As the chain's flagship, lines are common at peak times.

bio.background: This is the sohonten — the original headquarters — of Tenkaippin, the cult chicken-broth ramen chain that began in Kyoto. In November 1971, founder Tsutomu Kimura, having lost his job when the gallery company he worked for went bankrupt, had a friend build him a ramen cart out of scrap metal and started pulling a yatai (food cart) around the Ginkaku-ji area. On opening night he reportedly sold only 11 bowls. He kept experimenting, and after three years and nine months he perfected the now-legendary kotteri broth. With the goodwill of a stone-mason whose lot he was using, the cart was fixed in place and grew into his first storefront in August 1975 — on this Ichijoji site, which remains the chain's spiritual home.
bio.specialty: The signature is the kotteri (こってり) bowl: a thick, almost potage-like broth simmered from chicken bones (鶏ガラ) and eleven kinds of vegetables until it clings to the noodles — described as a soup you eat rather than drink, yet lighter on the palate than its richness suggests. The Ichijoji flagship is also the only Tenkaippin where you can order the chairman's own creations: 牛すじラーメン (sweet-savory braised beef-tendon ramen), 牛すじキムチラーメン, and the 豚重 pork-rice set.
bio.philosophy: Kimura's idea was a broth that is 'eaten, not drunk' — a slow-cooked chicken-and-vegetable kotteri so concentrated it behaves like a sauce. The beef-tendon special was developed personally by the chairman over many trial batches, balancing the sweet-savory tendon to harmonize with that signature soup, and the recipe is kept exclusive to the sohonten.

anecdotes.text: Founder Tsutomu Kimura started in 1971 with a roughly 37,000-yen scrap-metal cart and sold just 11 bowls on his first night before refining the recipe over three years and nine months.

The flagship-only beef-tendon ramen was developed by chairman Kimura himself more than a decade ago; staff note the tendon seasoning is blended specifically to match the kotteri soup, and it became popular as soon as it appeared on the menu.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles and wheat-based shoyu tare are core to every bowl; the same kitchen also handles gyoza, karaage and fried rice, so cross-contact with gluten is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The standard ramen tare is soy-sauce based, which in Japan is normally brewed with wheat; no wheat-free tare is offered.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The base broth is simmered from chicken bones; flagship specials use beef tendon and pork, so no preparation surface or stock is free of animal products.

lead: とことんとりコトコト
name_ja: とことんとりコトコト
cuisine: Ramen — ultra-rich chicken paitan (鶏白湯), a ladle-standing thick broth
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The ramen uses wheat noodles (from Kyoto noodle maker Teikaku/棣鄂) and the broth is built on chicken and vegetables with a typical tare; donburi sets add chashu and other components. No gluten-free menu, dedicated prep, or allergen accommodation is mentioned by any source. Treat as unsafe for celiac diners; ask in person if highly sensitive.
vegan_detail: Not vegan or vegetarian. The signature bowl is a chicken-based paitan broth (chicken simmered with vegetables); donburi sets include house-made chashu, egg yolk and shrimp oil. Although a large quantity of vegetables goes into the broth, it is fundamentally animal-based. No vegan or vegetarian option is offered.
address_ja: 京都府京都市南区西九条比永城町26-2
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 西九条比永城町26; within the Kyoto box. Location is near Kintetsu Toji Station / the Kujo-Omiya bus stop.
seats_text: 16 counter seats only
hours_raw: Tue, Fri-Sun: 11:00-15:00; Wed-Thu: 11:00-15:00 (L.O. 15:00) and 18:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:30). Closed Monday. Lunch service daily except Monday; dinner service only on Wednesday and Thursday.
closed_days: Monday
cultural_comfort_note: A small family-run counter ramen shop where ordering and a few words of Japanese will go further than English; a friendly, child-welcoming spot but not set up for English support.

bio.background: A husband-and-wife team who began as a pop-up in the Fukakusa area, running for about three months before opening this permanent shop near Toji Station in January 2021, taking over a former Chinese-restaurant space in Minami Ward. The couple draw on their own experience raising children to run a warm, family-friendly counter.
bio.specialty: An exceptionally concentrated chicken paitan (torigara hakutou) — the signature 'Tokoton Tori'. Chicken and a generous amount of vegetables are simmered thoroughly and gently for a long time (the literal meaning of the shop's name) into a broth so thick a ladle can stand upright in it. It is rich in collagen — enough that it sets when cooled — yet the vegetables keep the finish surprisingly clean rather than heavy. Flat noodles from Kyoto noodle maker Teikaku cling to the dense soup; sets pair the bowl with a donburi of house-made chashu, scallion, egg yolk and fried-onion shrimp oil, and fish-powder, chili and mazesoba variations are also offered.
bio.philosophy: The name itself is the philosophy: to thoroughly and patiently slow-simmer the chicken. The owners describe themselves as still refining the recipe, treating the bowl as a work in continual improvement.

anecdotes.text: The shop name spells out its method — 'tokoton' (thoroughly) plus 'tori' (chicken) simmered 'kotokoto' (gently bubbling away) — capturing the long, slow cook that gives the broth its body.
Started as a three-month pop-up in Fukakusa before settling into a former Chinese restaurant near Toji Station in January 2021.
The broth carries so much collagen that it solidifies when cooled, yet the vegetables simmered alongside the chicken keep it from feeling oily or cloying.

caveats: Phone is non-public per the listing. Dinner service (18:00-22:00) runs only on Wednesday and Thursday; all other open days are lunch-only. Hours and the lunch last-order should be reconfirmed seasonally. No allergen/gluten/vegan accommodation is documented by any source — treat GF and vegan as unavailable.

cuisine: 魚介×鶏の旨塩・醤油らーめん（無鉄砲出身／自家製麺）
neighborhood: 宇治田原町その他（京都南郊）
vegan_detail: A meat-and-fish ramen shop: the broth is built from chicken bones, whole chicken and vegetables combined with a seafood stock of clams, kombu, dried anchovies (niboshi) and mackerel. No vegan or vegetarian ramen is offered, and the dashi itself is animal-based. A vegan diner cannot eat the ramen here.
gf_detail: House-made noodles are made from 100% Hokkaido wheat flour, and the shio/shoyu tare almost certainly contains wheat-based soy sauce. The bowls are fundamentally wheat-based and not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners. No gluten-free option is advertised.
address_ja: 京都府綴喜郡宇治田原町南亥子90-1
geocode_note: GSI geocoding of 京都府綴喜郡宇治田原町南亥子90-1 returns [lng 135.85643, lat 34.8508] (chō-level confirms ~135.8549, 34.8494). This is a genuine, consistent result for Ujitawara Town, but it falls outside the configured Kyoto box (lat 34.93–35.13, lng 135.66–135.83) because Ujitawara lies in the far-southern rural fringe of Kyoto Prefecture not covered by that box. Coordinates set to null per instructions; true location is southern Ujitawara.
seats_text: About 22–24 seats: 8 counter seats, one 4-person table, and tatami (zashiki) seating (around two 6-person areas).
closed_days: 火曜日（Tuesdays）
cultural_comfort_note: A small owner-run countryside ramen shop with a ticket-machine ordering system and no advertised English; basic spoken Japanese and pointing will carry you through comfortably.
caveats: Cash/食券 (ticket machine) only — no cards, e-money or QR payments. No reservations. Lunch is the main service; dinner runs only on Friday and Saturday. The Kiramu chicken-paitan bowl is limited to about 20 servings a day and sells out. Rural location in southern Ujitawara with parking (around a dozen spaces) — effectively a drive-to destination.

bio.background: Owner-chef Okada is a Kyoto native from Kizugawa City who grew up obsessed with ramen, haunting local institutions like Yokozuna, Shinza and Daiichi Asahi. After early years chasing music and freelance work, the 2008 financial crisis pushed him to rethink his path and commit to ramen. At 26 he joined the famous Kizugawa tonkotsu powerhouse Mutetsupou (無鉄砲), aiming to go independent within three years; he ended up staying about five years, working across its Kyoto headquarters and Tokyo branches. He opened his own shop, Menya Kiramu, on Aichi's Chita Peninsula in 2017, then brought it home to Kyoto, reopening in his native region at Ujitawara on July 29, 2023 — even working a spell at Mutetsupou's Nara affiliate while waiting on the new property.
bio.origin: Kizugawa City, Kyoto (owner); shop relocated from Aichi to Ujitawara Town
bio.specialty: Despite his Mutetsupou tonkotsu pedigree, Kiramu's heart is a clean, layered bowl: the most popular is the shio (salt) ramen, alongside a soy-sauce version, built on a double soup that marries an animal-based chicken broth (chicken bones, whole chicken, vegetables) with a seafood dashi of clams, kombu, dried anchovies and mackerel. A limited daily run (~20 bowls) of a richer chicken-paitan ramen is the prized extra. Noodles are made in-house (自家製麺) from 100% Hokkaido wheat, including a fragrant whole-grain variation.
bio.philosophy: Okada says what matters most are the encounters and relationships ramen creates, and he wants Kiramu to be a shop that quietly accompanies daily life (日常に寄り添うお店). He leans on local ingredients from the Kizugawa and Joyo area to make a flavor he feels could only come from this place.

anecdotes.text: Okada trained for about five years at Mutetsupou (無鉄砲), the celebrated Kizugawa tonkotsu shop, working its Kyoto headquarters and Tokyo branches, and credits the owner Akasako and staff with teaching him both technique and a mindfulness toward every customer — yet for his own shop he chose a clear, restrained broth rather than thick tonkotsu.

The Aichi shop first opened in 2017 after Okada struggled to find a spot in Nagoya, settling on the Chita Peninsula; he later moved the whole operation back to his Kyoto hometown region, reopening in Ujitawara in July 2023 on a site with about a dozen parking spaces.
The shop frames its cooking around local Kizugawa/Joyo ingredients and an unflavored (無化調) double soup, pairing seafood and chicken stocks with house-made Hokkaido-wheat noodles.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The shio and shoyu tare are standard ramen seasonings; the soy-sauce (shoyu) version in particular relies on wheat-based soy sauce, and no gluten-free tare is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The base double soup is animal-based (chicken bones, whole chicken plus a seafood dashi of clams, niboshi and mackerel), so all bowls share an animal/fish stock with no plant-based alternative.

cuisine: アキラ系 豚骨醤油 中華そば（レトロ町ラーメン）
neighborhood: 伏見区淀・納所（京阪 淀駅／京都競馬場前）
gf_detail: This is a classic Showa-era Chinese-noodle (中華そば) shop built entirely around wheat. The bowls use standard wheat ramen noodles in a pork-bone soy-sauce broth, and the tare is almost certainly a wheat-based shoyu; noodles can even be swapped for udon (also wheat), and sides include yakisoba, yaki-udon and fried rice. There is no gluten-free menu, no indication of dedicated equipment, and shared boiling water and woks make cross-contact unavoidable. Not suitable for celiac diners; a gluten-sensitive diner should not eat here.
vegan_detail: A meat-and-broth ramen shop: the soup is a pork-bone (豚骨) soy-sauce base and the signature bowl is chashu-men piled with sliced pork. There is no vegan or vegetable broth option mentioned anywhere; even the bean-sprout and green-onion toppings sit in/on a pork stock. Not a place a vegan can eat a complete dish.
address_ja: 京都府京都市伏見区納所岸ノ下24-10
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returns [135.724976, 34.914501] for 納所岸ノ下 (full-address lookup gave [135.725037, 34.914768]). This is geographically accurate for southern Fushimi/Yodo near the Kyoto Racecourse, but lat ~34.914 falls below the specified Kyoto bounding box (34.93–35.13), so coordinates are nulled per instruction. The true location is the old Keihan-kokudo roadside near the Kyoto Racecourse parking entrance, about a 13–20 min walk from Keihan Yodo Station.
seats_text: No exact seat count published. Small retro shop with two seating types: standard table seats and a raised tatami (小上がり座敷) area; it fills to near-capacity within about 10 minutes of opening.
hours_raw: 10:00–14:00（麺が売り切れ次第閉店）。日曜・祝日定休。1日わずか4時間の営業。
