closed_days: 日曜・祝日（Sundays and public holidays）。麺が売り切れ次第早じまいすることあり。
cultural_comfort_note: A no-frills local lunch counter where regulars (construction workers, office staff) come and go fast; little to no English, so a few words of Japanese and a point-at-the-menu approach make the visit smooth.
caveats: Open only 10:00–14:00 (four hours) and closes early once the day's noodles sell out, so arrive near opening. Cash only. Closed Sundays and holidays.

bio.background: Hakkoken is a Showa-era neighborhood noodle shop that has been feeding the Yodo district of southern Fushimi for more than thirty years, tucked along the old Keihan-kokudo road near the Kyoto Racecourse. Step inside and it feels like a time-slip: worn tables, a raised tatami corner, and the unhurried rhythm of a true machi-chuka where locals have eaten the same bowl for decades.
bio.specialty: The house bowl is an 'Akira-style' tonkotsu-shoyu chuka-soba: a clear, refined pork-bone-and-soy broth that is gentle and low on salt — light enough to eat at breakfast — with medium-thick straight noodles served soft (firmer on request). The crowd favorite is the chashu-men, where thin slices of pork belly and lean chashu are buried under a generous mound of green onion and bean sprouts; regulars wrap the vegetables around the pork or eat it over rice.
bio.philosophy: Big portions, kind prices (most bowls under 1,000 yen), and an old-school 'eat it your way' generosity — diners can swap the ramen noodles for udon, add a tamago-toji egg finish, or order it like a humble diner dish.

anecdotes.text: Locals describe the tonkotsu-shoyu as reminiscent of the legendary Kyoto institution Daiichi Asahi — a clear, comforting broth in the classic Kyoto lineage.
In 2025 the shop drew fresh attention after being featured on the TV program Omouma-i Mise for its summer-only giant cold-noodle special — a 1.7 kg mountain of reimen for 1,200 yen.
It runs on an almost defiantly short schedule — just four hours a day, 10:00 to 14:00, closing early whenever the noodles run out — and fills to near-capacity within ten minutes of opening.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Built around wheat ramen noodles in a shared boiling pot; ramen can also be swapped for udon and sides include yakisoba/yaki-udon and fried rice, so wheat is everywhere and cross-contact is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is a soy-sauce (shoyu) tonkotsu base; standard Japanese shoyu/tare contains wheat, and no wheat-free soy sauce is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Soup is a pork-bone (豚骨) stock and the signature bowl is topped with sliced pork chashu; vegetable toppings are served in/on the pork broth.

lead: 中華そば専門店 ふくなが
name_ja: 中華そば専門店 ふくなが
cuisine: 濃厚醤油 中華そば（国産食材・自家製麺）
gf_detail: This is a traditional Kyoto-style rich-soy chuka soba shop. The noodles are wheat, the tare is a soy-based shoyu (almost certainly containing wheat), and the broth is a concentrated pork-bone (tonkotsu) stock. No gluten-free menu, no gluten-free noodles, and no published allergen accommodation. Not suitable for anyone requiring strict celiac-level avoidance of wheat.
vegan_detail: Every signature bowl is built on an animal foundation: a rich pork-bone and soy broth, topped with chashu (pork). Side dishes (fried rice, gyoza, karaage, roast pork bowl) are also meat-based. The "京都タンメン" is a vegetable ramen but is served in the same animal broth, so it is not vegan. There are no published plant-based options.
address_ja: 京都府京都市南区久世東土川町291-3
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI from the full address; coordinates [135.722809, 34.946548] fall within the Kyoto box. Note: the shop's mailing address is Minami-ku, Kyoto City (久世東土川町), right on the border with Mukou City; it is commonly described as being in the Mukomachi area, about a 17-18 minute walk from JR Mukomachi Station.
neighborhood: 向日市・向日町（JR 向日町駅／京都南西部、京都市南区久世）
seats_text: 20席（カウンター2×2、テーブル4名×4卓）
closed_days: 月曜（Monday）
cultural_comfort_note: A bustling local ramen counter where ordering is straightforward but staff communication is in Japanese; basic Japanese or pointing at the menu makes for a smooth visit.
caveats: Hours vary by day and sources disagree on Friday/Saturday evening: Tabelog lists 17:00-20:00, while the shop's own materials and local blogs describe late-night weekend service to around 2:00 (L.O. 1:30). Confirm current hours before a late visit. The hours_week field uses the conservative Tabelog evening close (20:00). Owner's personal name not published; chef-training detail (Shinpuku Saikan, ~10 years) is from a local food-media article, not the shop itself.

bio.origin: Opened in 2020 in the Kuze district of Kyoto's Minami-ku, on the edge of Mukou City near JR Mukomachi Station.
bio.background: Before opening his own shop, the owner spent roughly ten years training at one of Kyoto's most storied ramen institutions, Shinpuku Saikan (新福菜館) at Takabashi — the legendary home of jet-black soy chuka soba. He carries that Kyoto rich-soy lineage forward while adding his own refinements.
bio.specialty: The flagship bowl is the 醤油中華そば (rich-soy chuka soba), a concentrated pork-bone-and-soy broth that drinks surprisingly clean, finished with Kyoto's prized Kujo green onions. Other draws include the 宇宙一のまぜそば ("best-in-the-universe" maze soba), 濃厚担々麺, the vegetable-laden 京都タンメン, and a much-loved fried-rice set.
bio.philosophy: All vegetables and ingredients are domestically sourced, and the broth itself is the product of constant tinkering — described on the shop's own site as a rich-soy soup 'developed through improvement upon improvement' to land on a deep flavor with a light, clean finish.

anecdotes.text: The owner apprenticed for about ten years at the famous Shinpuku Saikan in Takabashi, Kyoto, before striking out on his own in 2020.
Unusually for the area, the shop offers morning ramen (asa-ra) — opening as early as 9:00 on Sundays and 10:00 most other days — so locals can start the day with a bowl.
The shop frames its signature broth as the result of repeated refinement: 'a rich-soy soup, light on the finish, developed through improvement upon improvement.'

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is a rich soy (shoyu) tare; Japanese soy sauce of this style virtually always contains wheat. No wheat-free tare is offered.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All bowls share a pork-bone broth and the kitchen handles pork chashu, gyoza and karaage; there is no separate vegan preparation.

positives.text: All vegetables and ingredients are domestically sourced, and the shop makes its own noodles in-house.

cuisine: 京都ラーメン（豚骨醤油 中華そば）・餃子
neighborhood: 左京区 聖護院（神宮丸太町駅／熊野神社前）
gf_detail: This is a traditional ramen and gyoza shop. The bowls are built on wheat ramen noodles in a pork-bone broth with a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare that is almost certainly wheat-based, and gyoza wrappers are wheat — so the menu is not suitable for celiac diners. No gluten-free menu, ingredient labeling, or allergy accommodation was found in any source. A celiac diner should not eat here.
vegan_detail: The shop's identity is chashu (braised pork) ramen over a pork-bone broth; gyoza are pork-filled and the bowls are topped with pork and egg. There are no vegetable or plant-based broth options noted anywhere. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
address_ja: 京都府京都市左京区聖護院山王町43-20
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned [135.778, 35.016899] for the full street address, within the Kyoto box.
seats_text: 21 seats: counter 7, plus 2-person tables (4) and 3-person tables (2).
closed_days: Monday (月曜定休)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, old-school neighborhood counter shop where ordering in Japanese (or by pointing at the set menus) is easiest; no indication of English support.

bio.background: Despite carrying the famous Daiichi Asahi name, this Kumano shop is not part of the celebrated honke (head shop) founded in 1947 by Kyoto Station's Takabashi. It is a survivor of an old chain expansion: when the original franchising company folded, individual shops like this one carried on independently, keeping the name and the recipes they had been built around. A yellowed signboard from that era — roughly four decades old — still hangs out front, a tangible piece of Kyoto ramen history.
bio.specialty: A Kyoto-style chuka soba: a pork-bone broth that reads surprisingly light and clean despite its richness, carrying a soy tare and a faint sheen of back-fat in the classic Kyoto manner. Bowls come piled with generous chashu — diners can choose leaner thigh or richer belly cuts — over medium-thin straight noodles, with bean sprouts, scallion and a soft egg. Spicy miso can be stirred in partway through to shift the flavor.
bio.philosophy: An unpretentious, value-driven neighborhood ramen-ya: hearty, chashu-forward bowls under 1,000 yen in a retro counter setting that locals have leaned on for years.

anecdotes.text: The shop is best understood as an independent descendant of the Daiichi Asahi lineage rather than a branch of the famous head shop — when the old chain company went under, locations like Kumano became their own establishments while keeping the name.
A roughly 40-year-old, yellowed signboard from the original chain era still hangs at the shop, evoking its place in Kyoto's ramen history.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The bowls are seasoned with a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare in the Kyoto chuka-soba style; standard shoyu is wheat-based, so the broth should be treated as containing wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is built around a pork-bone broth, pork chashu and pork gyoza; there is no separate vegetable preparation, so cross-contact with animal products is unavoidable.

caveats: No official website was found; details drawn from Tabelog and local food media. No dietary/allergy information is published, so gluten-free and vegan claims are inferred from the dish type and should be confirmed in person. Hours reflect listed regular schedule; one aggregator listed it as open year-round, but Tabelog and local sources indicate a Monday closing day.

cuisine: 町中華・中華そば（ラーメン提供あり）
neighborhood: 左京区・出町柳（吉田牛ノ宮町、京大近く／出町柳駅から徒歩約9分）
gf_detail: A classic neighborhood town-Chinese counter serving wheat-noodle 中華そば (ramen) and fried rice. The bowls rely on wheat noodles and, almost certainly, a wheat-based shoyu tare in a pork/Chinese-style broth, with shared woks and a fry station — not celiac-safe and no gluten-free preparation or menu is offered. No allergy program advertised; a celiac should not eat here.
vegan_detail: A meat-and-egg town-Chinese spot: the ramen and fried rice are built on pork/animal broth, lard, egg and Chinese seasonings. No vegan or clearly plant-based dish is offered or noted, and there is no separate-pan handling, so vegans cannot reliably eat here.
address_ja: 京都府京都市左京区吉田牛ノ宮町8-1
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned the first feature for 京都府京都市左京区吉田牛ノ宮町8番地 at [135.777756, 35.024956], within the Kyoto bounding box.
seats_text: 約40席（テーブル席と座敷あり）
closed_days: 土曜・日曜（祝日も休みの場合あり）。金曜は昼営業のみ。
cultural_comfort_note: A no-frills local Chinese counter near Kyoto University with cash-only ordering, set lunch/dinner routines, and posted house rules (including no photos or social media inside), so it is easiest to enjoy with some Japanese and a quiet, follow-the-flow approach.

bio.background: A weathered, easy-to-miss town-Chinese shop tucked into the old streets behind Demachiyanagi, near Kyoto University. Its plain machiya-style frontage blends into the neighborhood, but the room inside is surprisingly roomy with tables and a tatami area, and it draws a steady crowd of students and locals.
bio.specialty: Cheap, generous everyday Chinese: a simple Chinese-style ramen and fried rice, often ordered together as a set, plus big-portion plates like a heaping tenshinhan (crab-omelet rice).
bio.philosophy: Honest, filling, wallet-friendly cooking with no pretension - everything sits comfortably under 1,000 yen, and the shop keeps a calm, focused mealtime atmosphere.

anecdotes.text: Regulars describe it as a hidden-gem Chinese place found while wandering from Demachiyanagi - unassuming outside, roomier than expected inside, with kind staff and a calm mood.
The shop posts firm house rules and asks that customers not take photos inside or post about the food on social media, keeping the focus on eating.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Town-Chinese kitchen using shared woks/pans and a single fry station, so wheat and other allergens cross freely; no gluten-free handling indicated.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Chinese-style ramen and stir-fry seasoning almost certainly use standard wheat-based shoyu/Chinese sauces; no wheat-free tare or sauce noted.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth, fried rice and plates are built on pork/animal fat and egg; no separate vegan preparation available.

caveats: Cash only; no reservations. House rules prohibit photos and social-media posts inside — be discreet. Friday is lunch-only; closed Saturday/Sunday (and possibly holidays). Most details (no official site, no listed phone) come from a single aggregator listing, so confirm hours before a special trip. Not suitable for celiac or vegan diners.

cuisine: 二郎系まぜそば・ラーメン（和牛脂使用／汁あり・汁なし、塩・醤油）
neighborhood: 中京区・四条大宮（壬生坊城町、阪急大宮駅近く）
gf_detail: This is a Jiro-style ramen and mazesoba shop built on wheat noodles (thick, wide noodles from the Teigaku/established noodle maker) plus tare that is offered in a soy-sauce (醤油) variant. No gluten-free menu, dedicated equipment, or celiac accommodation is mentioned anywhere. Even the salt (塩) variant uses wheat noodles boiled in shared water. This is not a safe choice for anyone with celiac disease or a wheat allergy.
vegan_detail: The entire concept is built around wagyu (Kobe/Omi beef) fat, with beef Achilles tendon replacing the usual pork chashu. The broth and tare are animal-based. There is no vegan or vegetarian bowl, and no plant-based options are advertised. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区壬生坊城町8-27
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned coordinates for 京都府京都市中京区壬生坊城町8-27; first feature geometry.coordinates=[135.747009, 35.004257], within the Kyoto bounding box.
seats_text: Counter seating only (17 seats; expanded from 7 seats in November 2025). No private rooms.
hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Fri, Sat, Sun, holidays: 11:00-14:00, 17:00-23:00. Closed Wednesday and Thursday.
closed_days: Wednesday and Thursday
cultural_comfort_note: A small counter-only Jiro-style shop with a QR-code/cashless ordering system and a local, retro feel; a little Japanese or a translation app will smooth the order, and knowing Jiro-style topping customs (garlic/portion calls) helps.
caveats: Recently opened (September 2025) and expanded seating in November 2025, so hours and capacity may still shift. Cash and PayPay accepted via a QR-code ordering system.

bio.background: Menya Kanae opened in September 2025 in the Shijo-Omiya area of Kyoto's Nakagyo ward, tucked inside a converted traditional machiya house within the "Wagyu Kaikan" building, a short walk from Hankyu Omiya Station. It quickly drew attention as a new kind of Jiro-style shop for the neighborhood.
bio.specialty: The signature bowl is "KANAE no Jiro" (around 1,000 yen), a Jiro-style ramen offered four ways: with or without broth (汁あり/汁なし) and in salt (塩) or soy-sauce (醤油). What sets it apart is the use of premium wagyu fat (Kobe and Omi beef) in place of the usual pork back-fat, and beef Achilles tendon instead of pork chashu, with no cabbage. The thick, wide Jiro-style noodles come from the established Teigaku noodle maker.
bio.philosophy: The shop's distinguishing pursuit is wagyu fat, a difficult cut to source, prized here for its mellow sweetness and clean finish compared with heavier pork fat. The salt version is recommended as smooth and round, letting the fat's umami come forward, making this a Jiro style that even newcomers find approachable.

anecdotes.text: Rather than traditional pork back-fat, the shop builds its bowls on hard-to-source wagyu fat from Kobe and Omi beef for a refined sweetness without heaviness, which the owners describe as the feature that makes the shop their own.
Students are offered free large portions of noodles and bean sprouts, and a partial menu from a sister izakaya is also available at the counter.
The shop began with just 7 counter seats and expanded to 17 seats in November 2025 as it grew in popularity.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: One of the two flavor options is soy sauce (醤油) tare, which in Jiro-style ramen is conventionally a wheat-based shoyu; the noodles are also wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All bowls are built on wagyu beef fat and beef Achilles tendon; the broth and tare are animal-based with no plant-based option.

cuisine: 京出汁醤油らーめん・冷やしつけ麺
neighborhood: 中京区・烏丸
gf_detail: This is a classic wheat-noodle ramen shop with no gluten-free offering. The signature 京出汁醤油らーめん uses crimped hand-kneaded wheat noodles from Kyoto noodle maker 麺屋棣鄂, and the soy-sauce tare is made from honjozo/hon-mirin soy sauce (wheat-containing). Broth, noodles, and tare all contain wheat, and the cold つけ麺 is likewise wheat-based. Not safe for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly shop. The Kyoto dashi base is built on kombu plus fish/seafood stock (魚介・魚節) and white dashi, and bowls are topped with pork-belly chashu and Kansai-style aburakasu (fried beef intestine). No vegan or vegetarian ramen is offered.
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区阪東屋町664-7
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned coordinates within the Kyoto box for 阪東屋町664-7; consistent with the reported ~1-2 min walk from Karasuma Station.
seats_text: 8 counter seats inside a converted storehouse (蔵), reached through the BIGOLI pasta restaurant's storefront.
hours_raw: 11:30 - 21:00（営業時間・定休日は変更となる場合がございます）
closed_days: No regular closing day listed (open daily); hours may change.
cultural_comfort_note: An unusual fully self-service spot with a quirky black-rotary-phone ordering system and Japanese-only signage, so a little Japanese or comfort with figuring out an unconventional local system helps a lot.
caveats: Hidden location: there is no street entrance of its own — you enter through the BIGOLI Bolognese/pasta restaurant and are led to a back-room storehouse. Fully self-service (order, pay, make change, clear your own table). Cash only.

bio.background: らーめん 蔵 opened on March 9, 2024 in central Kyoto near Karasuma, tucked into a Taisho-era kura (storehouse) with no street entrance of its own — diners pick up a numbered wooden tag and walk in through the BIGOLI pasta restaurant's storefront to reach the hidden eight-seat counter out back.
bio.specialty: 京出汁醤油らーめん in three 'bloom' grades — つぼみ (bud, no chashu), 五分咲き (half-bloom, some chashu) and 満開 (full bloom, generous chashu) — built on a gentle Kyoto-style dashi of kombu and fish stock with white dashi, finished with a honjozo soy-sauce tare and a touch of fragrant mayu burnt oil. Hand-kneaded crimped wheat noodles come from the well-known Kyoto maker 麺屋棣鄂, and bowls are topped with thin pork-belly chashu, green onion and Kansai-style crispy aburakasu (fried beef intestine). A cold つけ麺 rounds out the lineup.
bio.philosophy: An almost playfully unpretentious place that leans on customer integrity: the whole shop runs on self-service — you order by lifting an antique black rotary telephone on the wall that rings straight to the kitchen, pay and make your own change, wipe down your table, and collect a 10-yen cashback yourself. The aim is honest, refined Kyoto flavor at a remarkably low price for the city center.

anecdotes.text: There is no door to the street — you take a numbered wooden tag and pass through the BIGOLI pasta restaurant to a hidden back-garden storehouse to find the ramen counter.

Ordering is done by picking up an old black rotary phone mounted on the wall that connects directly to the kitchen; the whole experience is completely self-service down to making your own change and clearing your table.
The entry-level つぼみ bowl is priced around 690 yen — strikingly cheap for a refined Kyoto-dashi shoyu ramen in central Kyoto.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The tare is made from honjozo (conventionally brewed, wheat-containing) soy sauce combined with kombu, fish stock and white dashi, so the shoyu base contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The Kyoto dashi broth is built on kombu plus fish/seafood stock and white dashi, and bowls carry pork chashu and fried beef intestine, so the kitchen handles animal products throughout.

cuisine: ラーメン（背脂醤油・濃厚豚骨／ワンタンメン）
neighborhood: 桂・洛西口（西京区 樫原）
gf_detail: This is a wheat-noodle ramen shop. The bowls use medium-thick wheat ramen noodles (from producer Teigaku) and a shoyu (soy sauce) tare over a pork broth, plus gyoza, fried rice and karaage on the menu. Standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, and there is no indication of any gluten-free noodle, tare, or dedicated handling. Not suitable for celiac diners; treat as not GF-safe.
vegan_detail: A pork-broth (豚清湯) ramen specialist whose signature is back-fat shoyu ramen with chashu pork; the brand explicitly centers on its soup and its hand-prepared roast pork (焼き豚), and sides are gyoza, fried rice and karaage. No vegan or vegetarian ramen is offered. Vegans cannot eat here.
address_ja: 京都府京都市西京区樫原畔ノ海道10-58
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned coordinates within the Kyoto box for the full address.
seats_text: 29 seats total: 13 counter seats plus four 4-person tables (16); L-shaped counter, family-friendly on weekends.
closed_days: 月曜日 (Mondays)
cultural_comfort_note: A casual neighborhood ramen counter in a residential pocket of Nishikyo; no English is indicated, so a guide or translation help is useful, though the menu is simple and ordering is straightforward.
caveats: Reservations not accepted; QR payment (PayPay, d-barai, Rakuten Pay, au PAY) accepted but no credit cards or e-money. Service can end early once the daily chashu sells out. About a 20-minute walk from Hankyu Katsura/Rakusaiguchi; parking is available beside the shop.

bio.background: Stamen (stylized START WITH MEN) opened in January 2023 in the Kashihara residential district of Nishikyo Ward, a roughly 20-minute walk from Hankyu Katsura/Rakusaiguchi. It is the second brand and third shop of the well-loved Kyoto ramen maker Ramen Mugyu, whose flagship sits in the Enmachi (Marutamachi) area.
bio.specialty: The signature is a back-fat shoyu ramen: a clear pork stock (豚清湯) generously crowned with chunks of melted back fat, balanced by negi, bean sprouts and chashu, served with medium-thick slightly-wavy wheat noodles from the established maker Teigaku. A wonton-men (ワンタンメン) and a richer 濃厚 bowl round out the lineup, with gyoza, fried rice and karaage as sides.
bio.philosophy: Described as a brushed-up, new-generation take on classic Kyoto-style back-fat shoyu ramen — refined and approachable rather than extreme, aimed at pleasing both young diners and families. The parent brand sums up its priorities as 自慢のスープ、こだわりの焼き豚 (a soup it is proud of and meticulously made roast pork), and the kitchen wraps up service once the day's chashu runs out.

anecdotes.text: Mugyu publicly introduced Stamen as its 三号店 (third shop) on the brand's official Instagram, framing it as part of the Mugyu family rather than a standalone newcomer.
Local food media described the bowl as a 'new-type' update of classic Kyoto back-fat shoyu ramen, built on a clear pork broth where the sweetness of the back fat blends smoothly into the umami.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The ramen is a shoyu (soy sauce) tare style; standard Japanese shoyu is brewed with wheat, so the broth/tare should be assumed to contain wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is a pork stock (豚清湯) and the brand centers on its roast pork (焼き豚); bowls are topped with chashu, so no component is free of animal products.

gf_detail: No gluten-free offering. The spicy bowl uses either curly egg noodles (wheat) or "konnyaku" noodles that are in fact made primarily from buckwheat/wheat flour rather than pure konjac, plus a spicy minced-meat tare and toppings such as garlic and scallion. There is no indication of a wheat-free broth, tare, or dedicated preparation, so this is not safe for celiac diners. Treat as not gluten-free; anyone highly sensitive should avoid.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly shop. The kitchen is built around spicy noodles topped with minced meat and egg, and the celebrated sides are braised pork cartilage (nankotsu) and a pork-cartilage rice bowl. Eggs feature throughout (egg noodles, raw/soft egg toppings). No plant-based ramen is advertised. A "0辛" (no-spice) version exists but only changes heat, not the animal content.
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区西ノ京西月光町27-5 ライオンズ御前三条 1F
geocode_note: GSI geocoded the full address to lng 135.736847, lat 35.008797, within the Kyoto box and consistent with the Nishioji-Sanjo / Gozen-Sanjo location. Note the official address is in Nakagyo-ku (中京区西ノ京), not Ukyo-ku (右京区) as the target hint suggested, though the shop sits very near the Nishioji-Sanjo area on the Randen line.
neighborhood: 西ノ京・御前三条（西大路三条／中京区）
seats_text: 19席（カウンター3席、テーブル16席）
hours_raw: 09:00-15:30（L.O. 14:50、スープがなくなり次第終了）。定休日不定休（金曜が多い）。
closed_days: 不定休（金曜日が多い）
cultural_comfort_note: A small, casual local counter-and-table shop where a few words of Japanese and pointing at the spice-level chart go a long way; no English menu is indicated.

bio.background: A small specialist ramen counter in Kyoto's Nishioji-Sanjo / Gozen-Sanjo area, devoted entirely to karaimen — Korean-influenced spicy noodles built around a fiery minced-meat, garlic, scallion and egg broth.
bio.specialty: The house spicy bowl, where diners dial in heat from 0辛 (no spice) up through extreme levels (each step past the base adds a small charge), choosing either curly egg noodles or the shop's signature thin or thick 'konnyaku' noodles. The braised pork-cartilage rice bowl (なんこつ丼) — pork rib cartilage simmered until meltingly tender and finished with a ginger-grilled-style sauce — is a celebrated second act.
bio.philosophy: '辛いが旨い' — spicy, but delicious: the heat is meant to amplify flavor, not bury it.

anecdotes.text: Spice runs from a gentle 0辛 all the way to extreme heat, with the price climbing by small increments as the level rises, so regulars can chase a personal-best burn.

Beyond the noodles, the kitchen is proud of its slow-braised pork-cartilage (nankotsu), served as a side in ponzu, ginger-grilled or spicy styles or piled over rice in small, regular and 'mega' bowls.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The spicy tare and seasoning are built on a minced-meat, garlic and scallion base typical of Japanese spicy noodles, which generally relies on wheat-containing soy/seasoning; no wheat-free tare is advertised.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All noodle and broth preparations share a kitchen centered on pork cartilage, minced meat and egg, so cross-contact with animal products is unavoidable.

lead: 魔界系ラーメン三冠馬
name_ja: 魔界系ラーメン三冠馬
cuisine: Ramen / aburasoba / mazesoba (rich "makai-kei" pork-bone & fish broth)
neighborhood: Saiin / Nishikyogoku (Ukyo-ku)
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation. The bowls are built on wheat ramen noodles with a soy-based (wheat shoyu) tare and a thick pork-bone and fish broth, so they are not safe for celiacs. No GF menu, dedicated equipment, or allergen handling is advertised.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. All three signature bowls are animal-based: pork-bone/fish broth and chicken broth, with pork chashu and egg toppings. No plant-based or vegetable broth option is offered.
address_ja: 京都府京都市右京区西院月双町3 アビジット 1F
geocode_note: Geocoded from address_ja via GSI; coordinates [135.72049, 34.999386] fall within the Kyoto box.
seats_text: 6 counter seats plus 1 tatami/zashiki space (the tatami space is reserved for groups of 3 or more with children).
hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Thu, Fri, Sat: 11:00-14:30 / 18:00-20:00 (L.O. 19:45). Sun & holidays: 11:00-14:30. Closed Wednesdays and the 3rd Tuesday of each month.
closed_days: Wednesdays and the 3rd Tuesday of each month
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny counter-focused neighborhood ramen shop with a warm, family-run feel; a little basic Japanese and patience with the small space go a long way.
caveats: Phone number is not publicly listed. Hours follow a split lunch/dinner schedule on most days but lunch-only on Sundays and holidays; the 3rd-Tuesday closure makes the weekly pattern irregular, so confirm before a special trip.

bio.background: A small, family-run ramen shop that opened in September 2022 in the Saiin/Nishikyogoku area of Kyoto's Ukyo ward. The interior is filled with horse-racing memorabilia, plush toys, and posters reflecting the owner's deep love of the sport.
bio.origin: The owner trained with and was supported by the popular Sendai shop 'Makai Ramen Gekko' (魔界ラーメン 月光) when opening, which is the source of the 'makai-kei' (魔界系) name describing the intense, rich broth lineage.
bio.specialty: Three signature bowls: '衝撃の豚' (a thick, creamy pork-bone and fish shoyu ramen), '黄金の鶏' (a golden chicken ramen), and '皇帝の油そば' (an emperor's aburasoba/brothless mixed noodle). The pork-bone fish bowl is praised as concentrated and mellow yet clean, with backbone-deep umami and none of the heaviness you might expect.
bio.philosophy: An at-home, easygoing neighborhood spot where the owner's passion for both ramen and horse racing sets the tone.

anecdotes.text: The owner is a horse-racing fan and named the shop '三冠馬' (Triple Crown horse). Remarkably, after opening, Liberty Island — a racehorse the owner had backed as a fractional (one-mouth) owner — went on to actually win the Triple Crown.
The '衝撃の豚' chashu was recently changed from pork belly to Iga-no-mochi pork loin, served in marbled slices alongside a soft-yolk seasoned egg.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The bowls use a soy-based (shoyu) tare, which in Japanese ramen is virtually always wheat-containing soy sauce.

cuisine: ラーメン（豚骨鶏ガラ＋魚介の醤油、元寿司職人の和テイスト）
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation. This is a standard wheat-noodle ramen shop whose broth is a pork-bone/chicken-carcass base finished with a fish-infused soy-sauce (shoyu) tare — the tare is wheat-based shoyu and the noodles are wheat. No celiac-safe options were found in any source, and the kitchen makes no allergen claims. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The ramen broths are animal-based (pork bone + chicken carcass, plus dried-fish/niboshi dashi), and the signature side dishes are seafood (marinated yellowtail zuke-don, sashimi). No plant-based or vegetarian ramen was found in any source.
address_ja: 京都府京都市右京区西京極午塚町26
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned first feature coordinates [135.712097, 34.999714] for 京都府京都市右京区西京極午塚町26番地; within Kyoto box.
seats_text: 26 seats total — counter seating plus tatami table seating.
closed_days: No regular closing day listed (open daily); lunch and dinner service with a midday break.
cultural_comfort_note: A neighborhood ramen counter run by a former sushi chef; expect Japanese-only menu and service, so a little Japanese or pointing helps.

bio.background: Shofukuya is a small neighborhood ramen shop near Hankyu Nishikyogoku station, run by an owner who trained as a sushi/Japanese-cuisine chef (元板前) before opening here. That washoku pedigree is what sets the place apart: locals and writers consistently point to the kitchen's precision with fish and rice as the reason a ramen shop turns out sushi-restaurant-quality side dishes.
bio.specialty: The signature bowl is a soy-sauce (shoyu) ramen built on a pork-bone and chicken-carcass base finished with a fish/dried-sardine dashi — light and approachable on the first sip but deepening into a quiet umami richness. A delicate shio (salt) ramen is offered in the evenings. But the dish the shop is most loved for is the zuke-don: vinegared sushi rice topped with nori, fresh wasabi, and thick-cut marinated yellowtail (hamachi), often available as a 1,000-yen ramen-and-zuke-don set and sold in limited quantity until it runs out.
bio.philosophy: A ramen shop seen through a sushi chef's eyes — restrained, balanced broth rather than heavy intensity, and the same care with fish and rice you'd expect at a sushi counter.

anecdotes.text: Writers describe the zuke-don as being at the level of a sushi restaurant's lunch — vinegared rice with nori, wasabi and marinated yellowtail — and explicitly credit the owner's background as a former sushi chef for that quality.
The standard ramen is characterized as light at first but, sip after sip, revealing real depth without being thin or cloying — a pork-bone base with dried-sardine (niboshi) dashi added.
The zuke-don uses plump, thick-cut yellowtail with an exquisitely judged soy seasoning and a touch of fresh wasabi, and is made in limited quantity each day.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles are boiled in the shared pot used for all ramen; no gluten-free preparation exists.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature ramen is a fish-infused soy-sauce (shoyu) flavor; Japanese shoyu tare is wheat-based, so the broth itself contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths are animal-based (pork bone, chicken carcass and dried-fish dashi); no separate plant-based stock is used.

caveats: Cash only and no reservations; lunch and dinner with a midday break. No official website found; details cross-checked from Tabelog and local Kyoto food blogs. No allergen, gluten-free, or vegan information is published — a diner with celiac disease or a strict vegan diet cannot eat the ramen here, and any allergy needs must be discussed directly in Japanese with staff (not confirmed accommodatable).
neighborhood: 西京極（右京区）／阪急 西京極駅

lead: 熟成細麺 宝屋 西小路五条店
name_ja: 熟成細麺 宝屋 西小路五条店
cuisine: ラーメン（背脂豚骨醤油・熟成細麺）
gf_detail: No gluten-free options. The shop's identity is built on wheat "熟成細麺" (aged thin wheat noodles) served in a back-fat pork-bone shoyu broth, and the tare is a wheat-based soy sauce. Noodles, broth tare, and gyoza all contain wheat; cross-contamination from a shared noodle-boiling pot and wok is unavoidable. Not suitable for celiac or gluten-sensitive diners.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The core broth is a pork-bone (and chicken-bone) shoyu base finished with back fat (背脂), and signature bowls are topped with char siu pork. There is no plant-based ramen or vegetable broth on the menu; even a plain bowl is built on animal stock.
address_ja: 京都府京都市右京区西院西溝崎町33-1
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 西院西溝崎町33; within the Kyoto box.
neighborhood: 西院・西京極（右京区）／阪急 西京極駅 徒歩約10分
seats_text: 24席（カウンター16席・テーブル8席）
closed_days: 水曜日（Wednesday）
cultural_comfort_note: A no-frills neighborhood ramen counter where staff and regulars speak Japanese; little to no English, so a translation guide or pointing at the menu helps.
caveats: No gluten-free or vegan options whatsoever; the entire menu is wheat-noodle, animal-broth ramen. Closed Wednesdays. Reservations not accepted.

bio.background: Takaraya was once a fast-growing name in Kyoto's ramen world, with a flagship in Pontocho and branches in Fushimi-Momoyama, Mibu, the Kyoto Ramen Koji food hall and beyond. When the operating company went bankrupt in 2011, most of those shops closed or changed hands, and the survivors reopened under new names such as Kourai (香来), Shinrai (神来) and Miyako (京). The Nishikoji-Gojo branch is the one and only shop still trading under the original 宝屋 name.
bio.specialty: The signature is a Kyoto-classic back-fat pork-bone shoyu bowl: a glossy 背脂 (back-fat) shoyu broth on a pork- and chicken-bone base, paired with the shop's namesake 熟成細麺 — aged thin noodles that stay firm with a springy, shikoshiko bite. Beloved companion orders include the char-siu ramen (チャーシューメン), the fatty toro-niku char-siu bowl, and the layered char-siu rice bowl (チャー丼).
bio.philosophy: Keep serving the unchanged, honest taste of an old-Kyoto ramen at an everyday price — bowls have long started around 600 to 800 yen — so the neighborhood always has its classic back-fat bowl to come home to.

anecdotes.text: After the Takaraya chain's operating company collapsed in 2011, this Nishikoji-Gojo shop became the sole storefront to keep the 宝屋 signboard, while its former siblings carried on as Kourai, Shinrai and Miyako.
Regulars describe it as a surviving piece of the old 背脂ちゃっちゃ (back-fat) Kyoto ramen lineage, with a retro wood-heavy interior and a 'Kyoto royal-road' bowl that hasn't changed.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles are boiled in a shared pot, making the broth and any bowl unsafe for gluten-free diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is a shoyu (soy-sauce) tare base, which in standard ramen contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All bowls are built on a pork- and chicken-bone broth finished with pork back fat; no plant-based stock is available.

lead: 大勝軒 千代原口店
name_ja: 大勝軒 千代原口店
cuisine: つけ麺・ラーメン（東京・東池袋大勝軒系の暖簾分け、京都仕様の温（熱）つけ汁）
gf_detail: Tsukemen and ramen built on thick wheat noodles, with a chicken-pork-seafood dipping broth that is almost certainly seasoned with wheat-based shoyu tare. There is no indication of gluten-free noodles, broth, or any allergy accommodation. This shop is not safe for celiacs; cross-contamination from a shared wheat-noodle kitchen is unavoidable.
vegan_detail: A classic meat-and-fish tsukemen house: the broth combines chicken bones, pork bones and seafood, and bowls come topped with chashu (pork), menma and narutomaki. There are no vegan or vegetarian options noted.
address_ja: 京都府京都市西京区山田車塚町5-2
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned coordinates [135.692581, 34.982994] for 京都府京都市西京区山田車塚町5-2, within the Kyoto box.
neighborhood: 上桂・千代原口（西京区） / 国道9号 千代原口交差点付近、阪急 上桂駅徒歩圏
seats_text: 14 seats — L-shaped counter (about 8) plus a 2-seat table and a 4-seat table; simple roadside interior with large windows.
closed_days: Monday (closed even when Monday is a public holiday)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, no-frills roadside tsukemen counter where service is kept to the essentials and there is no sign of English support, so a Japanese-speaking companion or a clear pointing-and-ordering plan helps.

bio.background: This is a Kyoto branch (暖簾分け) of the legendary 東池袋大勝軒, the Tokyo shop in the Higashi-Ikebukuro district credited as the birthplace of tsukemen. The dish was born by accident in the 1950s: while working at the original shop, owner Kazuo Yamagishi would eat noodles dipped in broth, zaru-soba style, as a quick staff meal; a regular customer saw it and asked to try the same, and from 1955 it joined the menu as tsukemen.
bio.origin: Tokyo / Higashi-Ikebukuro Taishoken lineage, adapted for Kyoto
bio.specialty: Tsukemen (dipping noodles) — chewy, thick-cut wheat noodles served alongside a sweet-leaning dipping broth that blends chicken bones, pork bones and seafood. The signature pair is the 元祖つけ麺 (original) and the 濃厚つけ麺 (rich, finished with fish powder); an atsumori option serves the noodles piping hot.
bio.philosophy: A defining Kyoto touch: because tsukemen began as a staff meal meant to be eaten fast, the dipping broth was traditionally served only lukewarm, but here it is served piping hot for local tastes. The dip is lighter and a touch sweeter than today's intensely thick tsukemen, loaded with chashu, menma and narutomaki, with seafood aromatics that bloom when you add soup to the leftover broth at the end.

anecdotes.text: Tsukemen was reportedly invented by accident: owner Yamagishi ate broth-dipped noodles as a quick staff meal at the original Tokyo shop, a regular asked to try it, and it became a menu item from 1955.
Unlike the original lukewarm dip, the Kyoto shop serves the dipping broth piping hot to suit local preferences.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat tsukemen/ramen noodles are the core product, prepared in a shared kitchen, so flour cross-contact is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The dipping broth blends chicken, pork and seafood and is sweet-savory, consistent with a wheat-based shoyu tare; no gluten-free tare is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is animal-based (chicken and pork bones plus seafood) and bowls are topped with pork chashu, menma and narutomaki; no vegan preparation exists here.

caveats: Hours and Monday closure confirmed via Tabelog. No official website found. Payment method (cards vs cash) and English-menu availability are not documented; cash_only set false provisionally — confirm at the shop. Seat layout described slightly differently across sources (8 counter + tables vs L-shaped counter + 2 tables); seat total of 14 is from Tabelog.

lead: ほそかわ 花屋町店
name_ja: 中華そば ほそかわ 花屋町店
cuisine: ラーメン（京風 鶏ガラ背脂醤油・細麺）
gf_detail: This is a traditional Kyoto-style ramen shop. The bowls are built on wheat noodles in a chicken-bone, backfat soy-sauce (shoyu) broth — the tare is soy-sauce based and Japanese soy sauce is virtually always brewed with wheat. There is no indication of gluten-free noodles, a dedicated fryer, or any gluten-free accommodation. This shop is not suitable for celiacs or anyone requiring strict gluten avoidance.
vegan_detail: The house style is a chicken-bone (鶏ガラ) broth finished with pork backfat (背脂), and the signature bowl is chashu-men topped with sliced roast pork. The broth, fat, and toppings are animal-based and the menu is built around them. There is no vegan or vegetarian ramen option indicated.
address_ja: 京都府京都市右京区西京極南庄境町15
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to lng 135.727921, lat 34.99234 — within the Kyoto box. Matches the right-bank Nishikyogoku (右京区西京極) location near Hankyu Nishikyogoku Station.
neighborhood: 西院・西京極（右京区）／阪急 西京極駅 徒歩約10分
seats_text: 22 seats (16 counter, 6 at tables)
hours_raw: Monday–Saturday 10:00–19:00 / Sunday closed
closed_days: Sundays (日曜日)
cultural_comfort_note: A no-frills, cash-only local ramen counter with a simple ramen-and-rice menu and no reservations or English support, so it is best approached with a Japanese-speaking guide or a clear plan for pointing at what you want.

bio.background: The owner, Mr. Hosokawa, trained for about eight years at the legendary Ginkakuji 'Masutani' (ますたに 北白川本店) — the North-Shirakawa shop widely regarded as the wellspring of Kyoto's backfat soy-sauce ramen — before opening his own counter on Hanayamachi-dori near Hankyu Nishikyogoku Station in 1985.
bio.specialty: Traditional Kyoto-style chuka soba: a chicken-bone and soy-sauce broth finished with carefully selected pork backfat (背脂ちゃっちゃ) over thin noodles. It looks rich and glistening but drinks surprisingly clean, with a relatively light aftertaste; the chashu-men, piled with sliced roast pork, is the bowl regulars reach for.
bio.philosophy: Carry on the Masutani lineage of Kyoto ramen honestly — keep the menu simple (essentially ramen and rice), let quality backfat do the work so a heavy-looking bowl eats light, and stay a neighborhood shop rather than chase trends.

anecdotes.text: The shop is a direct descendant of the famed Ginkakuji Masutani line; the owner spent roughly eight years there before striking out on his own in 1985.
Reviewers describe it as a locally-rooted, unpretentious shop serving 'simple and delicious' nostalgic Kyoto ramen whose generous backfat never turns heavy.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles are boiled in shared water and the soy-sauce-based tare contains wheat; there is no gluten-free preparation, so cross-contact is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is a backfat soy-sauce (醤油) style; standard Japanese soy sauce is brewed with wheat, so the tare should be assumed to contain wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The broth is chicken-bone with pork backfat and the signature topping is roast pork, so all bowls are animal-based with no vegan option.

caveats: Cash only; closed Sundays; no reservations; simple ramen-and-rice menu with no gluten-free or vegan options. The official site (kyoto-hosokawa.com) was intermittently unreachable during verification, so founding/lineage facts were corroborated via the Kyoto gourmet guide and Vacan magazine rather than the official page directly. Coordinates are address-level (chō-level) GSI geocode, not a rooftop pin.

cuisine: Duck ramen — domestic crossbred duck (aigamo) broth in shio, shoyu, miso, and tantan-men styles, plus duck rice bowls and sides
vegan_detail: This is a duck specialist: every ramen is built on a duck-based broth and the menu centers on duck meat (sliced duck, minced duck soboro, loin), duck fried rice, duck gyoza, and duck rice bowls. No vegan or vegetarian bowl is offered or advertised. Not a destination for plant-based diners.
gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The noodles are made with wheat (one source notes they contain whole-wheat flour / 全粒粉), and ramen tare/sides such as gyoza and teriyaki-style duck rice bowls typically rely on wheat-based soy sauce. No gluten-free noodle or dedicated allergy handling is mentioned on the official site or any source reviewed. A gluten-sensitive diner should treat this shop as off-limits.
address_ja: 京都府京都市伏見区中島鳥羽離宮町37
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned the chōme-level coordinates for 中島鳥羽離宮町37; within the Kyoto box. The shop sits on National Route 1 near Jōnangū Shrine.
neighborhood: 伏見区・城南宮そば（竹田駅 徒歩約16分）
seats_text: Seat count not published. Reviews describe a spacious, relaxed interior with a parking lot (about 6 standard + 2 compact spaces); the current Fushimi location is a larger relocation of the original shop.
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular closures — no fixed weekly day off; confirm before visiting)
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed, roadside ramen shop with parking and no English-specific service noted — a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu will carry you through comfortably.

bio.chef_name: 原田 浩（株式会社GRACE 代表取締役）
bio.background: 鴨LABO grew out of a duck wholesaler's move into ramen — a shop opened beside the company's processing operation in Minami Ward, then relocated in 2022 to its current home on National Route 1 in Fushimi, beside Jōnangū Shrine, for easier access and a roomier space. The name reads as a 'laboratory' for exploring what duck meat can be.
bio.origin: 京都市南区（創業地、2021年末まで）→ 2022年に伏見区へ移転
bio.specialty: Ramen built on domestic crossbred duck (aigamo): the broth carries the deep umami and natural sweetness of duck with a subtle acidity, yet stays light and refined rather than heavy. It runs across shio, shoyu, miso, and a signature kamo tantan-men with minced duck, scallions, daikon sprouts, and chili oil; the duck salt ramen is the reviewer favorite.
bio.philosophy: Operating as a duck wholesaler's own restaurant, the shop treats itself as a 'lab' for the possibilities of duck — sourcing its own birds and serving everything from ramen to roast duck, minced duck, and takeaway duck shabu-shabu sets, so guests can enjoy duck casually day-to-day or as a small occasion.

anecdotes.text: The shop is the ramen arm of a duck wholesaler, giving it direct access to domestic aigamo duck and letting it sell duck chashu, minced duck, and duck hot-pot/shabu sets to take home alongside the bowls.
Reviewers describe the duck salt ramen as a mellow, refreshing bowl with a 'healing' quality, served in a relaxed, spacious roadside space near Jōnangū Shrine.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Ramen noodles are wheat-based (one account notes whole-wheat / 全粒粉 flour), so the bowls are not gluten-free and there is no indication of separate gluten-free preparation.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Shoyu and tantan/miso bowls plus duck teriyaki rice bowls and gyoza rely on standard (wheat-based) soy sauce and seasonings; no wheat-free tare is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broths are duck-based and the menu is centered on duck meat throughout, so there is no plant-based broth to protect from cross-contact.

caveats: Seat count is not published by any source reviewed; coordinates are GSI chō-level for 中島鳥羽離宮町37. Hours are listed as 11:00–22:00 daily with 不定休 (irregular days off) — confirm the day before visiting since there is no fixed closing day. No allergy/gluten/vegan accommodation is documented; treat as wheat- and duck-heavy.

lead: 大黒ラーメン 本店
name_ja: 大黒ラーメン 本店
cuisine: ラーメン（豚骨醤油・地元老舗）
neighborhood: 伏見区（伏見桃山・桃山御陵前）
gf_detail: No gluten-free provision. This is a classic pork-bone-and-chicken shoyu ramen shop: the bowls use wheat (medium-thin) noodles and a soy-sauce-based tare that in this style is virtually always wheat-containing, and the popular yakimeshi (fried rice) is also seasoned with soy sauce. There is no published allergen information and no celiac-safe option. Not suitable for someone who must avoid gluten.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The single signature ramen is a pork-bone and chicken-bone broth, topped with char siu (pork), and the house yakimeshi contains char siu and egg. There are no vegetable or vegan ramen options. A vegan would not have a meal here.
address_ja: 京都府京都市伏見区京町大黒町118
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates [135.765045, 34.934376] for 京都府京都市伏見区京町大黒町118; within Kyoto bounding box.
seats_text: 33 seats: 5 counter, one 2-person table, two 4-person tables, three 6-person tables. Table-centric layout, family-friendly.
hours_raw: 火～日 11:00-22:30 / 月曜定休（Tuesday–Sunday 11:00–22:30; closed Mondays）
closed_days: Mondays
cultural_comfort_note: A bustling, no-frills local ramen counter with no English support or allergen info, so a guide is recommended for diners with strict dietary needs.

bio.background: A neighborhood ramen institution that opened in 1983 and has anchored the old Nara highway in Fushimi for roughly four decades. The shop occupies a renovated former sake brewery (旧酒蔵を改装), fitting for the famous sake district, and is marked by a tricolor red-yellow-navy awning. It is the original 本店 (main store) of a small local chain.
bio.specialty: A single, focused bowl of tonkotsu-shoyu (豚骨醤油) ramen: a golden broth simmered from pork and chicken bones, kept clean and light-bodied (サラッとした味わい) rather than heavy, paired with medium-thin noodles. The other star is the yakimeshi (fried rice) of diced char siu, egg and green onion fried to a fluffy, separated texture; locals famously order ramen and yakimeshi together.
bio.philosophy: Keep it simple and generous: serve one well-made bowl at an affordable price so customers leave full, and bring them back with a no-expiry 100-yen discount voucher. As the shop puts it, they want people to eat their fill of good ramen at a reasonable price.

anecdotes.text: Founded in 1983 with a four-decade single focus on tonkotsu-shoyu ramen, the shop hands every departing customer a 100-yen discount voucher with no expiration date to encourage their return.
The yakimeshi is so beloved that nearly every customer orders it alongside their ramen; the shop operates out of a renovated former sake brewery on the old Nara highway.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles are boiled in shared water and bowls use a soy-sauce-based tare; no gluten-free handling is offered.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is a tonkotsu-shoyu (pork-bone soy sauce) base; the shoyu tare in this style is virtually always wheat-containing.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is simmered from pork and chicken bones and bowls are topped with char siu pork; there are no vegan items and no separate preparation.

caveats: The provided cuisine label said 京都ブラック系 (Kyoto-black soy-pork-bone), but every source describes the signature bowl as a golden, light-bodied tonkotsu-shoyu — not the dark "Kyoto black" style. Cuisine corrected to 豚骨醤油. Hours and seat layout are from the listing; the official site (daikokuramen.jp) rendered only its title via fetch, so menu/allergen specifics could not be confirmed there. No published allergen statements exist, so GF/vegan safety notes are inferred from the dish type and marked low confidence.

lead: らーめん門扇 伏水酒蔵店
name_ja: らーめん門扇 伏水酒蔵店
cuisine: ラーメン（鶏白湯・酒粕ラーメン）

The bowls are built on wheat ramen noodles (sourced from the Kyoto maker Menya Teigaku) and the chicken broth is almost certainly seasoned with wheat-based shoyu tare; the signature sake-lees (kasu) topping is fermented from rice but the noodles and seasoning are wheat. No gluten-free menu, no dedicated equipment, and no allergen handling is mentioned by any source. A celiac diner should treat this as unsafe.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The house broth is a rich chicken-bone (tori-paitan / tori-gara) stock simmered with vegetables, and bowls are topped with chicken and other animal items. The sake-lees variations are also built on the same chicken base. No vegan or vegetarian ramen option is advertised.
address_ja: 京都府京都市伏見区平野町82-2 伏水酒蔵小路内
geocode_note: Geocoded from address_ja (平野町82-2) via GSI; coordinates fall within the Kyoto box. Shop sits inside the Fushimi Sakagura Koji food-court complex near Fushimi-Momoyama / Momoyama-Goryomae / Chushojima stations.
seats_text: 23 seats total: 15 counter seats plus four 2-person tables (tables can be combined).
hours_raw: 11:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:30); closed Tuesdays (open if Tuesday is a public holiday).
