cultural_comfort_note: A casual counter-and-table ramen spot inside a lively Fushimi food-court alley; a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu will carry you, though dedicated English support is not advertised.
neighborhood: 伏見区・伏見桃山（伏水酒蔵小路内）
caveats: No allergen, gluten-free, or vegan information is published by the shop or any source reviewed; the dietary assessments here are inferred from the dishes (wheat noodles, chicken broth, sake-lees topping) and should be confirmed in person before ordering for medical needs.

bio.background: Monsen is a Kyoto chicken-broth ramen house whose flagship is in Gion, with branches in Kiyamachi and here in Fushimi. The Fushimi Sakagura-ten sits inside Fushimi Sakagura Koji, a nostalgic food-court alley of around nine eateries in Fushimi-Momoyama, one of Japan's three great sake-brewing districts, where you can cross-order dishes between shops over a glass of local sake.
bio.origin: Fushimi, Kyoto (within the Fushimi Sakagura Koji complex)
bio.specialty: Their signature is the 'kuramoto-ga-eraberu sakekasu ramen' (brewery-selectable sake-lees ramen), a bowl exclusive to this Fushimi branch. A long-simmered chicken-bone-and-vegetable tori-paitan broth is finished with sake lees that lends a rich, remarkably smooth body and a gentle sake aroma; diners pick their lees from a rotating lineup of local Fushimi breweries (about eight on offer, such as Kizakura, Tsukinokatsura, Toyoiwai, Tamanohikari, Eishin, Kyohime, Tsuruga and Tomio). Thin, curly noodles from the established Kyoto noodle maker Menya Teigaku cling to the broth, topped with Chinese cabbage, daikon, carrot and green onion.
bio.philosophy: A bowl that could only exist in Fushimi: letting guests taste the character of the surrounding sake breweries through the lees that finish a carefully simmered chicken broth.

anecdotes.text: Letting customers choose their sake lees from the rotating lineup of nearby breweries is described as a service unique to Fushimi, where so many brewers are clustered together.

This branch carries Fushimi-only items not found at other Monsen shops, including the 'Montetsu ramen' done in collaboration with a neighboring teppanyaki spot and a lighter 'sake-tori ramen' balancing chicken and sake-lees flavor.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No source documents the tare composition, but a chicken-broth ramen of this style is conventionally seasoned with wheat-based shoyu; not confirmed gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The broth is a chicken-bone (tori-gara/tori-paitan) stock simmered with vegetables, so no bowl is free of animal product even before toppings.

cuisine: ラーメン（味噌・醤油、豚骨魚介系）
gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. This is a classic wheat-noodle ramen shop: house-blended wheat noodles (fine to thick), miso and shoyu tare, and broths built on pork bone, chicken, and dried-fish/seafood stocks. Miso and soy-based tare typically contain wheat. No gluten-free menu, no gluten-free noodle option, and no allergen labeling is published. A celiac diner cannot eat safely here.
vegan_detail: Not vegan or vegetarian. Every broth is animal-based — domestic pork bone, free-range chicken, and seafood/dried-fish stocks — and signature bowls feature seared chashu pork, fish, and tuna rice sides. No plant-based broth or vegan ramen is offered.
address_ja: 京都府京都市伏見区深草スゝハキ町24-3
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned coordinates [135.768295, 34.965862] for the full street address; within the Kyoto box.
neighborhood: 伏見区 龍谷大前深草駅（稲荷・伏見稲荷）
seats_text: About 8 counter seats in an L-shaped arrangement; orders via a ticket vending machine.
hours_raw: Mon, Wed, Thu, Fri, Sun: 11:00-14:00 and 17:30-21:00 (L.O. 20:30); Tue: 11:00-14:00 only; Sat: closed.
closed_days: Saturdays (Tuesday dinner also closed — lunch only)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, cash-only counter ramen shop with a ticket vending machine and no English menu published — basic Japanese or pointing at the ticket machine makes the visit smooth.

bio.origin: Shizuoka, then Machida (Tokyo), now Fukakusa, Kyoto
bio.background: Katsukyo began in Shizuoka in October 2006, opened a second shop in Machida, Tokyo in June 2017, and brought the operation to its current spot in Fukakusa — on Shidan-dori between Fushimi Inari and Ryukoku University — in October 2019. It sits in a student-heavy neighborhood and has built a steady local following.
bio.specialty: The signature is a rich, deep miso ramen (the 特製味噌 / special miso) layered with pork-bone, seafood and vegetable umami, served over house-made thick, flat wavy noodles and topped with seared chashu, two kinds of green onion, mizuna and nori. The lineup also runs to a seafood-tonkotsu, a shoyu-tonkotsu line and tsukemen, with rice sides such as a seared-mackerel and a wild tuna (toro maguro) bowl.
bio.philosophy: The shop's stated creed is that 'dashi is Japanese culture.' Broths are built entirely by hand — pork bone, chicken, and dried-fish/seafood stocks each simmered and refined over one to two days, strained meticulously and rested in cold storage to mature, with no chemical seasonings or instant products, aiming for a lower-sodium, body-friendly bowl. Ingredients are chosen with care: wild southern tuna sourced from Yaizu in Shizuoka, naturally sun-dried nori, domestic pork and free-range chicken, house-made menma, and a custom noodle blend rested three days.

anecdotes.text: The owner carried the shop across the country — founding it in Shizuoka in 2006, moving to Machida in Tokyo, then resettling in Fukakusa, Kyoto in late 2019 — before landing in this student quarter near Ryukoku University.
Its wild southern tuna is brought in directly from Yaizu in Shizuoka, a nod to the shop's Shizuoka roots, and appears on the menu as a toro-maguro rice bowl alongside the ramen.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles are boiled in shared water; the kitchen handles wheat noodles, miso/soy tare and flour-thickened broths throughout, so cross-contact with gluten is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Shoyu-tonkotsu bowls and the miso tare rely on standard Japanese soy/miso seasoning, which typically contains wheat; no wheat-free tare is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broths are animal-based (pork bone, chicken, seafood/dried fish) and there is no separate plant-based stock, so no dish can be made vegan.

positives.text: Broths are made entirely from scratch with no chemical seasonings or instant products, simmered and matured over one to two days, with a stated low-sodium, body-friendly aim.

caveats: Seat count (~8 L-shaped counter seats) and the move history come from a Kyotopi feature and the official site; the official site lists no chef/owner name, so chef_name is null. No published allergen labeling, so GF/vegan judgments are inferred from the ramen ingredients (wheat noodles, miso/soy tare, animal broths) rather than an explicit statement. Tuesday is lunch-only; Saturday closed.

lead: らーめんや たみん
name_ja: らーめんや たみん
cuisine: ラーメン（鶏・町ラーメン）
neighborhood: 伏見区 深草（龍谷大前深草駅）
address_ja: 京都府京都市伏見区深草直違橋10-162
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned [135.770889, 34.964725] for 京都府京都市伏見区深草直違橋10-162; within Kyoto box.
seats_text: カウンター5席のみ。少人数向けの小さな店で、多人数での来店は受け付けていない。
hours_raw: 営業日・営業時間は不定期で、店主のTwitterで随時告知。過去の目安として昼12:00〜14:00、夜18:00〜21:00頃（仕入れ・仕込みの都合で変動）。来店前にSNSでの確認が必須。
closed_days: 不定休（営業日はTwitterで告知）
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny five-seat counter shop with irregular hours announced only on Twitter and a food-ticket system; comfortable for a confident solo or pair visit with some basic Japanese, less suited to groups or those needing English support.
gf_detail: No gluten-free provision. This is a standard ramen shop: wheat ramen noodles in a rich chicken/pork-bone broth. There is no published indication of gluten-free noodles or any allergen accommodation, and the shop is a one-person operation with a single counter, so cross-contact is unavoidable. Not suitable for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The broth is animal-based (chicken / chicken-pork bone, with one source noting a sea-bream-and-chicken base) and bowls are topped with pork chashu and menma. No plant-based or vegetable ramen is offered. Vegans cannot eat here.
caveats: Hours are genuinely irregular and posted only on the owner's Twitter; the day-by-day schedule cannot be fixed, so hours_week is marked all-closed and visitors must check SNS before going. Menu prices vary across sources and over time. One blog reports a sea-bream-and-chicken salt base while Tabelog/other sources describe a chicken-pork-bone (鶏豚骨) rich broth, suggesting the lineup has changed; treat broth specifics as approximate.

bio.background: らーめんや たみん is a one-person neighborhood ramen counter tucked beside Ryukoku University Mae-Fukakusa Station in the Fukakusa area of Fushimi, occupying a spot once home to a shop called 金太 (Kinta). It is the kind of tiny, owner-run place that locals seek out rather than stumble upon.
bio.specialty: The signature is a 濃厚ラーメン (rich, concentrated ramen) built on a thick, glossy chicken-based broth, topped with notably generous, tender chashu sliced over a centimeter thick, scallion, and seasoned menma. Reviewers describe the soup as deeply umami yet gently salted rather than heavy-handed.
bio.philosophy: The shop runs on the owner's own rhythm: just five counter seats, a food-ticket system, and operating days announced as the prep allows on Twitter rather than a fixed calendar. Quality and freshness drive the schedule, and the owner personally warns each guest that the bowl arrives extremely hot.
bio.origin: 京都市伏見区深草（旧「金太」の場所）

anecdotes.text: One regular food blogger wrote that it took roughly a year of attempts before he finally managed to get in, because the shop opens only on irregular days posted to Twitter.
On serving the rich bowl the owner cautions diners 「熱いので気を付けて下さい」 (please be careful, it's hot) — the concentrated broth comes out scalding.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Single small counter run by one person serving wheat-noodle ramen; no separate preparation area, so gluten cross-contact is unavoidable.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All bowls are built on animal broth (chicken / chicken-pork bone) with pork chashu; no plant-based options, and everything shares the same pots.

cuisine: ラーメン
neighborhood: 伏見区 竹田（深草西浦町・名神高架下）
gf_detail: This is a traditional Kyoto-style shoyu-tonkotsu ramen shop. The noodles are wheat (medium-thick straight noodles supplied by Menya Tiekun), and the soy tare almost certainly contains wheat, served over a pork-bone broth. There is no gluten-free menu, no mention of allergen handling, and the kitchen is a single small operation with shared boiling water — not safe for celiac diners. No gluten-free options found in any source.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly shop. The entire concept centers on a pork-bone (豚骨) broth and an abundance of chashu (roasted pork belly) — the signature draw is a bowl piled with pork. No vegan or vegetarian ramen, and no plant-based options are mentioned in any source.
address_ja: 京都府京都市伏見区深草西浦町8-115 武一ビル 1F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to chō-level (深草西浦町八丁目); coordinates fall within the Kyoto bounding box. Block-level point, not the exact building.
seats_text: 19 seats total: 7 counter seats plus two 6-person tables.
hours_raw: 火～日 07:00-14:00、月曜定休（売り切れ次第終了）
closed_days: Mondays (and may close earlier when sold out — frequently sells out before 14:00)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, locals-first morning ramen counter where ordering in basic Japanese is smoothest, though an English menu is available to help you point to what you want.
caveats: Opens at 7:00 for morning ramen (朝ラー) and frequently sells out and closes before the listed 14:00; arrive early. Cash only, no reservations. Two parking spaces on site.

bio.background: After more than ten years of training at Kyoto's legendary Honke Daiichi Asahi Honten near Kyoto Station, the owner struck out on his own and opened Raamen Miya in September 2023, on the former site of a beloved ramen shop (Banrai-ya) that had closed. It sits tucked beneath the Meishin Expressway overpass, between Keihan Fujinomori and the Takeda subway/Kintetsu station, slightly toward Takeda.
bio.specialty: An 'Akira-style' (Daiichi Asahi lineage) original Kyoto ramen: a pork-bone broth finished with a clean, fragrant soy tare and crowned with sweet, fatty pork-belly chashu. The signature is the meat-heavy bowl (肉増し / chashu ramen) where slices of chashu are fanned across the surface like flower petals, mixing fatty and lean cuts — reviewers joke the chashu never seems to run out.
bio.philosophy: The owner deliberately lightened the broth compared to the richer Daiichi Asahi original so it would suit early-morning eating, leaning into the Kyoto tradition of 'asa-ra' (morning ramen). The menu is kept simple and honest: ramen, chashu, rice, and a chashu rice bowl.

anecdotes.text: Despite opening at 7:00 a.m., the counter fills to capacity during breakfast hours — one writer arrived before 9 a.m. to find nearly every seat taken.
The shop opened in September 2023 on the former premises of the much-missed ramen shop Banrai-ya.
The owner trained for over ten years at the famed Honke Daiichi Asahi Honten before opening his own shop.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles are boiled in shared water and the soy-based tare typically contains wheat, served over a pork-bone broth — not safe for gluten avoidance.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is finished with a soy (shoyu) tare in the Daiichi Asahi lineage; Japanese shoyu almost always contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The broth is pork-bone (豚骨) based and bowls are topped with abundant pork chashu; no plant-based preparation exists.

name_ja: らーめん門扇 伏水酒蔵店
cuisine: ラーメン（鶏がら・鶏白湯／名物 酒粕らーめん）
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The ramen is built on wheat noodles with a chicken-bone (tori-gara / tori-paitan) broth and a soy-sauce-style tare, so wheat is present in both the noodles and the seasoning. The signature sake-lees (kasu) ramen also contains sake lees, a fermented-rice byproduct that carries alcohol. No gluten-free noodles, dedicated handling, or celiac accommodation is advertised on any page reviewed. A celiac diner should not eat here.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians. The house broth is a long-simmered chicken-bone stock and bowls feature pork/chicken toppings; even the sake-lees and sesame tantanmen variants are built on the animal broth. No plant-based broth or vegan bowl is listed anywhere reviewed.
address_ja: 京都府京都市伏見区平野町82-2 伏水酒蔵小路内
geocode_note: Geocoded from address_ja via GSI; first feature coordinates [135.760452, 34.932484], inside the Kyoto box.
neighborhood: 伏見・伏見桃山（伏水酒蔵小路）
seats_text: Counter 15 seats plus 4 tables for 2 (about 23 seats), inside the shared Fushimi Sakagura Koji food alley.
hours_raw: 11:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:30); closed Tuesdays (open if Tuesday is a holiday).
closed_days: Tuesdays (open when a Tuesday falls on a public holiday)
cultural_comfort_note: A casual local ramen counter inside a lively sake-brewery food alley; a friendly greeting and pointing at the menu gets you by, though English support is limited.
caveats: Heads-up: the curation tag placed this in the 藤森・墨染 / 門扇本店系 group, but there is no Monsen shop physically in Fujinomori or Sumizome. The only currently-operating Monsen in Fushimi Ward is this Fushimi Sakagura branch (Hirano-cho), and the chain's actual honten is in Gion (Higashiyama). The cuisine was also tagged 背脂醤油 (back-fat shoyu); in reality Monsen is a chicken-broth (tori-gara / tori-paitan) house, with sake-lees ramen as its local signature. Verify the intended shop before publishing.

bio.background: Monsen is a Kyoto chicken-ramen house whose main shop sits in Gion; this branch lives inside Fushimi Sakagura Koji, a converted old laneway in the heart of Fushimi's sake-brewing district that gathers nine small eateries under one roof. Settling a ramen counter among the breweries let Monsen do something it can do nowhere else.
bio.specialty: The signature bowl is sake-lees ramen (kasu ramen): a rich, collagen-heavy broth from chicken bones and vegetables simmered for hours, rounded with the soft aroma of local sake lees. The twist unique to Fushimi is that you choose which brewery's lees goes into your bowl from the day's lineup, drawing on neighbors like Kizakura, Tsukino Katsura, Tamanohikari, Eikun and Tomioo.
bio.philosophy: A chicken-broth specialist that leans into its address, turning the surrounding sake culture into a flavor you can pick yourself rather than a fixed recipe.
bio.origin: Kyoto (Fushimi sake district); part of the Monsen group whose honten is in Gion.

anecdotes.text: Because the kitchen swaps in different breweries' sake lees by the day, the kasu ramen literally tastes different depending on when you visit, and you select the brewery yourself from the lineup on offer.
Sitting inside the Fushimi Sakagura Koji alley, the shop lets diners also order dishes from the other stalls in the complex, including a teppanyaki collaboration bowl made with the neighboring grill.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Ramen here uses wheat noodles in a chicken-bone broth with a soy-sauce-style seasoning, so wheat is present in both the noodles and the tare.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The base broth is a long-simmered chicken-and-vegetable stock; bowls are animal-based with no plant-based broth offered.

cuisine: Kyoto-style Chinese (京風中華・町中華); signature stir-fried noodle dish カラシソバ (Karashi Soba) with house-made noodles
neighborhood: 上京区・烏丸今出川（地下鉄今出川駅すぐ／京都御所・同志社大学近く）
vegan_detail: No vegan options indicated. The kitchen is a Chinese eatery built around pork (酢豚), meat-filled spring rolls and shumai, and a chicken-and-kelp-based stock for the noodle dishes. The signature カラシソバ rests on a chicken/kombu sauce. No plant-based menu is advertised; a strict vegan would have nothing reliable to order here.
gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The shop's identity is its 自家製麺 (house-made wheat noodles), and the signature カラシソバ combines those wheat noodles with a mustard-vinegar-soy-sauce dressing (soy sauce normally contains wheat) over a chicken/kombu sauce. Dumpling wrappers (shumai, spring rolls) are also wheat. There is no gluten-free menu and wheat is central to nearly every dish.
address_ja: 京都府京都市上京区今出川町333 第2高橋ビル 5F CD
geocode_note: Geocoded the post-relocation address (上京区今出川町333) via GSI to chō-level: [135.758789, 35.028854], inside the Kyoto box. The shop moved in March 2024 from its former Shinmachi-dōri address to the 5th floor of a building at the southwest corner of the Karasuma-Imadegawa intersection, directly by Imadegawa subway station.
seats_text: 37 seats (counter and tables) at the relocated Imadegawa premises
closed_days: Tuesdays and Wednesdays (open on holidays)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, busy local Kyoto-Chinese counter eatery where ordering and dietary requests are easiest in Japanese; English support should not be assumed.
caveats: The originally targeted Tabelog listing (26026814) is now flagged 【移転】(relocated) — it is the pre-March-2024 page. The shop continues to operate at a new address a short walk away by Imadegawa station; the current listing is 26040920. Record reflects the current/new location. The dish カラシソバ is a Kyoto-Chinese stir-fried noodle specialty rather than a soup ramen.

bio.background: The owner, Tetsuo Aiba, entered an apprenticeship at age 16 at the celebrated Kyoto Chinese restaurant 鳳舞 (Houmai), where he handled the noodle-making. 鳳舞 — a landmark of Kyoto-style Chinese cooking — closed in 2009 (Heisei 21), and 鳳舞楼, opened in December 2015, carries on its lineage. Within that 鳳舞 family of successor shops, 鳳舞楼 is noted as the only one that makes its own noodles in-house.
bio.specialty: カラシソバ (Karashi Soba) — the shop's number-one dish. House-made, very thin crinkled noodles (the dough is hand-pulled and rested for several days to develop its springy bite) are dressed with a mustard-vinegar-soy sauce over a chicken-and-kombu-stock sauce loaded with vegetables; the sharp mustard kick is what makes it addictive.
bio.philosophy: Aiba speaks of wanting to carry the flavor he inherited from his 鳳舞 master to people nationwide, not only in Kyoto, and refuses to compromise on the house-made noodles and the mustard seasoning.

anecdotes.text: The signature flavor was originally devised by Aiba's master at 鳳舞, the shop where he began training at sixteen; he has preserved it as a one-of-a-kind taste.
Even the shumai are wrapped in an ultra-thin house-made skin, reflecting the shop's commitment to making its noodles and wrappers from scratch.
In March 2024 the shop moved from its quieter Shinmachi-dōri spot to a far more accessible location right at the Karasuma-Imadegawa crossing, beside the subway and across from Doshisha University.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature カラシソバ is dressed with a mustard-vinegar-soy-sauce blend; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, so the sauce as well as the noodles carry gluten.

cuisine: 京風中華・町中華（広東料理）/ Kyoto-style Cantonese Chinese; signature karashi soba noodles
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation. The signature karashi soba is made with wheat noodles, and Chinese-style dishes here use soy-sauce/seasoning bases that typically contain wheat. There is no indication of gluten-free menu items, separate preparation, or celiac-aware handling. Not suitable for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan/vegetarian establishment. The signature karashi soba contains shrimp, chicken, and a chicken-and-kombu broth, and the kitchen is built around meat and seafood Cantonese cooking. No documented plant-based ramen or vegan options. Vegans cannot be reliably accommodated.
address_ja: 京都府京都市東山区八坂新地富永町131
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned chō-level point for 京都府京都市東山区八坂上町 (135.779587, 34.998505), within the Kyoto box and consistent with the Gion / Yasaka Shinchi Tominaga-chō location.
seats_text: 15 seats: 5 counter seats plus 2 tables seating about 5 people, in a small former snack-bar space
hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Fri, Sat, Sun: 12:00-14:00 (lunch), 17:30-20:00 (dinner). Closed Wednesday and Thursday.
closed_days: Wednesday and Thursday
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, husband-and-wife hidden-gem in an old Gion mixed-use building with a warm, homey feel; little to no English, so basic Japanese or a translation aid and a relaxed attitude help a lot.
neighborhood: Higashiyama-ku, Gion / Yasaka Shinchi (near Gion-Shijo Station)
caveats: Cash only. Tiny 15-seat counter-and-table space inside an unassuming multi-tenant building, so it can fill up and the entrance is easy to miss. Closed Wednesdays and Thursdays. Not suitable for celiac or vegan diners — the food is built on wheat noodles, meat, and seafood.

bio.background: A husband-and-wife team runs this tucked-away Cantonese kitchen in an old Gion building, carrying on the lineage of Hōbu (鳳舞), the legendary Kyoto Chinese restaurant often called the source of "Kyo-chuka" (Kyoto-style Chinese cooking).
bio.specialty: Karashi soba (カラシソバ) — wheat noodles tossed in mustard and topped with a savory sauce of shrimp, chicken, green onion, bamboo shoot, lettuce, and wood-ear mushroom, built on a chicken stock and Rishiri kombu base, offered in three spice levels playfully named like school grades (e.g., middle-school / high-school / university).
bio.philosophy: Quiet, homey hospitality over flash: a deliberately inconspicuous door, an intimate counter, and an owner who checks in on diners mid-meal so the place feels like eating at someone's home rather than a restaurant.

anecdotes.text: The karashi soba descends from a famous dish at the now-closed Hōbu, a foundational name in Kyoto Chinese cuisine, so a bowl here is a taste of that vanished lineage.
The mustard is potent enough that even the medium level can make your nose tingle from the aroma alone; the couple's own cartoon likenesses appear on the menu, said to resemble them closely.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The signature karashi soba uses wheat noodles and the kitchen is a small Cantonese operation cooking many wheat-containing dishes in shared woks, so cross-contact with wheat is effectively unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Cantonese/Kyoto-Chinese seasonings and sauces here are based on standard soy sauce and Chinese condiments, which normally contain wheat; no wheat-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The signature dish and broth contain shrimp, chicken, and chicken-kombu stock; the kitchen centers on meat and seafood, so any dish risks animal-product cross-contact.

lead: ラーメン大蔵 一乗寺店
name_ja: ラーメン大蔵 一乗寺店
cuisine: Ramen
neighborhood: Ichijoji (Sakyo-ku)
gf_detail: Standard Japanese ramen shop with no gluten-free offering. Bowls are built on wheat ramen noodles, the shoyu/shio tare is almost certainly wheat-based soy sauce, and broths are pork/seafood based — not celiac-safe. There is no published allergen menu, dedicated prep, or GF noodle option. A celiac diner cannot eat safely here.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan/vegetarian-friendly shop. The broths are animal-based (pork/chicken and a seafood-blend "shin-gyokai" stock made from ten kinds of fish), toppings center on chashu pork, and sides include karaage and gyoza. No plant-based ramen is offered.
address_ja: 京都府京都市左京区一乗寺赤ノ宮町20
geocode_note: GSI address search returned the exact street address; coordinates fall within the Kyoto box.
seats_text: 9 counter seats arranged in a U-shape around the open kitchen; easy for solo diners.
hours_raw: 11:00-05:00 (next day) daily; lunch sets available 11:00-14:00. (Note: the English Tabelog listing shows an older 11:00-22:00 with Tuesday closed; HotPepper/Gnavi list 11:00-05:00 open year-round.)
closed_days: None / open year-round (per HotPepper and Gnavi).
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny 9-seat late-night counter on Ichijoji's ramen street with no English support or allergen handling — comfortable to visit with a guide who can navigate the cash-only ordering and language.
caveats: Hours differ between listings: the English Tabelog page shows 11:00-22:00 closed Tuesday, while HotPepper and Gnavi show 11:00-05:00 open daily; confirm before a late visit. Cash only. Not safe for celiac or vegan/vegetarian diners.

bio.background: Ramen Okura sits on Ichijoji's famous ramen street in northeast Kyoto, a short walk from Eizan Ichijoji Station. It's a small, no-frills counter shop marked by a large red lantern and a neon sign, the kind of place that runs from lunch deep into the early morning for students, night workers and ramen-crawlers.
bio.specialty: The kitchen lets you pick your broth: shoyu, shio, a punchy garlic bowl, a rich kotteri, and a 'new seafood' (shin-gyokai) ramen built on a stock blended from ten kinds of fish for a clean, collagen-rich umami. The garlic ramen — crisp fried garlic, garlic-miso bean sprouts and a heap of green onion — is a local favorite.
bio.philosophy: Generous portions and honest value: lunch sets (11:00-14:00) add just 50 yen to a bowl and come with free rice refills, and the U-shaped counter is built so a solo diner can slip in and eat well without ceremony.

anecdotes.text: The seafood ramen leans on a broth made from ten types of fish ground to a powder and simmered in, giving a fish-forward, collagen-rich bowl the shop markets as especially popular with women.
Beyond ramen the menu spreads into yakibuta-don (roast pork rice bowl), karaage, gyoza and kimchi — set up as a late-night neighborhood spot rather than a single-bowl specialist.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Shoyu and shio tare at a standard Japanese ramen shop are made with conventional wheat-based soy sauce; no gluten-free soy sauce is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths are animal-based (pork/chicken and a ten-fish seafood blend) and the shared kitchen also fries karaage and cooks gyoza, so no plant-based bowl can be assumed clean.

cuisine: Chicken-based ramen (tori-paitan, shoyu chuka-soba, half-and-half, abura soba)
neighborhood: Kitashirakawa / Chayama (Sakyo-ku)
address_ja: 京都府京都市左京区北白川西伊織町13-5
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 京都府京都市左京区北白川西伊織町13-5 returned [135.788712, 35.037693], within the Kyoto box.
seats_text: 9 seats: 5 counter seats plus one table for 4.
hours_raw: Tue-Sat 11:00-14:00 (L.O. ~14:30 per Tabelog) and 17:00-21:00; Sun & holidays 11:00-15:00; closed Monday. Sources vary slightly on lunch close (14:00 on blogs, 15:00 on Tabelog) and evening open (17:00 vs 17:30).
closed_days: Monday (and irregular closures noted)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny owner-run neighborhood counter shop with no English menu noted; basic Japanese or a translation app will help.
gf_detail: Standard wheat ramen: straight wheat noodles in a chicken broth seasoned with a shoyu (wheat-containing soy sauce) tare, plus an abura soba (wheat noodle) option. No gluten-free items or accommodations are indicated. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Every bowl is built on chicken broth (the shop uses female local chicken bones), with pork chashu, and the broth/tare contain animal products throughout. No vegan or vegetarian ramen is offered.

bio.background: The owner took up ramen seriously later in life, studying the craft on his own after turning 50, and opened this small shop in a quiet Kitashirakawa residential corner just south of the famous Ichijoji ramen district on October 14, 2021. Despite the weathered, old-fashioned lantern signage that makes it look like a decades-old institution, it is a relatively new arrival.
bio.specialty: Chicken-forward ramen made entirely from poultry stock. The clear shoyu chuka-soba is the chef's calling card, with a clean, transparent broth and medium-straight noodles, while the rich tori-paitan (chicken white broth) is praised as so deep it tastes almost like it contains pork bones. The Half & Half pairs a transparent shoyu seitan broth with the creamy chicken paitan in a single bowl that grows richer as you stir.
bio.philosophy: He set out simply to become the neighborhood's beloved ramen shop, building bowls on pure chicken stock with female local chicken and refusing to muddy the soup. Offering a plain kake ramen for just 500 yen is read as quiet confidence in the broth and noodles alone.

anecdotes.text: The chef began studying ramen independently after turning 50 and opened the shop on October 14, 2021, driven by the wish to become a local favorite.

The signature shoyu broth is made from female chicken bones and described as a clear soup that 'penetrates the heart,' served with tender chashu carrying sweet fat notes; an oversized soy-and-garlic karaage over seasoned bean sprouts is a beloved side.
The Half & Half layers a clean shoyu seitan broth beneath a rich chicken paitan, so the bowl transitions from light to increasingly rich as the diner mixes it.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The shoyu (soy sauce) tare that flavors the broth is standard Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat.

caveats: Reported hours differ slightly between sources: blogs list lunch as 11:00-14:00 and evening open at 17:00 or 17:30, while Tabelog shows lunch to 15:00 (L.O. 14:30); closures are noted as irregular beyond the regular Monday. Solo owner-operated, so hours can shift. No official website found; the shop is active on Instagram/Twitter as @ramennagatadesu (not independently verified here).

lead: 京都ラーメン きんざん 七条本店
name_ja: 京都ラーメン きんざん 七条本店
cuisine: Kyoto-style black shoyu tonkotsu ramen (chuka-soba)
neighborhood: Nishioji / Shichijo (Shimogyo-ku)
gf_detail: Not suitable for celiac/strict gluten-free diners. The ramen is wheat noodles (from Kondo Seimen) in a pork-bone broth seasoned with wheat-based aged soy sauce tare. No gluten-free menu, no dedicated preparation, and shared wheat-noodle boiling water create unavoidable gluten exposure. No published allergen accommodation.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly option. The house specialty is a pork-bone (tonkotsu) broth finished with back fat and topped with two kinds of pork chashu; even the soy tare sits on an animal base. No plant-based broth or vegan bowl is offered.
address_ja: 京都府京都市下京区西七条南月読町92 山住ビル 1F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI from the chō address (西七条南月読町92); result lng 135.730301, lat 34.989094 falls within the Kyoto bounding box.
seats_text: About 15 seats: an L-shaped counter (around 7 seats) plus two tables. Compact, with an open kitchen.
hours_raw: 11:30-14:30 / 18:00-23:30 (last orders 14:30 and 23:30). Closed Sundays plus occasional irregular closures. Note: an English listing alternatively shows 11:30-14:00 / 18:00-02:00 daily, so confirm before a late visit.
closed_days: Sundays plus occasional irregular closures
cultural_comfort_note: A small, busy neighborhood ramen counter with ticket-style ordering and an open kitchen; little to no English, so a few basic Japanese phrases (or pointing at the menu) make it smoother.

bio.background: Kinzan opened in 2013 in the quiet Nishioji-Shichijo residential pocket of Shimogyo-ku, a roughly 15-minute walk from JR Nishioji Station. The owner trained at the respected Takabashi Ramen before striking out on his own, and the shop has since grown into a local favorite popular enough to open a second branch near Kyoto City Hall.
bio.specialty: The signature bowl is a 'Kyoto black shoyu' (京都黒醤油) ramen: a glossy, near-jet-black soup built on a pork-bone base, given sharpness and depth by aged soy sauce and a mellow sweetness by back fat. It is finished with two styles of Kyoto Highland pork chashu (lean and fatty, torched to order), Kujo green onion, bean sprouts, menma, and a slick of back fat. Straight medium-thin noodles from the long-established Kondo Seimen give a firm, wheaty bite.
bio.philosophy: A Kyoto-rooted approach: classic local-style tonkotsu-shoyu done with carefully chosen regional ingredients (Kyotanba/Kyoto Highland pork from Hiyoshi Farm, Kujo negi, aged soy from Oyama and Goko breweries, Kondo noodles), cooked in an open kitchen so guests can watch the bowl come together.

anecdotes.text: The owner trained at the well-regarded Takabashi Ramen before opening Kinzan in 2013.
The chashu is cut from Kyoto Highland (Kyotanba) pork raised at Hiyoshi Farm and torched to order, and the noodles come from the long-established Kondo Seimen.
Students get a discount (about 100 yen off, with a free size-up).

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles are boiled in shared water, and the soy-sauce tare is wheat-based, so gluten contact is inherent to every bowl.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The black soup is seasoned with aged soy sauce (from Oyama and Goko breweries), which is wheat-based.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is pork-bone based and finished with back fat; pork chashu tops the bowls, so there is no vegan-safe preparation line.

cuisine: Shiru-nashi tare-soba / abura-soba (soupless mixed noodles)
gf_detail: No gluten-free options. The dish is wheat ramen noodles dressed with a soy-sauce-based tare (soy sauce contains wheat), white vinegar and chili oil, topped with pork chashu. No GF noodles, GF tamari, or allergen accommodation is advertised anywhere. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian options. Every taresoba bowl comes with pork-belly chashu, and toppings/sides lean on meat (assorted chashu, mini chashu rice bowl) and egg (onsen tamago). The tare is soy-sauce based with no advertised plant-only build. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区西洞院通六角下る池須町423-2
geocode_note: Building-level geocode of the full street address returned 135.751709, 35.010365 with a mismatched chō title (西三坊堀川町), so used the chō-level result for 中京区池須町 (135.75528, 35.006748), whose title matches the actual address. Both points fall within the Kyoto box.
neighborhood: Karasuma / Shijo (Nishinotoin, Nakagyo-ku)
seats_text: 11 counter seats only
closed_days: Open daily (no regular closing day; occasional ad-hoc closures)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny 11-seat counter shop where ordering is simple (pick a sauce, mix it yourself) and a few words of Japanese plus pointing will carry you through comfortably.

bio.background: Osamu Shinkai, a Tokyo native, spent years as a head chef in hotel Chinese and yakiniku kitchens before relocating to Kyoto in 2023 to open his own counter shop devoted to a single idea.
bio.specialty: Taresoba (soupless mixed noodles): the signature soy-sauce bowl tops springy noodles with pork-belly chashu, chopped onion, white sesame, scallion and bamboo shoot, which you mix vigorously with a tare of special soy sauce, oil, white vinegar and umami chili oil before eating; spicy and a chilled mustard-sauce version round out the lineup.
bio.philosophy: Rather than building a bowl around a rich broth, the shop strips the soup away entirely so the noodles themselves take center stage, with a sauce blended in a 'golden ratio' meant to flatter the noodle and let diners boldly mix and customize to taste.

anecdotes.text: The shop opened on September 22, 2023 on a quiet lane off Nishinotoin near Karasuma, bringing a Tokyo-born take on soupless ramen to central Kyoto.
Diners are encouraged to fine-tune noodle firmness and topping amounts, and to add the table-side white vinegar and rayu themselves before mixing.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The tare is built on a special soy sauce, which in Japan is standard wheat-brewed shoyu; combined with wheat noodles this bowl is not gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Every bowl is finished with pork-belly chashu and shares a counter kitchen with meat and egg toppings, so no plant-only preparation is offered.

caveats: Address ward differs across sources: Tabelog and the official site list 中京区 (Nakagyo-ku); the target brief said Shimogyo-ku — Nakagyo-ku is correct. Building-level GSI geocode returned a chō title that did not match the address, so a chō-level (池須町) geocode was used. Hours are well-attested but minor day-specific closures can occur; no reservations taken.

cuisine: Mazesoba / abura-soba (mixed noodles)
gf_detail: This is a wheat-noodle mazesoba shop. The signature bowl is built on wheat ramen-style noodles tossed with a savory tare, topped with chashu (pork), soft egg, menma, Korean seaweed and scallion. Wheat noodles plus a typically wheat-based shoyu-style tare and pork toppings make this unsuitable for celiac diners. No gluten-free preparation, no dedicated equipment, and no allergen handling is advertised. Not safe for gluten-free.
vegan_detail: The only documented bowl is animal-based: chashu pork, soft-boiled egg, menma and Korean seaweed over wheat noodles, with no advertised vegetarian or vegan option. There is no indication any plant-based version is available. Not suitable for vegans.
address_ja: 京都府京都市南区東九条西山王町6
geocode_note: GSI geocode of address returned [135.760239, 34.98267], within the Kyoto box.
seats_text: 6 counter seats only
hours_raw: 11:00-14:00 (lunch only); same premises operates as a Korean restaurant in the evening. 定休日 不定休 (irregular closures).
closed_days: Irregular closures (不定休); no fixed weekly closed day listed.
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny 6-seat counter lunch spot with no English advertised; a little Japanese and pointing at the single mazesoba goes a long way.
neighborhood: Higashi-kujo, Minami-ku (Kyoto Station Hachijo exit area)

bio.background: A daytime-only mixed-noodle counter near Kyoto Station's Hachijo exit that shares its kitchen with a Korean restaurant. By day the six seats are devoted to mazesoba; by night the same room becomes the Korean spot Hanbaibo Puruda (韓杯房プルダ). The mazesoba operation opened on July 3, 2025.
bio.specialty: Mazesoba (mixed noodles), regular size around 1,000 yen, topped with chashu pork, soft-boiled egg, menma, Korean seaweed and shredded scallion, with an optional soft-cooked egg add-on. The Korean-seaweed topping nods to the kitchen's evening Korean identity.

anecdotes.text: The mazesoba shop opened July 3, 2025, occupying the lunch hours of a space that serves as the Korean restaurant Hanbaibo Puruda in the evenings.

caveats: Cash only. Lunch-only mazesoba operation (roughly 11:00-14:00); the same six-seat space turns into a Korean eatery in the evening, so the mazesoba menu is daytime only. Closures are irregular, so check before a special trip.

lead: 京都 鶏白湯そば 純 四条烏丸
name_ja: 京都鶏白湯そば 純 四条烏丸
cuisine: Tori-paitan (chicken paitan) soba / ramen
neighborhood: Shijo-Karasuma (Nakagyo-ku)
gf_detail: This is a chicken-paitan ramen specialist built around custom medium-thick wheat noodles served in a creamy chicken-and-pork-bone broth, with seasoning tare and shoyu/miso variants that typically contain wheat. No gluten-free noodle, dedicated prep, or celiac accommodation is mentioned anywhere. Not safe for celiacs; a strictly gluten-free diner should not eat here.
vegan_detail: Every bowl is built on an animal base: a rich chicken (tori-paitan) broth made primarily with Kagoshima chicken, supplemented with Miyazaki and Oita pork bones, and topped with rare pork chashu. There are no vegan or vegetarian ramen options listed. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区橋弁慶町222 ヒライビル1F
geocode_note: GSI geocoded to 中京区橋弁慶町222 at [135.758972, 35.005989], within the Kyoto box. Note: the shop is in Nakagyo-ku (per Tabelog and the official site), not Shimogyo-ku as the target line stated; it sits just north of Shijo near Karasuma, marketed as the "Shijo-Karasuma" branch.
seats_text: Around 11-12 counter seats; counter-only, well suited to solo diners. (Tabelog lists 12; a local guide lists 11.)
hours_raw: Lunch 11:00-15:00 (L.O. 14:30); Dinner 17:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:30). Closed Wednesdays per local guides; the official site states year-round open with no Obon closure, so hours may vary — confirm before visiting.
closed_days: Wednesday (per local guides; the official site advertises year-round open — verify)
cultural_comfort_note: A modern, tidy counter shop comfortable for newcomers and overseas visitors, though staff likely operate mainly in Japanese, so a friendly greeting and pointing at the menu go a long way.

bio.background: Owner Sakakimoto came to ramen the long way around, spending some fifteen years training in kappo (Japanese fine-dining) and sushi kitchens before striking out on his own. That fine-dining grounding shows in every detail of the bowl.
bio.origin: This is the second outpost of the Jun chicken-paitan brand, following the original near Marutamachi; it opened in the Shijo-Karasuma area on August 13, 2025.
bio.specialty: The signature is a tori-paitan (chicken white-soup) soba in an espuma-like, foamed broth. Rather than ladling from one big pot, the kitchen emulsifies the chicken soup bowl by bowl in a blender, whipping it into a mild, airy, almost cappuccino-like froth. The base is built primarily on Kagoshima chicken, rounded out with Miyazaki and Oita pork bones, and paired with custom medium-thick, chewy noodles and rare pork chashu.
bio.philosophy: Sakakimoto has said that if he was going to open his own place, he wanted to do something only he could do — hence the labor-intensive, single-serving emulsified broth and the kappo-trained attention to finish and presentation.

anecdotes.text: The bowl is built to be played with: a trio of flavored vinegars (including apple and orange) let you tune the soup as you go, and regulars finish by adding rice to the leftover broth — or a hit of pepper — for a carbonara-like second act.
Unlike the original Marutamachi shop, this branch adds wontons and rare chashu to the lineup, with a yuzu-salt foamed chicken paitan and a miso version among the headline bowls.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The shoyu and miso seasoning variants of the chicken-paitan soba are based on tare/sauces that conventionally contain wheat; no wheat-free seasoning is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broths are animal-based (chicken paitan plus pork bones) and bowls are topped with pork chashu, so there is no vegan-safe preparation; cross-contact with meat and animal stock is inherent.

caveats: Address ward corrected to Nakagyo-ku (Tabelog and official site), not Shimogyo-ku. Hours/closed-day conflict between sources: two local guides report closed Wednesdays and dinner ending 21:00, while the official site advertises year-round open (and Tabelog shows dinner to 22:00) — flagged irregular; verify before visiting. Seat count is ~11-12 (Tabelog 12, local guide 11). No allergen, gluten-free, or vegan/vegetarian accommodation is documented at this shop; do not represent it as safe for celiac or vegan diners.

cuisine: 二郎系ラーメン（ぼっかけ二郎・醤油/塩、和牛脂スープ）
vegan_detail: A Jiro-style ramen shop built around a beef-fat broth (rendered fat from 48-month-old female cattle), wheat noodles, and beef-tendon (bokkake) toppings. There is no vegan or vegetarian bowl here; the entire concept is meat-forward, so a plant-based diner has nothing safe to order.
gf_detail: This is conventional Jiro-style ramen: wheat noodles plus a soy-sauce (shoyu) or salt tare, served in an animal-fat broth. Wheat noodles and standard shoyu both contain gluten, and no gluten-free option or gluten-handling practice is mentioned anywhere. Not suitable for celiacs; treat as unsafe.
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区八百屋町112-2
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 京都府京都市中京区八百屋町112-2; within the Kyoto box.
neighborhood: 中京区・京都河原町（阪急 京都河原町駅 徒歩約5分、八百屋町）
seats_text: 15 counter seats; all non-smoking. The premises can accommodate private groups of up to about 20.
hours_raw: 11:00-22:00, open daily (no regular closed day listed). Note: the ramen offering reportedly runs alongside the evening izakaya operation; lunchtime is recommended for the ramen without a mandatory drink order.
closed_days: No regular closed day listed.
cultural_comfort_note: A small counter ramen shop sharing premises with an izakaya; no English information is available, so a visitor will likely need a guide or translation help to navigate ordering and the Jiro-style customs.
caveats: Allergen and cross-contact handling are not documented anywhere we could find. The shop shares its location with the tuna-and-wagyu izakaya Toyota, and the ramen is reportedly best experienced at lunch (the evening operation centers on the izakaya with drink orders). Hours and the ramen-serving window may shift, so confirm before visiting.

bio.background: Toyojiro opened on December 1, 2024 in the Kawaramachi area of Kyoto, growing out of the popular Kiyamachi tuna-and-wagyu izakaya Toyota and sharing its premises. It represents that team's leap from sake-side beef and tuna plates into a dedicated Jiro-style ramen concept.
bio.specialty: A wagyu-forward Jiro-style bowl: a broth built on rendered fat from 48-month-old female cattle from Okazaki Ranch, finished with a soy-sauce or salt tare and crowned with premium wagyu fat. The house signature carries over the izakaya's bokkake (slow-stewed beef tendon) as a topping, giving a bokkake-jiro that fuses wagyu and ramen.
bio.philosophy: To elevate the famously rough, fat-heavy Jiro genre with high-grade wagyu fat rather than ordinary pork back-fat, aiming for a smooth, deep umami that gives ramen fans a more refined take on the style.

anecdotes.text: The shop is an offshoot of the tuna-and-wagyu izakaya Toyota and runs from the same Yaoyacho premises, carrying over that bar's signature bokkake (stewed beef tendon) into the ramen.
The broth's defining ingredient is fat from 48-month-old female cattle from Okazaki Ranch, chosen for its melt-on-the-tongue quality and deep umami.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The bowls are offered in a soy-sauce (shoyu) or salt tare; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, and no wheat-free tare is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The broth is made from rendered beef fat and the toppings are beef tendon and wagyu fat, so the entire kitchen workflow is meat-based.

cuisine: Kyoto-style white miso ramen (specialist)
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature bowl is built on wheat ramen noodles and is topped with fresh fu (生麩, wheat gluten), so there are multiple unavoidable wheat sources in the dish itself. The broth is a white-miso tare with bonito/kombu dashi (miso typically contains wheat or barley as well). No gluten-free menu, dedicated equipment, or celiac accommodation is indicated. A celiac diner cannot eat the ramen here safely.
vegan_detail: Not vegan or vegetarian. Both signature bowls are built on animal stock — bonito (katsuobushi)/kombu dashi or a shellfish (kai) dashi — and are topped with smoked chashu pork and a soft-boiled egg. While the yuba (tofu skin) and fresh fu toppings are plant-based, the broth foundation is fish/shellfish-based, so the ramen is not suitable for vegans or vegetarians. No vegan version is advertised.
address_ja: 京都府京都市北区紫野東野町20-4
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates [135.7444, 35.041245] for 京都府京都市北区紫野東野町20-4; within the Kyoto bounding box (lat 34.93–35.13, lng 135.66–135.83).
neighborhood: 北区・紫野東野町（大徳寺／建勲神社の北大路エリア）
seats_text: Seat count not disclosed; described as a small, calm renovated Kyoto townhouse (machiya) where even solo diners feel comfortable.
hours_raw: 11:00-15:00 / 17:00-20:30 (soup-out close possible). Closed days irregular (不定休) — specific weekday closures not published, so the same hours are listed for all days with an irregular-schedule flag.
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular; no fixed closed day published)
cultural_comfort_note: A small neighborhood ramen specialist in a renovated machiya; menus and service are in Japanese, so a translation app or some basic Japanese helps, though ordering a signature bowl is straightforward.

bio.background: Menya Ichiki bills itself as a Kyoto white-miso ramen specialist. It first opened on October 6, 2025 as a tucked-away shop in the Nishijin area of Kamigyo Ward, then relocated and reopened on April 22, 2026 in a renovated Kyoto townhouse (machiya) in Shikino Higashino-cho, in the Kita Ward area near Daitoku-ji and Kenkun Shrine.
bio.specialty: The signature bowl is the 京の白味噌らーめん (Kyo no Shiro-miso Ramen): an original tare blending several Kyoto white misos for a gently sweet, creamy soup, married to a bonito-and-kombu dashi. A variation, the 京の白味噌貝だしらーめん, swaps in a shellfish (kai) dashi. Bowls come dressed in distinctly Kyoto ingredients — fresh yuba (tofu skin) and fresh fu (wheat gluten) — alongside smoked chashu pork, a soft-boiled egg, white negi and bonito flakes. The chashu is torched tableside to coax out its aroma and umami. Thick, straight noodles with a firm chew round it out. A rice-and-fried-chicken set can be added.
bio.philosophy: The shop leans into local Kyoto identity — white miso, yuba and fu — to set its bowls apart from typical tonkotsu or shoyu ramen, and keeps a calm, welcoming room where even solo diners are at ease.

anecdotes.text: Promoted as one of Kyoto's first white-miso ramen specialty shops, leaning on Kyoto white miso, fresh yuba and fresh fu for a distinctly local bowl.
Opened October 6, 2025 as a hidden-gem shop in a Nishijin residential neighborhood, then relocated and reopened April 22, 2026 in a renovated machiya near Kenkun Shrine.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The dish itself contains multiple wheat sources — wheat ramen noodles plus a fresh fu (wheat gluten) topping — so cross-contamination is moot; the ramen is inherently not gluten-free.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is a white-miso tare; Kyoto white miso commonly contains wheat or barley, and the dashi is bonito/kombu or shellfish based. No wheat-free tare is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is built on bonito/kombu or shellfish dashi and bowls include smoked chashu pork and egg; plant toppings (yuba, fu) sit in an animal-based soup, so a vegan-safe bowl is not offered.

caveats: Seat count and specific closed days are not published; hours are 11:00-15:00 / 17:00-20:30 with possible soup-out early close and an irregular (不定休) schedule, so the same hours are applied to all seven days. Owner/chef name not found. The shop relocated April 22, 2026 from its original Kamigyo/Nishijin location to the current Kita Ward (Shikino Higashino-cho) address; the target Tabelog page reflects the new location.

lead: 麺屋 かがり 木屋町店
name_ja: 麺屋 かがり 木屋町店
cuisine: 鶏白湯・京風湯葉ラーメン（深夜営業）
neighborhood: 中京区紙屋町・木屋町（阪急 京都河原町駅 徒歩約3分）
gf_detail: A standard ramen shop: wheat noodles with a chicken/seafood (and tonkotsu) broth and a soy-based tare, which means wheat is present in both the noodles and the seasoning. No gluten-free menu, gluten-free noodles, or allergy accommodation is mentioned anywhere. Not suitable for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: All bowls are built on animal broths (chicken paitan, seafood, tonkotsu) and topped with seared chashu pork, so nothing on the menu is vegan. The fresh yuba (tofu skin) topping that is the shop's signature is itself plant-based, but it is served in meat/fish broth, so it does not make any bowl vegan. No vegan option is offered.
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区紙屋町365

hours_raw: 21:00–06:00 (翌朝まで). One source lists daily operation; another notes closed Wednesdays.
closed_days: 水曜（情報源により記載差あり。深夜～早朝営業）
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, locals-oriented late-night counter shop with no listed English support, so basic Japanese and a willingness to point at the menu will make ordering easiest.
caveats: Hours and closed day vary by source (one lists daily 21:00–06:00, another notes closed Wednesdays); confirm before a late-night visit. No allergy, gluten-free, or vegan information is available for this shop.

bio.background: Menya Kagari's Kiyamachi branch is a small, hard-to-find ramen shop tucked into a back alley off Kiyamachi Street, just a few minutes from Kyoto Kawaramachi Station. It keeps unusual late-night-into-dawn hours (roughly 9 p.m. to 6 a.m.), which has made it a quiet institution for night-shift workers winding down, drinkers looking for a closing bowl, and early risers wanting ramen for breakfast.
bio.specialty: The kitchen leans on a rich, opaque chicken paitan as well as a cleaner chicken-and-seafood soy broth. Its most distinctive bowl is a Kyoto-style ramen finished with fresh yuba (tofu skin): the delicate sheets soak up the soup and add a soft, local character you rarely see on a ramen counter. A charred-garlic black ramen rounds out the late-night lineup.
bio.philosophy: A neighborhood late-night refuge built around comforting, broth-forward bowls and a hidden-gem atmosphere rather than flash.

anecdotes.text: Regulars describe it as a hidden gem worth the hunt down a Kiyamachi side alley near H&M, prized for its fresh-yuba ramen that pairs surprisingly well with the soy broth.
Locals favor it as a late-night and post-drinking spot as well as an early-morning meal, thanks to its 21:00–06:00 hours.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The shop's soy-based (shoyu) ramen uses a standard soy tare, which in Japanese ramen virtually always contains wheat; combined with wheat noodles this bowl is not gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Bowls are built on chicken, seafood, and tonkotsu (pork) broths and topped with seared chashu pork; the plant-based yuba topping is served in these animal broths.

lead: 熊本ラーメン 肥後もっこす
name_ja: 熊本ラーメン 肥後もっこす
cuisine: 熊本ラーメン（こってり豚骨、焦がしマー油）
neighborhood: 北区・北山（地下鉄烏丸線 北山駅すぐ、京都府立植物園近く）
address_ja: 京都府京都市北区上賀茂岩ヶ垣内町98-10
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 上賀茂岩ヶ垣内町98 returned [135.765503, 35.051552]; within Kyoto box, near Kitayama Station and the Kyoto Prefectural Botanical Garden.
seats_text: 18 seats (4 counter seats, 14 table seats)
closed_days: 月曜日（Mondays）
cultural_comfort_note: A small neighborhood ramen counter; expect Japanese-only ordering, so a little Japanese or pointing at the menu helps.
gf_detail: This is a classic Kumamoto-style tonkotsu ramen shop. The noodles are wheat, and the tare/seasoning and accompaniments (gyoza, karaage, paiku rice simmered in soy sauce) are wheat-based. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is advertised; not suitable for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: The entire menu is built on a rich pork-bone (tonkotsu) broth with pork toppings, plus chicken karaage and soy-braised pork rice. No vegetarian or vegan ramen is offered.
caveats: No official website found. Story/owner background is thin in available sources; signature-dish and broth details drawn from a Kyoto food blog and the listing page. No dietary-accommodation information could be confirmed.

bio.background: A small ramen counter sitting steps from Kitayama Station and across from the Kyoto Prefectural Botanical Garden, serving the Kumamoto style of tonkotsu in a city better known for lighter Kyoto ramen. The name itself is a wink to its roots: 'mokkosu' is Kumamoto dialect for a stubborn, headstrong, true-to-yourself sort of person.
bio.specialty: Kumamoto ramen: a thick, milky-white tonkotsu (pork-bone) broth finished with fragrant burnt-garlic ma-yu oil, served with medium-thin straight noodles. A favorite side is the paiku rice (パイクごはん) topped with pork slow-simmered for two days in a house soy-based sauce.
bio.philosophy: Diners describe an owner with a meticulous, conscientious touch in the service and presentation, leaning into a robust, full-bodied bowl rather than the lighter Hakata-style tonkotsu.

anecdotes.text: The shop name 'mokkosu' comes from Kumamoto dialect meaning a stubborn, headstrong person, signaling its proudly Kumamoto identity in Kyoto.
Its tonkotsu is noted as notably concentrated and sticky-rich compared with lighter Hakata-style bowls, finished with fried-garlic aroma.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles are boiled in shared water; not gluten-free.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Paiku rice is pork simmered in a soy-based sauce, and tonkotsu tare typically uses wheat-containing soy sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is pork-bone based with pork and chicken items throughout the menu; no vegan-safe preparation.

lead: 濃厚豚骨ラーメン 岩本家
name_ja: 濃厚豚骨ラーメン 岩本家
cuisine: 家系・濃厚豚骨醤油ラーメン
gf_detail: This is a Yokohama iekei (家系) ramen shop built on wheat ramen noodles (from Tokyo's Sugano Seimen / 菅野製麺所), a wheat-based shoyu tare, and a thick pork-and-chicken bone broth — none of which is gluten-free. No gluten-free noodles, tare, or menu options are advertised, and there is no indication of allergen handling. Not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
vegan_detail: A pork-bone (tonkotsu) and chicken broth iekei specialist; the signature bowl is topped with chashu pork, spinach, and nori. The broth, tare, and toppings are animal-based with no vegan or vegetarian ramen offered. Not suitable for vegans.
address_ja: 京都府京都市右京区西院巽町4 西東ビル1F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI from address 京都府京都市右京区西院巽町4; first feature coordinates [135.730377, 35.003315], within the Kyoto box. Matches the Saiin / Hankyu Saiin Station vicinity described by sources.
neighborhood: 右京区 西院（西院巽町・阪急 西院駅 徒歩約2分）
seats_text: About 24 seats: 12 counter seats at the front plus three partitioned 4-person tables in a long, narrow back room.
hours_raw: 11:00–翌1:00（L.O. 0:30、スープ無くなり次第終了）／年中無休
closed_days: 年中無休（無休）。ただしスープが無くなり次第終了。
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, cash-only late-night ramen counter where ordering is quick and simple; a guide or basic Japanese helps, as English support and allergen accommodation should not be assumed.

bio.background: Iwamotoya occupies the former 麺匠 一粒万倍 西院店, a branch of a Kyoto ramen group known for lighter chicken-based clear shoyu bowls. In August 2025 this Saiin location was completely reimagined and reopened on August 11, 2025 as a dedicated Yokohama-style iekei (家系) tonkotsu-shoyu shop, trading the group's delicate house style for something heartier aimed at late-night and younger diners in the Saiin area.
bio.specialty: The signature bowl is a straightforward iekei 'Ramen' (around 950 yen): a thick pork-and-chicken bone broth with a soy tare, topped in classic family-style fashion with a slice of chashu pork, spinach, and three sheets of nori. The noodles are sourced from Tokyo's well-known Sugano Seimen (菅野製麺所) and served a touch shorter than typical iekei noodles. Free oolong tea and a rice pairing round out the experience.
bio.philosophy: Rather than leaning on surface oil, the shop builds its identity on a genuinely concentrated, dense broth that still keeps a proper drinkable fluidity — an honest take on iekei conventions that aims for real depth of bone-and-marrow character rather than formulaic repetition.

anecdotes.text: The shop is a full rebrand of the former 麺匠 一粒万倍 西院店, swapping that group's signature light chicken-shoyu style for a bold tonkotsu iekei concept when it reopened on August 11, 2025.
A ramen critic praised the broth as '濃厚で濃密な味わい' (concentrated, dense), noting the shop achieves legitimate originality within iekei conventions while staying authentically in the category.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core menu is wheat ramen noodles in a shared pot, with a wheat-based shoyu tare and animal broth; no gluten-free preparation or separation is described.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: An iekei tonkotsu-shoyu shop whose tare is soy-sauce based; standard Japanese shoyu contains wheat, and no wheat-free tare is offered.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is pork-bone and chicken based and bowls are topped with chashu pork; no vegan or vegetarian option exists, so meat-free dining is not viable.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen information or accommodation is noted in available reviews or shop information.

caveats: Opened August 11, 2025 as a full rebrand of the former 麺匠 一粒万倍 西院店. Seat count (~24: 12 counter + 3 four-tops) and hours are from a detailed review and shop listing; exact prices beyond the ~950 yen base bowl were not fully confirmed. Cash-only and no-reservation policies confirmed via the listing. No allergen or gluten-free/vegan accommodation is documented; dedicated fryer status unknown.

cuisine: 京都系中華そば・町中華（鶏ガラ＋豚骨の淡麗醤油、背脂少々）
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation. This is a classic Kyoto-style chuka soba shop: wheat ramen noodles in a chicken-and-pork shoyu broth, where the soy-based tare almost certainly contains wheat. Sides (gyoza, fried chicken, egg-rice sets) are also wheat/flour-based. Not suitable for celiacs; no dedicated handling found.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly shop. The signature bowls are built on a chicken-bone and pork-bone broth with a touch of back-fat, and the menu centers on chashu ramen, gyoza, and meat/egg sets. No plant-based ramen or vegan options were found.
address_ja: 京都府京都市山科区日ノ岡鴨土町40-15
geocode_note: Geocoded from full address via GSI; within Kyoto box. ~1 min walk from Goryo (御陵) Station Exit 4 on the Tozai subway line.
neighborhood: 山科区（御陵駅近く）
seats_text: 16 seats total: 8 at the counter plus two 4-person tables.
closed_days: 日曜（Sundays）
cultural_comfort_note: A friendly, no-frills neighborhood diner run for locals; expect Japanese-only service and a casual, all-smoking room, so a little Japanese and easygoing manners go a long way.

bio.background: A long-standing neighborhood machi-chuka (town Chinese) diner sitting almost on top of Goryo Station on the Kyoto subway Tozai line, the kind of homey, street-side counter spot where local families and solo regulars have eaten for years.
bio.specialty: Kyoto-style chuka soba / chashu-men: a clear, light-tasting shoyu soup built on chicken and pork bones with just a touch of back-fat, served with medium-thin straight noodles that ride the broth cleanly. The kitchen rounds it out with gyoza, fried chicken, and affordable rice-and-set combinations.
bio.philosophy: Approachable, homemade comfort food at honest prices, welcoming to solo diners, families, and groups alike.

anecdotes.text: Regulars describe the bowl as nostalgic comfort food, light enough to finish easily, in the classic Kyoto chuka-soba mold.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles are boiled in shared water and the broth/tare is built on a wheat-containing soy base; no gluten-free preparation exists here.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Shoyu-forward Kyoto-style tare; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat and there is no indication of a wheat-free alternative.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is chicken- and pork-bone based with back-fat; menu is meat- and egg-centric, so cross-contact with animal products is pervasive throughout the kitchen.

caveats: Two address listings differ in chō name (日ノ岡鴨土町 per Tabelog/Hotpepper/Gnavi vs 御陵鴨戸町 per a map listing); used the consistent majority address 日ノ岡鴨土町40-15. Hours and the Sunday closure are consistent across Hotpepper and Gnavi; Tabelog's English page suggested daily operation, treated as less reliable. All seating allows smoking.

lead: 油そば 大革命 金閣寺店
name_ja: 油そば 大革命 金閣寺店
cuisine: 油そば・あえ麺（まぜそば系）
gf_detail: This is a wheat-noodle abura soba / ae-men shop. The noodles are wheat, the tare (sauce that coats the bowl) is soy-sauce-based and almost certainly contains wheat, and toppings include pork. There is no indication of any gluten-free option, gluten-free noodle, or allergy accommodation. Not suitable for celiac or gluten-avoidant diners.
vegan_detail: The core dish is built on pork (chashu / pork belly, meat-topped bowls) and a soy-based tare; meat-miso and meat-topped bowls are the signatures. There is no vegan or vegetarian bowl on the menu and no plant-based broth option. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
address_ja: 京都府京都市北区衣笠天神森町6-1
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 京都市北区衣笠天神森町; within the Kyoto bounding box.
neighborhood: 北区衣笠天神森町（金閣寺エリア／嵐電 北野白梅町駅）
seats_text: Counter seating; small shop, solo-diner friendly. Exact seat count not published.
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular holidays)
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, no-frills counter noodle shop popular with local Ritsumeikan students; staff are unlikely to speak much English, so order by pointing or a few Japanese words and you'll be fine.
caveats: Cash only. Irregular closures, so confirm before a special trip. The shop has changed concept rapidly: it was a きりん寺 (Kirin-ji) abura-soba franchise from 2021, became independent as 大革命 in April 2025, and around August 2025 reportedly shifted from oil-based abura soba to oil-free あえ麺 (ae-men) — the exact current menu may differ from older listings.

bio.background: A short walk from the famous Golden Pavilion (Kinkaku-ji), this is a casual, student-favorite noodle counter that started life in August 2021 as a branch of the abura-soba franchise Kirin-ji (きりん寺). In April 2025 the shop broke from the franchise and went independent under the bold new name 大革命 ("Great Revolution"). True to the name, the owner kept tinkering: around August 2025 the menu reportedly shifted away from classic oil-based abura soba toward oil-free あえ麺 (ae-men), where the sauce is mixed through the noodles rather than pooling with oil at the bottom of the bowl.
bio.specialty: The shop made its name on the 肉盛り油そば (meat-piled abura soba) — chewy, slightly wavy wheat noodles under a generous heap of soft, tender pork, eaten by mixing in chili oil (rayu) and vinegar to taste, with grated garlic and spicy meat-miso on the table to fine-tune each bite. In the ae-men era this carries over into hearty bowls like a niku-miso (spicy meat-miso) ae-men, often paired with karaage and rice as a set.
bio.philosophy: Hearty, junky, build-your-own-flavor comfort food that proposes 'a new way to enjoy abura soba' — a deliberate contrast to Kyoto's refined kaiseki tradition, aimed at filling up students and travelers cheaply and boldly.

anecdotes.text: The name 大革命 ('Great Revolution') marks the shop's independence from the Kirin-ji franchise in spring 2025, and the owner lived up to it by later dropping oil entirely and reworking the dish into mixed ae-men.

A Yahoo! News ramen expert highlighted the roughly ¥1,000 niku-mori abura soba as a 'junk and wild' Kyoto gem within walking distance of Kinkaku-ji, praising the tender, generous pork and chewy noodles.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The dish is built on a soy-sauce-based tare mixed through wheat noodles; standard Japanese soy sauce tare contains wheat, making the sauce as well as the noodles unsuitable for gluten avoidance.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Bowls center on pork (chashu / pork belly) and meat-miso, with karaage on set menus; the kitchen is meat-forward and offers no plant-based bowl.

lead: くそオヤジ最後のひとふり 河原町三条店
name_ja: くそオヤジ最後のひとふり 河原町三条店
name_en: Kuso Oyaji Saigo no Hitofuri Kawaramachi Sanjo
cuisine: 貝出汁ラーメン（あさり・しじみ・はまぐり）
neighborhood: 中京区恵比須町・三条河原町（地下鉄 京都市役所前駅 近く）
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区三条通河原町西入石橋町30
geocode_note: GSI returned no result for the full street address; retried at chō level (中京区石橋町) which resolved to lng 135.754669, lat 35.010994 — within the Kyoto box, at the Sanjo-dori / Kawaramachi area matching the listed location.
seats_text: 19 seats total: 1F counter (10 seats), 2F table seating (9 seats / 3 tables of 3)
hours_raw: 11:00–23:00 daily (last order around 22:30–23:30 depending on source; press release states 11:00–24:00 L.O. 23:30; Tabelog lists 11:00–23:00)
closed_days: 無休（年中無休）
cultural_comfort_note: A busy, popular ramen counter where you queue and order at the counter; little English support is expected, so a non-Japanese speaker may want a translated menu or guidance, but pointing at the clam-ramen options works fine.
vegan_detail: This is a shellfish-broth ramen specialist — every bowl (asari/shijimi/hamaguri) is built on a clam dashi with soy-sauce tare and contains clams; there are no vegan or vegetarian ramen options.
gf_detail: Standard ramen: wheat (medium-thick straight) noodles plus a soy-sauce-based tare (typically wheat-containing). Not celiac-safe and no gluten-free menu is indicated. The broth itself is clam dashi, but the noodles and shoyu tare make the dish unsafe for gluten avoidance.

bio.background: This is the first Kyoto location of くそオヤジ最後のひとふり, a clam-broth ramen brand under Osaka's UNCHI Co., the company behind the celebrated 'Jinrui Mina Menrui' (人類みな麺類). The original Kuso Oyaji opened in Osaka's Juso district in April 2013, and the brand has since reached overseas markets including New York, China, Korea and Nepal. The Kawaramachi-Sanjo shop opened September 15, 2023, set into the Sanjo shopping arcade with a refined Japanese-style interior — counter seats on the first floor and tables on the second.
bio.specialty: Clam-broth ramen, with the あさりラーメン (asari/short-neck clam ramen, around 980–990 yen) as the signature bowl — a clean clam dashi balanced with a blend of dark and light soy sauce, served with chewy yet firm medium-thick straight noodles and clams as a topping. The shijimi (corbicula) and a limited hamaguri (clam) ramen round out the lineup.
bio.philosophy: The parent company's stated guiding idea is to 'change the world through the power of ramen,' and the broths are made with custom water-purification equipment to draw out a clean shellfish umami meant to be finished to the last drop.

anecdotes.text: The Osaka original opened in Juso in April 2013 and the brand has since expanded internationally to New York, China, Korea and Nepal before this first Kyoto outpost.
About a week after opening, an evening visit around 7 PM found roughly ten groups waiting in line — a sign of the strong early buzz around the Kyoto debut.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The bowls are built on a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare — described as a blend of dark and light soy sauce over clam dashi — which typically contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All ramen is shellfish-based (clam dashi with clam toppings); there is no vegan/vegetarian preparation, so cross-contact with shellfish is inherent to the menu.

caveats: Hours vary slightly by source: the opening press release cites 11:00–24:00 (L.O. 23:30) while the listing shows 11:00–23:00 — confirm late-night closing before a late visit. Payment is cash only. No official standalone website found; chain operated by UNCHI Co. (人類みな麺類 group).

cuisine: 町中華系 中華そば（自家製麺の醤油ラーメン）と焼飯のセットが看板 / Machi-chuka-style chuka soba (house-made-noodle shoyu ramen) paired with fried rice
gf_detail: No gluten-free option. The signature bowls are built on house-made wheat noodles (a Hokkaido "yume-chikara" wheat blend with whole-grain flour) in a pork-and-seafood shoyu broth, so the noodles, the soy-based tare, and the fried rice all contain wheat. There is no indication of any GF menu, dedicated equipment, or allergy accommodation. A celiac or gluten-sensitive diner cannot eat safely here.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The broth is a pork-and-dried-fish (niboshi/katsuobushi) double stock, the bowls feature rare char siu (pork), and the fried rice contains roast pork and egg. There are no plant-based ramen or rice options noted. A vegan would have nothing suitable to order.
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区三条通河原町西入石橋町14-1
geocode_note: GSI address search returned no result at the full street-number level; retried at chō level (石橋町, Nakagyō-ku) and used that centroid. Coordinates fall within the Kyoto box and correspond to the Sanjo-meiten-gai arcade area just west of Kawaramachi-Sanjo, matching the listed location.
neighborhood: 中京区・三条名店街（河原町三条西入／地下鉄 京都市役所前駅・京阪 三条駅 近く）
seats_text: About 25 seats: counter seating on the first floor with additional table/sofa seating upstairs on the second floor.
hours_raw: Weekdays (Mon–Fri): 11:00–15:00 and 17:30–21:00. Saturdays, Sundays and holidays: 11:00–21:00 (continuous). Oil-based ramen (abura soba) served only from 15:00. Last order around 21:00.
closed_days: No fixed regular holiday; closures are irregular.
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, busy arcade ramen shop with no English menu noted; a friendly greeting and pointing at the signature set will carry you through, but staff are unlikely to navigate detailed dietary requests in English.

roles: Operated by the team behind the Osaka-area shop 中華そば ふもと (Hirakata)
bio.background: Yakimeshi to Men Kyoto opened on April 2, 2025 in the Sanjo-meiten-gai arcade just west of Kawaramachi-Sanjo, as the second location of a brand built by the people behind the well-regarded Osaka-area ramen shop Chuka Soba Fumoto (中華そば ふもと) in Hirakata. The concept is right in the name and refreshingly honest: 'we want to eat fried rice AND we want to eat noodles' — so rather than treating the rice as an afterthought side, the shop puts equal craft into both.
bio.specialty: The signature is the char siu ramen paired with a half portion of fried rice (chashu-men half-yakimeshi set). The bowl is a pork-and-dried-fish double-stock shoyu ramen — pork depth lifted by niboshi (dried sardine) and a finish of shaved bonito — served with house-made straight noodles blending Hokkaido 'yume-chikara' wheat with whole-grain flour, boiled firm. It is crowned with petal-thin rare char siu marinated in red wine and soy. The fried rice is its own point of pride: shredded roast pork and fluffy egg cooked to be moist yet crumbly, with attention paid to the rice's moisture.
bio.philosophy: Take both halves of the classic machi-chuka pairing seriously — neither the noodles nor the rice plays second fiddle. Yuzu peel and cayenne are set out so diners can tune the bowl to taste, and an evening-only abura soba (oil noodles, from 15:00) gives a different way to enjoy the same house noodles.

anecdotes.text: The shop's name literally translates to 'Fried Rice and Noodles,' born from the idea that you shouldn't have to choose between the two — so the half-fried-rice set was made the headline order.
It sits in the Sanjo-meiten-gai arcade and quickly became part of a wave of new ramen shops turning the Sanjo–Kawaramachi blocks into a ramen battleground.

gf_cross_contamination.text: House-made wheat noodles and wheat-based shoyu tare are central to every bowl, and fried rice is cooked on the same line — heavy shared wheat presence throughout the kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is a soy-sauce (shoyu) ramen and the char siu is marinated in soy sauce; standard Japanese shoyu contains wheat, so the tare and marinade should be assumed to contain wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is a pork-and-dried-fish (niboshi, bonito) stock and dishes feature pork char siu and egg; no plant-based preparation is mentioned.

caveats: Hours and the irregular-closure pattern are from blog and listing sources dated to the April 2025 opening period and may shift; QR-code cashless payment is accepted but physical cards/e-money are not, so carry some cash. Coordinates are a chō-level (block) centroid because the full street number did not geocode. No allergy-accommodation or dedicated-equipment information was found from any source.

cuisine: つけ麺・ラーメン（自家製全粒粉麺、濃厚海老つけ汁／鶏魚介）
gf_detail: A tsukemen specialist whose entire identity is its house-made wheat noodles (stone-ground whole-grain flour from Nagano blended with refined wheat). The dipping broths are wheat-thickened/seasoned and rich in shrimp, chicken and fish. There is no gluten-free noodle or menu, no dedicated equipment, and noodles are boiled in shared water — not suitable for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: Every bowl is animal-based: the signature dip is built on more than a kilogram of sweet-shrimp heads, and the standard option is a chicken-and-seafood (鶏魚介) broth. No vegan or vegetarian option is offered.
address_ja: 京都府京都市伏見区西大手町313-1
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned [135.758652, 34.9328] for 京都府京都市伏見区西大手町313-1; within the Kyoto box.
neighborhood: 伏見区西大手町（京阪 伏見桃山駅／近鉄 桃山御陵前駅 近く）
seats_text: カウンター9席、3人掛けテーブル1卓、4人掛けテーブル2卓（計20席）
closed_days: 不定休（定休日は月ごとに公式SNSで告知）
cultural_comfort_note: A small, locals-beloved counter shop with no English menu noted; basic Japanese and a willingness to follow the posted eating-order instructions will make the visit smooth.
sources: https://minaju.info/lunch/2-16-京都市伏見区-伏見桃山-麺心-よしだ-つけ麺/

bio.background: 麺心よし田 is the shop Fushimi locals name first when the talk turns to tsukemen. Tucked just west of Fushimi-Momoyama / Momoyama-Goryomae stations, past the Otesuji shopping arcade, it has built a devoted following over years — regulars describe coming back dozens of times since their student days for noodles that taste reliably the same.
bio.specialty: The signature is a rich shrimp tsukemen (海老味噌つけ麺): a dipping broth simmered from more than a kilogram of sweet-shrimp heads with vegetables, yielding a deep, almost French-bisque-like richness that earns it the nickname of a 'French-inspired' tsukemen. The classic alternative is a chicken-and-seafood (鶏魚介) dip.
bio.philosophy: The heart of the shop is the house-made noodle — milled from Nagano stone-ground whole-grain flour blended with refined wheat, giving a soba-like fragrance. The kitchen deliberately leaves the cold-rinsed noodles slightly moist, believing that texture carries the wheat aroma best, and serves regular and large portions at the same price.

anecdotes.text: The shop hands diners a posted 'way of eating' (流儀): start with the noodles as-is, then layer in onion and citrus to shift the flavor in stages, and finish the leftover dip as a consommé-style soup.
The shrimp dip is built from over a kilogram of sweet-shrimp heads, lending the broth its distinctive concentrated, bisque-like body.
A longtime regular writes of having visited dozens of times since student days, praising the unwavering consistency of the bowl.
anecdotes.source: https://minaju.info/lunch/2-16-京都市伏見区-伏見桃山-麺心-よしだ-つけ麺/

sources: https://minaju.info/lunch/2-16-京都市伏見区-伏見桃山-麺心-よしだ-つけ麺/

gf_cross_contamination.text: Noodles are wheat-based (whole-grain + refined wheat flour) and boiled in shared water; no gluten-free option exists.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broths are animal-based (sweet-shrimp heads, chicken, seafood); shared cookware means no vegan-safe preparation.

cuisine: 担々麺・四川料理
gf_detail: This is a tantanmen (dan dan noodle) specialist. The bowls are built on wheat ramen noodles in a sesame-and-chili soup that involves soy-based seasoning and pork, so they are not safe for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
