There is no gluten-free menu, no published allergen information, and no dedicated preparation area. A celiac diner should not eat the noodle dishes here; even the mapo tofu set is served with elements that are not confirmed wheat-free. Treat as not gluten-free safe.
vegan_detail: Every signature bowl is meat-based: the tantanmen soups carry minced pork and the shop also features Sichuan mapo tofu, which traditionally uses minced pork and a meat/animal-based seasoning. There is no vegan or vegetarian tantanmen on the menu, and no published information about plant-based options. A vegan diner cannot be accommodated here in any reliable way.
address_ja: 京都府宇治市莵道丸山1-116
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned coordinates [lng 135.799728, lat 34.884445] for 京都府宇治市莵道丸山1-116. The longitude is within the expected range but the latitude (34.884) falls below the supplied Kyoto bounding box minimum of 34.93. This is correct and expected: the shop is in Uji City, which lies legitimately south of Kyoto City proper. Coordinates nulled per the out-of-box rule, but the geocode itself is accurate (Mimurodo area, Uji).
neighborhood: 宇治市・京阪三室戸駅すぐ（三室戸寺近く）
seats_text: About 17 seats: a counter (around 5-6 stools) plus a couple of small tables (two-, four-, and six-person). A compact, intimate space that fills up and often has a lunchtime queue.
hours_raw: 11:30-15:30 (lunch only). Listed as closed Saturdays; closures can be irregular, so confirm before a special trip.
closed_days: Saturday (closures may be irregular)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny neighborhood lunch counter with a ticket machine and a local crowd; basic Japanese and pointing at the ticket machine will carry you through comfortably.

bio.background: Sangokushi is a small, owner-run tantanmen specialist tucked beside Mimurodo Station in Uji, a one-minute walk from the station and close to the famous Mimuroto-ji temple. It has grown from a neighborhood lunch spot into one of the names ramen fans associate with Uji's tantanmen scene, drawing regular lunchtime lines despite its tiny size. In January 2026 its reputation carried far enough that the shop opened its first branch in the Rokujizo area.
bio.specialty: Tantanmen done four ways, all at the same flat price: a creamy white-sesame bowl that is rich but gentle, a deeper black-sesame version, the shop's most popular mala (numbing-spicy) tantanmen with selectable heat levels, and a brothless dry tantanmen for spice lovers. The kitchen also turns out a proper Sichuan mapo tofu set heavy on tongue-tingling Sichuan peppercorn. Wavy medium-thin noodles, finely minced pork that melts into the soup, and a layered sesame-umami-bitter-numbing profile are the house signatures.
bio.philosophy: Range over a single gimmick: the same tantanmen is offered from creamy-mild all the way to fiercely mala, with spice dialed to the eater rather than the cook, so first-timers and chili veterans sit at the same counter. A small self-made pork-soboro rice bowl is the classic companion.

anecdotes.text: The shop has been featured on television and is documented as drawing celebrity visitors, part of how a tiny Uji counter built an outsized following.
Its popularity grew enough that a first branch opened in the Rokujizo area in January 2026, with a white facade and pop-yellow entrance and its own exclusive pork rice bowl.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All bowls are tantanmen built on wheat ramen noodles cooked in a shared kitchen; there is no gluten-free menu or separate preparation, so cross-contact with wheat is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Tantanmen soups of this style use soy-based and sesame seasonings that typically contain wheat-based soy sauce; no allergen breakdown is published for this shop.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Soups carry minced pork and the menu includes pork-based mapo tofu, so animal products are throughout the kitchen; there is no vegan option to protect from cross-contact.

caveats: Lunch-only shop (11:30-15:30); closed Saturdays with potentially irregular closures, so confirm before a dedicated trip. Cash only with a ticket-machine system, no reservations, and a small counter that queues at peak. Geocode places it in Uji City (south of the Kyoto-City bounding box), which is correct for this address; coordinates nulled per the out-of-box rule. No official website found.

cuisine: Chinese (Cantonese) restaurant; signature four-chili tantanmen
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation found. This is a Cantonese Chinese restaurant whose signature is a chili-laden tantanmen — wheat noodles in a sesame/chili broth, almost certainly built on a wheat-based shoyu/miso tare and a pork base. No allergen or gluten-free information is published on the official site, and nothing indicates celiac-safe handling. A celiac diner should treat this place as not safe.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan venue. A traditional Cantonese kitchen with a meat- and seafood-forward menu (the famous tantanmen and black-vinegar sweet-and-sour pork are both meat dishes). No vegan ramen or vegan set is advertised, and tantanmen broth is typically pork/sesame based. A vegan would need to ask, with little likelihood of a fully plant-based bowl.
address_ja: 京都府向日市寺戸町東田中瀬5-54
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned coordinates within the Kyoto box for the full street address 京都府向日市寺戸町東田中瀬5-54.
neighborhood: Muko City, Teradocho — about a 3-minute walk from Hankyu Higashi-Muko Station / JR Muko-machi Station, within the Muko-shi Gekikara (extra-spicy) shopping district
seats_text: 83 seats total: a 6-seat counter, three four-person tables on the ground floor, and a 2nd-floor tatami room seating up to 65 for groups.
hours_raw: Lunch 11:30–14:00; Dinner 16:30–23:00 (last order 22:30). Closed Tuesdays. (Tabelog lists lunch as 11:00–14:00; the official site states 11:30–14:00 — the official 11:30 start is used here.)
closed_days: Tuesdays
cultural_comfort_note: A long-running neighborhood Chinese restaurant where Japanese is the working language; a little prepared Japanese or a translation app will smooth ordering, and staff are used to spice-seekers from across Japan.

bio.background: Kirin-en opened in 1967, when authentic Cantonese cooking was still a rarity in the then-farmland district of Muko. Founder Miyaji Tamotsu set out to let local residents enjoy genuine Guangdong cuisine casually, and the place has stayed family-run, with Miyaji Ryo now serving as store manager.
bio.specialty: A fiery four-chili tantanmen that became the restaurant's calling card during Muko City's spicy-food town-revitalization push. In developing it the kitchen experimented with the bhut jolokia ("jolokia") pepper — once billed as the world's hottest — and the bowl can be ordered across escalating heat levels, with the milder steps at the base price and the hottest tiers carrying a surcharge.
bio.philosophy: The house creed for its spicy dishes is "美味くて辛い" — delicious first, then hot — chasing flavor and craft rather than heat for its own sake.

anecdotes.text: Founded in 1967 by Miyaji Tamotsu to bring real Cantonese cooking to a Muko neighborhood that was still mostly farmland.
The tantanmen earned the Gold Grand Prix at the very first KARA-1 spicy-food competition, the contest run by Muko City's gekikara shopping district.
A quieter favorite is the black-vinegar sweet-and-sour pork, made with traditionally brewed bottled black vinegar for depth beneath the tang — a dish especially popular with regulars.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat-noodle tantanmen cooked in shared pots in a Chinese kitchen that also fries and wok-cooks; no gluten-free handling is described anywhere.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a Cantonese restaurant and a tantanmen specialist, dishes rely on standard (wheat-containing) soy sauce and likely wheat-based tare; no wheat-free soy is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Meat- and seafood-forward kitchen with a pork/sesame tantanmen base; no separated vegan prep is indicated.

caveats: Hours differ slightly between sources: Tabelog lists lunch from 11:00, the official site from 11:30 (used here). The signature tantanmen comes in escalating heat levels — milder steps are at base price and the hottest tiers add a surcharge — so spice-averse diners should specify a low level.

lead: ラーメン てんぐ 西陣店
name_ja: ラーメン てんぐ 西陣店
cuisine: 京都ラーメン（豚骨スープに和風出汁をブレンドした醤油系・背脂入りの中華そば）
gf_detail: A wheat-noodle ramen shop. The signature bowl is thin wheat noodles in a pork-bone broth blended with Japanese dashi, almost certainly built on a wheat-based shoyu tare, with chashu and menma. There is no indication of gluten-free noodles, a dedicated preparation area, or allergen handling, and broth/tare are shared. Not suitable for celiacs or strict gluten avoidance.
vegan_detail: The base of every bowl is an animal broth (pork bone blended with Japanese dashi), and toppings center on braised pork chashu. No vegetable or vegan broth, no plant-based ramen, and no meatless menu is described. Not a vegan-friendly option.
address_ja: 京都府京都市上京区伊佐町199
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI from address_ja; first feature coordinates [135.747635, 35.032742], within the Kyoto box.
seats_text: Small neighborhood shop with a wrap-around counter plus a couple of low tatami/table areas seating about four each; exact seat count not published.
hours_raw: 11:30-22:00 (Tabelog) / some articles list 11:30-23:00. Closed Wednesday (some reports say irregular closures).
closed_days: Wednesday (some reports indicate irregular closures)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, local Nishijin ramen counter where ordering in Japanese and paying cash will go most smoothly; no English service is indicated.
neighborhood: Nishijin (Kamigyo-ku, near Imadegawa / Chieko-koin street)
caveats: Cash only; no reservations. Closing day is listed as Wednesday on Tabelog but some write-ups describe irregular closures and posted hours vary between 22:00 and 23:00 - confirm on the day. The soup is best treated as a pork-bone-plus-Japanese-dashi shoyu-style broth.

bio.background: A modest, locally beloved ramen shop in the Nishijin district of Kyoto's Kamigyo ward, set along Chieko-koin street near the old textile quarter. It is the second location of Ramen Tengu, whose main shop is in Uzumasa in western Kyoto, and it carries a nostalgic Showa-era chuka-soba feel with a hand-painted sign and a cozy room of wrap-around counter and a few low tatami seats.
bio.specialty: The house bowl, Tengu Ramen, is built on a pork-bone broth blended with Japanese-style dashi: it looks rich and is dressed with back-fat, yet drinks surprisingly clean and stays balanced to the last sip. It comes over thin, springy noodles with menma, nori and a chashu so slow-braised it falls apart and melts on the tongue. A generous heap of fresh, crisp scallions is served on the side so you can pile them in to taste, with an extra-scallion option for a few coins.
bio.philosophy: Keep it simple and let the broth do the work - a clean, comforting Kyoto-style bowl that regulars return for rather than a flashy specialty shop.

anecdotes.text: The chashu is so tender that visitors joke about how many hours it must be simmered - it crumbles apart when lifted with chopsticks and enriches the broth as it dissolves.
The scallions arrive on a separate dish so diners can add as many as they like; an extra-scallions option lets you load the bowl up further.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles are boiled in shared water and the broth is a pork-bone/dashi base with a presumed wheat shoyu tare; no gluten-free handling is described.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The bowl is described as a shoyu-style (soy-sauce) ramen, which in a shop like this is virtually always a wheat-containing soy sauce; no wheat-free tare is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All bowls are based on animal broth (pork bone plus Japanese dashi) and topped with pork chashu; no vegan or vegetable broth is offered.

lead: ラーメン京都てんぐ 常盤店
name_ja: ラーメン京都てんぐ 常盤店
cuisine: Kyoto-style tonkotsu/chicken-gara shoyu ramen (back-fat soy)
vegan_detail: Not a vegan or vegetarian establishment. The signature bowl is built on a pork-bone (tonkotsu) and chicken-gara broth finished with back fat (背脂), topped with chashu pork. The broth and the standard bowls are animal-based, so there are no genuine vegan or vegetarian ramen options here. A plant-based diner would have no suitable main; ask staff before ordering, but expect no accommodation.
gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. This is a classic wheat-noodle ramen shop: the noodles are thin low-alkaline wheat noodles (mirroring the Sugichiyo lineage), the tare is soy-sauce based (shoyu, which in Japan virtually always contains wheat), and gyoza/shumai/shoronpo sides are wheat-wrapped. The pork-bone/chicken broth itself plus heavy shared-pot/shared-fryer use means cross-contamination is unavoidable. No gluten-free menu or noodle substitute is offered.
address_ja: 京都府京都市右京区常盤北裏町1
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned coordinates for 京都府京都市右京区常盤北裏町1; within the Kyoto bounding box (lat 35.020, lng 135.707). Location is near Randen Tokiwa Station, ~3 min walk, in the Uzumasa/Tokiwa area of Ukyo-ku.
seats_text: 38 seats across counter/table and tatami (zashiki) seating; family- and child-friendly. Operated by about 3-4 staff.
closed_days: None (open daily, no regular closing day)
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed, unpretentious neighborhood ramen shop with tatami seating and a family-friendly feel; no English is advertised, so a guide or a few Japanese phrases will make ordering smoother.

bio.background: Opened in 2002 by a chef who had been the head cook (店主／元料理長) at Sugichiyo (杉千代), a legendary Uzumasa ramen shop that drew lines for its then-revolutionary bowls some two decades ago. When Sugichiyo's original shop was gone, Tengu became the refuge for its devoted fans, carrying its style forward as the flagship of the Ramen Kyoto Tengu group.
bio.specialty: Tengu Ramen (てんぐラーメン): a pork-bone and chicken-gara shoyu broth finished with back fat (背脂) that drinks lighter than it looks, paired with thin low-alkaline straight noodles in the Sugichiyo mold and chashu so tender it falls apart when lifted with chopsticks. A basket of chopped green onion is provided to season the bowl to taste, and rice is famously all-you-can-eat for 100 yen.
bio.philosophy: Carry the Sugichiyo lineage faithfully rather than chase trends: an honest, additive-conscious pork-bone shoyu bowl in a modest storefront, kept welcoming for families, children and women rather than dressed up as a destination shop.

anecdotes.text: The owner was the former head chef at the legendary Sugichiyo and founded Tengu in 2002, becoming the home for orphaned Sugichiyo fans.
The thin low-alkaline straight noodles deliberately mirror Sugichiyo's, and diners are handed a basket of chopped green onion to customize the bowl; the chashu is so soft it crumbles when picked up.
Regulars describe it as carrying on the lineage of the now-gone famous Sugichiyo ramen shop in Uzumasa.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles cooked in a shared boiling pot, plus wheat-wrapped gyoza/shumai/shoronpo on the same line, make gluten cross-contamination unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The bowl is a shoyu (soy-sauce) style ramen; Japanese soy sauce almost always contains wheat, so the tare is a wheat source.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is pork-bone and chicken-gara based and finished with pork back fat; chashu pork tops the bowl, so no dish is free of animal products.

caveats: Official site (ramen-tengu.com) could not be fetched due to a self-signed TLS certificate; its existence is confirmed via search results and it is listed as the brand's official URL. Allergen/GF/vegan policies are inferred from documented broth/noodle/tare composition rather than a stated allergen policy — no allergen menu was found, so a diner with celiac disease or vegan needs should treat this shop as unsuitable and confirm by phone (075-872-7223) if needed.
neighborhood: Tokiwa / Uzumasa, Ukyo-ku (near Randen Tokiwa Station)

lead: ラーメン専門店 大輝
name_ja: ラーメン専門店 大輝
cuisine: 京都醤油ラーメン（豚清湯のあっさり豚骨醤油）
gf_detail: This is a traditional Kyoto-style ramen shop. The bowl is built on wheat noodles in a pork-based clear broth seasoned with a light soy-sauce (shoyu) tare, which is wheat-based. Both the noodles and the tare contain wheat/gluten, and there is no gluten-free menu, no separate preparation, and no indication of allergy accommodation. This is not safe for celiacs or those avoiding gluten.
vegan_detail: Every bowl is built on a pork bone broth (made from Miyazaki-raised pork) and topped with fatty chashu pork, so the soup itself is animal-based. There are no vegan or vegetarian options on the menu.
address_ja: 京都府京都市右京区梅津西浦町16
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 京都府京都市右京区梅津西浦町16 returned [135.696533, 35.003117], within the Kyoto box. Consistent with the shop's location in Umezu, Ukyo-ku, roughly an 11-minute walk from Hankyu Matsuo-taisha Station.
seats_text: 12 counter seats (counter only)
closed_days: Mondays and the third Sunday of each month
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, cash-only, counter-only neighborhood shop with no English menu or service; some basic Japanese or pointing at the simple ramen-and-chashu menu will make ordering smooth.
caveats: Cash only and reservations are not accepted; with just 12 counter seats there can be a wait at lunch. Closed Mondays and the third Sunday. Wheat noodles and a wheat-based shoyu tare in a pork broth mean this is not suitable for gluten-free or vegan diners.

bio.background: A small ramen specialist tucked into the Umezu neighborhood of Kyoto's Ukyo ward, near the Hankyu Matsuo-taisha line on the Arashiyama side of the city. The shop has been quietly perfecting one thing for over four decades, opening around 1981-1982 and, in the owner's own words, building it up steadily over roughly 45 years in a corner of Kyoto.
bio.specialty: The signature bowl is a Kyoto-style shoyu ramen: a pork clear-broth (tonkotsu seitan) made from Miyazaki-raised pork, seasoned with a light soy-sauce tare. Unlike many Kyoto bowls it skips the heavy back-fat float yet still drinks rich, with a generous layer of oil on top - mild in flavor but satisfyingly hearty. It comes crowned with soft, fatty melt-in-the-mouth chashu, thin bean sprouts, and Kujo green onion over thin, low-hydration straight noodles.
bio.philosophy: The owner's stated aim is simple and warm: once a customer comes in, to earn their return and become a shop they patronize for a lifetime - 'a lifelong asset.' Guests are invited to step in without hesitation and give the ramen a try.

anecdotes.text: The chashu is made from fatty pork sourced from Miyazaki and is so tender it falls apart and melts in the mouth - a detail regulars single out as the highlight of the bowl.
The owner describes the shop as something built up steadily over about 45 years in one corner of Kyoto, and warmly invites newcomers to come in without hesitation and taste it once.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The ramen uses wheat noodles cooked in shared water and a wheat-based light soy-sauce tare; there is no gluten-free preparation or separate handling.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The bowl is seasoned with a light soy-sauce (薄口醤油) tare, which is wheat-based as standard Japanese soy sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The broth is a pork bone stock and the bowl is topped with pork chashu, so all menu items are animal-based; no vegan options exist.

cuisine: Sichuan Chinese (machi-chuka / 町中華・四川料理); noodle dishes including scallion-oil chicken noodles (葱油鶏面) and hot-and-sour noodles (酸辣湯麺)
vegan_detail: Not a vegan venue. This is a full Sichuan Chinese restaurant built around pork, chicken, seafood and egg dishes. A few vegetable-forward plates exist (e.g. spicy stir-fried eggplant, eight-treasure mixed vegetables, garlic eggplant), but these are typically cooked with meat-based stock, oils, soy/oyster-style seasonings and shared woks, so they are not reliably vegan. Plant-based diners would need to ask, and even then options are narrow.
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation indicated. The noodle dishes (scallion-oil chicken noodles, hot-and-sour noodles) use wheat noodles, and Chinese cooking here relies heavily on wheat-based soy sauce and shared frying/wok surfaces. There is no evidence of allergen handling or a dedicated setup, so this is not a safe choice for celiac diners.
address_ja: 京都市東山区三条通東大路西入ル北側（七軒町）
geocode_note: Geocoded at chō level (七軒町, Shichiken-cho, Higashiyama-ku) via GSI; within the Kyoto bounding box. The Tabelog street-level address (Sanjo-dori Higashioji nishi-iru, north side) did not return a precise point, so the chō-level coordinate is used.
seats_text: Up to about 80 seats (private rooms and group seating from 4 to 80 persons available).
hours_raw: Tabelog lists 17:00–23:00 daily; the official site and local press describe the main branch as open 17:00 until 05:00 the next morning, a well-known late-night spot. No regular closing day; open year-round (one source notes Sundays closed, but most sources indicate daily operation — confirm by phone).
closed_days: No regular closing day (open year-round per official site).
cultural_comfort_note: A bustling, authentic late-night Chinese spot where Mandarin flies between staff; a little Japanese (or pointing at the menu) goes a long way, and the lively, no-frills atmosphere is welcoming rather than precious.
caveats: This is a Sichuan Chinese restaurant (machi-chuka), not a dedicated ramen shop, though it serves notable noodle bowls. Hours differ between sources: Tabelog shows 17:00–23:00, while the official site and local press describe the main branch staying open until 05:00 — confirm late-night hours by phone. Cash only.
neighborhood: Higashiyama-Sanjo / Higashiyama Station area (subway Tozai Line); a few minutes' walk from Keihan Sanjo and subway Higashiyama Station.

bio.background: Ryumon is widely regarded as one of Kyoto's pioneers of authentic Sichuan cooking, with its original Higashiyama-Sanjo location anchoring a small family of branches across the city. The main shop is run with a genuinely Chinese kitchen and floor — Mandarin is spoken throughout, and the cooking leans toward Chengdu-style flavors that balance heat with depth rather than chasing pure spice.
bio.specialty: The scallion-oil chicken noodles (葱油鶏面) are the house signature — an inexpensive bowl that regulars describe as authentic enough to bring a tear to your eye. Alongside them, the fiery stir-fried eggplant and a properly numbing mapo tofu are the dishes locals come back for.
bio.philosophy: Honest, homestyle Sichuan food cooked the way it is in China — real Sichuan chilies and peppercorns, big flavors, late into the night — served in an unpretentious neighborhood setting.

anecdotes.text: The shop draws not only Kyoto locals but visitors from across Japan and even diners from China seeking a taste of home, a marker of how authentic the cooking is.
The dining room is so thoroughly Chinese in feel that one writer joked the inside is 'practically China,' with Mandarin flying between the staff and Sichuan aromatics filling the air.
Regulars single out the 650-yen scallion-oil chicken noodles as 'delicious enough to cry over' and praise it as the real, local flavor.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Sichuan Chinese cooking of this style relies on wheat-based soy sauce and seasonings; no gluten-free soy alternative is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegetable dishes are cooked in shared woks alongside pork, chicken and seafood and commonly use meat-based stock and animal-derived seasonings; no vegan-separation practice is indicated.

cuisine: 四川料理・中華（担々麺）
neighborhood: 西京区樫原平田町（阪急桂駅）
gf_detail: No gluten-free provision found. The house specialty is tantan-men, which is wheat noodles in a sesame-rich, meat-based sauce, and the kitchen is a Sichuan/Chinese operation that relies on wheat-based soy sauce and fermented bean paste (doubanjiang). Mapo tofu, yakisoba, and other listed dishes are all gluten-bearing. No dedicated gluten-free menu, no allergen labeling, and no cross-contamination controls were documented anywhere. Treat as not celiac-safe.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan or vegetarian-oriented shop. The signature tantan-men contains pork mince and meat/sesame sauce, and the broader Sichuan menu (mapo tofu, Peking duck, daily lunch sets) is built around meat and animal stocks. No vegan or vegetarian options were identified on any page. A vegan diner could not be reliably accommodated here.
address_ja: 京都府京都市西京区樫原平田町14-30
geocode_note: GSI geocode of the chō-banchi address returned [135.69342, 34.973343], within the Kyoto bounding box.
seats_text: 28 seats, four-person tables in a compact one-story house setting; a private room can be reserved for up to 20.
hours_raw: 11:30-14:30 (L.O. 14:00) / 17:30-21:30 (L.O. 21:00); closed Wednesdays. (Some listings show 11:00-14:30 / 17:00-21:30; Tabelog gives the 11:30/17:30 times.)
closed_days: Wednesdays (following day if Wednesday is a public holiday)
cultural_comfort_note: A neighborhood Chinese restaurant with no indication of English support, so arriving with your order chosen and a few Japanese phrases ready will make things smoothest.
caveats: Strictly speaking this is a Sichuan/Chinese restaurant rather than a ramen specialist, but its signature is tantan-men (white-sesame and black-sesame versions, plus a curry tantan-men). Payment is cash or QR-code (PayPay) only — no credit cards or e-money. Lunch draws lines, so reserving ahead at peak times is wise. Hours differ slightly between listings.

bio.background: Rosen is a small, calm one-story house restaurant in the Kashihara neighborhood of Kyoto's Nishikyo Ward, a short ride from Hankyu Katsura Station. The proprietor trained at well-regarded Sichuan kitchens in the Kansai region and continues to travel to China, bringing that grounding back to a tucked-away local dining room.
bio.specialty: Tantan-men is the house signature, offered in white-sesame and black-sesame versions (with a curry tantan-men alongside). The black-sesame bowl is the standout: a rich, deeply aromatic sesame sauce with gentle sweetness and restrained heat over springy noodles — mellow rather than fiercely spicy.
bio.philosophy: Authentic Sichuan and Chinese cooking served generously and affordably in an unfussy, settled space — confident portions and honest pricing rather than show.

anecdotes.text: Lunch sets start around 800 yen and the dining room fills with a queue at midday, drawn by the fragrant tantan-men and the mapo tofu offered in both mild and tongue-numbing spicy versions.

A reviewer singled out the black-sesame tantan-men for its mellow, sweet-leaning sesame richness and good noodle bounce, and praised the salt yakisoba made with wide flat noodles finished with a touch of oyster sauce.

gf_cross_contamination.text: House specialty is wheat-noodle tantan-men prepared in a shared Sichuan/Chinese kitchen alongside other wheat-containing dishes (yakisoba, etc.); no gluten-free handling was documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Sichuan and Chinese cooking here relies on standard wheat-based soy sauce and fermented bean paste; no wheat-free seasoning alternatives were mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Tantan-men contains pork mince and the menu centers on meat and animal stocks; no vegan/vegetarian preparation or separation was noted.

lead: とんこつらぁ麺 嘉晴
name_ja: とんこつらぁ麺 嘉晴
cuisine: 豚骨ラーメン (tonkotsu ramen) — Minoh-style pork-bone ramen, a rarity in Kyoto
gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. This is a classic wheat-noodle ramen shop — straight thin wheat noodles in a pork-bone broth, with gyoza (wheat skins) and fried rice/karaage on the menu. The tare and accompaniments almost certainly include wheat-based soy sauce. No gluten-free noodles, dedicated prep, or allergen accommodation is advertised on any source. A celiac diner should not eat the ramen here.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The entire identity of the shop is a pork-bone (plus chicken carcass and chicken-feet) broth simmered 24+ hours, topped with chashu pork. There are no vegetable- or vegan-broth options noted anywhere. Side dishes are rice/gyoza/karaage, also non-vegan. Not suitable for vegan or vegetarian diners.
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区西ノ京池ノ内町6-7
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates [135.74646, 35.00845] for 西ノ京池ノ内町6-7; within the Kyoto bounding box.
neighborhood: 中京区 西ノ京 (near Nijō Castle / Sanjō-kai shopping arcade)
seats_text: 24 seats — 12 counter, 12 table
hours_raw: Lunch: Tue-Fri 11:00-14:30, Sat-Sun/holidays 11:00-14:45. Dinner: Tue-Sat 17:30-22:00, Sun/holidays 17:30-21:30. Closed Mondays (or the following Tuesday when Monday is a holiday). Note: some listings show slightly different hours (e.g., dinner to 23:30 on Tabelog); the official site hours are used here.
closed_days: Mondays (closed the following Tuesday if Monday is a public holiday); occasional irregular closures
cultural_comfort_note: A small neighborhood ramen counter with a mobile-phone ordering system and self-serve sides; Japanese-language menu and a little Japanese will make ordering smoother, though pointing works fine.
caveats: Payment method is inconsistent across sources: the official site indicates cards are accepted and one blog notes QR payments, while Tabelog lists it as cash-only — carry cash to be safe. Reported hours also vary slightly between listings; official-site hours are used here. Confirm current hours and payment on arrival.

bio.background: Owner-chef Shoichi Iwasaki spent roughly 15 years training at a celebrated tonkotsu ramen shop in Minoh, Osaka, before opening Yoshiharu in 2013. After leaving the kitchen where he learned his craft, he spent extensive trial-and-error developing the broth that would become the shop's signature.
bio.origin: Opened March 2013 in the Nishinokyō neighborhood of Kyoto's Nakagyō Ward, near Nijō Castle and the Sanjō-kai shopping arcade.
bio.specialty: A 'Minoh-style' tonkotsu — distinct from Kyushu tonkotsu — built on a creamy white broth of pork skull and knuckle bones, chicken carcass and chicken feet (momiji), finished with quality back fat, pork skin and plenty of vegetables, then simmered for 24+ hours. The flagship bowl is the special tonkotsu ramen with straight thin noodles, soft sweet chashu, green onion and bean sprouts; the kitchen also offers shoyu, miso, and mayu (burnt-garlic-oil) variations.
bio.philosophy: Iwasaki aims for a broth that suppresses the heavy off-odor often associated with pork bone, delivering deep umami and richness with a clean, light finish — a balance of 'umami, richness, and depth.' The shop frames its mission as making ramen — broth, noodles and chashu — that cannot be replicated elsewhere.

anecdotes.text: The shop is regarded as a rarity in Kyoto for being a dedicated pork-bone (tonkotsu) specialist rather than the city's more common lighter styles.
The broth is built from pork skull and knuckle bones plus chicken carcass and chicken feet, back fat, pork skin and vegetables, simmered for 24+ hours.

cuisine: 柚子ラーメン / 鶏白湯・豚骨鶏ガラ（豆乳柚子もあり）
gf_detail: No gluten-free provision. This is a wheat-noodle ramen shop: the signature bowls use thin/thick wheat noodles in pork-and-chicken or fish-dashi broths, and standard ramen tare contains wheat-based soy sauce. No dedicated gluten-free menu, no allergen statement, and no celiac-safe handling is published. Not safe for celiacs; even the soy milk yuzu bowl is served over wheat noodles.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan shop. The two core bowls are pork-bone/chicken broth (一蓮) and fish-dashi broth (紗), each topped with tsukune (meatball) and chashu/egg. The 豆乳柚子 (soy milk yuzu) bowl is creamy but is finished with roasted bacon, so it is not vegan, and no vegan-modified version is advertised. A diner could ask, but no plant-based ramen is offered.
address_ja: 〒605-0826 京都府京都市東山区桝屋町349-20
geocode_note: GSI address search returned [135.780441, 34.998562] for 京都府京都市東山区桝屋町349-20, within the Kyoto box. Location matches the published access note: in the alley just off Ninenzaka, beside the Starbucks Yasaka Chaya branch.
seats_text: Counter 5 seats, plus a 2-seat and a 4-seat table on the ground floor and 2nd-floor table seating for up to 8 (about 19 total).
hours_raw: 11時～21時（時期により変更／詳細はインスタで告知）。Tabelog lists 11:30–18:00. Hours change seasonally.
closed_days: Wednesdays (if Monday is a public holiday the shop opens and closes the following Thursday instead). Also notes 不定休 / irregular closures; hours vary seasonally.
cultural_comfort_note: A polished, tourist-area yuzu-ramen shop near Kiyomizu-dera designed to welcome solo diners and women; staff are used to visitors, though a few words of Japanese and a friendly konnichiwa help.
neighborhood: 東山区桝屋町 — Ninenzaka, just below Kiyomizu-dera / Kodai-ji (near Gion-Shijo Station)
caveats: Hours shift seasonally (site says roughly 11:00–21:00, Tabelog lists 11:30–18:00) and the shop notes irregular closures, so confirm the day's hours on their Instagram before a long walk up Ninenzaka. No parking. Not suitable for celiacs or vegans.

bio.background: ichiren is a newer yuzu-ramen specialist tucked into an alley just off Ninenzaka, a couple of minutes' walk below Kiyomizu-dera and Kodai-ji, opened in May 2021. It is a fresh brand launched by the family behind Motomachi Ramen (元町ラーメン), a Kyoto ramen name carried across three generations, so the bowls draw on a long local lineage while being reworked into something refined and contemporary.
bio.origin: Kyoto — a new concept from the three-generation Motomachi Ramen family, set in a renovated traditional wooden machiya on Ninenzaka.
bio.specialty: Yuzu-forward Kyoto ramen. The namesake 一蓮 (Ichiren) is a rich double broth of pork bone and chicken bones brightened with yuzu; 紗 (Sara) is a clearer fish-dashi bowl (grilled flying fish, bonito, mackerel, sardine) also scented with yuzu; and the 豆乳柚子 (soy milk yuzu) bowl is creamy yet light, finished with black pepper and roasted bacon. Bowls come with tsukune, and tables offer tororo kombu, yuzu-shichimi and yuzu-kosho to shift the flavor mid-meal.
bio.philosophy: To carry forward Kyoto tradition while evolving it into a refined, distinctly Kyoto ramen — built around yuzu and a calm white-and-yuzu-toned interior meant to be welcoming for solo diners and especially women.

anecdotes.text: A new brand from the three-generation Kyoto ramen house Motomachi Ramen (元町ラーメン), aiming to inherit tradition while offering a more refined bowl.
Designed by a female owner around a soft, inviting white-and-yuzu-colored space so that solo diners and women feel comfortable.
Lanterns in the shop were made by Kojima Shoten, a well-known Kyoto lantern atelier, reinforcing the machiya atmosphere.
The signature yuzu ramen is presented as a refined Kyoto experience, and tables provide tororo kombu, yuzu-shichimi and yuzu-kosho to vary the taste.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core bowls are wheat-noodle ramen in pork/chicken and fish-dashi broths; no gluten-free noodles or gluten-free menu are offered, so the kitchen is wheat-heavy and not celiac-appropriate.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Even the plant-leaning soy milk yuzu bowl is finished with roasted bacon, and broths are animal-based; no vegan preparation is described.

cuisine: ちゃん系（東京風 清湯チャーシュー中華そば） / Tokyo-style "chan-kei" clear-broth chashu chuka soba ramen
neighborhood: 小野・山科区 (Ono, Yamashina-ku)
address_ja: 京都府京都市山科区小野西浦68-20
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned coordinates [135.813751, 34.960117] for 京都府京都市山科区小野西浦68-20; within the Kyoto box.
seats_text: 24 seats: 12 counter seats plus 3 tables (4 seats each).
hours_raw: Sun-Thu 11:00-19:00; Fri, Sat & holidays 11:00-21:00. Open year-round including New Year (年中無休).
closed_days: None (open year-round, 年中無休)
cultural_comfort_note: A small local ramen counter with ticket-machine ordering and no reservations or English menu noted, so a little Japanese or pointing helps, though the system is straightforward.
vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian options. The signature chuka soba is built on a pork-based clear broth (清湯) with sharp soy/salt seasoning and is topped with chashu pork belly, so the broth and standard bowls contain animal products.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free / not celiac-safe. The dish is wheat ramen noodles (flat noodles from Shinjuku Daruma Seimensho) in a soy-seasoned broth (wheat-based shoyu tare typical of the style). No gluten-free menu, dedicated equipment, or allergen accommodations are mentioned anywhere.
caveats: Recently opened (September 4, 2025); hours and operations may still settle. No allergen/dietary information published — gluten-free and vegan diners should treat this as unsafe unless they can confirm in person.

roles: independent ramen shop / chan-noren guild member
bio.background: Akichan Ramen opened on September 4, 2025, a short walk from Ono Station near Zuishin-in temple, becoming what local press called the first 'chan-kei' (ちゃん系) ramen shop to land in Kyoto. It belongs to the Chan-noren guild (ちゃんのれん組合), a federation of Tokyo shops such as Kanda Chie-chan, Shinjuku Ecchan, and Ikebukuro Hiro-chan that brought this style westward.
bio.specialty: Chuka soba (中華そば) — a Tokyo-style clear, fat-rich pork broth with sharp soy-and-salt seasoning, served with generous chashu pork belly, deeply flavored bamboo shoots, and white negi over flat, smooth specialty noodles from Shinjuku Daruma Seimensho. A favorite move is adding a raw egg to mellow the saltiness, often paired with negi rice.
bio.philosophy: Faithfully recreate the Kanto 'chan-kei' bowl — layered umami, rendered pork fat richness, and a clean clear broth — for Kyoto diners, with noodles kept slightly separate from the broth for texture.

anecdotes.text: Billed by local food media as Kyoto's first taste of the 'chan-kei' ramen wave that had been spreading through the Kanto region.
Uses flat, smooth specialty noodles sourced from Shinjuku Daruma Seimensho, a hallmark of the chan-kei guild shops.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The chuka soba is seasoned with a sharp soy-and-salt tare typical of the style; soy sauce in Japanese ramen is normally wheat-based, so the broth should be assumed to contain wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is a pork-based clear consomme and bowls are topped with chashu pork belly, so all standard items contain animal products.

cuisine: Jiro-style ramen (二郎系) — non-emulsified pork-and-soy broth (非乳化 豚骨醤油) with thick chewy noodles (極太麺)
gf_detail: Not safe for celiac/gluten-free diners. This is a Jiro-style ramen shop built around house-made ultra-thick wheat noodles and a soy (shoyu) tare, which in Japan is almost always brewed with wheat. The broth is pork-based and bowls are loaded with wheat noodles, so gluten is unavoidable. No gluten-free menu, noodle substitute, or allergen accommodation is advertised. Abura soba (brothless) is also wheat noodles. Treat as off-limits if you require strict GF.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. Every bowl is built on a pork-bone/pork broth with large pork chunks (chashu), and standard toppings include a boiled egg. There is no vegetable or plant-based broth option advertised. The free "call" add-ons (extra vegetables, oil, garlic) sit on top of an animal-based bowl and do not make it vegan.
address_ja: 京都府京都市伏見区深草野田町17-2
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates [135.768707, 34.959633] for 京都府京都市伏見区深草野田町17-2; within the Kyoto bounding box.
neighborhood: Fukakusa, Fushimi-ku — near JR/Keihan Fujinomori Station and Ryukoku University
seats_text: L-shaped counter seating only; exact seat count not published.
hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Thu, Fri, holidays: 10:00-14:00, 17:00-21:00; Wed, Sat, Sun: 10:00-14:00 (lunch only). Opened 2024-07-23. Hours can shift per the shop's social media.
closed_days: No fixed regular closing day published; lunch-only on Wed/Sat/Sun. Schedule changes are announced on the shop's social media, so confirm before visiting.
cultural_comfort_note: A no-frills, cash-only Jiro-style counter shop where ordering runs on the Jiro "call" system (vegetables/oil/garlic) in Japanese — comfortable if you know the basic ramen ritual, but no English support is advertised.
caveats: Cash only — no cards, e-money, or QR payments. Walk-in only, no reservations. Hours and closures shift; the owner posts updates on social media (Instagram/X @motuniteishoku), so check before going. Lunch-only on Wednesday, Saturday, and Sunday.

bio.background: The owner is a well-known local Jiro-style ramen proprietor who has long been tied to this Fushimi corner. He previously ran 'Ramen Dai' and later 'Gatts K Ramen' at this same location, and also did a stint training at 'Yume o Katare,' the famous Jiro-style shop. After stepping away, he returned to the neighborhood to open this shop in summer 2024.
bio.origin: Fushimi-ku, Kyoto — a ramen battleground near Ryukoku University
bio.specialty: A non-emulsified shoyu-tonkotsu Jiro bowl: a clean, sharply soy-forward, non-emulsified broth (rather than the thick cloudy style), paired with house-made wavy ultra-thick chewy noodles, big chunks of pork, a boiled egg, and a hit of black pepper. Diners customize with the free 'call' system — extra vegetables, oil (abura), and garlic — and can size noodles roughly 150g to 300g. Aburasoba (brothless) is also offered.
bio.philosophy: The shop name '夢は力なり' means 'a dream is strength' — an aspirational banner from an owner who left and came back to feed his old neighborhood, carrying the lineage of the Jiro-style shops he trained in and ran before.

anecdotes.text: Local food press framed the 2024 opening as a 'famous proprietor returning' to the ramen-dense Fushimi area, picking up where his earlier shops Ramen Dai and Gatts K Ramen left off at the very same spot.
The broth is described as soy-sharp and clearly defined — a non-emulsified style, distinguishing it from the heavier emulsified Jiro bowls — over self-made thick, wavy, springy noodles.
The owner spent time training at 'Yume o Katare,' a celebrated Jiro-style shop, before returning to run this Fushimi counter.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The bowl is a soy (shoyu) tare-based Jiro ramen; Japanese soy sauce / shoyu tare is brewed with wheat in nearly all cases, so the broth seasoning itself contains gluten in addition to the wheat noodles.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All bowls are built on pork/pork-bone broth with pork chashu and egg; no plant-based broth or vegan option is offered, so there is no vegan-safe preparation to protect from cross-contact.

cuisine: 町中華・旭系 醤油清湯中華そば（昼ラーメン／夜居酒屋・焼き鳥）
neighborhood: 円町・丸太町七本松（中京区）
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区聚楽廻西町182-9
geocode_note: GSI geocode of address_ja returned [135.739838, 35.018864]; within Kyoto box.
seats_text: About 30 seats; counter, tables, and tatami seating.
hours_raw: 11:00-14:45, 17:30-22:30 (lunch ramen / evening izakaya); Sunday closed. Some sources list lunch ending around 14:00.
closed_days: Sundays (日曜日)
cultural_comfort_note: A small neighborhood spot with no indication of English service; basic Japanese or a translation app will help, especially as it shifts to an izakaya in the evening.
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation found. The ramen is built on wheat noodles with a wheat-based soy sauce (shoyu) tare over a pork clear broth, so the signature bowls are not celiac-safe. Evening yakitori items often carry wheat-based tare/seasoning as well. No allergen handling or dedicated equipment is documented.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly venue. The core ramen uses a pork-bone clear broth (豚骨清湯) with pork chashu, and the evening menu centers on chicken yakitori, tsukune, and grilled pork. No vegan or vegetarian ramen or dishes were found.

bio.background: The owner trained at a ramen shop before opening Maruta Shoten in late May 2025, then built it as a two-faced (二毛作) neighborhood spot near Maruta-cho Shichihonmatsu in Kyoto's Nakagyo ward.
bio.specialty: A nostalgic Kyoto-style pork clear-broth (豚骨清湯) shoyu chuka-soba, lightly salted in the classic Kyoto manner and piled generously with chashu — sometimes arranged petal-style (花びらチャーシュー). The owner's ramen-shop training also shows up at night in the 'ramen-shop-style' grilled pork.
bio.philosophy: By day a straightforward ramen counter, by evening a yakitori-focused izakaya — one small kitchen serving comforting, restrained Kyoto flavors morning to night.

anecdotes.text: The shop runs as a 二毛作 (two-crop) operation: lunch is a dedicated light pork clear-broth ramen service, while evenings turn into a charcoal yakitori izakaya.
Reviewers compare the lightly-salted Kyoto shoyu bowl favorably to the well-known Kyoto ramen lineage, noting medium-thick straight noodles and an abundant chashu portion.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The bowl is described as a 醤油 (soy sauce) shoyu tare over Kyoto pork clear broth; standard Japanese shoyu is wheat-based, so the tare should be assumed to contain wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is pork-bone clear soup and toppings include pork chashu; evening dishes center on chicken and pork, indicating heavy animal-product use throughout the kitchen.

caveats: Opened May 30, 2025. Lunch end time varies by source (14:00 to 14:45); confirm before a late lunch. No allergen, gluten-free, or vegan information is published, so anyone with celiac disease or a vegan/vegetarian diet should treat this as unsuitable. Geocode is address-level via GSI.

cuisine: 鶏白湯（鶏パイタン）ラーメン
neighborhood: 京都駅前・京都タワー西側（下京区東塩小路町）
address_ja: 〒600-8216 京都府京都市下京区木津屋橋通室町東入東塩小路町579-24 ハウス24
geocode_note: Building-level address did not resolve; geocoded at the chōme/banchi level (東塩小路町579) via GSI, returning 34.987503N / 135.757889E — within the Kyoto box and consistent with the stated location just west of Kyoto Station and Kyoto Tower.
seats_text: Small counter-style shop (former standing izakaya space); exact seat count not published.
closed_days: 定休日の記載なし（unconfirmed）
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, walk-in ramen counter by Kyoto Station with QR-code/mobile ordering, so a non-Japanese-speaking diner can manage, but there is no confirmed English support or allergy handling, so come prepared to point and self-cater.
vegan_detail: The house soup is a chicken-bone-and-vegetable paitan (鶏ガラ・野菜) using Satsuma Chiran chicken, and signature bowls include chicken char siu, Kyoto nine-scallion chicken ramen and wagyu items — all animal-based. No vegetarian or vegan ramen is advertised.
gf_detail: Standard wheat ramen: medium-thin straight wheat noodles from the long-established Kyoto noodle maker 麺屋棣鄂 (est. 1931), plus a soy-based (shoyu) tare and table-side soy sauce — all wheat-containing. No gluten-free noodles or menu noted; not safe for celiac diners.
caveats: Newly opened (Jan 29, 2026); hours, closed days and seat count are not yet firmly published, so treat logistics as provisional. No Tabelog or official site URL was confirmed at time of writing.

bio.background: 麺家ろまん opened on January 29, 2026, in the space that previously housed the standing izakaya "すたんど ろまん家" near Kyoto Station, just west of Kyoto Tower. It is a ramen conversion of that yakitori-izakaya operation (the Kyoto "浪漫家／ろまん家" group), reborn as a chicken-paitan specialist.
bio.origin: 下京区東塩小路町 — a few minutes west of Kyoto Station / Kyoto Tower.
bio.specialty: The signature bowl is the 鶏白湯ラーメン (chicken paitan, 1,000 yen): a chicken-bone-and-vegetable potage-style broth made with Satsuma Chiran chicken (さつま知覧どり), finished with a soy-based tare and paired with medium-thin straight wheat noodles from the historic Kyoto noodle house 麺屋棣鄂 (founded 1931). The broth leans gentle and creamy-sweet, opening up as you stir the bowl and add pepper or the table soy sauce.
bio.philosophy: An approachable, Kyoto-leaning take on chicken paitan built around quality branded chicken and a respected local noodle maker, served as easy, sit-and-slurp comfort food a short walk from the station.
roles: Former standing izakaya converted to ramen by the Kyoto 浪漫家／ろまん家 group

anecdotes.text: The shop is a direct conversion of the group's standing izakaya すたんど ろまん家, which is why it keeps the "ろまん" name from the parent yakitori-izakaya brand.
The chicken paitan uses Satsuma Chiran chicken (さつま知覧どり) for the broth, and the noodles come from the long-running Kyoto noodle maker 麺屋棣鄂, established in 1931.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is finished with a soy-based (shoyu) seasoning and soy sauce is available at the table for adjustment — standard wheat-containing shoyu, not gluten-free.

sources: https://msearch.gsi.go.jp/address-search/AddressSearch?q=京都府京都市下京区東塩小路町579

cuisine: 豚骨醤油ラーメン (mild tonkotsu-shoyu ramen)
neighborhood: 左京区 出町柳
address_ja: 京都府京都市左京区田中下柳町12
geocode_note: Geocoded from chōme-level address 京都府京都市左京区田中下柳町12 via GSI; result (35.02813, 135.772964) falls within the Kyoto box and matches the Demachiyanagi location.
seats_text: Counter only, around 8-9 seats — a small, intimate shop.
closed_days: Sundays; Mondays vary by month (the shop posts each month's closed days on its website, so confirm before a Monday visit).
cultural_comfort_note: A small local counter shop in a student neighborhood with warm, attentive hosts; little to no English is expected, so a few words of Japanese or pointing at the menu will go a long way.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly shop. The broth is simmered from pork bones and chicken carcasses, the noodles are egg noodles, and the tare is soy-sauce based, so the bowls are fundamentally animal-based. There is no advertised vegan or vegetable-broth ramen.
gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The noodles are wheat-based egg noodles (たまご麺) and the broth is a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare, which is virtually always made with wheat. Combined with the pork/chicken broth, every bowl contains gluten, and there is no gluten-free option. People with wheat allergy or celiac disease should avoid this shop.

bio.background: 鴨町らーめん is a tiny counter-only ramen shop tucked into the Demachi neighborhood a minute's walk from Demachiyanagi Station, on a stretch with surprisingly few eateries that makes it a reliable local stop. It has been a fixture of this student-heavy corner of Sakyo Ward, beloved more by neighbors and Kyoto University-area regulars than by tourists.
bio.specialty: The signature bowl is a mild tonkotsu-shoyu ramen: pork bones and chicken carcasses, carefully cleaned, are simmered for hours with several kinds of aromatic vegetables into a broth that is slightly thick and mellow on the palate (「少しとろっとしていて、まろやかな口当たり」), with the pork richness leading over the soy. It is paired with straight medium-thin egg noodles and fatty, naturally sweet braised chashu. The menu is broad for such a small shop — miso, Hakata-style, chashu-men, tantanmen, a fish-dashi ramen, and stamina ramen — plus student-discount bowls and hearty karaage and gyoza set meals.
bio.philosophy: The shop sums itself up as 「味よし 麺よし 心よし」 — good taste, good noodles, good heart — and the spirit shows in small touches like complimentary house-made kimchi offered on request and uncooked take-home noodles to enjoy at home.

anecdotes.text: Regulars and writers single out the hospitality as much as the bowl: the owner and staff are described as approachable and as giving 「丁寧で気持ちがよい接客」 (courteous, pleasant service) — fitting the shop's '癒し系' (healing-style) tonkotsu reputation.
Ask and they'll set out free house-made kimchi alongside table condiments like spicy miso and fresh garlic; they also sell uncooked ramen to take home and prepare yourself.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is a tonkotsu-shoyu (pork-bone soy-sauce) base; soy-sauce tare of this kind is virtually always made with wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is simmered from pork bones and chicken carcasses, so all ramen here is animal-based and unsuitable for vegans/vegetarians.

positives.text: Noodles are explicitly egg noodles (たまご麺) — relevant for both egg and wheat allergies.

caveats: Hours and closed days conflict between sources: Tabelog lists closed Sun+Mon with evening service to 22:00, while the shop's own/gnavi listings show closed Sundays only (Mondays vary monthly) with service to ~22:30. The shop posts each month's exact closed days on its website, so confirm before visiting, especially on a Monday. Seat count reported as 8-9 counter seats. No allergen program is published; cash only.

cuisine: 鶏白湯ラーメン（tori-paitan）
neighborhood: 左京区 田中門前町（出町柳）
address_ja: 京都府京都市左京区田中門前町44
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 京都府京都市左京区田中門前町44 returned [135.779297, 35.029888]; within Kyoto box and consistent with a location about a 10-minute walk from Keihan Demachiyanagi station.
seats_text: 12 counter seats (all counter seating)
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular closures; no fixed weekly closing day)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, busy 12-seat counter ramen bar where a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu will carry you through; no special foreigner-facing service should be expected.
gf_detail: This is a wheat-noodle ramen shop built around house-made noodles, with a chicken-paitan broth and shoyu/shio tare. House-made wheat noodles plus a typically wheat-containing soy tare make every signature bowl unsafe for celiac diners, and no gluten-free option is offered.
vegan_detail: The kitchen is centered on chicken paitan (chicken white broth) and chashu pork, with egg toppings; there is no vegan or vegetarian ramen on the menu and no plant-based broth alternative.

bio.background: キラメキ☆JAPAN is one of the founding Kyoto shops of the Kirameki brand operated by Kirameki no Mirai Co., Ltd., a group that has grown to more than thirty ramen shops across Kyoto, Osaka, Shiga, Nara and even Shanghai. This counter-only Demachiyanagi spot opened in January 2016 and is built around the company's signature chicken-paitan style.
bio.origin: Kyoto, part of the Kirameki group founded and headquartered in the Kansai region (headquarters and noodle factory relocated in 2023).
bio.specialty: Tori-paitan ramen — a creamy, rich-yet-not-heavy chicken white broth paired with house-made medium noodles, alongside crowd favorites like the 醤油のキラメキ (shoyu) bowl and the hearty キラメキ二朗 (Jiro-style) bowl.
bio.philosophy: The group's stated belief is that the star of ramen is the noodle itself — 'ラーメンの主役は麺' — which is why every shop continues to make its noodles in-house rather than buying them in.

anecdotes.text: The kitchen finishes its chicken-paitan bowls with bright touches such as a slice of lemon and chopped raw onion, so the soup reads creamy and full from black pepper yet stays refreshingly light rather than heavy.
Fans describe the tori-paitan as having an almost carbonara-like richness from the creamy broth clinging to the house-made noodles.

roles: Kirameki no Mirai Co., Ltd. (operating company / brand)

gf_cross_contamination.text: House-made wheat noodles are the centerpiece of the shop, so flour and wheat are handled throughout the kitchen and shared boiling water is used for all bowls.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Signature bowls include a shoyu (soy-sauce) ramen, and Japanese ramen soy tare almost always contains wheat-based soy sauce; no wheat-free tare is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths are chicken-based (tori-paitan) and bowls are topped with pork chashu and egg, so all shared soup, surfaces and utensils carry animal products.

caveats: Address note: the chain's own shop-list page momentarily reads as if キラメキ☆JAPAN is not in Demachiyanagi, but its registered address 田中門前町44, 左京区 is genuinely about a 10-minute walk from Keihan Demachiyanagi station, matching Tabelog and Hitosara. This is a chain branch (Kirameki no Mirai Co., 33+ shops), so it is not independent. No gluten-free or vegan accommodation is offered or documented; safety notes are inferred from the wheat-noodle, chicken-paitan, pork/egg concept rather than from any stated allergy program.

cuisine: 担々麺（クリーミー系）／ラーメン・居酒屋
neighborhood: 左京区 出町柳
address_ja: 京都府京都市左京区田中下柳町20
geocode_note: GSI geocoded address_ja 京都府京都市左京区田中下柳町20; first feature coordinates [135.773254, 35.029575], within Kyoto box. Matches Demachiyanagi Station location (1-min walk).
seats_text: 34 seats — 5 counter seats, 1 two-top, 8 four-tops; up to 20 for a private group. Counter is designed to welcome solo diners.
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular closures, no fixed day off)
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed, half-open food-stall counter where a friendly greeting and pointing at the menu will get you by; no English service is advertised, so a few Japanese words help.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The tantanmen uses wheat noodles (thin noodles from Kushiro Seimen) and a sesame-based tantan tare that, like virtually all Japanese ramen, relies on wheat-containing soy sauce. No gluten-free noodle or dedicated prep is mentioned on any source. Celiac diners should not eat the ramen here.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. The signature tantanmen is built on a meat broth with large chunks of ground pork, and sides are pork karaage and chicken wings. No vegan or vegetarian ramen is offered or advertised.
caveats: Hours differ across sources: Tabelog lists a continuous 11:00–22:30, while the official site and Leaf KYOTO describe a lunch 11:00–14:00 plus evening 17:00–24:00 (L.O. 23:45) split; the split-service hours are used here. Address per Tabelog/GSI is 田中下柳町20. Closures are irregular (不定休) — worth confirming before a special trip.

bio.background: こういうもん家 Ro-ji opened on August 5, 2019, tucked down a narrow alley (the "ro-ji" of its name) just a minute south of Demachiyanagi Station. The space was originally a private company warehouse, reborn as a half-open, yatai (food-stall) style counter with no partitions — the kind of hidden-alley spot you stumble onto and feel you've found a secret.
bio.specialty: Creamy, mild tantanmen. The house bowl leans on a rich sesame aroma with gentle, not-overpowering heat, topped with plenty of ground pork; the kitchen will even hold back the finishing chili oil for milder palates. Variations include spicy, tomato, and curry tantanmen, and a popular half-size (the local move is to order izakaya snacks first and close the night with a half bowl, or turn the leftover soup into 'tantan rice').
bio.philosophy: A creamy tantanmen tuned to please across age groups rather than to chase extreme spice — approachable comfort food served stall-style, equally happy as a quick lunch or a late-night drinking spot.

anecdotes.text: Thin noodles are sourced from Kushiro Seimen, a Fushimi-based noodle maker, and served slightly soft; the counter sets out scorched garlic and sansho pepper so you can shift the flavor as you eat.
The shop doubles as a casual izakaya in the evening, with karaage (fried chicken) as a signature side and takeout item alongside the noodles.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Tantanmen uses wheat ramen noodles (Kushiro Seimen) boiled in shared water; no gluten-free handling is mentioned.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No source specifies the tare ingredients, but a sesame tantan tare of this style standardly uses wheat-based soy sauce; treat as containing wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is meat-based with ground pork topping; no vegan preparation described.

positives.text: Kitchen will omit the finishing chili oil on request, so the bowl can be made milder.

cuisine: 中華そば（鶏ガラ＋魚介・醤油）/ ramen
neighborhood: 左京区 神宮丸太町（吉田下大路町）
gf_detail: No gluten-free options. The signature 中華そば uses wheat straight noodles and a soy-sauce-seasoned chicken-and-dashi broth (soy sauce is normally wheat-based), with pork-belly chashu. No gluten-free noodles, dedicated prep, or celiac accommodation is mentioned anywhere. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The shop is built around a chicken-bone and fish/dashi broth with domestic pork-belly chashu; the soy-sauce tare is animal-adjacent and side dishes include pidan (century egg). No vegetable/vegan broth or plant-based bowl is offered or mentioned.
address_ja: 京都府京都市左京区吉田下大路町16
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 京都府京都市左京区吉田下大路町16, within the Kyoto bounding box.
seats_text: About 30 seats: counter, table seating, and a tatami room.
closed_days: 月曜・火曜（Monday & Tuesday）
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed neighborhood ramen-and-drinks spot run by a hands-on owner; little English is expected, so a few words of Japanese or pointing at the menu will go a long way.
caveats: Reported hours vary between sources (one lists Wed-Fri evenings only with weekend lunches); confirm the day's hours before visiting. The chashu-men price is quoted around 1,200 yen while the signature 中華そば is well under 1,000 yen.

bio.background: Mimio is a Kyoto institution that spent many years as a beloved late-night, after-drinks ramen haunt in the Gion entertainment district, where it quietly drew a following that included entertainers and professional chefs. In March 2016 it relocated across the city to a quieter spot in Yoshida, Sakyo Ward, near Jingu-Marutamachi, where it carries on the same bowl.
bio.specialty: The signature 名物中華そば is a clear, gentle soy-sauce ramen: a broth drawn carefully from chicken bones and dashi with select ingredients, paired with thin, springy straight noodles and a standout chashu of domestic pork belly sourced from a long-established butcher, simmered until it melts on the tongue. Pidan (century egg) is a popular small plate, and the same broth is even turned into a curry.
bio.philosophy: Rather than the usual fast-turnover ramen counter, the owner deliberately designed Mimio as a place to settle in - with counter, tables and a tatami room - so guests can slowly enjoy a drink alongside the bowl. The broth is portioned so you can comfortably finish it to the last sip.

anecdotes.text: Before its move, Mimio was a long-loved spot in Gion to cap off a night of drinking, frequented by celebrities and fellow chefs.
The noodles are straight noodles tied to a Kyoto noodle maker founded in 1879, and the chashu uses domestic pork belly from an established butcher, braised until tender.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature bowl is a soy-sauce-seasoned chicken-and-dashi ramen; soy sauce tare is normally wheat-based, so the broth should be assumed to contain wheat/gluten.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is built on chicken bones and fish/dashi with pork-belly chashu, and pidan (egg) is a popular side - no vegan-safe preparation is offered.

cuisine: 煮干そば（niboshi ramen） / fish-forward shoyu & shio ramen
gf_detail: No gluten-free option. The bowls are built on wheat noodles (medium-thick straight noodles made with whole-grain wheat flour from a local noodle factory) and almost certainly a wheat-based shoyu tare, served in fish/chicken broth. Nothing on any page indicates gluten-free accommodation, and this is a tiny solo-run counter shop unlikely to manage cross-contamination. Not celiac-safe.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. Every bowl is animal-based: niboshi (dried sardine), bonito, kombu, clam and chicken stock, with chicken oil and chashu (pork) toppings. No vegan or vegetarian ramen is offered.
address_ja: 京都府京都市左京区浄土寺西田町100-28
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 京都府京都市左京区浄土寺西田町100-28 returned [135.788422, 35.028294]; within the Kyoto bounding box.
seats_text: Roughly 6 counter seats; no private rooms.
hours_raw: 11:00-14:30 / 17:30-21:30 (Tabelog lists lunch 10:30-14:30 L.O.14:15, dinner 17:30-20:00 L.O.19:45). Closed Wednesdays; occasional irregular closures.
closed_days: Wednesdays (plus occasional irregular closures)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny solo-run neighborhood counter shop near Ginkaku-ji where ordering is straightforward but staff likely speak little English; basic Japanese or pointing at the menu goes a long way.

bio.background: Menya Kagura opened on February 3, 2021 in the Jodoji / Ginkaku-ji area of Kyoto's Sakyo Ward, taking over the address of Menya Himawari, a beloved salt-ramen shop that had operated there since 2006 before relocating to Shizuoka in late 2020. The new shop is run by family of the former Himawari owner (described as the previous owner's brother) and opened under the former owner's supervision, carrying the lineage forward in the same little space.
bio.origin: Kyoto (Jodoji / Ginkaku-ji area, Sakyo Ward), Japan
bio.specialty: Niboshi (dried sardine) ramen. The kitchen builds its stock from multiple dried sardines, thick-shaved bonito, kombu, clams and chicken. The signature 濃厚煮干そば is a mild-but-rich niboshi bowl with controlled bitterness and a clean sweetness, while the lighter 淡麗醤油そば and 淡麗塩そば show off fish-forward umami with aromatic chicken oil on top. Medium-thick straight whole-grain wheat noodles from a local noodle maker come topped with generous, well-seared chashu, nori, scallion, onion and fine chili threads.
bio.philosophy: A fish-driven ramen specialist standing apart from Kyoto's pork-bone-heavy norm, offering deliberately different lunch (lighter, delicate shio/shoyu) and dinner (richer niboshi) styles from one small counter.

anecdotes.text: The shop sits in the exact former location of Menya Himawari, a salt-ramen shop open since 2006 that moved to Shizuoka in November 2020; Menya Kagura opened there in February 2021 under the former Himawari owner's supervision.

In a Kyoto ramen scene dominated by pork-bone broths, Kagura's dedicated niboshi/seafood focus near Ginkaku-ji and Kyoto University is described as a rare specialty, with the owner running service solo.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Bowls are built on wheat noodles and a fish/shoyu base; the shoyu tare is the standard wheat-containing type with no gluten-free alternative mentioned on any source.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broths use animal stock (dried sardine, bonito, kombu, clam, chicken) with chicken oil and pork chashu; no vegan or vegetarian bowl exists, so shared pots make any vegan accommodation impossible.

caveats: Hours differ slightly between sources: blogs report 11:00-14:30 / 17:30-21:30, while Tabelog lists lunch 10:30-14:30 (L.O. 14:15) and dinner 17:30-20:00 (L.O. 19:45). Wednesday closed, with occasional irregular closures (solo operator) — confirm before a special trip. No official website found.

cuisine: 鶏白湯ラーメン (tori-paitan / chicken broth ramen)
neighborhood: 出町柳 / 今出川 (north of the Kyoto Imperial Palace)
gf_detail: This is a standard ramen shop built on wheat. The bowls use medium-thin wheat noodles (from a Kyoto noodle maker), and the chicken-paitan broth is almost certainly seasoned with a wheat-based shoyu tare. No gluten-free noodles, dedicated preparation, or allergy accommodation is mentioned anywhere. Not suitable for celiacs or strict gluten avoidance.
vegan_detail: The house style is a rich chicken (tori-paitan) broth, and the signature bowl is loaded with chicken chashu, chicken tsukune meatballs, and smoked poultry. The broth itself is animal-based, so even a no-toppings request would not be vegan. No vegetarian or vegan ramen is offered. (A cold tomato noodle and an oil/carbonara-style bowl appear on the menu but are not described as plant-based.)
address_ja: 京都府京都市上京区柳風呂町204
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 京都府京都市上京区柳風呂町204 returned [135.767014, 35.029919], within the Kyoto box.
seats_text: About 11 seats: a counter facing the kitchen on the right, plus a small tatami area on the left with two four-person tables (a layout inherited from the izakaya that previously occupied the space).
hours_raw: 11:30-15:30 (L.O. 15:00), 18:00-22:30 (L.O. 22:00). Sunday lunch only per Tabelog (11:30-15:00). Closed Sundays per shop guides; one source also notes Monday evenings closed.
closed_days: Sundays (one source also notes Monday evenings closed; Sunday hours vary by source)
cultural_comfort_note: A small neighborhood shop with no indication of English-language service; basic Japanese or a translation app will smooth the visit.
caveats: Hours and closed days vary between sources: shop guides say closed Sundays, while Tabelog lists a Sunday lunch (11:30-15:00) and one guide notes Monday evenings are also closed. The shop runs as a daytime ramen counter and shifts to an izakaya/omakase format in the evening, so the menu at night differs from lunch. Confirm same-day before going.

bio.background: Biden opened on July 16, 2021, just north of the Kyoto Imperial Palace in the Demachi neighborhood near Demachiyanagi Station, an area with very few ramen shops. The owner had previously cooked at a ramen restaurant over in the Saiin district before striking out on his own. The shop took over the premises of a former izakaya called YAOMON, which had closed after its previous owner fell ill, and Biden kept that izakaya bones, the tatami room and the counter, while reinventing it as a ramen specialist by day.
bio.origin: Kyoto (Demachi / north of the Imperial Palace)
bio.specialty: Tori-paitan ramen (around 800 yen): a creamy-looking chicken broth that drinks surprisingly light and refined rather than heavy, paired with straight medium-thin wheat noodles from a long-established Kyoto noodle maker that give the 'patsun' snap locals favor. The bowl is unusually colorful and generous, topped with thin-sliced smoked chicken chashu, chicken tsukune meatballs, sauteed king oyster mushrooms, curry-seasoned bell pepper, sweet thick-cut grilled scallions, and daikon sprouts.
bio.philosophy: A chicken-paitan style that is rich but elegant, a style the local guides note is rare in Kyoto, served in a relaxed, lived-in room.

anecdotes.text: The owner is a film and arcade-game enthusiast, and the walls of the shop are decorated with movie posters.

The space was a former izakaya (YAOMON) that closed when its previous owner became ill; Biden reused its layout, with tatami seating on the left and the counter and kitchen on the right, and still flips into an izakaya/omakase service in the evenings.
The signature feature is the thick-cut grilled scallion topping, described as a novel touch that turns sweet when chewed.

cuisine: 豚骨醤油ラーメン・台湾屋台風ラーメン・つけ麺（tsukemen）
neighborhood: 左京区 北白川（茶山・京都芸術大学駅）
address_ja: 京都府京都市左京区北白川上別当町1 第2青山荘 1F 6号
geocode_note: GSI geocoded at chō-level (北白川上別当町1); coordinates fall within the Kyoto bounding box. Building is 第2青山荘 1F, shared with sister shop 東龍 北白川本店.
seats_text: 18 seats: 8 counter, one 2-top, two 4-tops (per Tabelog; small intimate room).
hours_raw: 昼営業 11:30〜14:30 / 夜営業 18:00〜21:30（ラストオーダー）。定休日：火曜日・水曜日（連休）。※食べログ掲載では水曜定休（26年1月は火水連休）と表記があり、定休日表記に揺れあり。
closed_days: Tuesdays and Wednesdays (per official site). Tabelog lists Wednesday only, with a Jan 2026 note of Tue-Wed closures — confirm directly before visiting.
cultural_comfort_note: A small neighborhood ramen counter run by a local Kyoto operator; menus and signage are in Japanese, so a little Japanese or a translation app will smooth the visit.
gf_detail: Not safe for celiacs. The signature bowls are built on wheat ramen noodles, and the broth is a pork-bone/chicken (tonkotsu-shoyu) base seasoned with soy sauce, which in Japan is almost always brewed with wheat. There is no indication of gluten-free noodles, tamari, or any allergen protocol, and noodles are boiled in shared water. Treat as off-limits if you require strict GF.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The kitchen is built around a pork-bone and chicken broth with back fat, braised tendon, and minced Kyotamba pork; even the simplest bowls carry animal stock and pork toppings. No vegetable or vegan broth option is advertised.
caveats: 福仙楼 originally opened April 30, 2009 as an evening-only sister shop to 東龍, and the standalone storefront was reported closed May 10, 2020 with its menu folded into the adjacent 東龍 北白川本店 (a frozen-ramen vending machine for both brands was added in Sept 2022). However, the brand currently operates as a dine-in counter in the same 第2青山荘 building, and recent reviews (June and July 2025) confirm it is actively serving. There are two Tabelog listings for this shop (26034438 = target, and 26012742); treat them as the same place. Closed-day data conflicts between sources — verify before visiting.

bio.background: 福仙楼 is the台湾屋台 (Taiwanese street-stall) sibling of the well-known Kitashirakawa ramen shop 東龍, opened on April 30, 2009 along the same stretch of 北白川. The team behind it studied Taiwanese street-stall noodle cooking and brought that style back to this quiet student quarter near Kyoto University of the Arts, running it for years as an evening counterpart to 東龍's daytime service. Over time the two brands have grown intertwined in the same 第2青山荘 building, so a visit here is really a visit to a small two-headed ramen house with its own devoted following.
bio.specialty: 福仙楼そば — a rich pork-bone-and-chicken broth finished with back fat and a soy (shoyu) tare, poured over medium-thick, flat, straight house-made noodles and crowned with slow-braised tendon and juicy minced Kyotamba heritage pork, plus bean sprouts, scallion and garlic. Larger 福スペシャル and チャーシュー麺 versions pile on more pork.
bio.philosophy: Generous, hearty Taiwanese-street-stall-style bowls at student-friendly prices — comfort ramen made to fill you up rather than to chase trends.

anecdotes.text: The shop's identity is rooted in Taiwanese 屋台 (street-stall) cooking: its core bowl is literally called 屋台そば, and the house style was developed by studying Taiwanese street-noodle technique before adapting it to Kyoto.
福仙楼 and 東龍 are sister operations sharing the same 北白川 building, so regulars can taste two distinct house ramen — the creamy 東龍そば and the soy-rich 福仙楼そば — within steps of each other.

roles: sister shop of 東龍 北白川本店

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles are the base of every bowl and are boiled in shared water; there is no gluten-free noodle or separate-pot option mentioned.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is a pork-bone/chicken base finished with a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare; Japanese soy sauce is typically brewed with wheat, so the soup itself is not gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All bowls are built on animal stock (pork bone, chicken, back fat) with pork and tendon toppings; no vegan or vegetable broth is offered, so cross-contact with animal products is unavoidable.

cuisine: 鶏清湯・鶏白湯・伊勢海老白湯ラーメン（ヴィーガンラーメンあり）
neighborhood: 右京区 嵯峨嵐山（嵯峨釈迦堂門前、竹林の小径近く）
gf_detail: No gluten-free option. All bowls are built on wheat ramen noodles (sourced from Kiyokawa Seimen, including "whole-grain" noodles used for the vegan bowls — whole grain still means wheat, not gluten-free), and the tare is naturally-fermented shiro-tamari or soy sauce, both of which are wheat-based. There is no indication of a gluten-free noodle or a wheat-free seasoning, and no dedicated handling for celiac diners. Not suitable for strict gluten avoidance.
vegan_detail: Genuine vegan bowls are a fixed part of the menu, not an afterthought: a Kyoto-vegetable salt ramen, a "tonkotsu-style" vegetable ramen (plant-based, mimicking pork-bone richness), and a 100% vegan Shinshu-miso ramen, all on whole-grain (wheat) noodles, around 2,000 yen. However this is a small counter shop whose core trade is chicken-clear, chicken-paitan and spiny-lobster-paitan broths with pork chashu, so the vegan bowls are prepared in the same kitchen alongside animal broths. Good for plant-based diners by ingredient, but ask about shared equipment if you need strict separation.
address_ja: 京都府京都市右京区嵯峨釈迦堂門前裏柳町6-4
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned coordinates within the Kyoto box for the registered address.
seats_text: About 18 seats: a counter plus a few small tables. Solo diners and small groups welcome; fully non-smoking.
hours_raw: 11:00-19:00（営業時間・定休日は不定期の場合あり）
closed_days: Irregular holidays (no fixed closing day published); may close without notice, so check before visiting.
cultural_comfort_note: A small owner-run counter shop in a heavily-touristed area with a smartphone ordering system and English-friendly vegan options, so a few words of Japanese and a polite manner go a long way, but you will likely manage.

bio.background: RAMEN Arashiyama Chikurintei opened in December 2023, just off the famous bamboo grove in Saga-Arashiyama, run by a trained Japanese-cuisine (washoku) chef and his wife. The couple bring a kaiseki-style attention to stock-making and ingredient sourcing to a bowl of ramen.
bio.specialty: Their signature is the ise-ebi (spiny lobster) paitan ramen, a creamy broth built from Nagoya Cochin and Daiyama chicken bones and Ibaraki pork and finished with extract pressed from spiny-lobster heads, served with additive-free Kyoto-pork rare chashu and a shrimp terrine; the clear chicken (tori chintan) bowl, seasoned with Guerande salt or naturally-fermented shiro-tamari, shows the same restraint.
bio.philosophy: Quality ingredients and meticulous, additive-free preparation: named-breed chicken bones, kombu and bonito for the stocks, additive-free Kyoto pork for the chashu, and specially-milled noodles from Kiyokawa Seimen. The kitchen also keeps a real plant-based line so vegan and international visitors are not left out.

anecdotes.text: The spiny-lobster paitan gets its depth from broth pressed out of the lobster heads, layered over a chicken-and-pork base.
Run by a washoku professional and his wife, the shop is described locally as standing out as the best ramen in the Arashiyama area.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All ramen, including the vegan bowls, uses wheat noodles; the seasoning is wheat-based shiro-tamari or soy sauce. No gluten-free noodle or tare exists here, so the kitchen has no gluten-free workflow.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Tare is naturally-fermented shiro-tamari (a white tamari/soy) or soy sauce, both wheat-containing.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The vegan ramen (vegetable salt, vegetable 'tonkotsu-style', and 100% vegan Shinshu-miso) is genuinely plant-based by recipe, but it is cooked in the same small kitchen as chicken-clear, chicken-paitan and spiny-lobster broths with pork chashu, so shared pots/utensils are likely.

positives.text: Multiple dedicated vegan bowls are a permanent menu item, and the kitchen emphasizes additive-free ingredients, which is reassuring for plant-based diners by ingredient.

caveats: Seat count published as 18 on the listing but a local article gives roughly 20 (8 counter + 12 table); used the listing figure. No fixed closing day is published and hours/holidays can be irregular, so confirm before visiting. Vegan bowls are genuine but prepared in a kitchen that also handles chicken, lobster and pork, so cross-contact for strict vegans is unverified. No allergen-handling statements were found from the shop itself.

cuisine: つけ麺専門・ラーメン（イベリコ豚つけ麺、生醤油らぁ麺、豚骨鶏白湯らぁ麺）
gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The signature tsukemen uses straight wheat noodles (which include wheat bran) from a noodle maker, and the tare is a blend of two soy sauce varieties (wheat-based) cut with kombu water. Broth is pork-bone and chicken collagen. No gluten-free noodle or dedicated-prep option is indicated; a celiac should not eat here.
vegan_detail: Not vegan or vegetarian. Every signature bowl is built on animal stock — pork-bone/chicken-collagen broth, Iberico pork belly and low-temperature pork chashu, and soy-sauce tare. There is no plant-based ramen on the menu.
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区菱屋町33 珠光ビル三条 1F
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 京都市中京区菱屋町33; within the Kyoto box. Near 烏丸御池駅, consistent with listed location (~3 min walk).
seats_text: 18 seats — 8 at the counter, 10 at tables
closed_days: Open daily; closed January 1st (New Year's Day) only
cultural_comfort_note: A polished, design-forward lunch-only tsukemen counter intended to feel welcoming even to solo diners; a friendly greeting and pointing at the signature bowl will carry you, though staff likely operate mainly in Japanese.
caveats: Cash only, no reservations, and lunch service only (11:00-15:30, last order 15:00) — plan to arrive before mid-afternoon. Not suitable for gluten-free or vegan diners.
