neighborhood: 中京区・三条烏丸（地下鉄 烏丸御池駅すぐ）

bio.background: slurp is the fourth shop from the Kansai ramen group behind セアブラノ神 (Seabura no Kami), a name that built its reputation popularizing niboshi-and-tonkotsu style ramen in Kyoto through its Mibu flagship and the Fushimi-Gōriki and Nishiki branches. Opened in 2020 near Sanjō-Karasuma, slurp was the group's first venture into tsukemen, deliberately styled less like a traditional ramen shop and more like an upscale dining bar in black-and-white so that even a solo diner feels at ease walking in.
bio.specialty: The Iberico pork tsukemen — pitched as 'one bowl, five ways to enjoy it.' Straight noodles are first soaked in kombu (kelp) water and eaten plain to taste their fragrance and umami, then dipped into a clear-yet-rich broth of pork bone and chicken collagen finished with premium Spanish Iberico pork belly whose fat renders at low temperature for richness without heaviness; a squeeze of citrus refreshes the midpoint, and the leftover broth is cut with bonito dashi and finished over rice like a soup or risotto.
bio.philosophy: Treat a single bowl of tsukemen as an entertaining, multi-course experience, and make the room approachable enough that anyone — including women dining alone — feels welcome.

anecdotes.text: slurp was the established Seabura no Kami group's first move into tsukemen, designed as a stylish dining-bar-like space rather than a conventional ramen counter so solo and first-time diners feel comfortable.

The signature Iberico pork tsukemen is built to be eaten in stages — plain kombu-water noodles, then dipped, then brightened with citrus, then the broth finished with bonito dashi over rice — described as 'five ways to enjoy one bowl.'

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The tsukemen tare is described as a blend of two soy sauce varieties cut with kombu-infused water; standard soy sauce is wheat-based.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The dipping broth is a pork-bone and chicken-collagen base finished with Iberico pork belly, and the bowl is topped with pork belly and sous-vide pork chashu — animal-based throughout.

cuisine: 二郎系ラーメン (Jiro-style ramen)
gf_detail: This is Jiro-style ramen: thick wheat noodles in a pork-bone broth (or as a brothless 汁なし bowl) built on a soy-based tare. Wheat is present in both the noodles and the soy seasoning, and there is no indication of any gluten-free option or accommodation. Not suitable for celiacs or anyone strictly avoiding gluten.
vegan_detail: A pork-centric Jiro-style shop: pork-bone broth, large slabs of chashu, and pork back-fat are core to every bowl, with mountains of bean sprouts and raw garlic as standard toppings. There is no vegan or vegetarian bowl, and no indication that one can be made.
address_ja: 京都府京都市上京区四番町126-10
geocode_note: Geocoded from the full street address via GSI; result [135.741272, 35.024895] falls within the Kyoto bounding box.
neighborhood: 上京区 四番町（千本中立売・北野白梅町エリア）
seats_text: L字型のカウンター席のみ（席数は非公表）。店外で先に注文し、呼ばれてから入店するスタイル。
closed_days: 日曜（Sunday）
cultural_comfort_note: A no-frills, locals-and-students Jiro-style counter where you order outside, wait to be called in, and call your toppings ("ニンニク・アブラ・ヤサイ") in Japanese at the counter, so first-timers without a guide or some prep can find the ritual intimidating.
caveats: Seat count is not publicly stated. The current owner's name is reported variously as "Teru/おてる"; treat as approximate. Hours and cash-only status are from Tabelog and aggregator blogs and should be reconfirmed on arrival, as small Jiro shops often sell out or adjust hours.

bio.background: The shop sits in the lineage of "Yume Wo Katare Kyoto," the dream-fueled Jiro-style ramen project that opened in this Senbon-Nakatachiuri neighborhood in 2017. True to the Yume Wo Katare ethos, where a location either closes or is reborn under a new name after a set run, the second-generation shop was rebranded on April 1, 2025 as あけてもくれても under owner Oteru, carrying the same hulking bowls into a new identity.
bio.specialty: A classic Jiro-style bowl: ultra-thick, chewy wheat noodles in a sweet soy-and-pork-bone broth, crowned with a fat slab of chashu, a mountain of bean sprouts, back-fat, and garlic. The house also leans into a signature 汁なし (brothless) version, a nod echoed in the playful name.
bio.philosophy: Carry forward the Yume Wo Katare spirit of generous, soul-filling ramen and the discipline of reinventing the shop rather than letting it stagnate, while keeping the experience honest, hands-on, and built around the counter.

anecdotes.text: The shop's name, あけてもくれても ("whether it opens or closes / day in, day out"), winks at both its rebirth from Yume Wo Katare and its embrace of the brothless 汁なし bowl.
It traces directly back to Yume Wo Katare Kyoto, opened in 2017, whose tradition of renaming or restarting a shop after its run led to this April 2025 incarnation.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The bowls are built on a sweet soy-based tare over wheat noodles, so wheat is present in both the seasoning and the noodles.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Pork-bone broth, large chashu, and pork back-fat are standard across the menu, with no meat-free preparation noted.

cuisine: 二郎系ラーメン・担担麺（jiro-kei ramen & tantanmen）
gf_detail: A jiro-style ramen shop built on thick wheat noodles, pork-bone broth, and wheat-based soy tare — not safe for celiac diners. There is no gluten-free menu, no gluten-free noodle option, and no indication of allergen separation in the kitchen. Tableside condiments include vinegar and chili. Even the lighter chuka-soba/shoyu bowls use wheat noodles and wheat soy sauce. Treat as not gluten-free.
vegan_detail: Every bowl is built on a pork-bone (tonkotsu) base, and jiro-style ramen centers on pork, back fat, and minced pork toppings. The shoyu/chuka-soba options still use a chicken-derived flavoring base per local coverage, so there is no vegetarian-safe bowl, let alone vegan. No plant-based or vegetable broth is offered.
address_ja: 京都府宇治市小倉町久保67
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned [135.784088, 34.89669] for 京都府宇治市小倉町久保67. Latitude 34.89669 falls just below the Kyoto-city verification box (34.93–35.13) because the shop is in Uji City, genuinely south of central Kyoto near Kintetsu Ogura Station. Coordinates are correct for the actual location; only the prescriptive box does not cover Uji.
neighborhood: 宇治市小倉町（近鉄小倉駅 徒歩約10分／旧24号線沿い）
seats_text: Table-seating-focused rather than the usual counter-only jiro layout; exact seat count not published. Two parking spaces in front.
closed_days: 火曜（Tuesday）
cultural_comfort_note: A friendly neighborhood jiro-style shop with table seating that is welcoming to first-timers, but ordering and customization happen in Japanese with no English support, so a non-Japanese-speaking diner will want a guide or translation help.
caveats: Gluten-free and vegan/vegetarian diners cannot be accommodated here: it is a pork-broth, wheat-noodle, wheat-soy jiro-style ramen shop with no allergen-safe options. Hours are split (lunch and dinner) with a Tuesday closure; portions can be tailored, but the food itself is rich and meat-forward.

bio.background: Menya Buta no Kokoro opened on February 11, 2022 along the old Route 24 in the Ogura district of Uji, about a ten-minute walk from Kintetsu Ogura Station, taking over a space that had previously been a takoyaki shop. It quickly became something of a rarity for the area: a proper jiro-style ramen house in a part of Kyoto where that bold, garlicky, pork-heavy style is hard to find.
bio.specialty: The bowl that defines the shop is its own take on jiro-kei tantanmen (二郎系担担麺): a thick, foamy pork-bone broth layered with the aroma of sesame, the heat of chili oil, and the tingling numbness of Sichuan peppercorn, all balanced so the spice adds complexity rather than just burning. It is served over ultra-thick straight noodles with minced pork, bean sprouts, cabbage and green onion, with back fat lending a sweet, umami richness. The straight jiro-style 'pork ramen' is the other headliner.
bio.philosophy: Unlike the silent, counter-only jiro shops where regulars hunch over their bowls, this place is built around table seating and a relaxed, welcoming feel so that newcomers and families can enjoy the style without intimidation. The kitchen leans customer-first: noodle portions can be dialed in across a wide range (roughly 150g up to 400g), and diners adjust garlic, vegetables, back fat and (for the tantanmen) Sichuan pepper to taste at ordering — an approach the owners describe as deliberately gentle on an older local crowd as well as big eaters.

anecdotes.text: The shop occupies a former takoyaki stand on the old Route 24, with a couple of parking spots out front — a low-key roadside spot rather than a downtown destination.
Its signature jiro-kei tantanmen is praised for a foamy, mellow pork-bone finish where sesame, chili-oil heat and Sichuan-pepper numbness stack up in balance instead of overwhelming the palate.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Jiro-style ramen is built on thick wheat noodles and a wheat-based soy tare; nothing in the shop's coverage indicates gluten-free noodles or any allergen separation, so cross-contamination from wheat is effectively unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The menu uses standard Japanese soy sauce throughout (including a shoyu/chuka-soba bowl), which is wheat-based; no wheat-free tamari is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All bowls share a pork-bone broth and the kitchen handles pork, back fat and minced pork as core ingredients, so any vegetable element is prepared in a fully meat-based environment.

positives.text: Staff let diners customize portion size and toppings (garlic, vegetables, back fat, Sichuan pepper) at ordering, so it is easy to ask for lighter or adjusted bowls — though this does not extend to allergen-safe preparation.

cuisine: Ramen — Jiro-inspired (二郎インスパイア) extra-thick noodle bowls, with both broth (汁あり) and no-broth/mazesoba-style (汁なし) options
gf_detail: No gluten-free provision. This is a Jiro-inspired ramen shop built around extremely thick wheat noodles, served with a sweet wheat-shoyu-based tare and a pork broth — the entire concept is wheat-noodle-centric. There is no indication of GF noodles, GF tare, allergen handling, or a dedicated preparation area. A celiac diner cannot eat safely here.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The bowls are built on pork (chashu/meat toppings) and an animal-based broth with a soy/wheat tare; signature orders are explicitly "with meat" (肉あり). No vegan ramen option is mentioned anywhere in the sources.
address_ja: 京都府京都市下京区七条御所ノ内南町89 八条ビル 1F
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned coordinates [135.732193, 34.983891] for 京都府京都市下京区七条御所ノ内南町89, within the Kyoto bounding box. Matches the described location ~3-4 min walk north of JR Nishi-Oji Station.
seats_text: 10 counter seats only
hours_raw: Mon/Tue/Thu/Fri 11:00-14:00 & 18:00-22:00; Sat/Sun 10:30-14:00; closed Wednesdays (and holidays). Note: the owner sometimes closes on short notice when he judges the noodle quality is not up to standard.
closed_days: Wednesdays and holidays; occasional unscheduled closures when the owner deems the day's noodles below standard
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny 10-seat counter shop with ticket-machine ordering and no listed phone or English support — comfortable for a visitor with some Japanese or willingness to point and gesture, but plan to order by machine and dine quickly.
neighborhood: 下京区 西大路八条（near JR Nishi-Oji Station）
caveats: The app's intake hint described this as a 汁なし/まぜそば 極太麺 shop; the verified sources describe it more broadly as a Jiro-inspired (二郎インスパイア) extra-thick-noodle ramen-ya offering both broth (汁あり) and no-broth (汁なし, e.g. "シルナシノ神") bowls — both descriptions agree on the defining extra-thick wheat noodles and the owner's Nagoya「ら けいこ」background. Hours and the Wednesday closure are confirmed across sources, but the owner closes irregularly on quality grounds, so confirm the shop is open before traveling. No phone number is published. Tabelog rating/review figures intentionally excluded from diner-facing content.

bio.origin: Nagoya, via the Jiro-inspired ramen shop「ら けいこ」(Rakeiko)
bio.background: The owner cut his teeth at Nagoya's famed Jiro-inspired shop「ら けいこ」before bringing that gut-punching, big-portion style to a small storefront in southwest Kyoto, a short walk north of Nishi-Oji Station.
bio.specialty: Extra-thick (極太), chewy straight noodles in generous, hearty bowls — a sweet soy-based tare clinging to the noodles, piled with vegetables, meat and garlic. The shop runs both broth (汁あり) and no-broth (汁なし) versions, with a standout 'no-broth' bowl regulars nickname シルナシノ神 ('the god of soupless').
bio.philosophy: Noodles first, no compromise: the owner will close the shop on the spot if he judges the day's noodles aren't good enough — a quality-over-convenience stance that keeps the schedule a little unpredictable but the bowls honest.

anecdotes.text: Regulars know the shop as a 'knowing-people-know-it' hidden gem, and that the owner has been known to post that he is closing because the noodles didn't come out right that day — quality over keeping the doors open.
The defining feature is the gut-punch (ガツン系) extra-thick noodle, a direct inheritance of the Nagoya「ら けいこ」lineage the owner trained in.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Bowls are dressed with a sweet soy-based (shoyu) tare, which in this style is wheat-containing; combined with the wheat noodles there is no celiac-safe path here.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Bowls are built on pork and meat toppings with an animal-based broth; no vegan preparation is mentioned.

lead: 担担麺専門店 ENISHI 京都小倉店
name_ja: 担担麺専門店 ENISHI 京都小倉店
cuisine: 担々麺専門（汁あり・汁なし担々麺）／Tantanmen specialist
gf_detail: A dedicated tantanmen shop built around wheat ramen noodles (including a Kyoto-exclusive extra-thick noodle), a spice-and-sesame tare, and a meat-based broth. No gluten-free noodle or preparation is offered; the dishes are inherently wheat-heavy. Not suitable for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: Classic Sichuan-style tantanmen with a meat-based broth and minced-pork topping; the spicy oil and sesame tare and the included sides are built around the standard recipe. No vegan or vegetarian version is advertised.
address_ja: 京都府宇治市小倉町神楽田33-8 千歳ビル 105
geocode_note: GSI geocode for 京都府宇治市小倉町神楽田33-8 returned [lng 135.781372, lat 34.892696]. This is geographically correct for Uji City (near Kintetsu Ogura Station) but falls below the Kyoto validation box minimum (lat 34.93), since Uji lies south of central Kyoto. Coordinates nulled per box rule; chō-level retry returned the identical point.
neighborhood: 宇治市 小倉町神楽田（近鉄小倉駅前）
seats_text: 17席（カウンター5席、4人掛けテーブル3席）
closed_days: 水曜日（Wednesday）
cultural_comfort_note: A small, busy noodle counter near the station where you order, customize your heat level, and slurp quickly; basic Japanese for ordering helps, and a friendly konnichiwa goes a long way.
caveats: Cash only — no cards, e-money, or QR payment. No reservations; a 17-seat counter shop that can fill at peak. Closed Wednesdays. Coordinates could not be placed within the Kyoto validation box because the shop is in Uji, south of central Kyoto (see geocode_note).

bio.origin: 兵庫・神戸の灘（Nada, Kobe, Hyogo）
bio.background: ENISHI began as a tantanmen specialist in the Nada ward of Kobe, Hyogo, and grew into a small group with shops in Sannomiya (Kobe), Fukushima, and Fuse in the Osaka area. The Uji shop, which opened on February 9, 2026, is the brand's first venture into Kyoto, set in a former hair-salon space reworked into a calm, Japanese-style interior that deliberately avoids an overtly Chinese look.
bio.specialty: The signature bowl is the 汁なし担々麺 (brothless tantanmen) — a deeply savory, nutty-and-numbing dan dan built on a blend of more than 20 spices, with diners choosing their own balance of chili oil (ラー油), heat (辛さ), and Sichuan-pepper tingle (シビレ). The Kyoto branch adds a location-exclusive 超極太麺 (extra-thick noodle) with a hearty, chewy 'washi-washi' bite likened to Jiro-style ramen.
bio.philosophy: Customizable, spice-forward tantanmen done as a focused specialty rather than a broad ramen menu — let the guest dial in their own oil, heat, and numbing intensity, and round out the meal with free self-serve sides.

anecdotes.text: ENISHI's flagship (総本店) has been a perennial Tabelog Hyakumeiten (Top 100) tantanmen shop for roughly 8-9 consecutive years, and the Uji opening marks the brand's first store in Kyoto.
The shop offers free self-serve accompaniments — black oolong tea, Sichuan zāsài (搾菜), fried garlic, and shibazuke pickles — alongside the bowls.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles (including a Kyoto-exclusive extra-thick noodle) are the core of every dish, prepared in a shared noodle-cooking environment — not safe for gluten-free or celiac diners.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Bowls are built on a meat-based broth and minced-pork topping; spice oil and sesame tare are part of the standard recipe, with no vegan preparation advertised.

cuisine: Machi-chuka / taishoku-do — chicken-bone soy chuka-soba and home-style noodle, rice-bowl, and set-meal dishes
gf_detail: No gluten-free provision. The chuka-soba is wheat Chinese noodles in a chicken-bone soy (shoyu) broth, and the menu centers on wheat-based udon, soba, ramen, yakisoba, curry and fried tempura items. Soy-based tare and seasonings are standard Japanese shoyu (wheat-containing). There is no indication of allergen handling or a dedicated fryer, so this is not safe for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly shop. The signature chuka-soba uses a chicken-bone (tori-gara) soy broth with roasted pork and kamaboko (fish cake) toppings, and dishes broadly rely on dashi, egg, meat and seafood. No vegan options are advertised.
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区西ノ京北壺井町62
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned coordinates for 京都府京都市中京区西ノ京北壺井町62; within Kyoto box.
seats_text: Primarily table seating; a small number of counter seats are also described. Exact seat count not published.
hours_raw: Monday–Saturday 11:00–19:30; Sunday closed.
closed_days: Sundays (日曜日)
cultural_comfort_note: A long-standing local neighborhood diner with handwritten/wall menus in Japanese and no English signage indicated, so a little Japanese or pointing at the food samples will smooth the visit.

bio.background: Mansei is a much-loved old-style neighborhood shokudo (taishoku-do / machi-chuka) in the Nishinokyo–Enmachi area of Kyoto, near Enmachi Station and Hanazono University. It is the kind of culturally-valuable corner eatery (described locally as a 文化財的な街の食堂, a culturally-treasured town diner) that has fed the surrounding neighborhood for generations, with neatly kept food-sample cases in the window, an aged wall menu, manga to read while you wait, and a relaxed table-seat layout. Delivery to local homes and workplaces is part of its everyday rhythm.
bio.specialty: The signature bowl is the chuka-soba: a simple, body-warming chicken-bone soy (tori-gara shoyu) broth topped with kamaboko fish cake, roast pork, bean sprouts and chopped green onion, served with firm, low-hydration Chinese-style noodles. Beyond the chuka-soba the kitchen turns out a wide home-style range — udon, soba, yakisoba, rice bowls, curry, omurice and stir-fries — at gentle prices, with the tenjoji-don (egg-bound shrimp-tempura rice bowl, around 680 yen) a noted favorite.
bio.philosophy: Honest, affordable, comforting everyday food for the neighborhood — the appeal is the nostalgic, unpretentious shokudo experience rather than any single trend.

anecdotes.text: Reviewers describe the chuka-soba as a simple, comforting chicken-bone soy broth that seems to soak into your body (シンプルで、身体に染み入る鶏ガラ醤油の中華そば), with kamaboko, roast pork, bean sprouts and green onion over firm, low-hydration noodles.
The shop is praised as a culturally-valuable town diner with carefully kept food samples, a well-aged wall menu and manga to browse, with table seating that encourages a leisurely meal.
The tenjoji-don is highlighted: two shrimp tempura and rice enveloped in plenty of dashi-infused egg (海老天ぷら2尾とごはんを出汁を含んだたっぷりの玉子が包み込む), around 680 yen.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Shared kitchen turning out wheat noodles (udon, soba, ramen, yakisoba), tempura and curry; no gluten-free handling indicated, so high cross-contamination risk for celiac diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The chuka-soba is a chicken-bone soy (shoyu) broth; standard Japanese shoyu contains wheat and no gluten-free tare is offered.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths and dishes rely on chicken-bone stock, dashi, pork, fish cake and egg; no vegan preparation indicated.

caveats: Name reads "Mansei" (まんせい) per local coverage. Exact seat count and founding date are not published; bio history is inferred from neighborhood-diner coverage rather than an explicit founding record. No official website found; phone 075-811-5987.

lead: 麺スター む・ラッキー
name_ja: 麺スター む・ラッキー
cuisine: Tori-paitan & tori-shoyu ramen (chicken broth ramen)
neighborhood: Jujo-Omiya, Minami-ku (near Kintetsu Jujo Station)
address_ja: 京都府京都市南区西九条森本町18
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned coordinates for 京都府京都市南区西九条森本町18; within Kyoto box.
seats_text: About 19 seats: 5 counter seats plus table seating (four 2-person tables and one 6-person table). Mainly table-based with some counter.
hours_raw: Listed lunch hours 11:30-14:30 (some sources note an earlier 11:00 open and an evening service ~18:00-20:00/21:00); closed Sundays. Hours have varied between sources, so evening availability is uncertain.
closed_days: Sundays (日曜日)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, casual neighborhood ramen counter with no indication of English support, so a few Japanese phrases and pointing at the menu will go a long way.
gf_detail: This is a wheat-noodle ramen shop. The bowls use standard wheat ramen noodles (from a Kyoto noodle maker) plus a soy-sauce-based tare in the shoyu bowls, and the chicken broths are not gluten-free. No gluten-free menu, noodle substitution, or celiac accommodation is indicated. Not suitable for celiacs or strict gluten avoidance.
vegan_detail: Every signature bowl is built on chicken broth (tori-shoyu, tori-shio, tori-paitan) and topped with pork chashu; the mazesoba and side sets (fried rice, karaage, gyudon) are also meat-based. No vegan or vegetarian option is indicated.
caveats: Hours conflict across sources: Tabelog lists lunch-only 11:30-14:30 Mon-Sat, while blogs (2023-2024) mention an additional evening service (~18:00-20:00/21:00). Evening availability should be confirmed before visiting; hours_week reflects the consistently-confirmed lunch window. Owner's specific former shop is described only as "a popular Kyoto shop" without a name.

bio.background: A chicken-broth ramen specialist that opened in July 2023 in the Jujo-Omiya neighborhood of Kyoto's Minami ward, just north of a local yakitori shop and a short walk from Kintetsu Jujo Station. It is described as having been opened by someone who trained at a popular Kyoto ramen shop.
bio.specialty: Chicken-forward ramen in three styles - tori-shoyu (chicken-soy), tori-shio (chicken-salt) and the signature tori-paitan (creamy chicken broth), plus a mazesoba. The tori-paitan is the recommended bowl, with extra hand-cut scallions a popular add-on; the tori-shoyu pairs a sweet soy tare with chicken-and-bonito dashi.
bio.philosophy: A focus on the clean umami of chicken - both a light chicken clear broth and a richer chicken paitan - served in a bright, clean, casual room with springy straight noodles from a respected Kyoto noodle maker (Teikaku/Tairaku Seimen).

anecdotes.text: Opened July 17, 2023 by someone said to come from a popular Kyoto ramen shop, with chicken clear broth and chicken paitan as the signature menu.

The tori-paitan is recommended with negi-mashi (extra scallions, +50 yen); the fresh hand-cut scallions are praised for their sweetness and pleasant bite, over a gentle broth with four slices of chashu and sprouts.

The tori-shoyu uses a sweeter soy tare that pairs well with a light chicken clear broth and bonito dashi, finished with bonito flakes and benina sprouts; the half fried-rice set is praised for its fluffy, peppery wok-fry.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The tori-shoyu bowl is built on a sweet soy-sauce tare, which in Japanese ramen is virtually always wheat-based soy sauce.

lead: 京都金閣寺 らーめんなか澤
name_ja: 京都金閣寺 らーめんなか澤
cuisine: Ramen — chuka-soba / chicken paitan (鶏白湯). Signature "quadruple" clear-and-creamy broth built on Kyoto Akaji-dori chicken, pork bones, Shinji-ko freshwater clams (shijimi) and a seafood blend, plus a three-year-aged shoyu tare.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The bowls center on wheat ramen noodles (thin straight noodles), and the broths are paired with a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare and salt versions — shoyu is wheat-based unless stated otherwise, and no gluten-free noodle or tare option is mentioned anywhere. No dedicated handling for celiac diners. A celiac traveler cannot safely eat here.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The entire menu is built on animal stock — a chicken/pork/clam/fish "quadruple" paitan broth — and bowls are topped with multiple kinds of chashu pork and egg. No vegetable-based or vegan broth is offered.
address_ja: 京都府京都市北区平野桜木町38-7
geocode_note: GSI returned no result for the full banchi (38-7); used chō-level address 京都市北区平野桜木町. Coordinates fall within the Kyoto box.
neighborhood: 北区・平野（北野白梅町／金閣寺・北野天満宮周辺）。わら天神前バス停から徒歩約1分、北野白梅町駅から徒歩圏。
seats_text: 17 seats — counter plus table seating in a bright, café-like white-wood interior; comfortable for solo diners.
closed_days: Tuesday (火曜日)。Monday is lunch-only (no dinner service).
cultural_comfort_note: A small, friendly solo-run counter shop where a few words of Japanese and a smile go a long way; English support is unlikely, so pointing and basic phrases are the comfortable way to order.
caveats: No gluten-free or vegan options; cash only; no reservations; Tuesday closed and Monday dinner not served. Geocode is chō-level, not the exact storefront.

bio.origin: Born in Kyoto; his family moved to Toyama when he was in middle school.
bio.background: Nakasawa spent about 18 years cooking ramen across Toyama and Tokyo before coming home to open his own shop near Kinkaku-ji in October 2023. By his own account he never set out to be a chef in the abstract — as he puts it, for him there was only ever ramen.
bio.specialty: The signature is a chicken-paitan bowl built on a so-called 'quadruple' soup: Kyoto Akaji-dori chicken bones, pork bones, Shinji-ko shijimi clams and a seafood blend (niboshi, scallop, bonito and mackerel), finished with a three-year-aged shoyu tare. The flagship 特製 鶏パイタンらーめん comes with three kinds of chashu, a soft-set egg made with Fukuchiyama 'Tamago Dosue,' and fried enoki mushrooms — a topping the shop bills as rare, possibly a first, in Kyoto.
bio.philosophy: He originally planned a clear-broth (chintan) ramen, but mindful of the nearby Ritsumeikan University crowd he reworked it into a creamy-yet-not-overpowering paitan aimed at winning over younger diners. The care shows in details like charcoal-grilled chashu whose smoky aroma carries into the soup — he has been known to ask guests whether they can taste the char.

anecdotes.text: Nakasawa runs the kitchen solo, preparing every bowl himself. A noodle-making machine sits visible from the street, reserved for his plan to eventually make the shop's own noodles in-house; for now the thin straight noodles come from Menya Teigyaku.
The fried enoki topping on the paitan bowl is promoted locally as a Kyoto first, alongside the unusual four-component 'quadruple' broth.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Bowls are dressed with a three-year-aged soy-sauce (shoyu) tare; soy sauce of this type is wheat-based, making the shoyu bowls unsuitable for gluten-free diners.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The single shared broth is an animal stock (chicken, pork, clam, fish); there is no separate vegetarian or vegan preparation, so no bowl can be made vegan.

cuisine: Ramen — tori-tonkotsu (chicken-and-pork-bone), with shio / miso / shoyu variations
neighborhood: Kita-ku, Omiya / Misonobashi (near Kamigamo Shrine and Kyoto Sangyo University)
address_ja: 京都府京都市北区大宮南田尻町53-1
geocode_note: Geocoded from full address_ja via GSI; coordinates within Kyoto box.
seats_text: About 16 seats — counter facing the kitchen plus three wall-side tables.
hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Fri, Sat 11:30-14:30 & 18:00-22:00; Wed 18:00-22:00; Thu 11:30-14:30 & 18:00-23:30; Sun closed. (Sources vary; some report later night hours to ~01:00-01:30. Irregular closures occur — check ahead.)
closed_days: Sundays; plus occasional irregular closures.
cultural_comfort_note: A small neighborhood ramen counter run by local staff with a student clientele; little to no English is expected, so a few Japanese phrases or pointing at the menu will help.
gf_detail: Standard ramen shop: wheat noodles served in pork/chicken broth with (typically wheat-based) shoyu or miso tare. No gluten-free noodles, dedicated prep, or allergen accommodation found in any source. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: All bowls are built on a chicken-and-pork-bone (tori-tonkotsu) broth; toppings include chashu pork. No vegan or vegetarian ramen option is described. Not suitable for vegans.
caveats: Reported hours differ between sources (some list late-night service to roughly 01:00-01:30); the shop also has irregular closures, so confirm before visiting. Exact seat split (counter vs. tables) varies slightly by source.

bio.background: Ramen Saishin opened on February 19, 2017 in the Misonobashi area of Kyoto's Kita Ward, taking over the premises of a previous ramen shop called Ramen Akashin that had closed a month earlier. The new operation kept the predecessor's layout, giving the place the lived-in feel of a long-established neighborhood shop, and inherited some of its recipes.
bio.origin: Kyoto (Kita-ku, near Kamigamo Shrine and Kyoto Sangyo University)
bio.specialty: A chicken-and-pork-bone (tori-tonkotsu) ramen offered in shio (salt), shoyu (soy sauce), and miso versions. The salt bowl is notable for a smooth, golden, almost corn-potage-colored broth in which chicken umami leads over the pork-bone richness, served with medium-thin straight noodles, a single slice of charred chashu, menma, nori, bean sprouts and scallions.
bio.philosophy: Mellow, refined broths rather than heavy or aggressive richness — a simple, balanced bowl that locals describe as easy to finish, with thicker noodles paired to the miso and thinner noodles to the shoyu.

anecdotes.text: The shop carried forward a peppery nira (Chinese chive) side dish inherited from the previous tenant, Ramen Akashin.

Often run by a small team of women, the counter is described as welcoming for solo diners, and it is a lunchtime favorite of nearby Kyoto Sangyo University students — fried-chicken (karaage) sets come with generous rice.

cuisine: Mazesoba (まぜそば)
gf_detail: This is a wheat-noodle mazesoba shop. The thick flat noodles are wheat-based, and the sweet-spicy tare is almost certainly soy-sauce-based (wheat-containing). There is no indication of any gluten-free option, dedicated equipment, or allergen accommodation. Not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
vegan_detail: The standard bowl includes pork (肉), and the signature 戦車スペシャル adds extra pork plus fish powder (魚粉); a raw egg is also a standard topping. Toppings such as scallions, bean sprouts, garlic, and pickled ginger are plant-based, but the base dish and tare are not vegan and there is no advertised vegan option. Not suitable for vegans.
address_ja: 京都府京都市上京区北小路室町388 ファーストコート御所室町 B1F
geocode_note: GSI geocoded to 京都府京都市上京区北小路室町３８８番地 at [135.757797, 35.028576]; within Kyoto box.
neighborhood: 上京区・北小路室町（今出川烏丸、今出川駅 徒歩2分）
seats_text: 16 seats — counter (about 5) plus 3 tables (about 12). Small basement space.
hours_raw: 11:30-15:00 (L.O. 14:45). Lunch-only operation inside a live-music venue basement. Operates roughly 20 days a month with irregular closures — check the shop's SNS for the day's status.
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular; ~20 operating days/month — confirm via SNS)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, cash-and-QR student-district lunch counter run in Japanese with no reservations and irregular hours — comfortable for an independent visitor who can order in Japanese and roll with a casual, no-frills setup.

bio.background: Opened October 2, 2021, this is the Kansai-region outpost of a mazesoba concept produced by Saitama's 大宮まぜそば誠治 (Omiya Mazesoba Seiji). It runs as a daytime tenant operation in the basement of a live-music venue (PUB VOXhall / ファーストコート御所室町) near Imadegawa-Karasuma, in the heart of the student district.
bio.origin: Concept originates from Saitama (大宮まぜそば誠治); this is its first Kansai location.
bio.specialty: The signature bowl is the 戦車スペシャル (Sensha Special): the base mazesoba of flat thick wavy noodles from Menya Teigaku (麺屋棣鄂) tossed in a sweet-spicy tare, piled with extra pork (肉増し), chopped scallions (刻みネギ), and fish powder (魚粉), then finished with a 追い飯 rice refill so you can mop up the leftover sauce.
bio.philosophy: Customization-first: a mild, sweet-savory sauce as a canvas, with 10-plus toppings and free add-ons (garlic, pickled ginger, oil, raw egg) so each diner builds their own bowl — staff even suggest trying it once without garlic to taste the sauce.

anecdotes.text: Diners crack a raw egg over the noodles and eat it sukiyaki-style, then add a rice refill to finish the remaining sauce.
It has reportedly become an unofficial campus cafeteria for nearby Doshisha University students.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The mazesoba is served in a sweet-spicy tare (甘辛いタレ); such tare is standard soy-sauce-based and wheat-containing, though the shop does not publish its ingredients.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Base bowl and signature special contain pork and fish powder, and raw egg is a standard add-on; no vegan preparation is described.

caveats: Phone number not publicly listed. Hours are irregular (~20 days/month) — confirm via the shop's SNS before visiting. Lunch-only, no reservations. No GF or vegan options; not safe for celiac diners. Seat breakdown (counter ~5 + tables ~12 = 16) is approximate.

cuisine: Authentic Chinese / chuka-soba (chicken-and-seafood clear-broth ramen)
neighborhood: Yamashina (Nagitsuji), Kyoto
address_ja: 京都府京都市山科区椥辻西浦町38-9
geocode_note: GSI first feature geometry.coordinates=[135.813187, 34.974949] for 京都府京都市山科区椥辻西浦町38-9; within Kyoto box.
seats_text: About 12 counter/table seats; no private rooms.
hours_raw: 11:30-15:00 (L.O. 14:30), 17:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:30); closed Tuesdays. (Some listings note lunch to 14:30 and dinner to 20:50.)
closed_days: Tuesdays
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny neighborhood Chinese restaurant tucked deep in a Yamashina residential block; service is in Japanese and best approached with a guide or some Japanese, with simple points-and-smile ordering otherwise.
gf_detail: No gluten-free provision found. The signature bowls are built on wheat ramen noodles (and the crispy 揚げそば on fried wheat noodles) in chicken-and-seafood broth, with Chinese-style sauces that typically contain wheat-based soy sauce. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Not a vegetarian or vegan venue. The kitchen centers on chicken-bone and seafood broth, pork gyoza, and meat/seafood Chinese dishes; no vegan ramen or confirmed plant-based options were found. A vegetable stir-fry (野菜炒め) appears on the menu but is not confirmed meat/animal-stock free.
caveats: Address confirmed as 椥辻西浦町 (one Tabelog field rendered the ward text ambiguously as Yabuhara, but search, blog and Ekiten listings all confirm Nagitsuji Nishiura-cho). Hours vary slightly between listings (dinner last order 20:30 vs 20:50). No owner/chef biographical detail is published, so the bio story is limited to the food itself.

bio.background: Zuishin is a small, hard-to-find Chinese restaurant tucked deep within a quiet Yamashina residential neighborhood, reached through narrow back streets a short walk from Nagitsuji Station. Locals describe it as a hidden gem that carries the quiet confidence of a long-respected establishment.
bio.specialty: The signature bowl is the tori-soba (chicken soba): a refined, clear (清湯) soup built on chicken-bone stock rounded out with seafood umami, pairing mellow body with a clean finish. Silky noodles are matched to the soup. The crispy fried noodles (揚げそば), made by deep-frying flat wide noodles for a fragrant crunch, and the curry fried rice are other house favorites.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen positions itself as authentic Chinese cooking (中国料理) rather than the Japanized 中華 stereotype, leaning on the aroma of its ingredients - for example gyoza scented with genuine star anise - rather than heavy seasoning.

anecdotes.text: Diners note the soup balances chicken-bone richness with added seafood umami for a flavor that is both mellow and crisp, with the tori-soba treated as the standout.
The crispy fried noodles are flat, wide noodles deep-fried until fragrant, served with vegetables and pork in sauce.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Authentic Chinese dishes and tare here typically use wheat-based soy sauce; no wheat-free soy sauce is indicated.

lead: 辛麺屋 鯱輪 京都今出川店
name_ja: 辛麺屋 鯱輪 京都今出川店
cuisine: Spicy ramen / mazesoba (Nagoya Taiwan-style x Miyazaki karamen)
gf_detail: No gluten-free option. The bowls are built on wheat ramen noodles with a house chili tare (Japanese chili-pepper/soy tares are typically wheat-containing) over a meat broth, plus minced pork, chashu and back fat. There is no published allergen information and no dedicated handling, so this should be treated as unsafe for celiac diners. Expect wheat throughout.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The signature spicy bowls are topped with stir-fried minced meat, seared chashu pork and pork back fat, finished with a beaten egg, over a meat-based broth. No vegetable or plant-based ramen is offered.
address_ja: 京都府京都市上京区室町頭町278
geocode_note: GSI address search returned [135.757675, 35.033215] for 京都市上京区室町頭町278; within Kyoto box. Shop sits near the Doshisha University Imadegawa campus / Imadegawa Station.
neighborhood: 上京区・室町頭町（今出川駅）
seats_text: 11 counter seats (counter only)
closed_days: Closed Tuesdays; also closed the 2nd and 4th Wednesday of each month
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny 11-seat counter ramen bar with no English menu or allergen info; a knowledgeable guide is recommended, though a GF/vegan diner realistically cannot eat here.

bio.background: Shachirin opened on July 15, 2023 on Imadegawa near the Doshisha University campus, bringing the 'Nagoya karamen' style to Kyoto as a branch of the Nagoya brand 名古屋辛麺 鯱輪. The whole 11-seat counter shop leans into its theme — walls, chairs and towels are bright red, as if the room itself were a bowl of spicy ramen.
bio.specialty: Its signature is the umami-spicy bowl that fuses Nagoya Taiwan-style ramen with Miyazaki karamen: stir-fried minced meat, garlic, Chinese chives, bean sprouts and green onion over noodles, with optional seared chashu and sweet back fat, and a beaten egg to mellow the heat. Diners pick a spice level from normal up through 5 and then several steps beyond, and many pour the leftover spicy soup over a free bowl of rice.
bio.philosophy: House-developed chili heat built on three kinds of chili pepper blended for sweetness, aroma and spiciness, prepared cup by cup, layered onto a savory umami base.

anecdotes.text: The recipe was supervised by Naoto Niiyama, head of the acclaimed Nagoya ramen shop Menya Hanabi (麺屋はなび).
The interior is deliberately all-red — walls, chairs and towels — so the whole shop reads as a giant bowl of spicy ramen.
Noodle dishes come with free rice refills, and customers commonly finish by pouring the spicy broth over rice.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Bowls are built on wheat ramen noodles in a shared spicy-ramen kitchen; no gluten-free preparation is mentioned (per Leaf Kyoto).

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The menu includes a soy-sauce ramen and a chili-based spicy tare; Japanese soy sauce and chili tares are typically wheat-containing, though the page does not list allergens (per Osumituki).

vegan_cross_contact.text: Signature bowls are topped with stir-fried minced meat, seared chashu pork, back fat and a beaten egg over a meat broth; no plant-based option is offered (per Leaf Kyoto).

caveats: Spice level, garlic and back fat are customizable; cash only; closed Tuesdays plus the 2nd/4th Wednesday so confirm before visiting. No published allergen information — a GF or vegan diner realistically cannot eat here.

cuisine: 鶏ガラ醤油ラーメン（名物ネギラーメン／ファイヤーラーメン）
neighborhood: 中京区 二条城前駅（千本旧二条、二条城の北西）
address_ja: 京都府京都市上京区南伊勢屋町757-2 佐々木ビル
geocode_note: Geocoded from address 京都府京都市上京区南伊勢屋町757-2 via GSI; result lat 35.017376, lng 135.747574 is within the Kyoto box and matches the location just northwest of Nijo Castle (Senbon-Kyu-Nijo). Note: the task brief listed the area as 中京区, but Tabelog and the official site both give the address as 上京区南伊勢屋町; the shop sits near the Kamigyo/Nakagyo boundary just NW of Nijo Castle, consistent with the 二条城前駅 reference. Menu caveat: GF and vegan bowls are the reservation-only special set menu via TableCheck and are NOT on the regular walk-in menu, so dietary guests must book ahead; not a dedicated GF kitchen, so highly sensitive celiacs should confirm handling on arrival.
seats_text: About 20 counter seats; counter-only, renovated in 2019 with fire-resistant stainless-steel tables for the flame performance.
hours_raw: 11:00-21:00 daily (Tabelog/official). Lunch service approx 11:00-15:30 and dinner approx 17:00-21:00 per the official reservation page; the kitchen notes the meal takes considerably longer than a typical ramen shop due to the fire performance.
closed_days: None listed; open daily (irregular/seasonal closures possible).
cultural_comfort_note: This is a famous tourist-facing spot with an English-language website, English-speaking staff, online reservations (TableCheck) and printed safety/allergy guidance, so foreign visitors are very comfortable here.
gf_detail: The shop's official site states it serves gluten-sensitive guests using premium rice-flour noodles and dedicated pots and utensils "to minimize cross-contamination." This is a genuine, intentional gluten-free offering — unusual for a ramen shop, where noodles, shoyu tare and broth are normally wheat-based. However, it is not a dedicated gluten-free kitchen, and the GF/vegan special bowls must be booked in advance via the TableCheck reservation (the reservation-only "special set menu"); the regular walk-in menu is standard wheat ramen. Highly sensitive celiacs should reserve the GF set, state their needs clearly, and confirm cross-contamination handling on arrival.
vegan_detail: A full vegan version of the fire ramen has been offered since 2020: a plant-based soy-sauce soup with no animal broth, custom firm noodles, and tofu/vegetable substitutions, plus vegan sides such as gyoza and karaage (mock meat). Vegan orders are tracked with a colored number card at the seat. The vegan bowls are part of the reservation-only special set menu, so book ahead via TableCheck rather than relying on walk-in.

bio.background: Founded in 1984 in Kyoto, this counter ramen shop began serving conventional bowls before its owner stumbled onto the idea that would make it world-famous. Wanting to build a ramen around Kyoto's prized Kujo green onions, he experimented with how to keep the noodles screaming-hot while showcasing fresh raw scallions on top.
bio.origin: Kyoto, just northwest of Nijo Castle near Nijojo-mae.
bio.specialty: Negi Ramen, better known as Fire Ramen: a light yet deeply savory chicken-and-seafood soy-sauce broth piled with fresh Kujo scallions, finished tableside when the cook pours scallion oil heated past 300 degrees Celsius over the bowl. The oil ignites into a column of flame that chars the onions, drawing out their sweetness and aroma while keeping the bowl piping hot.
bio.philosophy: Treat ramen as both craft and spectacle: preserve traditional Kyoto shoyu ramen flavor while turning each serving into a joyful, memorable performance, and welcome guests from everywhere — including those with vegan, vegetarian, gluten-free and no-pork needs.

anecdotes.text: The flames are not just theater: scallion oil heated above 300C reacts with moisture in the broth and toppings to throw up a pillar of fire that chars the Kujo onions and keeps the bowl scalding hot. After a 2019 renovation the counter was rebuilt with fire-resistant stainless-steel tables, and staff give a safety briefing and paper aprons before lighting each bowl.

About a year after opening the founder discovered that pouring flaming oil over freshly chopped green onions produced an extraordinary aroma and flavor, and that discovery became the shop's signature Fire Ramen.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The shop says it uses dedicated pots and utensils to minimize cross-contamination for gluten-free guests, but it is a shared kitchen that also cooks standard wheat ramen, so it is not a fully dedicated gluten-free environment.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The regular menu is a soy-sauce (shoyu) ramen; standard shoyu tare is typically wheat-based. The gluten-free version is a separate reservation-only preparation rather than the default bowl.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegan orders are marked with a colored number card placed at the seat so staff serve the correct plant-based bowl, but the shop does not publish details on separate cooking surfaces for vegan items.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Gluten-free, vegan, vegetarian and no-pork (Muslim-friendly) bowls are offered as a reservation-only special set menu booked in advance via TableCheck; the regular walk-in menu cannot be swapped for these, so dietary guests should reserve ahead and state needs clearly.

positives.text: Rare among ramen shops, this kitchen intentionally caters to gluten-free, vegan, vegetarian and Muslim-friendly diners, with premium rice-flour noodles for gluten-free guests and a fully plant-based vegan fire ramen offered since 2020.
English website, English-speaking staff and online reservations make it straightforward for foreign diners to arrange and confirm special dietary bowls before visiting.

cuisine: 汁あり・汁なし担々麺（四川系・胡麻濃厚で花椒の効いた担担麺、夜は酒とアテも出す）
neighborhood: 左京区・出町柳（吉田／京大近く）
gf_detail: A tantanmen specialist built on wheat ramen noodles (the soup-less version uses thick flat crimped wheat noodles), a Sichuan sesame-and-ground-pork sauce, and chili/Sichuan-pepper seasonings. No gluten-free noodle or tare option is mentioned anywhere, and tantanmen of this style is essentially never celiac-safe. Treat as not gluten-free.
vegan_detail: A meat-forward shop: the signature bowls center on a ground-pork/meat-miso (肉味噌) topping and a rich creamy sesame broth, and side dishes include beef tataki, gyoza, and fried chicken. No vegan or vegetarian tantanmen is advertised. Not suitable for vegans.
address_ja: 京都府京都市左京区吉田泉殿町47-47
geocode_note: GSI address search returned [135.776505, 35.026871] for the building-level address; within the Kyoto box.
seats_text: 16 seats (8 counter, 8 table, plus standing room)
hours_raw: 11:00-15:00 / 17:00-20:00 (lunch and dinner service daily; closing days irregular)
closed_days: Irregular (不定休)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, student-friendly local noodle counter where you order by circling options on a paper slip, so basic Japanese (or pointing) makes ordering much smoother; no English service is advertised.
caveats: Opened May 2025, so it is a young shop; closing days are irregular, so confirm before a special trip. Ordering is done via a fill-in paper form (noodle firmness, scallions, scallion oil, fried onion, meat miso, chili-oil level, Sichuan-pepper amount), which is part of the fun but assumes some Japanese.

bio.background: Tucked down a side street just west of Higashioji-dori between Imadegawa and Higashi-ichijo, a short walk from Demachiyanagi and Kyoto University, this little tantanmen counter occupies a space that used to be a neighborhood sake shop with a standing-drink corner. It was reborn in May 2025, reworked under the guidance of Kinshiro (金鵄楼), a Gosho-minami tantanmen specialist that drew lines soon after it opened in 2024.
bio.specialty: Sichuan-style tantanmen in two forms: a rich, creamy soup version and a soup-less (mazesoba-style) version built on thick, flat, crimped wheat noodles that grab the sauce. The flavor leans on a fragrant sesame base lifted by the tingle of Sichuan pepper (huajiao), with a meat-miso topping blended for a deeper, more layered umami than the parent shop.
bio.philosophy: True to its name 'Noodles and Sake' (麺と酒の寅), the place is built around the idea of 'have a drink, then close the night with a bowl of tantanmen' — a casual student-and-local hangout where you tune your own bowl by circling choices on a paper order slip (noodle firmness, scallions, scallion oil, fried onion, meat miso, chili-oil heat, and how much Sichuan pepper).

anecdotes.text: The shop is a Kinshiro-supervised spinoff; rather than copy the parent, it deliberately uses thick flat crimped noodles for the soup-less bowl and a re-blended meat miso to create its own deeper, more complex flavor.

The concept is 'a drink first, then finish with tantanmen' (一杯やって担担麺で〆る), aimed in part at the nearby Kyoto University student crowd, with beef tataki, gyoza, and karaage to go with the sake before the noodles.

cuisine: Chinese (Cantonese & Sichuan); signature gekikara tantanmen
gf_detail: No gluten-free provision is published. The signature dish is wheat-noodle tantanmen, and the kitchen is a full Chinese restaurant relying on wheat-based soy sauce, broths and house-made meat miso. The hallmark heat comes from chili blends, not from any allergen-controlled prep. A celiac diner should treat this as unsafe; no dedicated equipment or GF menu is indicated.
vegan_detail: This is a meat-and-seafood Chinese restaurant. The famous tantanmen is built on a house-made pork "meat miso" (自家製肉味噌) over a chicken-bone (鶏ガラ) broth, and other signatures include black-vinegar sweet-and-sour pork. No vegan ramen or vegan menu is advertised; a vegan diner would need to ask, with little to expect.
address_ja: 京都府向日市寺戸町東田中瀬5-54
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned coordinates for 京都府向日市寺戸町東田中瀬5-54; lat 34.953709 / lng 135.707855, within the Kyoto box.
neighborhood: 向日市（阪急東向日駅／JR向日町駅、京都激辛商店街）
seats_text: 83 seats total: 1F counter (6) and tables (three 4-tops); 2F tatami rooms seating up to 65.
hours_raw: Lunch 11:00-14:00, Dinner 16:30-22:00 (official site lists dinner to 23:00 / L.O. 22:30). Closed Tuesdays (next business day if Tuesday is a holiday).
closed_days: Tuesdays (open the following day if a Tuesday falls on a holiday)
cultural_comfort_note: A long-running, family-run neighborhood Chinese restaurant with a roomy tatami second floor and no English noted, so a little guidance on ordering and on the spice scale helps.
caveats: Official site lists dinner closing as 23:00 (L.O. 22:30) while the listing shows 22:00; treat evening hours as roughly 16:30 until late evening. The shop is fundamentally a Chinese restaurant rather than a ramen-noodle specialist, included here for its award-winning gekikara tantanmen.

bio.background: Kirin-en opened in 1967 (Showa 42), when the area around Mukocho was still mostly farmland and authentic Chinese food was a rarity. The founder set out, in his words, to let the people of Mukocho enjoy real Cantonese cooking, and the place has carried on as a family-run restaurant ever since, today led by Tamotsu Miyaji with manager Ryo Miyaji.
bio.specialty: The house gekikara (extreme-spice) tantanmen: a chili blend once built around jolokia, then famed as the world's hottest pepper, layered over a house-made pork meat-miso for body and a chicken-bone broth finished with sesame for depth. It won the Gold Grand Prix at the very first KARA-1 contest. Beyond the noodles, the owner's own favorite is the black-vinegar sweet-and-sour pork, made with traditional bottle-fermented black vinegar prized by regulars.
bio.philosophy: Their stated aim with the famous bowl was not heat for its own sake but 美味くて辛い - delicious AND spicy - with a spectrum running from mild to brutally hot so anyone can find their level.

anecdotes.text: The tantanmen was born out of Mukocho's community-revitalization push that became the Kyoto Gekikara (extreme-spice) shopping street; Kirin-en's bowl took the inaugural KARA-1 Gold Grand Prix.
Founder's recollection that he resolved to bring authentic Cantonese cuisine to Mukocho residents back when the neighborhood was largely farmland.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a full Chinese restaurant, dishes rely on standard (wheat-based) soy sauce and seasonings; no wheat-free tare or sauce is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The signature tantanmen is built on house-made pork meat-miso over a chicken-bone broth, so shared pots and broths make vegan separation impractical.

lead: 味の天徳 京都四条木屋町店
name_ja: 味の天徳 京都四条木屋町店
name_en: Aji no Tentoku Kyoto Shijo Kiyamachi
cuisine: 油そば・まぜそば（油そば専門店）
gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. Abura soba is built on wheat noodles tossed in a soy-based tare (Japanese soy sauce almost always contains wheat). The "plant-based oil" point refers to the dressing oil being vegetable oil rather than lard — it has nothing to do with gluten. There is no indication of gluten-free noodles, dedicated prep, or allergen handling. Gluten-avoiders should not eat here.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. Despite using plant-based (vegetable) oil rather than animal fat, the standard bowl is topped with chashu (pork) and the soy-based tare typically contains animal-derived components. No vegan/vegetarian version is advertised. The "healthier plant oil" framing is a marketing point about fat type, not a plant-based menu. A vegan diner could ask whether a no-chashu bowl is possible, but the tare itself is not confirmed plant-based, so it cannot be recommended as vegan.
address_ja: 京都府京都市下京区真町455 第一小橋会館
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned [135.769791, 35.00378] for 京都市下京区真町455; within the Kyoto box. Chō-level point for 真町, accurate to the block.
neighborhood: 下京区・京都河原町駅／四条木屋町（高瀬川沿い）
seats_text: Counter only (about 8–10 seats); a small, cramped solo-friendly spot.
closed_days: 不定休（irregular）
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, cash-only counter abura soba shop where ordering is simple but staff likely speak little English and there is no allergen support, so basic Japanese and self-direction help.

bio.background: 味の天徳は神奈川（柿生本店）発祥の油そば専門チェーンで、株式会社天輝が運営する。京都には四条木屋町店と祇園店を構え、四条木屋町店は高瀬川沿い、阪急京都河原町駅から徒歩すぐの繁華街の一角にある小さなカウンター店。
bio.origin: 運営は株式会社天輝（油そば味の天徳ブランド、関東発・京都進出）
bio.specialty: 看板は油そば。中太のちょい縮れ麺を醤油ベースのタレと植物油で和えたスープのない一杯で、メンマ・ねぎ・刻み海苔・チャーシューがのる。途中で酢とラー油を加えて味変し『二度美味しい』のが定番の食べ方。醤油だれと塩だれが選べる。
bio.philosophy: 油そばを『第三の麺類』と位置づけ、動物性の脂ではなく植物性の油を使うことでヘルシーさを打ち出している点が京都での売り。客が酢・ラー油で自分好みに仕上げる参加型の一杯。

anecdotes.text: 京都初の油そば専門店をうたい、植物性の油を使うためあっさり食べられると紹介されている。
途中から酢とラー油を足して二度美味しい、というのが店の定番の食べ方として案内されている。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core dish is wheat abura soba noodles dressed in a soy-based tare; no gluten-free option or allergen separation indicated anywhere.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The abura soba uses a 醤油ベース (soy-sauce-based) tare; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Standard bowl is topped with chashu (pork); no vegan/vegetarian preparation is advertised.

positives.text: Uses plant-based (vegetable) oil rather than animal fat in the dressing — a lighter-fat bowl, though this does not make it gluten-free or vegan.

caveats: Seat count varies by source (Tabelog lists 10 counter seats; other listings say 8) — recorded as ~8–10. Hours are 不定休 (irregular closures), so confirm before visiting. Phone numbers differ across listings (075-221-7768 on aggregators vs 075-841-3131 on the corporate site). Operated by 株式会社天輝, so not strictly independent, but it functions as a tiny local counter shop.

cuisine: Japanese kappo / curry tsukemen / dashi ramen
neighborhood: Shimogyo-ku, Tominokoji-Shijo (near Kyoto-Kawaramachi Station)
vegan_detail: No vegan offering. This is a Japanese kappo whose signature noodle dish is a curry tsukemen built on a bonito (katsuobushi) dashi and served with chicken and egg; the broth, tare, and toppings are animal-based, and the evening menu centers on sashimi and fish. Nothing on the menu is plant-based by design, and there is no indication staff can prepare a vegan version.
gf_detail: Not safe for celiacs. The noodles are wheat (the curry tsukemen broth is also thickened to a heavy wheat-flour consistency), and a kappo kitchen serving sashimi, sake, and grilled fish relies on standard wheat-based soy sauce and seasonings. There is no dedicated gluten-free menu or any stated allergen accommodation.
address_ja: 京都府京都市下京区四条富小路上ル立売東町17-3 富小路四条マンション103
geocode_note: Street-number geocode returned empty; used chō-level (立売東町) GSI result, which falls within the Kyoto box.
seats_text: 14 seats: 6 counter seats plus a table for 2, with a small private room (2-6); up to about 10 can be seated for a reservation.
hours_raw: 17:00-22:00. Closed Mondays. (Note: a daytime curry tsukemen has historically been offered, but the official site currently describes the shop as a dinner-only, reservation-based kappo with very limited seatings; lunch availability is uncertain.)
closed_days: Mondays
cultural_comfort_note: A small owner-run kappo with counter seating and reservation-based, limited daily seatings; comfortable for a guest who can manage basic Japanese, with no indication of English support.

bio.background: A small owner-run Japanese kappo on Tominokoji just north of Shijo, a few minutes from Kawaramachi. It opened in December 2019 in a space that had previously housed the curry-tsukemen specialist Menya Itadori (麺屋虎杖), which closed at the end of September 2019. After a temporary closure during the pandemic, the shop resumed and continues today.
bio.specialty: By night it is a kappo for sashimi, grilled fish, and sushi paired with sake and shochu; its signature crossover dish is a curry tsukemen whose dipping broth is built on a strong katsuobushi (bonito) dashi so the Japanese dashi flavor holds its own against the curry, finished with aonori, served with thick noodles, summer vegetables, chicken, and a soft egg.
bio.philosophy: The owner frames the place around wholehearted hospitality and lively, enjoyable time shared with family and friends.

anecdotes.text: The shop carried on the curry-tsukemen lineage of the address: the previous tenant was Menya Itadori, a curry tsukemen specialty shop that closed on September 30, 2019, and Terado opened the following December offering its own kappo take on the dish.

What makes the bowl distinctive is the deliberate balance of a heavily bonito-forward Japanese dashi against a sweetened, aonori-dusted curry, an intentionally kappo-minded reworking of a casual lunch noodle.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The signature noodles are wheat and the curry dipping broth is described as having a thick wheat-flour consistency.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: A kappo serving sashimi and grilled fish; no wheat-free soy sauce or seasoning is indicated, so standard wheat-based soy sauce should be assumed.

caveats: The official site currently presents the shop as a dinner-only, reservation-based kappo with very limited daily seatings, while Tabelog and a ramen blog document a daytime curry tsukemen; lunch availability and exact hours may have changed, so confirm before visiting. Hours marked irregular for this reason. Geocode is chō-level, not the exact building.

lead: 油そば・まぜそば ロマン 百万遍店
name_ja: 油そば・まぜそば ロマン 百万遍店
cuisine: 油そば・まぜそば (oil noodles & mixed noodles)
neighborhood: 左京区・出町柳／百万遍（京大近く）
address_ja: 京都府京都市左京区吉田泉殿町1-85 フレッシュクワバラ 1F
geocode_note: Geocoded from address_ja via GSI; coordinates [135.777969, 35.027313] fall within the Kyoto bounding box.
seats_text: 20 seats (12 counter, 8 table)
closed_days: Irregular closures; closed Dec 31 - Jan 4 (year-end/New Year)
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, fast-paced student noodle counter near Kyoto University where a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu get you through ordering comfortably.
gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The signature bowls use house-made whole-wheat (全粒粉入り) thick noodles and a special shoyu tare, both wheat-based, with no gluten-free noodle or tare option found. The kitchen is built around these noodles, so cross-contact is unavoidable. Not suitable for anyone with celiac disease or a wheat allergy.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. Abura soba and maze soba are built on a meat-based formula — chashu pork, spicy minced meat, soft-boiled or raw egg yolk, and a shoyu tare with oil/fish dashi notes. No vegan or vegetable-only bowl was found. A plant-based diner would have no safe main dish here.

bio.background: A Kyoto outpost of the Osaka-born abura soba and maze soba specialist Roman (株式会社ロマン), this shop opened on May 16, 2022 along Imagawa-dori in the Hyakumanben district, a short walk from Kyoto University. Oil noodles and mixed noodles were still relatively uncommon in Kyoto at the time, which made the new student-friendly counter a notable arrival in the neighborhood.
bio.origin: Branch of the Osaka-headquartered Roman brand (parent company 株式会社ロマン, founded 2020), which has grown to more than a dozen locations across Osaka and Kyoto.
bio.specialty: Abura soba (brothless oil noodles) and maze soba — thick house-made noodles tossed with a special shoyu tare and seasoned oil. The abura soba comes piled with chashu, menma, soft-boiled egg, and nori; the maze soba layers spicy minced meat, chives, green onion, bamboo shoots, and egg yolk over the same noodles. Diners add vinegar and the house chili oil and mix everything vigorously before eating.
bio.philosophy: The brand's stated belief is 'シンプルだからこその素材の味を大事にしている' — because the dish is simple, the flavor of the ingredients matters most. That focus shows in the house-made whole-wheat (全粒粉入り) thick noodles developed specifically to suit abura soba.

anecdotes.text: The shop courts its Kyoto University neighbors directly, offering students a 100-yen discount, and noodle portions scale all the way up to a 4x challenge size.
An opening promotion gave the first 50 customers each day a free large portion or topping through the launch week in May 2022.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen centers on house-made whole-wheat thick noodles boiled in shared water, so wheat cross-contact is unavoidable throughout.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Both signature bowls are dressed with a special shoyu (soy sauce) tare, which is wheat-based and a core component of the dish.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Bowls include chashu pork, spicy minced meat, and egg, with shared utensils and prep surfaces; no plant-based preparation is offered.

caveats: Hours marked irregular due to noted irregular closures; daily hours (lunch 11:00-15:30, dinner 17:00-22:00) confirmed but a specific weekly closed day was not published. Card payment not accepted, but cash plus QR (PayPay, Rakuten Pay) are — so not strictly cash-only.

cuisine: 中華そば（クリア豚骨醤油・朝ラーメン）
gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The bowls are wheat ramen noodles (flat medium-thick) in a pork-bone shoyu broth, and soy sauce (containing wheat) is central to both the tare and the recommended free-rice topping. No gluten-free menu, no separate preparation, and no gluten-free claims appear on any page. A celiac or gluten-avoidant diner cannot eat the ramen here.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The single broth style is pork-bone soy (豚骨醤油), and toppings/condiments revolve around chashu (pork belly), boiled egg, and meat-based components. No vegan or vegetarian option is offered or mentioned. Wakame, menma, and green onion exist as toppings but the broth itself is animal-based.
address_ja: 京都府京都市南区西九条池ノ内町56
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned the exact banchi (56番地) on first query; coordinates fall within the Kyoto box.
neighborhood: 南区 西九条／東寺（東寺駅 徒歩約4分・京都駅 徒歩10分弱）
seats_text: 21席（1階カウンター6席・2階テーブル席15席）
closed_days: 定休日の記載なし（要確認）
cultural_comfort_note: A small, casual ticket-machine (食券制) morning-ramen counter where a few words of Japanese and a friendly greeting go a long way; no English service is indicated.

bio.background: 中華そば 尋（ひろ）は、2026年3月28日、再開発が進む京都駅・八条口エリアにオープンした朝ラーメンの店。西院で人気の「らーめん 尋」の姉妹店として知られ、ビル左手の路地奥に入口を構える。
bio.origin: 京都・西院の本店「尋」の流れを汲む姉妹店。
bio.philosophy: 朝7時から開け、一杯ごとにごはんを無料で添える——朝ラーを気軽に楽しんでもらうための、気前のよいもてなしを大切にしている。
bio.specialty: 看板は一杯の中華そば。澄んだ黄金色のクリアな豚骨醤油スープで、豚骨の力強い旨味を感じさせつつ塩分や醤油感は控えめに仕立て、朝に食べても重くならない一杯に仕上げている。麺は高加水の平打ち中太麺で、ぷりっとした弾力と滑らかな喉ごしが特徴。チャーシューは注文ごとに切り出す豚バラで、薄切り・厚切りの食感を楽しめる。

anecdotes.text: おすすめの食べ方は、無料のごはんにチャーシューとおろしにんにくをのせ、醤油をひと回し。卓上にはおろしにんにく・一味・醤油・きゅうりの漬物が用意されている。
メニューは中華そば・チャーシュー麺・わかめ中華そばの3種。基本トッピングはわかめ・メンマ・ねぎ・ゆで卵。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles are the base of every bowl and are boiled in shared water; there is no gluten-free option or separate preparation noted.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is a pork-bone shoyu (豚骨醤油) tare, and soy sauce is also part of the recommended free-rice topping and the tabletop condiments — soy sauce in Japan typically contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The single broth is animal-based (pork bone), prepared alongside chashu (pork belly) and egg toppings; no vegan preparation exists.

caveats: No official website found; details drawn from Tabelog and a Kyoto food-media article. Closed days are not stated on any source and should be confirmed before visiting. Owner/chef name not published. The shop opened 2026-03-28 and is newly operating.

lead: 豚骨魚介ラーメン 穣
name_ja: 豚骨魚介ラーメン 穣
cuisine: 豚骨魚介ラーメン／つけ麺（自家製麺）
neighborhood: 左京区 一乗寺（茶山・京都芸術大学駅近く）
address_ja: 京都府京都市左京区一乗寺西水干町32-2 コーポバイロン 107
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 京都府京都市左京区一乗寺西水干町32-2 returned [135.786423, 35.038544]; within Kyoto box.
seats_text: L字カウンター約10席（オープンキッチン）
closed_days: 火曜（月曜は昼のみ営業）
cultural_comfort_note: A small, newly opened counter ramen shop where ordering in basic Japanese is easiest; no English support is indicated.
gf_detail: Not safe for celiacs. The signature bowls are built on self-made wheat noodles, and a pork-bone-and-fish (tonkotsu gyokai) ramen of this style almost always uses a wheat-based shoyu tare. No gluten-free option is advertised and no allergen handling is described.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The entire concept is a heavy pork-bone (uses Niwa Kogen pork) and fish/dried-sardine broth with chashu pork toppings; there is no plant-based bowl.
caveats: Very new shop (opened May 18, 2026); details verified against Tabelog plus two local food blogs. Phone number not publicly listed. Seat count (~10) and L-shaped counter come from blog reports rather than an official listing. Cash-only and no-reservation status per Tabelog.

bio.background: The owner is a graduate of the celebrated Kyoto ramen house Raamen Touhichi (らぁ麺とうひち), a Michelin Bib Gourmand name, where he is described as the first disciple and trained for around seven years. He went on to serve as manager at Raamen Suguru (らぁ麺すぐる), then deepened his craft outside ramen at the yakiniku restaurant Nanzan (南山) before opening Minoru on his own on May 18, 2026.
bio.specialty: The signature is a rich-yet-mellow tonkotsu-gyokai (pork-bone and seafood) ramen at 1,100 yen with 180g of house-made medium-thick straight noodles, alongside tsukemen at 1,300 yen with 250g of noodles. A special ramen tops the bowl with four kinds of chashu made from Niwa Kogen pork, plus seasoned egg and extra charred nori.
bio.philosophy: He values handmade quality, making his own noodles and using domestically sourced ingredients as much as possible. He is confident enough in the seasoning balance that no table condiments are offered, asking guests to taste the bowl as built.

anecdotes.text: A line formed in front of the shop even before opening, and it quickly became one of 2026's talked-about new ramen openings, drawing fans from its first days.
The owner is called the first disciple of Raamen Touhichi, where he trained roughly seven years before stepping out on his own.
The open kitchen gives a live-cooking feel, and the broth shows layered umami over a calm, Japanese-style counter.

gf_cross_contamination.text: House-made wheat noodles are central to every bowl, so a shared boiling pot and wheat-laden kitchen make cross-contamination effectively unavoidable for a celiac.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No tare/seasoning ingredients are published, but a tonkotsu-gyokai ramen of this style almost always relies on a wheat-based shoyu tare; assume the seasoning contains wheat unless confirmed otherwise.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is built on pork bone and fish/dried sardine and toppings are pork chashu, so there is no plant-based preparation and animal products are throughout the kitchen.

positives.text: Noodles are made in-house and the shop emphasizes handmade quality and domestic ingredients, so staff know their components well even though no formal allergy program is described.

lead: 中村商店 京都拉麺小路店
name_ja: 中村商店 京都拉麺小路店
cuisine: Ramen — chicken & seafood double-broth shio (signature 金の塩 / "gold salt"), plus chicken paitan and chuka soba
gf_detail: No gluten-free options. This is a classic ramen shop: wheat noodles in a chicken-and-seafood shio broth, with a salt tare that almost certainly contains wheat-based soy sauce/seasonings. No gluten-free noodles, dedicated preparation, or allergen accommodation is advertised on the shop's pages. Not suitable for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: No vegan options. Every bowl is built on animal-based broth — chicken bones, whole chicken, kombu, and dried sardines (niboshi) — and topped with chashu pork. There is no plant-based ramen on the menu.
address_ja: 京都府京都市下京区烏丸通塩小路下ル東塩小路町901 京都駅ビル10F 京都拉麺小路
geocode_note: GSI geocode of the chō-level address (東塩小路町, 烏丸通塩小路) returned [135.759583, 34.992943], inside the Kyoto box and consistent with Kyoto Station Building. Exact point is the 10F Kyoto Ramen Koji food zone at the station.
neighborhood: 下京区・京都駅（京都駅ビル10F 拉麺小路）
seats_text: 19 seats (15 counter, 4 table)
closed_days: Open year-round; closed only on January 1.
cultural_comfort_note: A busy station food-court ramen counter where ordering is typically done at a ticket machine and a few words of Japanese plus pointing will carry you through comfortably.
caveats: Located inside the Kyoto Station Building on the 10F Ramen Koji floor; expect lunchtime queues. No gluten-free or vegan options — unsuitable for celiac or vegan diners. Hours and the New Year closure follow the shop's listing and may shift with station-building schedules.

bio.background: Nakamura Shoten is one of the ramen brands of the Kinsei group, founded in Takatsuki, Osaka in 2001 as 彩色ラーメンきんせい ("colorful ramen Kinsei") — named for the wish to make ramen that shines vividly, like Venus in the night sky. The group has spent decades refining chicken-and-seafood broths, and its Nakamura Shoten line carries that work into Kyoto. This branch on the 10th-floor Ramen Koji of Kyoto Station Building reopened as a renewal of the group's earlier Kinsei stall there.
bio.origin: Takatsuki, Osaka (Kinsei group); Kyoto Station branch
bio.specialty: 金の塩 ("gold salt") — a clear, golden shio bowl that balances chicken umami against a seafood dashi, finished with chopped and thin-sliced onion for texture and a leaf of shiso for aroma. Chicken paitan (鶏白湯) and a chuka soba round out the menu.
bio.philosophy: The group selects chicken bones, whole chickens, kombu and dried sardines by season and builds each bowl around the balance of that broth, with a stated aim of bringing smiles, comfort and a touch of nostalgia through a single bowl of ramen.

anecdotes.text: The parent brand was founded on November 8, 2001 in Takatsuki with the idea of ramen that shines as vividly as Venus in the night sky — the origin of the 'colorful ramen' name.
The signature 金の塩 is a golden shio broth balancing chicken and seafood dashi, dressed with chopped green onion, thin white onion and a shiso leaf for aroma.
At the group's Takatsuki home, the original 金の塩 is praised for a light, clear chicken-bone broth and a refined, 'お上品' presentation — counter-only and queued at lunch.

lead: ラーメン まきさだ
name_ja: ラーメン まきさだ
cuisine: ラーメン（京都風醤油ラーメン）
neighborhood: 中京区（烏丸御池）
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区高倉通二条西入松屋町53-2
geocode_note: GSI address search returned [135.761856, 35.01099] for the full street address; within the Kyoto box.
seats_text: カウンター10席のみ（割烹を思わせる落ち着いたカウンター）
closed_days: 水曜定休
cultural_comfort_note: A small 10-seat counter ramen shop run by a long-established Kyoto Chinese restaurant; staff likely operate in Japanese, so basic Japanese or a translation app will smooth the visit.
vegan_detail: The single ramen is an animal-based (dōbutsu-kei) shoyu broth topped with thin-sliced pork chāshū; the side shumai are pork. No vegan or vegetarian option is indicated.
gf_detail: Standard ramen profile: wheat straight thin noodles, a soy-sauce (typically wheat-based shoyu) tare, and an animal-based broth. No gluten-free accommodation is indicated; not suitable for celiac diners.
caveats: Hours differ slightly between sources (one blog lists 11:00-14:30/17:00-21:00); the Tabelog listing's 11:30-14:00/17:30-21:00 with last-order times is used as authoritative. Phone number not yet listed. No allergen, gluten, or vegan information published as of June 2026; dietary fields inferred from the dish description, not from a stated allergen policy.

bio.background: ラーメン まきさだは、烏丸御池からほど近い中京区高倉通二条西入に2026年4月27日にオープンした、京都の創作中華の名店「京中華 牧定」が手がけるラーメン専門店。割烹を思わせる落ち着いたカウンター10席のみの小さな店で、看板の一杯はもともと牧定でまかないとして振る舞われていたネギそばを、ラーメンとして一から再構築したもの。
bio.origin: 京都・中京区。母体である「京中華 牧定」の系譜を受け継ぐ新店。
bio.specialty: まきさだラーメン（1,100円）。たっぷりのネギと細切りチャーシューがのった京都風の醤油ラーメンで、動物系の出汁の旨みをしっかり感じつつ後味はすっきり。パツッと歯切れのいいストレート細麺と、じんわり広がるスープのコク、香り高いネギが身上。セット（ごはん・焼売）は+300円で、焼売はクワイ入りのジューシーな一品。夏には冷やしラーメン（1,200円）などの季節限定も登場する。
bio.philosophy: 店主は世代を超えて愛される地域の店を目指している――「幼い頃に親に連れられて来ていた子どもが、大人になって今度は自分の家族を連れて来る。そんなふうに世代を超えて愛され、地域に根付いた店にしたい」と語る。

anecdotes.text: 看板の『まきさだラーメン』は、母体の創作中華店『京中華 牧定』でまかないとして出していたネギそばを、ラーメンとして再構築した一杯。
ラーメンは『まきさだラーメン』の一種類のみという潔い構成で、ごはんと焼売のセットを付けられる。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: 看板のまきさだラーメンは醤油ラーメンで、醤油（しょうゆ）ベースのタレを用いる。通常のしょうゆは小麦を含むため、グルテン回避者には不向き。

vegan_cross_contact.text: スープは動物系の出汁で、トッピングは細切りチャーシュー（豚）、サイドの焼売も豚肉。植物性のみの選択肢は確認できない。

lead: ラーメン 豚やどっ
name_ja: ラーメン 豚やどっ
cuisine: 京都ラーメン（豚醤油・チャーシュー）
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The bowls are built on house-made wheat noodles (中太の角ストレート麺 with egg white added for a wheat aroma), seasoned with Kyoto Goko soy sauce tare, which is wheat-based. No gluten-free noodle or tare option is offered and no allergy/cross-contamination handling is described anywhere. A celiac or gluten-sensitive diner cannot safely eat the ramen here.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. The entire concept is pork-centric: the broth is made solely from animal ingredients (豚ウデ肉・背脂 — pork arm meat and back fat), and the shop's identity is generous chashu (sliced pork). The soy-sauce tare and side dishes are pork-based as well. There is no vegetable broth or plant-based bowl.
address_ja: 京都府京都市北区大宮薬師山東町42-1 コーポラス西賀茂 1F
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned coordinates for 京都市北区大宮薬師山東町42-1; within Kyoto bounding box.
neighborhood: 北区西賀茂（大宮薬師山・北大路エリア）
seats_text: 16 seats total: counter 8 + 2 tables (8 seats). Bright, spacious white-wood interior; parking available.
closed_days: Mondays (closed Tuesday instead when Monday is a public holiday)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, casual neighborhood Kyoto ramen counter aimed at locals; no English is indicated, so a few words of Japanese or a pointing-at-the-menu approach will make ordering smoother.
caveats: Tabelog lists an evening start of "18:14"; this is a data artifact — local Kyoto sources confirm 18:00. Cash only and no reservations. The shop opened February 7, 2026, so it is young and details may shift.

bio.background: Opened February 7, 2026 in the Nishi-Kamogamo / Omiya-Yakushiyama corner of Kita-ku, taking over a space that had previously housed another ramen shop. It is a small independent counter built around one idea: a Kyoto bowl you could eat every single day.
bio.specialty: The house pork-shoyu ramen. The broth is a pork clear-soup (豚清湯) made from only pork arm meat and back fat — deliberately no pork bone — so it lands light yet rich ("あっさりコクうま"), then seasoned with Goko, a traditional Kyoto soy sauce, for that familiar nostalgic Kyoto sharpness. The signature move is chashu, piled without restraint: the standard Buta-yado Ramen (¥950) carries 150g, and the Buta-yado Chashu-men (¥1,200) goes to a 210g mountain of tender, thinly-sliced pork arm. A lighter Buta-yasumi Ramen (¥700) keeps just 30g for a quieter bowl.
bio.philosophy: "毎日食べられるラーメン" — ramen you can eat every day. The seasoning is kept gentle on purpose, the chashu is cooked soft so it appeals across all ages, and the noodles are made in-house: medium-thick angular straight noodles at 25% hydration, rested a day, with egg white worked in to push the wheat aroma that Kyoto diners favor.

anecdotes.text: The owner names Daiichi Asahi and Ramen Fuji — pillars of old-school Kyoto ramen — as the inspiration for the style, positioning Buta-yado as a torch-carrier for the traditional Kyoto bowl rather than a trend chaser.

Regulars describe the chashu portions as "圧倒的に量が多い" (overwhelmingly large) and the meat itself as "とにかく柔らかい" (extraordinarily tender) — the bowl is a destination for people who simply want a lot of pork.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Bowls are built on house-made wheat noodles and a wheat-based Kyoto soy-sauce (Goko) tare; everything passes through the same shared noodle water and kitchen, so gluten is unavoidable and cross-contact is pervasive.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The tare is built on Goko, a traditional Kyoto soy sauce — a standard fermented soy sauce that contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The broth is made exclusively from pork (arm meat and back fat) and the shop's whole identity is heaped pork chashu; there is no plant-based option and animal product is in every bowl.

cuisine: 油そば（abura-soba）専門・ラーメン
neighborhood: 左京区下鴨（下鴨エリア）
address_ja: 京都府京都市左京区下鴨西半木町88
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned [135.766296, 35.043674] for 京都府京都市左京区下鴨西半木町88; within Kyoto box.
seats_text: テーブル席7卓・計14席（カウンター席もあり）
hours_raw: 11:00～14:30、17:00～翌1:30（不定休、休みはInstagramで告知）
closed_days: 不定休（休業日はInstagramで告知）
cultural_comfort_note: A small, recently opened neighborhood noodle counter where ordering in basic Japanese (and pointing at the menu) will be smoothest; no indication of English service.
gf_detail: This is an abura-soba and ramen shop: the noodles are wheat (made by long-established Kyoto noodle maker Menya Teigaku) and the signature dish is dressed in a house sweet-spicy soy (shoyu) tare, which is wheat-based. There is no published gluten-free menu or any allergen accommodation. Not suitable for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: The menu centers on abura-soba topped with pork chashu and stewed pork (kakuni), plus chicken-broth and dashi (fish/seafood) noodle bowls and Okayama-style fried chicken. No vegan or vegetarian option is advertised.

bio.background: 麺屋 月 is a small abura-soba specialist that opened on February 22, 2026 in Kyoto's Shimogamo neighborhood, on Kitaoji-dori near Kyoto Prefectural University in what is a competitive local ramen district. It is an intimate counter-and-table spot with just over a dozen seats.
