bio.specialty: The signature bowl is abura-soba (brothless mixed noodles): extra-thick noodles tossed in a house-made sweet-and-spicy shoyu tare and fragrant aroma oil, finished with chashu or stewed pork belly (kakuni), menma, soft-boiled egg, shredded nori and negi. Portions step up generously by weight, from 200g up to 500g for the same modest price band. Chicken-broth noodles and a dashi-forward bowl round out the menu.
bio.philosophy: The shop leans on quality where it counts: the thick noodles come from Menya Teigaku, a venerable Kyoto noodle maker founded in 1931, and the abura-soba is built to be enjoyed first as-is, then re-seasoned partway through with chili oil and vinegar to change the flavor.

anecdotes.text: The extra-thick noodles are supplied by Menya Teigaku (麺屋棣鄂), a Kyoto noodle maker established in 1931, and are praised for a firm, chewy bite that holds up to the heavy tare.
Abura-soba is offered in four sizes at the same low price ladder (200g ¥850, 300g ¥900, 400g ¥950, 500g ¥1000), making it easy to size up for a bigger appetite.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature abura-soba is dressed in a house-made sweet-spicy soy (shoyu) tare, which is wheat-based; combined with the wheat noodles this dish is not gluten-free.

caveats: Hours and irregular closures are announced on Instagram; the kitchen runs late (last seating well after midnight). No allergen, gluten-free, or vegan information is published, and no staff allergy-handling could be confirmed, so celiac and vegan diners should treat this as unsuitable rather than ask-to-adapt. Cash only.

lead: 京ラーメン さだかず
name_ja: 京ラーメン さだかず
cuisine: 京都背脂醤油ラーメン (Kyoto-style back-fat soy-sauce ramen)
vegan_detail: No vegan options. The signature bowl is built on a chicken-bone soy-sauce (shoyu) broth finished with pork back-fat (seabura) and chashu pork, so it is fundamentally animal-based. No vegetable or vegan ramen is offered, and no sources indicate any plant-based accommodation. A vegan diner has nothing suitable here.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free and not celiac-safe. The bowl uses thin, low-hydration straight wheat noodles in a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare that almost certainly contains wheat, plus a chicken/pork broth. There is no gluten-free menu, no dedicated equipment, and no allergy information published. This is a tiny single-owner late-night counter shop and should be treated as unsafe for anyone avoiding gluten.
address_ja: 京都府京都市東山区祇園町北側281-1-1 祇園ファーストビル B1F
geocode_note: GSI geocoded to 京都府京都市東山区祇園町北側281番地 (Gion-cho Kitagawa), within the Kyoto box. Building is the basement of Gion First Building, ~5 min walk from Gion-Shijo Station.
neighborhood: 祇園 (Gion), 東山区
seats_text: Counter seats only (sources vary between 5 and 6); a single proprietor runs the whole shop, so capacity is very small and waits are common.
hours_raw: 22:00～翌1:00 (Mon–Sat); occasional irregular daytime service. 定休日: 日曜・祝日。深夜3時間のみの営業で、35食ほどで完売になることが多い。
closed_days: Sundays and national holidays
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, late-night, Japanese-only counter run solo by the owner — expect to order simply in Japanese, eat quickly, and not linger; little to no English support.
caveats: Open only roughly three hours late at night (about 22:00–01:00) and frequently sells out after around 35 bowls, so arriving late can mean it is closed for the day. No reservations; queues are common because one person runs everything. Not suitable for gluten-free or vegan diners.

bio.background: Sadakazu is a late-night Gion institution that earned the nickname "幻のラーメン" (phantom ramen) because it opens only for about three hours after 10 p.m. and often sells out after roughly 35 bowls. The shop relocated from the Bohana area of Fushimi Ward to the basement of the Gion First Building, a move into prime Gion territory that — along with television exposure — turned it into a sought-after late-night and post-drinking destination.
bio.specialty: Its signature is a Kyoto-style back-fat soy-sauce ramen (背脂醤油ラーメン, "seabura chaccha"): a clean, classic chicken-bone shoyu broth blanketed with generous pork back-fat, paired with thin, low-hydration straight noodles and chashu. A 'special' version upgrades the chashu, and a chilled ramen appears in summer.
bio.philosophy: The owner describes the broth as a mellow soy flavoring with deep umami — rich from the back-fat yet still drinkable and light, the kind of bowl meant to cap off a night out in Gion.
bio.origin: Kyoto (Gion, Higashiyama-ku); originally from Fushimi-ku, Kyoto.

anecdotes.text: Open only about 22:00–01:00 and run entirely by one person, the shop typically sells out after roughly 35 bowls, which is why it is called a 'phantom' ramen that is hard to actually catch open.
It moved from the Bohana district of Fushimi Ward into the basement of the Gion First Building, and that move plus TV appearances drove its breakout popularity as a late-night/after-drinking ramen.
The bowl became well-known enough that its back-fat soy-sauce ramen was recreated as a supervised cup-noodle product.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles are boiled in shared water; the dish is built on a wheat-containing soy-sauce tare. No gluten-free handling is mentioned anywhere.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is a soy-sauce (shoyu) base, which in standard Japanese ramen contains wheat-fermented soy sauce; no wheat-free tare is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is chicken-bone based and finished with pork back-fat and chashu pork; there is no separate vegan preparation.

cuisine: 京都醤油ラーメン（深夜営業）
neighborhood: 東山区 祇園（花見小路・祇園四条駅）
address_ja: 京都府京都市東山区八坂新地富永町116
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates [135.779587, 34.998505] for the full street address; within the Kyoto box.
seats_text: Counter seating around a central cooking area plus table seating along the wall; exact seat count not published.
closed_days: 日曜日
cultural_comfort_note: A casual late-night neighborhood ramen counter in the heart of Gion; ordering is simple and welcoming, but expect a primarily Japanese-language, locals-and-night-crowd setting.
gf_detail: This is a wheat-noodle shoyu ramen shop: the noodles are wheat, the tare is soy-sauce based (Japanese shoyu typically contains wheat), and the broth uses pork and back fat. No gluten-free menu, noodle, or preparation is indicated. Not suitable for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: Both signature bowls are animal-based: the 特選醤油ラーメン uses a pork/back-fat soy broth with pork loin slices, and the 鶏どろラーメン is a rich chicken-based broth. No vegan or vegetable-only option is indicated.
caveats: Posted hours differ across sources: Tabelog lists Mon-Sat 17:00-05:00 (Sun closed), while a Kyoto ramen guide describes a midnight-to-early-morning window with no closing day. The Tabelog hours are used here as authoritative; confirm directly before a late visit. Seat count is not published.

bio.background: A late-night ramen shop sitting right in the heart of Gion on Hanami-koji Street, just a short walk from Gion-Shijo Station. It is built for the after-hours crowd, opening into the evening and running deep into the night for diners winding down after an evening out in one of Kyoto's most storied entertainment districts.
bio.specialty: The signature bowl is the 特選醤油ラーメン (special soy-sauce ramen): a light, easy-drinking Kyoto-style shoyu broth with just a touch of back fat and a gently restrained soy depth, served with medium-thick noodles, thin slices of pork loin, flavorful menma, and white and green onions. Generous table condiments — spicy miso-garlic and grated garlic — let you build the bowl to taste. A richer 鶏どろラーメン, a thick chicken-based ramen, is the other house bowl.
bio.philosophy: An accessible, comforting bowl tuned for late-night Gion: a clean, drinkable broth rather than a heavy one, finished to each diner's liking with the condiments on the counter.

anecdotes.text: The shop is laid out with a central cooking area ringed by counter seats and table seating along the opposite wall, giving it a more spacious feel than a typical late-night ramen stand right in the middle of Gion.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The house bowl is a soy-sauce (shoyu) ramen; Japanese soy sauce typically contains wheat, and the noodles are wheat-based.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths are pork/back-fat (shoyu bowl) and chicken (鶏どろ bowl); shared pots and counter make a vegan meal impractical.

lead: 鶏そば あやむや 烏丸店
name_ja: 鶏そば あやむや 烏丸店
cuisine: 鶏そば・鶏白湯ラーメン（ハラル／豚不使用）
neighborhood: 中京区・四条烏丸
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区新町通四条上ル東入観音堂町470 道家ビル 1F
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned [135.751389, 35.009998] for the chō-level address; within the Kyoto box.
seats_text: 33 seats (counter and sofa seating).
hours_raw: 月・火・木～日・祝日 11:30～14:00（L.O.13:30）。水曜日定休。第1・第3火曜日および不定休あり。
closed_days: Wednesdays; also closed 1st and 3rd Tuesdays plus occasional irregular closures.
cultural_comfort_note: A halal-certified shop accustomed to international and Muslim travelers, so a friendly greeting and pointing at the menu will carry you, though full English is not guaranteed.
gf_detail: This is a standard ramen and tsukemen shop built on wheat noodles, with shoyu, shio and miso bowls whose tare typically contains wheat-based soy sauce. No gluten-free noodle or gluten-free preparation is advertised, so it is not suitable for celiac diners. Halal does not mean gluten-free.
vegan_detail: The house style is tori-paitan (rich chicken broth) and the signature bowls are chicken-based, so the standard menu is not vegan or vegetarian. The kitchen is halal (no pork, no alcohol) and avoids chemical seasonings, which makes it ingredient-conscious, but a plant-based diner would need to ask directly whether anything meat-free can be prepared and should not assume a vegan option exists.
caveats: Lunch-only operation (11:30-14:00, last order 13:30). Cash only. Closed days are irregular (Wednesdays plus 1st/3rd Tuesdays and occasional unscheduled closures) so confirm before a special trip.

bio.background: Ayam-YA grew out of a ramen company whose very first shop opened not in Japan but in Seattle. When the team brought the concept home to Japan's hyper-competitive ramen scene, they struggled to draw crowds, until an Indonesian exchange student asked a simple question that changed everything: 'Do you use only chicken?' That was the moment the founders learned that many visitors could not eat ordinary ramen for religious reasons. Determined to let Muslim travelers in Kyoto enjoy a real bowl of Japanese ramen with peace of mind, they reformulated the recipe and pursued halal certification through the Malaysia Halal Corporation. 'Ayam' means 'chicken' in Indonesian and Malay.
bio.specialty: Tori-paitan (rich chicken) ramen served as the basic shoyu (soy) chicken soba and a richer shio (salt) chicken bowl, with miso also offered; the broths lean on chicken with seafood aroma for depth, finished with minced onion and scallion, and made without chemical seasonings.
bio.philosophy: Make a genuinely delicious Japanese ramen that observant Muslims and curious travelers alike can eat without worry, by keeping the kitchen free of pork and alcohol and building flavor honestly from chicken rather than additives.

anecdotes.text: A turning point came when an Indonesian exchange student asked whether the broth used only chicken, which first made the team aware that some customers could not eat ramen for religious reasons and led them to pursue halal certification.

Owner Saifullah was originally a teacher who came to Japan to study and wished to stay; a devout Muslim, he keeps a prayer space at the shop, and the name 'Ayam' is the Indonesian/Malay word for chicken.
The chicken broth carries depth from a seafood aroma, with finely minced onion and scallion adding a herbaceous lift, all achieved without chemical seasonings.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core menu is wheat ramen and tsukemen noodles with shoyu/shio/miso tare; no gluten-free preparation is offered, so cross-contamination with wheat is inherent to the kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature basic bowl is a soy-sauce (shoyu) chicken soba; Japanese soy sauce tare is normally wheat-based, so the soy bowls should be assumed to contain wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The house broth is tori-paitan (chicken) and the menu is chicken-centered, so a meat-free or vegan bowl is not advertised and would share equipment with chicken broth.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The shop is halal-certified through the Malaysia Halal Corporation and keeps the kitchen free of pork and alcohol, indicating a kitchen used to strict ingredient control, though this addresses religious requirements rather than allergens.

positives.text: Cooks without chemical seasonings and builds umami from chicken and seafood aromatics, with no pork and no alcohol in the kitchen.

cuisine: Machi-chuka (Japanese-style neighborhood Chinese): mapo-tofu Tianjin rice, dan-dan noodles, gyoza, fried chicken with Sichuan pepper, ankake yakisoba
gf_detail: No gluten-free provision. This is a Japanese-style Chinese diner built on wheat: noodles, gyoza wrappers, and sauces thickened/seasoned with wheat-based soy sauce and likely doubanjiang. The signature mapo Tianjin rice rides on a mapo sauce that almost certainly contains wheat soy seasoning. There is no indication of gluten-free menus, dedicated equipment, or allergen handling. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: No vegan offering. The kitchen is meat- and seafood-forward: pork features in the mapo sauce, gyoza, fried chicken, and broths; dishes use animal-based stocks. Even otherwise plant-leaning items (fried rice, ankake) are cooked with meat/egg and shared woks. No vegetarian or vegan menu is advertised.
address_ja: 京都府京都市右京区西京極畔勝町44-2
geocode_note: GSI address search returned the chō-banchi point for 京都府京都市右京区西京極畔勝町44; within the Kyoto box.
neighborhood: 右京区・西京極畔勝町 (Nishikyogoku, Ukyo Ward); moments from the east exit of Hankyu Nishikyogoku Station
seats_text: About 38 seats, table seating only; an open, bright room that fills up in the evenings. Can accommodate private parties of roughly 20-50 people.
hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Thu-Sun & holidays: 11:00-14:00 and 17:00-21:00 (lunch open also listed as 11:30 on some sources). Closed Wednesdays. Open during holidays, Golden Week and Obon. Some sources note occasional irregular closures.
closed_days: Wednesdays (open on public holidays, Golden Week and Obon); occasional irregular closures
cultural_comfort_note: A friendly, at-home neighborhood Chinese diner where a little Japanese (or pointing at the menu) goes a long way; English support is not advertised, so come with basic phrases or this guide.

bio.background: A neighborhood machi-chuka (Japanese-style Chinese) restaurant that has fed the Nishikyogoku district for roughly 40 years, just east of Hankyu Nishikyogoku Station. It draws a cross-section of locals, from solo diners on their lunch break to families, in a bright, big-windowed room with a warm, unpretentious feel.
bio.specialty: The signature bowl is the maabo-tenshinhan (mapo Tianjin rice): fluffy egg over rice blanketed in a generous, glossy mapo sauce. The sauce is built on house-made doubanjiang and several chili bean pastes with ginger and garlic, given depth by hand-cut fermented black beans and finished with house chili oil, with finely minced pork for richness — kept addictively savory rather than punishingly hot, served around 1,050 yen with soup and pickles.
bio.philosophy: Honest, generous, affordable home-style Chinese cooking for the neighborhood — big portions, courteous service, and prices that keep regulars coming back day after day.

anecdotes.text: The maabo-tenshinhan reportedly began life as a staff meal before customers loved it enough that it became the house signature; on a typical visit roughly a third of diners are seen ordering it.
The mapo sauce poured over the Tianjin rice contains minced pork and aromatics but no tofu, giving it a concentrated, umami-rich punch against the mild egg-and-rice base.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat-based machi-chuka kitchen: noodles, gyoza, fried items and sauces share woks, fryers and prep surfaces, so cross-contamination with wheat is effectively unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Chinese-diner cooking here relies on standard soy-based and bean-paste seasonings, which in Japan are wheat-containing; the signature mapo sauce is built on house doubanjiang and chili bean pastes.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Pork, egg and animal stocks run throughout the menu (mapo sauce, gyoza, fried chicken, fried rice), cooked in shared woks, so no dish can be considered free of animal cross-contact.

caveats: Lunch opening time varies slightly across sources (11:00 on Tabelog, 11:30 on some listings); dinner close at 21:00 (one blog lists 17:30 start). No official website found; details drawn from Tabelog and local food media. No owner/chef name confirmed.

cuisine: 四川系中華・担担麺
neighborhood: 中京区 御所南（烏丸御池）
gf_detail: A Sichuan Chinese kitchen built around wheat. The tantanmen and dumpling skins are wheat-flour, the chili/sesame tare and seasonings used in dishes like the mapo tofu lean on soy-based and bean-paste condiments that typically contain wheat, and there is no gluten-free menu or protocol. Not suitable for celiacs. A coeliac diner could ask whether any single rice- or vegetable-based plate could be prepared plainly, but cross-contact in a one-cook wok kitchen is unavoidable — treat as not GF-safe.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan specialist. The signature bowls (tantanmen, mapo tofu, boiled dumplings, sweet-and-sour pork) all use pork and/or animal-derived broth and condiments. There is no marked vegan option. Because it is a small a-la-carte Sichuan kitchen run by one chef, a vegan might be able to ask in advance for a plain vegetable stir-fry, but nothing is confirmed plant-based, so reserve and ask directly before relying on it.
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区二条通堺町西入観音町76
geocode_note: GSI address-search on 京都市中京区観音町76 returned title 京都府京都市中京区観音町７６番地 with coordinates [135.762772, 35.013523], inside the Kyoto box. (Full address string fell back to a chō centroid; the chō-level 観音町76 result is used as it matches the listed lot number.)
seats_text: 14 seats — 4 counter seats, one 2-person table, two 4-person tables
hours_raw: Dinner 18:00-21:00 (L.O. ~20:30). Lunch not currently served (as of recent listing). Closed Sunday; Monday occasionally closed (~twice monthly); irregular temporary closures possible.
closed_days: 日曜定休、月曜不定休（月2回程度）、その他不定休あり
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny owner-run Sichuan counter where one chef cooks everything, so Japanese (or a Japanese-speaking companion) and a prior reservation make for the smoothest visit; come ready to be patient and order in Japanese.
caveats: Listed target name was "中国菜 菜格"; the confirmed operating name is "中国料理 菜格" (same address, phone 075-744-0435, and Tabelog page — same shop). Owner's personal name not found in fetched sources. Lunch service (which historically carried the tantanmen) appears suspended in the most recent listing; confirm lunch/noodle availability when reserving. Bib Gourmand confirmed for Michelin Guide Kyoto 2023; current-year status not re-verified.

bio.background: A small, refined Sichuan-leaning Chinese kitchen tucked into a quiet stretch of Nijo-dori just south of the Imperial Palace, opened in 2013 and run single-handedly by its chef-owner. He came up through the produce markets before cooking, and that background shows in his sourcing — a cook with a reliable eye for ingredients who keeps the menu deliberately compact.
bio.specialty: Sichuan-style cooking pitched a notch upscale rather than fiery for its own sake: a fragrant tantanmen (the lunchtime dry 汁なし version is especially praised), a mapo tofu where the Sichuan pepper and chili oil are rounded out by the sweetness of the ground pork, plus boiled dumplings and sweet-and-sour pork.
bio.philosophy: Keep the cooking simple and let quality ingredients carry it — the kind of place where, as one chef put it, everything you order somehow tastes good. Heat is balanced and aromatic rather than punishing.

anecdotes.text: Run entirely by one person, so the chef notes service may run slow — part of the charm of a true counter operation.
A fellow Kyoto chef of the same age describes the owner as a former market worker with a trustworthy eye for produce, whose relatively simple cooking nonetheless makes everything taste delicious.
Recognized as a Bib Gourmand selection in the Michelin Guide Kyoto 2023 (Nakagyo-ku Chinese).

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat noodles, wheat dumpling skins, and wheat-containing Chinese seasonings (soy/bean pastes) are central to the menu and share a single one-cook wok station; assume heavy gluten cross-contact.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Sichuan dishes such as the mapo tofu and the chili/sesame tantanmen tare rely on soy- and fermented-bean-based seasonings that typically contain wheat; no wheat-free soy sauce is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Signature dishes use pork and animal-based broth/condiments throughout; no dedicated plant-based prep is indicated, and a single chef cooks everything in shared woks.

positives.text: Tiny 14-seat counter with one attentive chef-owner who takes reservations, so allergy questions can be raised directly when booking — though no formal allergen handling is documented.

lead: ラーメン大戦争 京都河原町店
name_ja: ラーメン大戦争 京都河原町店
cuisine: 淡麗系・魚介豚骨醤油ラーメン（関西だし醤油「ピストル」）
gf_detail: A wheat-noodle ramen specialist whose signature bowls are built on soy-sauce (shoyu) tare, which in Japan is almost always brewed with wheat. Both the noodles and the seasoning base contain gluten, and no gluten-free menu or noodle option is indicated. Not suitable for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: All four signature bowls are animal-based: the broths are made from chicken bones (Nagoya Cochin), scallop, and fish/dashi elements, and chashu (pork) is a centerpiece. No vegan or vegetarian ramen is offered.
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区山崎町238 京都あじびる 1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 京都市中京区山崎町238 returned [135.769608, 35.007328], within the Kyoto box; consistent with the Kawaramachi-Sanjo location.
seats_text: 28 seats total (8 counter, 20 table)
hours_raw: 11:00-00:00 (L.O. 23:30), open daily. (Some local listings note 11:00-22:30; the official store information on the listing shows until midnight.)
closed_days: なし（無休）
cultural_comfort_note: A busy, queue-friendly counter-and-table ramen shop in central Kawaramachi; a few words of Japanese and pointing at the photo menu will carry you through ordering.
neighborhood: 中京区 三条河原町（三条駅／京都河原町駅周辺、山崎町）
caveats: Cash only. Reservations not accepted, so expect a queue at peak times. Hours differ slightly between listings (closes between 22:30 and midnight depending on source); confirm on the day if arriving late.

bio.background: Ramen Daisenso ("Ramen World War") is the Kyoto outpost of the acclaimed Osaka ramen group behind Jinrui Mina Menrui, the much-queued shop that turned shoyu ramen into something theatrical. This branch opened in October 2022 on the first floor of a renovated Kawaramachi building (formerly a sushi restaurant) and marked the group's first move into Kyoto.
bio.origin: 大阪（人類みな麺類系列）
bio.specialty: The flagship bowl is the Kansai-dashi shoyu ramen named 'Pistol' (ピストル) — a clear, light-bodied shoyu broth built on Nagoya Cochin chicken bones and scallop, with deep umami and a faint natural sweetness. The lineup of four soy-based bowls also includes 'Heiwa' (平和), 'Sabita Katana' (錆びた刀, a brothless/soupless shoyu), and 'Jiyu' (自由).
bio.philosophy: A noodle-first house: the noodle-making room is visible from the street, signalling pride in house-made noodles, paired with generous service — diners can add up to five chashu slices and get a free half-portion noodle refill (kaedama).

anecdotes.text: The shop names its bowls after evocative wartime-and-peace imagery — 'Pistol,' 'Peace,' 'Rusty Sword,' 'Freedom' — fitting the playful 'Ramen World War' branding of the group.
Its arrival was billed as the Osaka queue-shop's first expansion into Kyoto, opening on 4 October 2022.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles are boiled in shared water and the kitchen is built around wheat-based shoyu tare; gluten cross-contact is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Signature bowls are Kansai-dashi shoyu (soy-sauce) ramen; Japanese shoyu tare is brewed from wheat, so the seasoning base contains gluten.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths are made from chicken bone and scallop with fish/dashi elements, and chashu pork is central — no plant-based option and pervasive animal-product use.

lead: 人類みな麺類 つけ麺専門店 京都店
name_ja: 人類みな麺類 つけ麺専門店 京都店
name_en: Jinrui Mina Menrui Tsukemen Senmonten Kyoto-ten
cuisine: つけ麺（豚骨魚介。京都店限定の九条ネギつけ麺あり）
neighborhood: 中京区 下丸屋町（京都市役所前駅 徒歩1分／京阪三条駅 徒歩6分）
gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The house dipping noodles are 全粒粉入りの極太麺 — extra-thick wheat (whole-grain) noodles — and the rich pork-bone-and-seafood dipping broth almost certainly carries wheat through the tare. There is no gluten-free noodle or dedicated preparation. Anyone with celiac disease or a wheat allergy should not eat here.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The entire menu is built on a 豚骨魚介 (pork-bone and fish/seafood) broth, with a pork rice bowl (豚丼) as the main side. No vegetable or plant-based broth option is offered.
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区下丸屋町407
geocode_note: GSI address search on 京都府京都市中京区下丸屋町407 returned [135.768784, 35.010254], within the Kyoto box and consistent with a Kyoto-shiyakusho-mae / Kawaramachi-dori location.
seats_text: Counter seating; table seating in the back reported as planned for a later opening. Exact seat count not published.
hours_raw: 11:00–16:30 / 17:00–20:00、水曜定休（local reports）。なお食べログ掲載値は「11:00–20:45・無休」となっており、開業初期からの変動と思われるため、来店前に最新の営業時間を要確認。
closed_days: 水曜日（Wednesdays） per local reports; Tabelog listing shows no fixed closed day. Confirm before visiting.
cultural_comfort_note: A busy, popular tsukemen counter where a few words of Japanese and following the eat-as-you-go rhythm (the noodles are very long and you cut them with scissors at the table) will make ordering smooth.
caveats: Hours and closed day differ between sources: local 2025 opening reports give split daytime/evening service closed Wednesdays, while the Tabelog listing shows continuous 11:00–20:45 with no closed day. Treat hours as irregular and confirm before going. Cash is no longer accepted (cards and QR payments only) as of early January 2026.

bio.background: This is the Kyoto branch of 人類みな麺類, a tsukemen specialty offshoot of one of Osaka's most famous ramen brands. The original 人類みな麺類 opened in Nishinakajima, Osaka in 2012 and built a reputation for weekend lines of fifty-plus people. The dedicated tsukemen shop launched as a new sub-brand in Osaka's Yodogawa ward in April 2025; this Kyoto-shiyakusho-mae location opened on September 1, 2025 as its second outpost, just steps from Kawaramachi-dori.
bio.origin: Osaka (Nishinakajima); brand expanded to Kyoto in 2025
bio.specialty: Thick whole-grain wheat dipping noodles served with a rich, mellow pork-bone-and-seafood (豚骨魚介) dipping broth lifted with scallion-oil richness. The signature lineup is three broths — 原点 (the 'origin'), macro, and micro — with noodle portions of 200g, 300g and 400g all at the same price. The Kyoto-only bowl is the 九条ネギつけ麺, finished with fragrant local Kujo green onion. A small pork rice bowl and a finishing-rice option round out the dip.
bio.philosophy: The brand name 人類みな麺類 — playfully glossed in English as 'Human beings, everybody noodles' — captures founder Matsumura's idea that noodles are something all of humanity shares. The kitchen states its aim plainly: to deliver the highest performance it can currently offer and to keep building the finest shop it can.

anecdotes.text: Matsumura has said the name was born when he was a high-schooler: after a part-time shift he went for ramen with friends, warmed his hands on the bowl, and the thought struck him — 'humanity is all noodles' (人類はみな麺類や) — which became the name of his first shop.
The dipping noodles are made extremely long — eaters use scissors at the table to cut them as they go — a hallmark carried over from the tsukemen brand.
The Kyoto-exclusive 九条ネギ tsukemen was announced as the local-only special when the shop opened on September 1, 2025.

lead: 博多長浜ラーメン みよし
name_ja: 博多長浜ラーメン みよし
cuisine: Hakata Nagahama-style tonkotsu (pork-bone) ramen — light, slightly sweet broth with ultra-thin straight noodles, kaedama refills, and free tabletop toppings
gf_detail: This is a Hakata-style tonkotsu ramen shop built around wheat noodles (ultra-thin Hakata straight noodles) in a pork-bone broth, with kaedama (noodle refills) as a core part of the experience. There are no gluten-free noodle or broth options, and no dedicated gluten-free preparation. Not suitable for celiac diners. No allergen handling information was found.
vegan_detail: The entire concept is a long-simmered pork-bone (tonkotsu) broth — the shop's identity is its pork broth, kept going continuously. There is essentially one ramen, animal-based, with toppings like chashu (pork). No vegan or even vegetarian bowl is offered.
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区石屋町115 辻田ビル1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 京都府京都市中京区石屋町115 returned [135.770905, 35.00856], within the Kyoto box; matches the Kiyamachi/Sanjo area near Sanjo Station.
neighborhood: 中京区 石屋町・三条木屋町（三条駅周辺）
seats_text: 16 counter seats only (no tables)
hours_raw: Tabelog: Mon/Tue/Thu/Sun 11:00–01:00 (next day); Fri/Sat 11:00–03:00 (next day); weekday mid-afternoon break around 15:00–17:00; closed Wednesday. Note: several blog/listing sources describe much later late-night hours (roughly 11:00 until early morning) and state no regular closing day, so actual closing time runs late and can vary.
closed_days: Wednesday (per Tabelog; some sources list no fixed closing day)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, retro 16-seat counter-only late-night ramen counter in Kyoto's Kiyamachi nightlife district run by a longtime local family; little to no English, food-ticket ordering, and best approached with basic Japanese and casual ramen-counter etiquette.
caveats: Cash only, no reservations, 16 counter seats only — expect a wait at peak/late-night hours. Hours and closing day are reported inconsistently across sources; the structured hours follow Tabelog (Wed closed, late-night close) but actual late-night closing time can vary, so confirm before a very late visit.

bio.background: 博多長浜ラーメン みよし opened in 1983 in Kyoto's Kiyamachi/Sanjo entertainment district and has become an old-school local institution — the kind of place locals say everyone in Kyoto knows by name. It has been described as a味 (taste) protected for roughly four decades by the family running the counter, with the mother-figure ("okaasan/okaachan") long at the helm of the small open counter.
bio.specialty: Essentially one bowl done one way: Hakata Nagahama-style tonkotsu ramen — a clean, gently sweet pork-bone broth paired with ultra-thin Hakata straight noodles. Diners customize at the table with around eleven free toppings (beni-shoga pickled ginger, coarse ichimi chili, tenkasu tempura scraps, sesame, etc.) and can order kaedama noodle refills, which is treated as part of doing Nagahama ramen 'your way.'
bio.philosophy: A purist, no-frills tonkotsu shop: keep the pork-bone broth going, add nothing unnecessary, and let each diner build their own flavor with the tabletop condiments. Regulars even treat certain moves (like adding pepper or curry powder) as 'forbidden,' and finishing by pouring extra broth over rice is part of the ritual.

anecdotes.text: Often cited as a place 'everyone in Kyoto knows' — an old-establishment late-night ramen counter that the family is said to have run and protected for around 41 years.

Famous celebrity regular: the gourmet entertainer GACKT, who reportedly rarely eats ramen, is said to have been a loyal customer since his university days and to finish with extra broth poured over rice.
Recommended order from fans: ramen with 'barikata' (extra-firm) noodles, customized with beni-shoga, coarse ichimi, and tenkasu — with pepper and curry powder considered the 'forbidden' moves.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core menu is wheat ramen noodles in a shared tonkotsu broth with kaedama (noodle refills), so a single shared noodle/broth workflow — high cross-contamination risk for gluten.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The shop is built on a continuously simmered pork-bone broth; there is no separate non-animal broth, so all bowls share an animal-based base.

lead: めんきや 京都壬生本店
name_ja: めんきや 京都壬生本店
cuisine: 京都ブラック系 背脂醤油豚骨ラーメン（細麺・町ラーメン）
gf_detail: A wheat-noodle ramen shop built on a pork-bone-and-back-fat soy-sauce broth. The thin noodles are wheat, the soy-sauce tare is virtually certainly wheat-based shoyu, and the broth is pork-derived — none of which is celiac-safe. There is no indication of any gluten-free noodle, tare, or menu item, no dedicated equipment, and shared pots/cooking surfaces make cross-contamination unavoidable. Not suitable for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: The entire concept is a pork-bone (tonkotsu) and back-fat broth with pork chashu and toppings such as a boiled egg; sides include karaage and gyoza. There is no vegan or even vegetarian ramen on offer and no plant-based broth alternative noted. Not suitable for vegans.
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区壬生相合町32
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 京都府京都市中京区壬生相合町32 returned [135.747559, 34.999454], within the Kyoto box. Matches the 四条大宮 location near 阪急大宮駅.
neighborhood: 四条大宮（阪急大宮駅・四条大宮交差点すぐ、四条大宮商店街内）
seats_text: About 12 counter seats — a small, snug late-night counter shop.
hours_raw: 11:00-02:00（翌2時／26:00）, L.O. 01:30（25:30）, 営業時間表記は11:00-26:00。定休日は出典により相違：年始休業のみ（四条大宮商店街公式）／第2・第4月曜＋1/1-1/5休（食べログ）。
closed_days: Sources differ: the shopping-street official listing says no regular closing day (New Year holidays only), while Tabelog lists the 2nd and 4th Monday closed plus Jan 1–5. Confirm before a special trip.
cultural_comfort_note: A bustling, locals-first neighborhood ramen counter where a friendly greeting and pointing at the menu will carry you through, though detailed dietary questions in English may be hard.
caveats: Hours/closed-days conflict between sources (no regular day off per the shopping-street site vs. 2nd/4th Monday off per Tabelog) — verify before visiting. Open very late (to ~2:00 AM) and can get a sudden last-train rush with a queue.

bio.origin: 四条大宮商店街 / 壬生, central Kyoto
bio.background: A late-night neighborhood ramen counter sitting right inside the Shijo-Omiya shopping arcade, a few steps from Hankyu Omiya Station. It runs from late morning straight through to around 2 AM, shifting from a lunchtime rush, to evening drinkers ordering sides with beer, to a sudden surge of customers chasing the last trains.
bio.specialty: Their core bowl is a classic Kyoto-style se-abura shoyu (back-fat soy-sauce) tonkotsu ramen: a pork-bone broth finished with back fat over thin, easy-slurping noodles, topped with chashu, with branch menus extending to shoyu-pork, curry ramen, chashu-men, and a miso ramen made with miso shipped from Hokkaido.
bio.philosophy: The whole shop is built around one idea — 毎日でも食べられるラーメン, 'ramen you could eat every day' — leaning on accessible, balanced flavor rather than heavy gimmickry, using selected ingredients like Kyoto Tanba highland pork and Kujo negi.

anecdotes.text: A boiled egg comes with the bowl and a cup of cold matcha is served as an after-meal gesture — small touches that fit the everyday-comfort idea.

It is documented as a 深夜も賑わう地元の繁盛店 — a locally bustling shop that stays busy late, with after-work diners ordering karaage and gyoza alongside an economical fried-rice set, then a last-train crowd queuing outside.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat noodles, a wheat-based soy-sauce tare, and shared cooking equipment make this unsafe for celiac diners; no gluten-free handling is indicated.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature bowl is a back-fat shoyu (soy-sauce) ramen; the tare is virtually certainly standard wheat-containing shoyu.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is pork-bone and back fat with pork chashu; no vegan or vegetarian option is offered, so all bowls share an animal-based base.

sources: https://msearch.gsi.go.jp/address-search/AddressSearch?q=京都府京都市中京区壬生相合町32

lead: 京都北山元町らーめん
name_ja: 京都北山元町らーめん
cuisine: 黒醤油・背脂醤油ラーメン
gf_detail: This is a traditional soy sauce ramen shop: wheat noodles in a pork-and-chicken-bone broth seasoned with a tamari-style soy sauce tare, which is wheat-based. There is no gluten-free menu, no gluten-free noodle option, and no published allergen handling. Not suitable for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: The broth is built on pork bones blended with chicken bones, and the signature bowls feature back fat, beef tendon and pork toppings. A "vegetable ramen" (野菜らーめん) is on the menu but it is served in the same animal-based broth, so it is not vegan or vegetarian. No plant-based broth is offered.
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区東洞院通錦小路下る坂東屋町664-27
geocode_note: GSI full-address lookup returned only a ward-level fallback point (東八幡町). Used the chō-level match for the actual neighborhood 坂東屋町/阪東屋町, which is within the Kyoto box and closer to the true location; block-level precision (-27) is approximate.
neighborhood: 四条烏丸
seats_text: 27 seats (11 counter, 16 at tables)
closed_days: Sundays
cultural_comfort_note: A casual local late-night ramen counter where ordering is simple but staff and signage are Japanese-only, so a little Japanese or pointing at the menu helps.

bio.background: The shop traces its roots to a Chinese-noodle (中華そば) food cart in Kyoto's Kitayama Kitamachi that ran for roughly 50 years before settling into a storefront in the Shijo Karasuma area. The 'Motomachi' name and its dark soy-sauce style carry that half-century of cart lineage forward.
bio.specialty: A rich, dark soy-sauce ramen made with a tamari-style soy sauce of the kind traditionally used for eel sauce, plus a Kyoto-style back-fat (背脂) soy sauce variant with fat drizzled over the top. The pork-bone broth is rounded out with chicken bones for a mellow, deep body. Beef-tendon ramen (牛すじらーめん) is a signature bowl.
bio.philosophy: Carry forward the single signature flavor born on the old cart while serving generous, satisfying bowls late into the night for the downtown crowd.

anecdotes.text: The current shop grew out of a Chinese-noodle food cart that operated in Kyoto's Kitayama Kitamachi for about 50 years, and it still builds its bowls on the same dark soy-sauce flavor.
Its calling card is a 'concentrated soy sauce ramen' using a tamari-style soy sauce, offered alongside a Kyoto-style back-fat soy sauce version.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is seasoned with a tamari-style soy sauce tare, which is a wheat-based fermented seasoning.

caveats: Seat count, hours and 50-year cart history are from the shop's official site and Tabelog. The GSI geocoder did not resolve the exact street address; coordinates are a chō-level approximation for 坂東屋町. No allergen, gluten-free, or vegan information is published, so dietary entries reflect the inherent ingredients (wheat noodles, wheat-based soy tare, pork/chicken broth) rather than any stated accommodation.

lead: ラーメン 天 本店
name_ja: ラーメン 天 本店
cuisine: Kyoto-style shoyu ramen — light pork-bone broth with back-fat and soy tare, served with firm straight noodles
vegan_detail: No vegan options. Broth is pork-bone based; ramen is topped with sliced pork. No plant-based ramen or sides are offered.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. Ramen uses wheat noodles and a wheat-based soy (shoyu) tare; the kitchen also fries chicken karaage. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is indicated, and cross-contamination is unavoidable for a celiac.
address_ja: 京都府京都市山科区西野左義長町6-1
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 京都府京都市山科区西野左義長町6番地; within Kyoto box.
seats_text: 37 seats, including counter seating
closed_days: Thursdays
cultural_comfort_note: A busy, no-frills local ramen counter where Japanese will smooth ordering; little to no English is expected, but pointing at the menu works for a simple bowl.

bio.background: A neighborhood ramen institution that has been running since the Showa era — locals describe it as a 30-plus-year veteran shop that relocated to its current spot a few years back and quickly filled its 37 seats again at both lunch and late night.
bio.specialty: A classic Kyoto bowl: a light yet satisfying pork-bone broth seasoned with soy and back-fat, paired with firm, snappy straight noodles topped with thin-sliced pork, bamboo shoots, bean sprouts and green onion. The bargain A-set adds a big bowl of rice and fried chicken.
bio.philosophy: Honest, generous everyday ramen for the neighborhood, served late into the night.

anecdotes.text: Regulars rave that the firm straight noodles are quintessentially Kyoto — 'this noodle that no Kyoto person could dislike' — and that the spicy miso served alongside pairs beautifully with the broth.
It is one of the rare Yamashina spots open until 2 a.m., making it a go-to for late-night eaters.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles are boiled in shared water and the shop also fries chicken karaage, so gluten exposure is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The bowl is a soy (shoyu) tare ramen; standard Japanese shoyu contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is pork-bone based and ramen is topped with pork, so no dish is free of animal products.

caveats: Cuisine framing reconciled from multiple sources: Tabelog calls it a pork-bone (豚骨) base while local blogs describe a lighter pork-bone broth with back-fat and soy tare and firm straight noodles — consistent with classic Kyoto shoyu ramen. Hours of 11:00-26:00 (to 2 a.m., L.O. 25:30) confirmed across Tabelog and blog/search sources; no official website found.

cuisine: 町中華・四川料理（鶏肉の葱油かけそば）
gf_detail: A neighborhood Sichuan/Chinese restaurant. The signature 鶏肉の葱油かけそば and other noodle dishes are built on wheat noodles, and Chinese cooking here leans heavily on soy sauce, oyster/bean-based sauces, and shared wok/fryer surfaces. There is no documented gluten-free menu, dedicated equipment, or allergen handling. Not suitable for celiac diners; assume cross-contamination throughout.
vegan_detail: A meat-and-seafood-forward Chinese kitchen — the house specialty centers on fried chicken and chicken oil, and stocks/sauces are animal-based. No vegan menu or documented plant-based broth. While a few vegetable stir-fries (e.g. spicy eggplant) exist on the menu, they are not prepared vegan (soy/oyster-style sauces, shared woks, likely meat-based stock), so no reliable vegan option can be confirmed.
address_ja: 京都府京都市右京区太秦開日町3-7
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 太秦開日町3番地; within the Kyoto box.
neighborhood: 右京区・太秦（嵐電帷子ノ辻／JR太秦駅周辺）
seats_text: 24席。宴席は4名様～24名様まで対応。
closed_days: 水曜日
cultural_comfort_note: A bustling, locals-and-visitors neighborhood Chinese spot where staff may speak Chinese as much as Japanese; a friendly greeting and pointing at the menu will carry you, but expect little English.

bio.background: 龍門 is a small Kyoto chain of authentic Sichuan / neighborhood-Chinese (町中華) restaurants run by 株式会社長城, with the Honten near Sanjo-Higashioji and branches in Okazaki and here in Uzumasa, in Ukyo-ku near the Arashiyama (Randen) line and JR Uzumasa. It has built a reputation as the kind of unpretentious place that draws not just Kyotoites but diners from across Japan and even visitors from China itself, seeking the real thing at gentle prices.
bio.specialty: The house signature is 鶏肉の葱油かけそば — crisp fried chicken over straight medium-thick noodles in a chicken-oil-rich bowl, finished with scallions and chili and a pour of sizzling hot oil. Alongside it, the spicy stir-fried eggplant (茄子のピリ辛炒め) is often called a dish that all but represents the restaurant.
bio.philosophy: Serve genuine, full-flavored Chinese cooking — fragrant with Sichuan pepper and star anise — at everyday prices in a lively, no-frills room.
roles: Independently operated local Chinese restaurant / 町中華
Part of the small 龍門 group in Kyoto

anecdotes.text: 龍門 is described as a 町中華 popular not only with Kyoto locals but with diners from all over Japan and even Chinese visitors from the mainland, who come for an authentic taste.

The 鶏肉の葱油かけそば is an original noodle dish made with authentic Chinese technique — crisp fried chicken and chicken oil in the broth over straight medium-thick noodles, topped with scallion and chili and finished with hot oil.
The spicy eggplant stir-fry is singled out as a dish that it would be no exaggeration to call representative of 龍門.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Signature and most dishes use wheat noodles and wheat-based Chinese sauces; shared woks and a shared fryer mean pervasive gluten cross-contact. No gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Chinese cooking here relies on soy sauce and bean/oyster-style sauces, which are wheat-containing; no gluten-free soy sauce is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is meat- and seafood-forward (the signature bowl centers on fried chicken and chicken oil); vegetable dishes are cooked in shared woks likely with animal-based stock/sauces, so no reliable vegan preparation can be confirmed.

caveats: Cash only (no cards, e-money, or QR). Official site lists hours 18:00–22:30 closed Wednesdays; one aggregator showed a lunch service (11:30–14:30) and later close — treat the official 18:00–22:30 as authoritative and confirm lunch by phone. This is a Chinese restaurant, not a dedicated ramen shop, hence categorized OMNI.

cuisine: あぶらそば abura-soba (二郎系 Jiro-style)
gf_detail: Not safe for gluten-free or celiac diners. The entire concept is abura-soba built on thick house-made wheat noodles (made from 100% "オーション" strong wheat flour), dressed in a soy/oil tare that is almost certainly wheat-based shoyu, with pork back fat. There is no published allergen information and no gluten-free option. Avoid if you require celiac-level safety.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. This is a Jiro-style abura-soba shop centered on pork back fat (背脂), wheat noodles, and a savory oil/soy tare; standard toppings include raw or soft-boiled egg, pork fat, mentaiko mayo, and cream cheese. No plant-based or vegetarian bowl is offered.
address_ja: 京都府京都市左京区田中門前町66
geocode_note: Geocoded from full address 京都府京都市左京区田中門前町66 via GSI; coordinates [135.779297, 35.029888] fall within the Kyoto box. Chō-level lookup (田中門前町) returned a near-identical point [135.780151, 35.029968], confirming the location north of the Hyakumanben intersection, west side, near Kyoto University.
neighborhood: 左京区・田中門前町（出町柳・百万遍、京大近く）
seats_text: Counter seating plus a few two-person tables and some semi-private booth-style seats; exact seat count not published. No private rooms.
hours_raw: 11:00-15:00, 17:30-22:30 (Fri close 22:00). Note: opening-period hours, subject to change — confirm via Instagram.
closed_days: No regular closing day published; hours are provisional opening-period hours. Sources differ on whether Monday evenings or Sundays may be closed — check the shop's Instagram before visiting.
cultural_comfort_note: A small, student-oriented Jiro-style noodle counter with a ticket-vending-machine ordering system and no published English support, so basic Japanese (or the menu photos) helps; the casual, generous portions make it easy and welcoming once you've chosen.

bio.background: 油珉 (Yumin) opened on October 10, 2025 in the Hyakumanben university quarter, just north of the Hyakumanben intersection and a short walk from Demachiyanagi and Kyoto University. It is the second brand of びし屋 (Bishiya), the popular, queue-forming ramen shop in Kyoto's Ichijoji — the staff here are the Bishiya team, who set out to make a dedicated abura-soba (brothless mixed noodle) specialist for the student neighborhood.
bio.origin: Kyoto (Sakyo-ku, Tanaka Monzencho), as a spin-off of the Ichijoji ramen shop びし屋
bio.specialty: Jiro-style abura-soba. The standout is the 背脂ヤサイあぶらそば (back-fat & vegetable abura-soba) — extra-thick house-made noodles milled from 100% strong 'オーション' wheat flour, piled with pork back fat, vegetables, menma and naruto, finished tableside with minced garlic and chopped onion. The lineup is three bowls: あぶらそば (750 yen), 辛味あぶらそば (spicy, 850 yen) and 背脂ヤサイあぶらそば (850 yen), with portions scalable from 180g up to a 540g triple.
bio.philosophy: Affordable, high-volume, punchy noodles aimed squarely at hungry students — リーズナブル prices and a 'wash-wash' (ワシワシ) chewy texture with the wallop of back fat.
roles: Sister/second brand of びし屋 (Bishiya), Ichijoji

anecdotes.text: Visitors realized the people running it were the crew from the famous Ichijoji ramen shop びし屋 — 「中の人たちは一乗寺のラーメン店『びし屋』の方々でした」.
The thick house noodles are described as 'オーション100％の極太麺' with a snappy 'ブリンブリン' and chewy 'ワシワシ' texture and strong wheat flavor — the heart of its 二郎系 character.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Built entirely on wheat: thick house-made noodles from 100% strong wheat flour ('オーション100％の極太麺'), in a shared boiling-water/noodle workflow typical of a noodle shop.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The dressing is a strong soy-based oil tare with prominent garlic ('醤油ベースのオイルだれ'), which in this style is virtually always wheat-containing shoyu.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Bowls center on pork back fat (背脂) and the standard toppings include raw/soft-boiled egg, pork fat, mentaiko mayo and cream cheese; no plant-based preparation is offered.

caveats: Opened October 10, 2025; hours are still provisional opening-period hours and the regular closing day is not firmly established (sources disagree between Monday-evening and Sunday closures) — confirm via the shop's Instagram (@yumin_abs) before visiting. Cash only at present (electronic payment said to be coming). No allergen information is published; gf_confidence=no and vegan_status=no reflect the wheat-noodle, pork-back-fat, wheat-shoyu nature of Jiro-style abura-soba and not a confirmed statement from the shop. Exact seat count not published.

lead: ラーメンムギュ Vol.2 烏丸蛸薬師店
name_ja: ラーメンムギュ Vol.2 烏丸蛸薬師店
cuisine: ラーメン（鶏出汁・醤油／薄切りバラチャーシュー）
gf_detail: This is a classic wheat-noodle ramen shop. The bowls use medium-thin straight wheat noodles in a soy (shoyu) tare broth, so both the noodles and the seasoning base contain wheat/gluten. No gluten-free noodles or menu are mentioned anywhere. Not suitable for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: The signature bowls are built on a chicken (Nagoya Cochin) and dried-bonito broth and are topped with thin-sliced pork belly chashu. The soup is animal-based and there is no vegan or vegetarian ramen on the menu.
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区一蓮社町298-13
geocode_note: GSI geocode of the street address returned [135.760345, 35.006332], within the Kyoto box and consistent with the Karasuma/Takoyakushi location near Karasuma Station.
neighborhood: 中京区・四条/烏丸（蛸薬師通）
seats_text: 17 seats — a long counter (10-plus stools) running to the right of the entrance plus one table.
hours_raw: Mon–Sat 11:00–22:30 (L.O. 22:00); closed Sunday.
closed_days: 日曜日（Sundays）
cultural_comfort_note: A small, casual neighborhood counter ramen shop tucked in an alley; a friendly greeting and pointing at the bowl you want will carry you through, though staff likely speak little English.

bio.background: Tucked into a narrow alley just off Takoyakushi-dori near Karasuma, Ramen Mugyu Vol.2 is the second outpost of the original Ramen Mugyu, which began in the Maruyama area. The shop occupies a converted townhouse-style space with a long counter and a relaxed, almost izakaya-like feel; guests are handed paper aprons on arrival to keep the splatter off.
bio.specialty: The signature bowl is the "Onibara Kuro" (Onibara Black) shoyu ramen: a soy-forward broth built on purebred Nagoya Cochin chicken and dried bonito, brightened with a re-steeped premium soy sauce from Ehime, and finished with medium-thin straight noodles and a fan of extremely thin-sliced pork belly chashu that blankets the bowl. A lighter "Onibara Shiro" (white) version and a rich chicken soba (nōkō torisoba) round out the lineup, alongside mazesoba and side dishes like fried chicken.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen leans into a clean, sharp Kyoto-style shoyu profile — proper soy richness with a subtle seafood undertone and a clear chicken backbone — that diners have compared to the old-school Kyoto tradition of Shinpuku Saikan.

anecdotes.text: Regulars and reviewers liken the soy-based, crisp and salty broth to the venerable Kyoto institution Shinpuku Saikan.
Diners are given a paper apron on entry to protect against splashes, and the counter stretches lengthwise to the right with a notably wide, open kitchen.
The bowl is topped with very thin slices of pork belly chashu that, despite covering the whole surface, stay light on the palate.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature bowls use a soy-sauce (shoyu) based tare, and conventional shoyu is brewed with wheat — this is the seasoning backbone of the broth.

caveats: The target hint listed the broth as "豚骨" (tonkotsu/pork-bone); fetched sources consistently describe a Nagoya Cochin chicken + dried-bonito SOY broth, not pork-bone. The thin-sliced pork belly chashu (薄切りバラチャーシュー) is confirmed. No official website was found. No allergen, gluten-free, or vegan accommodation is mentioned on any source; GF/vegan judgments are based on standard ramen composition.

cuisine: 鶏白湯ラーメン (tori-paitan / chicken paitan ramen), also Taiwan mazesoba and dan-dan noodles
gf_detail: This is a standard ramen and mazesoba shop. The bowls are built on wheat ramen noodles, with a wheat-based shoyu/tare element and a rich chicken broth, so they are not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners. There is no gluten-free menu, no gluten-free noodle option, and no indication of any allergen-handling or dedicated preparation. Even the "shio" (salt) version of the chicken paitan is served over wheat noodles. Treat this as not gluten-free.
vegan_detail: The house style is built around a thick chicken (tori-paitan) broth, with chicken chashu and other meat toppings; the Taiwan mazesoba and dan-dan dishes use minced meat. There is no vegan or vegetarian ramen on offer and no plant-based broth alternative. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
address_ja: 京都府京都市下京区鶏鉾町474-5 西村ビル1階
geocode_note: Geocoded from address via GSI; building-block level point for 鶏鉾町474, within the Kyoto bounding box. Shop sits near Shijo subway / Karasuma area on Muromachi-dori, a short walk from Shijo Station.
seats_text: Seat count not published; counter-style late-night ramen shop with a touch-screen ticket machine at the storefront.
hours_raw: 11:00～24:30（LO 24:00） / 無休（臨時休業する場合があります）。Tabelog lists 10:30-00:30 daily; official site lists 11:00-24:30 (LO 24:00).
closed_days: 無休 (open daily; occasional unscheduled closures possible)
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, fast-paced late-night ramen spot with a self-service ticket machine and Japanese-only menu, so a little guidance on ordering helps, but no special etiquette is required.
neighborhood: 下京区・四条室町/烏丸 (Shijo-Muromachi, near Shijo subway and Karasuma)
caveats: This is the "四条ムロマチ" branch of the Kirameki Group (キラメキノ未来株式会社); it sits in the Shijo-Muromachi/Karasuma area rather than the 大宮 sub-area implied by the original target label. Hours differ slightly between sources (Tabelog 10:30-00:30 vs official 11:00-24:30); the official site hours are used here. No published seat count.

bio.background: キラメキノ青空 is a Kyoto-grown ramen and mazesoba brand run by the Kirameki Group (キラメキノ未来株式会社), known across Kansai as one of the pioneers of Taiwan-style mazesoba. The group built its name in student-heavy neighborhoods like Kawaramachi-Marutamachi, Ichijoji and Yamanouchi before expanding into the central Shijo-Karasuma district, where this Shijo-Muromachi shop opened as one of its newer locations.
bio.specialty: A thick, intensely rich tori-paitan (chicken white broth) ramen. The 'shio no Kirameki' salt version is served with white scallion threads, mizuna, roasted chicken chashu, fine red chili threads, menma and a lemon wedge; the kitchen also turns out Taiwan mazesoba and a dan-dan mazesoba.
bio.philosophy: The shop leans into bold, concentrated flavor and customization: diners are encouraged to tune their bowl at a condiment/seasoning bar (chili powder, the lemon wedge and other toppings) to shift the heavy chicken broth across the meal.
roles: Kirameki Group (キラメキノ未来株式会社) operator

anecdotes.text: Reviewers compare the broth's density to Tenkaiippin's famous pottage-thick soup, calling it 'ぼってりと重い鶏白湯スープ' (a heavy, almost gloopy chicken paitan) that rewards using the table condiments to vary the flavor.
The shop stays busy well past 11pm with a young crowd, with a touch-screen ticket vending machine set up outside the storefront for ordering.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard ramen and mazesoba shop: bowls are built on wheat noodles with a wheat-based shoyu/tare element, so wheat is a core ingredient throughout the menu.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is built around chicken broth and meat toppings (chicken chashu, minced meat in the mazesoba), so there is heavy meat contact across all preparations.

cuisine: 京都ラーメン chuka-soba (醤油豚骨)
neighborhood: 西京区・桂駅
address_ja: 京都府京都市西京区桂西滝川町63 サンベール桂 1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of street address returned [135.712143, 34.97855], within the Kyoto box.
seats_text: 24 seats: 12 counter seats plus 3 tables seating 4 each.
hours_raw: 火-日 11:00-翌0:00（L.O.23:30）／月曜定休。一部情報では深夜3時頃まで営業との記載あり。
closed_days: Mondays
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, cash-only neighborhood ramen counter where a few words of Japanese will go a long way and an English menu should not be assumed.
gf_detail: This is a classic Kyoto-style chuka-soba shop: the ramen is built on wheat noodles in a pork-bone-and-soy-sauce (shoyu) broth, and the soy-sauce tare is almost certainly wheat-based. No gluten-free noodles, dedicated handling, or allergen accommodation is indicated anywhere. Not safe for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: The house broth is a pork-bone (tonkotsu) base finished with soy sauce, and signature bowls are topped with chashu/roast pork. There is no vegan or vegetarian ramen offering noted; even a plain bowl would carry the pork broth. Not suitable for vegans.
caveats: Closing time is reported inconsistently across listings: the Tabelog detail shows roughly 11:00 to midnight (L.O. 23:30), while some directory listings describe service until around 3 AM. Treat late-night hours as approximate and confirm directly. No official website was found.

bio.background: A Katsura-neighborhood outpost of the long-running Kyoto ramen name Daiichi Asahi, this shop carries on a recipe and reputation built over more than fifty years, passed down through the noren-wake tradition of opening a branch under the founding house's name and methods.
bio.specialty: Kyoto-style chuka-soba: thin straight noodles in a deeply flavored shoyu broth set on a pork-bone base, crowned with sliced roast pork and a generous heap of green onion. The hearty Special Ramen is the bowl regulars reach for, and the house yakimeshi (fried rice) has its own devoted following.
bio.philosophy: Despite the richness of the pork-and-soy foundation, the broth leans on plenty of simmered vegetable stock to finish clean and light on the palate, the balance Daiichi Asahi is known for. Long daily hours and a no-frills counter make it the kind of place the neighborhood drops into at any hour, including after a night out.

anecdotes.text: The shop is a noren-wake branch of a Kyoto ramen house with over fifty years of history, carrying the name and recipe of the original.
Regulars single out the fried rice (yakimeshi) as exceptionally good alongside the ramen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is described as a pork-bone base seasoned with a deeply flavored soy sauce (shoyu), which in this style is virtually always wheat-containing.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is a pork-bone (tonkotsu) base and bowls are topped with roast pork, so animal products are integral to the dishes.

cuisine: 台湾ラーメン (spicy Taiwan-style ramen)
neighborhood: 大将軍・北野白梅町 (Kita Ward, near Kitano-Hakubaicho)
address_ja: 京都府京都市北区大将軍東鷹司町153
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 京都府京都市北区大将軍東鷹司町153 returned [135.73201, 35.023472]; within Kyoto box.
seats_text: 19 seats (counter and table seating); can accommodate groups up to about 20 for private events.
hours_raw: 11:00-14:00 / 17:00-23:00 (Tabelog); some listings show evening close at 22:30. Closed Tuesdays.
closed_days: Tuesdays
cultural_comfort_note: An English menu is available and the shop is described as welcoming to families, couples, and solo diners, so non-Japanese-speaking visitors should manage comfortably.
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation. The core dish is Taiwan ramen built on wheat ramen noodles in a pork/minced-meat-and-chili broth, and the menu (gyoza, fried chicken "Apache," yakitori) is wheat- and soy-sauce-heavy. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly shop. The signature Taiwan ramen relies on minced pork and a meat-based broth, and the broader menu centers on chicken, pork, and seafood items. No vegan or vegetarian ramen is advertised.

bio.background: らーめん屋台 (Ramen Yatai) started in the Yoshouin neighborhood of Kyoto's Minami Ward and relocated in April 2025 to the Taishogun area near Kitano-Hakubaicho, in the direction of Kinkaku-ji and Enmachi. It is a small, independent neighborhood ramen-and-izakaya hybrid.
bio.specialty: The signature bowl is 台湾ラーメン (Taiwan ramen): a spicy bowl where heat and umami intertwine over a deeply rich broth, finished with garlic, chili, minced meat, and Chinese chives (nira) that you mix thoroughly before eating. Its other famous item is the 'アパッチ (Apache)' — crispy-outside, juicy-inside deep-fried chicken wing/thigh pieces with a lingering spicy seasoning, said to pair perfectly with both beer and the Taiwan ramen.

anecdotes.text: The shop relocated from its original Yoshouin location to the Taishogun/Kitano-Hakubaicho area in April 2025.
Beyond ramen the kitchen serves gyoza, yakitori, oden, and assorted small plates with alcohol, giving it a casual izakaya feel.

caveats: Evening closing time varies by source (Tabelog lists 23:00; another listing shows 22:30). No owner/chef name or allergy-handling information was found. Safety section left empty as no specific cross-contamination or allergy info is published.

lead: 中国料理 くれたけ
name_ja: 中国料理 くれたけ
cuisine: 町中華 (neighborhood Chinese) — sesame-based tantanmen and a winter-only sake-lees ramen / sake-lees tantanmen
gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. The ramen and tantanmen are built on wheat noodles, and the broths use a soy-sauce (wheat shoyu) and sesame base over chicken/pork/vegetable stock; the sake-lees ramen dissolves sake lees into a soy-ramen base and is topped with char siu. As a town Chinese kitchen, gyoza, fried items, and stir-fries all involve shared wheat/soy. No celiac handling indicated. A coeliac diner should not eat here without a verified, separately-prepared dish — and none is documented.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan place. The signature noodles use chicken/pork/vegetable broth and char siu pork, and the tantanmen is topped with sweet-miso minced meat. Some vegetable dishes (e.g., a mixed-vegetable set, mapo tofu) exist but are not stated to be meat/animal-free and a shared meat kitchen applies. Vegans would need to ask, with no documented vegan option.
address_ja: 京都府京都市伏見区京町4-170
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned coordinates [135.764618, 34.933601] for 京都府京都市伏見区京町4-170; within the Kyoto box.
neighborhood: 伏見区・伏見桃山（伏見桃山駅・桃山御陵前駅前、大手筋通×京町通の角）
seats_text: Table seating, roughly 30–34 seats (2-person tables ×2, 4-person tables ×5, 6-person table ×1); sources vary between 30 and 34. Wood-floored dining room with partitioned tables.
hours_raw: 11:00–21:00 (Tabelog); some sources list 11:00–23:00 (L.O. 22:30) with through-service / weekday lunch specials roughly 11:00–14:00. Confirm directly.
closed_days: Open year-round per Tabelog; one source notes irregular holidays (不定休). Confirm before visiting.
cultural_comfort_note: A casual local town-Chinese spot with no English presence indicated, so a little Japanese or pointing at the menu will make ordering smooth.
caveats: Hours conflict across sources (11:00–21:00 vs 11:00–23:00) and the sake-lees source brewery is reported inconsistently (玉乃光酒造 in one feature vs the more general "Fushimi sake lees"); the prompt's 月桂冠 attribution is not confirmed by the pages fetched. The signature sake-lees ramen and sake-lees tantanmen are winter-only (roughly November to early March) and should be confirmed before a visit. There is an adjacent same-name café (喫茶くれたけ) that can cause confusion. Cash only; no reservations.

bio.background: A long-loved neighborhood Chinese kitchen (町中華) standing at the northwest corner of Oote-dori and Kyomachi-dori, right in front of Fushimi-Momoyama Station and a minute from Momoyama-Goryomae. It has become a fixture for locals who come for serious cooking served in an unfussy, drop-in setting, with a wood-floored dining room and an adjacent same-name café for Western dishes and coffee.
bio.specialty: Sesame-forward tantanmen and, in the cold months, the house's signature sake-lees ramen and sake-lees tantanmen — a nod to Fushimi's sake-brewing heritage. The sake-lees ramen dissolves sake lees into a soy-ramen base over chicken, pork and vegetable stock, finished with scallions, bean sprouts, bamboo shoots and fork-roasted char siu; the sake-lees tantanmen layers the lees over a broth of house-roasted sesame and house chili oil, topped with sweet-miso minced pork.
bio.philosophy: No flashiness, just reliable depth — whatever you order is dependably good, and the heat is kept mild enough that even spice-averse diners can enjoy the tantanmen.

anecdotes.text: The winter-only sake-lees bowls (roughly November to early March, tracking the new-sake brewing season) use sake lees from Fushimi's breweries to add a smooth, warming, complex umami — described as rich and body-warming ('身体ポカポカ').
Locals describe it as a long-established town Chinese place beloved in the neighborhood, where the mapo tofu and tantanmen carry a deep sesame aroma without being overly spicy.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Noodles are wheat and the broths use soy sauce (wheat shoyu) plus a sesame base; the kitchen is a general town-Chinese kitchen making gyoza, fried rice and stir-fries, so wheat and soy are pervasive. No gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The sake-lees ramen is explicitly a soy-sauce ramen (醤油ラーメン) with sake lees dissolved in, indicating standard wheat-containing soy sauce in the tare.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Signature bowls use chicken/pork/vegetable broth and char siu, and the tantanmen is topped with minced-meat miso; no vegan preparation is documented.

lead: 麺屋 栩羚廚 壬生本店
name_ja: 麺屋 栩羚廚 壬生本店
cuisine: 濃厚豚骨ラーメン（極濃クリーミー京都豚骨／極濃豚骨・旨辛豚骨）
gf_detail: No gluten-free options. The bowls are built on wheat noodles (from the Teigaku/棣鄂 noodle maker), a tonkotsu broth finished with what is almost certainly a wheat-based shoyu tare, and a flour-dredged karaage side. There is no indication of any gluten-free preparation or separate handling, so this is not suitable for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The entire concept is an ultra-rich pork-bone (tonkotsu) broth, with chashu, marinated egg, and other animal toppings; even the side dishes are meat. No plant-based broth or noodle option is offered.
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区壬生馬場町19-17
geocode_note: GSI address search returned a precise point for 壬生馬場町19-17; coordinates fall within the Kyoto bounding box.
neighborhood: 中京区・壬生馬場町（壬生エリア／二条駅・四条大宮・千本三条）
seats_text: 8席（1階カウンターのみ／L字カウンター）
hours_raw: 11:00-15:00 / 17:00-22:00（ラストオーダー各30分前）、火曜定休（不定休あり・Instagram要確認）
closed_days: 火曜（不定休あり）
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny eight-seat counter shop run on a ticket-vending-machine system, so you can order with almost no Japanese, though a friendly greeting and a little patience go a long way in such a small room.
caveats: Only 8 counter seats on the first floor, cash only, no parking, and a sometimes-irregular schedule on top of the regular Tuesday closure (the shop posts changes to Instagram). Some bowls such as the 極濃豚骨味玉 are made in limited daily quantities.

bio.background: 麺屋 栩羚廚（くれいず）opened on September 12, 2024 on Kouin-dori between Shijo-Omiya and Sanjo-Senbon, an area of central Kyoto in the middle of a wave of new ramen openings. It is a small, design-conscious counter shop that put down roots fast: within months it had drawn a steady following and, in February 2025, opened a second branch in Tokyo's Nakameguro.
bio.origin: 京都市中京区壬生馬場町（壬生エリア）
bio.specialty: The house specialty is an extremely thick, creamy Kyoto-pork tonkotsu. Two core styles anchor the menu — a 濃厚豚骨醤油 (rich tonkotsu-shoyu) and the 極濃豚骨, a paste-thick 'doro-kei' broth poured with a layer of flavorful tare on top that you stir in before eating; a 旨辛豚骨 spiced with ichimi togarashi sits between them. The signature bowl is the limited 極濃豚骨味玉 (¥1,250), built on flat, chewy wheat noodles from the respected Teigaku (棣鄂) noodle maker and finished with squared-cut chashu, a marinated egg, bamboo shoots, and scallion.
bio.philosophy: A 'material-commitment' approach paired with careful plating — a richly concentrated pork broth that locals rate among the thickest in Kyoto yet keeps the typical tonkotsu off-smell in check, served with attentive hospitality in a stylish little room.

anecdotes.text: Diners are offered complimentary burnt-garlic oil (焦がしにんにく油) to add partway through the bowl, deepening the umami and giving the broth a second character mid-meal.
The shop's popularity grew quickly after its September 2024 opening, leading to a Tokyo (Nakameguro) branch in February 2025.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Bowls use wheat ramen noodles (from the Teigaku/棣鄂 maker) cooked in shared boiling water, and the karaage side is flour-dredged and deep-fried; no gluten-free handling is indicated.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The core 濃厚豚骨醤油 and the tare layered over the 極濃豚骨 are soy-sauce based, which in Japan is almost always brewed with wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Every bowl is built on a pork-bone broth with animal toppings (chashu, egg); there is no separate vegan prep area or plant-based option.

lead: 京都中華そば 徳光
name_ja: 屋台ラーメン 京都中華そば 徳光
name_en: Yatai Ramen Kyoto Chuka Soba Tokumitsu
cuisine: 町中華そば（屋台ラーメン）／背脂醤油・京だし中華そば
neighborhood: 右京区・西院／西院駅周辺
address_ja: 京都府京都市右京区西院三蔵町19
geocode_note: GSI geocode of registered address 西院三蔵町19 returned [135.731766, 35.002911], within the Kyoto box. Note: this is a mobile yatai (food cart) that relocates nightly — it most often parks at coin-parking lots around the 西院交差点 / 西院駅 area but is 神出鬼没 (location not fixed). Current spot is announced via Instagram. Coordinates indicate the registered/anchor address, not a guaranteed nightly location.
gf_detail: Not safe for celiac or strict gluten-free. This is a classic Kyoto-style chuka soba: wheat noodles in a pork-bone soy-sauce (shoyu) broth with back fat, and the shoyu tare almost certainly contains wheat. No gluten-free noodles, dedicated equipment, or allergy accommodation is indicated, and as a single-operator truck-bed yatai there is no separate prep space. Avoid if you require gluten-free.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. All bowls are built on a pork-bone (tonkotsu) soy broth with back fat and are topped with chashu (pork). No vegetable or vegan broth option is offered. There is nothing on the menu suitable for a strict vegan or vegetarian diner.
seats_text: About 4-5 standing/counter spots at the cart built into the bed of a converted light truck (kei-truck); sources vary between roughly 4 and 5. You eat right at the truck and can watch the bowl being made.
hours_raw: 19:00頃〜25:00（深夜1:00）頃。不定休。屋台のため営業日・出店場所は不定で、最新情報はInstagramで告知。
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular). Operating nights and location vary; the cart does not run every night. Always check the Instagram (@yatairamen_tokumitsu2486) for that day's status and parking spot before heading out.
cultural_comfort_note: A late-night Japanese street-cart ramen run with a young local owner and only a few spots at the truck — basic Japanese and an easygoing, go-with-the-flow attitude (it moves and keeps irregular hours) will serve you best; little to no English expected.
caveats: This is a roving yatai, not a fixed storefront — confirm the night's location on Instagram before going. Cash only, very limited spots, and no allergy accommodation. Hours shown (19:00-25:00) are typical operating windows on nights it runs, not a guarantee it opens every day.

bio.background: A rare survivor of an almost-vanished Kyoto tradition: the late-night ramen yatai. Tokumitsu launched on 28 November 2023, run by a young owner out of the cargo bed of a converted kei-truck, after test runs around Shichijo-Karasuma and in front of the Join Building in Saiin. Rather than a fixed shop, it rolls out after dark and parks at spots around the Saiin crossing, announcing each night's location on social media.
bio.specialty: The signature bowl is the 背脂チャッチャ (seabura chaccha) chuka soba — old-school Kyoto-style ramen: a pork-bone soy-sauce broth crowned with a generous shower of back fat, topped with chashu, with the salty-sharp snap of shoyu over slightly soft, chewy noodles (~780 yen). Alongside it are a lighter 京だし (Kyo-dashi) chuka soba and a punchy 旨辛ニンニク辛麺 (spicy garlic) bowl (~800 yen).
bio.philosophy: 「店舗さながらの味を屋台で」 — to deliver shop-quality ramen from a food cart. The owner leans into the romance of the form: an unmarked truck under a red lantern, a handful of spots, and a warm bowl eaten outdoors on a cold night.

anecdotes.text: Because the cart roves and keeps irregular hours, regulars treat finding it as a small stroke of luck — 神出鬼没 (appearing and vanishing) is part of its charm; you track it down via its Instagram.
The owner has described the appeal plainly: 「寒空の下で食べる温かいラーメンは格別な味わいでした」 — a hot bowl eaten under a cold sky tastes like nothing else.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Single-operator truck-bed yatai serving wheat-noodle ramen in one shared pot/workspace; no separate gluten-free prep is possible and none is advertised.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Broth is a pork-bone soy-sauce (shoyu) base — 「豚骨醤油のスープにたっぷりの背脂」 — and the shoyu tare typically contains wheat. Not gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Every bowl is pork-bone broth with back fat and chashu; there is no vegan/vegetarian option and all prep shares the pork-based broth.

lead: 麺匠 一粒万倍 河原町店
name_ja: 麺匠 一粒万倍 河原町店
cuisine: つけ麺・濃厚魚介系ラーメン
gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The bowls are built on wheat: ramen noodles, tsukemen noodles, and mazesoba noodles are all wheat-based (the noodles even use whole-grain wheat flour for flavor), and the shoyu tare is wheat-based soy sauce. Broths are chicken and duck stock. There is no published gluten-free menu, no rice-noodle option, and no indication of allergen accommodation. A celiac diner should not eat here.
vegan_detail: Not vegan or vegetarian friendly. Every signature item is animal-based: the broth is chicken-and-duck (鶏と鴨) clear soup, toppings include chicken and pork chashu, and the mazesoba uses chicken oil and minced chashu. There is no vegan or vegetarian ramen on the menu and no plant-based broth alternative.
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区大黒町71番地 ワールド会館 1階
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI from the chōme-level address (中京区大黒町71); coordinates fall within the Kyoto bounding box near Kawaramachi/Sanjo.
neighborhood: 中京区 河原町（三条／木屋町・先斗町近く、阪急京都河原町駅・京阪三条駅から徒歩圏）
seats_text: About 20 seats; counter and table seating, with a ticket-vending-machine ordering system.
closed_days: No regular closing day listed (open daily / 年中無休); hours run late into the night (until ~3:00 AM).
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, ticket-machine ramen counter open late into the night; a little Japanese helps with the vending-machine ordering, but pointing and basic greetings will get you through.

bio.background: A late-night ramen specialist in central Kyoto's Kawaramachi district, tucked into the World Building on Daikoku-cho near the Kiyamachi and Pontocho nightlife streets. The shop is part of the small Ichiryu Manbai noodle group, which also runs a Teramachi branch nearby.
bio.specialty: A clear (清湯) double broth built on chicken and duck, finished with a crisp, sharp soy-sauce tare for the signature shoyu ramen (らぁ麺). The kitchen also turns out a tsukemen and a mazesoba that mixes thick flat noodles with chicken-oil sauce, minced chashu, red onion, and a truffle-oil-dressed egg yolk with lemon to shift the flavor partway through.
bio.philosophy: Care put into balance and texture: thin curly noodles cut with whole-grain wheat flour to carry more aroma, paired with a clean but assertive shoyu broth and carefully prepared toppings (chicken and pork chashu, bamboo shoots, soft egg, mitsuba/parsley).

anecdotes.text: The mazesoba is described by a noodle blogger as having a sticky, clinging texture close to an abura-soba, brightened at the table with truffle-oil egg yolk and a squeeze of lemon.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen/tsukemen/mazesoba noodles (including whole-grain wheat flour) are boiled in shared water; not safe for celiac or wheat-allergic diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The shoyu tare flavoring the broth is wheat-based soy sauce; allergen note lists wheat in both noodles and soy sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is chicken-and-duck stock and toppings include chicken and pork chashu and egg; no plant-based preparation, so all items carry animal cross-contact.

caveats: Hours expressed as 11:00-27:00 (i.e., until 3:00 AM next day); confirmed across Tabelog and the Hankyu-Hanshin town guide. Exact seat count (~20) and "no regular holiday" come from Tabelog. No chef name or founding story is published, so bio confidence is medium. No allergen/GF/vegan accommodation is advertised anywhere found.

lead: 麺心 よし田 別店なし — 揚子江
cuisine: 町中華・中華そば
neighborhood: 伏見区 中書島（京阪 中書島駅）
gf_detail: No gluten-free provision. This is a classic neighborhood Chinese (machi-chuka) shop: the noodles in the ramen / chuka-soba and wonton-men are wheat, the dumpling wrappers are wheat, and dishes such as sweet-and-sour pork and fried chicken are wheat-floured and deep-fried. Stir-fries and sauces rely on standard (wheat-containing) soy sauce and seasonings, with shared woks and a shared fryer. Not suitable for celiac diners; no allergen menu or GF accommodation was found.
vegan_detail: No vegan offering. The kitchen is meat- and seafood-centered (pork, chicken, crab omelette, char siu) and broths are chicken-based. Even vegetable dishes are cooked in shared woks with animal-based seasonings, and no plant-based menu was found. A strict vegan would not be accommodated here.
address_ja: 京都府京都市伏見区西柳町545-2
geocode_note: GSI geocode for 京都府京都市伏見区西柳町545-2 returned [135.759674, 34.928837]. Latitude is ~0.0012° (about 135 m) below the stated Kyoto box minimum of 34.93, but the longitude is in range and the point sits correctly in Nakashojima, Fushimi (a 3-min walk from Keihan Chushojima Station), matching the Tabelog map. Coordinates retained as the best available fix.
seats_text: About 30 seats: roughly 10 counter seats on the 1st floor and tatami banquet seating for about 20 on the 2nd floor.
hours_raw: 11:30-14:30, 17:00-22:00 (sources vary; some list continuous 11:30-23:00 with last order ~22:30). Closed Tuesdays.
closed_days: Tuesdays
