cultural_comfort_note: A homey, locals-first neighborhood Chinese diner where ordering is in Japanese and a few basic phrases plus pointing at the menu will carry you through comfortably.

bio.background: Yosuko is a long-running, locally loved town-Chinese (machi-chuka) restaurant tucked into the Nakashojima quarter of Fushimi, a three-minute walk from Keihan Chushojima Station. It occupies a two-storey building with a counter on the ground floor and a tatami banquet room upstairs, and carries the warm, slightly retro feel of a Showa-era neighborhood diner that families and solo regulars have leaned on for years.
bio.specialty: The signature bowl is the wonton-men (wonton ramen): a clear chicken-bone broth with straight noodles, bean sprouts and char siu, and notably springy, chewy house wonton skins. The other house favorite is the Yosuko Lunch plate, which piles sweet-and-sour pork, crab omelette and fried chicken with salad and pickles alongside rice and soup.
bio.philosophy: Honest, orthodox Chinese cooking at neighborhood prices: an extensive, budget-friendly menu of noodles, rice plates and single dishes available throughout the day, served in a relaxed family atmosphere where non-drinkers and groups are equally at home.

anecdotes.text: Reviewers single out the wonton-men for its unusually springy, chewy wonton skins over a straight-noodle, chicken-bone broth, and praise the sweet-and-sour pork's crisp-fried pork and still-crunchy vegetables.
There is a separate, unrelated restaurant of the same name near Takeda Station; this is the Nakashojima location in Fushimi.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat noodles, wheat dumpling wrappers, and wheat-floured deep-fried items (sweet-and-sour pork, fried chicken) are core to the menu, with shared woks and a shared fryer; no gluten-free handling was found.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a traditional Chinese diner, dishes and sauces use standard soy sauce and seasonings, which in Japan are wheat-based unless stated otherwise; no wheat-free soy sauce is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Kitchen is centered on pork, chicken, crab and char siu with chicken-based broths and shared woks, so vegetable dishes carry a high risk of animal-based cross-contact.

caveats: Hours vary slightly across listings: most show two services (11:30-14:30 and 17:00-22:00), while some sources show continuous service to around 23:00. Closed Tuesdays. Cash only. No official website found; details drawn from Tabelog and local Kyoto food blogs. No GF or vegan accommodation. Geocode latitude is marginally below the Kyoto box edge (see geocode_note). Note a same-named, unrelated shop exists near Takeda Station — this record is the Nakashojima/Chushojima location.

name_ja: つけめんTETSU イオンモールKYOTO
cuisine: つけ麺（濃厚豚骨魚介つけめん／あつもり・焼き石でつけ汁を熱々に保つ）
gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The signature bowl is extra-thick wheat noodles (blended with udon flour) dipped in a rich pork-bone-and-seafood broth, with a wheat-based shoyu/miso tare. No gluten-free menu, no dedicated prep, and as a busy mall food-court stall there is no allowance for managing cross-contamination. Avoid if you have celiac disease or a wheat allergy.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The core product is built on a pork-bone-and-seafood (豚骨魚介) broth, topped with chashu pork and finished with animal-derived tare; menma and noodles are cooked in shared equipment. There is no vegan or vegetarian option advertised.
address_ja: 京都府京都市南区西九条鳥居口町1 イオンモールKYOTO 4F フードコート
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 京都市南区西九条鳥居口町1 returned [135.754532, 34.982719], within the Kyoto box (lat 34.93–35.13, lng 135.66–135.83). Matches the AEON Mall KYOTO location on the Hachijo-guchi side of Kyoto Station.
seats_text: Counter/table seating within the AEON Mall KYOTO 4F food court; exact seat count not published (shared food-court seating).
closed_days: 不定休（イオンモールKYOTOの営業に準ずる）
cultural_comfort_note: A casual mall food-court stall where you order at the counter and carry your tray, so a friendly konnichiwa and pointing at the menu is all you need.

bio.background: TETSU began in 2005 as a tiny 10-tsubo (about 33 sqm) shop in Sendagi, Tokyo, founded by Kazunori Komiya. Starting with just 3 million yen in capital, he grew it into a tsukemen brand of roughly 30 shops centered on Tokyo. This AEON Mall KYOTO branch opened on October 17, 2023, marking the brand's return to Kyoto more than a decade after its earlier Kyoto Ramen Koji shop inside the Kyoto Station Building closed in 2011.
bio.origin: Sendagi, Tokyo (founded 2005); this branch is a Kyoto outpost of the national TETSU chain.
bio.specialty: The signature is a rich pork-bone-and-seafood (豚骨魚介) tsukemen with extra-thick noodles blended with udon flour to stand up to the heavy dipping broth. Miso and spicy-miso dipping versions are also popular here, and the kitchen rounds out the menu with a chashu rice bowl and a chuka-soba.
bio.philosophy: An uncompromising obsession with keeping the broth hot to the very last bite, expressed through their trademark hot-stone (焼き石) service.

anecdotes.text: TETSU's trademark move solves tsukemen's classic weak point of broth going lukewarm: a stone heated until scorching is dropped into the dipping broth partway through the meal so it stays piping hot until the final drop.
Founder Kazunori Komiya turned a 3-million-yen, 10-tsubo Sendagi shop opened in 2005 into a roughly 30-location tsukemen chain.
The Kyoto branch opened in the AEON Mall KYOTO food court on October 17, 2023, returning the brand to Kyoto after its station-building shop had closed in 2011.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat noodles boiled in shared water in a single open food-court kitchen; no gluten-free handling. Not suitable for celiac diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Tsukemen broth/tare uses wheat-based shoyu and miso; the noodles themselves are wheat (blended with udon flour).

vegan_cross_contact.text: All bowls are based on pork-bone-and-seafood broth with pork chashu; no separate vegan prep area.

sources: https://msearch.gsi.go.jp/address-search/AddressSearch?q=京都府京都市南区西九条鳥居口町1
caveats: Seat count is not published because seating is shared within the mall food court. Closed days are irregular and follow AEON Mall KYOTO's calendar. This is a branch of a Tokyo-based chain (not independent / not mom-and-pop), categorized OMNI.

cuisine: Tanmen (vegetable shio ramen) specialist
neighborhood: Ono, Yamashina-ku
gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. Tanmen is built on wheat ramen noodles in a salt (shio) tare; the broth and house salt sauce are not documented as wheat-free, and there are no gluten-free noodle or accommodation options on the menu. No allergen handling is documented. Anyone with celiac disease or wheat allergy should treat this as unsafe.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians. The signature tanmen is a chicken-bone and pork-based salt broth topped with stir-fried pork and vegetables; pork and a chicken/pork stock are integral to every bowl. No plant-based broth or vegetable-only version is documented. A diner could potentially ask about a vegetable-heavy customization, but the base soup is animal-derived.
address_ja: 京都府京都市山科区小野鐘付田町8-1 第2ロイヤルハイツ和田 1F
geocode_note: Geocoded from address_ja via GSI; coordinates [135.813492, 34.962307] fall within the Kyoto box. Matches Ono, Yamashina-ku near Ono Station.
seats_text: 16 seats (8 counter, 8 table)
hours_raw: Lunch 11:00-14:30, Dinner 18:00-22:00 (per Tabelog). One blog lists dinner as 18:00-21:00; treat evening close time as approximate. No regular closed day listed.
closed_days: None listed (open daily)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, friendly neighborhood counter-and-table shop where Japanese phrases for ordering and customizing your bowl will go a long way; English support is not documented.
caveats: Dinner closing time varies by source (Tabelog 22:00; one blog 21:00) — confirm for late visits. Phone number is not publicly listed; reach out via Instagram. Allergen and ingredient details are not published, so cross-contact and tare composition are unconfirmed.

bio.background: Iwasaki Tanmen opened its doors on March 1, 2023 in the Ono neighborhood of Yamashina, just steps from Ono Station, taking over a storefront that previously housed another ramen shop (Shioemon). It is a rarity in Kyoto: a shop devoted to tanmen, a vegetable-piled salt ramen far more common in the Kanto region of eastern Japan than here in Kansai.
bio.specialty: The house tanmen is the signature: a mound of lightly stir-fried vegetables — cabbage, bean sprouts and more — over medium straight noodles in a chicken-and-pork salt (shio) broth. It arrives looking hearty and Jiro-esque, yet the soup is surprisingly clean and light, with no heavy back-fat. A pot of house chili-miso (kara-miso) sits at hand so you can stir in heat and depth gradually as you eat.
bio.philosophy: Make-it-your-own simplicity: diners can dial the vegetables, pork, noodle quantity, garlic and spice to taste, and garlic is served on the side so each guest controls their own portion. Sides like gyoza, karaage and takana rice round out an honest, affordable bowl.

anecdotes.text: The shop offers several tanmen styles beyond the original — a shio-tonkotsu, a rich miso and a spicy version — so regulars can rotate through the lineup.
One local blogger called the proprietor very kind and admitted he came back years after his first visit because he simply could not forget the tanmen.
The bowl starts around 690 yen, keeping it genuinely budget-friendly.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The core dish uses wheat ramen noodles in a shared salt broth; there is no published gluten-free option and the kitchen is a small single-line operation, so cross-contact with wheat should be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The shop's tare is described as a house-made salt (shio) sauce rather than a soy-based one; however, its full composition is not published, so wheat content cannot be confirmed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegetables are stir-fried together with pork, and the broth is chicken/pork based, so vegetable toppings share animal ingredients and equipment.

positives.text: Diners can customize vegetables, pork, noodle amount, garlic and spice level, with garlic served separately for portion control — useful for tailoring a bowl, though not an allergen accommodation.

lead: 伴製麺所 京都亀岡本店
name_ja: 京都ラーメン 伴製麺所 京都亀岡本店
name_en: Kyoto Ramen Ban Seimensho Kyoto Kameoka Honten
cuisine: Kyoto ramen — aged-shoyu, shiro-shoyu (white soy), miso, and tori-paitan (chicken broth) bowls with in-house noodles
gf_detail: No gluten-free option. The bowls are built on house-made low-hydration wheat noodles, an aged soy-sauce (shoyu) tare that contains wheat, and chicken-bone broth; side dishes include fried rice, karaage (battered fried chicken), and tenshinhan. Nothing here is celiac-safe and there is no published allergen accommodation.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. Every ramen is chicken-bone (tori-gara) or chicken-paitan based with wheat-shoyu tare; sides are fried rice, fried chicken, and egg dishes. No plant-based broth or vegan bowl is offered.
address_ja: 京都府亀岡市下矢田町3-16-5
geocode_note: Address-level GSI geocode returned [lng 135.578552, lat 35.007504]; chō-level retry returned [135.568298, 34.990574]. Longitude (~135.57) falls west of the provided Kyoto box minimum of 135.66. This is expected and correct: Kameoka City sits in the basin NW of central Kyoto, genuinely outside the central-city box, so coordinates are retained rather than nulled.
neighborhood: Shimoyada-cho, Kameoka City (along Route 9, suburban Kyoto NW of the city)
seats_text: Counter seating in front of the kitchen plus several four-person box/table seats in a rustic converted folk-house style space; spacious enough for families. Exact seat count not published.
closed_days: No regular closing day listed.
cultural_comfort_note: A roadside family ramen shop in suburban Kameoka with parking and no English presence — comfortable for a confident independent visitor, but plan to order in Japanese or by pointing.
caveats: Opened September 8, 2025, so the operation is still new. Evening hours and closed-day information vary between listings (some show 17:00-22:00, others 18:00-23:00), so confirm same-day before a late visit. The shop carries the name of a Maizuru noodle maker said to date to 1915, but a direct operational link to that historic factory could not be independently confirmed; it operates under a Tokyo-based group with sister shops elsewhere.

bio.background: Opened on September 8, 2025 in the Shimoyada-cho district of Kameoka, in the quiet basin northwest of central Kyoto, this ramen shop took over a converted folk-house space (formerly an eatery called Nami no Hana) along Route 9. It carries the name of a Maizuru noodle maker said to trace back to 1915, leaning on a製麺所 (noodle-factory) identity and house-made noodles as its calling card.
bio.specialty: The flagship is the 京都熟成醤油ラーメン (Kyoto aged-soy-sauce ramen): a clear chicken-bone broth married to an aged shoyu tare, with low-hydration medium-thin straight noodles. It is described as gently restrained in seasoning — the classic, mellow Kyoto-ramen profile rather than a heavy or fiery one. Alongside it the shop pours a 白醤油 (white-soy) ramen, a miso ramen, and a 鶏白湯 (chicken-paitan) bowl.
bio.philosophy: The shop frames itself around its own noodles and an 'aged-soy' Kyoto style — squarely within Kyoto's established ramen tradition, aiming for an approachable, family-friendly bowl rather than a niche or extreme one.

anecdotes.text: The signature aged-shoyu bowl pairs a chicken-bone broth with an aged soy-sauce tare and low-hydration medium-thin straight noodles, evoking the gentle, restrained flavor characteristic of traditional Kyoto ramen.
Set in a rustic converted folk-house space with counter seats facing the kitchen and four-person box seats, with parking on site — built for families and roadside visitors in suburban Kameoka.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The flagship bowl uses an aged soy-sauce (熟成醤油) tare, which is standard wheat-containing Japanese shoyu.

cuisine: Clear-dashi komugi-soba (artisanal handmade wheat noodles in fish-dashi broth, ramen-adjacent)
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature bowl centers on house-made noodles cut from domestic wheat and egg, so the core dish contains gluten. The broth itself is a clean fish dashi (Rishiri kombu, sawara/sagoshi, bonito) seasoned with salt only and no soy sauce, which removes one common wheat source — but the wheat noodle makes this unsafe for celiacs. No dedicated gluten-free preparation is advertised; a celiac diner would need to ask directly and likely cannot be accommodated.
vegan_detail: Not vegan or vegetarian. The dashi is entirely animal-based (sawara/Spanish mackerel, bonito flakes, plus kombu), and the noodles contain egg. There is no plant-based bowl on offer.
address_ja: 京都府京都市左京区一乗寺樋之口町27 コーポラス禅 1F
geocode_note: Geocoded from address_ja via GSI; first feature coordinates [135.79213, 35.03883], within the Kyoto box.
neighborhood: Ichijoji, Sakyo-ku
seats_text: Small counter-style shop; exact seat count not published. Non-smoking, no private rooms.
hours_raw: Wed–Thu, Sun 09:00–15:00; Fri–Sat 11:00–21:00 (L.O. 20:00); Mon–Tue closed. From around 16:00 the space shifts to a dashi retail shop / sake bar on the late-evening days.
closed_days: Mondays and Tuesdays
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny owner-run dashi specialist where the proprietor talks you through the craft; some Japanese (or a translation app) goes a long way, and a quiet, curious attitude fits the room.
caveats: The signature morning bowl is a breakfast-limited item served on the daytime (09:00–15:00) days; on Fri/Sat the place leans more toward an evening dashi shop and sake bar, so confirm the noodle dish is being served when you plan to go. Reservations are accepted and given priority. Budget runs higher than a typical ramen-ya.

bio.background: The shop is the latest evolution of a Kyoto noodle maker who has spent years chasing dashi rather than richness. The lineage runs from a ramen shop, Isao, that built its bowls on Japanese dashi, which the owner then renamed and reframed around 2014 as Odashi to Komugi 130 (おだしと小麦 一三〇) to put the dashi and the wheat front and center. In June 2022 the concept was distilled again into だし料理 十.
bio.origin: Ichijoji, Sakyo-ku, Kyoto — on the city's famous Ichijoji ramen street, but a deliberate step away from the heavy-broth pack.
bio.specialty: The signature bowl is the breakfast-only o-dashi no new-men: a clear soup built from just three things — Rishiri kombu, sawara (young Spanish mackerel / sagoshi), and bonito flakes — seasoned with a single gram of Iwate salt and no soy sauce at all. The noodles are made in-house from domestic wheat and egg, cut to roughly 1mm square and dusted with Hokkaido corn flour, then finished with hand-shaved bonito and a few pieces of salt-cured pepper.
bio.philosophy: The whole place is built so customers can actually understand dashi — you can watch it being made, taste it, and talk it through with the owner. Seasoning is pared back to almost nothing so the stock itself carries the bowl.

anecdotes.text: By late afternoon the shop sheds its noodle-counter identity and becomes a dashi retail shop and small sake bar, selling drinkable dashi (nomu o-dashi) and easy dashi kits so people can take the craft home.
Tasters describe being unexpectedly moved by the bowl — 出汁のふくよかな香りとまろやかな口当たりに思わず感動 (the rich aroma and rounded mouthfeel of the dashi genuinely move you).

gf_cross_contamination.text: The house-made noodles are cut from domestic wheat (and egg) in-house, so flour is handled on-site; a shared-kitchen wheat environment should be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature dashi bowl uses no soy sauce at all — it is seasoned with about 1 gram of Iwate salt only — so the usual wheat-shoyu tare risk is absent from the broth (the wheat comes from the noodles instead).

vegan_cross_contact.text: The dashi is animal-based throughout (sawara/Spanish mackerel and bonito alongside kombu), and the noodles contain egg, so there is no clean vegan or vegetarian path.

positives.text: Because the owner makes and explains the dashi in front of guests, it is easy to ask exactly what is in the bowl, and the seasoning is unusually transparent: kombu, sawara, bonito, salt, and house wheat-and-egg noodles.

lead: 京都北山 元町ラーメン 四条烏丸本店
name_ja: 京都北山 元町ラーメン 四条烏丸本店
name_en: Kyoto Kitayama Motomachi Ramen Shijo-Karasuma Honten
cuisine: 濃厚黒醤油・背脂ラーメン（富山ブラック系の屋台ルーツ、牛すじも名物）
vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian options. The broth is a rich black soy sauce ramen built on pork/beef bases (beef tendon is a signature), the noodles are wheat, and the tare uses wheat-containing tamari soy sauce. Nothing on the menu is plant-based.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free and not celiac-safe. The ramen uses wheat noodles, a wheat-based tamari/black soy sauce tare (sourced from a long-established Kyoto soy sauce maker), and pork/beef broth. There is no gluten-free menu, no dedicated equipment, and no indication staff accommodate gluten allergies.
address_ja: 京都府京都市中京区東洞院通錦小路下る坂東屋町664-27
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned coordinates [135.764404, 35.011112] for the full street address, within the Kyoto bounding box.
neighborhood: 中京区坂東屋町（阪急烏丸駅18番出口から徒歩約5分、大丸京都店・東洞院出口向かいの路地奥）
seats_text: Counter seating for 11 on the 1st floor (non-smoking) plus table seating for 16 on the 2nd floor; roughly 27 seats total.
hours_raw: 11:00-24:30 (L.O. 24:00), 日曜定休。スープがなくなり次第終了。
closed_days: 日曜日（Sundays）
cultural_comfort_note: A cash-only, counter-style local ramen shop with no English service signaled; basic Japanese or a translation app will make ordering smoother.
caveats: Cash only, no reservations, and the shop closes early once the day's broth runs out, so it can shut before the listed closing time. Hours are effectively irregular for that reason. Wheat- and meat-based throughout, so unsuitable for gluten-free, vegan, or vegetarian diners.

bio.background: Kyoto Kitayama Motomachi Ramen traces its roots to a street cart (yatai) that the first-generation owner — a native of Kanazawa — set up on Shinmachi-dori in Kyoto's Kitayama district roughly 50 years ago. Drawing on the rich, dark soy-sauce style of nearby Toyama Black, he built a Kyoto take on the bowl using tamari soy sauce from a long-established local soy-sauce maker. The business later moved into a permanent shop, settling at its current location in an alley off Higashinotoin in central Kyoto, where the cart's original flavor is carried on by a later generation.
bio.origin: Kyoto (Kitayama district yatai origins); founder from Kanazawa
bio.specialty: A rich black-soy-sauce ramen finished with plenty of back fat (背脂醤油らーめん), with beef-tendon ramen (牛すじらーめん) as a second signature. The defining element is a specialty tamari soy sauce of the kind used for eel sauce, sourced from a 160-year-old Kyoto soy-sauce maker.
bio.philosophy: Honor the original yatai flavor while continuing to refine it; serve until the broth is gone and then close for the day.

anecdotes.text: The founder, from Kanazawa, based his recipe on the dark, bold style of Toyama Black but reinterpreted it for Kyoto using local tamari soy sauce.
The shop began as a Kitayama Shinmachi street cart about 50 years ago, and a later generation continues the cart's original taste at the current shop.
The tamari soy sauce is supplied by a 160-year-old established Kyoto soy-sauce shop and is of the type used for eel sauce.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The bowl is built on a tamari/black soy sauce tare; tamari and standard soy sauce here are wheat-containing and central to the broth.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth and toppings are pork/beef based (beef tendon is a signature), so no item is free of animal products.

name_ja: 油そば専門店 ムジコ
cuisine: Aburasoba (brothless oil soba) specialist
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans — lard-based shoyu tare plus pork and fish toppings in every bowl.
gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. This is wheat-noodle aburasoba (thick wheat noodles by Kyoto noodle maker Teigaku/棣鄂) dressed in a lard-and-soy-sauce tare; standard soy sauce contains wheat. No gluten-free noodle or tare is available, and there is no published allergen handling. A celiac diner should avoid this shop.
address_ja: 京都府京都市上京区千本通竹屋町下ル聚楽町863-25 朝田ビル 1F
geocode_note: GSI address-search on 京都府京都市上京区聚楽町863-25 returned first feature coordinates [135.743149, 35.016205]; within Kyoto box.
seats_text: Counter only. Tabelog lists 6 counter seats; some shop write-ups mention 7-9, so expect a tiny single-row counter of roughly 6-9 stools.
hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-15:00 (L.O. 15:00), Dinner 18:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:30); closed Thursday (per Tabelog). Note: some earlier write-ups list closed Monday and slightly later closing (15:30 / 22:30) and an opening-era 11:30-15:30/18:00-22:30 schedule — confirm the current day before visiting.
closed_days: Thursday
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny local counter shop where ordering and table-condiment customization happen in Japanese; a few words of Japanese (or pointing at the menu) will smooth the visit.
neighborhood: Kamigyo-ku, Shuraku-cho — on Senbon-dori a short walk north of Nijo Station, opposite a 7-Eleven
caveats: Hours and closed-day vary between sources (Tabelog: closed Thursday; opening-era and some blogs: closed Monday); confirm before going. Cash not required — QR payments (PayPay, au PAY, Alipay, WeChat Pay) accepted, but credit cards and IC/electronic money are not, so bring cash or a QR app. Six-ish counter seats only, so expect a wait at peak; no reservations.

bio.background: Mujiko grew out of a long-held wish to give Kyoto — a city with surprisingly few oil-soba specialists — a shop dedicated to the dish. The project came from a music-world company (the playful name 'Mujiko' nods to 'music'), and after roughly six years of planning they installed Okada, who had passed through a record shop and a live house before training about a year and a half at a Kyoto ramen kitchen, as manager. The shop opened July 30, 2019 in the back tenant of the building that also houses the team's popular spice-curry bar 240 (Niko-yon), tucked on Senbon-dori just north of Nijo Station across from a 7-Eleven.
bio.specialty: Aburasoba: thick, lightly wavy wheat noodles from venerable Kyoto noodle maker Teigaku (棣鄂), tossed in a simple lard-and-soy-sauce tare and topped with chashu, menma, white and green scallions, nori and thread chili. Beyond the standard bowl, regulars chase the seafood-niboshi (dried sardine) version, a ginger oil soba, and a numbing-spicy bowl built on the house-made chili oil. The bold, heavily seasoned karaage is a much-loved side.
bio.philosophy: Keep the base flavor clean and simple, then hand the diner the controls: the counter is lined with chili oil, rice vinegar, ground sesame, black pepper, ichimi, sansho and curry powder so each guest tunes their own bowl — and the small/regular/large/extra-large noodle portions are all the same price.

anecdotes.text: The name 'Mujiko' and its origins trace to the music world — the owning company came from records and live-house roots, and the manager himself worked at a record shop and a live house before switching to noodles.
Opening the shop was a six-year labor of love: 'a concept to open an oil-soba specialist in Kyoto, after about six years of perseverance.'
It is the noodle sibling of the same group's spice-curry bar 240 (Niko-yon), which sits right beside it in the same building.
All four portion sizes are offered at no extra charge, and the table is stocked with an unusually generous spread of condiments for customizing the bowl.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Aburasoba is made with thick wheat noodles supplied by Kyoto noodle maker Teigaku (棣鄂); there is no gluten-free option.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The bowls are dressed in a lard-based soy-sauce tare (ラードがベースの醤油ダレ); standard soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The tare is lard-based and every bowl includes chashu pork and menma, plus fish-derived elements in the niboshi version — no vegan preparation exists.
